Anthropic’s agents won’t kill SaaS. But something else will.

The “SaaSpocalypse” narrative is everywhere right now. When Anthropic dropped Claude Cowork plugins in Feb, $285 billion+ evaporated from software valuations in 48 hours. 
I’ll be the first to admit: I have skin in the game. As an early-stage AI investor and founder of Freshworks, my perspective is naturally colored by that journey. But looking past the hype, here is my honest take on where we are actually headed.

1. Will “Vibe Coding” Kill SaaS? 
There’s a popular idea that because anyone can now “vibe code” an app in minutes with Claude Code, the value of SaaS drops to zero.
First, vibe coding is not a SaaS death sentence.
Yes, you can now describe your requirements in plain English and Claude/Cursor spits out a working app in minutes. I’ve seen solo founders replace $300/mo tools overnight. Cool.
But that’s a weekend prototype — not a system that survives 10k concurrent users, SOC2 audits, 17 legacy integrations, and the 3 a.m. pager storm.
But the most important point is you are vibe-coding yesterday’s legacy systems based on structured forms. The right approach should be to start reimagining what software should feel like when AI is the operating system. 

2. Agents vs. Systems of Record
 The “Claude agents will kill SaaS” argument misses a fundamental architectural reality.
If an agent sits on top of HubSpot, Salesforce, or Freshworks to deliver intelligence, how exactly does that kill the underlying platform?
These applications are the System of Record. They are the crucial data input layer. To be effective, agents need:• Structured data from CRMs and ERPs• Unstructured data from emails, Zoom summaries, and call recordings
The agent is the brain. The SaaS platform is the memory and the nervous system. One cannot function effectively without the other. (Aaron Levie nailed it recently: agents will be SaaS’s biggest users.)
There is another theory that you don’t need the underlying system of record anymore. Just connect the zoom meetings, emails and call transcripts and AI can figure out everything by itself - thats a very flawed assumption and we will do a separate post on why and how that will fail

3. So, Who Will Kill SaaS? 
SaaS isn’t going to be killed by a specific LLM release. It will be disrupted by whoever reimagines the solution from the ground up.
Adding an AI layer on top of legacy architecture is a stop-gap—a “wrapper” strategy. 
The real killer will be either:• An incumbent brave enough to disrupt its own legacy architecture, or• An AI-native startup that builds a system where AI isn’t an “add-on,” but the core foundation of the logic.
The Path ForwardWe are moving from a world of “software you use” to “software that does.” The transition won’t be overnight, but the blueprint is changing fast.

In my next post, I’ll outline exactly what a reimagined system of enterprise software—built from day one for the AI era—actually looks like.

Sales has a brutal feature most careers don’t have:

You can do everything right and still lose.

The call can be good.
The problem can be real.
The prospect can like you.

And the deal still disappears.

Maybe the budget froze.
Maybe the CEO stepped in.
Maybe a competitor came with a discount.

Sales outcomes are rarely controlled by effort alone.

But here’s the part most people misunderstand:

Bad days in sales are not signals to slow down.
They are signals to increase exposure.

One quiet day can erase weeks of effort if you stop.

That is why top reps build a system that survives emotional swings.

Here are a few practical rules experienced reps follow:

1. Separate effort metrics from outcome metrics

Deals closing is an outcome.
Calls made, conversations started, meetings booked — those are effort metrics.

You control effort.
The market controls outcomes.

Strong reps track things like:

• Calls per day
• Conversations per day
• Meetings booked per week
• Pipeline created per month

If those numbers are healthy, results follow.

2. Never evaluate a single day

Sales is a probability game.

A bad day feels personal, but the math doesn’t work that way.

Evaluate performance over:

• 2 weeks of activity
• 30 days of pipeline
• quarterly deal cycles

Daily results are noise.
Consistency is the signal.

3. Losses are part of pipeline math

If you are not losing deals, you are not playing a big enough game.

Professional sales pipelines often look like this:

• 100 prospects contacted
• 20 conversations
• 8 meetings
• 3 real opportunities
• 1 closed deal

Which means 99 interactions produce no revenue.

That’s normal.

4. Emotional discipline is a real sales skill

A bad day tests mindset more than skill.

Average reps take rejection personally.
Strong reps treat rejection as data.

They ask:

• Was the ICP correct?
• Was timing wrong?
• Did the deal stall earlier?
• Did the champion disappear?

Then they adjust the process and keep moving.

5. Momentum beats motivation

Motivation disappears when results are slow.

Momentum comes from routine.

The best reps protect a few habits every day:

• Call block in the morning
• Follow-ups before lunch
• Pipeline review in the evening
• Learning one improvement daily

Routine keeps the engine running even when confidence drops.

The Supply Chain Threat You May Not Have Seen Coming

Power-hungry data centers and other risks threaten supply chains’ access to reliable electric energy.

A key challenge for supply chain organizations is to anticipate, prepare for, and mitigate risks to a company’s operations and business continuity. Many organizations focus their attention on “traditional” disruptions like supplier failures, material and inventory shortages, and natural and human-caused disasters. Inadequate electric power may not be high on their priority list—if it is included at all—because this ubiquitous resource is often taken for granted. Such complacency could prove to be a costly mistake.

Uninterrupted electric power is not a given. On a macro scale, potential threats include long-term drought in the Western U.S. that is constraining hydroelectric power generation; opposition to construction of power lines from Canada into the northern U.S.; the Trump Administration’s efforts to dismantle both offshore and land-based wind-power projects; and cyberthreats from foreign powers like China and Russia, which have been probing the U.S. power grid for vulnerabilities.

At the local level, utilities in many areas are struggling to meet demand for the high-capacity electrical service required to support automation in warehouses and manufacturing plants, the electrification of trucks and forklifts, and the e-commerce-fueled construction of new warehouses. One participant in an MIT Center for Transportation & Logistics roundtable said that his company had committed to adopting electric vehicles (EVs) at its distribution centers, but the wait for upgraded lines and service would be at least two to three years.

Recently, a new potential threat to reliable electric service has come on the scene: data centers.

These facilities support internet, software hosting, and data operations. By one count, there were more than 5,000 data centers in the U.S. in early 2025, but that number is already outdated. According to the industrial real estate developer Prologis, the growth of artificial intelligence (AI) and cryptocurrency transactions is the main reason data center development in the U.S. doubled in 2024 compared to the previous year. This pace is expected to accelerate. For example, Amazon and OpenAI both recently announced that they will spend billions of dollars to build more data centers in the U.S. over the next few years—and they are not the only companies doing this.

 Potential Competition for Electricity

Data centers require gigawatts of electricity to power computing, servers, network- and data-storage equipment, and cooling systems. The U.S. Dept. of Energy forecasts that by 2030, data centers’ energy consumption could account for up to 12% of all U.S. electricity use. It’s not a stretch to say that data centers could soon be in competition with industrial facilities for energy resources.

This possibility prompts several questions: If a data center is built near an existing factory or DC, will that affect the reliability of electrical service? Who will get priority if resources—whether electricity itself or the ability to upgrade or provide new infrastructure—become limited? Will the advent of data centers constrain siting options for companies that want to expand or build new facilities? These are not theoretical questions, especially in areas where local governments are encouraging construction of data centers. Fairfax and Loudoun counties in Northern Virginia, for instance, have more than 270 operational data centers, with 126 more in development. Some communities in more than a dozen countries outside the U.S. reportedly have experienced power and water disruptions after data centers began operating.

Communicate with the Community

Some companies are starting to recognize that energy reliability is becoming a serious supply chain risk. According to Prologis’ 2026 Supply Chain Outlook Report, nearly 90% of the companies the developer surveyed in 2025 had experienced some kind of energy-related disruption during the past 12 months. About 70% said they worry about power outages more than any other supply chain disruption.

Worrying will only go so far, though. While construction of many more data centers seems inevitable, there are some steps you can take to protect the power needs of your own facilities.

The first is to monitor local business news and announcements by economic development agencies and state, county, and local governments. This will help you find out about proposed data centers and any related hearings or requests for comment.

The second is to review each facility’s service agreement with the local utility so you’re aware of what the contracts do and do not guarantee. Then, proactively reach out to senior decision-makers at the utilities. Ask about their plans for service upgrades and infrastructure improvements, how they prioritize customers, and how they plan to ensure reliable power for existing customers if and when energy-hungry data centers enter the picture.

And third, develop relationships and work closely with local communities, governments, chambers of commerce, and economic development agencies. Make sure they’re aware of your current and anticipated energy needs, and that you want to collaborate not only for your own benefit but also for the economic good of communities in the area. It wouldn’t hurt to also emphasize your facilities’ contributions to the local economy, including tax payments and employment.

We don’t know yet how many data centers will eventually be built, or exactly how they will impact their neighbors. If you want to ensure that your facilities have reliable energy now and in the future, collaboration and communication with local governments and the communities your company operates in are among the most important steps you can take.

WAR IMPLICATIONS:

- Oil, gas, shipping- all disrupted
- Costs rising across everything
- Supply chains becoming unreliable
- Inflation staying high
- Growth slowing down
- Planning getting harder

Big shift: Availability matters more than price.

NEW RULEBOOK FOR CORPORATES:

- Stop chasing lowest cost- build resilience
- Have backup suppliers and routes
- Keep extra inventory (just in case)
- Watch geopolitics, not just markets
- Take decisions early, don’t wait for clarity

Simple truth: The fastest won’t win.The most flexible will.

Eight months.

That's how long I waited before launching Zouk's first bag.
Fixing the stitching. Adjusting the price. Wondering if India needed another bag brand. Wondering if I was the right person to build it.

Nobody told me that waiting is not the same as preparing.

I learned that the hard way. Here's what else I learned.

1. Your product will never be ready - Ship it anyway. The women who bought Zouk's first bag loved what I almost scrapped. Perfection is fear wearing a productive face.

2. The first believer has to be you - Everyone had opinions on whether the market was ready. I stopped asking for permission and started collecting proof instead.

3. Being India-made will cost you more - Do it anyway. A supplier once offered me 40% less — if I moved production out of India. I said no. Our margins took the hit. But every time I walk our factory floor and see the craft, I know exactly what Zouk is for.
Your values are your moat. Even when they're expensive.

4. No one will take you seriously in a room full of manufacturers - Until suddenly, everyone does. I was talked over. I was asked if my husband would be joining the meeting.
I stopped shrinking to fit the room. I let the work speak instead.
Your difference is not your disadvantage. It never was.

Pradeep Krishnakumar

Our first exhibition stall cost ₹40,000.
We came back with 23 customers, 6 wholesale enquiries —
And something no Instagram ad has given us in the years since.

—
I stood at that stall for two days straight.
Exhausted. Terrified. Wondering if 12 months of believing in this idea was about to be proven wrong in one afternoon. Then the women came.
One picked up a bag, felt the weight of it, and immediately called her friend over. Another traced the Warli print with her finger and went quiet for a moment — the way you do when something feels familiar and beautiful at the same time.

That moment told me India doesn't need to borrow from the West. She just needs someone to hold up a mirror.
A third looked at me and asked: "Does the artisan know how good this is?"

—
You cannot get that from a heatmap.
That single day taught me more about Zouk's customers than six months of analytics ever had.
→ What question she always asks first. → What makes her hesitate. → What makes her smile.
Every product decision, every packaging choice, every story we tell — came from standing in that room.
Not from a dashboard.

—
I still do exhibitions. Not because we have to.
Because standing in front of your customer, with your product in her hands, reminds you exactly why you started.

—
When did you last stand in a room with your customer?
Not behind a screen. In a room.

Incoterms decide who pays.
Who controls.
Who carries risk.

Yet they’re treated like three-letter logistics codes.

They are not.

They are financial switches.

Flip the wrong one and:

• Insurance attaches to the wrong party
• Risk transfers earlier than expected
• Carriers are pursued without standing
• Claims collapse at first scrutiny

Here’s the distinction professionals must internalise:

E-Terms → Departure responsibility
F-Terms → Main carriage unpaid
C-Terms → Main carriage paid
D-Terms → Arrival obligation

That classification alone explains 80% of contractual confusion.

Then comes the real discipline:

Where exactly does risk transfer?
Not where the invoice says.
Not where the container is.
Where the rule says.

FOB is not for containers.
CIF does not mean the seller carries risk to destination.
DDP is not just “seller pays everything.” It is import liability exposure.

This sheet breaks it down visually:

• Obligation split
• Insurance alignment
• Export and import duty allocation
• Risk transfer points across the chain

Incoterms are not about shipping convenience.
They are about balance sheet protection.

If you negotiate contracts, insure cargo, or handle claims, this is foundational architecture.

Read it slowly.
Then check your last contract.

Procurement is the only department where:

Vendor says: 
"Sir, this is the best price in the market. "

Finance says: 
"Why is this so expensive?"

Management says:
"Can we get better quality at lower cost?

And you're sitting there thinking: 
"Can I get better coffee at lower stress?"

Being in procurement means:
• Negotiating like a lawyer
• Saving like an accountant
• Forecasting like an astrologer
• And firefighting like operations

Nobody notices when you save money. Everyone notices when something is delayed.

But honestly?

There's something satisfying about closing a tough negotiation, securing the right vendor, and protecting margins silently.

Procurement isn't about buying. It's about balancing expectations.

“Vishal ji… I don’t have money to pay this month’s salaries.”
An MD of an ₹80 Cr manufacturing company called me — in panic.
He somehow managed salaries by dipping into his personal savings.

Next day he said:
 “Sir, I never want to face this situation again. Please guide me.”

When we reviewed his business, one core issue became clear:
❌ Customer payments were delayed
 ❌ Credit limits were casual
 ❌ No structured follow-up system
 ❌ Working capital gap kept widening

He wasn’t running a factory…
 He was running on hope and credit.

The Turning Point: A Simple 10-Point Checklist
To break this cycle, we sat with the Sales and Finance team and made a simple 10-point checklist.
Each point was rated 1 to 5 (1 = Poor, 5 = Excellent).

Here’s the same checklist for you 👇
1️⃣ Is our Quality + Delivery consistently aligned with customer expectations?
 2️⃣ Do we treat timely collection as a moral obligation?
 3️⃣ Do we have a written Due Diligence Process for setting customer credit limits?
 4️⃣ Do we ensure a valid PO / Contract before dispatch?
 5️⃣ Do we clearly know the decision-makers in the customer organisation?
 6️⃣ Do we have a Credit Lock System to stop over-exposure?
 7️⃣ Do we fully understand the customer’s internal payment workflow?
 8️⃣ Do we follow a written Collection SOP (aging review, cadence, templates)?
 9️⃣ Have we documented common customer excuses and our counter-actions?
 🔟 Do we capture and share best practices across the team?

The Result
We worked on the low-scoring areas.
 Aligned the team.
 Fixed leakages.

📈 Within 3 months, the company became positive cash flow.
 No loans.
 No panic.
 No salary struggles.
Just discipline + clarity.

💬 What’s your score on this 10-point checklist?

"Sir, pls keep your ego below the logo (company)."
I told this to the Operations Head of a packaging factory.
For years, his team had been maintaining the machine in a particular way.
 It worked well… until customer requirements changed.
 Now, customers wanted higher accuracy and better finish.

A new team member shared—
 👉 “In my previous company, we maintained the machine slightly differently. Can I show you?”
We agreed to a demo.
 The results were clear:
 ✅ Faster setup
 ✅ More efficient operation
 ✅ Improved quality that matched the new customer standards

But the Operations Head felt offended.
 He thought, “If I accept this, I’ll be proved wrong.”
 So, he resisted.

That’s when I sat with him and said—
🔹 “Your years of experience are invaluable. But learning is a never-ending process.”
 🔹 “If the new method helps the company and satisfies customers, isn’t that a win for your leadership?”
 🔹 “Sir, ego is personal. But the logo represents everyone’s livelihood.”

He paused… and then smiled.
 👉 “Okay, let’s try this out.”
Within a few weeks, the factory saw:
 📈 Higher machine uptime
 📈 Improved customer ratings
 📈 Team members feeling more open to share new ideas

Leadership is not about being right.
 It’s about keeping the company’s growth above personal pride.
Keep ego below the logo.

I would like to share a short story about how when a CEO decides to implement change, it shows up in conduct of workers.
 
 When TK Balaji had to transform his manufacturing operation from process layout to flow manufacturing, he bypassed the union and went directly to the workers.

For six months, twice a week, he held small group meetings on the shop floor. Around twenty workers at a time, until he had covered the entire factory. His message was clear and uncomfortable: the Japanese are coming, this is how they perform, and if we do not change, we will not have a business. He told them he was not asking for more hours, only a different way of working.​

There was no negotiation and no promises. Just the truth.

The union kept asking, "What will we get for this?" His answer remained the same: nothing immediate. If the change worked and the business grew, they would benefit, but he would not promise anything upfront.​

After six months of this, he asked for volunteers to start the first manufacturing cell. Enough people came forward because they believed his intent. Once the first cell was running and workers saw that the new way of working was actually smoother and easier, resistance began to disappear. Soon, the question on the shop floor changed from "Why should we do this?" to "When will you take me into the program?"​

What many thought was impossible in a unionized, legacy manufacturing environment became reality because the leader was prepared to stand in front of his people, on their turf, and tell them the unvarnished truth.

Sometimes real transformation does not start in the boardroom. It starts in a small circle on the shop floor.

I don’t care about your resume. I just finished hiring my Growth team at Clay and they have ZERO traditional marketing backgrounds. No ex-Google, no ex-Uber, no ex-Hubspot. Here's who I hired instead (and why):

- Talia built a $250K/year jewelry business from her dorm room going viral on TikTok during Covid. She hacked the algorithm by rage baiting her followers leading to viral success.

- Emily was Chief of Staff + Head of Content for a non-profit... while crushing a BizOps job at a $5bn+ tech company... while building a floral business. 

- Nicole is an Olympian (ranked #5 in USA) & a NCAA Champion in Synchronized Swimming. She would train from 7am to 3pm every day and then do school at night.

- Andrew has experience across product, growth, and ops. During his interview process, he randomly joined a livestream I was hosting and started answering audience questions on my behalf.

Founders ask me all the time: "What background should I look for in a Growth hire?". My honest answer is that I don't have one: I don't look for pedigree, I look for evidence of obsession with something. ANYTHING.

What these four have in common is that they found something they cared about and refused to be mediocre at it. That kind of obsession doesn't stay contained: it bleeds into everything they do, including your work.

So when I review a resume, I'm not looking for a career path. I'm looking for evidence of that obsession: the hobby that got out of hand, the side project that took over weekends, the “never quitting” mentality.

People who've been #1 at anything know what it takes. And they'll bring that same energy to your team.

Stop looking at resumes. Start talking to people and understand what makes them tick.

Most companies don't actually have product management.

They have project management with a better title.

And it's costing them growth.

Product Management is the only function designed to look outside the company.

Not inside.

Sales? Focused on closing the next deal.
Marketing? Focused on pushing messages out.
Engineering? Focused on building and shipping.

All necessary.

None of them are built to understand the market.

Product Management is.

And it lives in the tension between:

What customers need
What the business wants
What's actually possible

It zooms out.
Then zooms in.
Then forces the hard trade-offs.

That's why real Product Management breaks silos.
It doesn't sit inside them.

So if your "Product function" is just:

• Managing tickets
• Sitting in sprint ceremonies
• Translating requirements
• Vibe coding prototypes

You don't have Product Management.

You have delivery coordination.

And that's a leadership problem, not a PM problem.

I've seen this play out in companies of every size.

Real Product Managers:

Know they're not the smartest person in the room.
Constantly learn.
Ask the difficult questions.
Push back when needed.
Drive forward anyway.

That discomfort is the job.

Good Product Management challenges you.
Bad Product Management agrees with you.

If what you really want is someone to:

Hit dates you already promised
Keep Engineering busy
Execute without questioning

Don't hire Product Managers.

If you say you want:

Customer insight
Product strategy
A real roadmap

But reward:

Speed
Compliance
Engineering alignment

You still don't want Product Management.

You want the illusion of it.

Most leaders don't want Product Management.

They want control.

And Product Management takes that away.

Great Product Management only works when leadership can handle being challenged.

Most can't. And it shows in the product.

If you don't like being played for a fool, don't become a Product Manager. When are we being fools? How to battle it?

Well, it's all about the responsibility for a Product with rarely given actual ownership and accountability. "Bullshit management," they call it. Product Managers are often mislabeled as the "CEO of Product" due to this misconception. No, the CEO of Product is just the CEO. The Product Manager is there to execute her/his vision with limited independence.

The problem arises when you are almost completely stripped of this freedom and instead are being told what to do, while still holding the perceived ownership.

I saw tons of times when Product Managers had to smile and support, even cheer, for decisions they privately despised and strongly opposed until the very end.

I had to leave one of my most successful projects in a state of a petty MVP, as management forced me to pivot to a project that made no sense even on the conceptual level. I was never allowed to polish something that bumped main metrics by 20%, even in its final form, and had to waste time pushing someone else's political agenda.

Ultimately, both were removed from the product...

The 1st (successful) one: I wasn't there to defend the vision and results. Those were mysteriously removed from the company's knowledge base...

The 2nd: Removed as it "never made sense, to begin with".

Unfortunately, my story is not an isolated incident, but a common antipattern in the industry.

So, how not to be a fool?

For one thing, speak up in the crowd. You don't want to complain to your team, but to your fellow PMs and management, by all means! The worst that can happen is being fired for speaking the truth. 

Use data to back up your claims. Show that the direction chosen is not the best one.

If this doesn't work, craft an experiment that can quickly show who is right. 

As a "fool", you need to drop the silly hat and show your true Product Management spirit. Be brave enough to speak up and step on someone's ego. Help others who think the same, find the strength to speak up as well, and back you up.

Why be so daring? Cause as the fool you are the scapegoat if the stupid project indeed turns to shit. Rather than see if it's through, you might as well try to prevent it from happening.

This is why you were hired as a Product Manager, not a fool.

To know better.

To collect and use data.

To be aware of many different perspectives. 

To do right BY THE PRODUCT!

Not to be a fool and a PR expert for someone else's bullshit.

No need to be a CEO of Product. Being a Product Manager will work just fine!

What do you think? Are you a Product Manager or a jester of your Product?

You may like it or not, but politics is a part of the Product Management world. How can data-driven PMs drive their vision despite that? Easily:

Let me tell you, initially in my career, I was almost allergic to politics. All I wanted to do was do my job to the best of my abilities and not care for other people's ambitions and egos. It took a little time until I fully understood that in most companies, there is simply no escaping this. If you want to be kept on the payroll and improve your product, you will have to be politically engaged on some level. 

So, how can we ensure our data-driven vision and discovery efforts are not trumped by someone else's personal goals?

1) Build Strong Relationships
Start with what any PM should do, politics or otherwise. Be a charming, transparent, reliable, inspiring, and empathic leader and colleague. When people know and trust you, they're more likely to support your ideas and defend you from others' egoism.

2) Communicate Clearly
Present your data and insights in a way that resonates with different audiences. Also, aim to say the right stuff, supported by data, to the right crowd at the right time via the right medium. In that case, lots of "rights" make an even greater right! Sidenote: Less is more in terms of clear comms :) Also, always focus on demonstrating tangible value for the clients. That can help overcome political obstacles.

3) Align with Business Goals
You may be sick and tired of politics, but at the same time have some pet projects you'd like to push despite weak data backing or poor alignment with current goals. Police yourself in this regard, and you will have fewer angles of potential political attack open for your "opposition".

4) Know when to let go
Sometimes, the best investment would be to simply do what you are asked. As long as it is sporadic and can win you allies long-term, it's a strategic move. Just keep the paper trail in case the request you took upon yourself backfires. 

5) Leverage Allies
Once you build a set of allies and authority, use it to have a stronger voice in the room. In other words: Use politics to build the best product possible. Career growth and recognition will follow organically, and you don't need to change your career into a Product.

6) Don't let it get to you
You may execute the previous 5 points flawlessly and still feel like everything is playing to office politics. Just stay strong, don't let it get to you, and be a beacon of reason and sound decisions. Hopefully, you will lead by example!

So, do you have to struggle with politics at work?

Do you feel frustration as you are building a product to get the management off your back, rather than for the users? Here are 6 ways to become user-centric again:

1) Prioritize in a transparent way

This is a great place to start. If your backlog is prioritized based on data and potential opportunity, risk, and cost, it will be easier to put forth user-centric initiatives ahead of those that came from upstairs. At the very least, you will have a good basis for an educated discussion. 
 
2) Utilize users' perspective using user stories and personas

If your team understands the users and their problems, it will be easier to craft something great that will later appeal to the same users. Just keep up the empathy of creating something by people for other people, and don't let some metric magically go up!

3) Understand user problems

If everyone in the company can see the themes that come from user feedback, it will be way harder to ignore it in favor of some corporate nonsense. Let those voices be heard by everyone! What if there are 100,000s of voices? 

4) Have the NPS and user ratings at the forefront

The same goes for a single metric representing the general product sentiment. If the number is low or, worse, is going down and everyone can see that, the responsible Product Manager has to react.

5) Focus on your product goals

Now, upstairs mandates might not be the only distraction you face when trying to improve your product. To survive them all, focus on one thing: your product goals. This will allow you to demonstrate that you are doing what you are asked for, and you can use user feedback and points 1-4 to pursue those goals. Thus, it's like killing 2 birds with 1 stone. However, you can also simply:

6) Have the confidence to say "No."

Not all company/legal/management requests can be ignored. Sometimes changing the law or a wider company initiative will require you to comply, and that is OK! However, there will also be times when someone will try to force your compliance. This is where you need to be confident and exercise your Product Manager's independence, especially when there is no data to support a specific request.

There you go! My 6 ways you can become a user-centric Product Manager. 

Do you put your users first in your product?

Hiring ex-FAANG-type Product Managers or Product Leaders into growth-stage SaaS companies often fails.

I'm not here to bash FAANG Product Pros, but I come across so many scenarios where it's not worked out for the company and the individual.

On paper, it looks like a great move for everyone! 

But in reality, the difference between large tech companies and growth-stage companies is often huge.

At large tech companies, PMs and Product Leaders will often have:

- Clear structures to operate within.
- Established systems to work with.
- Significant resources are available to them.
- Well-defined roles with specific responsibilities.

At a $25M–$75M SaaS company, it’s often the opposite. 

You’ll be dealing with:

- High levels of ambiguity to deal with.
- Incomplete/inconsistent data to pull from.
- Competing priorities are the norm.
- Constant trade-offs are par for the course.

The Product Managers and Product Leaders who thrive in that environment are usually scrappy, extremely pragmatic, comfortable operating at 1000 feet and in the weeds, and they are used to building from the ground up.

The best hires are the ones who’ve operated in similar levels of chaos before.

In 1969, a German mathematician proved something that still makes traffic engineers uncomfortable: adding a new road to a network can make everyone's commute slower.

It's not a thought experiment. When Stuttgart closed a major road, traffic improved. When Seoul tore down an elevated highway, congestion dropped. The network performed better with less capacity.

The mechanism is counterintuitive but elegant. When you add a tempting shortcut, everyone individually optimizes for it. The shortcut gets congested. The routes it connects to get disrupted. The system as a whole degrades even though every individual driver made a rational choice.

I keep thinking about this in the context of product development.

Every feature you add is a new road in the network. It connects to existing flows, creates new decision points, introduces new paths. And if you've ever watched a user freeze on a screen that your team considers "feature-rich," you've seen the Braess paradox play out in real time. Every feature individually justified. The product collectively worse.

The uncomfortable question the Braess paradox raises is not "should we remove features?" That's obvious. The uncomfortable question is: what if the feature driving the most individual usage is the one degrading the overall experience? What if your most popular road is the one making traffic worse?

Nobody audits for this. We measure feature adoption. We don't measure feature interference.

→ SPEED: "We cut vendor onboarding from 30 days to 5 — R&D launched their pilot 3 weeks earlier than planned."

→ RISK AVOIDED: "We caught a security gap during renewal that would have cost $500K+ in breach response — before it became a problem."

I just deleted all my MCPs.
Skills + CLI is all you need.

MCPs were a solution for humans pretending to build for agents. But agents aren't humans. They're processes. And processes talk to the world through CLIs and APIs. Always have. Always will.

GitHub's official MCP server: 54,000 tokens to load the spec. Fifty-four thousand. Before I even ask it to do anything. 
Meanwhile, `gh --help` costs 562 tokens. And the model already knows it from training data.

MCP is a JSON bureaucracy pretending to be infrastructure. It's the enterprise middleware of AI, lots of ceremony, zero value add. 

Skills are 30-50 tokens until triggered. Progressive disclosure. They teach the agent how to think. CLIs are the what- battle-tested, version-controlled, debuggable without a spec document.

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

AEs: Please stop asking outbound prospects, "So, what got you interested in [Company]?

Your SDR didn’t just wait for this person to show up. They spent weeks researching the account, navigating gatekeepers, and finally scheduling a meeting with them.

When you start a discovery call by asking why they are interested, you aren't just being lazy, you’re undoing all the hard work your SDR did to create tension and curiosity.

At Reveneer, we believe the Warm Handoff is the only way to protect that momentum. Here is how to do it right:

1. The SDR Stays on the Call: They are the bridge of trust. They introduce the AE, recap the specific pain points discussed in the initial outreach, and state the goal of today's meeting. Also, An SDR who hears how an AE handles objections to the meeting they booked is getting better training than any roleplay session could provide. They hear exactly which "pain signals" led to a qualified opportunity, and which ones led to a dead end.

2. The "Pre-Flight" Briefing: The AE and SDR should huddle for a few minutes before the call. The AE needs to know exactly what "hook" the SDR used. SDR and AE can exchange notes so both are fully up to speed on expectations for the call. 

3. Lead with Context, Not Questions: Instead of "What got you interested?", the AE should start with: "[SDR] mentioned that you’re currently struggling with [Specific Pain], and that’s exactly why we wanted to connect today."

The result? The prospect feels heard, the SDR feels valued for their grind, and the AE looks like a consultant rather than just another sales rep.

Outbound is a team sport. If you treat it like a relay race, make sure you don't drop the baton during the handoff.

Do your SDRs stay for the handoff, or are they already onto the next dial the second the invite is accepted?

If your PM is “also a designer” or your designer is “basically a PM,” you don’t have a unicorn - you have a problem.

PMs are trained to own why a product exists, what gets built, and when. They see the world through the lens of business and market outcomes.
UX Designers are trained to own how to make it work for humans. They see through the lens of behavior, cognition, and usability.

When PMs start to design or vibe-code, three things happen:
- You lose the problem space by jumping to a solution before fully understanding the user’s reality
- Designers become an order-taking function
- Bias locks in early, and your first sketch becomes “the plan,” even if it’s wrong. 

When PMs stray into design, the role stops being strategic and turns into a feature factory.
When UX takes on strategy without the authority to drive it, the best ideas die in Figma instead of reaching the users.

If you want both roles to perform at their best:
PMs: Solutions are not your thing. Leave them alone. You have fantastic ideas that should be considered but not blindly followed. 
UX: Don’t skip the business case. It’s not only about the user; it’s also about the business. It's not enough to make it usable, it must be also sellable.

Clear swim lanes and shared context aren’t “nice to have”, they are the operating system of high-performing product teams.

When PM and UX stay in their lanes and meet in the middle on insight, you get speed, creativity, and execution.

When the lanes blur, ownership gets distorted, you get politics instead of collaboration, and a product no one fully owns.

Every UX course teaches that users think in tasks, not features.
Every product manager has heard of jobs-to-be-done and can write one if woken up in the middle of the night. 

This knowledge is decades old and yet. Open any enterprise tool, and you'll find navigation organized by what the system can do rather than what you're trying to accomplish. Yes, I am still not over, Azure.

Heard of Conway's Law? Me neither, until recently. It's basically when the UI reflects the org chart. The "Billing" team ships a Billing page. The "Analytics" team ships an Analytics page. Nobody owns the workflow that crosses three teams.

In addition to that, we have the feature trap. Features are shippable, measurable, and make a beautiful sprint demo. "We added export to CSV" fits in a release note, while "We reorganized the entire flow so you don't need to export anymore," doesn't photograph well.

And of course, "workflow friction" rarely makes it to the metrics people care about. Nobody's OKR is "reduced cognitive load by 40%." So the problem stays invisible in dashboards while being painfully visible in use.

The gap isn't knowledge. It's that fixing workflows requires reorganizing ownership. And that's more expensive than letting users figure it out.

So they do. And we call it "learning curve."

I still do all my own sales at The Birdhouse. People think that's crazy, but when you're selling high ticket packages prospects want to talk to the founder.

That doesn't mean I haven't worked on my sales skills though.

The big shift for founder-led sales = selling results instead of tactics.

Early on I used to hop on calls and explain everything we do. The content strategy, the DM scripts, the posting cadence, the analytics.

I'd basically give away the whole playbook on a 30-minute call.

And people would say "this is great, let me think about it" and then go try to do it themselves.

Now, I just talk about where they are and where they want to be.

If someone tells me they want to double or triple their revenue, I'm not explaining our content pillars. I'm asking them what's broken in their current setup, what they've already tried, and why it hasn't worked yet.

By the time I've asked the right questions, they've sold themselves on needing help. I just made the gap between where they are and where they want to be extremely clear.

The shift is this:

Value content attracts competitors and admirers. Insight attracts buyers.

On calls, it's the same thing. If you explain HOW you do everything, they leave thinking "I can figure this out." If you show them WHERE to look and make the gap obvious, they leave thinking "I need someone to take me there."

Also, I don't need to "close" any of the deals. People can smell desperation from across a Zoom call. When you have a full pipeline and super high retention rate, you can be honest about whether someone is actually a good fit. 

That honesty closes harder than any sales technique.

So if your close rate is low, ask yourself:

Are you selling tactics instead of results? Are you explaining the process instead of making the gap clear?

Fix the positioning and the close rate takes care of itself.

For me, one of the most useful thing I've done with it has nothing to do with putting code in production. I use it to prototype my own PRDs before a review.

Here's how that works in practice:
→ Write the PRD first. CUJs, edge cases, states. Thoroughly. 
→ Upload it to Google AI Studio (or your favorite vibe-coding app) with a screenshot of the existing product for visual reference. 
→ Prompt it to build specific sections, two or three user journeys at a time. 
→ Click through every flow as your user would. 
→ Something feels off. Describe what you see and ask it to fix it. 
→ Take what you found back to the PRD before the meeting.

Two types of things come up when you do this.

The obvious gaps that should have been in the document to begin with (Personal note: don't be lazy and spend more time doing product thinking and improving your PRDs vs. vibe coding AI or Human slop)

And the subtler UX friction that only shows up when you're actually using the thing. An interaction that made complete sense in a document but felt cold and wrong the moment you had your thumb on it. 

That second kind is what made me keep doing this.

There is a new intent type in AI search, and it accounts for 37% of all prompts.
I pulled a sample from our dataset of tens of millions of real ChatGPT prompts and mapped every single one to an intent bucket. Here's what jumped out:

The new intent type is 'Generative' and it's the largest intent (37.5% of prompts): "write X", "create Y", etc. Users arrive expecting the answer engine to create the asset, copy, code, image, plan, outline, whatever it is. That's an entirely new job to be done, one which Google is only starting to take seriously now with AI Mode. This is the massive shift happening right now in everyone's organic traffic.

Only ~2% of ChatGPT prompts are navigational, in traditional search, 32.15% of queries are navigational.

32.7% of prompts in AI search are informational vs 52.7% in traditional search: users do still ask for explanations, definitions, synthesis. But because generative output is the whole point, plain "what is X" in trad. search has to compete with "explain X in an intuitive way like Steve Jobs." 

6% of prompts in AI search are transactional: they explicitly ask the model to buy, book, or order something ("draft my Amazon return", "write a cancellation email"). That's 9× Google's transactional share. Brands should watch this space.

'No Intent' prompts account for 12% of AI searches. These are the 'glue' of LLM interactions, and are seriously important. Right now it's unclear how brands can plan or play in these spaces. How does a 'please' or 'thank you' affect the recommendations later in the chat? Only time will tell on this one.

Only 2.6% of earned media citations in AI search come from Tier 1 publications. I pulled from a dataset of 27 million citations across ChatGPT, Gemini, AI Overviews, Perplexity, Copilot, and Claude to figure out where they're actually coming from:

- 2.6%: Tier 1 media (AP, Bloomberg, etc.)
- 97.4%: everything else in earned media

That 97.4% is trade publications, niche industry blogs, specialist review sites, regional outlets, vertical-specific media. This is where answer engines are actually pulling their citations at scale, and at n=27 million, the signal is clear.

The prevailing playbook in comms right now is: get into Tier 1 outlets, win AI visibility and brands are focusing accordingly. But the data says that entire strategy is optimizing for roughly 3% of their earned media citation surface area. That's a lot of budget aimed at a very small target.

Here's what I think is actually going on (and let me know your thoughts below):

1. Tier 1 publishers have spent the last two years aggressively blocking AI crawlers, filing lawsuits, and negotiating licensing deals. A large part of me thinks the system is working as they intended: they don't want to be cited.

2. Answer engines don't reason in media tiers. When ChatGPT is resolving "best ERP system for mid-market manufacturers," it's completely indifferent to whether the source is the Wall Street Journal or a specialist manufacturing technology blog.

And this is where I think the real shift is, one that goes way beyond "focus more on niche publications":

For 50+ years, Tier 1 media has bundled two functions together: Discovery + Validation

Forbes got you found and Forbes made you credible. This data is the first hard evidence I've seen that AI search is unbundling them.

Discovery has moved. It's being routed through niche publications, trade media, specialist review sites, the entire long tail that accounts for 97.4% of citations. That's where AI is going to find the answer to a buyer's question.

But Validation hasn't moved. You still want the Forbes logo on your homepage. You still need the WSJ hit for your Series B deck. Investors, board members, enterprise buyers doing final diligence, they still check Tier 1, that function is intact.

The problem is that most PR budgets are structured around discovery. And if discovery has migrated to publications that cost a fraction of Tier 1 placements, then earned media is fundamentally misaligned right now.

Niche publications are delivering multiples of the AI visibility at a fraction of the cost, and almost nobody is allocating against that because they're still operating on the old bundled model.

I believe the moment niche publishers see their own citation data (and they will soon btw) their standing will increase. Brands that refocus now'll get coverage that quietly earns outsized AI visibility.

What happens to the PR industry's economic model when media is valued by citation impact instead of circulation? 👀

In 2026, AI search becomes a visibility engine:

(And this shift will reshape your content strategy)

For years, marketers optimized pages to rank in search.

Write a page → Add keywords → Compete in SERPs

SEO and AI answers operated in parallel:

- Rankings were an SEO win
- AI answers felt unpredictable

They rarely connected.

Except for basic optimization and indexing: make the page crawlable and hope it ranks.

- An SEO team could win rankings but never appear in AI answers.
- A content team could publish heavily but still miss citations.

But in 2026, AI changes this reality:

AI search tools now retrieve, evaluate, and synthesize content from sources that show authority, clarity, and strong entity signals. And structured, retrieval-ready content is what gets surfaced.

So here's how it works:

1. A user asks a question and the AI refines intent
2. Retrieval systems scan the web for credible sources
3. The model selects, builds, and cites the best answers

Now of course, it is still early to lock a universal playbook.

But here is the big opportunity for content teams:

Your content must now perform twice.

- In traditional search: rank pages, drive clicks, capture traffic.
- In LLM answers: get retrieved, get cited, influence decisions.

(via Semrush):
90% of ChatGPT citations come from pages outside the top 20 in Google.

Which means:
You can show up in AI answers without ranking high organically.

Because LLMs look for:

Chunked content: Bite-sized, answer-ready info
Niche utility: Solving specific questions
Contextual fit: Relevance over raw domain authority

Structure for retrieval. Write for citation. Win in the answer layer.

At least once a week, I still see people asking a ridiculous question: Should marketing report to the CRO? To me that's just as crazy as asking if product should report to the CRO. 

I mean they all care about your ICP and user persona right? They all care about revenue, and it's the Chief Revenue Officer right? 

But the answer is no for both teams, and it comes down to two things: What metrics drive their behavior and what timeframe different functions operate within.

Here is a breakdown:

1. Product runs on adoption as a metric and has a long clock. It’s usually the last lever you pull because it takes the longest to show up in the market. Call it 1 year before it becomes a meaningful revenue driver beyond early adopters.

2. Marketing’s metrics change like the weather, which sucks for marketers. But broadly, it’s qualified pipeline driven by brand plus efficient demand. Across our customer base, marketing owns the prospect for about 150 days out of a 211-day cycle before sales gets involved.

3. Sales has one metric: closed revenue in the form of new business deals. They’re obsessed with it, as they should be. Public companies report quarterly, and private companies are auditioning to be public. So sales lives and dies by a 90 day quarter. (Some of you live month to month. That also sucks. But your CFO and board are judging quarters.)

4. Post-sales is closed revenue in the form of retention. Gross or net, pick your poison. Either way, retention gets the same exact runway. It’s still judged on a 90-day timeframe.

So yes, it makes sense why CROs own sales and post-sales. The metrics align, the timeframes align.

But marketing is different.

So what happens when a marketing team with a 211-day horizon starts reporting into a leader forced to live on a 90-day horizon?

It breaks.

Brand gets cut first. Pipeline deteriorates, ICP drifts, and CS inherits the mess.

You had an impatient board a year ago so they fired the CMO or demoted them to work under sales.

Who is next?

A year later, that’s the CRO, when they aren’t hitting revenue goals because they don’t know marketing and can’t resolve a 90-day cycle with a much longer cycle.

If you force marketing into a 90-day cadence, you’ll get 90-day marketing.

Marketing deserves a seat at the table - on the exec team, in the board room. Side by side with all of the other functions core to running a business.

BREAKING NEWS: Meta buys Dreamer. Boy that was fast!

Meta just made a quiet move with loud implications.

They’ve effectively acqui-hired the team behind Dreamer — a startup focused on helping people build autonomous agents — including co-founder Hugo Barra (Google/Meta) and David Singleton (former CTO of Stripe).

Two details matter more than the headline:

This is about people, not product. Dreamer’s tech isn’t coming along for the ride.

The destination is agents, not chat.

Put those together and the pattern becomes pretty clear. Meta isn’t just building better chatbots. They’re assembling a roster to build the agent layer.

Here's my take:

If chat was the “UI” of the first wave of AI, agents are the “UX” of the next.

From “ask me a question” → “get this done.”

That shift is bigger than it sounds. Because once agents become the primary interface:

Apps become tools for agents.

Workflows become programmable.

Attention shifts from screens to outcomes.

In other words, the battle isn’t for better answers.  It’s for better execution.

Billions of users won’t just interact with AI…they’ll delegate to agents.

That's very much the world we're building towards at HubSpot with our Agentic Customer Platform. It'll be the "operating system" for GTM agents (both ours and others) all working off a common set of shared primitives and rich context.

We are at the start of the agentic transformation.

The next big AI milestone I'm watching for: Artificial Capable Intelligence (ACI). Can an agent take $100k and legally turn it into $1M - without human assistance? The latest agentic models get us a LOT closer.
 
The original Turing Test was about AI's language skills. ACI is about it taking action. As AI becomes agentic, the benchmark shifts from how naturally they speak to how much they can actually get done.
 
10x-ing an upfront investment is no small feat, and part of the fun (and challenge) is that it's open-ended. There are a ton of different paths possible to prove ACI. Maybe an agent starts and runs a organic cotton apparel business. Maybe it generates educational videos and monetizes them. Maybe a combination of several ventures.
 
Whatever path it takes, it'll have to operate and execute in the real world. Call APIs, buy or sell things, write emails, make phone calls, make decisions, analyze its progress and so on, for as many times and as long as it takes to get to $1M.
 
"As long as it takes" is key here. And we're not there yet, but agents are making exponential bounds on task duration. The latest METR research shows we just jumped from agents doing 6-hour tasks to 12-hour ones, doubling what was previously possible.

To me, ACI is the modern Turing Test - and this chart has me convinced that AI's going to pass it sooner rather than later.

Had meetings and a dinner with 20+ enterprise AI and IT leaders yesterday. Lots of interesting conversations around the state of AI in large enterprises, especially regulated businesses. 

Here are some of general trends:

* Agents are clearly the big thing. Enterprises moving from talking about chatbots to agents, though we’re still very early. Coding is still the dominant agentic use-case being adopted thus far, with other categories of across knowledge work starting to emerge. Lots of agentic work moving from pilots and PoCs into production, and some enterprises had lots of active live use-cases.

* Agentic use-cases span every part of a business, from back office operations to client facing experiences from sales to customer onboarding workflows. General feeling is that agentic workflows will hit every part of an organization, often with biggest focus on delivering better for customers, getting better insights and intelligence from data and documents, speeding up high ROI workflows with agents, and so on. Very limited discussion on pure cost cutting.

* Data and AI governance still remain core challenges. Getting data and content into a spot that agents can securely and easily operate on remains a huge task for more organizations. Years of data management fragmentation that wasn’t a problem now is an issue for enterprises looking to adopt agents. And governing what agents can do with data in a workflow still a major topic.

* Identity emerging as a big topic. Can the agent have access to everything you have? In a world of dozens of agents working on behalf, potentially too much data exposure and scope for the agents. How do we manage agents with partitioned level of access to your information?

* Lots of emerging questions on how we will budget for tokens across use-cases and teams. Companies don’t want to constrain use-cases, but equally need to be mindful of ultimate token budgets. This is going to become a bigger part of OpEx over time, and probably won’t make sense to be considered an IT budget anymore. Likely needs to be factored into the rest of operating expenses.

* Interoperability is key. Every enterprise is deploying multiple AI systems right now, and it’s unlikely that there’s going to be a single platform to rule them all. Customers are getting savvier on how to handle agent interoperability, and this will be one of the biggest drivers of an AI stack going forward. 

Lots more takeaways than just this, but needless to say the momentum is building but equally enterprises are acutely aware of the change management and work ahead. Lots of opportunity right now.

Everyone talks about how AI will change software development. We need to talk about what won’t change.

If anything, AI is forcing a return to first principles, increasing the importance of the SDLC:
➰ If code is generated, specs need to be precise. Bad instructions = scalable garbage.
➰ If output is probabilistic, reviews need to focus on intent, not syntax.
➰ If no one wrote every line, testing becomes your only source of truth.

AI is less a replacement for engineering rigor than an efficient machine for punishing its absence. The abstraction is rising, but the surface area of complexity is exploding underneath: more services, more dependencies, more non-deterministic behavior. Someone still needs to reason about architecture, failure modes, and tradeoffs.

This isn’t a critique of the technology. It’s excellent and improving quickly. But using it well still requires systems thinking and technical judgment.

Generating code is not the same as building software, in the same way producing words is not the same as making a legal argument. Engineering starts after the code exists: validating it, integrating it, deploying it, and ensuring it doesn’t break something downstream at 2AM. Today, everyone can build - but few can build systems that last. That distinction is about to get expensive.

Engineering orgs run by non-technical leadership are especially likely to learn this lesson the hard way. Expect a wave of premature cost-cutting followed by rehiring cycles once the Sev 1s start stacking. If you don't understand the distinction between “more code” and “more reliability,” AI will eventually explain it to you in production.

This dynamic is amplified by the internal politics of large orgs. Scope gets claimed by overpromising, timelines get pulled forward to win resourcing. AI pours fuel on this - making it easier to show rapid early progress and paper over weak foundations, at least temporarily. The bill still arrives.

On the ground, engineers are already seeing the pattern. AI works best in existing codebases with strong patterns - it learns structure and extends prior decisions. But when used to “vibe code” from scratch, it often produces brittle systems: inconsistent abstractions, awkward interfaces, and code that looks fine until it’s asked to scale.

So teams have two choices:
1️⃣ Let the system emerge through prompting → fast, messy, brittle
2️⃣ Define architecture and constraints upfront → slower, but durable

Most teams are choosing (1). Not because it’s better - but because it’s incentivized. When output is measured in PRs and velocity, there’s little reward for designing clean systems or thinking ahead. Complexity gets deferred and paid back later with interest.

AI is making coding easier but it won't make engineering less ‘technical’. Eventually, every builder will be forced to internalize the tenets of the SDLC: version control, testing discipline, structured iteration.

5 questions every PM should ask before building: 

What real problem are we solving? If this disappeared tomorrow, would anyone care? Would they miss it? Don’t build for buzz, build for painkillers. 

Who exactly is this for? If you can’t describe your customers life, their day, their frustrations, you don’t know your user. 

What will make this 10x better than what exists today? If it’s not dramatically better, disrupting an entire industry then it’s not worth your time. 

What’s the emotional “hook” the why? and how fast can we deliver it? Users don’t fall in love with specs. They remember how a product makes them feel. Get them there fast. 

What else does it unlock? Think in systems, not features. Great products aren’t endpoints, they’re platforms.

Wild: Goldman Sachs dropped the most uncomfortable AI stat yet - $450 billion was spent on artificial intelligence last year, but it contributed basically zero to economic growth 😳

All of 2025 was supposed to be the “AI boost” year.
Instead, the data says… nothing happened.

But not because AI is fake.

Because the money didn’t go where people think.

↳ $130B+ went to Nvidia
(we built the shovels, not the gold)

↳ Massive buybacks
(layoffs → higher margins → stock up)

↳ Imported hardware
(GPUs built in Asia = cancels out in GDP)

↳ Early productivity gains
(real, but trapped inside teams - not the economy)

Meanwhile:

→ Meta cut 21,000
→ Atlassian cut 1,600
→ Block cut ~40% of staff
→ Amazon keeps automating warehouses

The jobs moved fast. The growth didn’t.

We’re now in the “burn first, benefit later” phase of AI.

Companies restructured ahead of the payoff.
Markets priced in the upside.
But the actual economy hasn’t caught up.

Historically, this lag is normal.

Electricity, the internet - same pattern.
3-5+ years before productivity shows up in GDP.

But the difference this time is that the layoffs came first.

So now we’re sitting on a strange moment:

Record AI spend.
Record infra buildout.
Very real job displacement.

And basically no GDP growth…

This is either the setup for a boom or the cleanest bubble we’ve seen in years.

Most startups are worrying about "moats" way too early.

Founders think about defensibility like they're building a castle. Walls. A drawbridge. Anything to keep competitors out.

But for an early-stage startup, competition is the least of your concerns. Focus on your customer instead.

Customers are tourists with money. They can leave whenever they want. And if a better alternative comes along, they will.

Software doesn't grant you the right to keep customers. Customers must continually re-award you that right.

So stop asking "how do I keep invaders out?" Ask instead: why would a customer choose to stay?

That's the difference between a moat and a magnet. A moat tries to keep people in. A magnet gives them every reason to stay.

Magnets are built from things like:

- A product that gets smarter the more they use it
- Workflows so embedded they feel native
- Data and insights that compound over time
- A community they don't want to leave behind
- An experience that feels like it was built for them

These aren't walls. They're pull. And pull survives product cycles, competitor launches, and market shifts.

The test is simple: Imagine your customer could leave with no effort, no cost, no risk. Would they feel a sense of loss?

If yes, you're building a magnet.

If no, you're building a trap they'll escape the moment they can.

I've been selling for 22 years. We're living in a new era of exec-level uncertainty. The closest I've experienced was the 2008 financial crisis. Here's the best advice I can share re: what I got wrong when selling in an uncertain market + what ended up working. 

I was working at CEB at the time. We sold an annual best practice research subscription to CXOs and their teams. An annual subscription cost ~$40k-70k. 

Exactly the type of line item a CFO was eager to cut when the economy was falling apart. 

"Ahhh what's this one? Access to an online portal of best practices, webinars & meetings?". 

I'd try to defend the value of the product. They'd tell me, "It's this or letting go of an employee." These were Enterprise CXOs. Yet, you could hear the desperation in their voice. They were worried about losing their jobs, too. 

I'd love to write a LinkedIn post saying how I miraculously turned them around. I didn't. I lost a lot of clients that year. 

Because I was talking ABOUT the value of our product. I wasn't delivering value in the conversation. 

"Access to our best practice research will help you understand how others are navigating this crisis! There's never been a time when this type of research matters more!". 

The following year, CEB conducted the Challenger Sales research. 

The research studied the question, "What type of sellers are winning in this environment?". 

Instead of pitching the value of the membership, we started booking convos on the back of that question. 

Execs came to those calls curious about what others were doing. 

"What are you seeing from other Manufacturing companies? Are they hiring for a different profile? Are they reorganizing? Where are they making cuts?"

My Sales calls became "here's what we're seeing" convos. I barely talked about the product.  

So, I stopped pitching our product in my outbound. I started teeing up convos to share what we were seeing/hearing from other CXOs navigating this mess. 

We ended up winning a ton of those clients back (and new ones, too). 

Eras of uncertainty (like the one we're in right now) can make or break us. 

Pitch a product ("Our AI Agent helps companies....") and you'll often be met with "Let's hold off for a few months". 

Pitch a relevant POV ("Saw your CEO talking about X. ACME tested a few different tactics to achieve that. Open to hearing what they tried, what worked, and what didn't?") and you'll often be met with curiosity. 

Execs are hungry to know what others in their position are doing. What worked? What failed? What bets are other CXOs testing? It's why execs go to dinners and events. 

Remove your product, your pitch, and your value prop from your outbound + discovery.

Right now, POV that helps execs gain decision confidence is a better door opener than what you sell.

Here's what's happening: Typically, a SaaS company goes through a significant change approximately every 18 months. 

We call this a "phase shift," which means they need to rethink or redo how they run their business.

For example, when a company reaches around $5-10 million in revenue, they phase-shift away from Founder-Led sales (SLG) or Founder-Led Development (PLG). Later, they shift from startup mode, where growth depends on people, to scaleup mode, which is more about following established processes. Another phase shift might happen when they go public or get acquired.

Normally:

⏳   Phase shifts are anticipated and prepared for 18-mo's in advance

🕑  Phase shifts occur at different times for each company.

☝   Phase shifts occur one at a time

However, the SaaS market as a whole is currently going through multiple phase shifts, regardless of who they are:

#1. A Financial phase-shift: Moving from focusing on scalable growth to sustainable cost management.

#2. A Technology phase-shift: Transitioning from automation to embracing AI and other advanced technologies.

#3. A GTM phase-shift: Shifting from a product-centric approach to a more go-to-market-centric strategy.

#4. A Work Culture phase-shift: Changing from emphasizing "activity" to prioritizing "productivity" in a hybrid work environment.

And here is the kicker: All four of these phase shifts happen unexpectedly and they affect all of us, and at the exact same time. 

So far, as an industry, we have taken these actions:

✂ ) Cost Cutting: While this was long overdue, we now see diminishing returns. After all, you can achieve only so much by cutting costs.

💤 ) Stabilizing/Do nothing: This isn't due to a lack of determination but rather a deliberate decision. If you're unsure about your direction, sometimes it's better to stay put.

📚 ) Observe/Learn:  It seems that overall, people get the PLG/PLS is neither a LeadGen campaign nor a Sales Strategy and that AI is Everything, Everywhere All At Once, so that the best you can do is "keep up."

None of these options will solve 2024 by itself. And this is why we need to get together.  

Thankfully Sam Jacobs from Pavilion who runs the most dedicated community has decided to bring GTM thought leaders together to Nashville 🎸  and challenge us, on behalf of his community, to solve this:

💯  Not for one company but for ALL companies. 

🌤  Not for just this winter season but for MANY seasons to come.  

So, we need to talk about this.. not as individuals but as a community.  Having been on the road with Sam this entire summer and having spoken with 100+ CxOs across six cities, it is clear that this is a crucial moment in time.

AI isn’t disrupting GTM... It is exposing its weakness. It's revealing what we should’ve fixed in 2018, 2019, 2020…
- Outdated processes,
- Bad execution,
- Disconnected data systems,
- Lack of an operating model,
and so on and so forth.

The good news? AI can help us rebuild GTM the right way.

Let me rephrase what I said earlier: 

AI didn’t disrupt GTM; it revealed how unscientific it was. And that points to a solution. Today's hypergrowth doesn’t come from doing more campaigns or hiring more people. 

It comes from faster feedback loops.
It comes from outputs fueling inputs. 
It comes from happy customers generate pipeline and usage. 
It fuels acquisition and expandsion.

That’s behind a compound growth engine. And that is not new. What is new is the velocity it operates at!  AI-Natives growth operate at 2x–10x the speed of traditional SaaS.

Get this: AI-Natives are doing to SaaS what SaaS did to On-Prem/Perpetual Software. 

And when speed becomes the differentiator, systems become the solution. Let me elaborate with an example:

Over a century ago, Amelia Earhart flew her first solo-flight in an airplane that obeyed the same laws of lift, drag, thrust, and gravity as the ones flying today. But today we fly at 10x the speed, not because the laws changed or humans operate faster, but because they are the result of advanced systems that operate faster.

Growth works the same way. The laws of compounding, feedback, and decay they still govern growth. What’s changed is our ability to design systems that operate at 10x the speed.

So the question is no longer “How do I do more?”

The real question is “How do I do it faster, with greater precision?”

It is the physics of growth that governs the future - not effort.

We see it every day.  Our clients operate anywhere from $10M to $10B. Those operating on incremental tweaks applied to broken process fail, those that are based on network effects (closed loops) succeed, and they do so faster and bigger!  

What our successful customers have in common is that they all adhere to growth principles rooted in science.

Conventional wisdom says the best way to convince a CFO to invest in brand is with data. I have a better approach.

Most CMOs make the case with attribution studies, web traffic, or share of voice models. The problem is that presenting data to a skeptical executive tends to make them more skeptical, not less. You're inviting the CFO to poke holes in your data, and CFOs are very good at that. Psychologists call this reactance: the more directly you challenge a belief, the harder people defend it. The smarter the executive, the stronger the reflex.

That’s why I think there's a more effective path, and it starts with a question.

THE TECHNIQUE
Next time you're sitting across from a CFO who wants to cut your brand budget and double down on MQLs, don't open with a data. Ask them to walk you through the last time they selected a new platform — perhaps something like Anaplan or Adaptive Insights, or an HRIS.

Ask it like this:
🔷 How did you first hear about the vendor you chose?
🔷 Before you ever talked to sales, what had you already decided?
🔷 Did you download a gated ebook? Attend a webinar? Click a banner ad?
🔷 Or did it come up at an audit committee dinner, or in a conversation with a peer CFO you trust?

Let them answer, without editorializing.

Most CFOs, if they're being honest, will describe something that looks nothing like a demand gen funnel. They'll describe reputation, word of mouth, and a vendor who was already on their mental shortlist before sales ever said a word.

WHY THIS WORKS
When people arrive at a conclusion through their own reasoning, it sticks in a way no slide deck can match. This is called self-persuasion — conclusions we arrive at through our own reasoning feel like convictions, not arguments we were talked into, and convictions are much harder to walk back. You're not changing their mind; you're helping them use it.

WHAT COMES NEXT
Once you've had that conversation, you can make your ask on different footing: "I'm not asking us to abandon measurement. I'm asking us to measure the right things."

For example, brand tracking surveys — even just 100 targeted respondents from your ICP a quarter — will show directional movement in awareness, perception, and preference. That's not the same as proving causation. But it's the honest version of measurement for a process that's genuinely nonlinear, and it tracks exactly the factors that drove your CFO's own last purchase decision.

I explored this topic and many others on the Passetto podcast with Carolyn Dilks — link in the comments.
