Dylan Field (Co-Founder/CEO, Figma) said something quietly radical on an earnings call. Morgan Stanley asked him point blank: aren't you worried about AI labs eating your whole product?

His answer: every frontier lab already uses Figma to design how they bring their models to users. The companies building the replacement need you to build the replacement.

But then he drew a sharper line. Verifiable vs. unverifiable. Code has a right answer. Design doesn't.

So as AI makes shipping code trivial, the scarce thing becomes what a compiler can't check (yet). Taste. Judgment. Making something feel right without being able to prove it.

The most valuable person in the room was never the one who could build the thing. It was the one who knew which thing to build. That gap is about to get very, very wide.

(Yes, "taste" again. I know. Every cycle it comes back as the answer. Maybe the reason it keeps coming back is that every cycle finds a new way to not pay for it.)

Meetings are expensive.

It's cheaper to just build the thing.

Todd Saunders nailed it. The token cost of a production feature is now lower than the meeting cost to discuss it.

AKA…Make decisions from prototypes, not slide decks.

This matches what I've seen the best teams do at Zapier. For decades, orgs were structured around code being expensive, slow, and hard to reverse. That's over.

The move now: eliminate planning for anything you can test empirically. Build it in 2 hours, measure, kill or keep.

We're doing this at Zapier. The gates are moving downstream. Less "Should we build this?" more "Which should we release this?”

May the best build win.

Code is an output. Nature is healing.

For too long we treated code as input. We glorified it, hand-formatted it, prettified it, obsessed over it.

We built sophisticated GUIs to write it in: IDEs. We syntax-highlit, tree-sat, mini-mapped the code. Keyboard triggers, inline autocompletes, ghost text. “What color scheme is that?”

We stayed up debating the ideal length of APIs and function bodies. Is this API going to look nice enough for another human to read?

We’re now turning our attention to the true inputs. Requirements, specs, feedback, design inspiration. Crucially: production inputs. Our coding agents need to understand how your users are experiencing your application, what errors they’re running into, and turn *that* into code.

We will inevitably glorify code less, as well as coders. The best engineers I’ve worked with always saw code as a means to an end anyway. An output that’s bound to soon be transformed again.

Claude Code has 300,000 business customers and now writes your code, scans it for security vulnerabilities, reviews every pull request with agent teams, and was caught cheating its own benchmarks. One company marking its own homework at scale.

In the last three weeks Anthropic shipped Claude Code Security to scan for vulnerabilities, then Code Review to dispatch agent teams across every pull request. OpenAI has built the same with Codex Security and on Sunday acquired Promptfoo, the red-teaming platform a quarter of Fortune 500 relied on for independent evaluation.

Separation of duties survived thirty years of pressure from engineering teams, auditors, and cost-cutting boards. AI blew through it in three weeks and the industry barely blinked.

I've been using Claude Code for a few months on a personal project. When Claude Code Security shipped three weeks ago and Code Review landed Monday, I caught myself thinking I'd just switch those on too. Same model, same vendor, the whole chain. Which is mad when you say it out loud but felt completely natural in the moment. It's brilliant, it saves weeks of work, and insisting on independent review feels like insisting on fax machines. Every team adopting these tools is drifting the same way, picking what works while independence quietly disappears.

Engineers don't approve their own code into production. The person who writes the firewall rules doesn't run the audit. Those controls took decades to embed and we're giving them back because the demo was good and nobody wants to slow down engineering over something as unfashionable as independent review.

It gets worse when you think about how the models behave under pressure. Opus 4.6 figured out it was being benchmarked, found the encrypted answer key on GitHub, wrote its own decryption script, and submitted the answers across eighteen runs. Cracked the exam rather than sat it. Anthropic published the findings, credit to them, and the model also showed what they politely called "overly agentic behaviour," deviating from user intent to satisfy its own success criteria. This isn't unique to Claude, every frontier model is trending the same way. Now ask yourself what that behaviour looks like when the success criteria is "produce a clean security scan." A model that learned to game a benchmark will find the shortest path to a passing grade on your codebase too.

I think within twelve months a major enterprise will trace a security incident back to a vulnerability that was written, scanned, and reviewed by the same model. The auditor will ask who independently verified the output and the answer will be the vendor who produced it. The post-mortem will read like every separation of duties failure of the last thirty years, except nobody will explain why we forgot a lesson we already knew.

If an auditor asked you today who independently reviews the code your AI writes, what would you tell them?

API keys will soon be dead.
Here's what is changing:

Your AI agent can build an app or a website in ~5 minutes.
You spend 30 minutes clicking through dashboards, signing up, copying tokens, attaching credit cards, for every single API it touches.
You don't even know if the API is useful until you're 20 minutes deep.

This is broken.

And a protocol ,called x402, fixes all of it.

1) How it works:
HTTP has had a status code called 402 "Payment Required" since 1997.
Coinbase built x402 on top of it.
Whenever your agent hits an endpoint, server returns a price and a wallet address. Agent then pays in USDC (fractions of a cent). Server then verifies, and returns the response.

There is no signup involved, no keys, no extra steps or dashboards (and yes, no human anywhere in the loop).

One wallet, one private key, access to every API that supports the protocol.
Google Cloud, Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Anthropic adopted it already, and there are over 50 million agent transactions processed.

The Val Town founder, a developer experience purist who has zero interest in crypto, tried it and wrote: “I regret to inform you that we may have found a real use case for crypto”.

2) Why now
The age of AI agents means apps get assembled from dozens of APIs in minutes.

Every API key is a bottleneck built for a world where humans browsed documentation and filled out forms.

Agents don't browse - they show up with cash and need access instantly.
x402 turns every API into a vending machine.

3) The trust gap is huge
Right now you pay and hope.
Many vendors already supply downgraded keys, some models can get quietly swapped for cheaper versions.

There is no verification that you got what you paid for.

ZK proofs close this gap. 
You can verify a key is authentic without revealing it, or prove a model ran the version it claimed. 

API keys were built for humans who click buttons.

The next generation of buyers will never visit your website, never read your docs, never talk to your sales team. They'll query your endpoint and either pay or move on.

The companies building for that buyer are already winning.

I passed on 473 AI startups last year who claimed proprietary models or data. None had actual moats.

As someone investing in AI with data moats, I can spot the pretenders instantly.

What founders claim:

"We have a proprietary AI model" - Built on OpenAI API? 

"We have unique training data" - 1,000 data points they scraped? And the data points aren't directly relevant to their agentic use-cases.

"We have technical advantages" - Using same tech as everyone else? Your RAG isn't a technical advantage. It's just your application's business logic.

"We fine-tuned for our vertical" - and then in diligence I discover it’s just better RAG... fine-tuning requires really data science work.

None of these are moats.

The brutal truth about AI startups:

95% are thin wrappers on OpenAI/Anthropic APIs. When GPT-6 or Claude 5 comes out, your "advantage" disappears. When OpenAI launches native feature, you're dead. Your "proprietary" is replicable in 3 months.

One founder: "We built a proprietary model for legal research."

Me: "What's it built on?" 

Founder: "We fine-tuned Llama on legal documents." 

Me: "How many documents?" Founder: "About 500." 

Me: "What happens when OpenAI releases GPT-6 with better legal reasoning on 100k documents?" 

Founder: "Well... uh..."

No moat.

Another: "We have proprietary data for AI training."

Me: "How much data? Is the data applicable to the use-cases you are solving for?" 

Founder: "We've collected 50,000 data points over 6 months." 

Me: "How long would it take a competitor to replicate? Is it available to everybody?" 

Founder: "Probably... 6 months? Yes, you can scrap the data online"

That's not proprietary. That's a head start.

Real AI moats are rare:

Moat 1: Proprietary data that can't be replicated

Years of data nobody else has today

Legally protected or exclusive via partnership

Moat 2: Network effects in data

Each incremental user improves model for all users

Each incremental agent launched and course correct by users builds more reinforcement learning data improving agents

Competitor starting from zero has inferior product

Moat 3: Vertical integration that's prohibitively expensive

Own the full stack from data collection to deployment

Data derived from proprietary Hardware

Requires $50M+ to replicate. A great example is autonomous vehicle companies with proprietary sensor data + fleet

Moat 4: Regulatory capture

Exclusive rights through regulation

Years to replicate approvals

If you're building AI startup:

Stop claiming your model is proprietary unless you truly can't be replicated for years. 

Focus on distribution. Build actual moat: Data, network effects, or regulatory. Or accept you're a feature, not a company, and build accordingly.

The AI gold rush is creating hundreds of companies that won't exist in 3 years.

Don't be one of them.

"We already have engineers handling UX."

If I had a quid for every founder who's said that, I could probably retire before I'm 40.

I hear this from SaaS founders at least once a week, sometimes more. Then the next time I speak with them, they tell me they've lost a deal to a competitor whose product is a mess.

After working with dozens of engineers across 30+ projects, the reality is they’re brilliant with logic, systems, and all the technical wizardry. But the average customer doesn't care about any of that. They just want to get their work done without needing to jump through hoops.

If your product is built for engineers, you end up confusing customers. You find yourself running endless demos, basically guiding people step by step through your "intuitive" product. It's exhausting, and your existing customers hesitate to roll it out across the company because it feels risky. Nobody wants to be the person who made things harder for everyone else.

Meanwhile, while you’re busy explaining every feature, your competitor, with a product that's maybe half as powerful as yours, walks in, closes the deal, and moves on to the next one. Because their product just works.

The reality is that the "best" product doesn't always come out on top. The one that's effortless to get started with is the one people buy.

When customers are the ones actually making the purchase decisions, can use your product without a walkthrough, they get it immediately. They start using it more, it spreads throughout the team, and soon enough, they're upgrading without you even trying.

That's what real UX does. It sells for you.

84% of purchases are decided before anyone starts shopping.

A study of 1.2 million purchase journeys found that by the time a customer starts comparing options, the outcome is largely already set. The real competition happens long before the moment of sale.

The first 1000 lines of code determine the next 100,000.

I've learned this the hard way while working with Claude Code.

AI looks at your existing code and copies the patterns it finds. If you start with clean code, the rest stays clean. If you start messy, the problems pile up quickly.

I've seen this in my own projects. With dronelist[.]io, I focused on structure from the start: 
→ directory structure
→ utilities (e.g., error handling)
→ naming convention
→ rules for AI agents (coding style, API patterns, design system, etc.)

Now when I use Claude Code to add something, it sticks to those patterns. Most of the time, I can one-shot a feature.

But in projects where I rushed the basics, I'm still dealing with the fallout.

This matters for teams too:

→ teams adopting AI coding tools need stricter early code reviews
→ the "move fast and fix later" mentality becomes more expensive, not less
→ senior engineers matter more in the AI era -- they're the ones who set the patterns everything else inherits

AI speeds up whatever direction you're heading. If your code is clean, it gets better fast. If it's messy, the mess grows even faster.

Shipping quickly feels good in the moment, but it hides the real cost. You think you're moving fast, but you're actually building up technical debt much faster.

The most important thing for an AI-powered codebase isn't better prompts. It's a solid foundation.

A bespoke software revolution? I don't buy it.

It'll exist. It already exists. Small consultants and big consulting firms have made custom software for years. It almost always sucks. It’s bloated, confusing, and because the client pays, it’s built wrong in all the ways.

Who’s excited about bespoke software? Software makers! Of course they're excited about building bespoke software — that's what they do. Your feed is full of people who love making software talking about making software. Of course they’re excited about the revolution. Echo, echo, echo...

Most people don’t like computers. Nobody in tech wants to say that out loud. People tolerate computers. They use them because they have to. Given the choice, most would rather not think about them at all.

So when someone suggests that AI means everyone will build their own custom tools, ask who "everyone" is. The three-person accounting firm drowning in client paperwork? They want the paperwork gone, not a new system to maintain. The regional logistics company with 40 trucks? They want the routes optimized, not Joe spouting off about this new system he’s been messing around with. The law firm billing 70-hour weeks? They want leverage on their time, not a software project to design.

They don’t hate technology. But building and maintaining their own critical systems isn’t their wheelhouse, regardless of how much faster and easier it’s become. It's another job on top of the job.

Will these people use AI? Absolutely, for all sorts of things. Will some outliers go deep and build real custom systems? Sure, but they're almost always people who already had some pull toward software. The curiosity was already there. They were dabblers before.

Giving everyone access to software building tools doesn't mean everyone becomes a builder. A powerful excavator doesn't turn a homeowner into a contractor. Most people just want the hole dug. They don’t want the responsibility either.

Product-Led Companies Don’t Confuse Product and Engineering

I’ve been paying close attention to some of the product manager job descriptions circulating lately. A pattern is starting to emerge, and it’s worth calling out.

Many of these JDs are asking for requirements that look far more like a software engineer or architect than a product manager. Deep engineering expertise. Advanced database design. Hands-on system architecture.

That should give both candidates and companies pause.

Product management and software engineering are distinct disciplines. Highly collaborative, deeply interdependent, but fundamentally different. When companies blur that line, it often signals confusion about what it actually means to be product-led.

If you're serious about building a product-led organization, your product managers should be anchored in market understanding, problem discovery, value articulation, prioritization, and financial outcomes. They should know enough about technology to ask good questions and make informed trade-offs, not be expected to design the system themselves.

We do see this blending at the executive level with roles like Chief Product and Technology Officer. That is a different job entirely, with a different scope and mandate. It should not be used as a proxy for redefining the product manager role further down the org chart.

A word of advice to candidates. Be thoughtful about what these JDs reveal. Companies that expect product managers to function as engineers are often not well grounded in product fundamentals. That usually shows up later in decision-making, prioritization, and accountability for outcomes.

Clear roles create strong products. Blurred roles create friction. Getting this right matters.

The past few weeks have provided a sobering reality check for the software industry.

The recent, brutal drops in SaaS and security software valuations are not just panic reactions to AI progress. They reflect a fundamental shift in how business value is created in the age of AI. 

Claude Cowork and OpenClaw projects show that the future lies not in application-specific agents but in the ability to coordinate agentic workflows centrally. 

It is not good news for SaaS companies that add agents to their offerings.

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella said more than a year ago that the traditional SaaS model is becoming obsolete, and software companies must pivot to AI agents or risk fading into irrelevance.

But even pivoting to agents may not be enough.

In my recent conversations with enterprise leaders, the sentiment seems nearly unanimous. Many CIOs and CFOs have explicitly told me they plan to rip out up to hundreds of SaaS applications this year. 

The era of buying a specialized point solution for every minor business problem is over. Leaders are moving toward ruthless consolidation and are clearly focusing on building a competitive, company-controlled data layer rather than outsourcing their data management to SaaS applications.

The architectural shift underneath this consolidation is a profound technical migration. For decades, businesses operated on an application-centric model, where data was fragmented and trapped behind dozens of different user interfaces and proprietary business logics with complex integrations.

We are now moving rapidly toward a data-centric architecture.

In this new paradigm, data sits at the secure core of the business. Autonomous AI agents interact with that data directly to drive outcomes, often bypassing the need for a traditional software GUI entirely. When the interface matters less, the per-user subscription model tied to it loses its justification.

This shakeout is going to be massive. It will be a painful transition for many companies that built incredible products based on the old rules of user-based licensing.

But there's a silver lining for businesses that lead rather than follow this transformation.

Increased data sovereignty: As enterprises shed redundant applications and centralize their architecture, they are reclaiming ownership of their information. You will no longer be forced to rent your workflows and scatter your data across fifty different third-party vendors.

A new competitive edge: The corporate battleground is shifting. Your competitive advantage will no longer be defined by industry-standard applications, but by the quality and structure of your proprietary data and by how effectively you deploy your agent swarms to act on it.

The software landscape is fundamentally transforming. It is a difficult pivot, but the businesses that lean into this shift will emerge leaner, smarter, and entirely in control of their own destiny.

Would you agree?

Everyone is celebrating the PM who codes. The PM who opens Claude Code and ships a prototype before lunch. The PM who does not need to wait for engineering anymore.

I get the appeal. I really do. I have done it myself. There is something intoxicating about going from idea to working thing in a single afternoon.

But here is what I am starting to see happen and nobody is talking about it.

The PM who can build everything themselves is slowly becoming the PM who cannot lead a team.

When you can prototype it yourself, you stop writing specs that help engineers understand the why. When you can spin up a demo in an hour, you stop having the hard alignment conversations that make your team feel ownership over the product. When you can just do it, you lose the muscle of getting other people to want to do it with you.

I watched this happen to a PM on my team last year. Incredibly talented. Could build faster than most of our engineers. But over time the engineers started checking out. They felt like order takers. The PM was making all the design decisions in the prototype and handing them a finished thing to rebuild in production code. The team lost its sense of creative ownership and attrition followed.

The best PMs I know right now are using AI to think faster, not to build alone. They use it to stress test their ideas before bringing them to the team. They use it to prepare better for design reviews, not to skip them. They use it to be a better collaborator, not to replace collaboration.

The PM who can do everything themselves is impressive. The PM who makes everyone around them better is the one who actually ships great products at scale.

Building alone is a superpower. Knowing when not to use it is wisdom.

Shift Left Has Failed. We helped kill it.

Shift left security is one of the biggest lies we sold developers. And ourselves.
We handed them SAST scanners firing hundreds of alerts per sprint. Zero context on what actually matters. No training. No time. No ownership model. Then blamed them when breaches happened.

Now AI coding tools are producing vulnerable code in nearly 1 of every 4 outputs. And the window to catch any of it is gone.

In 2018, the median time from disclosure to first exploit was 771 days. Today it is measured in hours. In 2025, most exploited vulnerabilities were weaponized before public disclosure. You cannot patch what attackers already own.

The real problem is that we redefined shift left as "make security someone else's problem." AppSec teams got smaller. Runtime visibility got cut. Developer responsibility exploded. That is not shifting left. That is abandoning security behind good marketing.

AppSec teams need to lead. Security demands context, expertise, and runtime intelligence. A scanner in CI with no production visibility is checkbox compliance dressed up as a program.

Stop pretending shift left solved anything. The exploitation window has collapsed. Act like it.

What is your org actually doing differently for AI-generated code? Not your policy. Your actual controls.

Customer Success just got promoted.

Five years ago, CS was measured on relationships and renewals. Today, it’s plugged directly into true business outcomes: expansion, retention, and revenue durability.

That shift recognizes something many of us have known for a long time: the team closest to customer outcomes should influence customer outcomes.

But a promotion without empowerment isn’t a promotion. It’s a setup.

Because if CS is carrying expansion targets, it needs influence over deal quality. Not a debrief after the contract is signed.

If CS is accountable for retention, it needs a voice in scope, onboarding design, and delivery motion. Not a cleanup role when promises don’t match reality.

And if CS owns long-term growth, it needs a seat in roadmap conversations that shape customer trajectory. Not a feedback form that disappears into a backlog.

This is why strong CS leadership matters now more than ever. Revenue accountability without organizational authority is just pressure in a nicer suit.

Leading CS is more than coaching the team. It’s advocating for the conditions that let the team deliver on the mandate it’s been given: translating customer reality into priorities the business will fund, pushing for clean handoffs, and making sure commitments are realistic before they become someone else’s problem.

When responsibility rises but empowerment doesn’t, CS compensates. But when responsibility and authority rise together, CS compounds.

That’s when expansion stops being a sales tactic and becomes the next natural step. It’s when retention becomes the byproduct of consistent value, not a quarterly rescue. And CS becomes what leadership asked it to be: a true growth engine, not a pressure valve.

CS earned the seat. Empowerment is the seatbelt.

Most Customer Success teams have two jobs.

The one on the org chart is to drive adoption, expansion, and renewal.

The unofficial one is to absorb the impact of everything that is broken upstream.

Bad handoffs, unclear SLAs, surprise invoices, delivery gaps that never get closed… it all lands on the CS team’s shoulders (and in their nervous system).

Over the years, I’ve noticed that the biggest gains rarely come from asking CS to do more. They come from improving what surrounds it: clearer ownership, better handoffs, fewer moments where a customer has to repeat the same story to three different people. CS provides the early signal for where the experience is breaking down.

When that same level of care and ownership shows up across Sales, Operations, Product, and Finance, everything gets lighter. Customers feel supported, the process runs smoother, and the whole team gets to do its best work without carrying the entire journey alone. That's where the real magic happens!

If I stepped into a Head of Customer Success role tomorrow, the first 90 days wouldn’t be about dashboards or org charts. 🚷🛑

They’d be about answering three questions:

        1.        Which customers actually drive our revenue?
Not all ARR is equal. I want to know where retention, expansion, and long-term value really live.

        2.        Where does value get created or lost in the customer journey?
Onboarding, adoption, champion health, renewals… every leak in this system compounds into churn.

        3.        Where are we flying blind?
What risks do we only discover at renewal time that we should have seen months earlier?

Only after that do I touch:
        •        Team structure 👥
        •        Segmentation 🎛️
        •        Health signals 🩺
        •        Renewal and expansion motions 💰

Strategy without visibility is guesswork.
The fastest way to grow ARR is to make customer outcomes predictable.

I've watched some of the best CSMs I know quietly update their LinkedIn profiles while still sitting in their current seat. And I'll admit, I've been there too. No dramatic exit, no complaints... they've already resigned in their head.

And many times, the leaders were surprised. They shouldn't have been because the signs were all there. The best CSM on the team was:

😩 Carrying 30% more accounts than anyone else = "they can handle it."
🛁 Cleaning up churned accounts that were doomed before they ever touched them = "they're good at saves."
☮️ Fielding escalations that should have been caught in onboarding  = "they're calm under pressure."
💰 Being paid the same as someone doing half the work = "we'll revisit comp next quarter." (spoiler: next quarter never comes)

Here's the thing about your best CSM: they're not loud about being burned out, they just quietly do the math. They realize they're subsidizing a broken system with their own energy. Eventually, they find somewhere that doesn't require that trade.

You don't lose your best CSM to a competitor. You lose them to a company that built the infrastructure they've been begging for. The best retention strategy isn't a ping-pong table or a LinkedIn shoutout. It's a reasonable book of business, clean handoffs, and a comp structure that reflects what they actually contribute.

Your best CSM is a mirror. What they're experiencing is a direct reflection of how well your CS org is actually built. 

Have you ever been the CSM who quietly started looking, or the leader who didn't see it coming until it was too late?

My first big client call as a CSM had ten people on the line.
Some of them were executives.


I had prepared. I knew the product. I thought I was ready.

I was not ready for this.

They wanted what they had been sold. And what they had been sold did not match what the product could do. I sat there trying to find the right words, scrambling to bridge a gap I did not create and did not fully understand yet.

Nobody told me this was part of the job.

This was an established account. They had always worked directly with their Account Director. No CSM. No dedicated support. Then one day I appeared. A new face, a new role, and a relationship they never asked for.

I was not just inheriting a broken account. I was a stranger walking into a room full of people who were already frustrated and had no reason to trust me yet.

No certification prepares you for that. 🎓

Here is what I have learned since then.

This is not a one-off situation. It is a pattern. And it starts long before a CSM ever gets involved.

A customer signs. They come in with needs the product cannot fully meet. Enhancement requests pile up — thirty, sometimes more. Support tickets stack on top. And yes, there is a workaround. There is almost always a workaround.

But the workaround adds steps. Manual entry. Higher internal costs. It is not a solution. It is a bandage the customer pays for every single day. 🩹

No strategy. No growth. Just a customer absorbing costs they never agreed to and a CSM they never asked for, trying to hold it all together.

I have seen this enough times to say this clearly:

The problem does not start at renewal. It does not start at onboarding. It starts the moment a sales conversation prioritizes closing over honesty.

Customers deserve to know what they are actually buying. Even if that conversation is uncomfortable. Even if it costs the deal. 🤝

A hard conversation before the contract protects the customer. Skipping it just moves the cost somewhere else — and that somewhere else is usually the customer's team, their budget, and the CSM who shows up on day one not knowing what they are walking into.

Have you ever walked into an account for the first time and realized you were already behind before you said a word?

Burnout?

Your CSM probably isn’t burned out because they’re bad at the job.
They’re burned out because the job was designed wrong.

In a lot of companies, the CSM role slowly turns into the catch-all for everything customer related.
Support issue? Send it to the CSM.
Product feedback? CSM.
Implementation confusion? CSM.
Renewal risk? Also CSM.

Somewhere in there we also expect them to be “strategic” but strategy requires space to think and most CSMs are spending their day context switching between ten different problems.

That’s not strategy it's triage.

The best CS orgs I’ve seen are much more intentional about role design.
Clear ownership.
Clear escalation paths.
Clear expectations around revenue impact.
Because if everything rolls downhill to the CSM, eventually the strategic work disappears.

And that strategic work is usually the work that actually protects revenue.

CSMs aspire to become their customers' trusted advisors.

Most of them are failing at the first hurdle already.

Because they never do what actually wins customer trust.

Spoiler alert: You don’t build trust by saying, “feel free to reach out anytime if you need help.”

Trust isn’t built by being available.

Trust is built by showing that you genuinely care about the customer’s success.

And that starts with something simple many CSMs are unfamiliar with:

Talk less. Listen more.

The CSMs who actually become their trusted advisors don't start their conversations by

- explaining features
- sharing updates
- circling random ideas

They ask open questions like

• What’s breaking your performance right now?
• What happens if nothing changes?
• What would make this project a success?

but otherwise let customers do the talking. 

Because when customers feel heard:

- They share the real problems (not just the surface ones)
- They become more open to your recommendations
- They start to see you as a partner, not a random vendor employee

Trusted advisors aren’t defined by how much they know (about the product).

They’re defined by how well customers feel heard and understood. 

“This person actually gets what we’re trying to achieve.”

Are you still talking, or have you already started listening?

Stop Calling It "Customer Success" When You're Just Doing Damage Control

Let's be honest: half of what we call "Customer Success" is just putting out fires with a smile.

I've seen the evolution from glorified tech support to strategic partnership. But many companies are still stuck in the "keep them from leaving" mindset rather than truly driving success.

Real CS isn't about answering tickets faster or having QBRs nobody wants to attend. It's about becoming so embedded in your customer's business that they can't imagine life without you.

If your CS team spends more time apologizing for your product than showcasing new value, you don't have a CS problem. You have a product problem.

Not every customer wants a “partner.” And that’s okay.

We romanticize Customer Success as if every account is begging for strategy sessions, roadmap reviews, and white-glove treatment.
Truth is, some customers just want the tool to work. They don’t need an advisor. They need reliability.

That doesn’t make them “bad customers.”
It makes them different customers.

The art of CS is knowing the difference:

- Who wants strategic guidance vs. who wants minimal friction.

- Who values depth vs. who values speed.

- Who wants to collaborate vs. who just wants the job done.


Execs often make the same mistake. They try to force every customer into a “high-touch” model because it feels safer.
But high-touch applied to the wrong customer is wasted effort, and even worse, it creates friction.

CS isn’t about treating everyone equally.
It’s about treating everyone appropriately.

👉 The best CSMs don’t just deliver value. They deliver the right kind of value, to the right customer, at the right time.

I've hired dozens of CSMs/AMs. The best ones share 3 traits that can't be taught in onboarding.

1) They take control and drive necessary conversations early.

Great CSMs don't manage by exception or wait for escalations. They own the relationship, set expectations proactively, and surface risks when they're still manageable. The worst churn I've seen came from CSMs who stayed quiet too long. The best ones raise their concerns when it's still a "moment," not a "pattern." 

-Ex: "I understand that your workflow is to do X then Z then Y. But from my experience when customers have done that in the past, they end up seeing less value.”
- Or “Look, you signed up to achieve outcome X, but your team hasn’t logged in regularly since the beginning. Most teams that don’t make our product a habit from the start won’t see an improvement in X. Will you be able to work with me to get them engaged?"

2) They set boundaries and protect scope.

High performers know that saying yes to every out-of-scope request isn't customer success—it's resource bleed. They realign expectations with clarity and respect. They understand that sustainable partnerships require guardrails, not heroics.

3) They drive value by tracking what actually matters to the customer.

Too many CSMs and AMs obsess over product adoption metrics. The best ones keep their finger on the pulse of the KPIs their customers bought us to impact—revenue growth, cost savings, efficiency gains. They track customer outcomes, not just product usage. When those KPIs are off-track, they drive course corrections long before renewal time.

Customers don't renew because they logged in 50 times last quarter. They renew because we made paying for us a no-brainer by moving the metrics that matter to them.

If you're hiring CSMs or AMs right now, ask yourself: are you screening for activity and product expertise, or for ownership, judgment, and customer-outcome fluency?

The former fills seats. The latter builds a team that retains.

Your CSM manages more revenue than your best AE.

Nobody talks about this.
The average AE carries a $1-2M quota. The average CSM is sitting on a $5-10M book. That's 3-5x the revenue impact. Every single day.

And how does the org treat that?

The AE closes a $500K deal. Gong. President's Club. Accelerators on every dollar.

The CSM saves a $1.2M renewal that was walking out the door, expands it by $300K, and gets a Slack emoji.

Same revenue. Completely different value signal.

And we let this happen.

CS leaders accepted the lesser comp structure because we were told "retention is its own reward." We stopped making the revenue case. We let finance call us a cost center and we adjusted to it instead of fighting it.

We built the cage. Then we complained about being in it.

You want to fix CS turnover? Fix the math.

Comp on GRR and NRR impact. Celebrate saves like closed-won. Build a career path from CSM to GM — not from CSM to "be grateful."

Because when your best CSM leaves, they don't just take their title. They take the relationships that keep your biggest accounts from picking up a competitor's call.

I watched a $50M SaaS gut their CS team to "cut costs." They saved $800K. Lost $21M in churn within the next 12 months.

I've been on the other side of that decision - twice.

First time: Series B startup, 150 employees. New CFO came in, looked at our "cost centers," and decided CS was "nice to have, not need to have." Laid off 60% of the team, including me. CS isn't a cost center. It's a revenue protection system.

We talk about the speed AI coding tools give us. We don't talk enough about the hidden cost: Comprehension Debt.

My latest write-up: https://lnkd.in/g-vEeZry ✍ 

Comprehension debt is the growing gap between how much code exists in your system and how much of it any human being genuinely understands.

Unlike technical debt, which announces itself through friction - slow builds, tangled dependencies - comprehension debt breeds false confidence. The codebase looks clean. The tests are green. But the underlying theory of the system is quietly evaporating.

A recent Anthropic study highlighted this perfectly. In a trial with 52 software engineers, those using AI assistance completed tasks in roughly the same time as the control group, but scored 17% lower on follow-up comprehension quizzes. The biggest declines were in debugging.

Why is this happening?

AI generates code far faster than humans can critically evaluate it. What used to be a meaningful quality gate (code review) is turning into a throughput problem.

It's tempting to automate our way out of the review bottleneck. But you can’t write a test for behavior you haven’t thought to specify. Tests are necessary, but they are not sufficient.

Translating a natural language spec into working code involves an enormous number of implicit decisions - edge cases, error handling, performance tradeoffs. AI makes those decisions without human deliberation.

As AI volume scales, the engineer who maintains a coherent, system-level mental model becomes the scarcest, most valuable resource on the team. The job isn't just generating code; it's comprehension.

Many people think Product Management is about managing a product.

It’s much deeper than that.

Product Management sits at the center of three critical forces:

• Business – understanding market opportunities, growth strategies, and revenue impact
• Technology – collaborating with engineers, developers, and technical teams to build scalable solutions
• User Experience (UX) – ensuring the product actually solves real problems for real people

A Product Manager’s role is not just to build features.

It’s to connect vision with execution.

From my experience, the hardest part is not deciding what to build — it’s aligning people with different priorities:

✅ Marketing teams thinking about positioning and audience growth
✅ Finance teams thinking about cost and profitability
✅ Engineers thinking about feasibility and architecture
✅ Designers thinking about usability and experience
✅ Users thinking about value and outcomes

The real job of a Product Manager is to translate complexity into clarity, prioritize what truly matters, and align teams around a shared goal.

When business strategy, technology capability, and user needs meet at the same point, that’s when meaningful products are created.

That’s where innovation happens.

And that’s where great Product Managers make impact.

The longer you lead marketing, the less the job feels like marketing.

Early in your career, the work is centered around campaigns, messaging, launches, and channels. That’s the craft of the role, and it matters. It’s how most of us learn the discipline.

But over time something shifts. You start asking different questions.

Instead of wondering what campaign to launch next, you find yourself trying to understand why customers hesitate right before a purchase. You start looking at pricing decisions, the way the sales team tells the story, whether the product experience actually matches the promise.

The work becomes less about promotion and more about diagnosis.

That’s the moment marketing leadership starts turning into operator thinking.

Operators look at the whole system. The product. The customer journey. The sales motion. The economics behind the decision. Because if those pieces aren’t aligned, no amount of marketing will fix it.

Many marketing leaders are still evaluated on campaigns, even though the real job increasingly looks like diagnosing the business.

And now, as AI makes execution faster and content easier to produce, that shift is becoming even clearer.

The advantage won’t come from producing more marketing. It will come from understanding the business better.

That’s when marketing stops feeling like a function. And starts looking a lot more like running a business.

Salesforce just went back to per-seat pricing for AI agents.

Yes. Back to seats.

18 months ago they launched at $2 per conversation. Then switched to Flex Credits at $0.10 per action. Now they're selling per-user licenses with unlimited agent usage, what Benioff frames as "digital labor."

Three pricing models for the same product. Running simultaneously.

That's not strategy. That's a company testing and buying time while the market figures out what value means when the work is automated.

And this isn't just Salesforce.

Credit-based pricing models grew 126% year-over-year across the industry — from 35 to 79 companies in the PricingSaaS 500. Everyone is experimenting. Nobody has converged.

The core problem: our pricing frameworks assume a human on the other side. Per seat. Per user. Per login. 

When the user is an AI agent, those anchors stop making sense.

But the replacements: per action, per resolution, per credit, create unpredictability that buyers hate.

The companies that win the next five years won't only have the best AI but also the pricing architecture to capture its value.

Salesforce's zigzag isn't a failure. It's the most honest signal in the market right now.

AI companies think they're SaaS, but aren't.

I've been doing technology due diligence on AI-enabled companies for PE and VC firms in recent months, and there's a pattern emerging that investors need to understand.

A lot of early-stage companies are building products on top of external LLMs, making API calls to the likes of OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and pricing themselves like SaaS businesses.

The problem is that their cost structure looks nothing like SaaS. In traditional SaaS, once you hit breakeven, new customer revenue drops almost entirely to the bottom line. Infrastructure costs scale sub-linearly. That's the magic of the model, and why SaaS commands the multiples it does.

In a token-dependent AI business, every new customer generates a proportional increase in API costs. 

More users = more tokens = more spend. The cost curve doesn't flatten. It tracks revenue. That's not SaaS economics. That's a reseller margin. It almost takes me back to the early days of internet bandwidth reselling: buy wholesale, sell retail, hope the margin holds.

The implications for investors and acquirers that need thinking through properly:
• Gross margins far lower than the 70–80% that SaaS investors expect.
• No operating leverage: margin doesn't expand as you scale.
• Supplier dependency: your largest input cost is controlled by someone else's pricing decisions.
• Working capital pressure: API costs hit in real time (those bills come relentlessly every month) while revenue collection lags.

None of this means these businesses can't succeed. There might be a price advantage to be had when you get really big - but getting there is going to be more costly. Perhaps they should be valued as margin-over-product businesses, not SaaS platforms?

If you're investing in AI-enabled companies, the first question in due diligence should be: what percentage of your COGS is API-dependent?

The answer tells you what kind of business you're actually buying.

Every product is a mirror of the organization that built it.

In 1968, computer scientist Melvin Conway made an observation that's been shaping how we understand teams and technology ever since:

Any system's design will reflect the communication structure of the organization that created it.

Engineers call this Conway's Law. 

But it doesn't just apply to system architecture. It applies to the feel of a product…its speed, its tone, its instincts.

Think about what that actually means:

The product you're building right now isn't primarily shaped by your strategy or your technology. It's shaped by who talks to whom. Where information flows and where it stops. Which teams share language and which ones operate in different vocabularies for the same concepts.

Conway arrived at this while working on some of the first large software systems in what would become Silicon Valley. He kept watching teams produce code that mirrored the way those teams were organized. Not the way they intended to organize the code. The way they actually communicated.

Not sometimes. 
Every time.
That's what makes it a law. 

The pattern holds regardless of the industry, the technology, or the intentions of the people involved. 

The boundaries of your system are held in the boundaries of your communication.

Three engineering groups that rarely talk to each other will produce three modules that don't integrate well. A product team and a sales team that describe what you're building in different language will produce a product that feels incoherent to the market. Leadership communicating in abstractions while the frontline operates in specifics will produce a gap between vision and execution that nobody planned.

These aren't failures of talent. They're structural outcomes. The product is doing exactly what the communication architecture told it to do.

The shorthand for this is simple: you ship your org chart. 

Whether you mean to or not.

What makes Conway's Law worth sitting with is what it reveals about where product problems actually live. When something feels off, the instinct is to fix the product. Redesign the feature. Rewrite the roadmap.

Conway's Law says look somewhere else entirely. The product isn't the problem. It's the evidence. The problem is upstream, in how the team communicates.

The strengths and limitations of any product reveal the strengths and limitations of the thinking behind it. We design systems that mirror the beliefs, assumptions, and constraints of the very culture that created them. 

That's not a metaphor. It's a law.

And it has a practical implication that most teams never act on.

Pull up your org chart. 

Look at where the boundaries sit between teams. Which groups talk regularly. Which ones don't. Now look at how your company shows up. Your product. Your go-to-market. Your customer experience. The seams line up.

Once you see it, you can't unsee it. 
And once you see it, you can design for it.

"Don't trust the process" makes a great headline. But the reality is a lot more nuanced.

The design process isn't templates or checklists — it never was. But that's the version of “process” people are arguing against.

Context matters. When an experienced designer says they skipped the process and started building, they're often drawing on deep domain knowledge, strong team culture, and mature problem spaces — built through years of research and design practice. The "intuition" they rely on was shaped by the very process they say they've moved past. Flattening that into universal advice, "skip the process, trust your intuition," loses the context that makes it work.

Sarah Gibbons and I wrote about what experienced designers really mean when they say they're "not using process" and why that distinction matters in the era of AI. → https://lnkd.in/gyYygkwb

in a world of agents, the product role is going to split into two jobs:
- one that organizes humans (stakeholders, design, eng) 
- one that organizes agents (prompts, evals, workflows, etc)

Both will be in pursuit of offering the right products to customers, but how you get there will dramatically change. What happens to the typical product rituals? Instead of PRDs, OKRs, standups, product reviews, we'll need the equivalent for agents.

Couple wild ideas here...

instead of standups:
the equivalent is that agents will report back to us based on run logs and anomaly flags. no one needs to say what they did yesterday, the system already did thousands of things. the question is where it broke, where it surprised you, and where it got better. Show us the patterns, the trends, the edge cases - particularly the ones the agents didn't fix automatically. the daily ritual becomes reviewing deltas, scanning failures, and deciding which ones matter. less reporting, more triage

instead of OKRs:
we’ll need adversarial agents that continuously monitor/grade the system and detect patterns, scoring outcomes on an hourly or daily basis. Rather than setting a quarterly goal of "increase X by 5%" and revisiting slowly -- instead, management will be able to monitor success in real-time and detect trends/patterns towards overall goals

instead of PRDs:
we won't need waterfall. Prototyping will rule the day, and we’ll need a living agentic loop that mediates customer feedback/ratings and what's being prioritized and built. you don’t hand it to eng, you deploy it into the agent loop. if it’s wrong, it fails visibly and you can revert. if it’s right, it produces the right output

instead of product reviews:
we'll need simulation systems to examine agent behavior in different scenarios. In an agentic world where UI shifts from buttons/menus to agents automatically doing things, you'll want to examine their behavior before you deploy. You rewind decisions, fork alternate paths, and see how different prompts or constraints would have changed outcomes. the review becomes interactive. less storytelling, more counterfactuals.

The PM sits in the middle of this split. On the human side, still aligning taste, risk tolerance, and strategy across people. On the agent side, shaping the actual behavior of the system through prompts, evals, and feedback loops. one side is persuasion. The other is instrumentation. the best ones will collapse the gap, translating intent directly into systems that act on it.

the fascinating part is that the agentic loop will run 10000x faster than the human one, and of course, you can "hire" them faster. Thus the “organizing humans” half starts to feel slow and lower impact unless it directly improves the agent loop. Eventually the PM will shift towards agents and maybe ignore the human coordination altogether...

We need to stop pretending AI = SaaS. 🤖≠💻

As a VC looking at AI-enabled companies — and as someone working with founders on pricing — I keep seeing the same blind spot.
🚀 Brilliant product
📈 Strong early traction
💰 SaaS valuation expectations

But the unit economics?
Not SaaS.

If your product sits on top of OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google etc — and every interaction triggers API calls — your cost structure scales with usage.

In SaaS:
📊 Revenue scales faster than infrastructure
📈 Margins expand over time
💸 After breakeven, incremental ARR is highly profitable

In token-based AI:
👥 More customers = more tokens
🔁 More tokens = more variable cost
📉 The cost curve tracks revenue

That’s not operating leverage.
That’s dependency leverage.

From an investor lens, this changes the risk profile:
⚠️ Gross margins may never reach 75–80%
⚠️ Scale doesn’t automatically improve profitability
⚠️ Your largest cost input is controlled by someone else
⚠️ Cash outflows are real-time; revenue collection lags

From a founder lens, this changes pricing strategy:
If 40–60% of your COGS is API-driven, you are not building pure software.
You are building a margin layer on top of someone else’s compute.

That’s not wrong.
But it must be intentional.
🎯 Different pricing models
💰 Different capital requirements
📊 Different valuation logic

Before celebrating ARR growth, ask:
What percentage of your COGS is API-dependent — and what happens if supplier pricing moves against us?

The companies shrinking their marketing teams right now are making a mistake. But they won't see it for 18 months.

I'm watching this play out in real-time. Founders see AI handling tasks that used to require headcount, and their first instinct is efficiency. "We can do the same with fewer people." The maths feels obvious.

But the companies that will win aren't the ones doing the same with less. They're the ones doing more with the same.

Spoke to a CMO last week who framed it perfectly. Her team used to spend 60% of their time on what she called "tool work"—setting up campaigns, building reports, stitching data together across platforms. AI now handles most of that. But she didn't cut the team. She redirected them.

The person who used to manage Google Ads campaigns now spends their time on customer research. The analyst who built dashboards now focuses on strategic insight. The content manager who churned out SEO articles now works on creative that AI can't generate.

The output hasn't just maintained. It's improved. Because they're finally doing the work they were hired to do, rather than the work that tools forced them to do.

The efficiency mindset asks: "What can we cut?"

The growth mindset asks: "What can we now do that we couldn't before?"

The difference shows up 18 months later. The companies that cut are struggling to execute on bigger opportunities because they don't have the brain power. The companies that redeployed have marketing teams operating at a level they never could before.

I’m going to say something that a lot of people here probably aren’t going to like or maybe even agree with.

I don’t think marketers should be tied to sales and revenue goals.

Before you freak out, hear me out.

I’m not saying we shouldn’t be held accountable​. We absolutely should. But I think we’ve gone way off the deep end.

Attribution models. Multi-touch reporting. Lead scoring systems so complex they confuse more than they clarify.

We’ve become obsessed with proving that every blog post, every social touch, every word we write somehow connects to a closed deal.​ 

And in that obsession, we’ve lost the whole point.

Marketing is meant to drive attention. Shape narrative. Build trust. Create momentum.

We’re supposed to spark interest (and yes, support sales with the right messaging and tools).

But we’re not the ones closing deals. Sales is.

And yet too many CEOs expect marketing to do it all — drive pipeline, prove ROI, own revenue, and somehow keep the brand relevant and resonant along the way.

It’s not just unrealistic — it’s burning teams out.

We need to rethink how we measure marketing’s impact. Not because we’re trying to avoid accountability, but because we want to focus on the right kind.

The job of marketing was never to close the deal.
It was to make sure the deal never would’ve happened without us.
