Marketers weren’t hired to design.
But every campaign still needs visuals.
And every time, it’s the marketer who ends up making them.

You’re already wearing the strategy hat. 
The copywriting hat. 
The research hat. 
The project management hat. 
The “can you also update the sales deck” hat.

And then the campaign is ready to go live and someone needs to make the visual.

So you put on another hat.

You open Canva. You drag a few text boxes around. You try one layout. Then another. 

You wonder if that font is on-brand.
You export it, look at it on your phone, hate it, go back in, tweak it, export again.

Forty-five minutes later you’ve got something that’s… fine.

Not because you’re a designer. Because it’s 4pm and nobody else was going to do it.

And the thing is - this isn’t really a complaint. 

It’s just how marketing works now. Small teams, fast timelines, too many deliverables to send everything through design.

The smart teams protect their designers for the work that actually shapes the brand. 
And they find faster ways to handle the rest.

This is what PlayPlay Design does.

I tried it when I needed a quick visual for a post. Prompted the idea in. Got an on-brand visual out. Published it.

No template wrestling. No Slack thread about font sizes. No 45-minute detour.

Just one less hat to wear.

A Marketing Director said something to me recently "The hardest part of my job isn't the strategy. It's doing the strategy while also defending the need for strategy."

Every single week she's in rooms explaining why marketing isn't just execution. 
Why brand isn't a nice to have. 
Why you can't measure everything in a 30 day window. 
Why cutting the budget in Q1 will hurt the pipeline in Q3.

She's not doing this once.
She's doing it on a loop.
While also trying to actually do her job.
And she's up there with the best marketing leaders I know.

Imagine what you could do if you were able to spend the energy on the work instead of justifying the work.

If your Marketing leader is spending more time defending marketing than doing it
That's not a marketing problem.

That's a leadership alignment problem.
And it will cost you more than you realise.

Most marketing roles don’t fail because the marketer isn’t capable.

They fail because the role itself isn’t clearly defined.

When “marketing” owns brand, demand, lifecycle, events, tooling, and growth — but isn’t given decision rights, priorities, or success metrics - even strong leaders end up looking ineffective.

The best roles I’ve held (and built) had three things in common:
 • Clear ownership
 • Clear expectations
 • Clear accountability to revenue

When those are in place, marketing becomes a growth engine, not a support function.

You didn’t hire a marketer to be your hands.
You hired them to be your head. 

So why are you still making all the decisions?

Most marketing teams don’t fail because they’re bad.
They fail because they’re second-guessed to death. 

Their strategies get watered down because it's not what the CEO likes.

The fastest way to kill great marketing?
Make it a group project. 🚩 

Because marketing isn’t “what looks good.”
It’s what moves people.

If you want marketers to perform, give them this:

- A clear goal
- A clear audience
- A clear offer
- Clear decision rights
- Clear feedback loops

Then step back. 
Let your team test. 
Let them learn. 
Let them lead.

Trust the process, respect the data, and watch the growth follow.

One of the worst places to work as a marketing professional is a company that does not really believe in marketing.

They just hired you to “see if this marketing thing works.”

That setup is broken from day one.

Because what they usually mean is:

👉 they want results, but do not want to commit budget
👉 they want growth, but do not want to fix the product, sales process, or customer experience
👉 they want you to “run ads,” but have no patience for testing, learning, and iteration
👉 they blame marketing for revenue problems caused by the whole business

Marketing is not magic.

It works best when leadership already understands that growth is a system.

You need the right offer.
You need a product people actually want.
You need sales and operations that can convert demand.
Then marketing helps you scale what is already working.

If a company hires a marketer as an experiment, they usually make the marketer the experiment too.

And that is why many good marketers fail in bad companies.

Before you take the job, ask one question:

Do they want marketing support, or are they looking for someone to blame when growth does not happen fast enough?

I love new marketing ideas.
I do not love random acts of marketing.

 If you work in marketing, you’ve probably heard things like:
“We should be on every social platform.”
“Can we do a webinar next week?”
“What if we did a billboard?”
“My neighbor’s startup got leads from [insert shiny tool here]. We should try it.”

Ideas aren’t the problem.

In fact, I love teams full of creative, curious people.
The problem is when everything is treated as equally urgent, equally strategic, and equally aligned to…nothing in particular.
My job isn’t to say yes to every idea.

My job is to ask:
Does this support our core strategy?
Does it move a real business metric?
Do we have the capacity to do it well?
What are we saying “no” to if we say “yes” to this?

Sometimes that means the most exciting, fun, cool, bright, and shiny idea doesn’t make the cut—at least not right now.
That’s not being negative. That’s being responsible.

Good marketing leadership is less about inventing ideas and more about protecting focus.

 How do you decide what not to do when the idea list keeps growing?

Marketing is not:

👉 writing a few posts.
👉 sending a few emails.
👉 making things "pretty."

It's strategy. 
It's positioning. 
It's psychology. 
It's understanding numbers 
It's understanding emotions.

Good marketing is never an accident.
It's a system — not a shot in the dark.

Last week I spoke with two startups who were looking for a CMO. After an hour with each of them, my advice was: you don't need one.

Great companies, great founders, fantastic products. But they weren't yet at the stage where hiring a CMO made sense.

The first had never hired a marketing person before. They wanted to go straight to CMO because "we need someone senior who can build the function."

What they actually needed was a bit of strategic guidance, and someone who could get their hands dirty. They needed to build the foundations - then hire a CMO.

Hiring a CMO to do execution work is expensive and usually ends badly. 

Most CMOs haven't done hands-on campaign work in years. And the ones who have? They're not looking for that kind of role.

The second startup had a marketing team of three. Good people, doing good work. The founder wanted a CMO to "take it to the next level."

But when I asked what success looked like in 12 months, the answer was vague. More pipeline. Better brand awareness. Faster growth.

That's not a CMO brief. That's a wish list.

Here's the bit most founders miss.

A full-time CMO doesn't just cost £150-200k in salary. They need resource to make the role work—team, budget, tools. Senior leadership without adequate support is just expensive frustration for everyone.

For both these startups, that kind of pressure on the P&L didn't make sense yet.

CMOs aren't magic.

They can't fix unclear positioning, under-resourced teams, or founders who haven't figured out what marketing needs to deliver.

What they can do - when the conditions are right - is take a marketing function that's working and scale it. Build a team. Own the strategy. Connect marketing to revenue in ways that drive real growth.

But you need the foundations first. And you need the budget to support them properly.

My advice to both?

Start with a fractional CMO - a part-time thinker - and a Marketing Manager - a full-time doer.

For half the price of a permanent CMO, you get the best of both worlds. 

Strategic direction without the full P&L commitment. Hands-on execution that actually moves things forward.

Then, when you've got clarity on what's working and the revenue to support a proper marketing function, hire the CMO.

One is now exploring the fractional route. The other is hiring a Marketing Manager and keeping the strategy in-house for now.

Neither decision is as exciting as landing a CMO. But both are considerably more likely to work.

Sometimes the best hire is the one you don't make yet.

AI can write user stories. It can't decide which stories are worth writing.

That distinction matters more than most product managers realize right now.

The tools are genuinely impressive. AI can draft documentation, synthesize research, analyze usage data, and generate communications faster than any PM could. Teams that aren't using it for these tasks are leaving real productivity on the table.

But I keep seeing a version of this that worries me. PMs who are using AI to move faster without asking whether they're moving in the right direction. The execution speed goes up. The judgment stays the same.

What AI is commoditizing is the craft of producing output: writing, summarizing, structuring. What it can't replace is the judgment required to decide what's worth producing in the first place. Which customer problem actually matters. Which stakeholder needs to be convinced and how. Which tradeoff to make when the data points in two directions.

Those are the skills that separate product managers who are managing a backlog from product managers who are shaping a business.

The best PMs I know are using AI to get the documentation done faster so they can spend more time in customer conversations. They're using it to clear the analytical work so they can focus on the strategic call. They're treating it as a way to reclaim time for the parts of the job that require a human in the room.

The craft of product management isn't disappearing. It's being sorted. AI is raising the floor for everyone and simultaneously raising the ceiling for people who know where to invest the time it frees up.

The question worth sitting with: when AI handles more of your execution work, what will you do with the time?

Agentic software engineering adoption is on fire at Uber: 1,800 code changes per week are now written entirely by Uber's internal background coding agent, and 95% of our engineers now use AI every month across all the tools we track. 

This is a real reset moment for engineering; it's one of the most exciting times to lead. This shift requires builders to be curious and hands-on. I’m incredibly lucky to be surrounded by a team that’s doing exactly that.

The best part is that the strongest adoption isn’t being pushed top down from leadership announcements; it’s coming from engineers who are quietly experimenting, quietly shipping, and quietly pushing things forward. 

I love spending time with those engineers because there’s no substitute for being close to the work.

Over the last few months, we leaned in hard, and the results have been phenomenal.

The bigger shift: going agentic.

84% of AI users are now working with agent-style workflows, not just tab completion. Claude Code usage nearly doubled in 2 months (32% → 63%), while IDE-based tools have largely plateaued.

Engineers are moving from accepting suggestions to delegating tasks. Even within traditional IDEs, ~70% of committed code is now AI-generated.

Background agents are writing code autonomously.

Our internal background coding agent went from <1% of all code changes to 8% in just a few months. There is zero human authoring. Engineers review and approve, but the code is written entirely by AI agents.

The role of the engineer is shifting - from writing every line to architecting systems and reviewing AI-generated code.

The first time you see an engineer build something in 45 minutes that would have taken a week a year ago, but then see it not ship for another 6 weeks, you will be radicalized.

AI has dramatically accelerated the velocity of coding but for most non-startups that isn’t translating into a higher velocity of shipping products. The gap between those two things is where organizations are bleeding the majority of their AI advantage, primarily because leaders are asking their employees to change their working methods (which is good!) but are often intransigent when it comes to changing their own working methods ("We need this giant 50 slide deck update every week otherwise we don't know what's going on." "We need this 12-step review process to protect the brand." etc…)

In most mature product organizations, the journey from "code complete" to "in users' hands" passes through a gauntlet of reviews:
Strategy, Product, Design, Engineering, Privacy, Legal, Accessibility, Comms
Experimentation setup and approval
Experimentation results review

Each of these exists with good reason (most were created in response to real incidents where something went wrong). And the answer is certainly not "kill them all." But they were designed for a world where writing the code was the bottleneck, so we needed to make sure to get it right the first time and not waste coding time. That world is gone.

When code for some features takes only hours to write, your old review cycle is now 95% of your timeline. The critical path has changed so the calendar hasn’t.

You need to confront three things:
1. Your process architecture is now your speed limit. The scaffolding around shipping (approvals, reviews, stakeholder loops) is what determines how fast you actually move. You have a F1 car going down a street with stop signs every 100 feet and wondering why it’s not faster.

2. Too many orgs treat every process as sacred. Some reviews are incredibly important. But does HOW you are doing this review justify the current cost (and is your current cost accurate)? The way you did reviews at 10 deploys per month probably doesn’t makes sense at 100 deploys per month.

3. AI should be eating process, not just coding. Have the AI traverse your codebase and collect your evidence for the privacy review and have another AI grade the privacy review. They go back and forth until (a) they need human intervention because they are stuck or (b) they need humans to look at the final results and sign off on the right path forward. It can pre-populate your comms review. It can flag accessibility issues before a human ever looks. Changing many of these to human in the loop will save you literal weeks.

Change needs to come at *all* levels of the org, otherwise there is no change.

At the risk of sounding like a broken record, I'll repeat what I've said before. “Vibe coding” is a dangerous fantasy for code that ships to production.

Prompting an AI and hoping the generated code works isn’t engineering; it's spray and pray. Real systems have reliability, security, and long-term maintenance constraints.

The responsible path is agentic engineering: structured workflows where AI agents explore codebases, propose implementations, generate tests, and run evaluations.

Engineers still design the architecture and control what ships.

The long-term winners in the AI era won’t vibe code. They’ll orchestrate agents to build real systems. 🚀

I've been thinking hard about the future of programming — and I believe we are on the verge of a more fundamental shift than most people realize. Not just better tools or faster code generation, but a change in what programming is.

To see where we're going, it helps to understand where we've been. And that story starts with a fork in the road that the industry took ninety years ago — and never looked back from.

In the 1930s, two giants laid the foundations of computing. Alan Turing gave us the Turing machine — built around how computation proceeds, step by mechanical step. Alonzo Church gave us lambda calculus — built around what a computation is, expressed as precise mathematical functions.

Here's the key insight: to specify a function in Church's sense, you are forced to precisely characterize inputs, outputs, and the relationship between them. Church's solution spec is a problem spec. Turing's approach carries no such obligation — you can write operations without ever articulating what problem you're solving.

Guess which approach won?

Turing machines → assembly → C → C++ → Java → Python. Each generation raised the abstraction of implementation. We got better at describing solutions. We never crossed over to rigorously describing problems.

The result: poorly designed systems, unnecessary complexity, and a mountain of tech debt.

Now here's why everything is about to change.

Formal methods — TLA+, Coq, Z notation — tried to fix this for decades and never went mainstream, because someone still had to bridge the gap between a formal spec and working code.

AI removes that barrier.

AI can serve as the compiler between a precise problem specification and working implementation — exactly as high-level compilers once spared us from writing assembly. The what becomes the human's job. The how becomes the machine's.

The next generation of programming won't be about writing better code. It will be about writing better problem specifications. And I believe we will soon converge on a formal language purpose-built for exactly that — a structured, precise language for describing what you want solved, which AI then compiles into working implementation. This problem spec language will be the new programming language and the problem specs in this language will be the new source code.

If you are technically inclined, here is what I think the formal spec language might end up looking like and how we would work with this language: https://lnkd.in/gk6BHZ5k

Until that language emerges, what should you do? Get obsessed with specifying problems clearly — using natural language, typed schemas, mathematical notation, whatever you have. The more structure you bring to your problem spec today, the more leverage you get from AI now.

The future of programming is not less rigorous. It is more rigorously focused on the right thing.

If you blame the market, you probably built the wrong thing. Yes, timing matters. A deeper issue: you didn’t listen, you didn’t test, you built what you wanted, not what users needed. You fell in love with the idea, not the problem or the user! 

I’ve seen it over and over. A beautiful deck. Big vision. No traction. 

I think back to when we were building the iPhone. There was pressure and internal pushback on not having a physical keyboard. Blackberry was king at the time. And a phone without buttons…insane people said. We focused on users. Typing on those tiny plastic keys was slow, frustrating, limiting. So, we bet everything on a touchscreen. We didn’t follow the market. We followed the pain. We doubled down on actual user pain and future potential. That changed the industry! You don’t get breakthroughs by following the market. You get them by getting closer to the pain!

It might be denial, but almost no one in sales is talking about how AI is changing how buyers buy. Which is wild, because it’s already showing up in deals. Here are 4 ways every AE will feel it in 2026 (and how to adapt, now):

1. MISSING PREP WORK IS GOING TO COST YOU DEALS LIKE NEVER BEFORE.

By the time a buyer meets you, they’ve:

- ChatGPT’d the problem
- asked it for competitor research
- and what questions to ask you

So “Tell me about your challenges…” will feel tiring.

Match their level of research like the deal depends on it. Either you show up with a point of view and their org mapped... or you lose to the seller who did.

—

2. KNOWLEDGE IS COMMODITIZED. REPS BECOME EXPERT ADVISORS.

AI already gives buyers decent advice and research. Soon, it’ll be very good advice. That means the average rep won’t feel special anymore. Not because they’re bad. But because “good enough” advice and research are now "free."

So what’s left for us humans? The hard stuff:

- reading political risk
- spotting stakeholder misalignment
- surfacing uncomfortable tradeoffs
- predicting where decisions actually break

Sales becomes judgment, not knowledge.

—

3. TRANSACTIONAL SELLING WILL FADE AWAY

If your deal sounds like: “Here’s the product.” “Here’s the price.” “Ready to move forward?” AI will do better for buyers. And be cheaper for vendors. And it’s good news: Transactional sales was never great. Tech is finally good enough to replace it.

What to do instead? Reskill from selling to enabling buying:

- Have advisor-level conversations
- Agree on next steps that help both sides
- Enable champions and multithread early
- Make sense of the info buyers already have
- Help buyers run internal steps (business case, security, execs)

—

4. THERE WILL BE FEWER CALLS. DEALS WILL BE WON BETWEEN THEM.

The deep buying work is moving out of meetings. It happens when buyers are alone:

- comparing vendors with AI
- building the business case
- syncing internally to align stakeholders
- answering hard questions (finance, security, execs)

AI makes all of this easier for buyers. So if your process depends on more meetings to “stay in control”… buyers will slip away.

What to do instead? Optimize for designing the decision when you’re not there:

- guide them on critical steps they should run without you
- equip champions with copy-paste narratives (not decks)
- anticipate the internal objections before they show up
- create a space for easier, faster buying alignment (e.g. deal room)

In the AI era, calls are just checkpoints.

Momentum happens between them.

——

AI won’t replace sales.

It will replace “good enough.”

Help buyers make a decision.

You’ll be fine.

MEDDIC didn’t die in 2025, it just got exposed.

Not because it stopped working.

Because too many sellers stopped thinking.

For years, sales teams worshipped acronyms like they were strategy.

If you can recite MEDDIC, BANT, SPIN… 

Congrats you passed Sales Kindergarten.

But buyers evolved.

Tech exploded.

Speed doubled.

Expectations shifted.

And a lot of sellers didn’t. 

Or at least their silly old sales leadership didn't.

Buyers care that you know them.

Sales cycles aren’t linear anymore, they’re chaos in motion.

AI made “ask 20 qualifying questions” look amateur.

Control-based selling lost to collaborative selling.

Memorization got replaced by mastery.

In 2026, your reps won't be winning because they're box-checkers.

They’re problem-diagnosticians.

They show up already knowing.

They co-design outcomes.

They earn trust through insight, not interrogation.

MEDDIC didn't fail.

YOU did.

When you treated a framework like a substitute for curiosity, intelligence, and preparation.

The new game isn’t about acronyms.

It’s about understanding the problem better than the buyer… and proving it.

Acronyms don’t close deals.

Depth does.

P.S. - I now worry that AI has become a substitute for curiosity and intelligence.

If your AI startup can’t bill model costs cleanly, you have a leak.

Stripe just previewed something very on-the-nose: tooling for AI companies to track, pass through, and even profit on underlying model fees.

That sounds like boring payments plumbing. It isn’t.

It’s a recognition that for a huge chunk of “AI apps,” the real business problem isn’t prompts. It’s gross margin.

When your biggest cost line item is a third-party model, you’re selling on rented land.

Your customer asks for a simple thing: “What am I paying for?”

And your finance brain asks a nastier thing: “Am I subsidizing usage without noticing?”

Stripe is basically saying: we can make that legible. And billable.

But here’s the part founders should care about:

The pricing trick becomes a commodity.

Once you can pass costs through cleanly, you stop hiding behind “AI is expensive” and you start competing like a normal software business.

With transparency and clear value engineering your differentiation can be significant lower cost and overhead on LLM API calls.

Everyone will be able to expose, allocate, and markup model fees.

So the power shifts back to what you actually own and value you add on top of LLMs when it comes to cost, efficiency, and quality.

Lesson:

If your margins depend on a spreadsheet and vibes, you’re one usage spike away from a down round.

If your pricing depends on a black box, procurement will rip you apart. Eventually.

And if your product is “model output,” your customer will eventually ask why they can’t just buy the model access themselves.

Do this this week:

- Instrument model spend per customer and per feature.

- Decide what is pass-through vs bundled vs. marked-up and why. Put it in writing, in the invoice.

What part of your product still works if your model cost goes to zero and your competitor can bill pass-through perfectly?

What part of your AI company is still defensible when "billing the model" becomes a solved problem?

Sales is the most mentally demanding job in any company.

I've lived it. Ten years in. And some days still hit different.

Targets shift mid-quarter. You adjust. Then they shift again. Rejection comes in waves. Not once. Not twice. Every single day. The pressure? It never clocks out.

Here's what nobody tells you when you sign up:

• Your wins reset to zero every month
• Your confidence gets tested constantly
• Your mental game matters more than your pitch

I've watched top performers burn out. Seen hungry reps lose their fire. The job doesn't just test your skills. It tests who you are.

But here's the thing. The same pressure that breaks some people builds others.

The key? Stop fighting the chaos. Learn to move with it.

• Accept that uncertainty is part of the deal
• Build routines that protect your energy
• Find people who get it

Sales isn't for everyone. And that's okay.

For those of us still in the arena — respect. We chose the hard path. 💪

What's your strategy for staying mentally sharp in sales?

If I wanted a VP of Marketing job right now, I’d approach the process differently than most.

I wouldn’t spend time proving I’ve done it before. I’d focus on showing I can do it for them now.

That’s the shift most senior marketers miss. They talk in the past tense. “I built,” “I led,” “I scaled” None of that matters if you can’t connect it to how you’ll move their business forward today.

When I think about interviews at that level, these are my non-negotiables.

⸻

1. Partnership over politics
I ask how the CEO wants to be challenged.
If they don’t, I’m out.
I’m not signing up to take orders. I’m looking to build a partnership where tension creates better ideas, not fear.

⸻

2. Data and judgment, not just dashboards
I ask how they define “data-driven”
If the answer is charts, not context, it’s a red flag.
The best marketing leaders know numbers only matter if they inform judgment. That’s the skill that compounds.

⸻

3. Brand isn’t fluff
I ask how brand shows up in board conversations.
If it doesn’t, marketing’s already boxed in.
You can’t lead demand strategy if brand is treated like design work.

⸻

4. Patience for compounding
I ask about their appetite for long-term marketing bets.
Most companies say they want growth, but what they really want is relief.
Relief from pressure. Relief from board expectations.
Compounding only works when you stop chasing quick wins and start building enduring ones.

⸻

5. Shared accountability
I ask how marketing, sales, and finance align on goals.
If I hear, “We have weekly syncs,” I push further.
Alignment isn’t meetings. It’s shared math.
If the math isn’t shared, the incentives aren’t either.

⸻

When you’ve been in this game long enough, you start to see interviews less like a test and more like a mirror.

You’re not trying to impress. You’re trying to understand what you’d be walking into.

Can you lead? Can you challenge? Can you build something that compounds?

Those are the questions that matter.

Everything else is just a jargon on a resume.

The most important word I've learned as a Head of Marketing isn't a framework or a methodology.

It's no.

And it’s hard to say when Product needs a launch campaign or sales needs a new vertical. And the board wants to see more activity in a market you haven't touched yet.

But your team is already at capacity.

The instinct is to find a way to do it all. 

That instinct will kill your marketing strategy faster than any competitor will.

Here's what I've learned.

Every activity you say yes to that’s outside your strategy is a tax on everything else you're running.

And that’s a problem because you’re strategy aligns your activities to the growth forecast.

The truth is this: the best marketing comes out of a roadmap built around three priorities.

Not ten. Not six. Three.

The way to get buy-in is to say no with context.

“If we do that we need to cut that campaign that is core to Q3 targets. Have the priorities changed since we agreed the growth forecast?”

That's protecting growth - and the business.

The hardest part isn't saying no.

It's building enough trust with the business that when you say no, they believe you're doing it for the right reasons.

That trust takes time. 

But it starts with the first no you say clearly, calmly, and with evidence behind it.

What's the hardest no you've had to say as a marketing leader?

Most companies say they want strategic marketing leaders.
What they actually want is someone who will execute faster.

Strategy sounds good in theory.
In practice, strategy does uncomfortable things:
 • It says no to projects that feel exciting.
 • It forces trade-offs between good ideas.
 • It exposes when the real constraint isn’t marketing at all.

Execution is easier.
You can launch campaigns.
 Ship content.
 Run ads.
 Build dashboards.

Everyone stays busy.

But strategy asks a harder question: “What actually needs to change for growth to happen?”

And sometimes the answer isn’t more marketing.

The future of sales is going to destroy a lot of careers much faster than you believe, and arguing with those who believe it won't make it different.

Not because AI is magic. Because the work was never that hard and companies only created those roles because they didn't want all reps to prospect.

The reps who survive won't be the best at sequences or cadences or tools.

They'll simply be the ones who can walk into a room, command trust immediately, and close deals without a script.

Relationships. Presence. Credibility.

You can't automate that [yet]. Start building it before the floor drops out.

Head of Sales: I want to cut the sales team from 12 reps to 6.
CEO: We just spent $800K hiring those people.. Explain.
Head of Sales: And we're about to spend another $800K carrying people who won't hit quota.

CEO: Everyone deserves a ramp period.

Head of Sales: They've been here 9 months. 4 of them haven't closed a single deal. 2 more are at 30% attainment.

CEO: So coach them.

Head of Sales: I have been. For 6 months. You can teach someone to sell better. You can't teach them to care.

CEO: What happens to their pipeline?

Head of Sales: That's the part nobody wants to hear. Their "pipeline" is 200+ deals at 10% probability that they report to make dashboards look full. It's fiction.

CEO: But 6 reps can't cover our territory.

Head of Sales: They can if they're not spending 3 hours a day in internal meetings, pipeline reviews, and forecast calls for deals that were never real.

CEO: The investors want headcount growth.

Head of Sales: The investors want revenue growth. Those are different things. Last year we grew headcount 40% and revenue 12%. That's a failure nobody's talking about.

CEO: What's the plan with the savings?

Head of Sales: $400K back to the 6 who stay as accelerators and better tooling. The rest goes to marketing to feed them pipeline they can actually close. Right now our top 6 are starving while the bottom 6 waste what they get.

Finally, someone said it.

The President of Y Combinator just called MCP out.

He got so frustrated with context window bloat and auth headaches that he:
- vibe coded a Playwright CLI wrapper in 30 minutes.
- 100 lines of code.
- worked 100x better than the MCP version.

And it's not just Garry Tan.

Perplexity just launched Computer, their flagship agent product.

They looked at how power users were duct-taping MCP servers together and decided to skip the protocol entirely.

When one of the biggest AI companies ships their most ambitious product without MCP, that's a market signal.

Here's what a year of MCP in production has actually revealed:

1/ A typical MCP server dumps 13,000 to 18,000 tokens of tool metadata into context.

2/ The equivalent CLI approach does the same job in 225 tokens. That's 80x overhead.

3/ Your agent isn't slow because the model is bad.

4/ It's drowning in protocol bloat before it even starts working.

5/ 78% of MCP implementations have no proper authorization.

6/ Someone analyzed 1,400 public MCP servers. Most are thin wrappers around CLI tools and APIs that agents could already call directly.

MCP as a concept is sound.

A standard way for AI to communicate with tools is genuinely useful. Maybe even necessary.

But what the ecosystem built on top of it wasn't a standard.

Every API got its own MCP server. 
Every server reinvented auth from scratch. 
Every deployment was a standalone silo with zero shared knowledge about how integrations actually behave when real users hit them.

MCP isn't dying.

But the idea that you can wrap every API in its own server and call it an integration strategy is dead.

$300 million in venture capital went into startups trying to kill PowerPoint. Almost all of them are dead.

Nine-figure raises, massive launches, millions of users, and then silence.

The common explanation: they lost to Microsoft. The real explanation involves a 1990 experiment with coffee mugs.

Nobel Prize-winning psychologist Daniel Kahneman gave people mugs, then asked how much they'd sell them for. The answer was 2x what buyers would pay for the same mug.

We overvalue what we already have. By a lot.

Harvard professor John Gourville turned this into a framework:

Consumers overvalue their current tools by 3x. Founders overvalue what they've built by 3x. Multiply those and you get a 9-to-1 mismatch between what founders think customers want and what customers will actually switch for.

I think about that mismatch constantly. Every person you need to convert is weighing one question: "Is this really worth changing how I work?"

Prettier slides didn't clear that bar. Smarter templates didn't either. A 2x improvement against a 9x gap still lost to PowerPoint.

What clears it is rethinking the question:

Not why do slides need better design, but why do they need to be designed by hand at all? Not how do I find the right template, but what if the starting point isn't a blank page?

That reframe guided everything we built at Gamma. And I'd bet it applies to whatever you're building too.

What would you build if you stopped improving what already exists?

Everyone gets ICP wrong.

What people think ICP stands for is 'Ideal Customer Profile.'

But here's the problem:

Most companies define it like this:

→ 200+ employees
→ Technology industry
→ Series B or later
→ VP of Sales is the buyer

That's not an ICP.

That's demographics and firmographics.

I want you to think about ICP differently.

ICP = Ideal Customer PROBLEMS.

Your real ICP isn't a company size or an industry.

It's the customers who have the specific problems you solve.

I was recently speaking at a conference with 150 CEOs in the room. I asked them: "What problems do you solve?"

Four or five of them answered.

Every. Single. One. talked about benefits. Not problems.

"We help companies scale faster."
"We improve operational efficiency."
"We drive revenue growth."

Those aren't problems. Those are outcomes.

Problems sound like:

"Our reps are wasting 3 hours a day on manual data entry."
"We're losing deals because our follow-up takes 5 days."
"Our managers have no visibility into pipeline until it's too late."

THAT'S the level of specificity you need.

Here's the truth:

There are plenty of 200-person tech companies that don't have the problems you solve.

And there are 50-person companies outside your "ICP" that are DESPERATE for what you do.

Firmographics are just prerequisites. They increase the likelihood of the problem existing.

But the problem is the actual qualifier.

When you take a problem-based approach:

→ Your prospecting gets sharper
→ Your messaging gets clearer
→ Your discovery gets deeper
→ Your win rates go up

Stop defining ICP by company size.

Start defining it by customer problems.

This will change how you target, who you target, how you message and most importantly how quickly you can close.

I completed a demo request form last week and quickly got a call from a SDR. 

They asked me a bunch of questions, clearly based on BANT, and 10 minutes later booked me in with their AE. 

No value provided, no excitement built for the next call. 

I joined the call with the AE. What’s the first question they ask me?

“I’d love to hear more about your business and the specific issues you’re looking to solve with our solution.”

What? Really? 

❌ No mention of the call with their SDR and all the info I gave them.

❌ No summary of what they already knew about my business. 

❌ No proposed agenda for the meeting to align on. 

❌ No way I’m doing business with them. 

Either the SDR didn’t hand over the notes from our call or the AE didn’t bother to read them.

Either way, what an awful first impression. 

When working with an inbound prospect, consider doing these 5 things instead:

1️⃣ Train your SDRs to have a business conversation, not a qualification interrogation. It’s fine to qualify me, but do it subtly by asking questions about my business with genuine curiosity. Also, find a way to add some value in some way. 

2️⃣ Sell the next meeting. Increase my chances of showing up for the AE by selling the next step. What's the benefit to me of attending? Get me excited about the meeting in some way. 

3️⃣ Ensure a quality handover to the AE. I shouldn’t be asked the same questions twice. What a waste of everyone’s time. AEs should summarise the notes from the SDR and confirm everything is accurate. Then probe further. 

4️⃣ Ensure AEs align with the prospect on the agenda. At the top of the call, tell me how we’re going to use the next 30 mins, focussing on what value I will get by the end of the meeting. 

5️⃣ Consider sending inbound prospects straight to an AE. I know, I know, we don’t want to waste an AEs time on unqualified opps, but these are the hottest leads in your business! If inbound, they’re likely talking to your competitors too so let’s move fast to get them to someone who can add value, quickly, and skip the SDR step. 

We often talk about the cost of sales & marketing misalignment. But what about the cost of SDR & AE misalignment?

The Pepsi Super Bowl ad as a Rorschach test on how you think advertising works

I have watched the reactions to the Pepsi Max Super Bowl ad with the Polar bears with interest. I think this ad works nicely like a Rorschach test to reveal how you really think advertising works.

Love the ad?
If you love it, admire it, think it will work, then you likely see advertising as a persuasive force. Persuasive ads require you to provide compelling information to change the viewer’s mind so that they choose your brand. In this case we have a clever emotional appeal - if a polar bear that has represented Coke for so long really prefers Pepsi Max over Coke Zero, might you as well? 

Now the ad can’t work unless either the polar bear or the can of Coke Zero evokes the Coca-cola brand for the viewer, and that they understand why a polar bear might be embarrassed to prefer Pepsi Max in a blind taste test. Otherwise the creative might just raise questions - like why is a polar bear seeing a therapist over a drink?

Not so sure about the ad?
If the ad made you uncomfortable or you have doubts then you likely see advertising through a mental availability lens. It has many brand references to Pepsi, but its the references to Coke that have you worried. You might think, could reminding people who know enough about Coca-Cola to know that a Polar bear is one of the brand’s Distinctive Assets about Coke/Coke Zero be a risky tactic? Could it neutralise all the good work to bring Pepsi Max to the viewer? 

Advertising that works through building mental availability is not going to waste money evoking a competitor brand, as there are many messages a brand can advertise that don’t need the presence of a competitor brand to work. The act of retrieving a brand makes it stronger in memory and more likely to be retrieved in the future. Now you might hope that if Coke Zero is more mentally available, attached to it is this doubt as to whether it tastes as good as Pepsi Max, but that new memory will typically need to compete with years of brand knowledge building. So it had better be a strong one…..

Ah I hear you say, the strong presence of Pepsi means that it will work as a mental availability ad for those who don’t notice the Coke can or that don’t trigger Coke when they see Polar Bears. But that’s like saying you hope the creative doesn’t work.

Finally, maybe I am a bit weird but if you ask me who wins the cola wars here, it would be Coldplay…..

If I could communicate one thought for the week it would be:  
Distinctive is as Distinctive Does.  

I have been thinking about this for a while, and I don't know if I have quite landed my thoughts on paper yet, but here goes (and at least you know AI did not write this!)

No brand is inherently distinctive, no asset is inherently strong.  Both depend on where and how they are deployed.  Having strong Distinctive Assets is useful in advertising, but you still have to use them effectively.  So let's stop talking about one brand is more distinctive than another like it is a medal to award.  One brand might have stronger Distinctive Assets than another but that only matters when they use them well.

Also no Distinctive Asset is the best choice all the time.  A logo is hard to use when advertising on a podcast, while a jingle is hard to use when advertising on a train. Let's also stop trying to give assets prizes for being the best and move the conversation forward to about whether the asset was effectively deployed such that it helped achieve long term branding goals.  

Any asset ranking study has very little value when deciding which Distinctive Assets your brand should build.  At best they can give you some insight into the challenges of building some types of assets - for example we know color is one of the most mentally competitive assets and this has implications for those who want to build a colour asset.  But such a study should not determine if you build a color asset or not, there are much more important factors to consider.

I keep seeing this no PRD (Product Requirements Document) trend everywhere, and honestly? I'm not buying it.

Anthropic's Claude Code team is building dozens of prototypes instead of writing specs. Figma says "prototypes are the new PRDs." Everyon is racing to move faster, iterate quicker, ship sooner.

I get the appeal. But I have gone through a similar process. Here's what nobody talks about: six months later, someone asks "why did we build it this way?" The person who made that call is gone. The prototype that was supposed to be temporary? It's now production code. And nobody remembers why.

La Fontaine had it right with the Hare and the Tortoise. The hare lost not because it was slower, but because rushing without thought leads to mistakes.

Think about AWS. In 2003, Andy Jassy's team did not prototype Amazon’s way into cloud computing. They wrote it down. That written proposal became the foundation for the world's largest cloud platform.

I am not against prototyping and vibe code myself: fast feedback loops matter. But when we skip documentation entirely, we are saying "the people here right now are the only ones who will ever need to understand this." That's never true.

The best teams do both. They prototype to learn fast, but they write down the why. Are PRDs dead, or are we just forgetting why they existed in the first place?

99% of the best product managers I’ve met have one thing in common:

Here is what they do differently:

They talk to users now instead of relying on assumptions

They kill ideas quickly instead of protecting them
They do not fall in love with features. They fall in love with outcomes.

It is to shorten your learning loops, get feedback early, and keep iterating.

Enterprise Sales is a different beast. You’re thinking about it all wrong. The difference between a $50K and a $500K deal is NOT fancy Negotiation/Disco skills. You need to learn BUSINESS ACUMEN like a VP. I’ve worked 100s of 6-7 fig deals. Here are the 5 hardest lessons I wish I knew before going upmarket:

1. AEs Don’t Close Deals – They Rally The Troops.

Lone wolves don't close 7-fig deals. Enterprise AEs are like film directors – connecting champions, execs, and influencers across both companies, so the deal feels inevitable. It’s never about one hero; it's about orchestrating every player: CEO who shares the vision, VP Product who tackles tough questions, Exec Sponsor who secures buy-in. High-stakes deals demand the best your company can offer. Great AEs know how to get it.

2. Complex Sales = World Class Project Management.

In enterprise deals, you’re more PM than a seller. Big deals die in the details: missed tasks, unaligned stakeholders, and endless email threads. New people jump in mid-cycle, each needing context. Your job: bring order to chaos. Protect momentum, keep everyone aligned, and ensure nothing slips. Top AEs co-create timelines, organize materials in Deal Rooms, and tailor every detail.

3. AEs Master Buying (not Selling).

My biggest breakthroughs came not from sales training but from buying software and interviewing CXOs. That’s when I realized: If you understand how budgets, approvals, and internal priorities work, you don't need sales tactics. Empathy becomes your superpower because you know what each stakeholder needs (financially and politically) to say YES. Want to excel at enterprise? Study how companies justify ROI, CFOs think, and champions navigate approvals.

4. There’s No Sales Process – Only a Buying Process.

Your buyer doesn’t care if you’ve hit Stage 3 in your CRM. They care about their own maze of priorities, budgets, and internal politics. Top AEs ‘dance’ around the sales stages. They choreograph moves based on what the deal needs next – like looping in a board member to champion them behind the scenes or going after end-users to outshine a competitor who started at the top.

5. AEs Think Transformation, Not Pain Points.

Execs won’t write $1M checks to fix a clunky spreadsheet workflow. They need to see a solution driving company-wide impact – like a strategic pivot or entering a new market. If you’re only uncovering small headaches, expect a small deal. But connect those symptoms to a transformation – and the CFO listens.

——

Enterprise sellers think and act like business leaders.

Not salespeople who want to close deals.

Yes, they know the fancy sales tactics.

But that's not the point…

When buyers see you think like them.

When you work a deal like it’s their internal project.

Not every VP of Sales is CRO material.

I see founders make this mistake constantly - they promote their best sales leader into a CRO role and wonder why it doesn't work. Because a VP of Sales owns sales. A CRO owns the entire revenue engine. They're fundamentally different jobs.

Having served as a (3X) CRO and GTM advisor to numerous startups, here's what I believe a CRO actually does:

A CRO doesn't manage a sales team. They design, build and lead the system that makes revenue predictable.

That means connecting marketing, sales, customer success, and partnerships into one cohesive motion. It means owning the full customer journey - not just the sales stage. It means designing the go-to-market playbook, not just executing it.

When growth stalls, a CRO doesn't just push the team harder. They step back and ask whether the GTM motion itself still works.. whether the ICP has shifted, whether the channel mix needs rethinking, whether the sales motion needs to evolve. Then they build the new strategy and drive it across functions.

When deals aren't converting the way they should, a CRO doesn't point at one function. They analyze the system holistically - from lead quality to sales process to handoff to onboarding, find the areas that need optimization, and build a plan to fix each one.

When the board asks for a forecast, a CRO doesn't just roll up rep estimates. They deliver a system-level view of how revenue is built, where the risks are, and what levers are available.

It's orchestration. Not management.

So when should a founder actually hire a CRO?

Mark Roberge lays out a framework in his book - The Science of Scaling that I think about often. He describes three phases every B2B company goes through: product-market fit, go-to-market fit, and moat. Each phase has different demands and different leadership needs.

In my experience, a CRO becomes essential in the go-to-market fit phase. That's where a company needs to take what's working in pockets and turn it into a repeatable, scalable engine with strong unit economics. A CRO can help find go-to-market fit, and then design and execute the system that sustains it. They connect the dots between functions that have been growing independently. They build the system before you need to pour fuel on it.

Hire too early before you've proven product-market fit, and you'll overpay for a skill set you can't yet leverage. Hire too late after your functions have already grown in silos and the CRO spends their first year untangling instead of building.

A few signals it might be time:

– Revenue is growing but feels fragile and hard to predict
– Sales, marketing, and CS are operating without a shared plan
– The CEO is still the one connecting the dots between functions
– You've proven PMF and are ready to build a repeatable go-to-market motion

Get the role right before you fill it. A misaligned CRO hire doesn't just cost you a salary, it can cost you a year.

Salesforce laid off 4,000 people last year.

I keep hearing the same pattern from companies right now:
"AI will handle this." "We'll offshore the rest." "We can do more with less." "We don't need that RevOps or Systems role anymore."

Then six months later:
"Why is our Salesforce instance a mess?" "Why are deals getting stuck in the pipeline?" "Why does every process take three times longer than it should?"

Here's what happened: They cut deep in systems, RevOps, and GTM support thinking AI plus a cheap offshore team would fill the gap.

It didn't.

The work didn't disappear. It just turned into:
- Manual workarounds and constant context-switching · Rework and firefighting across time zones 
- Lost context and tribal knowledge 
- Sales reps screaming about broken workflows 
- Offshore teams waiting on answers because no one onshore actually owns the system anymore

I'm not anti-AI or anti-offshoring. Both have their place. But here's the thing: AI doesn't replace the person who understands your tech stack, your data architecture, and how your revenue engine actually works. And offshore teams can't fill that gap if there's no one left who owns the context to delegate to them.

You can't offshore institutional knowledge. You can't prompt-engineer your way out of bad system design.

Companies are quietly realizing: "Holy crap, we might've gone too far."

That's when they start calling me. Not because they're suddenly growing like crazy. Because the business is choking on its own decisions. The AI isn't delivering magic. The offshore team is stuck. And the person who actually knew how everything fit together? Gone.

If your company gutted operations thinking AI and offshore would be the answer, and now everything feels harder, you're not alone. And yeah, those roles are coming back.

The question is: did you lose the people who actually knew how to run your systems?

Because that's a lot harder to replace than a headcount number.

“Salespeople are liars” is a lazy opinion people use when they’ve never sat on a real sales call.

Yes, bad salespeople lie.
But serious closers don’t survive on lies - they survive on alignment.

If you think sales is about manipulation, spend one week as an AE and listen to what actually happens on calls:

• “We’re very interested” → no reply for 3 weeks.
• “Budget is approved” → suddenly next quarter.
• “Send proposal” → ghost.
• “We’re deciding internally” → they already chose a competitor.

The truth:
Both sides protect their position.

Prospects hide risk.
Sales reps hide urgency.
Procurement hides budget.
Founders hide doubt.

It’s not a morality battle. It’s information asymmetry.

And this is where amateurs lie — because they don’t know how to handle uncertainty.

Professionals don’t lie. They clarify.

They ask:
– What happens if you don’t fix this?
– Who else is involved in this decision?
– What does “soon” actually mean on your side?
– If budget wasn’t an issue, would this move forward?

Real sales is not about convincing someone to buy something useless.

It’s about:

- Finding real pain
- Testing seriousness
- Removing risk
- Walking away when there’s no fit

The fastest way to kill your career in sales is to lie.

Because this game is long. Reputation compounds.

The best reps I’ve seen don’t oversell.
They disqualify fast.
They speak clearly.
They document everything.
They follow up with proof - not pressure.

If someone still thinks all salespeople are liars, here’s the uncomfortable reality:

When trust is low, everyone looks dishonest.

Sales doesn’t create distrust.
It exposes it.

And if you want to win long-term in outbound, cold calling, or enterprise deals, your edge isn’t scripts.

It’s credibility.

Build that, and you won’t need to lie.

If you’re in sales and especially doing cold calls...you already know this pattern. 

The first few attempts feel like you’re talking to a wall. 

You start questioning your tone, your script, your offer, even yourself. 

But here’s the uncomfortable truth: most prospects don’t decide on the first conversation. 

They decide after repeated exposure.

Silence does not mean rejection. It usually means you are not a priority yet. 

Your job is to stay present without being desperate.

“I’ll think about it” does not mean no. It often means they don’t have enough certainty. 

That certainty comes from repetition, clarity, and confidence over time.

And yes, you will mess up. You will say something awkward. You will lose control of a call. That doesn’t end the deal unless you disappear after it happens.

Consistency builds familiarity.
Familiarity builds trust.
Trust creates deals.

If you want to apply this properly in your outreach, do this:

Plan your follow-ups in advance. Don’t “wing it.” 

Decide that every qualified prospect gets at least 8–10 structured touchpoints.

Change the angle as you move forward. Don’t repeat the same pitch. 

Share a short insight, a result, a case example, or a sharp question that makes them think.

After “I’ll think about it,” ask directly what they need to feel confident. 

Timing? 
Budget? 
Risk? Bring the real objection to the surface.

Track progress by engagement shifts. 

Did they go from ignoring you to replying? From short replies to asking questions? That’s movement.

Big deals rarely come from one perfect moment. They come from controlled persistence.

The difference between average salespeople and high earners is not talent. 

It’s tolerance for discomfort.

The ninth call only happens if you survive the first eight.

The easiest way for a company to hide its problems is simple:

“Blame the sales team.”

Targets get missed…
Pipeline slows down…
Deals start slipping…

And suddenly every department becomes confident about one thing:

“Sales isn’t doing their job.”

But look closely at the picture.

The sales team is standing inside the boat, waist-deep in water, desperately trying to throw buckets out to keep the company afloat.

The rest of the company is sitting comfortably on the dry side saying,

“Good thing the hole isn’t on our side.”

This is the quiet reality inside many organizations.

Sales becomes the shock absorber for every mistake made upstream.

If the product solves a weak problem, sales must convince harder.
If pricing doesn’t match the value, sales must negotiate more.
If marketing attracts the wrong audience, sales must filter the noise.
If leadership misunderstands the market, sales must face the rejection.

And when things go wrong, the easiest narrative appears:

“Sales performance is the issue.”

But sales is the department that faces the market every single day.

They hear the hesitation in a prospect’s voice.
They hear when competitors are winning deals.
They hear when the product promise sounds exciting in a presentation but confusing in real conversations.

Sales doesn’t create the reality.

Sales exposes it.

Think about how a company actually reaches a customer:

Product builds the solution.
Marketing tells the story.
Leadership sets the direction.
Sales carries that entire story into the market.

If there is a crack anywhere in that chain, sales is the first place where the pressure shows up.

That is why struggling sales numbers often reveal something deeper:

* A positioning problem
* A product-market fit issue
* A pricing gap
* A weak ICP definition
* Messaging that sounds good internally but collapses in real conversations

Sales is simply where the market pushes back.

Great companies understand this.

They treat sales conversations as intelligence, not excuses.

They ask:
“What are prospects actually saying?”
“What objections repeat every week?”
“Where do deals break down?”

Because inside those conversations sits the truth about the business.

When sales starts drowning, the smartest companies don’t shout instructions from the dry side of the boat.

They grab a bucket and start fixing the hole.

Most pipelines look impressive.

Until you actually try to sell to them.

I saw this meme and laughed because every salesperson has experienced this moment.

Your manager says:

“You’ve got plenty of good leads in your pipeline… have you called them yet?”

And you’re sitting there thinking:

Those “good leads” are actually:

• People who downloaded one PDF 14 months ago
• Students researching a university assignment
• Someone who clicked a LinkedIn ad by accident
• A generic inbox like info@company.com
• Someone who has already bought a competitor

A full pipeline means nothing if the quality isn’t there.

In sales, activity matters.

But qualification matters more.

Good salespeople don’t just chase volume.

They focus on:

• real problems
• real budgets
• real decision makers
• real timing

Because 20 qualified opportunities will beat 200 random leads every single time.

A pipeline full of clowns still produces a circus.

Not revenue.

Curious to hear from other salespeople:

What’s the worst “lead” you’ve ever been told to call? 🎪

Most sales fail because we sell the wrong outcome to the wrong person.

Same product.
Same pitch.
Wrong language.

And the buyer switches off.

Ever walked out of a meeting thinking:

“They just didn’t get it.”

But actually… you didn’t get them.

Not because your product wasn’t good.
Because your message wasn’t landing where it needed to.

Here’s the filter I use before every deal 👇

If you’re selling to a:

CEO → Talk revenue
• Growth
• Market expansion
• Risk reduction
• Valuation
• Shareholder impact

Manager → Talk productivity
• Time saved
• Process improvement
• Team efficiency
• Reduced firefighting
• Clear reporting

Employee → Talk ease
• Less manual work
• Fewer errors
• Simpler workflows
• Faster tasks
• Less stress

Same product.
Three completely different narratives.

People don’t buy features.
They buy relief.

Do you think this theory is actually true — or is it oversimplified?

Pipeline vs forecast.
One looks good on paper.
The other looks like Justin Bieber in Crocs.

If you’ve worked in sales, you’ve felt this pain, whether you’re an SDR, AE, or VP.
The pipeline never matches the forecast.

But here’s the truth → forecasting will never be perfect.
The only thing you can control? How much clarity you create along the way.

Here’s what’s helped me close the gap:

1️⃣ Ask better questions early
– “What are your approval timelines?”
– “Who else needs to sign off?”
– “Is there an ideal timeframe to get this live?”

2️⃣ Always lock the next step
Don’t end a call without a date in the diary.
If they don’t book, ask: “How long do you need to review?” then set the follow-up.

3️⃣ Set boundaries with stalled deals
If I’ve chased 3x with no reply, I’ll say:
“Just so you know, I won’t follow up again until [end of month / quarter].”
You’d be surprised how often that reopens the conversation.

Forecasting will always feel messy.
But the more clarity you create, the cleaner your pipeline and the more trust you’ll build with your manager.

Sales is everybody’s job. Whether you believe it or not.

It’s not just the people with “sales” in their title.

It’s how the phone gets answered.
How accounts are handled.
How billing issues get resolved.
How quotes, bids, and estimates are prepared.
How crews show up in the field.

If you touch the customer, you’re in sales.

At its core, sales is simple:
An agreement between two parties to exchange value.

That exchange starts long before a contract is signed
and continues long after the work is done.

Every interaction creates an impression.
Every handoff either builds confidence or erodes it.

That’s what too many companies miss.

They focus on closing the deal
but ignore the dozens of moments that decide
whether a customer ever comes back.

Sales isn’t a department.
It’s the experience.

And everyone is accountable for it.

Every struggling CDO I've met has the same problem.

All the responsibility. None of the authority.

CEO: "Fix everything. Fast."
CFO: "No budget."
CTO: "Don't touch our systems."
CMO: "Our data is fine."
Sales VP: "We have our own process."
CHRO: "HR data stays with HR."
Legal: "Compliance first. Everything else second."

CDO: "Figure it out."

The most powerful title with the least authority.

I've watched this play out dozens of times.

The CDO gets hired to fix data quality, enable AI, and drive analytics transformation.

But nobody gives them the authority to actually do it.

They can't touch systems. Can't get budget. Can't override departmental definitions. Can't centralize anything.

So they spend years negotiating and building consensus while the CEO wonders why the data strategy isn't moving faster.

Here's what actually works:

The CDO needs executive authority over three things:

→ Data definitions: final say on what metrics mean
→ Change approval: who can modify core business logic
→ Escalation rights: authority to break ties when domains disagree

Without those, the CDO is a coordinator, not a decision-maker.

And coordination without authority just creates more meetings.

"Nobody's hitting quota, why are bringing on 10 more reps."

Your CEO: "Each one we hire adds $307,000 in annual productivity." ...  Unfortunately:

  --  Sales teams aren't run on a spreadsheet
  --  YOUR failure is built into your CEO's model
  --  This line of thinking presents well to the board
  --  Typically the addressable market size isn't factored into the equation

When researching a new organization (or evaluating your current one), make sure you have a detailed understanding of the TAM, including

How many prospect accounts are there?
How are they doled out?
How do they translate into territories
How big of a team can the market support?
Where is the org in growth and getting to that team size.
What product functionality is being added to increase the TAM

It's all going to be going really well as the org grows into the market, but once that's inverted things become tough.

If tools like Claude or Clay have replaced your sales or SDR team, let me tell you something straight: you never had a real sales team.

Those tools should free your sellers from the busy work, so they can spend their time on what actually closes deals: talking to other humans.

Most enterprises are losing the AI race. They just don't know it yet.

When the smartphone hit, every company rushed to show up on mobile. The ones that actually changed people's lives didn't just shrink their website. They threw out the old playbook entirely and rebuilt around a new behavior. The phone wasn't the point. What people could do with it was.

Different era. Same problem.

The models aren't the bottleneck. Dropping powerful AI into infrastructure that wasn't built for it doesn't transform anything. It creates expensive chaos. And right now, that's exactly what most enterprises have.

The winners aren't chasing better models. They're building better foundations.

"SaaS is dead. SaaS is dead. Bring out your SaaS."
They're yelling it in the streets like plague-era pallbearers. Every LinkedIn post. Every think piece. Every trader on every desk.

$285 billion in market cap. Gone in a week.

Great headline. Dramatic. Clickable. And completely wrong.

Not wrong because SaaS is perfect. Wrong because they've buried the wrong body.

The Great Misread
Here's what the market is saying: AI agents can replace per-seat software. Why pay for 10 licenses when one agent handles the workflow?

Fair question. Wrong question.

The right one: why did enterprises need 367 different applications in the first place?

For 20 years, every team got their own system. Sales got a CRM. HR got a portal. IT got a ticketing tool. Everyone got a dashboard. Nobody got coherence.

Dozens of systems of record. Endless versions of the truth. Teams stitching workflows together with APIs and prayers.

Then AI showed up. The great reviver, they said.

Instead, it lit the mess on fire. Every app shipped its own copilot. Every team deployed its own agent. On top of all that patchwork. And despite record AI investment, enterprise AI maturity declined 20% this year.

Read that again: Twenty percent.

AI didn't expose that software is unnecessary. AI exposed that fragmented software is useless. There's a massive difference.

The Wrong Scoreboard
The narrative says: AI will eat software. Go to the API layer. Vibe-code your way to freedom.

And it works. For the first 20%.

You can prototype an app in an afternoon. Spin up a workflow with a prompt. Impressive. But then you need role-based access for 50,000 employees. Compliance across six regulatory frameworks. Audit trails that hold up in court. You need it to work at 3am when the on-call team in Singapore needs an answer and nobody's awake to babysit the agent.

The first 20% is easy. The remaining 80% is where the cost, the complexity, and the actual value live.

The real hierarchy isn't "AI replaces software" vs. "software survives AI." Some software was always doing real work. Some was just a pretty interface on data anyone could access.

The SaaSpocalypse didn't kill SaaS. It sorted it.

More Agents = More Software, Not Less
Here's the irony nobody's processing.

When enterprises run 100x more AI agents than people, they don't need less software. They need more governance. More guardrails. More systems that enforce how those agents behave. As Aaron Levie put it: 100x more agents means 100x more opportunities for value creation or risk if it's not managed well. Deterministic workflows and probabilistic AI aren't competing. They're complementary. One without the other is either dangerous or useless.

The companies with the deepest data, the most complex workflows, and the most system connections? Their moat just got wider, not narrower.

Intelligence Is the Commodity. Execution Is the Moat.
Here's the part almost nobody is talking about:

Models are commoditizing. Fast. The cost of intelligence is dropping toward zero. In 18 months, the LLM powering your enterprise AI will matter about as much as which brand of gas you put in your car. Shell, Exxon, Mobil. Doesn't matter.

What matters is the vehicle.

The model is the commodity. The workflow is the product. The outcome is the point.

An AI with access to raw data knows what did happen. It doesn't know what should happen. "Should" requires context. It requires understanding how work moves across teams, systems, and policies. It requires decades of encoded business logic, not 20 minutes of prompt engineering.

Simple example. An employee submits a ticket: "I can't access internal apps."

A standalone LLM suggests steps. Restart your VPN. Check your network. Helpful. Generic. It doesn't know who you are, what device you're on, or that a security certificate expired overnight.

Now imagine a system that already knows all of that. It sees the expired cert, pushes a refresh, revalidates permissions, tests connectivity, documents the resolution, and closes the ticket. No human involved. Minutes, not days.

That's not AI giving advice. That's AI doing work.

What Actual Productivity Looks Like
Adobe consolidated service and operations across IT and HR. 25% faster outage resolution. 30% quicker case handling.

Lenovo collapsed the stack. 35% decrease in resolution time. Six mailboxes became one platform.

Hitachi Energy unified 50,000 employees on a single portal. 26,000 hours saved. 25% reduction in service desk requests. First week.

And at ServiceNow, 90% of our IT service requests are handled with AI. And we're saving 1,700 hours per week, which we deploy to higher-value areas.

None of that happened by bolting a copilot onto a fragmented stack. It happened by connecting the work. One data model. One platform. Workflows that don't stop at departmental boundaries.

The Real Funeral
Everyone's got a front-row seat at the SaaSpocalypse. Lots of mourning. Lots of hot takes.

They're right about one thing. Something is dying.

But it's not software. It's fragmentation. It's the 367-app patchwork. It's the illusion that intelligence alone, without context, without governance, without execution, is enough.

The companies positioned to win aren't chasing the next tool. They're building coherence. The kind enterprises can trust and AI can actually operate inside.

That's a very different funeral. And most people are still at the wrong one.

Good riddance to fragmentation. Now the real work begins.

Recently sold our company. Along the way, we got dozens of term sheets, from seed to Series B. I’m stepping away from VC for a bit, so this feels like a good time to share my early-stage fundraising playbook:

 1. Raise only when it’s obvious you’re ready.
 2. Until then, tell investors you’re busy building. Don’t waste time with them at cocktail parties. Go build a great company, culture, product, and traction. They’ll come to you eventually.
 3. Ignore associates and juniors chasing you. They’re mostly filling quotas. If they really want in, the partner will show up.
 4. Trusted signal is everything. The right angels can 10x your odds.
 5. Time market demand well. Investment comes in waves. Don’t be too early to the party or too late. Time it as well as you can.
 6. Seed rounds should close in 2-3 weeks.
 7. Longer usually means you weren’t ready and going to fail.
 8. Compress the process. Keep a tight calendar, reply fast, and build momentum and FOMO.
 9. Hope is not a strategy. 48 hours of silence = dead.
 10. “We were impressed, but we’ll wait for the next round” or “come back when you cross $X ARR” usually means they didn’t like you enough.
 11. Angels and small funds should decide in 1-2 meetings.
 12. Big funds get 2-3 meetings plus a partner meeting. More than that is a waste of your time and they are just fishing.
 13. Skip pre-seed. Weak signal and unnecessary dilution.
 14. Skip accelerators. If you need a cheerleader, you’re in the wrong profession.
 15. Fundraising is 90% the CEO. Accept it, for better or worse. It’s your show.
 16. Be confident and direct, but never a douchebag.

I think we have lost some sense of judgment and moderation when it comes to product building currently.

The moment you turn something into a universally celebrated metric, whether that is token burn, prototype count, or percentage of agent-written code, you start losing sight of what actually matters.

I have felt the same way for a long time about overusing data and A/B testing to build products. The moment you reduce product quality or productivity to a metric, you stop shipping value and start shipping numbers.

A lot of what people are doing with AI makes directional sense. The missing piece is counterbalance:

1. AI should help engineers build better products. Leaderboards and adoption metrics can be useful as directional signals. They do not tell you what is being built, whether it is good, or whether it should exist at all.

2. Users do not care what percentage of your code was written by agents. They care about the outcome. Faster output is useful. Like usually, faster doesn't seem to add to quality, clarity, or stability of products. Power to build should not become an excuse to lower quality bars.

3. LLM-generated prototypes can feel like late-night whiteboarding sessions. They look exciting in the moment and feel productive very quickly. Then a few days later you realize the idea was shallow, distracting, or simply wrong. The same trap shows up in jumping straight to code and solutions more broadly. You may just be building the wrong thing more efficiently. Prototyping has its place. So do clear thinking, good design, and a real understanding of the user’s problem. In terms of activities or momentum, the main quest and the side quest can both feel productive but only one actually moves the mission forward.

4. Adding more to products is still dangerous as ever even if time or effort to add it has gone down. Every addition creates complexity, maintenance cost, and user confusion. New features should be pushed back unless they clearly show it should exist and how it improves the product.

5. Not everything needs to be an agent shaped. A simple scheduled task does not need a full LLM sandbox. Making something agentic because it feels current or impressive does not make it right-sized, correct, or effective.

The core ideas are: 
- even if you can, maybe you should not. 
- more power we have to build should not reduce our need to think, it should increase it.

CEO: "We need to align marketing and sales."

Me: "The 1st step is to delete this misconception:"

Marketing stops when the sales process starts.

Here's how that mistake usually goes:

- Marketing is here for awareness and leadgen;
- When those 2 boxes are ticked, it's finished;
- "Ok we sent you a lead, we've done our job!"
- Then it's time for the sales team to shine!

This "funnel" looks like the perfect handoff in theory.

But from the buyer perspective it's the same brand and the same journey.

They don't instantly forget the ads, social content, and videos when they start speaking with your sales team.

Marketing still largely impacts the buying decision during the sales process for 2 reasons:

1. The marketing messages anchored them

They come into the sales process with beliefs and feelings they got from your marketing actions. The messages and insights you shared before have an impact on the sales conversations (objections, objectives, questions).

2. They still see your marketing actions during the sales phase

While talking to your sales team, your prospects keep seeing your posts, videos, emails, or case studies on your website. These actions have a new impact on their decision (it can reinforce a positive feeling or the contrary).

That's why you need to do this:

- Make sure you share the exact same messages across marketing and sales for a coherent journey.

- Don't alternate intense marketing seasons with low marketing seasons. Instead, run evergreen marketing with consistency.

And remember:

For buyers, it's the same brand and the same journey.

I think Stripe just mass produced a business model for every AI startup on earth and increased their TAM exponentially. 

Customer buys a shirt for $40, Stripe takes 2.9% + 30 cents. It's a simple formula, but token billing is completely different.

AI costs are variable. They shift by model, by provider, by week. A startup using Claude for 40% of inference and GPT-4o for 60% has a blended cost structure that changes every time Anthropic or OpenAI adjusts pricing... which is constantly.

Stripe is now ingesting those real-time model prices, applying the startup's target markup, metering per-customer usage, and generating the invoice automatically.

That's constructing unit economics at a scale and complexity far beyond anything in traditional payments.

And the data asset being created by them is a massive moat.

OpenAI knows what OpenAI charges, and Anthropic knows what Anthropic charges. But Stripe will know what every model charges, what every startup pays, what every startup marks up, and what every end customer actually consumes.

But the gateway is where this gets really interesting.

Stripe's AI gateway routes inference, returns the response, and attributes tokens to the customer in one API call.

Today it's "pick the best model."

Tomorrow it's "Stripe recommends the model that optimizes your target margin across 12 providers in real time."

The moment that recommendation engine turns on, model providers start competing on Stripe's terms. Pricing power inverts from provider to platform.

This is AWS turned sideways (or i guess diagonally).

Amazon didn't build apps... they built the infrastructure every app depended on, then used the data to optimize the infrastructure itself.

Stripe is running the same playbook on AI economics instead of AI compute.

I truly believe that token billing will make Stripe the most strategically important company in tech that doesn't train a model.
