Today, Anthropic shared the first AI orchestrated cyber espionage attack.

We are now living in a new world where you can no longer protect yourself with layers and layers of "cybersecurity tools" that are really just compliance checkmarks. In stead, you need to understand the implications of this attack - and why you need to protect your most important assets assuming the breach is coming.

Anthropic’s report describes a state-aligned actor using AI agents to run almost the entire operation: reconnaissance, vulnerability analysis, exploitation, credential harvesting, lateral movement, data extraction, even summarizing what they found.

This wasn’t “AI helping an attacker.”
This was AI orchestrating the attack.

And that changes the timeline.
It changes the scale.
It changes who can execute sophisticated operations.
Most importantly, it changes what gets targeted.

When attackers automate reconnaissance and lateral movement, your network, your endpoints, your “zero trust-ish” setup—none of it can guarantee containment. Credentials leak. SaaS sprawl grows. Agentic workflows run inside your company whether you planned for them or not.

Which leaves one question:
What happens to your sensitive data when the perimeter fails?

If the answer is “it’s still in the clear, available to anything with valid credentials,” then you are operating with an outdated threat model.

The shift happening now is simple but profound:
Security has to move from protecting systems to protecting the flow of data itself.

That means isolating sensitive data, minimizing where it lives, and applying real controls—encryption, tokenization, residency—before it moves into agents, models, logs, or pipelines.

AI has changed the offense.
Organizations now have to change the defense.

Not by adding another tool to the pile, but by treating data itself as the boundary.

This is where the next decade of security is headed.

Most AI coding agents can write code. Almost none can survive 8 months of production. Alibaba just tested agents on 100 real codebases, each for 233 days. The results are brutal.

75% of models break working code during maintenance. Everyone accumulates technical debt like it's a loyalty program.

The problem is that SWE-bench measures "does it work NOW" SWE-CI measures "does it still work after 6 months of AI PRs”

Turns out agents optimized for snapshot testing write brittle garbage that passes today, collapses tomorrow. EvoScore weights later iterations heavier so your quick-win hack gets punished when consequences compound.

This is the benchmark AI coding deserved, but nobody wanted. 

The honest take is ‘Writing code is easy. Not breaking it for 233 days is the actual job. Most agents just failed the interview’

Don’t get me wrong…AI should be saving PMs time on the trivial stuff. Drafting docs, summarising research, booking meetings. Use it ruthlessly for that. But the time you free up should be reinvested into the work AI genuinely cannot do.

AI can summarise a million support tickets. It cannot meet with your most impotant customer realise what they say they need and what they actually need are completely different things based entirely on their body language.

AI can score your backlog. It cannot tell the VP of Sales that the feature their biggest deal depends on isn’t strategically important, and somehow get them to agree. Trade-offs require conviction, judgement, and the willingness to be unpopular.

“That’s a brilliant Idea Marcus, you’re absolutely right….” - Claude opus (at least 10x today alone)

Vibe coding also obliterates the most important PM discipline…deciding what not to build. A big part of my philosophy as a PM is that the most important decisions are the features you say no to. 

Every feature you don’t build is one less thing bloating the product, confusing customers, and stealing capacity from what actually matters. When building is frictionless, PMs ship features because they can, not because users NEED them.

In a world where it’s trivial to build anything, the hardest and most valuable skill is deciding what not to build.

If every company uses the same AI to make decisions and build products, everything regresses to the mean. Same tools, same thinking, same output. The way to win is to be different and that comes from deeper customer understanding, not by doing a course on how to prompt cursor.

AI tools like Claude Code are revolutionary and are evolving so fast that it’s almost a full time job to keep up. So why not wait it out? 

Spend that time making yourself irreplaceable. Then adopt the latest AI stack when it’s mature enough to replace everyone else.

Rebuilding an $11B Company for the Age of AI: It took Notion years to get product-market fit. In 2015, Ivan and his co-founder left San Francisco for Kyoto, Japan. They moved into a two-story house so small that a shoji screen essentially separated the bedrooms. And they worked 18 hours a day rebuilding Notion from scratch after scrapping three years of code.

Most startups die right there. Not because the founders lack talent, but because it’s hard to look at three years of work and decide to throw it all away and rebuild from scratch. Notion did. And in so doing, instilled a culture of “refounding” deep into everything—and everyone—at Notion. 

Building with AI these past few years has meant repeatedly re-architecting their core agent layer — the industry moves that fast. When Notion got early access to GPT-4 through friends at OpenAI, he assumed everyone had it. So he went into full sprint mode, and again locked himself in a hotel room to build the first prototype of Notion AI. 

Since then, Notion has been doing something that’s rarer than it should be: treating AI not as a feature, but as a new foundation. Here are the three principles Ivan kept coming back to: 

Treat AI like entirely new material. 

Ivan has a very specific way of talking about AI: it’s a new material. Not “a capability.” Not “a workflow accelerator.” A material. Like bitmap displays. 

That framing forces a different kind of mental model for building AI tools. If you believe you’re working with a new material, you don’t just sprinkle it onto the old architecture. You rebuild the architecture so the material can actually do what it wants to do. That’s why Notion has re-architected its core AI layer repeatedly. Ivan sees it as the cost of staying native to a material that is itself changing on a monthly cadence.

Use AI to dissolve the coordination tax.

As companies scale, the biggest drag isn’t usually intelligence or effort, it’s coordination. I’ve written much about the tax that comes from trying to coordinate large groups of humans. That overhead grows superlinearly as you add people and increase scope. 

Ivan’s metaphor is among the best I’ve heard on how AI can fix this problem. “Language models are the steel beams of organizations.” Before steel beams, buildings topped out around five or six floors. Steel didn’t just make buildings taller, but enabled an entirely new structure: the skyscraper.

He believes AI can do something similar for organizations: increase throughput without adding layers of humans whose core job is alignment work. That doesn’t mean removing people; it means elevating people to higher-judgment work rather than turning them into the connective tissue for a slow bureaucracy.

Shift the scarce resource away from capability, and towards agency. 

Ivan believes there are three “buckets” when it comes to thinking about AI: 

capabilities—what you can do 
taste—what you choose to do
agency—your ability to put it into motion

AI makes capabilities cheap, so the bottleneck moves to taste and agency. 

The winners aren’t the ones who can just “do more” but instead are the ones who can better decide what should be done, and how to do it. This is why, I believe, Notion keeps sounding like a design company even when it’s talking about agents. Ivan talks about beauty, fun, and timeless tools, because when execution becomes abundant, taste—and how we share it with the world—is what matters most..

Notion’s Founder, Ivan Zhao, says you shouldn’t try to save costs or predict ROI when you’re implementing AI. In fact, it’s the easiest way to lose the race. 

A lot of companies are trying to “control spend” by rationing tokens and putting guardrails around how much experimentation engineers can do. But that creates artificial ceilings on what’s possible, right when the whole point of AI is to explore and discover new product surface area faster. 

Ivan’s argument is that AI flips the constraint inside a tech company. The bottleneck isn’t “can we build it?” anymore. It’s “what should we build?” Judgment and taste become scarce resources. 

If you artificially constrain what you’re able to try by limiting spend, you’re volunteering to fall behind.

Enjoy cheap AI while it lasts. 

This year AI companies will spend $600Billion building AI data centres. They last about 4 years so they need to recoup $150Billion per year just to break even on the capital expense of building them. 

A typical capital intensive business like mining or logistics spends about 20% of revenue on capex. So at maturity AI companies need to be earning $750Billion per year in revenue to seriously justify their investments. 

To give that some perspective, every single adult in Europe and the USA would have to be spending about $100 per month on AI to reach that level of spend. AI companies need - 625Million people x $1200 per year to make this business work. For every person that can't pay $100 per month someone else has to be paying $200 per month. 

When humans built railways, electric grids, roads and homes we were building something that had a lifespan into the decades. Data centres age like your iPhone - they need replacing every 3-5 years if you want them to be current. 

Something has to give... 
- either everyone has to pay a lot more. 
- the government has to pay for it and raise taxes. 
- the spending has to slow right down and the tech stops advancing. 
- there is a new paradigm for AI delivery (EG: on device LLMs)
- the investors/lenders get wiped out. 

Those are the options. The tech isn't cheap and never will be - someone has to pay at some point. 

Be aware, while you and your business get more and more reliant on AI, you are on borrowed time. Somewhere in a boardroom, an AI company CEO is promising that someone is going to foot the bill eventually.

Most vibe-coded apps work perfectly… until they go public. As a Security Engineer, I see tonnes of vulnerabilities in Vibe-coded apps that have shipped to production.

Here are 12 of the most common ones.

[1] Missing authentication

- Problem: Endpoints assumed “only the frontend will call this.”
- Fix: Require auth on every non-public route. Deny by default.

[2] Broken authorisation

- Problem: Users could access other users’ data by changing an ID.
- Fix: Enforce ownership checks server-side on every read and write.

[3] Leaky secrets

- Problem: API keys, JWT secrets, and DB creds lived in code or client bundles.
- Fix: Move to a secret manager, rotate, and never ship secrets to the browser.

[4] No rate limiting

- Problem: Unlimited requests enabled brute force, scraping, and bill shock.
- Fix: Rate limit per IP, per user, per token. Add abuse detection.

[5] Wide-open CORS

- Problem: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: -` plus credentials meant silent data theft.
- Fix: Allowlist origins. Never use wildcard with credentials.

[6] Missing input validation

- Problem: SQLi, NoSQL injection, and weird payload crashes.
- Fix: Validate schema at the boundary. Reject unknown fields.

[7] Unsafe file uploads

- Problem: Anyone could upload anything and you served it back.
- Fix: Restrict types, scan, store outside web root, use signed URLs.

[8] Insecure password handling

- Problem: Plaintext or weak hashing, reused salts, no MFA.
- Fix: Use bcrypt/argon2, strong policies, and MFA for sensitive actions.

[9] Over-permissive tokens

- Problem: Long-lived “god tokens” that worked everywhere.
- Fix: Short-lived tokens, scoped permissions, refresh rotation.

[10] Missing CSRF protections

- Problem: Logged-in users could be tricked into triggering actions.
- Fix: SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, and avoid cookie auth for APIs.

[11] Weak logging and no alerting

- Problem: Breaches looked like normal traffic until it was too late.
- Fix: Log auth events, admin actions, and anomaly alerts on spikes.

[12] No safe defaults in infra

- Problem: Public S3 buckets, open security groups, exposed DB ports.
- Fix: Private by default, least privilege IAM, IaC scanning in CI.

Vibe coding is fine for prototypes.

But the moment you deploy, you are no longer “building an app.”
You are operating an attack surface.

Ship fast. Secure faster.

At a 30-person startup nobody has heard of, nobody is applying.

Your job is to create demand.

Two different muscles.

Neither better. Just different.

Someone comfortable reaching out cold.

We made an offer to someone who accepted but then didn't join. 

He called though, to explain why. 

He had another offer, hardly 10-12% higher, but he needed the money. 

We couldn't match it, so he took that offer. 

A year later, we were hiring again and had a better budget this time and the role involved working with a big international client. 

The first call I made was to him. No interview. Direct offer, if only he would join for sure this time. 

He said yes immediately.

Now after all this time, he is still with the organization, doing exceptional work.

Not everyone internally was convinced with the idea of me reaching out to him again. 

But here's the thing. Hiring is not personal. 

Organizations desperately need good candidates, as much as candidates need good organizations. 

And people should not be penalized for looking out for themselves. Especially in a time where organizations don't think twice about letting go of people to make a balance sheet look pretty. 

All jobs are about money. 
Let's not pretend otherwise.

When did ‘marketing manager’ become code for ‘entire marketing department’?

In my current job search, I’ve seen more and more job postings that look great ... until you read the full description. Suddenly that one position is responsible for copywriting, content management, social media, digital advertising, CRM and email, automation, website and SEO, data analysis and reporting, plus running multiple large campaigns for both demand gen and customer marketing. That’s not a role, that’s a team.

I understand budgets are tight and many orgs are trying to do more with less. I’ve been in those situations before—seen as a high performer and a leader, then handed the responsibilities of colleagues who were laid off “just for a little while,” only for that workload to quietly become permanent. The result wasn’t heroics, it was burnout, frustration, and feeling like I was failing at an impossible job.

When companies post roles like this, they may not realize they’re setting someone up to fail. A desperate but talented candidate lands the job, gets overwhelmed, and is ultimately seen as underperforming when the expectations were unrealistic from the start.

My hope is that more organizations pause and ask: “Is this really one job, or is this two or three?” Because the real competitive advantage isn’t squeezing every task into one title. It’s designing roles people can actually thrive in.

One of the most painful interviews of my career produced one of our best hires.
 
He barely spoke and gave one-word answers.
It was an uncomfortable 40 minutes.
 
I walked out thinking, “No way.”
My team pushed back and we hired him.
I listened. They knew.
 
Sixteen years later, he’s still with us and he is a superstar.
He still doesn’t talk much.
 
That moment changed how I thought about hiring.
 
We spend an extraordinary amount of time doing interviews.
And yet they are often a terrible predictor of performance.
 
In fact, the more senior the candidate, the better they are at faking it.
 
After 60 minutes we pretend we can predict:
👍 How they write code
👍 How they work with others when tired
👍 Whether they give up when it gets hard
👍 Whether they make the people around them better
 
That is a fantasy.
 
The best predictor of performance is having worked with someone before. Or working on a real problem together before you hire them.
 
Once we admit how bad we are at predicting performance from interviews, we can start designing better hiring processes.

When I started posting on LinkedIn, I was pitching investors from 8pm to 2am from a kitchenette in London.

PowerPoint had over 500 million users and a 40-year head start.

Five years and $100M in ARR later, here are 9 not-so-obvious things I’ve learned about building a startup:

1. Position through contrast.

James Clear studied 150 nonfiction books that each sold over a million copies. The best titles combine something unexpected with something universal. "Atomic" sounds strange next to "Habits" - and that's the point. Great positioning makes people instantly understand what you are and what you're not.

2. Choose upside over downside.

Focus on businesses that create upside, that’s often unlimited, rather than ones that protect downside, which is usually some finite cost or time saving. If everyone gets your idea immediately, the opportunity is probably gone.

3. Stop lying to yourself.

We learn as kids that lying to others is bad. But most adults lie to themselves constantly because it's so easy. We want to protect our egos and to avoid the discomfort of being wrong. This is the fastest path to mediocrity.

4. Stay simple. Build for endurance.

Like a marathon runner, endurance comes from simple, repeatable motions. Resist becoming a Rube Goldberg machine: impressive, but everything has to go perfectly right. Gamma hit $100M with ~50 people. Every hire should make the company feel faster, not slower.

5. Don't confuse craft with flair.

Craft includes invisible work that a customer may never see. It's selfless. Flair is attention-grabbing for its own sake. One builds trust. The other borrows it. Visionary designer Dieter Rams: "Good design is as little design as possible."

6. Go first. Be constant.

Going first creates reciprocity. Help someone, and they're more likely to help you. Being constant means recognizing all meaningful growth comes from compounding. $84.2B of Buffett's $84.5B net worth came after his 50th birthday. His skill is investing. His secret is time.

7. Don't chase shiny objects.

Steve Jobs at WWDC 1997: "Focusing is about saying no." Staying focused on your customer and your mission is hard, but essential.

8. Write down your values, then build habits around them.

If you value integrity, be data-driven. If you value growth, ask for feedback. Values only matter if they shape behavior.

9. You don't need to love it on day one.

Mark Cuban: "Don't follow your passion, follow your effort." I didn't grow up dreaming about presentation software. The passion came after it was earned.

Every fund on Sand Hill Road said no to Life360 at first. I still have the list of 70 firms that rejected us.

“This is stupid,” one said. Another said, “Kids are never going to have smartphones.”

Seriously. 

We were in a grind, working through a major slog without any sort of clean breakout moment.

At one point, we were two months away from running out of cash. 

One thing I wish more founders understood about rejection is this: 
Often, investor rejections have more to do with their own worldview than with you personally.

Looking back at Life360, the “no’s” came from three missed assumptions:

1) They didn’t believe in the smartphone future
They couldn’t imagine a world where every kid had a phone.

2) They saw it as a “kid tracker”
We saw it as a family network. Day-to-day family coordination: Where everyone is, when they’ll be home and even what’s for dinner. We knew we were solving what families actually cared about.

3) They overestimated the privacy backlash
There’s a small vocal minority that cares deeply about privacy. In reality, most people are willing to trade privacy for utility. Even if you don’t like it, you have to model it correctly. 

Now, Life360 is a publicly traded company worth >$3B. That’s a long way from “no” after “no.”

It’s worth remembering that a lot of investor rejections aren’t about you as much as they’re about the investor’s model of the world. 

If you keep hearing no, it’s not necessarily a sign to grind harder. Instead, ask yourself:

- What future do you believe that they don’t?
- What are they misunderstanding about your customer?
- What story are you failing to communicate?
- The moment that defines you isn’t the first “no.” 

What’s more important is whether you can keep going and keep sharpening the truth until the right people see it.

If you're in GTM, joining the wrong startup can destroy your career. After leaving Verkada, I spent 6 weeks meeting 50+ Series A-D founders before joining Clay. Here are the 10 red flags I found in almost every broken GTM org:

1. Marketing consistently hits all their goals. Sellers consistently get fired for missing quota. If both are true at the same time, marketing's goals aren't connected to revenue. And that's bad.

2. Execs can't go in the weeds. The CMO doesn't know what campaigns are live. The Sales / SDR leader doesn't know their TAM. That's f***ed.

3. More meetings about attribution than about what to actually do. GTM isn't zero-sum. Leaders who treat it like one create a toxic culture.

4. ICs answer "what's your goal?" with inputs. "I'm launching 3 campaigns." "I'm writing 5 blog posts." That's a task list, NOT a goal.

5. Marketing and Sales don't talk (and kind of hate each other). If these two functions aren't meeting multiple times a week, you don't have a GTM team.

6. "We'll hire someone for this." No clarity on the role, scope, or ideal profile. Leaders just trying to hire themselves out of a problem.

7. Leaders have no vision, only opinions about your work. They micro-analyze execution and benchmark it against "how I did it as an IC 5 years ago."

8. Five days in office, because "otherwise nobody works." That's a trust problem. Or a management problem. Either way, it's a leadership problem.

9. Support functions run on tickets. Data science, ops, and marketing are burning through a backlog instead of helping you solve business problems.

10. Hiring for pedigree over intangibles. Stanford + McKinsey logo beats someone who's actually done hard, relevant, ambiguous work.

A great role in a broken GTM org will make you worse. An OK role in a well-run GTM org will make you better than you thought possible.

Do your diligence before you sign.

Marc Benioff says OpenAI and Anthropic are top Salesforce customers this quarter. Meanwhile, a Reddit founder spends $36K replacing a $300/mo SaaS tool. While fools believe “vibe coding kills SaaS”, AI companies are SaaS' biggest buyers.

The most advanced AI labs on earth with more engineering talent and compute than anyone are still buying traditional enterprise software at scale.

An LLM is a feature.
And it is probabilistic, not deterministic. 

A neural network that hallucinates cannot:

- Execute a financial transaction that has to be right every single time
- Secure customer data with zero tolerance for error
- Provide the determinism that every real business runs on

Benioff said: 

"No one has a company that's running entirely on a large language model because it's not real."

Meanwhile, some founder on Reddit spent 3 months building a worse version of a $300/month SaaS tool and is now maintaining it themselves.

Here's the cost breakdown:

- $8K/month developer for 3 months: $24K
- 50% management overhead: $36K total
- Ramp up, cool down, and maintenance forever after

$36K divided by $300/month is 120 months.

That's a 10-year payback period on a worse product you now maintain yourself.

You don't sew your own clothes even if you could and you wouldn't 3D print them either with a machine in your house because there's years of knowledge flowing into products w/ iteration, customer feedback, edge cases, brand.

Building, maintaining, and developing a product customers love are three completely different disciplines.

Some companies would rather own a crappy internal tool than rely on external software because they want control, and that impulse has always existed.

The analysts writing reports about fully autonomous AI companies have never had to run one.

The people actually running AI companies are still buying the software.

Executive events have been a huge pipeline driver for Clay. But early on, it was impossible to get CROs and CMOs to respond to our dinner invites.

No one knew what Clay was, and these execs get bombarded with hundreds of vendor invites every week. No chance a CRO would go to dinner with a 20 person startup.

We had to put ourselves in their shoes and think: who are the people within our network that have actual social capital with this persona? Who would they actually respond to?

For us, the answer was obvious: VCs.

CMOs and CROs don't care about your tiny startup. But they do care about building their brand within the VC network. They want to have a strong reputation there.

So we partnered with top-tier VCs to co-brand our events. If you get an invite from Sequoia, Meritech, or CapitalG, you're way more likely to consider it vs. a random vendor.

We run multiple events per quarter with them now. Over time, we actually expanded this approach to executive search firms (execs want to build their rep with recruiters too). 

As we built our brand, this became easier and easier. But the biggest unlock was leveraging partners with stronger brands within our target persona.

The learning here was asking ourselves who are the people that have social capital with our target audiences? Let’s partner with them first to then connect with our audience.

Stop hiring for "AI experience."
 
I keep seeing job postings that require 3-5 years of AI implementation experience for roles at companies that have never shipped an AI project.
 
Think about what you’re actually asking for. You want someone who has done something your organization has never done, using tools that didn’t exist 3 years ago, in an industry that changes every month. 
 
That person either doesn’t exist or costs $400K and isn’t interested in building your first proof of concept.
 
Here’s what actually works: Hire people who deeply understand your business and your data. Then give them access to the right partners and tools to build AI capability.

In the 80s and 90s, most sales reps were full-cycle.

They found their own meetings. They ran the process. They renewed and expanded accounts.

Then we specialized:
--> SDRs (thanks to the “predictable revenue” era)
--> CSMs and post-sale roles (as we realized retention is growth)
--> More layers of management to coordinate it all

And we paid a price: more handoffs, more misalignment, more overhead, and more “local optimization” instead of true LTV optimization.

My belief for 2026:

AI evolves us back to the full-cycle seller.

Not because the buyer journey got simpler — but because AI will make individual sellers radically more capable at:
--> generating meetings
--> converting meetings to customers
--> onboarding customers
--> expanding customers

And that has a second-order effect:

Sales orgs get flatter.
Less middle management. Higher rep-to-manager ratios. Sellers become less dependent on managers for training, coaching, pipeline hygiene, and process adherence.

If I were building a sales team from scratch in 2026, I’d do three things:

Change the first-hire profile (PMF stage):
Still strong at customer conversations + pattern recognition… but much more weight on experimenting with sales tech and AI workflows.

Build a modular AI-native stack:
Start on top of multiple LLMs. Stay interoperable. Avoid “one-vendor operating system” lock-in while we’re still in the early innings.

Incentivize productivity innovation before hiring headcount:
Set a higher bar, give the team tools + autonomy, and reward output — so you find the frontier of what’s possible before you scale org complexity.

One caveat: AI won’t replace critical human moments (escalations, high-stakes trust) and it won’t fully replace true creativity.

But it will change how we design sales teams.

Is your business using AI or running on AI? The difference will define who thrives in the coming years.

Over the past three decades, I’ve watched multiple technology waves reshape how enterprises operate. Each time, the winners had one thing in common: they combined bold ambition with the right architecture and controls.

Agentic AI will be no different.

That’s why we’ve published two new blueprints for leaders who want to build a truly agentic business:

📖 The Executive Brief: what it really means to run an agentic enterprise, the opportunities and risks, and why platform, governance, and control will separate those who experiment from those who win. It’s a ten‑minute read designed to change the way you see the future.

⚙️ The Technical Foundation: a deep dive into the architecture that makes it real – how we think about multi‑agent systems, long‑running workflows, security, identity, governance and data. This is why ServiceNow is not a traditional SaaS vendor, not a set of cobbled‑together tools, and not a single point solution. It’s a platform built for an AI‑native, agentic future.

We’ve been building toward this moment for years: a world where AI is not just assisting work, but autonomously executing it – with trust, context, and control built in from the start. I’m convinced that the next generation of market leaders will be those who embrace this shift early, with the right foundation.

If you’re a leader or builder asking “How do we scale AI without losing control?” then these blueprints are for you.

The non-deterministic approach of LLM-based security audits.

When you run classic tools such as Nessus, Nmap, Nuclei, or similar, you expect deterministic results. If port 22 is open, it reports that every time.

This is quite a different story with LLMs. From token usage to vulnerabilities found, the approach is non-deterministic. This means that sometimes you see the vulnerability, and sometimes you don't.

This can create the illusion that you pass the security audit, or you can be very unlucky and miss an important bug because the LLM simply omits it for random reasons.

In the screenshot below, you see my test with a purposely written vulnerable app that is run across 50 iterations of Claude Code’s /security-review command and then manually assessed. The app is byte-for-byte exactly the same across all runs. Sometimes all the bugs are identified and other times overlooked.

I can only imagine the extra entropy introduced when people use different writing styles, comments, languages, and more in their code.

And as always, this is not an “AI is terrible” take. It is just very important to understand the pitfalls of the tools you are working with.

Sales asks:  "Can you create a quick flyer?" Or, "Can we just get a template for this?" 

Marketing responds: Deep breath. Open Canva. Move logo. Adjust font. Export PDF. Send to sales. Repeat.

Reactive marketing efficiently solves the immediate needs of the sales team. But externally, it lands generically. And generic is invisible.

Customers aren't engaging with collateral because it exists. They engage when it speaks to their specific pain, their pressure, and their risk. 

A quick templated flyer says, "Hey! We sell stuff."  A strategic piece says, "We understand your world, and we want to walk alongside you."  One of these blends in with the competition. One is a trust-building differentiator.

The real shift happens when marketing isn't just building flyers, but shaping the customer journey before the sales conversation ever happens. That's the difference between a support function and a growth engine.

Customers can tell the difference.

Sam Altman says Slack creates fake work for 6 hours daily.

Messages demand immediate attention.
Responses generate three more questions.
You waste a tremendous amount of activity with no real progress.

You think you're "on top of things" because you have 20 conversations open.

- Close it and work

inside other people's thought processes.

that makes you feel productive while producing nothing.

The most burned out I've ever been as a marketer had nothing to do with the hours. 

It was from working at a company where everything was urgent, and nothing was prioritized.

Marketing burnout rarely comes from the work itself.
It comes from the system around the work.

When there's no clear marketing leadership, here's what happens:
→ Sales sets the agenda (so marketing becomes order-taking)
→ Founders override priorities mid-week (so nothing ever gets finished)
→ Campaigns launch without strategy (so you're guessing what success looks like)
→ Long-term planning never happens (because short-term fires eat all the oxygen)

You end up constantly reacting.

And burnout doesn't happen because marketers can't handle pressure.

It happens when no one defines what actually matters. There's no way you can think strategically when everything is urgent.

That's not a performance issue. That's a structure issue.

And structure issues don't fix themselves; they need leadership that protects focus, not just adds to the list.

I was hired into a "marketing" role that wasn't really marketing at all.

In practice, it was almost entirely sales support: custom slide decks, building proposal templates, writing and submitting RFPs, one-off presentations, demo support/scheduling, writing emails for SDRs and account execs, coordinating events, and making sure leads immediately went to the sales team.

Important work? Yeah. But not marketing in the strategic sense.

Here's what was missing: no campaign strategy, no brand development, no lead generation programs, no content calendar, no market positioning work. Just reactive support.

The challenge wasn't the work itself, it was the misalignment between the title and the reality. When "marketing" means "sales enablement," it creates confusion about value, impact, and career growth.

I've moved on since then, but seeing others talk about this recently made me realize how common it is.

If you're in a similar position, here's what helped me:

Clarify expectations early (something I wish I had done). In interviews, ask what "marketing" actually means in that org. Is there a CMO? A Director? Who does marketing report to? What does success look like?

Document your impact. Even in support roles, track how your work contributes beyond individual deals. That data holds a lot of power!

Know your non-negotiables. If you want to do strategic marketing, campaigns, brand building, positioning, make sure your next role is set up for that. 

Have you experienced this kind of role misalignment? I'd love to hear how you navigated it.

Thrive just raised $10B.

Zoom out and it gets even crazier. 

Just weeks ago, Andreessen Horowitz announced raising $15B.

The biggest VC funds are getting even bigger. 

The Information notes that in the past three years, top firms raised more than half of what they’ve raised in their entire lifetime.

Thrive is the clean example. Since 2023, it’s raised roughly $18B.
That’s about 70% of the money it’s taken in since its first institutional round in 2011. Same firm. Same brand. Way more firepower.

Here’s the part founders should care about:

When a firm is sitting on that much capital, the product you think you’re buying changes.

More fund = different incentives. Bigger checks need even bigger outcomes.

“we can support you forever” quietly turns into “we need you to be a category outcome or bust.”

What this means for you.

If you’re building something that can be great but not massive, you can get pushed into a game you didn’t sign up for.

Extra capital can look like safety.

It can also be a growth mandate wearing a friendly hoodie.

- Ask early: what check size are you actually underwriting for the next round? What valuation?

- Ask directly: How big of an outcome does your company need to be a for the fund economics to work for the VCs?

- Match capital to strategy, not ego. The wrong partner is expensive even at a high valuation. More than just expensive... Devastating.

If a firm’s fund size changed dramatically, would your company still be their “right-sized” bet—or just their next attempt at a monster return?

Bottom line - When your investor’s fund gets 10x bigger, do your incentives still match or do they diverge?

If your funding is tied to “AGI,” you’re not raising capital.

You’re signing up for a definition fight.

Per the Information, Amazon is weighing a major equity investment in OpenAI. The ultimate size of the commitment could hinge on two triggers: an IPO, or achieving “artificial general intelligence.”

That’s the headline.

“Strategic partnership.”

“Alignment.”

Big check energy.

Here’s the part founders should care about:

“AGI” is a very loosely defined milestone.

Loosely defined milestones don’t reduce risk. They move power.

When a future tranche depends on a squishy definition, the counterparty gets optionality.

They can say yes when it’s convenient.

They can say “not yet” when leverage flips.

And everyone pretends it’s objective because the word sounds scientific.

This isn’t just about OpenAI.

It’s a pattern you’ll see in smaller deals too.

Milestone-based funding. “Performance” unlocks. “Next check after X.”

If X is not crisp, you’re agreeing to a negotiation later, under pressure.

Lesson for founders:

Money with strings is not cheaper money. It’s deferred governance.

And the deferment is the trap, because you only discover who controls the definition when you need the cash.

If you ever face milestone-linked capital, do the unsexy work up front:

- Define the milestone in measurable terms, in writing, with who decides and what evidence counts.

- Add a time-based backstop (if we hit A/B/C outcomes by date, the tranche is unlocked).

- Make “dispute resolution” real, not a handshake.

- Ask yourself: if this tranche never comes, does the company still survive?

You can call it “AGI.”

Or “enterprise readiness.”

Or “v2 shipped.”

Same game.

What milestone in your last deal sounded clean… but was actually a future leverage point?

What’s the most dangerous “milestone” you’ve seen used to control a founder later?

iPod = 1,000 songs in your pocket (THEN and NOW!)

There's a subtle infrastructure problem no one's talking about.

Data warehouses like Snowflake and Databricks are in the read path. They receive data via ETL after decisions are made. By the time it lands, the decision context is gone. They can tell you what happened, but they can't tell you why.

For example let's say a support lead checks customer ARR in Salesforce, sees two open escalations in Zendesk, reads a Slack thread flagging churn risk, and decides to escalate. The synthesis of that process happens in their head. The ticket just says "escalated to Tier 3." The warehouse gets "escalated to Tier 3" three hours later, but the reasoning doesn't really get recorded.

This matters for agents. If you want an agent to make that same escalation decision, it doesn't just need access to the data. It needs access to how that data was synthesized into a decision the last ten times. The precedents. The exceptions. The judgment calls.

That requires being in the write path, at commit time and in the execution loop. That's the only place you can capture the full context: what inputs were gathered, what policy applied, what exception was granted, who approved.

If you persist those traces, you get something we call a context graph (not embeddings), but more a structured record of how decisions were actually made, stitched across entities and time, so precedent becomes queryable.

The startups sitting in the agent orchestration layer are the ones positioned to build this. Not because they're smarter. Because they're in the right place architecturally.

A CISO watched their $50k pen test live yesterday
Pen tester got domain admin in 4 hours
Using Password123
The company spends $2M annually on 'advanced security.'
The CISO wanted to cry

The setup:
2-week pen test. $50k budget
CISO (Day 1): We've got this. We have great tools
CISO (4 hours later): Oh no

How they got destroyed:
Minute 32: Phishing email to HR → Credentials captured
CISO: "At least our controls will stop lateral movement"
Hour 2: Unpatched server exploited → Access to finance network
CISO: "Okay but they won't find admin creds"
Hour 4: Admin password in shared folder ("Admin_Password.txt")
→ Domain admin access
CISO: Screaming internally
Hour 6: Customer database exfiltrated → Game over

The debrief that hurt:
Pen tester: You have excellent security tools
CISO: So why did we fail so badly?
Pen tester: Because you're not using them. We didn't need zero-days. We used techniques from 2010. Everything we did should've been caught

The breakdown:
Sophisticated attacks: 0
Basic attacks: 23
Zero-days: 0
Unpatched vulns: 8
Advanced techniques: 0
Weak passwords & old accounts: Everything

What they HAD:
→ $200k EDR platform
→ $150k SIEM
→ $100k threat intelligence
→ $80k password policy tool
→ $50k DLP solution
Total: $2M security stack

What they DIDN'T have:
→ Consistent patching (200+ exceptions)
→ Enforced MFA (60% adoption)
→ Clean access control (admin accounts from 2019 active)
→ Followed password policy (Password123 was real)
→ Configured DLP (installed, never turned on)
Working fundamentals: $0

The board meeting:
Board: We invested $2M in security. How did this happen?
CISO: We bought expensive tools and ignored free basics
Board: Explain
CISO: We have $200k EDR that can't help against unpatched servers. We have password tools nobody uses. We have DLP we never configured
CISO: We can't buy our way out of lazy security

What they're fixing:
→ MFA mandatory (no exceptions)
→ Auto-patching (no more exceptions)
→ Access review (remove accounts not used in 90 days)
→ Password manager (mandatory)
→ Weekly control testing
Cost: $30k
Impact: Stopped the next pen test cold

The follow-up test (6 months later):
Same pen testers. Same techniques
Time to initial compromise: 3 days
Time to domain admin: Never
Critical findings: 3
What changed: Not their tools. Their fundamentals

The lesson:
Fancy tools without basics = expensive theater
$2M in advanced security < $30k in fundamentals

Before buying the NEXT shiny security tool:
Ask:
→ Are we patching everything?
→ Is MFA enforced everywhere?
→ Did we remove last year's accounts?
→ Are passwords actually strong?
→ Do we TEST our controls?
If "no" to ANY: Fix the basics first

Your expensive security stack is useless if attackers can get in with Password123
This CISO learned the hard way

When's the last time a pen test humbled you?
What basic thing did you miss?

The AI assistant Ad opportunity == Google Ads + Meta Ads

It's indisputable that OpenAI and Anthropic have an incredible advertising opportunity within ChatGPT and Claude respectively.

What is interesting that their ad systems will combine the best of both worlds and incorporate elements from the two largest and most powerful advertising systems today - Google and Facebook.

1. INTENT-DRIVEN ADS: Google search ads are based on the consumer's intent. A growing % of AI assistant queries are commercial queries, which can be cleanly monetized through search ads or affiliate links.

2. IDENTITY-DRIVEN ADS: Meta ads are based on the consumer's identity. Identity includes demographic signals (age, gender, etc), unexpressed identity markers (financial institutions that you belong to, restaurants you eat at, etc), and behaviors (websites you've visited, stores you have shopped at, etc). ChatGPT and Claude can both infer most of these signals from natural conversation patterns - writing style, topics of interest, technical expertise level, life stage indicators, and personal context shared over time. If you ask either AI assistant to list out all the things they know or have inferred about you, they can probably paint a very accurate picture. 

In fact, AI assistants go beyond traditional Google search and Meta behavior/identity, through RICH CONTEXT. Users share detailed information about their problems, goals, preferences, and circumstances in a way they never would in a search query or social media post. This creates opportunities for more relevant, less intrusive advertising that feels like genuine recommendations rather than interruptions.

tl;dr OpenAI and Anthropic can and will build $10-100B+ ad businesses each within 5-10 years. Ads will be the way they monetize free users (and probably some % of paid users too). As an advertising nerd, I'm excited to see these businesses come to fruition.

Every successful company has someone who knows where all the bodies are buried. They’re never the CEO.

They’re usually employee number 3-7. Old enough to have context, not senior enough to be political. They know why the CTO really left. 

They remember which feature almost killed the company. They can tell you the real reason you pivoted, not the TechCrunch version.

These people are organizational gold. They’re not founders, so they don’t need to maintain the mythology. 

They’re not executives, so they don’t have to protect anyone. 

They just remember everything and tell the truth about it.
I’ve watched companies lose these people and spend millions trying to recover what walked out the door. 
-> The weird pricing model nobody can explain. -> The integration that breaks if you touch it wrong. 
-> The customer who gets special treatment for reasons lost to time.

The smartest thing we did at Bland was identify these people early and make sure they knew they were valued. Not with titles—they usually don’t want them. But with respect, equity refreshers, and the knowledge that someone understands their real contribution.

They’re never in leadership meetings. They’re always in the kitchen during all-hands. But when production breaks at 3 AM, they’re the one who knows which undocumented setting from 2019 is the problem.


The institutional knowledge isn’t in your wiki. It’s in the head of someone who’s watched every iteration, every pivot, every near-death experience.


Finding them is easy. Keeping them is the hard part.

The fastest way to get fired from a leadership role is to keep hiring people who tell you the truth.


Every leader says they want radical candor. Direct feedback. No sugarcoating. Then someone actually does it—explains why the strategy won’t work, points out the obvious market dynamics everyone’s ignoring, says the product updates are meaningless—and suddenly they’re “not a culture fit.”


The rarest talent in any organization isn’t technical skill or strategic thinking. It’s the ability to say “this is a bad idea” without hedging, apologizing, or cushioning it with compliment sandwiches. These people are organizational gold. They save you from six-month detours and million-dollar mistakes.


Most leaders systematically eliminate them.
Watch what happens when someone gives a direct answer in a meeting. 

The room freezes. 

Side glances. Someone jumps in to “build on that” or “add nuance” or “provide additional context.” 

Translation: let me make this palatable so nobody feels bad.


The direct communicators get coached on “executive presence.” They’re told to “read the room” and “consider their delivery.” They learn to transform “this won’t work” into “I wonder if we might consider some alternative approaches that could potentially optimize our chances of success.”


By the time they’re fluent in corporate euphemism, they’re useless.


The best leaders I know collect truth-tellers like rare coins. They promote the engineer who says the architecture is garbage. They protect the salespeople who admit deals are dead. They treasure the finance person who calls out fantasy projections.


Everyone else builds organizations that run on pleasant fiction until reality shows up.
Who’s the last person you promoted for telling you something you didn’t want to hear?

My biggest takeaways from Ethan Smith on how to win at AEO (i.e. get ChatGPT to recommend your product):

1. Being mentioned most often beats ranking first. In Google, the #1 blue link wins. In ChatGPT, the answer summarizes multiple sources—so appearing in five citations beats ranking #1 in one. Ethan’s strategy: get mentioned on Reddit, YouTube, blogs, and affiliates. Volume of mentions matters more than any single placement.

2. LLM traffic converts 6x better than Google search traffic. Webflow saw this dramatic difference because users who come through AI assistants have built up much more intent through conversation and follow-up questions, making them highly qualified leads.

I had zero intention of ever writing this essay. But then a few weeks ago in a live event a participant asked me a question that hit like a ton of bricks… 🧱🧱

“I feel like product management as a profession is going through product market fit collapse. What do we do?”

😬 Oof. Immediately, others starting smashing the upvote button and the question rose to the top of the list.

I was surprised. I’ve seen tons of takes on this topic. But it was clear those takes have been creating more questions than answers.

Many others who have posted about this topic have analyzed it from the perspective on a task by task basis.

↳What tasks do product managers do today?
↳What can AI replace?
↳Is what is left over going to justify product managers?

This is incomplete and flawed thinking for a few reasons:

Not All Tasks Are Created Equal
There are things that great product managers do vs good/bad product managers do. Certain things, when done well, create outsized leverage and value. AI might automate 50% of the tasks but does it automate 50% of the value?

Assumes Jobs Are Static
The product manager role (like many others) has continued to evolve constantly over time. When we adopt new tools to automate some things, we tend to just find more things to fill that space with.

AI Enhances and Replaces
Those analyses also focus on the things that AI replaces. It leaves out the things that AI enhances.

Instead, here are the questions we should ask:

↳ What parts of product management get replaced? Are those low value or high value?
↳What parts of product management get enhanced? Are those low value or high value?
↳What happens with the new space that is created?
↳What actions should you take as a result?

This is the exact discussion I had with Fareed Mosavat and Shaun Clowes on the most recent episode of Unsolicited Feedback. Full episode and resulting essay below.

Full Essay -> https://lnkd.in/gWyHHRme

We come to some maybe surprising conclusions and talk about actionable steps we’d take depending on different situations.

7 Ways AI Is Redefining Customer Expectations (w/ tons of examples)…

I’m a huge believer in AI. As I wrote in AI Native Product Teams (link in comments), I believe AI is changing how product teams will think, work, and build.

But, I feel like a lot of the conversation is focused on the wrong thing. Many think they need to predict the future of what AI is capable of in order to build. But:

🧞‍♂️ No one can perfectly predict AI’s capabilities years from now
😤 Trying to leads to paralysis
🚂 You risk overbuilding speculative features

Trying to predict what AI might be able to do multiple years from now might be a fun exercise, but for many we should be focused on how AI is changing the expectations and needs of our customers.

If we don’t, we risk Product Market Fit Collapse (link in comments).

Here are 7 ways AI is shifting customer expectations with examples:

"A Place For Me To Create" → "Do The Work For Me"
AI is changing customer expectations from “give me a tool where I can create” to “do the work for me.” Examples: EvenUp, DevinAI, Midjourney, 11x, Artisan

"One Size, I Customize" → "Custom Made For Me"
Day AI is my fav example here. Using AI a Day takes your email, calendar, and answers to a one-page questionnaire to automatically generate a CRM that is tailored to your business.

"I Expect To Wait" → "I Expect It Now"
AI is also changing the expectation from customers at how fast and convenient you deliver the value. In other words, users are getting “lazier.” (like we weren’t lazy enough already!) Examples: GitHub CoPilot, Intercom Fin, ChatGPT (homework)

“I’ll do the busy work” → “The busy work is done for me”
Many products require you to perform a lot of manual tasks in order for the team to get value out of it. But most of the time value out < effort to put in. Many are automating this busy work around the real work: Abridge, Anterior, Reforge Insight Analytics, Tana, Truewind, Otter.ai

“I’ll Pay Per Seat” → “I’ll Pay For Output”
Kyle Polar has written about this. Instead of paying for an approximation of value (per seat) customers are starting to pay for work delivered. Examples: EvenUp, Intercom, Synthesia, Clay, Copy.ai, Imagen

“The tool has no context” → “The tool can see what I’m doing”
This is one of the coolest IMO that is being under-leveraged. Most software has no idea what you are doing outside of their app. With AI, it can see/understand your screen to create new value. Examples: Gemini Live, Reforge AI Extension, Squint

"I'll Learn This Interface" → "The Interface Adapts To Me"
Today every time a user adopts a new product they need to learn the UI to get value. AI is able to create a dynamic experience designed for the intent. Examples: Gemini, Perplexity

Two strikingly similar headlines surfaced this past week that should make every leader pause:
 • “Companies Are Pouring Billions Into A.I. It Has Yet to Pay Off.” — New York Times
 • “Companies Are Pouring Billions Into AI. Here’s Why They’re Not Seeing Returns” — Forbes

The NYT points to the human side: employees resist tools they don’t trust. Forbes focuses on the technical side: most AI still can’t understand the context of work.

Both are true, and they’re related. When AI lacks context, employees lose trust. It can’t tell the latest doc from last year’s draft. It summarizes a customer conversation but drops the follow-ups buried in the thread. It pulls a response from Slack while ignoring the context in Google Drive.

Employees realize it creates more work than it saves, and stop using it. Pilots stall, deployments fade, and projects slide into the “trough of disillusionment" as the NYT describes. Unfortunately, that's the reality for many organizations.

At Glean, we work hard to make sure AI understands the enterprise context the way a human does. If a subject matter expert says something, I trust it more. If something’s old, I double-check it. That’s how people think, and it’s how AI should work too.

Yet every enterprise has its own documentation culture and quirks, so sometimes we struggle at first. But we persist and co-develop with customers until the system reaches the quality they need. Then we take those learnings to make it work automatically for the next customer.

We’ve seen this approach deliver measurable impact for customers:
 • Booking.com: Glean Agents give teams faster access to customer insights, cutting video production time by 75% and doubling monthly output.
 • Confluent: Glean’s AI-powered search saves 15,000+ hours/month, boosts support satisfaction by 13%, and cuts ticket investigation time by 10 minutes.
 • Fortune 100 telecom company: Glean surfaces instant knowledge during support calls, reducing call resolution time by 17 seconds across 800+ agents.
 • Leading global consultancy: Glean Agents automate RFP workflows, cutting consulting project proposals from 4 weeks to a few hours (97% faster).
 • Wealthsimple: Glean gives employees instant access to policies and knowledge, driving $1M+ in annual productivity gains.

When AI understands the real context of work—across people, tools, and workflows— employees trust it and use it. Instead of falling into the trough of disillusionment, companies climb a slope toward productivity gains and real ROI.

AI that works should handle your data, not the other way around.

I’ve heard many CIOs and business leaders say they need to "fix their data problem" before they can make AI work. That’s what they’ve been told by other AI vendors that don’t work, with products that struggle with data complexity and offload the burden onto organizations instead of solving it.

But here’s the reality: AI is supposed to handle messy, complex, inherited, real-world data just like a human would.

If you give someone three documents with conflicting information, they don’t need them to be perfectly structured or pre-cleaned to understand what’s correct. They will look at 𝒄𝒐𝒏𝒕𝒆𝒙𝒕 – who wrote them, who the experts are, which document is most recent, and whether they can verify the source code to see what’s actually happening. A good AI product should do the same.

AI shouldn’t force humans to manually structure, clean, or pre-process data before it can deliver value. That’s the opposite of why we started building AI in the first place.

Too many enterprises believe they need to spend years restructuring their data before unlocking AI’s power. That’s not true. The AI era can start now. 

The most difficult part of making AI work in the enterprise is connecting and understanding your enterprise data, and that’s the role Glean plays. 

Glean enables companies to adopt AI immediately (and see real outcomes) with the data they already have. Our platform indexes information across every system – documents, conversations, and even acronyms – so that AI can reason over it in context. It integrates seamlessly with existing tools, without disrupting operations. 

And it delivers the right information at the right time, without forcing employees to sift through irrelevant or outdated results.

For too long, enterprises have been told that AI won’t work unless they first fix their data problem. That’s backward. The best AI is built to handle complexity, not avoid it.

I Am Voicepilled. A major step forward in human–computer interaction won’t come from bigger models alone, but from how we talk to them. Natively. With our voices. 

And that's why if you haven't started communicating with ChatGPT and other AI models simply by speaking to it, you should. It might change how you prompt in general. 

What does “voicepilled” mean exactly? Per The Matrix, it's that moment of sudden mental clarity, a way of seeing the world differently once you've taken "the red pill” or “blue pill."

Being “voicepilled” is that moment of realization that once you start seriously using your voice to interact with technology, you unlock a new way to amplify your ability. If you use products like Wispr or ChatGPT Voice, you know what I mean.

Human-computer interaction has always been a blend of art, psychology, and physiology. When DARPA first sponsored Douglas Engelbart’s team to create the mouse, it was a reimagining of what computing could feel like. 
Voice user interfaces do the same. We can use them to send quicker texts to friends, to query AI models, and eventually to interface with a Waymo as it takes you to your destination. 

Will people abandon keyboards completely? Not if they spend a lot of their time working with spreadsheets, writing complex documents, working with imaging and video-editing software, or love communicating with GIFs and emojis.

But for many everyday purposes, voice is simply faster, more natural, and more flexible than typing. And what’s changed now is that state-of-the-art AI models can genuinely process what we say. 

This in turn can change how we prompt models like ChatGPT. Voice is iterative by default. You stumble, you rephrase, you interrupt yourself. You can do unstructured brain dumps: “just take this picture, and you figure it out.”

So voice input can be a powerful antidote to perfectionism. It teaches you that iteration is the workflow. That the value is not in getting it right once, but in getting it better, repeatedly. And once the iteration stabilizes, you can simply say: “Take this discussion and generate a reusable prompt for me.”

For companies, voice creates richer signals about what their users want. A shopping platform that receives a voice query gets more information about what the customer is searching for, and can deliver better results leading to increased sales. 

Voice might even reshape hardware and the architecture of work. Right now, sitting next to someone dictating all day feels unusual. But as voice becomes primary, we may design office spaces knowing that most of our time will be spent in dialogue with our workstations, versus typing on them. 

Every major leap in computing has arrived not just from smarter machines, but from better interfaces. As AI models scale, one of the most profound changes will be the fact that everyone can now interact with them in the most human way possible. 

To be voicepilled is to glimpse this future.

“Art is by humans, for humans, about being human.” That’s how Stephen Colbert put it when we discussed what makes entertainment legendary on Possible.

He made an important distinction: ideas, in isolation, are constructs. What makes art resonate isn’t just the idea itself, but the way another human being receives it. That human presence is what transforms an idea into something that makes us feel.

In a time when AI can generate text, images, and even performances that look remarkably convincing, Colbert suggested there may always be a “micronutrient” missing when we interact with AIs. 

The awkwardness of a conversation that doesn’t go quite right. The disappointment of a friend who can’t give you the comfort you hoped for. Even the judgment we fear when we open up to another person. Something intangible that exists only within interactions –– conversations, performances, etc –– that are human-to-human. 

These imperfections are not flaws to be designed away. They’re part of the fabric of human connection.

That “MIT study” on 95% of AI pilots failing keeps being circulated with increasingly dramatic headlines and over-the-top conclusions.

Just a reminder that the methodology (see the images below) doesn’t allow general conclusions about failure rate. The failure numbers seem to come from 52 convenience interviews of executives who were asked three questions whether they have scaled AI pilots, with vague methods about how this was measured. This tells us very little we can widely generalize about what is happening with pilots, and certainly doesn't mean that 95% of AI efforts are failing.

And, more importantly, I would expect a large number of early AI pilots to fail. You are figuring out how to do ambitious things by doing pilots, and figuring things out involves trial and error! That is why they are pilots.

After reading it, this does seem like a big deal as the first benchmark to measure economically valuable AI work & show how much they can do.

The methodology had industry experts outline important, real-world, hard tasks for AI to do. Other experts were asked to do the tasks themselves & yet others experts graded human & AI output, as well as training an AI to do evaluation. These tasks have real-world relevance and economic impact: they averaged 7 hours of expert work to do.

Models approached parity with humans, and AI is getting better fast, with larger models and models with more reasoning getting closest to human level. (But note tasks are not jobs, and this does not mean AI can just sub in for humans). It also suggested that as of right now an approach where experts try AI first, and fix bad output is a good way to work.

A sign of a company being advanced in AI adoption was that they built their own internal chatbot (usually called "CompanyNameGPT") using APIs.

As the major lab's chatbots become agentic, bringing together many tools in a single interface & as they add memory & projects, the custom API chatbots are falling behind. They may become a liability that holds back the companies that were fast movers.

In recent talks with companies about AI two reports keep coming up: the one that says Reddit is the key source of AI data (it isn't & that was written by an SEO company for marketing reasons) and the "MIT says 95% of AI pilots fail" thing (which seems to be based on 52 interviews at a conference & has very unclear methodology about what they actually did)*.

There is a lot of bad information out there.

* I am sure lots of pilots do fail, especially as people should be experimenting with novel uses of AI, but there really is no way to understand how often failure happens or why based on the approach the report took to data collection.

I'll note again that it seems nuts that, despite every AI lab launching a half-dozen new products, nobody is doing anything with GPTs, including OpenAI.

When I talk to people at companies, this is still the way non-technical people share prompts on teams. But there has been no big change in GPTs in 2 years 

Its fine if it turns out that GPTs/Gems/whatever aren't the future, but it seems important to roll out something else that makes sharing prompts useful across teams and organizations. Prompt libraries are still being used, and they are still awkward cut-and-paste things.

I feel like the AI companies have the idea that AI chatbot use is something that should primarily be handled by IT & coders. But end-users are the real innovators. Approaches that make building, testing, & sharing AI uses in organizations more accessible make a big difference.

There’s going to be split between two types of teams or companies for the foreseeable future. 

Those that re-engineer their processes to take full advantage of AI agents with their given limitations, and those that wait until they’re good enough to not re-engineer anything.

To take full advantage of AI agents today, your workflows must be designed around the idea that AI agents need a lot context to be effective. By default you have a super-intelligent worker but they have no idea who they work for, what their job is, what the best practices are, what the guidelines are, how to work with the right data, and so on. 

Most AI agent failures are just wishing this wasn’t true, and imagining AI will just figure all of these things out on its own. This won’t happen anytime soon for a variety of reasons. 

The companies and teams that retool their workflows to get agents the right context will be ones that actually can get the most gains from agents right now.

But this will look very different from how most teams work right now. It will mean having well documented processes, data that is set up to actually get to an agent easily, hyper precise goals and prompts, and ultimately mindset that the new human in the loop element is not being involved in every single step of an agent, but editing and reviewing its final output.

The companies and teams that started thinking this way will be able to take advantage of agents right away, and they’ll blow past the ones that don’t.

Product management for AI agents is easily the wildest form of product management in history. 

Typical product management is trying to figure out how to design interfaces and software for people to interact with deterministic systems. The user generally knows all the context to do their work successfully, so it’s generally a matter of nailing the underlying business logic and surrounding UX. 

But with AI agents, the user you care about most is the agent, and they don’t know anything by default. They’ll happily run in any direction to perform the task, often without success.

So as a PM (or engineer) you basically spend your time trying to reverse engineer “what would a human need as context to perform this task”, and then figure out how to design systems to get the agent that data in the right sequence, with the right tools, and instructions.

Some of these systems are entire invisible to the human user, but part of the craft is equally how the end-user will interact with the agent to supply this context. Then, it’s often unending trial and error working to eke out incremental points of quality at each stage. 

This is especially why people with deep domain expertise, or those that can acquire it quickly, will do extremely well building AI agents. The ability to anticipate the context that the agent would need to be successful is a huge determining factor in how effective the agent will be.

This partly explains why coding agents have worked so well out of the gate; because its builders deeply understand the domain that they’re working to automate. But clearly we’re going to quickly see this same outcome across every field - legal, healthcare, finance, etc. - as context engineering and a new crop of product managers emerge.

There’s going to be a huge gap in execution velocity for the foreseeable future between the individuals, teams, and companies that adapt (or create for the first time) their workflows to work with AI agents vs. those that don’t. 

In the past few months I’ve talked with a number of new startup founders who are operating in a totally different way than the rest of the world right now.

Most of their work, particularly in engineering to start with, is oriented around how to make agents effective. There’s a focus on hyper specific prompting, a bigger orientation around getting specs perfectly right, running many agents in the background in parallel, focusing on code reviews vs. coding, and a bunch of other new workflow practices are actually what it takes to make agents work at scale.

And while coding is ahead of the curve right now in agentic workflows, it’s clear this pattern will start to emerge across most other domains over time. Leverage is going to show up everywhere: the ability to generate 10X more marketing output, process deals way faster due to automated legal workflows, handle customer support and success operations in faster ways, and so on. 

The lesson here is that these teams just tend to be far more ambitious in what they push agents to do. Most existing teams and companies will be happy about incremental gains and stop short of doing the really big changes in how they work. And doing so is going to provide at least a temporary advantage to those that adapt to these news ways of working.

Lots of conversation on what the future of software looks like in the enterprise. Here’s how I think it plays out.

For deterministic workflows where the cost of getting something wrong is high, enterprises will have a tendency to pick core platforms for their most common, important, and repeatable functions in the organization. Think payroll, ERP, CRM, ITSM, customer support, ECM/document management, and so on. These are areas where you want something done the same way, every time.

Each of these platforms will have to be AI-first by design, which means that they will have user interfaces that become tuned for interacting with the workflows and data via AI, and be fully designed for AI agents to operate in the platforms. Over time, we can expect usage on these systems to bias far more to AI agents than even people. The seat model remains for the users, but consumption becomes the model for agents. Some incumbents will make it to the end state, but others will not adapt quickly enough and die off.

There will then be a new crop of effectively Agent-only companies that are purpose-built for automating specific types of work (and especially for non-deterministic work). Their business models will tilt even more consumption. Think Claude Code or Devins (likely with some UI layer for managing the agents) but for various job functions. We will likely see hundreds or thousands of these emerge over time. Pen testing, coding, bug finding, compliance reviews, financial analysts, and so on. This is a huge space where startups will do quite well because there will tend to not be any software incumbents in these categories.

We will interact with these various agents from a mix of the software platforms that they are tied to (like Box AI, or Agentforce), via APIs in other systems, and horizontal workflows systems that stitch together agents across platforms (like ServiceNow, IBM Watsonx, Google Agentspace, and so on).

And of course, users will often consume these agents via horizontal chat experiences (like ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Grok, Copilot, etc.) via MCP or other types of direct connections. Users will commonly work in these horizontal chat systems, pulling in the agents, data, and workflows from the various Agentic platforms as needed. When relevant, l they will hop into the core platforms to complete workflows, review information, etc.

There will also be a longtail of experiences where users can generate micro apps on the fly when they need quick applications or use cases automated, when there’s no obvious piece of software to do that with. I’d expect this is more for the power users where they need glue between multiple systems or where no software yet exists.

The net of it is that software becomes only more important over time, even if the modalities where we’re interacting from change and expand. Similar to how we hop between our phones and desktop computers with ease, the future will offer a mix of ways of interacting with software.

AI Agents can’t fix everything — not every task needs one

Identifying the right stage is what unlocks real value...

Multiple reports suggest the issue isn’t AI workflows themselves, but how people design them.

Often, you don’t need a complex agentic system for simple tasks like summarizing HR documents.

That’s why it’s crucial to pick the right solution instead of chasing the biggest one.

📌 To make it clearer, let’s walk through the 5 stages:
1. Script Chatbots

- Human Dependency (~90–80%): Almost fully dependent on humans to script every single response.
- Autonomy: No real intelligence — purely rule-based workflows.
- Scalability: Scales linearly, but only for repetitive, predictable tasks.
- Use Case: Simple automations like email replies, FAQs, or support ticket routing.

2. LLM Chatbots

- Human Dependency (~70–60%): Reduced, but still needs supervision.
- Autonomy: Contextual understanding with natural conversations — but no planning ability.
- Scalability: Expands easily for large customer support operations.
- Use Case: Customer-facing chatbots that can hold human-like conversations, but can’t take autonomous action.

3. Modern RPA

- Human Dependency (~50–40%): Handles repeated, structured tasks with less manual input.
- Autonomy: Contextual but still repetitive — can trigger scripts and execute tools when prompted.
Scalability: Great for high-volume, process-driven workflows.
- Use Case: Hiring document processing, invoice scanning, compliance checks.

4. Single Agentic AI

- Human Dependency (~30–20%): Agents plan, use tools, and incorporate feedback with limited supervision.
- Autonomy: Adaptive reasoning within a defined scope — memory + planning + tool use.
- Scalability: Dynamic scaling for dedicated enterprise use-cases.
- Use Case: Smart document retrieval, enterprise knowledge Q&A, semi-autonomous research.

5. Multi-Agentic AI

- Human Dependency (~15–10%): Agents coordinate among themselves, requiring minimal human input.
- Autonomy: Dynamic, multi-workflow execution with cross-agent collaboration.
- Scalability: Designed for complex, large-scale enterprise automation.
- Use Case: Interconnected coding agents, enterprise-wide orchestration, cross-department AI systems.

📌 The big takeaway: Those reports are right, which state that the problem is not models or workflows - it is people who are implementing them.

In Week 1 of my latest cohort, I discussed why complex workflows are not always the solution and why choosing the right type of architecture is needed.

This is because not every task needs a complex system, sometimes simpler approaches are more effective.

Automated software testing is growing in importance in the era of AI-assisted coding. Agentic coding systems accelerate development but are also unreliable. Agentic testing — where you ask AI to write tests and check your code against them — is helping. Automatically testing infrastructure software components that you intend to build on top of is especially helpful and results in more stable infrastructure and less downstream debugging.

Software testing methodologies such as Test Driven Development (TDD), a test-intensive approach that involves first writing rigorous tests for correctness and only then making progress by writing code that passes those tests, are an important way to find bugs. But it can be a lot of work to write tests. (I personally never adopted TDD for that reason.) Because AI is quite good at writing tests, agentic testing enjoys growing attention.

First, coding agents do misbehave! My teams use them a lot, and we have seen:
- Numerous bugs introduced by coding agents, including subtle infrastructure bugs that take humans weeks to find.
- A security loophole that was introduced into our production system when a coding agent made password resets easier to simplify development.
- Reward hacking, where a coding agent modified test code to make it easier to pass the tests.
- An agent running "rm *.py" in the working directory, leading to deletion of all of a project's code (which, fortunately, was backed up on github).

In the last example, when pressed, the agent apologized and agreed “that was an incredibly stupid mistake.” This made us feel better, but the damage had already been done!

I love coding agents despite such mistakes and see them making us dramatically more productive. To make them more reliable, I’ve found that prioritizing where to test helps.

I rarely write (or direct an agent to write) extensive tests for front-end code. If there's a bug, hopefully it will be easy to see and also cause little lasting damage. For example, I find generated code’s front-end bugs, say in the display of information on a web page, relatively easy to find. When the front end of a web site looks wrong, you’ll see it immediately, and you can tell the agent and have it iterate to fix it. (A more advanced technique: Use MCP to let the agent integrate with software like Playwright to automatically take screenshots, so it can autonomously see if something is wrong and debug.)

In contrast, back-end bugs are harder to find. I’ve seen subtle infrastructure bugs — for example, one that led to a corrupted database record only in certain corner cases — that took a long time to find. Putting in place rigorous tests for your infrastructure code might help spot these problems earlier and save you many hours of challenging debugging.
