Death by a thousand dashboards

Dashboards were supposed to be our saviors, promising the professionalism of a plane cockpit with clean gauges, clear dials, and a single view of what’s going on. One glance, and you’d know if your product or business was flying smoothly.

That was the dream.

The reality is the feeling of being trapped in a control room with 10,000 blinking screens. Instead of clarity, we’ve ended up with chaos.

Here’s what life in dashboard hell actually looks like:
1. Conflicting truths. You open five dashboards and get five different numbers for the same metric. Which one do you believe?
2. Endless scavenger hunts. To answer a simple question, you have to stitch together views from multiple dashboards. If you want to understand whether weekly active users are growing among U.S. iOS users who are male and between 13–17 years old, you might need four separate dashboards. There’s no fast way to drill down.
3. Surface, not depth. Dashboards often tell you what happened, but not why. Metrics go up or down, but the root cause stays hidden.
4. Cognitive overload. The sheer number of dashboards creates noise. Teams spend more time trawling for useful signals than making decisions.
5. Lack of trust. With no single source of truth, dashboards stop being instruments of confidence and start being sources of debate.

The answer isn’t “just one more dashboard,” it’s a reinvention of how insights and analysis are told, not just shown. Dashboards need to stop being static grids of KPIs and start becoming living narratives that answer questions.

Your 'lack of focus' is actually your greatest career asset.

The fastest path to success is embracing your generalist nature. Develop many interests, get good at different things, then build a career that leverages this diverse skill set.

This advice counters conventional wisdom, which makes it particularly powerful.

Most people think they need to find a specific “track” to follow, driving them towards deep specialization. Eventually, though, you need to become great at many different disciplines. For instance, a product leader needs to understand the features, GTM, data/analytics, competitive positioning. And 80% of the job is working cross-functionally with engineers, designers, marketing.

This is not to say that you should be mediocre at a lot of things. You need to great at most things and have several ‘spikes’ where you are genuinely world-class. 

At most companies, there’s a natural ceiling to specialization. Specialists become valuable individual contributors but rarely break into leadership roles.

Instead, if you’re a generalist you can bop around to multiple disciplines, learn them, and eventually run the department. You also have more likelihood of being a great founder, switching fields, or being resilient to new trends.

Specialists get stuck when industries evolve. Generalists learn fast and thrive.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang’s best career advice: “Passion isn’t enough, you’ve got to endure.”


I believe "Follow your passion” is the most overhyped career advice on earth
If someone’s telling you to “just follow your passion,” they’re probably already living in abundance!

Here’s the uncomfortable truth:
Passion usually follows mastery, not the other way around.
 
Get good first. Then the prestige, pay, confidence, and interesting problems make you… passionate.

A better playbook that I followed:
Pick something you can be great at. One clear lane, real demand.

Go deep for a decade. Reps > inspiration. Grit beats vibes.

Measure progress, not feelings. Hard day ≠ wrong path. 
Work is hard, expect injustice, friction, and boredom.

Earn the right to edit. Mastery buys you optionality: interesting projects, better teams, better life.

Early on, balance is a tradeoff. Most meaningful careers require a season of asymmetric effort.

 Later, mastery lets you buy back balance, time, control, boundaries.
Do your passions on weekends


Don’t ask: “Do I love this today?”

Ask: “Can I become great at this, and is it worth being great at?”
That’s how you build a career you’re proud of and yes, one you might just become passionate about.

After 6 months of using AI coding tools (Cursor and Claude) almost every day, and not just for "toy apps" but actually making things that I have subsequently deployed, I can say a few things:
1. It's not just hype. If you are unable to generate a competent full-stack application or microservice with AI coding tools, you're almost certainly using the tools incorrectly.
2. Coding LLMs are working with *small context sizes* compared to the overall challenge space of keeping tens of thousands of lines of code working from commit to commit. You MUST have the AI write tests for everything it generates. You MUST tag every working checkpoint so that it (the AI) can compare working with non-working.
3. When you tell an AI tool to "plan", you are working a different part of the model - or even potentially a completely different model - when you are telling it to "execute". "Deep thinking" models are better at analyzing code and planning than they are at executing. Sometimes even "dumb" models are better at executing to a plan than the smart ones, and they are certainly CHEAPER.
4. You MUST break planning from execution, just like you would do if you were writing the code yourself, and you must have the AI write planning files that it can follow. If you or your devs are executing plans with the most expensive models, you're almost certainly just wasting money.
5. You must AUDIT the code for every feature cycle. My "flow" is basically:
* Plan (write this as a .md file, save in plans/ directory)
* Execute (write the code)
* Write tests
* Run tests
* Re-execute as necessary until tests pass
* Audit - check the code against Plan.md

You also don't need a lot of fancy prompts to do any of this. You can literally write out your high-level goals and then have the tool write the plan, then read the plan back to you, correct its assumptions, then proceed with steps 2-6.

The results will surprise you. GIGO applies to AI coding just as much as everything else. If you are sloppy and undisciplined in how you do it, you'll get predictably bad results.

All data teams regularly face the same fork in the road: Build a dashboard, or ship a workflow? 
There’s only one right answer. ALWAYS pick the workflow.

On paper, dashboards look like progress. They're visual, polished, and check the "data visibility" box. In the right context, they have their place. But in practice, they get misread, ignored, or relegated to someone’s bookmark graveyard. 

I can count on one hand the number of times I've seen a dashboard pulled up in an executive meeting. People forget to check dashboards and eventually move on. Building a dashboard hoping it will drive decisions is often low or even negative ROI.

Real leverage comes from workflows that don’t rely on someone checking a chart. The best ones don’t even need a human in the loop. Think fraud detection: If your workflow can automatically flag and block suspicious activity, that’s direct business value, no dashboard required.

Put simply: Dashboards are great for measuring the impact of a workflow or process, but workflows make it happen. The business needs things automated and that's the surest way to actually have an impact as a data team.

It’s not about flooding people with data, but about surfacing the right data at the right time to actually influence decisions:

– A rep gets a risk alert in Slack before the deal slips
– A PM gets a user drop-off spike in Sheets while prepping their roadmap
– An ops team gets automated metrics piped into the tools they already live in

This is why we see so many of our best Fabi.ai users skip the "build a dashboard" step entirely. They go straight to automation, context, and delivery.

Dashboards aren't dead, but the ROI is fickle and depends on stakeholders remembering to visit the dashboard and then taking action.

Tesla's Ex-Director of AI Andrej Karpathy dropped the bombshell about AI Agents!

He said: AI agents like Claude or Codex are still far from acting as real “employees.” They lack memory, multimodality, and true learning. 

Fixing that will take about a decade, not because it’s impossible, but because deep intelligence just takes time to build.

--

What this means for builders:

You’ll win by shipping systems, not chasing sentience.

1) Build workflows, not ‘workers’
Think pipelines, not people. Chain tools and decisions that produce reliable outcomes.

2) Small agents > grand agents
Design 3–5 step micro-agents that do one job 10x better:

Ingest → ground facts → act → verify → deliver

Add a review step (auto or human) before you call it done.

3) Thin wrappers can be powerful
“Thin” doesn’t mean trivial. Look at Perplexity: a lean UX + retrieval + strong ranking loop = massive utility.

Your wrapper = orchestration + guardrails + clarity, not just a chat box.

4) UX is the moat
Cursor showed that great process + interface beats raw model magic:

Tight context controls, multi-file edits, agentic actions, visible plans

Reliable outputs people trust > clever demos they don’t adopt

5) Memory, but make it practical
Until long-term learning is native, use working memory:

Task files, run manifests, audit logs, and checklists the agent reads/writes

Treat memory like a shared workspace, not a black box

✅A simple playbook to ship this week:

 A. Frame a single high-value workflow
 Example: “Weekly customer health summary” or “Invoice QA before payout.”

 B. Chain 3–5 steps
 Ground: fetch sources (CRM, tickets, docs)
 Synthesize: produce structured draft (JSON/Markdown)
 Validate: fact checks, schema checks, policy checks
 Repair: auto-fix common failures (missing owner/dates)
 Output: send to Slack/Email/Notion with next actions

 C. Add thin-but-sharp UX
 Clear inputs, visible plan, one-click approve, one-click revise
 Show your work: sources, diffs, and confidence notes

 D. Measure what matters
 First-try-right %, handle time, rework %, user saves/edits, incident rate
 Promote versions that beat baseline, retire the rest

Common traps (skip these):
 “General agent” fantasies (do everything, does nothing well)
 Endless chats with no definition of done
 Hidden memory (no artifacts, no auditability)
 Evaluating with vibes, not metrics


We don’t need artificial employees to create value today.
We need well-designed workflows, small dependable agents, and great UX.

10:1. That's the ratio of my Harvard MBA class in tech who chose product roles over marketing roles. And it's killing startups in the age of AI.

I'll be honest — I was almost part of that statistic. Even with my Bain and VC background, I spent years avoiding the 'marketing' title, opting for 'growth' instead. Like somehow that made me more strategic.

I've been in rooms where founders pitch their "growth strategy" for 30 minutes. They say things like "We need to figure out distribution." 

What they're saying is literally... marketing. Content, ads, PR, events, community. But heaven forbid we call it that. Why?

For the last decade, tech's brightest minds chose product over marketing. The result? Marketing missed out on a generation of strategic thinkers who could have transformed the function.

But AI is flipping the script.
With the rise of vibe-coding, building is becoming commoditized. Getting anyone to care about what you built? That's the new moat. And suddenly, every founder desperately needs "distribution."

All those essays on how "taste" will win? You guessed it, that's another way of saying brand and design.

What every founder should be doing to build up strong 'distribution moats' (aka marketing teams):

**Have high expectations of your marketing team.** Give marketers real P&L ownership, and EXPECT your marketers to know their metrics as well or better than your PMs. If they can't tell you their CAC:LTV ratio or revenue per campaign, they're not thinking strategically enough. Stop asking for vanity metrics and start measuring revenue impact.

**Hire differently.** I've seen ex-consultants and former founders crush it in marketing roles. They bring the strategic thinking the function desperately needs.

**Kill the euphemisms.** Call it marketing. Own it. The best talent sees through "Head of Growth" anyway. GTM Engineering is what you should have expected out of Marketing Ops for the past decade. Be proud of building the engine that actually drives revenue.

The irony? The companies that figure this out first will win the AI era. Everyone else will wonder why their perfect product has zero customers. 😇

I recently spoke with a product leader who runs a deceptively simple PM interview to evaluate just one thing: curiosity. Most people fail.

They present candidates with a real problem their team is facing, then say just one thing: "Your job isn't to solve it. Your job is to ask me questions."

That's the entire exercise.

It's designed to test curiosity by seeing how well someone gathers context before jumping to solutions.

And almost no one passes it.

Most candidates can't stay in inquiry mode for long. They start describing what they'd ask instead of actually... asking it.

Or they immediately start pitching solutions without understanding the problem.

And almost no one asks the most revealing question of all: "What have you already tried?"

This product leader isn't screening for intelligence or creativity. They're screening for something much rarer: the ability to slow down and think, "What do I need to know before deciding what to do?"

The candidates who fail this screen are probably super smart and capable. But they don't spike on curiosity.

They can't resist the urge to show how smart they are by solving problems quickly.

The ones who pass understand that asking great questions is more valuable in the eyes of this hiring manager than generating smart answers.

And if you're a hiring manager, consider adding a curiosity screen to your process. I think this is truly one of the most important traits of top 1% PMs.

It tells you more about how someone will operate in ambiguity than any behavioral question ever could.

The best product managers aren't the ones with all the answers. They're the ones who know which questions to ask.

I laughed at them. Then went on a rant.

The fastest way to tell leadership you don’t know what you’re doing is to plan 52 work items, knowing that more than half will not get done.

None of them had time estimates.

Just a pile of work.

Meanwhile, we had real fires burning.
Major issues that actually mattered.
They might’ve been somewhere in the 52… but who could tell?

That’s the point.
When everything matters, nothing does.
It was a pile of distractions dressed up as a plan.

A good leader doesn’t measure success by how much work is listed.
They measure it by whether the right work gets done.

Stop pretending 52 things matter.
Pick the few that truly do.
Then finish them. 
Then repeat.

Software engineering planning is that simple... if you make it that simple.

There is a reason FOCUS is one of the key pieces of the Product Driven model in my book.

Interviewing software engineers is easier than most people think.

Describe a real business problem.
Then ask: How would you solve it?

A CTO will ask about alignment with company strategy, trade-offs between speed and cost, risks, and how the decision impacts hiring and future roadmaps.

A senior engineer will focus on architecture, reliability, testability, edge cases, and how to deliver value quickly while leaving room to evolve.

A junior engineer will ask where the requirements are, which framework to use, or for an example to follow.

Same question.
Radically different answers.

At Full Scale, this is how we interview engineers.
Because in the era of AI, coding tests don’t prove much anymore.
The only thing that matters is how someone thinks and solves problems.

Software engineering is mostly about two universal truths.

Kidlin’s Law: If you can write the problem down clearly, the matter is half solved.

Pareto’s Law: 80% of outcomes come from 20% of causes.

Every project I’ve ever seen succeed did two things well:

1. Defined the problem in plain language before jumping into code.

2. Focused energy on the 20% of features, bugs, or technical debt that actually drove 80% of the impact.

We love complexity. But clarity + focus beats complexity every time.

If your team struggles with velocity, don’t ask for more hours. Ask if the problem is clearly written down. Ask if you’re chasing the right 20%.

Because software engineering isn’t just about code. It’s about solving the right problems, in the simplest way possible.

"AI is eliminating junior roles but we still need senior people."

As a junior dev today, do you:

🧠 Write everything manually to truly learn (but fall behind on productivity)?

But here's what AI CAN'T do:

"People talk about 'vibe coding' something over the weekend. You vibe coded a simple function that doesn't actually solve big complex problems."

The first few years of engineering used to be an apprenticeship. Learning the language. Building habits.

But if AI is writing all the junior-level code, who's doing the apprenticing?

This is going to bite us. Hard.

If you're a junior dev, how are you navigating this? If you're senior, how are you thinking about training the next generation?

AI is slowing down our product team.

Not because the tech isn’t useful.
But because we’ve started using it for the wrong things.

My product manager used to write simple, clear requirements.
Now they spend hours generating hyper-detailed mockups and polished prototypes. 

Most of them get thrown away.

It’s easy to fall in love with the tools.
To play with them.
To convince yourself you’re being productive because something looks good.

But like any tool, you have to ask:

Is there a real ROI here, or are we just polishing artifacts that never get used?

Instead of empowering engineers with a goal and a conversation, we’re spoon-feeding them pixel-perfect specs they don’t even need.

If your product team is using AI tools to overcompensate for weak engineers, you’ve got the wrong engineers.

And if they’re doing it because they think engineers need that level of direction, you’ve got the wrong mindset.

Sometimes the best “prototype” is a five-minute call.
A whiteboard.
A Slack message that says:

“Here’s the goal. Here’s what matters. Go make it great.”

AI isn’t the problem.

But using AI to overengineer clarity instead of trusting your team?

That’s what’s slowing you down.

There is significant unmet demand for developers who understand AI. At the same time, because most universities have not yet adapted their curricula to the new reality of programming jobs being much more productive with AI tools, there is also an uptick in unemployment of recent CS graduates.

When I interview AI engineers — people skilled at building AI applications — I look for people who can:
- Use AI assistance to rapidly engineer software systems
- Use AI building blocks like prompting, RAG, evals, agentic workflows, and machine learning to build applications
- Prototype and iterate rapidly

Someone with these skills can get a massively greater amount done than someone who writes code the way we did in 2022, before the advent of Generative AI. I talk to large businesses every week that would love to hire hundreds or more people with these skills, as well as startups that have great ideas but not enough engineers to build them. As more businesses adopt AI, I expect this talent shortage only to grow! At the same time, recent CS graduates face an increased unemployment rate, though the underemployment rate — of graduates doing work that doesn’t require a degree — is still lower than for most other majors. This is why we hear simultaneously anecdotes of unemployed CS graduates and also of rising salaries for in-demand AI engineers.

When programming evolved from punchcards to keyboard and terminal, employers continued to hire punchcard programmers for a while. But eventually, all developers had to switch to the new way of coding. AI engineering is similarly creating a huge wave of change.

There is a stereotype of “AI Native” fresh college graduates who outperform experienced developers. There is some truth to this. Multiple times, I have hired, for full-stack software engineering, a new grad who really knows AI over an experienced developer who still works 2022-style. But the best developers I know aren’t recent graduates (no offense to the fresh grads!). They are experienced developers who have been on top of changes in AI. The most productive programmers today deeply understand computers, how to architect software, and how to make complex tradeoffs — and who additionally are familiar with cutting-edge AI tools.

Sure, some skills from 2022 are becoming obsolete. For example, a lot of coding syntax that we had to memorize back then is no longer important, since we no longer need to code by hand as much. But even if, say, 30% of CS knowledge is obsolete, the remaining 70% — complemented with modern AI knowledge — is what makes really productive developers. 

Without understanding how computers work, you can’t just “vibe code” your way to greatness. Fundamentals are still important, and for those who additionally understand AI, job opportunities are numerous!

Parallel agents are emerging as an important new direction for scaling up AI. AI capabilities have scaled with more training data, training-time compute, and test-time compute. Having multiple agents run in parallel is growing as a technique to further scale and improve performance.

We know from work at Baidu by my former team, and later OpenAI, that AI models’ performance scales predictably with the amount of data and training computation. Performance rises further with test-time compute such as in agentic workflows and in reasoning models that think, reflect, and iterate on an answer. But these methods take longer to produce output. Agents working in parallel offer another path to improve results, without making users wait.

Reasoning models generate tokens sequentially and can take a long time to run. Similarly, most agentic workflows are initially implemented in a sequential way. But as LLM prices per token continue to fall — thus making these techniques practical — and product teams want to deliver results to users faster, more and more agentic workflows are being parallelized.

Some examples:
- Many research agents now fetch multiple web pages and examine their texts in parallel to try to synthesize deeply thoughtful research reports more quickly.
- Some agentic coding frameworks allow users to orchestrate many agents working simultaneously on different parts of a code base. Our short course on Claude Code shows how to do this using git worktrees.
- A rapidly growing design pattern for agentic workflows is to have a compute-heavy agent work for minutes or longer to accomplish a task, while another agent monitors the first and gives brief updates to the user to keep them informed. From here, it’s a short hop to parallel agents that work in the background while the UI agent keeps users informed and perhaps also routes asynchronous user feedback to the other agents.

It is difficult for a human manager to take a complex task (like building a complex software application) and break it down into smaller tasks for human engineers to work on in parallel; scaling to huge numbers of engineers is especially challenging. Similarly, it is also challenging to decompose tasks for parallel agents to carry out. But the falling cost of LLM inference makes it worthwhile to use a lot more tokens, and using them in parallel allows this to be done without significantly increasing the user’s waiting time.

I am also encouraged by the growing body of research on parallel agents. For example, I enjoyed reading “CodeMonkeys: Scaling Test-Time Compute for Software Engineering” by Ryan Ehrlich and others, which shows how parallel code generation helps you to explore the solution space. The mixture-of-agents architecture by Junlin Wang is a surprisingly simple way to organize parallel agents.

There is now a path for China to surpass the U.S. in AI. Even though the U.S. is still ahead, China has tremendous momentum with its vibrant open-weights model ecosystem and moves in semiconductor design and manufacturing. In the startup world, we know momentum matters: Even if a company is small, a high rate of growth compounded for a few years makes it an unstoppable force. That's why small a scrappy team with high growth can threaten even behemoths. While both the U.S. and China are behemoths, China’s hypercompetitive business landscape and rapid diffusion of knowledge give it momentum. The White House’s AI Action Plan released last week, which champions open source, is a positive step for the U.S., but by itself it won’t be sufficient to sustain the U.S. lead.

Now, AI isn’t a single, monolithic technology, and different countries are ahead in different areas. For example, even before GenAI, the U.S. was ahead in scaled cloud AI implementations, while China was ahead in surveillance technology. These translate to different advantages in economic growth and soft and hard power. Even though nontechnical pundits talk about “the race to AGI” as if AGI were a discrete technology to be invented, AI technology progresses continuously, and there's no single finish line. If a company or nation declares that it has achieved AGI, I expect that declaration to be less a technology milestone than a marketing milestone. A slight speed advantage in the Olympic 100m dash translates to a dramatic difference between winning Gold vs. Silver. An advantage in AI prowess translates into a proportionate advantage in economic growth and national power; while the impact won’t be a binary one of either winning or losing everything, these advantages matter.

Looking at LLM leaderboards, the top proprietary models were developed in the US, but the top open models are from China. Gemini 2.5 Pro, OpenAI’s o4, and Claude 4 Opus are all strong models. But open alternatives from China such as DeepSeek R1-0528, Kimi K2 (designed for agentic reasoning), Qwen3 variations (including Qwen3-Coder, strong at coding) and Zhipu’s GLM 4.5 (whose post-training software was released openly) are close behind, and many are ahead of Meta’s Llama 4 and Google’s Gemma 3 — the US’ best open-weights offerings.

Because many U.S. companies have taken a secretive approach to developing foundation models-a reasonable strategy-the leading companies spend huge numbers of dollars to recruit competitors' team members that might know some “secret sauce." So knowledge circulates, but at high cost and slowly. In contrast, in China’s open AI ecosystem, many companies undercut each other on pricing, make bold PR announcements, and poach each others’ employees and customers. This Darwinian life-or-death struggle will lead to the demise of many of the existing players, but the intense competition breeds strong companies.

The best leaders don’t focus on control; they focus on context. They understand what drives people, what frustrates them, and what helps them do their best work.

Leadership is personal. Every individual brings different strengths and perspectives. When you treat people as individuals instead of roles, trust grows and performance follows. 

But trust only works when it’s paired with debate and action. Teams need space to challenge ideas and the discipline to decide. You want to get to a point where everybody feels heard. Without that balance, organizations either stagnate or spin in circles. 

Authenticity matters too. People can tell if a leader acts in the best interest of the mission or themselves. Transparency and shared purpose aren’t slogans; they’re what make teams durable through change.

Culture is ultimately defined by who you keep. Support those who raise the bar, and move on from those who don’t. That single choice often determines whether a team thrives or fails.

Leadership isn’t about control. It’s about creating the conditions for talented people to do extraordinary things together.

If you’re working on an AI agent right now that works perfectly for any given task, you’re making the wrong bet.

AI model capabilities are only going to get better, context windows will get longer and have less rot, tool use will improve, compute efficiency will get better, and so on. 

The best thing to do is to be working on agents that can accomplish a smaller unit of work today, but necessarily require continued improvements to expand to broader tasks over time. This will mean you can get near term adoption and quick feedback now, but model gains will accrue to your agent so it steadily solve more complex work.

A corollary to this also is if you’re in an enterprise and spending time evaluating agents that work perfectly today for all your use-cases, you may not be pushing them hard enough. We’re going to see major performance gains in the coming year, and you want to be in a position to get all that upside.

Stop treating product management and project management like they're competing for the same job.

They're not. And this confusion is costing organizations everywhere.

Product management owns the why. We solve problems, create value, and figure out what actually matters to customers.

Project management owns the when. They coordinate resources, manage timelines, and ensure execution happens on schedule.

What often happens in reality is that PMs are juggling roadmaps AND resource coordination. That created a dangerous misconception that project management is all we do.

It's not. Project management is just one piece of our role.

I've seen this work beautifully in large organizations. Product managers define strategy and priorities. Program managers coordinate complex initiatives that cut across multiple teams. Neither role is threatened because they're solving different problems.

The real issue? When product managers become glorified project coordinators instead of strategic problem-solvers. That's when companies fall into the build trap, shipping features without understanding the why behind them.

The best outcomes happen when both functions collaborate, each playing to their strengths.

Most Agentic AI implementations fail because they are building canals in the age of railroads.

In the early 1800s, canals connected towns to ports, enabling efficient transport for coal and grain. 

Then came the railroads.

Unlike canals, where barges moved slowly, trains were fast and ran on fixed timetables. Local timekeeping started to become a problem, as noon in one town could be fifteen minutes different from noon in the next. 

Faster railroads resulted in a coordination failure. To coordinate trains safely across long distances, the industry had to impose standardized time zones.

That act of forcing distant towns and cities to operate on the same clock had far-reaching economic consequences. 

1. It meant grain in Chicago could be priced against demand in New York on the same schedule. 
2. It allowed financial markets, shipping companies, and manufacturers to plan production and deliveries with a new degree of precision.

Canals made transportation cheaper.
But Railroads rewired the economy.

---

Fast-forward to today.

Many of today's agentic AI experts cut their teeth in RPA (Robotic Process Automation) and accordingly measure progress in headcount saved or hours automated.

They see agentic AI as a faster, cheaper way to get the same work done.

But that’s the wrong frame.
RPA optimized tasks within workflows.
Agentic AI has the power to eliminate or even reimagine workflows entirely (to quote Howard Yu). 

---

Accordingly, most implementations today fail for two reasons:

1️⃣ They automate workflows that should really be collapsed instead: 
RPA taught practitioners to see a workflow as a string of discrete steps to be mapped and optimized. With the same hat, agenta are framed as a substitute for a task, rather than as participants in a network of interactions.
This task orientation completely misses why agentic AI matters.
When agents can perceive context, make decisions, and negotiate with one another, the need to route work through a rigid sequence of steps no longer holds.

2️⃣ They focus on workflow execution instead of governance: 
When agents are not simply automating tasks but deciding and interacting with other agents and humans, efficient execution is not the issue. 
What matters is under what rules, policies, and standards they coordinate. 

Governance becomes the key determiner of a successful agentic AI implementation. 

It defines: 
1. what goals agents pursue
2. how much autonomy they are allowed
3. when they escalate exceptions
4. who is accountable when outcomes diverge from intent

In other words, the real challenge is not teaching agents to act 
but aligning their actions with organizational objectives.

---

Every tech wave starts with efficiency experts selling scraps before the system architects capture the real prize.

Agentic AI is at that same fork in the road. 

Most implementations are still trying to build a faster canal rather than a railroad that rewires the entire system.

The term "AI Ranking" is incredibly misleading. LLMs don't work like Google/Search Engines. They aren't information retrieval systems. They don't crawl and rank websites. They don't assign PageRank scores..

Think about it: How do you rank something that gives different answers to identical questions? You can't! LLMs are probabilistic, meaning variability isn't a bug, it's the keystone of this tech. The entire concept 'rankings' (by way of consistency) doesn't apply here.

How do you show up in LLMs/AI Search? 

Instead of trying to reverse engineer AI results (which you can't), focus on the two proven approaches to increase the probability/chances of your brand showing up:

1. Grow Brand Authority: Increase consistent brand mentions online (and specifically within the context you want to be known for: brand positioning/messaging). <--PR & Content Marketing Strategy
2. Foundational SEO: SEO best practices still matter because AI/LLMs pull in real-time search results for updated information.

Note: You can't reverse engineer AI visibility. There are entire research labs dedicated to 'interpretability' (attempting to understand the reasoning behind a machine learning system's decisions or outputs), and the best of the best PhD Machine Learning Researchers struggle to understand this.

Instead of chasing algorithmic mysteries you can't control, invest in what you can: create exceptional content, build real relationships, and establish true expertise.

The Buttered Cat Paradox

“The test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two opposed ideas in mind at the same time and still retain the ability to function.”
— F. Scott Fitzgerald

Physicist Robert Matthews once showed that toast lands butter-side down not because of bad luck but because of design.
The height of the average table gives toast just enough time to rotate half a turn before it hits the floor.
The outcome is due to structure.

That same logic applies to organisations.

We seek innovation, agility, experimentation.
Yet we design for efficiency, predictability, control.

We want exploration, but measure exploitation.

It’s the folly of expecting one behaviour while rewarding another — exactly what Steven Kerr described in On the Folly of Rewarding A While Hoping for B. Organisations frequently bonus people for behaviour A while hoping for behaviour B — then wonder why B never shows.

The contradiction is baked in.

Enter the Buttered Cat Paradox:

Cats always land on their feet.
Toast always lands butter side down.

So, what do you get if you strap buttered toast to the back of a falling cat?

Humourist John Frazee proposed they’d hover — spinning midair, caught between two forces, neither able to triumph.

Many organisations live in that kind of tailspin.

They know they need to exploit — drive performance, serve current customers, deliver returns.
They also know they must explore — search for new models, innovate, adapt for the future.

James G. March’s classic essay Exploration & Exploitation in Organizational Learning shows this tension is real, persistent, and often destructive if poorly managed. 

He argues that systems tend to drift toward exploitation — refining what they know to get more immediate returns — while exploration is neglected because it’s riskier, slower, and its rewards are distant. 

Exploration is uncertain, delayed, and doubtful. 
Exploitation is immediate, reliable, and proximal. 

March warns that this drift — favoring exploitation over exploration — is self-destructive in the long run. 

The paradox is not about desire but design.

If innovation keeps “landing butter-side down,” perhaps the system — the incentives, the KPIs, the processes — is rigged to favour safe bets.

To resolve the paradox, we must redesign the structure.
We must reward exploration as courageously as we reward exploitation.
We must structure time, slack, and autonomy so experiments can breathe.

Only then will the cat land on its feet — butter side up.

Jeff Bezos built Amazon on one principle: the law of increasing returns.

Most people know the law of diminishing returns: growth slows as you expand.

But in a software-defined business, the opposite happens.
The more people use it, the more data is created.
The more data, the more value.
The more value, the greater the market cap.

That’s why Amazon could grow 35% per year and still expand margins and cash flow. The machine runs it.

When your marginal cost approaches zero, you create exponential returns.
That’s the foundation of trillion-dollar companies.

Most founders are fighting the wrong battle.

They obsess over funding rounds, hiring co-founders, and building partnerships.

But I learned something the hard way:

The real competition isn't external.

It's internal.

Three years ago, I realized I was reacting to everything instead of creating anything meaningful.

My mind was running on autopilot, programmed by notifications, news cycles, and other people's opinions.

That's when I understood a simple truth: if you don't control your mind, you'll never control your business.

Here are 7 principles that transformed how I operate:

1. Own Your Outcome 

Control 95% of everything you touch.

The moment you give up control is the moment you give up respect.

Investors steal control. 
Partnerships split focus. 
Co-founders create conflict.

2. Program Your Mind Daily 

Your mind is the operating system for reality.

If you don't program it intentionally, others will.

React less.
Read more. 
Consume less. 
Produce more. 

3. Accept Your Current Position 

You're exactly where you should be for your level of development.

You aren't where you want to be because you don't have the mind of the person in that position yet.

And that's okay, changing it is within your control.

4. Filter Your Inputs Ruthlessly 

Parents, teachers, content, notifications, news, music.

They're all writing code in your head.

If you don't filter, test, and edit the code, you don't control your life.

5. Focus Intensely, Then Zoom Out 

Train your ability to focus on one thing with intensity.

Then develop the discipline to zoom out and let go of everything.

This balance is your competitive advantage.

6. Reprogram Old Patterns 

If you don't reprogram your mind, old patterns will continue to run your life.

Discover why this is the key to controlling emotions and developing memory.

7. Train and Recover 

Your mind is a muscle.

It needs both intense work and deep recovery.

Protect both phases equally.

This isn't just about productivity hacks or morning routines.

It's about understanding that your mind is the foundation of everything you build.

Your business will only grow as much as your mind allows it to.

The future belongs to founders who master their internal operating system first.

Everything else follows from there.

At some point in the past 18 months, it became trendy to say that software engineering was dead. Why learn to code when you can tell an LLM, “make me an app with a login screen and a database” and out pops code? Voilà. Startup in a weekend. Series A by Thursday.

Thus was born vibe coding - the art of building software by manifesting it. Mix 1 part natural language, 1 part vague ambition, and 1 part blind confidence as you paste mystery code into production.

For a brief, shimmering moment, it almost felt like the future. Until it didn’t.

AI code assistants unlocked a wave of creative energy. Non-technical founders spun up MVPs. Engineers offloaded boilerplate. Students built full apps in a weekend.
But prototype-grade code isn’t production-grade code. Many teams that began with LLM scaffolds are now spending weeks refactoring. Some have even declared “code bankruptcy.”

Because there’s a difference between writing code and building software.
The former is syntax. The latter is systems thinking.
At some point, every serious technical team has the same realization:
you don’t just need code - you need engineering.

Vibe coding isn’t a tech failure, it’s a categorization error. It assumes that the problem in software development is generation speed. 
But for any company past the napkin stage, that’s not the bottleneck. It is:
- Understanding and reflecting business logic
- Architecting clean, extensible code
- Managing state, latency, auth, concurrency, observability
- Reasoning through edge cases and failure modes

LLMs don’t reason through trade-offs or hold long-term context. They don’t ask, “Why does this route even exist?”

So when teams use LLMs to generate full features - or worse, entire codebases - they end up with systems that appear functional but are structurally hollow. Like a house with beautiful wallpaper but no load-bearing walls. There’s a market for vibe-coding. It’s just not software.

This is the real distinction: vibe coding and AI in software development are not the same thing.
- Vibe coding tries to replace engineering. Hand the keys to the model, hope for the best.
- AI in software development amplifies engineering. Accelerate rote work while owning architecture, logic, and trade-offs.

The first treats AI as a substitute; the second treats it as a lever.
Vibe coding is fantastic 0 → 1. It’s a liability 1 → 100.
It’s like bringing a balloon animal to a knife fight. Wonderful at a birthday party. Less helpful in real combat.
There’s a real market for fast, disposable, front-of-the-house code. But most tech companies are in the business of building the kitchen, not just plating food.

The panic about “engineering being dead” comes from people who don't understand it. Engineering isn’t syntax. It’s constraint management, abstraction design, knowing when to optimize and when to punt.

Ironically, as AI makes building easier, the value of engineering judgment goes up. The faster you can go, the more you need someone to steer.

If you only watch one AI interview this month; make it this one. Andrej Karpathy (ex-OpenAI, ex-Tesla AI lead) just dropped one of the clearest takes on the road to AGI.

The episode with Dwarkesh Patel is a masterclass in cutting through the noise.

Here are the three big takeaways that every exec should understand:

1. “Reinforcement learning is terrible — but everything else is worse.”
Karpathy explains why today’s LLMs aren’t learning like humans.
They imitate, they don’t interact.
Until models can experience the world , not just train on it, we’ll keep hitting ceilings.

Don’t expect self-improving systems. You need tight feedback loops and adaptation pipelines in production.

2. AGI won’t be a shock. It’ll be background noise.
Karpathy doesn’t believe in a “singularity moment.”

He sees AGI blending into the slow march of 2% annual GDP growth.

For leaders, this is the unlock: the value isn’t in the hype. It’s in quietly embedding AI into every process now — before your competitors do.


3. The problem isn’t launch. It’s scale.
Self-driving cars had great demos years ago.

What took forever? Making them work everywhere.

AI will follow the same arc: most systems will break in the real world unless you build for drift, edge cases, and feedback from day one.

This is where most enterprise AI fails today.

This isn’t about countdowns to AGI.

It’s about whether your systems are ready for the decade-long integration arc we’re already in.

Users don’t care about the model. They care about the outcome. The best AI features don’t feel like AI. They feel invisible, but indispensable.

🎭 Think about Face ID.
 You don’t see neural nets. You just see your phone unlock instantly.
 Invisible. But once you’ve used it, you can’t imagine going back.

That’s the pattern:
Autocorrect didn’t advertise itself as “AI.” It just made typing easier.

Spotify’s “Discover Weekly” never mentioned ML. It just became the playlist you had to check.

Gmail’s spam filter doesn’t brag about models. It just keeps your inbox clean.

The common thread? Great AI vanishes into the product.
 Users don’t care about the model. They care about the outcome.
 Reliability → trust. Seamlessness → adoption.

As builders, that’s our bar:
Don’t ship features that scream “look, AI!”
Ship experiences that solve pain points so smoothly people forget there’s AI underneath.

LLM-assisted coding killed Agile.

No business leader who saw how software can be made will ever want to wait while you t-shirt-size your features, write user stories, conduct sprint planning ceremonies, hold daily stand-ups, run retrospectives, maintain a backlog grooming ritual, estimate story points using those dumb planning poker sessions that take longer than actually building the feature, create JIRA tickets with mandatory fields that add no value, schedule cross-functional alignment meetings to align on alignment meetings, and produce burndown charts that nobody reads—all while the competition ships working software and captures market share.

A business leader will tell you what feature they need, and you will bring this feature tomorrow. The technical debt problem will be solved next year if there's still business next year.

Here’s some humbling truths about product management. The more you do it, the more you realize:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗹𝘄𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝗻. Distribution, timing, and positioning matter just as much as great features. Learn how to do these well. 

𝗚𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗮 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗮 𝗯𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘀 𝗯𝗮𝗱 𝗲𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗮 𝗯𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗮. Ideas are abundant. How you execute them is the differentiator. 

𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗲-𝗼𝗳𝗳𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗷𝗼𝗯. There will never be enough resources, time, or data. Resisting this only makes your job harder. 

𝗕𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗺𝗮𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗻𝗲𝗴𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. It's a constant balance of customer needs, business goals, and team capacity. Lean into the challenge.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝗻𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗱𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝘄𝗲𝗹𝗹. It’s not easy to see product management’s connective glue work. The smoother it feels on the outside, the harder the work was behind the scenes.

𝗦𝘂𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝘂𝘁𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗹. Sometimes, it's not about the product. Market shifts, leadership transitions, regulatory changes. Adaptability is a competitive edge.

These truths can hit hard, but they keep us grounded. They remind us that product management is a career of humility. ❤️

Wikipedia traffic is collapsing — and it’s not just because of AI.

Wikipedia just reported an 8% drop in human visits in just a few months.

The reason?

AI systems — the same ones trained on Wikipedia — are now answering questions instead of sending users there.

The free encyclopedia is being replaced by the knowledge it taught.

That irony stopped me cold.

I’ve always seen Wikipedia as the internet’s moral compass — messy, human, collaborative.

When I was learning about anything new, I didn’t go for perfection. I went for context.

Now I rarely visit it.

AI gives me the answer instantly — but never the understanding that came from scrolling, cross-checking, exploring footnotes.

Somewhere along the way, convenience quietly replaced curiosity.

Here’s what’s really going on beneath the numbers:
 → AI is not just summarizing information — it’s absorbing the audience that once sustained the sources.
 → When answers appear directly on search pages, the human loop of reading, editing, and donating breaks.
 → And as fewer humans visit, fewer volunteers contribute — shrinking the very ecosystem AI depends on.
It’s the classic paradox of automation:

AI is killing the teachers it learned from.

If knowledge itself is becoming automated, we need to rebuild the habit of participation.

Here’s what I believe that looks like:
 ✅ Credit and link back to the human sources behind AI summaries.
 ✅ Support open, editable knowledge platforms — not just polished AI outputs.
 ✅ Remember that understanding comes from reading, not just receiving.

Because if we stop feeding the commons of human knowledge,

We won’t just lose Wikipedia —
We’ll lose the curiosity that made the internet worth exploring in the first place.

Goldman Sachs calls it “jobless growth.” Powell calls it a “low-hire, low-fire” economy. I call it a warning.

The U.S. economy is growing. But jobs? Not so much.

Goldman economists say we’ve entered a new normal — an era where AI drives productivity, yet people can’t find a foothold.

It’s growth without opportunity. Output without inclusion.

Everyone’s asking why Gen Z can’t find jobs.

But maybe the better question is why we’re still using 20th-century hiring logic in a 21st-century economy.

For decades, growth meant hiring.

Now, growth means automation.

We built systems that reward output, not opportunity — and AI is simply exposing that flaw.

The irony?

Gen Z was trained perfectly for the world we said we wanted — digital, creative, adaptive.

But they’re entering a world run by the incentives we never changed — risk-averse, credential-driven, efficiency-obsessed.

That’s why they’re shut out.

Not because they lack skills, but because the system still measures “readiness” in years, not adaptability.
The Hidden Lesson

AI isn’t replacing Gen Z.
It's replacing the trust gap we never fixed — the space where new talent used to prove itself slowly, through small wins and mentorship.

We removed the ladder and called it “efficiency.”

Now we wonder why no one can climb.

If companies want to stay relevant, they’ll need to rebuild the ladder — not just reskill workers.

 ✅ Shorter apprenticeship cycles.
 ✅ Open-entry hybrid roles where young talent co-works with AI.
 ✅ New metrics that measure learning speed, not tenure.

Because the future of work isn’t “jobless growth.”

It’s trustless growth — until we redesign the system that connects potential with opportunity.

A current Amazon VP shared a 1-sentence litmus test to determine if someone should be promoted. If the person is otherwise completely qualified, the final determining factor is this question:

"Do your spirits lift or sag when the person enters the room?"

This is a key deciding factor for many promotions, but especially high-level ones and in low-growth environments.

In a slower growth economy, there are fewer opportunities to move up. As a result, attitude becomes the determining factor among equally strong candidates.

The reason for this is that a person can be smart and capable, but still unpleasant or draining to work with. Emotional energy is a limited resource, so the energy that someone brings to their leaders, teammates, and reports is a real and valid promotion criteria.

Someone who saps energy from a room through constant criticism, nitpicking, or negativity is a liability, no matter how "talented" they may be. On the other hand, someone who brings a sense of excitement and empowerment to the team is a resource.

At the highest levels and in times of slow growth, you will need exceptional skills and an exceptional attitude to be promoted.

Here is something to consider when thinking about your attitude and energy:

Many aspects of a bad attitude come from a love of being "right."

Everyone loves to have the sharpest insight on a problem, and sometimes this leads to overwhelming or unnecessary naysaying.

The important thing to remember is that just because you are right does not mean that your input is welcome. Criticism without a solution or an offer to help makes you feel clever, but it also makes you unpleasant to work with.

This is something that has personally challenged me. I love to find the flaw and to make the clever observation so I can feel like the smartest person in the room. To control this tendency, I check my impulse to point out flaws by asking myself a question:

"How can I help this person succeed?"

If you pass your criticism and fault-finding through this filter, you will bring a much more positive energy to the room. Then, when leaders talk about who should move up and who they want to spend more time with, it will be you.

You will be top of mind as someone with a leader’s attitude and front of the line for a promotion.

How do you bring positive energy to a room?

Over the last week, I've seen three agencies either releasing or hyping up reports on fandom and microcommunities. And look, welcome to the party. I mean that. The more people paying attention to how fans actually function in culture; reshaping everything from IP to commerce, the better. This stuff matters

But something is making me twitch a little: these reports all have the same breathless energy, like they've just discovered fire. Yet the insights are things like "fans prefer authentic stories," "people are organising around passions," "brands must contribute to the culture, not exploit it"

I mean... yes? And? This is all true, but it's also the stuff we've been saying for half a decade. Participation equals identity. Fragmentation is the new normal. Fandoms are networked power structures. We know

We're stuck in 101 when the actual frontier is miles ahead. No one's talking about the difficult, genuinely interesting questions: What do new business models look like when fans ARE the infrastructure? How does open IP work legally and practically? What are mutual economies instead of extractive ones? Where are the new incentive structures?

You know what's actually interesting? The fact that Larian Studios used early access fan feedback to rework entire character arcs in Baldur's Gate 3, turning it into one of the most critically acclaimed games of the year. That HoYoverse measures Honkai: Star Rail's success not just in revenue but in cosplay density and how quickly fan edits spread. That Critical Role built Daggerheart and Candela Obscura iteratively with fans, co-authoring from the ground up

Or how about the fact that we need entirely new KPIs? Not social listening or sentiment analysis. Fanfic velocity. Remix response lag. The health of fan micro-economies. These tell you whether you've built something that matters enough for people to make it their own. Whether your IP lives out in the world, actually woven into how people create and organise

That's the difference between a brand playing in a space and actually being part of what makes that space generative

And the people who get this are actually pushing the conversation forward, introducing new possibilities, exploring new models and incentives. MetaLabel are creating DFOS (Dark Forest Operating System), software and methodologies for collaborative economics. TYB is building new structures for how brands and communities can co-create value. Epic Games is re-architecting its entire economy so creators share in engagement-based revenue. Not theories. Tools. 

If you're going to release something about fandom in 2025 and call it new, at least push to the edges. Talk about IP as orchestration, not ownership. About elastic canon and layered participation models. About licensing as platform, not permission. About what it takes to build systems that treat fans as true partners, not just audiences with purchasing power. Please.

Otherwise it's just groundhog day with a bit of nice typography.

Lately, I’ve seen a lot of advice about hustle and founder mentality on here. Most of it misses the mark.

High agency. Low ego. That’s the formula that actually works. 

I’ve seen this play out in my own life. When I was 13, I started a DJ business. Later, when my family hit financial trouble, I learned screen printing at McGill University to help us get by. That bias to action, figuring things out fast, was my first lesson in high agency.

I also think of my grandfather. He came to Montreal after the war with no English, no money, and no options. To survive, he opened a small egg stall at Jean Talon Market. That immigrant mentality shaped me. It taught me that if something doesn’t defy the laws of physics, it’s solvable.

And at Shopify, I’ve learned the other side of the equation. A bias to action only works when it’s paired with humility. We never make it about us—it’s always about the entrepreneurs we serve. We succeed when they do.

High agency without low ego leads to arrogance and burnout. Low ego without high agency leads to passivity.

But when you put them together, you get something powerful: the ability to move quickly, adapt, and build. Without losing sight of the mission or the people you serve.

That’s the kind of entrepreneur the world needs more of.

These are not CGI.

The Internet Was Built Without Telcos. Can the AI Economy Be Too?

In 2024, the internet economy generated over 7.1 trillion dollars in global revenue. Telcos captured 25% of that by building and operating Layer One: access, transport, and spectrum.

But.... they only earned 8% in net profit. They built the foundation, carried the traffic, paid for the infrastructure, and watched value flow upward.

Because telcos didn’t build the internet, they only powered it, they never captured control of the higher layers. They missed the cloud layer, which earned over 80 billion in profit last year. They missed the platform layer, where Google, Apple, Meta, and Amazon collectively took more than 200 billion in net income. 

They also missed the application and monetization layers, where interface owners and ad platforms extracted 40% margins with near-zero marginal cost.

This was a structural error. Telcos stayed at the bottom and never integrated into the logic of the stack. They exposed no APIs, owned no identity, and controlled no interface. The economic outcome was baked into the architectural role they accepted.

Now, AI is forming a new stack. And telcos are again outside of it. 

LLMs run on hyperscaler infrastructure. Agents orchestrate workflows over HTTP. Inference flows across CDNs. No model queries a telco network API. No agent waits for a 5G slice. Machines optimize for latency and cost, not for network branding.

To avoid becoming only Layer One again, telcos must move upward through the AI stack. At Layer Two, they must host trusted, low-latency edge inference nodes with deterministic compute. At Layer Three, expose tokenized APIs that allow agents to bid for bandwidth, locality, and compute without human provisioning. At Layer Four, integrate network context directly into agent decision loops such as latency, jitter, SLA violations, and real-time congestion, all as machine-readable primitives. And at Layer Five, monetize participation in agent workflows through contracts that embed the network as an execution partner, not a pipe.

That is how they climb the AI stack. Otherwise, they will build the roads and watch others collect the tolls. Again.

CREDENTIALS DON’T MATTER

Over the last few months, I’ve seen several A+ execs and operators flounder and fail at AI startups, while young “inexperienced” builders have crushed execution and performed superbly. 

It’s the biggest discontinuity in talent evaluation I’ve seen in my 25+ years in technology. 

All of one’s prior experiences, credentials, and traditional markers of success have become nearly irrelevant when the fundamental rules of the game have changed so dramatically.

The shift to AI represents such a paradigm break that institutional knowledge often becomes institutional baggage. The executive who masterfully navigated enterprise sales cycles finds themselves lost when the product builds itself. The operator who optimized for predictable growth metrics struggles when the core capability improves exponentially overnight.

Meanwhile, the 22-year-old who’s been fine-tuning models in their dorm room intuitively understands token economics, reasoning patterns, and capability scaling in ways that no MBA program ever taught. These “inexperienced” builders don’t carry the weight of how things “should” work. They don’t waste time trying to force AI capabilities into traditional product frameworks or business models. And so, they win.

If you’re an executive wanting to find a role at an AI company, be humble and accept that what got you here might not get you there. Put in the work. Use the tools and create a personal AI stack. Train yourself to think differently - probabilistically, not deterministically. Be open to an IC role if needed. It’s not magic, but it will take real work.

If you’re an AI company founder, ensure your hiring process is not blinded by credentials. What matters is raw building ability (in EVERY role, not just Eng/Product/Design), comfort with uncertainty, and the ability to iterate and experiment exceptionally fast. 

The past (of someone's experience) no longer predicts their future.

A sales guy I interviewed years ago gave me a tip that added 5-10% to every enterprise renewal. And most customers didn't even blink.

In the middle of scaling OneLogin, I met a sales leader who shared a simple tactic: he'd added a "technology innovation fee" to his master services agreements. Basically a clause allowing a 10% price increase each year.

So when customers pushed for big discounts (which happens on every large deal), he'd agree. But in exchange, he'd get that annual uplift built into the contract.

I told my sales leader about the idea and we immediately worked the concept into our MSA.

What surprised me was how rarely customers pushed back. Legal might negotiate it down, but they'd rarely remove it. It became a quiet way to recover margin over time, long after the deal is signed and was essentially free money.

But there's a catch: Most billing systems can't automate these uplifts. So companies either forget to apply them or create manual work tracking them, which defeats the point.

With enterprise subscriptions, small improvements add up. If you protect your upside when discounting and stay on top of churn, it can easily make a 10% difference in net revenue retention. Add to that more products to sell and you're suddenly growing your existing revenue much faster.

This is one of those areas where billing flexibility has a real impact on SaaS margins and growth rates. The difference between average and exceptional performance often comes down to flexibility and automation.

Bunny can automate these for you. Don’t keep doing it manually or leave it to be forgotten.

🫧 BENEFITTING FROM AI BUBBLES

Far from being a threat, the AI bubble might be the best thing that could happen to pragmatic adopters. Consider what speculative excess delivers: billions in venture capital funding R&D you’d never justify to your board; the world’s brightest minds abandoning stable careers to join AI startups, working on tools that you’ll eventually be able to use; infrastructure being built at a scale no rational actor would attempt, driving down future costs through overcapacity.

While investors bet on which companies will dominate AI, you can cherry-pick proven tools at competitive prices. While speculators debate valuations, you will be implementing solutions with clear ROI. When the correction comes, you’ll also be able to benefit from fire-sale prices on enterprise tools, seasoned talent seeking stability, and battle-tested technologies that survived the shakeout.

The dotcom bubble gave us broadband infrastructure and trained web developers. The AI bubble will leave behind GPU clusters and ML engineers. The smartest response isn’t to avoid the bubble or try to time investments in it perfectly. It is to let others take the capital risk while you harvest the operational benefits. The bubble isn’t your enemy. If you play your cards strategically, it can be a major benefactor.

I saw this meme recently:

It’s funny but also painfully real for anyone who lives in the intersection of sales, product, and tech.

RFPs were created to bring clarity and fairness to the buying process.

But more often than not, they create:

- Vagueness (“We’re still figuring out what we need”)
- Checklist overload (hundreds of requirements, few that matter)
- Shifting priorities halfway through the process
- Wasted energy defending features that no one will ever use

In the end, buyers feel they’ve been “thorough,” vendors feel exhausted, and neither side is closer to solving the actual business problem.

Here’s what we can do differently:

1. Start with outcomes, not specs.
Don’t begin with a 200-point feature list. Begin with: “We want to cut processing errors by 80%.” Features should follow outcomes, not the other way around.


2. Allow iteration.
Issue lighter RFPs, run discovery workshops, or launch a short pilot. It’s faster and cheaper than producing binders of requirements that no one can realistically validate.


3. Reward transparency.
Good vendors will tell you where trade-offs exist. Encourage that instead of penalizing it.


4. Be honest about readiness.
If you’re not budget-approved or aligned internally, say it. Vendors would rather hear the truth than pour weeks into a phantom project.

When done wrong, they become bureaucratic theater.

And at Invofox, we only sell to software companies so we know exactly who, what, and why we’re selling.

That focus lets us skip the noise, speak the same language, and sell to teams that actually ship.

If 30% of people don't opt out of product management training, you're probably doing it wrong.

At one company I worked with, we discovered that about 30% of people wanted to leave once they truly understood what product management entailed. The constant ambiguity, the need to make decisions with incomplete data, the relentless focus on evidence over opinion, it's not for everyone.

And that's actually a good outcome.

Most organizations measure training success by completion rates. But effective PM training should help people self-select out if they're not suited for the role's realities. Better to discover this during a 3-month program than after 18 months of poor performance and frustration.

Product management involves thriving in uncertainty, defending unpopular decisions with data, and saying no to stakeholders who don't want to hear it. Some people love this challenge. Others realize they prefer more structure and predictability.

Stop treating high completion rates as your only success metric. The real win is helping people understand what they're signing up for before you invest heavily in them.

Have you seen this self-selection happen in your organization? What percentage would you expect to opt out?

You can draw whatever conclusion you want:

 • Pre-training scaling has hit diminishing returns as high-quality data sources are exhausted, and further increases in compute, data, or model size no longer yield predictable gains (Vetterle, 2024).

 • Scaling reasoning introduces complexity with little benefit, sometimes degrading performance (Chen et al., 2024).

 • Test-time compute offers better returns than scaling model size; “thinking longer” at inference outperforms larger models (Vetterle, 2024).

 • Beyond a complexity threshold, reasoning accuracy collapses to zero in large reasoning models (LRMs), even with explicit algorithms, highlighting an inability to execute true logical steps (Shojaee et al., 2025).

 • Reasoning effort peaks and then declines with increasing complexity, revealing architectural limitations despite adequate resources (Shojaee et al., 2025).

 • Two performance regimes exist: standard LLMs lead on simple tasks, LRMs on moderate ones, but both fail on high-complexity reasoning (Shojaee et al., 2025).

 • CoT is not genuine reasoning; it’s pattern matching and interpolation from training data (Goedecke, 2025).

 • CoT fails under distribution shifts, showing it lacks robust logical foundations (Goedecke, 2025).

 • Language itself is a poor medium for precise reasoning (Werner, 2025 reviewing Chen at al., 2025).

 • Transformers have structural limits for AGI (Clintell, 2025).

 • LLMs remain data-bound, unreliable statistical models with limited generalization (Mindprison, 2025).

 • True semantic novelty is out of reach; LLMs recombine, not create, new ideas (Mindprison, 2025).

 • Multimodal models inherit unimodal limitations, offering no fundamental reasoning leap (Wang et al., 2022).

 • Multi-agent systems scale to a point, then plateau or regress due to coordination overhead (Chen et al., 2024).

Most of the recent news about AI seems to involve staggering amounts of money. OpenAI and Nvidia sign a $100b data center contract. Meta offers researchers $100m salaries. VCs invested almost $200b in AI startups in the first half of 2025.

Frankly, I think we’re in a massive bubble that dwarfs the dot-com boom, and we’ll look back on these as crazy decisions. One of the reasons I believe this is because I’ve seen how much is possible running AI locally, with no internet connection, on low-cost hardware. The video I've included is one of my favourite recent examples. It comes from a commercial contract we received to help add a voice assistant to appliances. The idea is that when a consumer runs into a problem with their dishwasher, they can press a help button and talk to get answers to common questions.

What I’m most proud of here is that this is cutting-edge AI actually helping out with a common issue that many of us run into in our daily lives. This isn’t speculative, it’s real and running, and it doesn’t pose a lot of the ethical dilemmas other AI applications face. Here’s why I think this matters:

 - The consumer doesn’t have to do anything beyond pressing a button to use it. There’s no phone app to download, no new account to create, and no Wifi to set up. The solution works as soon as they plug the appliance in. This is important because less than half of all smart appliances ever get connected to the internet.

 - It’s using Moonshine and an LLM to do a much better job of understanding natural speech than traditional voice assistants. The questions I asked in the demo were off-the-cuff, I deliberately used vague and informal language, and it still understood me.

 - It addresses a genuine problem that manufacturers are already paying money to solve. They are currently spending a lot on call centers and truck rolls to help consumers. This solution has the potential to reduce those costs, and increase consumer satisfaction, by offering quick answers in an easy way.

 - Running locally means that audio recordings never have to go to the cloud, increasing privacy.

 - Local also means fast. The response times in the video are real, this is running on actual hardware.

This doesn’t require a GPU or expensive hardware. It runs on a Synaptics Incorporated chip that has just launched, and will be available in bulk for low-single-digit dollars. This means it can be added to mass-market equipment like appliances, and even toys. Since it’s also able to run all the regular appliance control functions, it can replace similarly-priced existing SoCs in those products without raising the price.

More functionality, like voice-driven controls, can easily be added incrementally through software changes. This can be a gateway to much richer voice interactions, all running locally and privately.

The positive thing about AI tooling going mainstream and setting expectations high (e.g. "anyone can build software with AI") is that a large group will learn what us devs know already:

Creating good software is hard and it's hard to explain to outsiders in a way they understand.

We have millions of non-technical ppl believe that they are equipped to create software that works as they imagined, using AI + vibe coding

...only to crash and burn.

They are now not outsiders, and might be open to understand why software is hard (and how they can get better)

As to why building good software is hard: where do I start?

Because:

- Good software solves a problem people/customers have

- Good software has users and customers

- Good software is not always good code, and good code is not always good software

- Craft and commercial viability are not always connected

- Tech debt is hard to appreciate from the outside, but you feel it every day in the inside

- After a while, code is the trivial part, and everything else (people, the business, politics etc) become the hard parts

... and many, many more things.

If you ever worked as a developer at any company on a software product, you get to learn so many things that are counter-intuitive from the "outside."

And don't forget that "good software" is very different for a startup looking for product-market fit, a scaleup that found it and needs to grow rapidly, or a stable and mature business that prioritizes keeping existing customers happy vs getting new ones.

Building good software has always been hard, but rewarding when you get it right. I don't think AI changes this - in fact, these tools can create the illusion that it's now easier than before to create it.

While many think having a "largest customer" is an asset, it's actually a significant weakness.

If you can feel a single customer leave, you're in a delicate spot. If you have a customer you can't afford to lose, you're in an even worse position.

When that happens, the door cracks open for something new.
