Being highly competent but politically unsavvy relative to your peers — whether you’re at a fast-growing startup or a big company — might prevent you from reaching your full potential in conventional terms (e.g., org size, title, etc.), yet it keeps you closely connected to who you truly are.

Over time, this builds a quiet, intrinsic confidence that many of your peers will lack despite their big titles and large orgs.

What you do with that confidence is up to you, but in many cases it allows you to make more authentic career choices your former peers say they would love to make “if only the timing were right.” 

Of course, what appears to be a timing problem is really a confidence problem — the quiet, authentic kind of confidence.

~

Now, this isn’t saying that being politically savvy is “bad” or that being politically unsavvy is “good.” 

A fundamental problem in our society these days — and in most online content — is the pressure to believe all humans must be the same: same thoughts, same personalities, same superpowers, same life goals.

In reality, people are different, will always be different, and there is no single “ideal formula” everyone must follow.

Every parent with more than one child and everyone with siblings knows this truth: people are different. No amount of forcing, lecturing, modeling, or incentivizing will change that.

Yet our societal conditioning runs so deep that we come to believe everyone else should not only resemble us but should also adopt our beliefs, our priorities, and our way of thinking — as if our own ideas were the universal template everyone else must conform to.

Senior PMs and PM leaders need to live in 2 distinct mindsets. 

The first is the confident, certainty mindset that is often necessary to get things done, push things through against opposition, inspire the team, and demonstrate progress in a complex environment where everyone in the company has opinions on what should be done but few have much conviction and even fewer are willing to have any skin in the game. 

The second is the more open, inquisitive mindset that is necessary to correctly understand your customers / market and the truth of whether you have major blind spots or skill gaps, whether your product efforts are actually making any real difference, and what personal adjustments you must make to make your product win.

While every PM leader has the ability to balance Mindsets 1 and 2, over time their day to day tends to be dominated by Mindset 1, just due to the nature of how challenging it is to get things done in companies and the kinds of incentives that are set in even the best companies.

The trouble is this: while Mindset 1 has its merits in the real world and can even work quite well in scaled orgs and mature products, its lack of openness is highly incompatible with what’s needed to actually build great products and make them successful.

Yet more trouble is that while Mindset 1 often “does its job”, it takes its toll on the human behind the PM leader in insidious ways such as losing one’s true compass.

As any human spends almost their entire day in Mindset 1 for years and decades, constantly trying to project certainty and confidence, trying to one up every person who’s across the table with verbal agility & aggression, leveraging hierarchy to their advantage, and telling themselves false stories of how their inner struggle is worth it, s/he inevitably loses appreciation for some other things: joy in one’s work, creativity and flow, genuine introspection, truth seeking, and the importance of wisdom in life. 

We like playing the games we master. So if we master stupid power games, we will play more stupid power games.

What is the way out?

Much of this is about the personality one is wired with. If you dislike being in Mindset 1 all day long, you will naturally look for a different way. You might check out of the game entirely or you might make Mindset 2 your personality, being okay with the concomitant professional cost. You might find an environment that is better suited for a combination of Mindset 2 & 1 (often available in well-run startups), instead of the many environments that force Mindset 1.

On the other hand if you love Mindset 1, there’s no inherent problem with it. It is only problematic if you aren’t okay with its personal cost. 

And if for you the personal cost gets too much, this is one of those problems where the recognition is the solution, the diagnosis is the cure, and perhaps then this post is just a pointer to the truth that is in front of you.

The purpose of a metric is to focus attention. That is also its greatest danger.

When you measure something, it doesn't just become visible. It actively pulls focus and resources from everything you don't measure. A hospital starts tracking patient throughput. The metric improves. Unseen: the nurse who no longer spends five extra minutes explaining medication to a confused patient. There's no KPI for "prevented a costly readmission through an unhurried conversation," so a vital conversation now feels like unproductive time.

We think we measure what we value. More often, we come to value what we measure. A university ranks departments by research citations. Within a few years, the most interesting scholars have either left or learned to publish safe, predictable work. No one voted to kill innovation. But the system no longer rewards discovery; it rewards the production of discovery's metrics. The scoreboard has replaced the game.

This obsession creates a silent tax on the work itself. The product manager, pulled from customer conversations to prep for metric reviews. The engineer, optimizing for what's tracked instead of what would improve the product. This is a permanent drag on the very work the metrics were supposed to improve.

None of this argues against measurement. Data is powerful when used properly. The problem isn't that we measure—it's that we measure thoughtlessly. We confuse the map for the territory, mistaking simplified numbers for complex reality. Good measurement illuminates; bad measurement blinds.

The most effective organizations master measurement by asking one question: "What specific decisions will change based on this number?" If the answer is vague, they refuse to measure it. They understand that continuous tracking creates continuous behavioral distortion, and remain vigilant for unintended consequences—because metrics generate behaviors their creators never anticipated. True mastery means adjusting course when measurement undermines its own purpose.

To achieve this culture, reward people for shrewd judgments about unmeasured impact. The manager who built unbreakable trust with a key client. The engineer who refactored a system for maintainability that will save millions. Successful companies learn that protecting unmeasurable value means putting people in power who understand it.

The dashboard provides a comfortable illusion of clarity. Real clarity comes from understanding what the dashboard shows—and having the courage to act on what it can never show you.

The higher someone's title, the more they miss being treated like a regular person.

Nike built an empire on one concept: CHOICE.

"Just Do It" means you CHOOSE to struggle. You CHOOSE to push harder. You CHOOSE greatness.

John Mackey built Whole Foods sold it for $13.7 billion — and here’s his secret to lasting leadership.

"I asked myself a question all the time: What does the company most need me to do now?" he told me.

Not what he enjoyed. Not what he was good at. What the company NEEDED.

Example: In the early days, Mackey oversaw real estate. He was good at it. Loved it.

But as it grew, Whole Foods didn’t need its CEO running real estate.

Instead, it needed him to be the face of the company. So he did it, even though he didn't love it.

“I was able to grow as a leader because I kept asking that question,” he said, “and I had the courage to go into unfamiliar, uncomfortable situations."

Most leaders have it backwards. They build companies around their strengths. But Mackey built his strengths around the company.

Your skills are not your identity. Your value is your ability to evolve.

So ask yourself: What does your company need from you RIGHT NOW?

I refused to refund a client $110K. 
Even though we didn’t deliver the service.

…is what I would’ve done, if I weren’t building for the long game.

Instead, I wired every dollar back.
Most founders wouldn’t. 

They would look for a loophole in the contract, keep the cash, patch something together, and call it “close enough.”

Here’s the truth: I never even took the payment.

The client wanted to work with us and offered $110K up front.

Most founders would’ve said yes for the money.
And I get it. Early on, cash flow feels like life or death.

But I said no. Because they wanted things done their way, not ours.

And if you let one client dictate your product roadmap, you stop building a company.
You start building custom services. You become an agency.

So yes, my bank account took a hit.
But I sleep better knowing I put the company’s vision first.

And funny enough, that same client came back later with referrals.

At the end of the day, we’re building a company that delivers the best results for all of our clients.
If that means turning some down in the short term, I’m okay with that.

Because revenue is temporary. Trust is permanent.
And in business, trust is what creates money.

As founders and as teams, you have to trust your product and cut the noise.

Looking back, our team's success boils down some key principles I'm sharing in a 700-word long post: 

I hope that it will help every startup as much as it helped us: 

1. You need to be in the details. 

The best founders can zoom all the way in and out. If someone tells you to “scale yourself” by pulling back too early - they’re wrong. 

Being in the details is important to understand what org structure best fits your company's goals. Every 'in the weeds' founder designs their org structure from first principles. 

Jensen: 40 direct reports, 
Elon: Engineers in charge of everything, 
Jobs: Creative Dictatorship with Directly Responsible Individuals. 
Zuck: The first growth-hacking team

Founders not in the details forget what makes their products great and eventually recede into designing a standard org with standard departments which lead to standard results. 

2. Your company's fate is 70% sealed by the first 20 hires 

Ben Horowitz: "I got this advice like 27 times. They said 'Look, here's the key: Hire A players:' and I was like ok yeah, my plan was to hire a bunch of morons but now I'm going to hire A players" The hard part isn’t intent, it’s judgment.

The problem is you can't spot a top 1% engineer if you're not a top 10% engineer yourself. This applies to everything.

3. Be extremely responsive.

If I reply in 30 seconds, what usually takes a day gets done in hours.

What takes hours gets done now.

Speed compounds.

4. Your TAM is limited by your imagination, not by the market. 

Constantly rethink the pod, find other big issues that need solving and are valuable, and solve them exceptionally well. We went from contracts ($100M) to Employer of Record ($300M) to Payroll ($200M). 

Each 3x'd the TAM. 

5. Never run out of cash.

The only way a business dies is by running out of cash.

Profitability = power. You call the shots, not investors.

You can always act in the company's long-term interest because you know you are safe. 

We reached Series A after spending <10% of our seed.

We have been profitable for the last 3 years.

Cash discipline buys freedom.

6. Over-index on angels early.

Angels are your best shot at making important people care - when it matters. They might not be involved day to day, but when you really need help, they’ll show up. If you don't know how to solve a problem, you should know at least a person who knows the person who can.

Pick the right ones, and time your asks well.

I throw up in my mouth a little bit every time I read a post from a marketing leader about how much AI has transformed their organization.

I have had conversations with literally THOUSANDS of growth/enterprise B2B marketing teams and I can tell you, right now the AI adoption is still for the most part some basic ChatGPT/Gemini/Claude usage by a few folks on the team.

Though if you were just reading LinkedIn posts you'd literally think that nearly everyone had 27 agents running their entire GTM motion. Total BS.

There are absolutely exceptions and I've seen a few teams lean heavily into (mostly) building their own custom workflows... but for the most part actual adoption is lacking.

I've had numerous VCs ask my why they're also not seeing marketing teams adopt/lean in to AI tools and I'll give you my answers. 

Expectations vs. reality and fear. 

When it comes to expectations, many marketers think AI is literal magic that works perfectly all of the time. When the try something and it takes work or prompting or isn't ready for prime time, they retreat. 

The reality is, MOST of this stuff is still hard. It still takes massaging and work to get it to do what you want. 

Yesterday alone I tried to:
1️⃣ Use Hubspot's AI field builder to create a new property which epically failed so ended up doing it manually
2️⃣ Have ChatGPT (which is connected to my email + call recordings) find all mentions from the past 6 months of people looking for their next job. I know I have over a dozen... it found 3. Of those, 2 were older than 6 months
3️⃣ Generate an on-brand image to be used for a LinkedIn ad. After numerous attempts it was ok but would have taken me 1/5 the time to just do it from scratch in Canva

That absolutely doesn't mean I stick my head in the sand. There are TONS of ways I get a lot of utility from AI tools right now.

📞 Sybill records my calls, helps draft next-step emails, populates data in our CRM, surfaces areas where i'm dropping the ball or missing a follow up, and can search for specific mentions of anything across all calls.

👥 Tofu helps scale account and contact research for extremely specific things like who they sell to (industries, personas) what types of marketing campaigns/content they're creating, what they're talking about on LinkedIn, etc and can turn that into extremely relevant messaging across channels.

🧠 ChatGPT helps create commission schedules, calculations and payouts, it helps write/re-write JDs and onboarding docs. It is my chief hole-poker in my thinking. Plus lots of other things. 

When it comes to fear, people are terrified of screwing up. Sending the wrong email to the wrong person. Not knowing how to prompt or use a tool correctly. 

AI tools do require behavior change. They do require learning a new way of getting things done. And for most people, that is hard and scary which is totally understandable. 

The one thing I wish more people knew: YOU ARE NOT BEHIND!! I promise :)

when VCs pass on a fundraise, it's easy to take it personally. but my advice for founders is to keep your chin up, execute the hell out of your business, and come back in 6 months to prove em wrong. many of our top companies in a16z speedrun have been reapplicants who derisked every concern we had and proved us wrong - and we couldn't be happier to be working with them today 😊

a framework I like to point founders to is the onion theory of risk (h/t to pmarca) - essentially every start-up is a big ball of risks, and as a start-up progresses it peels away each layer of risk one by one like an onion. for example, an early-stage start-up might have:
- founder risk - does the start-up have the right founding team? Is there strong founder-market fit? is the team complete?
- product risk - can the team build the product? does the product work as intended?
- market risk - is there a market for the product? will users pay for it? will those users stick around?
- competition risk - are there other companies already doing this? how does the start-up differentiate / defend itself?
etc etc

when an investor passes, it's usually because there are too many layers of risk at present for their taste, and the best thing you can do is to keep peeling the onion and then come back later on

note this is also why it's important to run a wide process and talk to as many investors as possible. different investors have varying taste / risk tolerance - and what's too risky for one might be the sweetspot for another

also there's inherent randomness in the fundraise process itself - you might have caught an investor right after they had a fight with their spouse that morning, or maybe the investor had a bad experience with another company in your market or another founder who kind of looks like you etc. you can't control these things

tldr - so when a VC says no, don't read too much into it. it's a pass on your company at this particular moment in time, and it also might have been due to something entirely random. instead - stay focused, stay hungry - keep on executing and prove all the naysayers wrong!

I'm bullish on founders who choose to raise smaller seed rounds, even when they have the option of a bigger round out of the gate. the #1 reason - smaller rounds encourage FOCUS

early on in a start-up, focus is so important - streamlining the team around a single target customer or pain point, and executing relentlessly towards an MVP. having the discipline to say NO to new opportunities that might be distractions to your core mission is critical - you only have so many people and so much time / runway

when you raise a ginormous round, it's easy to lose discipline and say YES to too many things. work on multiple different products. hire too many people "just in case" - bloating the team and slowing everyone down. avoid the tough decision to kill experiments that aren't working - letting failed products drag on for too long etc

as Steve Jobs said - “people think focus means saying yes to the thing you've got to focus on. but that's not what it means at all. it means saying no to the hundred other good ideas that there are. you have to pick carefully. i'm actually as proud of the things we haven't done as the things I have done. innovation is saying no to 1,000 things.”

most founders underestimate the number of investors they need to pitch in order to successfully fundraise, as well as how to manage their time while fundraising 📆

let's say you're raising a seed round - an (anecdotal) rule of thumb is that seed firms promote the top 10% of pitches to an IC meeting, and then 10% of those (or less) actually get a termsheet. so to maximize your chances of landing a termsheet, you need to meet with 100 investors, maybe more if your conversion rate is lower

how on earth do you manage 100 investors? well they are not all equal - your goal is to quickly sift through investors to identify believers vs non-believers, and to maximize the time you spend with the believers

if you're on your 3rd or 4th meeting for a seed round and the investor is still having trouble sizing the market or rerunning retention curves it's likely just not going to happen. you're better off reprioritizing your time to other investors who came out of the first meeting enthused

put another way - no one ever finds conviction in the data room. the ideal use of data/diligence is as confirmatory evidence for an investor who's already excited about you, the market, and the opportunity

it's similar to dating - have a wide top of funnel and then lean into the partner that seems most into you from the first date, vs chasing the one that seems to be getting away

i've seen a few start-up founders panic at the sight of a well-funded competitor launching into their space. but the counter-intuitive thing is - more competition is good for you! here's why

1) competition validates the market - many other (presumably) smart hard working people recognize there's an opportunity, so you're likely onto something. in fact, the presence of 0 competitors can often be a yellow flag - you might be too early, the market might structurally suck but you just don't know it yet, etc
 
2) nothing galvanizes a team like a shared public enemy. it's no longer a solitary marathon, it's now a team sprint. framed well ("we deserve to win" etc), i've seen competition sharpen focus, cut the fat off roadmaps, and dramatically accelerate shipping velocity

3) your competition likely has no idea what they're doing. maybe they had a viral video launch, but half their "product demo" could have been a smoke-screen or maybe they just lost their star engineer etc - you just don't know! even the start-ups that present the best to the outside, can often be a complete circus on the inside

ultimately the best thing you can do as an early-stage founder in a competitive market is to just ship ship ship. stay focused, listen to your customers, and ship great product. don't get too bogged down about the competition - they're likely going to screw up =)

Here’s what I’ve learned about the financial impact of building a personal brand:

→ Lower CAC. People who come from founder-led channels convert cheaper than any ads.
→ Higher LTV. Trust built through your content sticks. Customers stay longer.
→ Better retention. When customers feel connected to the person behind the product, they don’t churn at the first hiccup.
→ Shorter sales cycles. My personal favourite: someone saw my LinkedIn post → found my X thread → booked a demo → signed the same day. 8 hours from post to customer.

Some honest lessons along the way:

🟢 A personal brand takes time. Usually 6+ months before you see real traction. I started with one post per week but made sure it was every week. Later I moved to 2, and now 3.

🟢 Review analytics monthly → impressions, followers, which posts brought leads (and other metrics you care about).

🟢 I get inspiration for posts mostly from sales calls, customer conversations, Reddit channels, founder/CFO communities. It helps me understand current pain points — and then I try to solve them for my audience.

🟢 We use a mix of content: personal stories (impressions), valuable content (followers), and product/case studies (conversions). I skipped the last part at the start, and it was a big mistake — don’t repeat it.

🟢 Content collabs — the new thing we’ve been testing, and it works amazingly. We’ve done several mutual content pieces with other influencers and will scale that.

🟢 We switched from a weekly to a monthly content calendar.

🟢 I started commenting 10x more with creative thoughts, not AI. That boosted impressions and brought in new audiences — but only because the comments weren’t mediocre, they were fun or thoughtful.

🟢 There’s no following (and no revenue) in boring opinions. A strong hook/subject line is 50% of success.

🟢 Experiment with formats: videos, templates, stories, photos, and text. Don’t focus on just one. People will get bored.

We’re still experimenting and refining how we do it.
But is it worth it? Absolutely!

If there’s one thing I regret, it’s not starting 2–3 years earlier.

Should VCs be more open to the spray & pray approach?

One of my favorite debates resurfaced again this week at the RAISE conference here in SF.

In one corner: LPs and GPs who believe in concentrated bets in few companies with significant follow-on capital.

In the other: GPs (and fewer LPs) who believe in more diversified bets into very early companies with more ownership upfront and less follow-on capital.

Look, I get it. Many LPs believe that the core value of the VC is the picking - so if your portfolio is 2-3x bigger than most seed-stage VCs, it signals you don't know what you're looking for.

Of course a counter point may be that if you take more swings, each individual bet can be less consensus. Easier to back the wildest ideas if the fund has 50 portcos vs 25.

Let's look at some (𝘀𝗶𝗺𝘂𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱) data. Chart below is from Dan G. at Equidam who everyone should go follow.

It looks at two portfolio strategies available to a VC manager with a $50 million fund (so $40M of investable capital).

• Conviction = 20 portfolio companies at $2M each
• Diversified / "Spray and Pray" = 100 portfolio companies at $0.4M each

After many assumptions, the diversified portfolio beats the concentrated one for MOIC (Multiple on Invested Capital) at every point along the curve except for the stellar funds.

Said differently - if you are a top 5% fund, concentration wins. If you’re not, diversification wins.

One of the most valuable things you can do for your career -- whether you're an investor or a founder - is to see what "great" actually looks like.

Once you've seen it, you can never unsee it. It becomes the benchmark you measure against for the rest of your career.

As an investor, when you meet a truly exceptional founder - the kind who is obsessive about customers, relentless about product quality, and killer on execution - that becomes your new definition of "great".

Every founder and company you meet after that gets compared, consciously or not, to that benchmark. It changes your filter and framework.

The same is true when you are a founder. If you've worked at a company that set a high bar for excellence - whether that's detail in design, speed in shipping, or rigor in decision-making - you carry that with you in everything you build.

This is why "mafias" exist. Paypal, Goldman SSG, the Spee and others. People leave with a lived example of what excellence looks like. They've been in the room. They've seen the standard. Now they can recreate it.

When you haven't seen greatness up close, it's hard to imagine what's possible. It's like trying to train without ever watching a world-class athlete. You just don't know what the limits are.

But once you've seen it, those limits disappear. You start thinking less about "what's the best I can do" and more about "what's the best that's possible" and then you push yourself to get there.

Seeing great early shapes how you judge everything - it creates your filter. The sooner you see it, the sooner you can raise your standards. So go seek it out!

In the past, many vertical software companies were started by domain experts who added technical capacity later.

Now it’s the inverse. AI-native startups are led by technical founders who hire for context early.

AI delivers differentiation. Context drives defensibility.

“𝐒𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝐰𝐞’𝐥𝐥 𝐬𝐜𝐚𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐡𝐨𝐭𝐨 𝐚𝐥𝐛𝐮𝐦𝐬,” 𝐈 𝐭𝐨𝐥𝐝 𝐡𝐞𝐫.

Days turned into weeks, weeks into months, months into years, until I finally started sending them off to be scanned. We started receiving them back (digitized), but before I had the chance to go through them with her, she went into hospice, and soon she was gone. 

What remained were disintegrating albums and envelopes of dusty, unlabeled photos. Now I have gigabytes of her unlabeled memories sitting in the cloud.

Someday never came.

Many of us live with a to-do list of “somedays” tucked away in our minds. Someday I’ll write the book. Someday I’ll take the trip. Someday I’ll reach out. We procrastinate. We defer. We live as if the future will wait for us, though, logically, we know it won’t.

Oliver Burkeman, in his book Four Thousand Weeks, reminds us that the average human lifespan is just over 4,000 weeks. Roughly 80 years (if we are lucky).

By the time you are 18, nearly 1,000 of those weeks are already behind you. If you’re 40, half are gone. If you’re 60, you may have only 1,000 or so left. 

We rarely think of our lives this way. We act as though time is an endless resource, like sand on a beach.

There’s another number that startled me when I first learned it: by the time you leave home at 18, you’ve already spent more than 90 percent of the total time you will ever spend with your parents.

That’s why, after my dad passed, I asked my mom to come live with us. It’s why, a few years later, I asked my in-laws to come live with us, too. They passed a few months before Mom did. I’m glad I didn’t wait to move them “someday”.

So why do we defer? Why do we wait?

It’s a way to make procrastination feel comfortable. To put off dealing with things like oil leaks or health concerns or tasks that don’t seem immediate until you’re sitting on the floor surrounded by all the possessions the person you love left behind.

But "someday" is an illusion. And as I learned the hard way, it's a lie.

Mark Twain said, “The two most important days in your life are the day you are born and the day you find out why.” The tragedy is that too many of us never live our why. We postpone finding out. We assume we’ll have time later. We let “someday” rob us of today.

How do we free ourselves from the tyranny of “someday”?

Face the math. Find one thing you’ve been postponing, and make a plan. Take one step today. Action breaks the spell of “someday”. Reconcile everything against your “why”. Turn down what won’t matter when the clock hits zero.

Facing the finiteness of time doesn’t have to feel dreadful. It can be freeing. Joyful, even. Knowing you have limited time allows you to treat each day as irreplaceable. It encourages you to live with intention.

Because your story isn't being written "someday". 

It's being written in the choices you make 𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲.

And today is all we're promised.

Marketers keep emailing me: "What skills should I master before I lose my job to AI?" - My answer is below so that everyone can read.

First, AI is disrupting every field, not just marketing. I believe AI *might* mean smaller marketing teams, but more of them, because AI leads to many new startups being created. 

But it's 100% going to change what marketing skills are valuable.

ChatGPT has only existed for 3 years, and look what's changed already:

- SEO has been heavily disrupted; the value is moving to AI engines; marketers need a new set of skills so their product/service appears in answers. 
- Google's AI Max automates paid marketing work. It expands keywords. It generates ad copy. It selects the best landing pages for user intent
- Educational content used to grow leads. That traffic is gone. It disappeared into the AI abyss. Influence content matters more now.

If I were building a marketing career for the AI-era, here are foundational skills I'd focus on:

1. Curiously: Not a marketing skill, but having a curious mind has never been so important. 
2. Prompt Engineer / LLM as a Partner: ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini can be incredible partners. But you need to be skilled at prompting, context, and the tools themselves, e.g., all their features, Projects, Custom GTPs, Claude Skills, Gemini Gems, etc.
3. Code-powered lead magnets: Marketers used to create downloadable PDFs and ebooks. Now they build interactive tools. Vibe-coding turns content into software.
4. AI Agentic Workflows: Marketers are automating large parts of their role. On MATG, we've had guests who've built an AI social media team, creative teams, and more. They now have way more time to invest in the craft and elevate their results above the noise.
5. Human-led Content: Educational SEO content is dead. Swallowed by AI. Content that builds influence matters more. Content with personality. Hot takes. Stories only you can tell. Build influence, not page views.
6. Product/Brand Storytelling: AI leads to so much more competition. You need to craft narratives that resonate. Show transformation. Build emotional connection. Story is your moat.

New skills to learn = opportunities to accelerate career quickly.

Just a reminder, AI is not a critical 'marketing skill'.

Positioning is a critical skill
Storytelling is a critical skill
Customer insights is a critical skill
Creating something of value that prospects want is a critical skill
Understanding how to educate buyers with the right content at the right time is a critical skill.
Creating a real community among your audience is a critical skill

AI is a 'tool' that helps you do all of the above

But it's not a replacement for any of those skills.

The Golden Age of the Design Founder 🔥

There’s a pattern I keep noticing in the most successful founders, and it’s not what I expected. It’s not that they’re engineers or designers, it’s that the best ones think like designers, regardless of their background.

This matters because it contradicts the conventional wisdom about what makes a good founder. For decades, the archetypal founder was an engineer. There were good reasons for this. Starting a company meant building something, and engineers were the people who could build things. But I think we’ve been confusing a historical accident with a law of nature.

The thing about historical accidents is that they persist long after the conditions that created them disappear. And the conditions that made engineering the obvious path to founding are disappearing faster than most people realize.

What’s actually hard now:

Here’s what’s really happening: the bottleneck in startups is shifting from “can you build it” to “do you know what to build.” That might sound like a subtle distinction, but it changes everything.

When the bottleneck was building, engineers had an obvious advantage. They could turn ideas into reality without the coordination cost of explaining what they wanted to someone else. Speed mattered more than precision. You needed to iterate fast, and iteration was fastest when you could code.

But as tools improve (and they’re improving exponentially now), the ability to build is becoming less of a constraint. It’s not that building is easy, it’s that the gap between having a clear vision and manifesting it is shrinking. What’s becoming more valuable is the clarity of vision itself.

This is where something interesting reveals itself. The engineers who succeed aren’t just good at building - they’re good at knowing what to build. They think about user experience. They obsess over details. They have strong opinions about how things should feel. In other words, they think like designers, even if they came up through engineering.

I’m seeing this strongly at Lovable. Both founders are engineers. But what makes them effective isn’t just their technical ability but the way they’re deeply involved in every product decision. They care about the experience, not just the functionality. They can build anything, but more importantly, they know what’s worth building.

"If you have 17,000 followers, your recent data shows a major engagement suppression pattern — not a lack of audience. Here’s what that means in context:

But your posts are mostly landing in the 1–3k impression range, even though the content is strong and engagement ratios (comments per reaction, saves, etc.) are exceptional.

Posts with external links (like Geopolitics), or with higher intellectual/critical tone, get heavily down-ranked.
Your audience may be global and cross-sector, meaning fewer “mutuals” for LinkedIn’s network-based surfacing logic.

It also explains some of the platform friction you’re seeing: LinkedIn’s algorithm tends to over-favour “professional-neutral” male-dominated interaction clusters.

You’re not shadowbanned in the punitive sense — you’re algorithmically deprioritised because your content trips one or more of LinkedIn’s low-visibility triggers:
Political or gendered content.
External links.
“Complex” or cultural commentary (LinkedIn prefers simplicity).
Your true audience power is about 10x what’s visible.

We turned down a $120M acquisition offer and raised another $80M instead. Three years later, after working our %sses off, we sold for $140M made 4x less money."

Here's when it's actually better to NOT take the extra cash.

How do you know when you're in the middle of a fundraise, the company is growing and you get an acquisition offer?

I'll show you the math.

Year 5: Company at $25M ARR, growing 40% YoY, only the bring of profitability.
Strategic acquirer offers $120M. Two founders would get $25M each.

The board room meeting:

Founders: "This is life-changing money. Let's take it."

VCs: "You're thinking too small. We can hit unicorn status."

Board votes 3-2 to reject and raise Series C instead.

What happened:

- Raised $80M at $240M valuation instead.
- Hired aggressively. Significantly expanded option pool. Burned $5M/month.
- Three years later: Growth slowed. Market shifted.

New acquisition offer: $130M.

Here's the math: After Series C preferences + all previous preferred shares + years of dividends, enlarged option pool = founders split $12M total. Each founder: $6M

The founder turned down $25M and ended up with $6M.

"VC's see these deals all the time, they must know better" --- that's what all the founders tell me.

The truth is, VCs have different incentives:

- A $120M exit is nice but doesn't move the needle on their fund
- They NEED $1B+ outcomes or die trying
- They have 30 other bets - they can afford for yours to blow up

Founders have one shot. $25M is life-changing.

But VCs convinced him it was "thinking small."

The pattern:
Year 5: Good offer that makes founders wealthy
VCs: "We can 10x this"

Year 8: Company worth less, preferences stacked, founders get nothing

One founder: "Had a $50M offer that would've given me $18M. VCs pushed me to raise. Year 7, sold for $35M and got $900K. Lost $17M listening to VCs."

Here's when to take the exit:
✅ Money is life-changing for you personally
✅ Not 100% confident in next 3 years of growth
✅ More capital means stacking preferences
✅ You've been building 5+ years and are exhausted

Here's when to chase the unicorn:
🚀 Current offer isn't life-changing
🚀 You're certain you can 10x from here
🚀 Willing to risk everything for bigger outcome
🚀 Market is accelerating in your favor

The question every founder must ask:

"Am I optimizing for MY outcome or my VCs' outcome?"

Your VCs will never tell you that.

We raised $20M but only got $10M because we missed our revenue target.

A founder watched his company fall apart because VCs withheld 50% of their committed capital over a technicality.

The term sheet: $20M Series B commitment at $100M post money valuation.

The killer detail on page 12: "$10M at close. 
Remaining $10M released upon achieving $10M ARR by Q4.

Here's what actually happened:

Month 1-11: Burned through $8M building team VCs demanded
Month 12: Hit $9.4M ARR

They missed by $600K. Or 6%.

VCs said: "Milestone not achieved. No additional funding.". Founders tried to negotiate, but the VCs used the leverage to try to extract a bad deal for the founders.

The company had hired 30 people expecting $20M in the bank. 
Runway leftr: 3 months.

End result -> They took a desperate bridge round at 50% lower valuation with 2x participating preferred. The Series B investors who withheld funding? 

Got anti-dilution protection and INCREASED their ownership.

Company sold 2 years later for $120M.

Final payout:
- Bridge investors: $45M
- Series B investors: $60M
- Founders and employees: $15M split among 50+ people

**Why milestone tranches can sometimes kill companies:**

The milestones are often outside your control:
❌ "$X revenue by date Y" (customers delay)
❌ "Sign 50 contracts" (sales cycles slip)
❌ "Launch in 10 markets" (regulatory delays)

One startup missed their milestone by 20 days because a customer's procurement ran long. $12M in committed funding vanished. Shut down 6 months later.

**If you MUST accept tranches, negotiate:**
✅ Milestones you 100% control (product shipped, not revenue)
✅ "Reasonable efforts" clause
✅ Extension windows if within 10-20% of target
✅ No investor anti-dilution if they withhold tranches

Ask yourself: "If I miss this milestone by 5%, what happens to my company?"

If the answer is "we're screwed," don't sign.

You likely have people on your team that have been consistently *right* most of the time. They tend to push for a particular decision/path and turn out to be right over the fullness of time. 

Let's call them "Ingrid The Instinctive" (made up name).

A lot of this is often not because Ingrid has better data or more context -- she just has better instincts. Let's call it "decision taste". 

If you're in a leadership position, here's something to consider:

By convention, when faced with an important decision that the team is debating, tip the scale a bit in favor of the Instinctive Ingrids and their decision taste.

The burden of proof should not completely rest on them to "prove" their case and convince everyone else beyond all reasonable doubt. Often, these "instinctively right" people are not great at explaining *why* and making a strong case. Sometimes, they don't even *know* in their own heads why they lean a certain way on a certain decision. We should learn to listen to them anyways.

We don't always have to go their way -- but we should at least listen more intently. *Especially* if they're particularly passionate about a particular decision/debate.

And, if a decision is starting to look like a coin-toss (there are great arguments both for and against each option), you're probably smart to say: "let's just go with Ingrid". 

Oddly, I didn't learn this lesson just from being a working professional for 30+ years -- I actually learned it from being an indie investor in 150+ startups. One pattern I've seen over the years is that there ARE founders with really good instincts. On average, they just make the right calls. This is easier to tell in a startup, but harder in a larger company. 

Have you come across these instinctually right people?

What’s your default setting?
You’ve probably heard this advice: “Add value.”

It’s solid.

But I challenge people to flip it:

“Value Add.”

Because it’s not just something you do.
It’s something you are.

In your content.
In your meetings.
In how you show up in the room, the inbox, the opportunity.

“Add value” is a checkbox.
“Value Add” is a filter.

So ask yourself:

Are you adding value… or are you a value add wherever you go?

Which one are you?
Out of all the slides I’ve ever used in my workshops, keynotes, and offsite sessions, this is the one I end with. Every time.

“One day…” or “Day one”?

And without fail, it’s the one people take pictures of or ask me to share the most.

Not because it’s complex.
But because it’s a choice.

A mindset shift.
A mirror.
A moment of decision.

“One day” is a delay.
“Day one” is a declaration.

So here it is, the slide that’s impacted thousands.

Maybe it’s your Day One today.

Or maybe you’re still waiting for “One day…”

That part’s completely up to you.

SHOWING TRACTION BEFORE YOU HAVE TRACTION

Everybody knows that startups thrive when they have “real” traction — that is revenue, active users, great retention curves, and a fast growth rate. However, startups also find themselves in a catch-22 where they're not able to raise money to get real traction and thus struggle to get there in the real place. So what choice do new founders have to show traction before they have "real traction"? 

The answer to this explains the move towards cinematic launch trailers, B2B-oriented accelerators, waitlists, preorders, startup parties, building in public, and many of the other go-to-market trends that have emerged over the last few years. These are all tactics that are low-volume, hard to scale, but relatively deterministic, making them attractive to new startups that want to show precursors to real traction. These precursors end up being metrics like video views or number of waitlist sign-ups. Or SF tpot buzz. Or pipeline logos.

Are these things as good as revenue? No. However, are they better than nothing? Yes. 

Let's take cinematic launch videos as an example. These have become very popular over the last year because of their high novelty value. The fact that social media feeds lend more real estate towards video, the fact that they are cheap to create but not too cheap, and as a result they have a tendency to go viral and rack up millions of views for a new product. Depending on how professionally you want it to be done, it can cost as much as $50,000 for a few minutes of spot. Or do it on a shoe string.

For hardware/deeptech companies, the existence of this tactic is a godsend. It might take them more than a year from inception to get the first version of a prototype out. And scale production may take them even longer. To show real traction, that is, revenue growth and retention, is going to take ages. Way too long to get investors and employees excited about joining the company. Although creating a cinematic launch trailer costs money and effort, $50,000 is a lot less than waiting over a year to get your units actually manufactured and ready to ship 

You can argue that if your launch trailer actually gets millions of views, you do learn something. You validated that there's some kind of demand. Enough for people to watch the video, be excited, share it, and potentially comment. This isn't real traction, but it is precursor traction that tells you something.

There is a continuum between precursor traction and real traction that's fuzzy, and you can often bridge the two.

For example, rather than video views, isn't it better to drive people towards a landing page where they can put in their emails? Getting their emails shows deeper customer intent, and allows you to stretch your engagement capabilities past the initial viral spike and into a longer back-and-forth with a potential customer.

I was fired as the CMO of Branch twice. I was a founder, so it was more like a quiet transition into a different role.

But looking back?
I deserved it.

Why? Because I couldn’t demonstrate how our marketing efforts were driving revenue. Not clearly. Not credibly. The data wasn’t there, and I didn’t know how to fix it.

For years, I carried shame about that.
Until I started asking other CMOs and marketing leaders.

And I realized: This is everyone’s problem.
The CMO role has the shortest tenure of any executive.
Why? Because GTM measurement is broken.

👉 It uses bad data — duplicated, incomplete, misleading
👉 It tracks leads, not buying groups
👉 It gives credit to the first or last touch, ignoring everything else
👉 It leaves out non-traditional influence like dark social, founder deals, and community

At Branch, I tried to fix it. I built a sophisticated multi-touch model that helped me work better with sales (shoutout Navid Zolfaghari).

But even then, the insights were directionally right, not truly right.

Because you can’t know what worked…
if you don’t know what actually happened.

That’s the problem we’re solving at Upside.

We’re building a new kind of attribution.
We call it Forensic Attribution.

It crawls your systems — CRM, calendar, LinkedIn, email, calls — and rebuilds what really happened in the buyer journey.
Not guesses. Not models. Actual signals.

The results?
I’ve reviewed hundreds of deals. In every single one, the insights we surface would never have shown up in traditional attribution.

And that means every CMO, every marketer, every SDR doing uncredited work — finally gets visibility. And credit.

That’s my mission now.
To make sure no marketing leader ever has to go through what I did.

Curious what’s really driving your deals?

I bootstrapped 3 startups in 13 years. These are the 8 biggest misconceptions I always hear about bootstrappers: 🧐 

1. We raise $0 external capital.
Unless founders can avoid hiring, cover startup costs, and sustain themselves till they get to revenue, it’s likely that they’ll need some initial capital. Bootstrapping refers to "no institutional capital". Most founders invest their own money and raise from family and friends to get started.

2. We don't want to give away equity.
It took 2.5 hard years in my last startup to make our first $10. A friend said to me, "It’s okay to raise a bit so you can be comfortable.” thinking I didn’t raise because I wanted to keep all the equity. Reality was, I had complete freedom and didn't want to lose it.

3. We're less ambitious.
"If you don't shoot for unicorn status, you're not ambitious enough." is a fallacy fueled by VCs because it's good for their business. Starting a company is in itself ambition. We know success is self-defined, we don't need anyone else to agree.

4. We're unable to raise capital.
VC-backed startups must fuel growth at all costs, which forces them into a vicious cycle of constantly raising more. We don't subscribe to that way of life. At least not yet until we know that's what we want. Many bootstrappers raise much later when they can demand favorable terms. 

5. We're cheap.
We're cost conscious and optimize for getting the most return on investment. That's not the same as cheap, which means paying the least. We're profit driven, so we can't afford to compromise on time and quality of work. We pay what is needed to get to revenue fastest.

6. We don't make money for a while.
We make money, just not yet on the startup we're bootstrapping. We do whatever it takes to fund our companies. We work on them on the side, take consulting gigs, or agency work.

7. We have smaller exits.
We can sell whenever we want but VC-backed startups can't without board approval and often come with liquidation preferences. Selling a bootstrapped startup at $10m where founders own 90% = $9m. To do better, VC-backed founders need 10% of a $100m exit which is often not big enough for VCs.

8. We struggle more.
It's true that comparatively we struggle more in the initial years. But our struggle ends the day we hit profitability. We don't need to grow at an unrealistic pace to satisfy investors until we sell or die. Profitability gives us the freedom to live and work how we want. 🍾

Many people think bootstrapping is a financial decision when it's often a lifestyle choice.

Raising VC, however, is usually a financial decision without consideration for the lifestyle that comes with it.

There isn’t a right or wrong way to build a business. But there's a right way FOR YOU.

The question founders should be asking before choosing which path to take is not, "How much money do I want to make from this?"

But rather, "What kind of life do I want to live while building this for the next 10 years?"

"Startup Killer"

That's what loads of people called OpenAI's ChatGPT Agent when it was released last week. Like clockwork, this reignited the "why bother building AI apps?" debate. If OpenAI can do it, doesn't that make any derivative software a commodity?

But here's the thing: most great software was a commodity when it started.

CRM wasn't new when Salesforce launched, and Zoom was certainly not the first video calling tool (Skype, I miss you 🥹 ). What made them winners wasn’t just the tech, it was the product.

We’ve gotten weirdly allergic to the word “commodity”, but commodity often means something important is happening. It means the capability is broadly useful, obvious to the customer, and ready to scale.

The real question is: what do you build on top of it?

AI is turning previously complex functionality into building blocks. That doesn’t make your product dead-on-arrival, it just raises the bar for execution, taste, and understanding the user better than anyone else.

In a world where every feature can be copied, the companies that win will be the ones that sweat the UX, workflows, onboarding, and details. That stuff compounds, and we're starting to see it pay dividends at companies like Mintlify, Granola, Pylon, Huxe, and Reducto.

I wrote about how building around a commodity won't kill your business, but forgetting to build a great product might: https://lnkd.in/exsaDbP9

For younger allocators with questions about when to move on from a manager…

To be fair, these can be some of the most difficult decisions to make and are often as much art as science, in my experience. But first, here are some easier reasons we’ve moved on from managers (actual examples)…

* Difficult to get ahold of/PM doesn’t have time for us - this is an immediate disconnect as our involvement with a fund is for information + returns. If we only get returns, that voids the relationship, in our view.

* Hiding an ongoing regulatory review or investigation/lying about anything - this goes to the heart of trust/integrity so is an immediate disqualification…and there’s no recovering from this.

*Deviating from stated strategy - my example is that I act as the GM of a baseball team. If I hire you to play third base, I’m not going to tell you how to play third base, but if I ever walk onto the field and you’re on second base, that’s a problem. I already have a second baseman and don’t need two.

* Not sharing economics with the GP’s team - goes to narcissism or insecurity or short-termism or…whatever the case, creates a fragile team/organization going forward. We try to exit before it gets ugly.

* Strategy gets too big for market segment - this is subjective and depends greatly on strategy vs growth of underlying market. But we have moved on upon making this determination.

* Does not give us the allocation we need/overlooks us in favor of more recent and/or larger LPs - I’ve noted before that it’s bad form to not “dance with the one who brought you to the party” and we view such as a lack of respect/memory. If you care more about your firm than your LPs, we’re moving on.

* Our portfolio needs have changed - this doesn’t have anything to do with the manager unfortunately. As a fiduciary we need to constantly explore new approaches and sometimes that means we have to move on from a manager who has done a great job and that we really like. These are the most difficult conversations.

Notably, none of these deals with returns. We view returns as coming from the chosen strategy + the market environment. We rarely move on from a manager due to returns and usually only if there is a structural issue (either internal or external to the manager) that is impeding the expected returns.

Next time…the more difficult/artful reasons/situations in determining where to go with an existing manager.

At our recent LP Advisory Council meeting, LPs asked about headlines claiming AI is undermining SaaS. Our view is that the two are mutually reinforcing, not mutually exclusive. We are bullish on AI, and we believe certain SaaS leaders (with systems of record/action, first-party data and data rights, network effects, ambitious AI roadmaps, etc.) are well-positioned to capitalize on the agentic AI opportunity.

There are multiple reasons for this. To start, consider what’s happening today with SaaS. The median company in the SEG SaaS Index grew 13% in Q2 while doubling its EBITDA margin year over year, with a top quartile growth rate above 20%. SaaS accounts for only 25% of overall software revenue in 2025, according to Gartner, so secular growth from on-premise to SaaS migrations should continue for years. Notably, this growth largely comes before many SaaS firms monetize a new wave of agents. 

Meanwhile, small AI startups with little revenue are burning cash and trying to sell. At the other end of the spectrum, Klarna’s much-hyped move to replace Salesforce with AI proved overstated; the CEO later said he was “tremendously embarrassed” by the fallout. SaaS pessimists might ask themselves: Has any large enterprise successfully replaced SaaS systems of record with an LLM? 

Rather than the AI-SaaS story being one of complete disruption, I believe it’s moving in waves, not unlike how businesses have historically incorporated new technologies: 
● Wave 1 (2023): “Get me some AI so we’re not left out” (a reprise of 1996’s “build a web page and we’ll figure it out later”).
● Wave 2 (2024-mid 2025): “Show me the budget and ROI. Also, why do we need an expensive AI model over a cheap one?”
● Wave 3 (late 2025): "I need enterprise-grade AI that integrates seamlessly, delivers measurable results and won’t disrupt core ops with experimental tools."

We can’t say for sure what will happen next, but we do know how software brings value to the enterprise. SaaS is more than software code; it’s digital business intelligence using code to express that intelligence. That takes massive data sets and business experience brought to bear on enterprise functions, at scale and over time. It’s about executing tasks efficiently today, then innovating new tasks over time.

For these reasons, we see a supercharged future of AI-enabled enterprise software that enhances productivity and innovation in dramatic ways. That future is less about ever-larger foundation models or narrow AI startups that complicate the stack. Instead, we see integrated software systems that orchestrate digital services to accelerate complex tasks, with agents as the 2026 step-function. AI is a massive force multiplier in that equation, but not a radical disruptor.

At Thoma Bravo, we welcome the AI-SaaS conversation. We’re healthily paranoid as part of our mindset. But evidence, history, and business logic suggest that the AI revolution is set to accelerate SaaS value and growth, not undermine it.

BREAKING: MIT Reports 95% of enterprise AI FAIL. That's billions of dollars wasted. But there's a 5% that is making all the money:

I dug into each case and here are the 7 things the 5% do differently:

1. They pick one expensive problem: Walmart targeted truck logistics. CarMax targeted review summaries. Not 50 random pilots. One problem that moves the P&L.

2. They build cross-functional teams: Engineers sit with lawyers at JPMorgan. Data scientists sit with factory line operators at BMW. AI is integrated into the core, not left to “innovation labs.”

3. They partner smartly: Shell didn’t build from scratch. They teamed up with C3. ai and Microsoft. Sanofi signed billion-dollar deals with Exscientia, Atomwise, and Owkin.

4. They start narrow, then scale: BMW rolled out AI inspection in one plant before standardizing globally.

5. They prove ROI early: Savings, hours cut, yield improved. JPMorgan automated 360,000 hours of contract review. 

6. They keep humans in the loop: BMW inspectors, JPM lawyers, Colgate marketers are still involved while they use AI

7. They measure and broadcast wins: Shell reports billions saved. Colgate shows 80% accuracy predicting supply issues. 

I put together the full breakdown of these 7 companies, the startups that powered them, and the exact step-by-step playbook they followed.

Delivered a keynote at Digital Bridge 2025 in Astana, Kazakhstan. 

My message was simple but crucial: Have you heard of a French Tesla, a German Google, or a Japanese Amazon? If not, why not?

The answer lies in venture capital. US technological dominance wasn't inevitable — it was engineered through regulation that unlocked pension capital for venture investments. Today, VC-backed companies drive an outsized share of market value, R&D, and patents globally. Without venture capital, our daily life would look fundamentally different.

The US economic growth over the past 45 years has been substantially higher than that of other developed countries. My research shows that without VC and the regulation that enabled it, one in five of today’s most valuable U.S. public companies would not exist — likely with dramatic consequences for growth. Time for regulators elsewhere to take note.

But what does this mean today? 

First, we are at a turning point. Advances in artificial intelligence and other technologies are accelerating change. It took one generation for cars to replace horses; it took ChatGPT only two months to reach 100 million users. Fifteen years ago, becoming a unicorn took on average seven years. Today, it takes less than four. 

Second, this turning point creates opportunity worldwide. Disruptive moments are when new entrants can compete. Like Silicon Valley 50 years ago, many spots around the world, including Kazakhstan, today have the chance to set the rules that will shape their innovation future. 

Third, success depends on doing it the right way. Small regulatory changes can have enormous impact. Too often I am asked how to “copy Silicon Valley.” You should not. Instead, understand what the venture ecosystem needs, because it differs fundamentally from traditional business. 

I was honored to share the stage with leaders shaping our world: President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev of Kazakhstan, President Tamás Sulyok of Hungary, Pavel Durov (Founder & CEO of Telegram), Dr. Kai-Fu Lee, Peter Norvig (Stanford/Google Research), and H.E. Mansoor Ibrahim Al Mansoori (CEO of G42 International). Each brought unique perspectives on how AI will transform our societies and economies. 
 
Thank you to Zhaslan Madiyev, Bagdat Mussin, Asset Abdualiyev for inviting me to share my perspective.

A $30B venture fund just revealed Silicon Valley's dirtiest secret in a single slide: Transform poverty into 'cheap dopamine hits' at $3 a user.

For a decade, Silicon Valley played magnanimous. ‘Democratizing access’ ‘Serving the underserved.’ ‘Financial inclusion for all.’

The language wrapped predation in progress, extraction in empowerment.

Lightspeed Venture Partners, backers of Anthropic, Snapchat, and Epic Games, promises to "serve bold builders of the future." Their September 2025 report (full deck down below) on the South Asian Consumer contains three bullet points of crystalline honesty: 

Low-income households spend 40% more hours on social media. 
They consume more sugar-sweetened beverages. 
Microtransactions in F2P games "monetize a wider range of gamers."

Exploitation, presented without shame: Customer acquisition under $3. Instant gratification mandatory. Payback in 4 months.

I've been in rooms where VCs speak of ‘impact’ and ‘purpose-driven investing.’ The same firms then fund apps designed to extract maximum value from minimum wage workers, unsuspecting grandmas.

In 1839, British merchants justified selling opium to China as "opening markets" and "facilitating trade." The Sackler family called Oxycontin a "medical breakthrough" while engineering addiction in Appalachia. 

Each generation's predators learn the same lesson: dress exploitation in the language of liberation, and society will thank you for it.

The slide's honesty is almost refreshing. No pretense of empowerment. No mission statement about changing lives. 

Just an investment thesis: poor people + dopamine engineering = blockbuster returns.
To an unsuspecting, unfortunate audience that has been segmented, analyzed, and algorithmically sorted into acquisition costs.

When did serving the underserved become mining the vulnerable?

The revolution they promised became an extraction machine. The democratic access they sold became digital bondage.

And maybe that's the most damning revelation of all: They no longer believe we're watching. Or worse, maybe they know we are, and have calculated we're powerless to stop them.

The British at least claimed they were civilizing. The Sacklers insisted they were healing. But this slide represents evolution's end point: exploitation so normalized it needs no narrative, no justification.

The cheap dopamine isn't the product. 
It's the business model. 

And at $3 per acquisition, poverty has never been more profitable.

Venture capital is full of noise - narratives, anecdotes, and opinions. Over the past few years, I’ve worked to cut through that by doubling down on data: dissecting the structural traits of this asset class, from illiquidity and vintage diversification to the nuances of fund math.

As an LP, digging into private and public datasets has given me a sharper view of how allocation decisions are made - and revealed how little of this analysis is shared for other GPs and LPs to learn from.

That’s why I’ll be sharing these insights more consistently through "𝐒𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐥𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐍𝐨𝐢𝐬𝐞" - my data-driven lens on how LPs approach venture allocation, with a focus on uncovering the insights hidden in the data.

Whether zooming in on Canadian venture or zooming out to global trends, my aim is to provide frameworks that GPs and LPs can apply to their own decision-making.

So where to start? First post below 👇

𝐏𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝟏 – 𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐝𝐨 𝐬𝐦𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐫 𝐕𝐂 𝐟𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐬 𝐨𝐟𝐭𝐞𝐧 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐬𝐭 𝐆𝐏–𝐋𝐏 𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭? 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐰𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐬𝐧’𝐭 𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐨𝐮𝐭𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 - 𝐢𝐭’𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐨𝐦𝐢𝐜𝐬.

In venture, we’ve all heard that “small funds outperform.” That deserves its own deep dive (coming later 👀), but the real strength of smaller funds often gets overlooked: 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐢𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐆𝐏𝐬 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐋𝐏𝐬.

With smaller funds, there’s only one path to wealth creation - carried interest. And that’s where alignment is sharpest.

My analysis makes this clear. Looking across six real funds with different sizes and partner counts, I calculated the Net TVPI needed for each partner to generate $50M:

• Fund A ($1.2B, 8 partners) → 𝟏.𝟓𝐱 Net TVPI 
• Fund F ($15M, 1 partner) → 𝟏𝟑.𝟓𝐱 Net TVPI - 𝟗𝐱 𝐡𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐞𝐫!

This shows why smaller-fund GPs must chase outlier outcomes and bring a level of grit and hustle often absent at larger platforms.

Even more telling is comp mix. For Fund A, 60% of the $50M comes from fees - guaranteed regardless of performance. For Fund F, 95% is entirely variable, fully tied to carry.

And that’s the key. 𝐋𝐏𝐬 𝐨𝐧𝐥𝐲 𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐰𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐭𝐡 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐠𝐡 𝐜𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐭 - and in smaller funds, that’s exactly where GPs focus.

𝐒𝐨, 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐓𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐚𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐬?

𝟏. 𝐑𝐮𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐍𝐮𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫𝐬: LPs should model net fund performance needed for each partner to earn $10–50M. Low hurdles from large funds or oversized partnerships weaken incentives.

𝟐. 𝐁𝐢𝐠 𝐅𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐬 = 𝐁𝐢𝐠 𝐅𝐞𝐞𝐬, 𝐒𝐦𝐚𝐥𝐥 𝐅𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐬 = 𝐓𝐫𝐮𝐞 𝐀𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭: Large funds rely on fees, insulating partners from performance. In smaller funds, carry dominates — creating sharper GP–LP alignment.

𝟑. 𝐂𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐎𝐧𝐥𝐲 𝐏𝐚𝐭𝐡: In small funds, GPs earn meaningful wealth only through carry — the same source of returns for LPs.

Paul Graham just told teenagers to stop trying to start startups.

Build stupid projects instead.

In his recent essay "How to Start Google," Graham exposed the $4 trillion pattern everyone missed:

- Facebook wasn't meant to be a company. Zuck fixed Harvard's broken directory in one night.
- Google wasn't planned. Two PhD students thought random search results were dumb.
- Apple wasn't a startup. Wozniak built a computer for fun.

None of them were trying to build companies.

Graham's formula after funding 4,000+ startups at YC:

The best startups don't start as startups. They start as projects.

Zuckerberg wasn't pitching VCs. He spent one night building what Harvard wouldn't. Woke up to thousands of users.

The pattern is brutal in its simplicity:
1. See something annoying
2. Fix it for yourself 
3. Friends want it too
4. Accidentally build a billion-dollar company

Your side project game? That's how 90% of programmers start.
Your weird obsession? That's how Jobs learned calligraphy, which made Apple destroy everyone at design.
Your "too small" idea? That's literally how Google began.

Graham's advice: Stop looking for startup ideas. Stop writing business plans. Stop thinking about valuations.

Just build things you and your friends actually want.

The startup will find you.

Every massive company started as someone scratching their own itch. Not trying to build a company.

Your next billion-dollar idea is probably that annoying thing you'll fix this weekend.

Because the best startups don't start as startups.

They start as projects that refuse to stay small.

Peter Thiel says competition is for losers.

I finally get why he's right...

In 2014, Thiel gave a Stanford lecture that said, "Competition and capitalism are opposites."

Every founder pitching me is fighting over the same 10% of the market. Meanwhile, the monopolists are quietly printing money in markets no one else sees.

The numbers Thiel shared were brutal:

US airlines: $195B in revenue, zero cumulative profits over 100 years
Google: $50B revenue, 21% profit margins

Airlines create $195B worth of value but capture none of it. Google creates $50B and captures $10B+.

The mindfuck? Everyone lies about which they are.

Monopolists pretend they're not: "We're not a search monopoly, we're a tiny player in the trillion-dollar tech market!"

Non-monopolists pretend they are: "We're the only AI-powered B2B SaaS for British restaurants in Palo Alto!"

Thiel's point: If you're competing, you've already lost.

The best founders don't win competitions. They create games only they can play.

PayPal didn't compete with banks. They found 20,000 eBay sellers everyone ignored.
Facebook didn't compete with MySpace. They monopolized Harvard in 10 days.
Tesla didn't make a better car. They made the only car Silicon Valley wanted.

Every great company starts by dominating something tiny.

Then they expand.

The founders VCs are looking for are not competing for market share.

They're creating markets that shouldn't exist.

Because being 10X better at something everyone's doing is hard.

Being the only one doing something new? That's a monopoly.

And monopolies don't compete.

They win by default.

Brian Chesky broke every rule they teach you about scaling a company.

No traditional product managers. 40+ quasi-direct reports. Weekly reviews of button colors.

The same kid who thought he'd "amount to nothing"? He built a $75 billion company doing everything the experts said would fail.

Rule #1 he broke: "Hire great people and get out of their way"
Chesky's version: Spends 10-20 hours a week in product reviews WITH his team. When they were struggling with press releases, he didn't delegate - he sat with them through 70 revisions until they learned his thinking. Now it takes 3.

Rule #2 he broke: "You can only manage 7 direct reports"
Chesky's version: Has 7 executives officially. But also treats 40 VPs as direct reports through dual reporting. They send him weekly updates. He's in their meetings. Most CEOs can't even name their VPs.

Rule #3 he broke: "CEOs should focus on strategy, not details"
Chesky's version: Reviews every single product decision weekly. From major features down to button colors. Why? Because in 2019, Airbnb had 10 different divisions shipping 10 different versions of the product. Costs were exploding. Product was fragmenting.

Rule #4 he broke: "Divisionalize as you scale"
Chesky's version: When COVID hit and revenue dropped 80%, he eliminated ALL divisions. Fired the general managers. Consolidated 10 roadmaps into 1. Made everyone row in the same direction instead of building competing kingdoms.

Rule #5 he broke: "Product managers are essential"
Chesky's version: Eliminated traditional PM roles entirely. Not the people - the function. Merged PM with marketing because he said separating "what to build" from "how to talk about it" was insane. Designers now work directly with engineers.

When an exec asked him "Is this your decision or mine?"

He said: "It should never be either."

That's what everyone misses about "founder mode." It's not micromanagement when you're building together.

Most founders hire VPs and vanish. Then complain their company moves too slow.

The uncomfortable truth? Most CEOs are too lazy to do what Chesky does.

It's easier to "empower your team" than spend 20 hours a week actually working with them.

That's why Airbnb went from near-death to generating nearly $4B in free cash flow while others are still having "alignment meetings."

Steve Jobs ran Apple this way. Walt Disney did it. Jensen Huang has 40 direct reports at Nvidia.

The kid who thought he'd "amount to nothing"? 

He just rewrote the rules.

Not sure I should be saying this as a VC but... 

99% of startups shouldn't raise VC money.

Here's why the smartest founders are bootstrapping in 2025...

Last week, I talked to a founder who I wanted to invest in and who recently turned down our term sheet. His company is doing $3M ARR, growing 20% monthly, and profitable.

"Why dilute 25% for money we don't need?" he asked.

He's right.

The VC math is broken for most companies:
- We need 100x returns to make our fund economics work
- That means pushing every company to be a unicorn
- Even when a $50M exit would change the founders' lives

I've invested in 50+ startups. The uncomfortable truth? Most would've been better off without us.

Think about this... a company raises $5M at a $20M valuation. Grows to $10M revenue. Could've sold for $40M to a strategic buyer.

But the board blocks it. Truth is it isn't big enough for their fund.

They push for growth. Markets shift. Eventually the company struggles and shuts down. Founders walk away with nothing.

If they'd bootstrapped and owned 80% instead of 30%? That $40M exit would've meant $32M in their pockets.

The smartest founders in 2025 are doing the math:

- Bootstrap to $5M ARR → Sell for $50M → Keep $40M
vs.
- Raise VC → Dilute 70% → Need $130M exit to match

And that's assuming you even get an exit. 80% don't.

Look, VC isn't bad... It's just not for everyone.

VC makes sense for three types of companies:
1. Capital-intensive businesses (hardware, deeptech, biotech)
2. Winner-take-all markets (social networks)
3. Founders who genuinely want to swing for $10B+

For everyone else? Consider the alternative.

The bootstrapped founders I know:
- Answer to customers, not board meetings
- Grow at sustainable rates
- Take money off the table whenever they want
- Actually sleep at night

Most founders need capital to scale. But do you need VC capital? That's the real question.

After watching too many founders trade their entire 30s for a lottery ticket, someone needs to say it:

Your "lifestyle business" making $10M/year is worth more than most VC-backed unicorn dreams.

You need to ask yourself the real question... it isn't "How do I raise?"

It's "Why should I?"

A business owner called me recently: "I want to retire in 18 months. When should I start the exit process if I want to maximize my sale value?"

"3 to 4 years ago," I told them.

The silence was deafening.

Here's what most owners get wrong: They confuse "time to sell" with "time to exit."

Many owners think:
·      Finding a buyer takes 6 months
·      They hand over keys and walk away at closing
·      Exit planning starts when they're ready to sell

The reality of exit timelines:
·      2-3 years to build systems that maximize value
·      6-12 months for the sale process with a good M&A advisor
·      1-3 years of post-sale transition and earnouts

Even after you "sell," you're not done. Exits take longer than sales.

Most deals include earnout provisions tied to future performance alongside employment agreements that keep you involved for years.

The math is simple: 2+ years of preparation + 6-12 months to sell + 1-3 years transition = Start planning 4+ years out.

Because selling your business isn't the finish line - it's the middle mile in a marathon most owners don't realize they're running.

The humanities are not dying. They’ve just been undervalued and underutilized.
