AMs and AEs - try this.

Pick a customer or prospect who recently bought from you and needed executive buy-in for the purchase. 

Ask them this Q:

“Would you be willing to share what visuals you used to make the business case to your CXO for this purchase?”

I used to send my prospects a 40ish page slide deck with a detailed implementation plan. 

The first time I asked a prospect this, they emailed me 4 slides. None of my 40 slides were in it.

Instead, it was:

Slide 1 - framed the business problem and “so what” 
Slide 2 - framed how they were solving it today and what negative consequences were resulting from that approach 
Slide 3 - alternative solutions they explored (3 of them, one was DIY, two were vendors) + pros and cons
Slide 4 - our solution and why they preferred it 

One of the best lessons I ever learned.

Every cloud provider faces the same AI infrastructure challenge: chips need to be positioned close together to exchange data quickly, but they generate intense heat, creating unprecedented cooling demands.

We needed a strategic solution that allowed us to use our existing air-cooled data centers to do liquid cooling without waiting for new construction. And it needed to be rapidly deployed so we could bring customers these powerful AI capabilities while we transition towards facility-level liquid cooling. Think of a home where only one sunny room needs AC, while the rest stays naturally cool – that’s what we wanted to achieve, allowing us to efficiently land both liquid and air-cooled racks in the same facilities with complete flexibility.

The available options weren't great. Either we could wait to build specialized liquid-cooled facilities or adopt off-the-shelf solutions that didn't scale or meet our unique needs. Neither worked for our customers, so we did what we often do at Amazon… we invented our own solution.

Our teams designed and delivered our In-Row Heat Exchanger (IRHX), which uses a direct-to-chip approach with a "cold plate" on the chips. The liquid runs through this sealed plate in a closed loop, continuously removing heat without increasing water use. This enables us to support traditional workloads and demanding AI applications in the same facilities. By 2026, our liquid-cooled capacity will grow to over 20% of our ML capacity, which is at multi-gigawatt scale today.

While liquid cooling technology itself isn't unique, our approach was. Creating something this effective that could be deployed across our 120 Availability Zones in 38 Regions was significant. Because this solution didn't exist in the market, we developed a system that enables greater liquid cooling capacity with a smaller physical footprint, while maintaining flexibility and efficiency.

Our IRHX can support a wide range of racks requiring liquid cooling, uses 9% less water than fully-air cooled sites, and offers a 20% improvement in power efficiency compared to off-the-shelf solutions. And because we invented it in-house, we can deploy it within months in any of our data centers, creating a flexible foundation to serve our customers for decades to come.

Reimagining and innovating at scale has been something Amazon has done for a long time and one of the reasons we’ve been the leader in technology infrastructure and data center invention, sustainability, and resilience. We're not done… there's still so much more to invent for customers.

At an event yesterday, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar used a single word that detonated across financial Twitter and tech boardrooms: backstop. 

The remark came during a mundane discussion about funding AI infra:
Friar: “This is where we’re looking for an ecosystem of banks, private equity, maybe even governmental... uh, the ways governments can come to bear.”
Interviewer: “Meaning like a federal subsidy or something?”
Friar: “Just, first of all, the backstop—the guarantee that allows the financing to happen.”

That sound you heard was markets flinching. 
The reaction was instant - and deafening. 
Because backstop doesn’t just mean support. It means “we’ll make you whole if things go south.” 

In modern financial history, it is a loaded term. It evokes 2008 and the Too Big to Fail era when the U.S. Treasury backstopped $700B of toxic assets under the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) to prevent a full-scale banking collapse. During this time, the government became the lender, insurer, and buyer of last resort. 

These interventions may have stabilized the system, but they also cemented a new regime: a market where the largest actors operate with the implicit guarantee of rescue.

The system was saved, but trust was not. Gains were privatized, but losses were socialized. It birthed a decade of public outrage and backlash. Remember Occupy Wall Street?

To utter “backstop” onstage - especially as the CFO of the most-watched company in AI - is like yelling “fire” in a crowded theater, or worse, an overheated market. The subtext is impossible to ignore:
Are we already admitting AI infra bets are too big to fail?
Are taxpayers being lined up to absorb the next downside?
To the capital markets, it screams: “We’ve built something so large and leveraged that the only way to finance it further is with Uncle Sam’s signature.” It’s the return of moral hazard - but with GPUs instead of mortgages.

Until now, the AI infra boom has been seen as capital-intensive but fundamentally sound - a rational response to extraordinary demand. 
But if insiders are invoking backstops, it raises an existential question: is the capex cycle outrunning real utility?

In the past few months, Big Tech’s capital spending has gone vertical. OpenAI alone has stacked up $1.4T in compute commitments. The market’s working assumption has been that revenue will scale just as fast, that we’re witnessing the monetization of a once-in-a-generation platform shift.

But that assumption rests on a demand curve that’s still immature and wildly volatile. If the projections miss, the result isn’t just slower growth - it’s capital destruction. 

This is why Sarah Friar’s comment rang so loudly. Maybe she misspoke. But to a market already jittery about inflated valuations and unsustainable capex- it sounded like a quiet admission that the only thing holding up the world’s most ambitious buildout may be a promise, not a profit model.

You should kill your analytics as an early-stage startup. For real...

Last month, a founder showed me his dashboard.

47 metrics. 12 graphs. Real-time updates.

His business was dying.

But his data was beautiful.

Here's the truth nobody admits:

Data is where startups go to avoid hard decisions.

1)Analysis Paralysis Is Real
I've watched founders spend 3 months debating why activation is 42% not 50%.
Running cohort analyses. Building predictive models. Hiring data consultants.

The actual problem? A broken link in the onboarding email.

One customer could tell you in 30 seconds. But asking customers is scary. Data feels safer.

2) Data Becomes The Excuse
One founder I know argued for 6 weeks about pricing. Models. Spreadsheets. Forecasts.
Then just raised prices. Nothing bad happened. Revenue up 30%.
Six weeks of analysis to avoid one uncomfortable decision.

3) Correlation ≠ Causation (But Everyone Pretends)
Real examples I've seen:
"Users who click the logo 3x convert 80% better!"
—> Reality: Confused users click everywhere. The UX sucks.

"Dark mode users churn 50% less!" 
—> Reality: Power users customize everything. They'd stay anyway.

Companies build entire roadmaps on these "insights."

-->Features for logo-clickers. Dark mode as a quarterly OKR.

All BS.

What Actually Works:

Call 5 churned customers every week.

Not a survey. Not an email. A phone call.

And ask: "Why did you leave?"

The answers:
"I'm shutting down my busniess"
"The Chrome extension broke"
"Forgot I was paying for this"

No dashboard reveals this.

The Only 3 Numbers That Matter:

1 --> Money in bank (Can you survive?)
2 --> Money from customers (Are you growing?)
3 --> Money to acquire customers (Is it sustainable?)

Everything else is procrastination dressed as strategy.

Try This:
--> Delete all analytics for 30 days. 
--> Call 1 customer every morning
--> Check revenue before bed
--> Ship something daily

You'll learn more in week 1 than in a year of dashboards.

The uncomfortable truth:

Founders worship data because it feels safe. 
Scientific. Objective. Defensible.
"The data made me do it."

But building a business isn't science. 

It's about understanding humans.

And humans aren't data points.

A 15-minute call with an angry customer teaches more than 15GB of analytics.

But one requires courage. The other just requires a credit card.

That's why everyone chooses data.

And that's why most startups fail.

They're optimizing metrics while customers are screaming the answers.

Kill your analytics. Pick up the phone.

I was on a call with a founder who struggles to hire "A-players"

He asked me how to “hire better”… 

Often, “hiring” is not the problem... 

Everyone wants A-players. 

But no one builds the environment for them to thrive.

The real reason most startups hire poorly isn’t budget, bad luck, or bad interview questions - it’s confusion.

You want creativity, but give checklists.

You want autonomy, but ask for daily updates.

You want initiative, but never explain the mission.

You’re not hiring the wrong people. 

You’re attracting exactly what your systems are designed for.

Fix the system first, and then you'll see the changes you want.

🤖 Which Matters More Now — AI Literacy or Emotional Intelligence?

A few months ago, I watched a manager reject a candidate who couldn’t use ChatGPT effectively.

A year ago, that same manager would’ve hired them for their empathy and leadership potential.

That moment made me pause — have we started valuing tools over traits?

I think we’re missing something deeper here.

AI literacy isn’t just about mastering tools — it’s about how those tools start reshaping our expectations of people.

The more fluent we become with AI, the less patient we become with humans.

AI answers instantly, so we subconsciously expect people to as well.

It’s not just a skill gap we’re creating — it’s an empathy gap.

In my opinion, the next leadership challenge won’t be technical—it’ll be emotional endurance.

Knowing how to stay grounded, reflective, and patient in a world that rewards speed over stillness.

Here’s what I’ve started doing to rebalance that tension:
 ✅ Use AI to expand thinking, not replace it.
 ✅ Ask one human question every day that you could’ve asked a model.
 ✅ Reflect once a day: Did I optimize for clarity or connection?

Because the future won’t belong to those who simply work with AI—It’ll belong to those who stay emotionally awake while doing it.
So I’m curious — as AI becomes more fluent, are we becoming less human in how we respond?

My biggest takeaways from Figma CEO Dylan Field:

1. View AI as a growth accelerator, not a cost-cutting tool. Despite measurable productivity gains from AI tools, Figma continues expanding headcount across all functions. The company sees AI as enabling teams to do more ambitious work and tackle bigger problems, not as a way to reduce staff.

2. Obsess over time-to-value—how quickly someone experiences something special about a product. For Figma, that means getting users to create something or have a collaborative moment as fast as possible. With AI tools like Figma Make, the focus is on getting to a good starting point that users can refine, rather than sweating every detail for years.


3. Sometimes “fun” is the smartest competitive advantage. A month before launching FigJam, the team realized the product felt soulless. Dylan made the controversial decision to differentiate through “fun” rather than traditional features. The team ran a design sprint that generated ideas like cursor chat, which became definitional to the product. Context matters—this worked for a brainstorming tool where you want people to open up, but wouldn’t work for professional design software where users want zero interference.

4. Tackle blockers as aggressively as you add features. Figma created a dedicated team literally called “Blockers” that systematically eliminated obstacles preventing users from adopting the product. Each time they removed a blocker, they saw measurable improvements in retention and activation. This unglamorous work proved just as important as shipping cool new capabilities.

5. You can systematically develop taste. Experience something, decide if you like it, ask yourself why, then build your repertoire by exploring more. Understanding the historical context and canon that led to something helps you develop your own point of view. Not everyone becomes a tastemaker, but anyone can improve their judgment through this process of being curious, learning from diverse fields, and reflecting on their preferences.

6. As AI makes building software easier, design quality becomes a key competitive advantage. AI and better developer tools are making code creation faster, but that means design is now how products stand out. “Good enough” has become mediocre—you need excellent experiences to win.

7. Leadership means learning the same lessons repeatedly, getting slightly better each time. Dylan describes leadership development not as linear progress but as cycling through the same lessons multiple times—like how to create clarity or unpack context for teams—forgetting them, and relearning them with incremental improvement. Junior people can teach senior leaders when they approach problems from completely different mental models.

Post content:
My biggest takeaway from Chip Huyen: 

1. Most AI product problems aren’t AI problems. When companies think they have an AI performance issue, it’s usually a user experience problem, an organizational communication gap, or a data quality issue. One company thought their AI lead scoring system was broken, but the real issue was that the marketing team wasn’t asking the right questions to get useful data.

2. Your best performers benefit most from AI tools. In a controlled experiment, the highest-performing engineers got the biggest productivity boost from AI coding assistants, not the lowest performers. Senior engineers who already knew how to solve problems used AI to work even faster, while low performers often just copied and pasted code they didn’t understand.

3. How you prepare your data matters more than which database you choose. Companies see their biggest AI performance gains from better organizing and preparing their information—breaking content into the right size chunks, adding summaries, converting content into question-and-answer format—rather than agonizing over which technical infrastructure to use.

4. The biggest improvements to your AI product come from talking to users and understanding their feedback, not from adopting the latest models or staying glued to AI news. Many companies waste time debating which technology to use, when the real wins come from better user experience and data preparation.

5. Fine-tuning should be your last resort. Before investing in fine-tuning a model, try simpler solutions first: improve your prompts, add basic post-processing scripts, or fix your data pipeline. One company caught 90% of its model’s mistakes with a simple script. Fine-tuning creates ongoing maintenance headaches and should only be used when everything else has been maxed out.

6. You don’t need to be perfect to win. Many successful companies choose “good enough” over perfect when implementing AI systems. They calculate whether investing two engineers to improve accuracy from 80% to 85% is better than using those same engineers to launch an entirely new feature. Often, the new feature provides more value.

7. AI productivity is nearly impossible to measure. Companies invest heavily in AI coding tools but can’t clearly prove they work. When forced to choose between expensive AI subscriptions for their team or hiring one additional person, many managers choose the person, not necessarily because AI doesn’t help but because headcount feels more tangible.

8. Many people don’t know what to build despite having powerful tools. Even with AI tools that can build almost anything, many employees face an “idea crisis”—they simply don’t know what to create. The best approach: spend a week noticing what frustrates you in your daily work, then build small tools to solve those specific pain points.

My top takeaways from Google's head of search Robby Stein:

1. The next year of AI products will establish user habits for many years. People are building their new habits right now, like how quickly everyone started relying on ChatGPT. This creates incredible urgency because whoever captures these habits now will have a lasting advantage. Google recognized they couldn’t let users develop the habit of going elsewhere for AI-powered answers. This is the critical window for establishing how people will search and find information for the next decade.

2. Choose clarity over cleverness. Using standard icons and familiar patterns gives you enormous leverage. Creating a custom camera icon that looks “mostly like AI” confuses users. Simple naming matters too—changing “Favorites” to “Close Friends” dramatically increased how many people users added to their lists. When users instantly understand what something does, you get much more adoption.

3. Great products require relentless dissatisfaction with the status quo. Successful product leaders constantly question why things work the way they do, down to tiny frustrations most people accept. One example: a sticker that tears a fruit’s peel when removed. This mindset of noticing and refusing to tolerate small annoyances drives breakthrough improvements.

4. When users hack your product, they’re showing you what to build. Instagram users created multiple fake accounts to share privately with different groups—this workaround signaled an unmet need that eventually became Close Friends. Similarly, Google saw people typing “AI” at the end of searches to trigger AI responses, revealing demand for AI Mode. Pay attention to these signals.

5. Understand the job people hire your product to do, not just what features they ask for. Instagram’s Close Friends wasn’t about creating lists—it was about feeling connection through DMs. Understanding this emotional job helped the team realize users needed 20 to 30 people on their list, not 2 or 3, to ensure someone would respond. Study the exact moment someone first decides to use your product—that’s where the most critical insights live.

6. Small usability details make copied features feel native. Instagram Stories succeeded not just by copying Snapchat’s format but by adding key differences: letting users upload from their camera roll, adding a pause button, and using different creative tools. When adding major new features to mature products, give them their own distinct space rather than modifying what already exists.

7. The “lean startup” mentality can backfire—some breakthroughs need substantial resources. Keeping teams too small for too long can actually slow progress. Instagram’s Close Friends took two years partly because the team stayed too lean. While lean teams work well for early validation, products that require technical breakthroughs need enough resources to build real momentum and get good enough.

At Ramp we ship a new major feature every day - it's impossible for leaders stay up to speed. 

To keep a high bar without slowing folks down, teams can ship to early access tier whenever they want but need review for general release. 

Crazy fact: 10% of customers opt into early access because they can't get enough. That's 5000+ businesses. Plenty to work with. 

To release to general public, teams need to prove this product works and get sign off for heads of eng, product and design. Here is our template: 

1. What did we build and why
2. What's the demo in < 3mn (loom)
3. Did we meet our goals in early access (hex dash)
4. Are customers raving about this (LLM on Zendesk tickets, Sprig surveys, and Gong transcripts)
5. Will customers easily discover and start using it (first time user journey)
6. Is sales ready to sell, AM ready to activate, and support ready to troubleshoot
7. Do we have a clear rollout plan (launch tier, pricing, coms)

This helps us document decisions, serves as a strong checklist, and feeds our release notes. Most importantly, it keeps the bar high (we expect at least 1 rev of feedback). 

The best part: most of this template is automated using AI connected to the rest of our business systems.

Leadership has 48h to review or it ships. 

We think it's a great way to balance speed & empowerment with visibility & quality.

"Why should companies pay for SaaS (HR/CRM/ERP/etc.) when they could just vibe code them?"

I get variations of this question or comment with some regularity (granted, it's sometimes just me talking to myself). 

Here are some biased (but hopefully, well-considered) thoughts:

1) I am a big proponent and user of vibe coding (what I call "agentic coding"). I do it every day, 7 days a week, including Sundays. It's amazing.

2) My company, HubSpot is a software company. We have hundreds of professional engineers -- just about all of them use AI for product development too. They are brilliant and know how to build production-grade products.

3) Even with this powerful army of talent, the number of internal, core SaaS applications that we have replaced with a vibe-coded variant is exactly ZERO. The number of applications we plan to replace is also exactly ZERO. 

4) It's not the absence of talent that keeps us from rolling our own SaaS apps, it's the presence of focus. It would be silly to try and replace our HR, team collaboration, expense tracking and 100+ other SaaS apps we use when we can just buy them. Just doesn't make sense.

5) That's us -- as a software company at some scale. If you're a non-software company it makes even less sense for you. Doesn't matter how good the AI coding tools get. Let's say you *could* vibe code a replacement for that SaaS app you're using, who's going to maintain it? Who's going to keep up with industry trends? What are you going to do when the 20-something genius that vibe coded it over a weekend leaves the company? Who do you call when there's a major bug? 

6) If you're a Fortune 500 company at some scale, perhaps you could pull this off for some discrete use cases and the tradeoffs are worth it. You have an IT/Engineering department that is larger than the population of some countries. You can take on the pain in return for the positives.

For the millions of others, my advice is:

Spend every calorie possible on creating value for your customers.

To be a great C-suite leader today, you have to be a doer, not just a delegator.

You delegate to scale.

If you just add headcount, you gain capacity but not impact.

Communicate relentlessly. Share what’s known, what’s unknown, and how decisions are made. No mystery, no drama—just context.

The expectations of leadership are shifting.
The best leaders today don’t lead through decks, dashboards, or delegation.
They lead by doing.

We're going to need new metrics to define what 'success' is in b2b marketing.

The number one question I get asked by marketers about AI isn't on Agents, or Prompting; it's "How do I show the value of marketing to my CEO?"

Over the past 10 years, most b2b marketing was a mix of SEO, paid advertising & marketing automation. Highly measurable. 

SEO is moving into LLMs, and is measured via share of voice vs. measurable clicks. Less measurable. 

Since ChatGPT, it's estimated the cost per click has increased between 50% to 100%. More paid budget will switch from direct response advertising to brand advertising. Less measurable. 

With AI and agents, marketers can also automate work they weren't previously able to - sales prospecting, sales discovery & qualification, and enable sellers with AI functionality to improve close rates. 

These improve deal velocity, shorten time to close, should increase deal to closed-won rates, and give sales time back to increase productivity.

These aren't typical metrics associated with marketing teams. It means we need new marketing dashboards.

- Grow Influence (indirect metrics): through brand search volume, share of voice in LLMs, impressions/engagement via brand advertising, and impressions/engagement via creator partnerships.

- Grow Demand (direct metrics): Direct response advertising, SEO transactional, prospecting. 

- Grow Revenue: Deal Velocity (close deals faster, in fewer touches), Closed-won rates (increase pipeline quality with better discovery/qualification), time back to reps.

In the AI era, marketing can drive more value. We need the right metrics to show how.

A $250B company just revealed the new math: AI handles the customers, humans handle the 72 hour weeks.

I spent 12 years implementing Salesforce across dozens of companies.

Imagine if a restaurant behaved like your average product team.

The kitchen is packed. Everyone is moving. Every station is busy. Prep lists are long. Meetings are constant. 

There is always something to do.

Chopping, rearranging, documenting, planning, replating. 

But plates rarely reach customers.

When they do, they’re late. Or wrong. Or cold. Or oddly disconnected from what the diners said they wanted. Yet the kitchen isn’t “failing,” exactly. It never looks like a crisis. No one storms out. No one flips a table. Diners don’t riot. They just lower their expectations and stop coming back.

Inside the kitchen, though, the staff feels productive. Everyone is exhausted. Everyone is “at capacity.” Everyone can point to a dozen tasks they completed. They can even argue those tasks were important. And in isolation, many of them were.

But restaurants are not judged by how busy the kitchen is. They are judged by how consistently they deliver great food, on time, to the people who ordered it.

Product development is strange because this feedback loop is muted. 

There is no instant revolt. 

A team can be unbelievably heroically busy without producing much that actually moves the needle.

That’s the trap: in software, effort is easy to generate, activity is easy to justify, and impact is surprisingly easy to avoid.

How valuation multiples shift by sector

How your sector shapes your sale price

Two companies with identical profits can sell for wildly different prices. The difference lies in their valuation multiple – and the story behind it.

High-growth sectors such as software and healthcare can reach 10–20× EBITDA because buyers are paying for future potential. 

Meanwhile, asset-heavy or cyclical industries like construction or manufacturing often trade closer to 4–6×.

Timing matters too. When credit is cheap and sentiment is high, multiples expand. When rates rise or demand slows, they shrink fast.

If you’re planning an exit, know your sector benchmark and what makes your business outperform it – that’s where negotiation power begins.

The Rise of the Founding Designer 🔥

Something has changed in the last few years. You see it in the cap tables: designers with real equity, joining at single-digit employee numbers. Not as the person who makes the logo. As a founder.

This would have seemed odd in 2010. Back then, startups had founding engineers and maybe a business co-founder. Designers came later, after product-market fit, after the Series A. They were hired, not founding.

Now some of the most successful startups have designers in the founding team. And it’s not a coincidence.

What Changed:

The obvious answer is that design matters more now. Products are more competitive, users are more sophisticated, the bar for “good enough” has risen. All true, but not the real reason.

The real reason is that the nature of product differentiation has changed.

In the early 2000s, if you could build something that worked at all, you had a shot. Just getting the technology to function was hard enough that execution barriers protected you. If you built a web app that didn’t crash, you were ahead of most people.

Now everyone can build. The tools are better, the knowledge is distributed, there’s an open source library for everything. Technical execution alone doesn’t protect you anymore.

So what does? How users feel about your product. Whether they understand it. Whether it fits into their mental model of how things should work. These aren’t secondary concerns anymore. They’re primary.

And that’s what designers are trained to think about.

The Founding Designer Advantage:

When a designer is there from day one, something interesting happens. The product doesn’t just look different. It’s conceived differently.

A founding designer doesn’t arrive to a spec and make it pretty. They’re in the room when you’re deciding what to build. They’re asking “why would someone do this?” before you write any code. They’re thinking about the user’s mental model while you’re thinking about the data model.

This isn’t the same as having a designer you hire early. There’s a psychological difference between someone who joins a company and someone who co-creates it. Founding designers have the standing to push back. They can say “this entire feature is solving the wrong problem” without it being a career-limiting move.

I’ve seen founding teams with designers make better architectural decisions. Not because designers understand databases, but because they won’t let you build something users won’t comprehend. That constraint forces clarity.

The Pattern: 

Here’s what I notice in startups with founding designers: the product does fewer things, but each thing makes more sense.

Design is about to become the most important skill in tech 🔥

When anyone can build anything, the question is no longer "can you create it?" The question becomes: should this exist, and why?

Design is moving from making things look good to deciding what’s worth building in the first place. It becomes the filter, the strategy, and ultimately your definition of taste. Great design will help you stand out.

And while this has always been true when building products, AI puts it even more in the foreground.

We’re still trying to solve 2025’s problems with 1990’s mental models.

Every field keeps offering its own “fix” for climate change, inequality, resource depletion, biodiversity loss—yet each of these challenges already leaks across every boundary we draw. The world has become a megasystem, but we’re still behaving as if isolated expertise will be enough.

Systems thinking helped. It taught us to map complexity, find leverage points, and escape linear cause–effect logic. But even systems thinking has a border. You still decide what counts as the system.

And today’s problems refuse to stay inside those lines.

What we’re facing now is everything-all-at-once, stretched across time. The feedback loops interact with their own histories. Ethical considerations mutate as conditions shift. What seemed like a contained issue becomes global in months.

This isn’t a failure of expertise. It’s a failure of dimensionality.

The old model relied on the Renaissance individual. One brilliant mind pulling multiple threads together. That era closed a long time ago. Knowledge expanded faster than individuals could synthesize it.

The National Academies said it outright: solutions now sit beyond the scope of any single discipline.

If one person can’t hold the whole system, then the only path forward is the Renaissance team—not in name, but in the way it operates:

• holding multiple viewpoints at the same time
• treating challenges as timelines rather than snapshots
• tracing causal relationships across space and duration
• adapting instead of defending a “finished” solution

This isn’t collaboration as we usually define it. It’s closer to continuous reconstruction. Shared understanding that expires and must be rebuilt. A team architecture that expects instability.

But ignoring the instability doesn’t make it go away.

We’re surrounded by proof.
An AI exam prediction that collapsed in three months.
The warmest year ever recorded.
Interlinked crises—drought, fire, flood, migration, biodiversity collapse—each amplifying the others.

These aren’t separate stories. 
They’re chapters of the same system.

The uncomfortable truth: no single framework, discipline, or leader can carry this alone. Not anymore.

If you haven’t begun the shift into systems thinking, that’s your starting line. But the real work begins after that—when you step into the fourth dimension and stop pretending that any boundary you draw around a problem is anything more than a temporary convenience.

Complexity isn’t the enemy. 
But treating it like a series of isolated tasks might be.

The next decade belongs to the teams who can think across dimensions we’re only beginning to see—and who can work inside the uncertainty rather than trying to flatten it.

The #1 mistake leaders make when implementing AI isn't picking the wrong technology.

It's starting with the technology instead of the problem.

I see this constantly. A company hears about AI agents, gets excited about the possibilities, and immediately starts evaluating vendors. They spend months on demos, build out technical requirements, and invest in platforms.

Six months later, they have powerful tools that nobody uses because they didn't solve a problem anyone actually had.

The companies that succeed with AI do it backwards from most. 

They start by identifying the expensive, time-consuming manual processes that directly impact revenue, profitability, or cash flow. 

Then they ask: what would it be worth to cut this process time by 50%? What would it mean if we could eliminate 80% of errors?

Only after they understand the business value do they begin evaluating technology solutions.

This matters because when you start with the problem, you can measure success in business terms. 

You're not tracking "AI adoption rates" or "number of tools deployed." You're tracking DSO reduction, margin improvement, win rates, improved actuals vs. estimated, or hours saved per week on energy-depleting low-level tasks.

When you start with the technology, you end up measuring vanity metrics that don't correlate to actual business outcomes.

Starting with technology leads to pilot purgatory. You test a bunch of things, nothing graduates to production, and a year later, you're still trying to figure out if AI actually works for your business.

Most leaders already know this intellectually. But the excitement around AI makes it easy to skip the problem definition step and jump straight to solutions.

If you're evaluating AI investments right now, ask yourself: Do I have a clear problem statement that includes the current cost of the manual process and the expected business value of solving it?

If the answer is no, you're not ready to buy technology yet.

P.S. If you want help defining which problems AI should solve in your business before you start evaluating vendors, let's talk. DM me, and we'll work through it together.

Dear, first business owners... 95% of your problems are old problems wearing new clothes... please understand this framework 👇🏾

That supplier who's ghosting you? Caesar had the same issue with grain merchants 100s of years ago.

That investor who keeps moving the goalposts? The Medici banks dealt with that in 1400s Florence.

Your co-founder drama? Jobs and Wozniak. Gates and Allen. Every founding story ever...

Do not convince yourself your situation is special.

Or that your market is different.

Or that your generation is unique.

Stop trying to solve everything from first principles, you're not the first human to run a business...

Someone probably solved the problem that is keeping you up at night in 1987. And 1993. And 2004. And last Tuesday.

I've been in business for about 15 years and it's taken me 12 of them to realise that the first question to ask yourself when you're facing a challenge is...

"Has anyone solved this before?"

When Netflix started streaming - genuinely new. Nobody had done it at scale.

When I started hiring people - ancient problem. Millions had done it before.

When Uber fought city regulations - uncharted territory. The playbook didn't exist.

When I tried to motivate my first team - timeless challenge. Ask any Roman general.

In the last couple of years, I became wildly more productive when I started asking one question: "Who's already solved this?"

If someone has, I find them. Pay them. Hire them. Ask to meet them.

Whatever it takes to not spend three years learning what they can tell me in three minutes.

If nobody has - if it's genuinely unprecedented - then I experiment like hell. No advisors. No books. No "best practices". Fail as fast as I can.

👴🏻 OLD PROBLEMS = FIND EXPERTISE
👶🏼 NEW PROBLEMS = FAST EXPERIMENTATION

The tragedy I see all the time is founders wasting their prime years solving solved problems...

They're brainstorming HR policies that P&G nailed in the 80s.

Their revolutionary approach to email marketing? Someone A/B tested that in 2015.

That radical pricing strategy? It's chapter 4 in a book from 1992.

The smartest founders I know are intellectual thieves - they steal everything that's been solved and pour all their creativity into the 5% that's genuinely unprecedented...

For many founders ego makes us think we're too special for other people's solutions.

Inexperience convinces them their situation is unique.

Insecurity makes them think they look weak for asking for help.

So we sit alone, solving solved problems, while our competitors are already three problems ahead.

You do not need to reinvent the wheel... you just need to find someone who'll explain how the thing works! 🛞

The most expensive education I've gotten is learning things myself that someone twice my age would have told me for free over coffee... 😅

Let's talk about the math nobody mentions:

To make $2M as a VC-backed founder: You need a $50M+ exit.

Why?

→ Investors own 20-40% after funding rounds
→ Liquidation preferences (investors paid first)
→ Dilution from follow-on rounds
→ Split with co-founders and early employees

To make $2M bootstrapped: You need $200K/year for 10 years.

Or $400K/year for 5 years. Or $100K/year for 20 years.

Which path is actually easier?

The $50M exit requires everything to go right. 

Perfect timing. Perfect execution. Perfect market conditions.

The $200K/year path just requires building something people want and not destroying it with complexity.

I chose the math that actually works.

Not because I'm anti-VC - but because I'm pro-freedom.

Freedom means owning 100% of something small over 5% of something that might be big.

Your math might be different.

But at least do the math.

Most founders quit between years two and five.

Not because the idea was bad or because the market wasn't there.

But because they couldn't sustain interest in working on the same problem for a decade.

What if the conventional wisdom about "finding your passion" is actually what's burning founders out?

There's a Japanese concept called “Ikigai” that means “reason for being.” It's the intersection of what you love, what you're good at, what the world needs, and what you can be paid for.

This is what separates founders who last from those who burn out.

Unsuccessful founders usually have three out of four elements, but not all four.

Here are the three patterns I see most often:

1. The "Passion Project" Founder

They have something they love, something they're good at, and something the world needs - but can't figure out how to get paid for it. These startups die slowly and sadly, usually after the founders drain their savings and can't raise money.

2. The "Mercenary" Founder

They see a market opportunity (something people will pay for), and they're good at execution, but they don't actually care about the problem. These founders make it further because they have revenue. But they usually quit after a few years because making money turns out not to be enough of a reason to work 80-hour weeks.

3. The "In Denial" Founder

They love something and think the world needs it, but aren't actually good at building it. These founders keep pivoting, keep trying different approaches, but the fundamental problem is they lack the skills for what they're attempting.

The best founders have all four.

But most successful founders didn't start with all four elements. They found them over time.

Instagram started as Burbn, a location-based social network. But Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger noticed people mainly used it to share photos. They were good at building photo-sharing features, people loved it, it was something the world wanted, and they could monetize it. So they followed their ikigai and pivoted.

Here's the strategy: start with the three elements you can know for certain (what you're good at, what people will pay for, and what the world needs), and search within that space for something you love.

The Silicon Valley advice of "follow your passion" gets this backwards. Passion isn't the starting point - it's the destination.

A startup is a years-long relationship with a problem.

And like any long relationship, it only works if it's the right fit.

That's when starting a company stops feeling like work and starts feeling like coming home.

"That was my shot. I blew it."

If I'd believed that in 2020, Gamma wouldn't exist - the presentation tool used by over 70 million people today.

I joined Optimizely in 2013, eventually serving as interim CFO and Head of Legal, Finance, & Accounting. We did everything the venture playbook said to do. Raised big. Grew fast. Closed deals. And still ended up selling - well short of the unicorn trajectory we'd been on. We had amazing founders, top tier investors, and a talented team. But it wasn’t enough.

I walked away in my late 30s, no longer the 20-something founder that Sand Hill Road idolizes. The conventional wisdom said I should’ve sought stability at a later-stage company.

The startup narrative tells us options close as you age. Every wrong hire narrows your future. Every closed door limits what's possible. Most founders staring at past mistakes believe the walls are closing in.

The reality is the opposite:

Behind you, there's one path: the one you took. In front of you, paths are still branching. The past is a single point. The future isn’t.

The lesson wasn't avoiding mistakes through better planning. It was understanding that closed paths are useful information.

The Optimizely experience showed me exactly which branches to take with Gamma.

In 2020, an investor hung up mid-pitch and said Gamma was the “worst idea [he'd] ever heard.” We raised funding later that year. That closed door became a data point, not a dead end.

Because failure teaches you what you can't teach yourself:

Pattern recognition.

Which risks matter.

Which branches to explore.

Airbnb tried cereal boxes and door-to-door photography before finding traction. Zoom entered video conferencing years after Skype and WebEx. Facebook launched after Friendster and MySpace had already dominated social networking.

"Too late" and "missed your window" are statements about paths already closed. They say nothing about the ones still open.

The next time you think your mistakes define your ceiling, remember: your unique path through closed doors gives you vision on open ones nobody else can see.

The future is wide. You just have to look in the right direction.

Building a startup is harder than I ever imagined. 14 lessons in the order I learned them:

1. Find your obsession. If it doesn't feel like your life's work, don't do it. Tinker till you find a problem that consumes you. And don't raise capital until you do.

2. Jevons paradox (efficiency increases demand) applies to nascent markets. When technology increases efficiency, demand surges. Gamma aims at people who need to communicate visually but lack tools. By making it much easier, we unlock more usage. New use cases = untapped TAM.

3. Your conviction is everything. Market opportunity means nothing without belief. "The world will ask who you are, and if you do not know, the world will tell you."

4. Word of mouth is the only thing that matters at first. Conviction drives products people brag about. Do their faces light up? That's what amplifies distribution.

5. Your luck surface area has 2 dimensions: Time and people. Surround yourself with people who share your values and level of ambition, and give yourself enough time to execute.

6. Compounding is the 8th wonder. Hard work compounds exponentially. 1% better daily means 37x after a year. If you just focus on winning today, you’re missing out on winning big later. Play the long game.

7. Parkinson's law (work expands to fill available resources) applies to time and money. Give people $1M budget, they'll spend $1M. Give one week, it takes one week. Constraints beget creativity. Learn when and how to apply them.

8. Power laws dictate outcomes. Allocation reveals what works. 2-3 features drive usage. 2-3 channels get users. Start broad, identify what works, double down. Experimentation is the key to understanding.

9. Know where you're at and when to evolve. v1 is for enthusiasts. v2 is for early believers. v3 is for masses. Seize the market and become the standard.

10. Strong brands have clear values. Nike is speed. Apple is aesthetics. Berkshire is integrity. “Don't be the best, be the only.”

11. Being ignorant is fine. Staying ignorant is not. Most hate sales thinking it's manipulation. Good sales is listening and solving.

12. Your story is your ticket. “People remember 2-3 things about you. Don't let them be random. Reality bends to whoever tells the better story.”

13. Become extremely literate. "Your success in life will be largely determined by your ability to speak, your ability to write, and the quality of your ideas. In that order."

14. All moats are temporary. Xerox is dead. Polaroid is gone. Kodak is history. Leverage anything you can to make your company antifragile.

Skip obsession and your conviction crumbles. Skip word of mouth and distribution fails. Skip power laws and you reach local maximum.

Master them all and they compound.

Three buttons to one. That was the entire product strategy.

In 1980, Apple hired a designer named David Kelley to reinvent the mouse. (He later founded IDEO. At Stanford, he was my mentor.)

Xerox's version cost hundreds of dollars and was basically a lab instrument.

Great for researchers. Terrible for everyone else.

Apple's brief was simple: make it cheap, make it tough, make it easy enough that you could explain it without scaring people.

David's team pushed for a single button.

Not because they didn't understand the power of three.

Because they understood the anxiety of zero.

"One button was all most people could emotionally handle the first time they touched a computer."

Not an engineering statement. An empathy statement.

We're in the one-button era of AI right now.

Most people are still at the edge of the pool, wondering if it's safe to get in.

I learned this the hard way at Gamma.

We launched. Got the spike you're supposed to get. Product Hunt trophies. Good press. Traffic.

Then the curve flattened. People tried it, but they weren't telling their friends.

So we did something that felt almost irresponsible:

We stopped chasing users and spent months fixing only the first 30 seconds.

The goal: any normal person lands, types one thing, clicks one button, and sees something undeniably useful.

Only after we nailed that did growth become self-propelling.

That was our one-button moment.

Here's what David's lesson taught me about building AI:

1. Founders underestimate how demoralizing it is for someone to open your product and feel stupid in the first 30 seconds. They blame themselves, not you, then quietly churn.

2. The people who compound your growth aren't debating model benchmarks. They're the PM with eight meetings who needs a deck for the fifth one.

3. Your enemy isn't lack of features. It's intimidation.

Power users will always ask for more knobs, more scripting, more ways to break your guardrails.

That's fine. Just be honest about what’s currently holding you back..

Here's the question I'd ask any builder:

What would David Kelley fight to turn into one button in your product?

Answer that first. The three-button roadmap can come later.

"We can't afford to give raises," the division leader said.

"But we're hiring aggressively."

I was reviewing their Q3 budget.

"What's the average raise request you've denied?" I asked.

"About 15-20%."

"And what are you paying new hires?"

"Market rate. Usually 30% more than internal folks."

"So you won't pay someone 15% more to stay,
but you'll pay someone else 30% more to start?"

He shifted. "That's different."

"What about Jennifer?" I asked.
"She asked for 18K more. 
You said no. She left. 
You just hired her replacement."

"Yeah. Took three months to fill."

"What did you pay the replacement?"

"85K."

"Jennifer was making 62K. Asked for 80K."

"Right."

"So you saved 18K by saying no,
then spent 23K more to get someone new.
Plus signing bonus?"

"10K."

"What about lost productivity while they ramp up?"

"Maybe six months to get to Jennifer's level."

"That's another 30K in lost output.
Plus recruiting costs?"

He opened a spreadsheet.
"Recruiter was 17K. Training about 15K."

I wrote on his whiteboard:
- Salary increase: 33K
- Signing bonus: 10K 
- Lost productivity: 30K
- Recruiter fee: 17K
- Training costs: 15K
Total: 105K

"You spent 105K to avoid paying 18K."

He stared at the board.

"How many Jennifers did you lose this year?"

"Twelve."

"So you burned 1.2 million
to save maybe 200K in raises."

A few months later, he called.

"I just approved every raise request in the queue."

"All of them?"

"Cost me 340K.
Would have cost me 2 million in replacements."

"How'd your team react?"

"Shocked. Then productive.
We're hitting numbers we haven't seen in years."

Here's the truth about retention economics:

Companies will spend a dollar to save a dime.
Then spend ten dollars to fix what broke.

We treat current employees like costs
and new employees like investments.

But retention isn't an expense.
It's the highest ROI investment you'll ever make.

The VP of Sales made $420K last year. 
She brought in $5.2M in new ARR. They lost $9.4M to churn the same year.
The VP of Customer Success made $155K.

The CS team of eleven managed $51M in existing revenue on a $1.4M budget.
Sales had thirty-four people.

It cost $1.70 to acquire every dollar of ARR.
$0.20 to retain every dollar of ARR.

Guess where all the new headcount went?

Every board meeting started with pipeline talk.
Churn was treated like weather (inevitable, uncontrollable).

But it wasn't actually mysterious.
They knew exactly why customers left.

- Bad onboarding.
- Mis-sold features.
- Zero executive alignment.
- Slow response times.
- No measurable ROI.

All Customer Success problems with no resources to fix them.

The CFO runs the numbers:

If they woud have froze new sales for six months.
They could improve retention from 81% to 91%.
Net result would be $4.8M more profit than doubling the sales team.

Six months of no new logos would've made them MORE money.

The CRO would have tried to kill it 
by saying retention wasn't "growth-oriented."

That's the mindset that destroys SaaS companies.

You will never out-sell a retention problem.

But most companies treat Customer Success like janitors.
Clean up the mess after Sales makes the promise.

Then wonder why the bucket keeps leaking.

In a Product Operating Model transformation, the product managers you need may not be the ones you think you’re looking for.

If you're implementing a Product Operating Model in a traditional organization, you need much more than pristine product skills. You need someone who understands change management and thrives in ambiguity.

Most companies get this backwards. They hire a stellar PM from a well-oiled software company, expecting them to transform their legacy bank or manufacturing firm. But these PMs often struggle because they've never had to build coalitions, navigate entrenched stakeholders, or operate in less-than-perfect product orgs. They know how to get the work done but they don’t know how to convince people they should be doing that work in the first place.

Instead, think about hiring for a balance of change management experience and "ideal product experience." While you want people to learn from those who have product chops, you first need someone who's successfully led digital transformations at traditional companies to pave the path for your product managers to execute.

Organizational transformation is fundamentally a people problem, not a process problem. You can always upskill the product competencies through a combination of courses like Product Institute and mentorship within the org, but teaching someone to manage organizational resistance? That's much harder.

The perfect product manager for your messy transformation might not have the perfect product background.

What's been your experience hiring for transformation roles? Have you seen this play out?

The product vs. project management confusion isn't a role definition problem. It's a leadership problem.

Last week's LinkedIn discussion about my Product Thinking with Melissa Perri podcast episode (episode 252 if you want to check) revealed something interesting. Most people understand the distinction between product and project management, but their companies still struggle with it daily.

Leadership is creating cultures where being "on track" matters more than being "on the right track." When executives consistently ask "are we on schedule?" instead of "are we solving the right problems?" they're telling the organization what they actually value.

The result? Product managers get reduced to coordinators, discovery gets squeezed into "sprint zeros," and teams optimize for delivery over outcomes. No amount of role clarity will fix this if leadership behaviors don't change.

If you're stuck in this trap, start by shifting the conversation upward. Map out what delivery-focused culture is costing you in missed opportunities and wrong bets. Show leadership two paths: stay focused on predictable delivery with incremental results, or embrace discovery with the potential for breakthrough value.

The companies that get this right aren't just clearer about roles. They're clearer about what success looks like.

What questions is your leadership actually asking? Are they strategic or operational?

Summarizing 2025 in AI in a five bullets:
1) No sign of a slowdown in exponential pace of gains across many measures of ability
2) Jaggedness remains the main issue of AI
3) it is the early days for deployment, but many companies are now reporting positive ROI
4) GenAI became an industry, with industry-level impacts
5) AI is still weird

The longer version:
1) Predictions that AI "will hit a wall" have not yet come true, though the future always remains uncertain. However, lots of alternative approaches to continuing gains whether using LLMs or new architectures or hybrids are now being developed, and there is a lot of money to experiment with. I see a lot of confidence in continued gains from insiders.

2) The nature and shape of the jagged frontier have changed (math used to be a major weakness of LLMs) but the fact that AIs still underperform humans by a large amount in a variety of tasks creates bottlenecks in complex tasks that limit what work AIs can do. A growing frontier may alter that in the future.

3) AI went from a theoretically useful tool to one that companies are starting to see value from. But it is early. Actual transformation of processes and organizational structures has not yet begun, and any changes to the job market are unclear at this point.

4) As an industry, AI impacts are much larger scale than before, whether you consider that in terms of electrical use or economic impact (and risk) or political power. Viewing AI as a product alone obscures the fact that it is now a big business.

5) We have not resolved the core questions that make AIs weird: why a next-token prediction system is as good as it is, how to measure and interact with these systems, and what they may eventually become.

It has been three years since ChatGPT launched

GenAI is the fastest adoption of an economically consequential technology in human history. And the speed & breadth of that adoption (as well as its continued exponential improvement) is why we have no handle on what it all means.

When a powerful general purpose technology is in the hands of a billion people, a lot of things are going to happen at once. A lot of things are already happening at once. There are few aspects of society - from education to work to the arts to how we communicate - that are not seeing early impact from AI. And likely much more change ahead.

My first published academic paper was on Moore's Law and right now AI development looks similar: the steady exponential growth of Moore's Law was not the result of a single technology, but rather many different technologies over many decades that were ready to take over when one chip-making approach faltered. The regular pace of the Law served as a coordinating function so that an ever-changing group of competitors were pressured to create a self-fulfilling prophecy of continual capability growth.

Similarly, AI development has already hit a number of speedbumps that had to be overcome with new techniques and research (synthetic data approaches, reasoning, new uses for RL). But unless you are an insider (or follow AI closely on LinkedIn), you don't see those speedbumps: just steady exponential progress.

Given the amount of money and talent in the space, I expect that even if pre-training or whatever hits a wall, we will see a quick transition of the entire industry to one or more of the many other approaches people are developing. You can see this already: work on world models, alternatives to LLMs, new training methods, etc. Even alternative ecosystems that are betting on the rise of small, fine-tuned models, etc. Some of these techniques are coming from startups, others are being developed in the AI labs themselves.

People on social media tend to get into the little details, treating AI like a sport, cheering for or against teams and approaches. But over any reasonable span of time, it is possible that AI development looks like a smooth exponential on many metrics to everyone else.

This week, Jensen Huang the founder of a company now worth over $3 trillion sat down with Joe Rogan, and what followed wasn’t a tech interview.
It was a masterclass in what "founder mode" really looks like: resilience, fear, obsession, humility, and the psychology of building something world-changing from nothing.

Having worked with so many founders on their journeys, this really landed for me, and I hope it does for you too.

Here are the 10 founder principles hidden in that conversation that every builder can study:

1. Fear of Failure > Desire for Success

Most chase the high of winning. Jensen runs from the cliff edge.
“I have a greater drive from not wanting to fail than wanting to succeed. Every morning I wake up thinking we’re 30 days from going out of business.”
The principle: mastery looks clean on the outside, but privately, it’s pressure that shapes excellence.

2. Stay Humble Enough to Pivot

He built the wrong tech. Followed the wrong strategy. Nearly died several times.
“All three technology choices were wrong.”
Humility isn’t soft. It’s a survival skill.

3. Go All-In When Logic Says Don’t

He bet half the company’s remaining cash on a machine from a company going out of business.
When no move is safe, founders still choose a direction.

4. Radical Honesty, Even at Maximum Cost

He told Sega their product wouldn’t work, and still asked them for $5M to stay alive.
“If you invest, you’ll likely lose it. If you don’t, we die.”
Real founder mode requires uncomfortable truth.

5. Do the Work No One Sees

He reads thousands of emails a day. Works every day. No off switch.
“Every moment I’m awake, I’m working.”
Behind every “overnight success” is invisible grind.

6. Vulnerability Enables Speed

He invites being corrected.
“If you’re always right, you can’t pivot.”
Ego slows companies. Vulnerability accelerates them.

7. First Principles or Nothing

Always break the problem down. Always re-evaluate. Always assume yesterday might already be wrong.

8. Keep the Survival Mindset Forever

Even as a trillion-dollar CEO:
“The feeling doesn’t go away, the vulnerability, the uncertainty.”
Comfort kills founders faster than competition.

9. Build When No One Understands You

When he launched CUDA, the room was silent.
When he launched DGX1, the room was silent.
He built anyway.
Founders see tomorrow long before anyone believes them.

10. The Immigrant Grit Story Is the Founder Story

Knife fights in Kentucky at nine years old, to building Nvidia.
“Suffering is part of the journey.”
It’s not glamour. It’s grit, risk, and responsibility.

Founder Mode isn’t a vibe, it’s a psychological marathon.

Jensen is proof that the traits that sustain founders aren’t hype, confidence, or charisma, but fear, discipline, humility, and an unbreakable willingness to keep going when every path looks impossible.

Enjoy.

📣 Founders, 🙏 please... stop mixing up ARR, CARR, pilots, and pipe.

Let’s translate 🤓 :
- ARR (Annualized Recurring Revenue): recurring revenue that’s live, deployed, billing, and earned. 
----> Married and living together. Bills paid. Joint Spotify account.

- CARR (Contracted ARR): includes ARR + recurring contracts that are signed but not yet live.
----> Engaged 💍 , invites sent, date locked. Unless someone cheats, it’s happening.

- Pilots: non-recurring tests.
----> Dating. Sometimes exclusive, often not. Not ready to commit. 

- Pipe: future opportunities that might convert into pilots or ARR. 
----> Hinge matches. Maybe you exchanged a few messages. Maybe you set a date to meet up for coffee. 

ARR = live, billing, earned. 
CARR = signed and committed.
Pilots = non-committal experiments.
Pipe = pinky promises and hope.

Stop putting your Hinge chats in your financials!

Why it matters: 👇
 1️⃣ As a founder: you need to know which relationship you’re in.
 If you think you’re engaged but your “partner” is still on Hinge, that’s not PMF... that’s delusion.

2️⃣ For investor reporting (and fundraising marketing): this isn’t semantics.
I’ve seen “a customer promised to expand in the future” or “almost-signed contracts” counted in CARR. Or worse, a 1-month, $10K definitely non-recurring pilot annualized into “$120K ARR.”
To be clear: that’s not clever accounting, that’s borderline fraud... the kind of frothy markets always seem to encourage.

Being naive or “confused about definitions” isn’t an excuse.

There's a professor I know who teaches introductory programming and she told me something last week that's been sitting in my head ever since, which is that half her students can't debug their own code anymore because they never wrote it in the first place and the other half are building things in three weeks that used to take a full semester and she can't figure out if she's watching education get better or watching it collapse.

This is the situation every CS department is dealing with right now and nobody has good answers because the students using AI coding assistants are getting different educations than the ones who aren't, and we won't know which group learned something real until they're five years into their careers and by then it's too late to fix it.

The students think they're being efficient. They have a problem, they ask the AI, it gives them code that works, they move on to the next thing. And from a pure productivity standpoint they're right because they're shipping faster than any previous generation of students could have imagined, building projects that look impressive, getting things done.

But there's a difference between knowing how to get code and knowing how to think about code, and that gap doesn't show up until you hit a problem the AI can't solve and suddenly you realize you never learned how to reason through the hard parts because you always had something else do it for you.

I've talked to hiring managers at companies you've heard of and they're starting to notice something strange in their junior engineer interviews, which is that candidates can talk about impressive projects and show polished portfolios but then struggle with basic debugging questions or can't explain why their own code works the way it does. Not because they're not smart, because they outsourced the learning to a tool that made them faster but not better.

This isn't an argument against AI assistants. They're here and they're not going away and students who refuse to use them are probably making a mistake too. But there's a version of this where we raise a generation of developers who are incredibly good at prompting and incredibly bad at programming, and we won't know we did it wrong until we're already living with the consequences.

The students who figure out how to use these tools to accelerate their learning instead of replace it are going to be unstoppable. The ones who let the AI do the thinking are going to hit a ceiling they don't see coming.

I made the worst enterprise sales mistake possible. It accidentally proved our product-market fit.

A Fortune 500 fintech company had 35 employees using Wispr Flow daily. They loved it. Their IT department reached out about getting an enterprise license for the whole company.

Simple email: "Can we discuss enterprise pricing?"

I was sick. Took me 48 hours to respond.

In enterprise sales, 48 hours might as well be 4 weeks. To them, it signaled we didn't value their business. Fortune 500s expect vendors to treat them like royalty - responses within hours, not days.

Their IT department sent a company-wide memo: "Wispr is unresponsive and unprofessional. Access will be terminated immediately."

35 employees lost access instantly.

Then their Slack exploded.

Not complaints. Full revolt. Hundreds of messages in an hour. People couldn't write docs. Couldn't respond to emails efficiently. Their muscle memory kept reaching for Wispr commands that no longer worked.

When our official pilot period ended, we followed protocol and fully turned off access.

Within one hour: Full enterprise contract signed. Deployed company-wide.

Here's what I learned: There are two types of product-market fit.

First: People like your product.

Second: People revolt when you take it away.

The second is the only one that matters.

Yann LeCun occupies a strange position in modern AI: both a founding father of the deep learning revolution and its most persistent internal critic. For years, he has stood alone, waving a bright red flag from an island the rest of the field pretended didn’t exist.
While the mainstream sprinted into scaling laws - bigger models, bigger datasets, bigger miracles - Yann insisted that scaling alone wouldn’t get us to intelligence, let alone AGI. The prevailing paradigm wasn’t wrong so much as incomplete: a powerful prosthetic for pattern recognition but not a road to reasoning or autonomy.
That message earned him professional eye-rolling and Twitter dunking. He was the “old guard” shouting “this won’t scale forever!” in rooms full of people who believed parameter counts were destiny.

Then last week, the wind shifted. Ilya Sutskever - OpenAI’s co-founder and a chief architect of the scaling era - told Dwarkesh that scaling alone won’t carry us to AGI and that “something crucial is missing", the same claim LeCun has made for years.

Noam Brown’s take captures where the field actually is. If you listen to researchers, not twitter shitposters, there’s surprising consensus:
- The current paradigm will deliver enormous economic impact
- It will not, on its own, deliver AGI or ASI
- Breakthroughs beyond today’s architecture are likely within 5–20 years
Even eternal optimists like Demis, Chollet, and Dario acknowledge the missing ingredients. It just took Ilya’s heresy for everyone else to look up.

So, what has Yann been saying? Not that LLMs are useless, but that they are architecturally incomplete. 
The mainstream has bet heavily on a simple idea: if we scale a model large enough and expose it to enough data, intelligence will emerge as an emergent property of size. LeCun rejects this premise outright. Prediction, he argues, cannot substitute for comprehension.

You can memorize every cookbook and still not know how to scramble an egg. Human intelligence isn’t about replaying patterns; it’s about having an internal sense of how the world works. We carry around mental models - of objects, forces, intentions, physics, causality - that are constantly refined as we move through the world. These models let us navigate unfamiliar situations, infer intentions, imagine consequences, and make decisions under uncertainty.

LLMs lack such models. They are 'surface learners' that generate fluent text without embedded notions of physics or psychology. LeCun champions self-supervised learning beyond text: systems that learn by watching the world, forming expectations, and updating when surprised.

If the transformer era is AI’s Newtonian phase - where data and compute explain everything - LeCun is already arguing for its Einsteinian correction: a paradigm that restores geometry, causality, and embodied prediction to the center of cognition. Whether or not his vision prevails, the field is finally drifting toward the island he refused to leave.

In the coming months and years, there will be more conversations about what the American workforce looks like in the age of AI. Instead of asking whether AI will eliminate ALL our jobs, we should be thinking about how to unlock new jobs created by the AI boom. Today’s WSJ piece highlights the good, well-paying jobs being created right here in the US by AI investment.

From the article: An investment boom in artificial intelligence is creating a thirst for massive data centers—and a bonanza for the workers building them. The "gold rush" for talent in the data-center industry means that welders, electricians and construction workers are earning 25% to 30% more, says staffing firm Kelly Services.

This is the kind of second-order effect that is routinely underestimated in conversations about who benefits from AI. The reality is that benefits from technology spill outward, often dramatically, into the broader workforce. When industries invest with urgency and ambition, the ripple effects are both broad and egalitarian. Decades ago, America saw this in aerospace manufacturing. Later, in cloud computing. Now it’s data centers.

America’s dynamic economy gives us a unique advantage: when a new sector emerges, we can build it here. In a world where China is making massive state-backed bets to dominate advanced technologies, our ability to mobilize capital, talent, and innovation domestically is an essential form of competition. And it’s good for American workers. These projects create high-quality jobs that are rooted in local communities, not outsourced abroad. They strengthen the middle class while reinforcing our position as the global leader in the next era of technology.

The AI boom has also granted the United States a real chance to reboot domestic energy production. We’re in an energy race with China, and right now, they’re lapping us. China added over 300 gigawatts of new energy capacity in 2024 alone, including 216 GW of solar and 75 GW of wind. The U.S., by comparison, added around 45–50 GW across all sources. 

If we applied the same national ambition to domestic energy (particularly clean, abundant, reliable energy) the gains would be similarly dramatic. Modernizing transmission lines, building next-generation nuclear, expanding geothermal, and upgrading grid storage: these projects require tens of thousands of skilled workers. They produce long-term, geographically diffuse economic value. And they accelerate innovation by giving entrepreneurs and scientists a stable foundation to build upon.

We’re on the cusp of a generational economic opportunity. AI is pushing American industry to invest in ways that have immediate, measurable benefits for everyday workers. If we extend that ambition—if we apply the same spirit of innovation and coordinated action to energy—the result won’t just be technological leadership. It will be a broad-based renewal of the American workforce.

Always doubt a "systems thinker" who talks like they understand how everything works. 

We all have had individual experiences that shaped what we notice and what we ignore.

We all grew up surrounded by a particular context that feels universal to us, but is not.

We all carry blind spots / focal points from the environments that rewarded us and the ones that punished us

We all built mental shortcuts based on what worked for us once, somewhere, for someone.

We all mistake familiarity for truth (do we believe it when we see it, or see it when we believe it?)

We all overgeneralize from the tiny N of our own lives.

We all have cognitive styles that make some explanations feel “obvious” and others feel “nonsense.”

We all have mixes of temperament, neuro-chemistry, and instinct that filter how we see cause and effect.

We all rely on models that break the moment the context changes.

We all believe our interpretations are more objective than they actually are.

We all fill in gaps with stories that make things look more coherent than we/they actually are.

We all drift into certainty when we should stay with humility and curiosity (and maybe even naiveté ) a bit longer.

We all get seduced by elegant explanations, even when the world is messier. This includes "elegant" explanations including system dynamics, etc.

We all selectively remember the moments that reinforce our worldview, and block out the rest.

We all unconsciously defend the systems that gave us identity, status, or success. If we're rewarded for "connecting dots", then that gives us identity.

We all “see” systems differently depending on our role in them.

And that might even be the true systems thinking :)

Imagine waking up tomorrow and realising your entire digital presence has been wiped out. 

That’s the story that played out last week, just on a much bigger scale.

Two days ago, X removed the European Commission's advertising account.

Not a random creator.
Not a business.
A government institution.

And it happened days after they were fined €120 million for breaking transparency rules.

I've advised enough teams on platform risk to know this: 

Most people underestimate how fragile their digital presence actually is.

Your entire online footprint - your audience, your distribution, your content - exists on platforms that can shut the door without warning.

No explanation.
No appeal.
No conversation.

And no compensation of the damages they caused.

And if they can do it to a government, imagine the leverage they hold over you.

Think about it: this is textbook operational risk.

Whether you're a founder, creator, leader, or marketer, you need to take this seriously.

Because you are building your business on rented land.

And the landlord is getting less predictable by the month.

Remember: most people only realise they’re in trouble after they’ve lost everything they built.

What actually happened:

The EU fined X €120 million for violating digital transparency regulations (misleading verification badges, hiding ad data, blocking researcher access).

X's response? Delete their account.

They accused the Commission of "exploiting" their ad system to artificially boost a post about the fine.

A corporation just punished a regulatory body for doing its job.

What most people don't want to hear:

If your entire distribution sits inside one platform, you don't own a business.

You own a dependency.

The smartest operators I know do three things extremely well:

1. They own their data

Email lists. Websites. Direct relationships.

Platforms are megaphones, not foundations.

You control what you own.

2. They diversify their presence

If one platform pulls the plug, the system keeps running.

LinkedIn. Newsletter. Blog. Community.

Never let a single company hold your entire audience hostage.

3. They build defensibility into their digital identity

Portable data. Clear rights. Transparent governance.

Support decentralized alternatives where you control your identity (Mastodon, Bluesky, owned platforms).

You deserve a digital presence that isn't dependent on a billionaire's mood.

So, consider this:

Your voice shouldn't exist at the pleasure of shareholders.

Your reach shouldn't disappear because you criticized the platform.

Your years of work shouldn't vanish with one decision you can't appeal.

Most people won't take this seriously until it happens to them.

By then, it's too late.

If you vanished from your main platform tomorrow, would you still have a business?

Most people wouldn't. And that's the real problem.
