Massive Study Finds Synthetic Humans Aren't Human

What happens when someone has the brilliant idea to ask 2K Americans hundreds and hundreds of questions to create a digital twin of each of them, with the purpose being to use these twins as a source for market research insights?

This is exactly what academics at  Columbia Business School, with collaborators from Barnard, Yale, and Yeshiva did over the period April- July this year. 

What Information Did They Gather? 

500+ survey answers that included- personality batteries, cognitive tests, economic preferences and pricing tasks, political questions, privacy judgments, UX nudges, emotional primes, creativity tests, brand perceptions, and news-sharing tasks. 

What Happened When Researchers Questioned the Digital Twins?

The twins that were stuffed with hundreds of human data points, but when asked about targeted advertising, humans felt intruded upon and got defensive, the twins responded like calm privacy officers weighing trade-offs. 

For news-sharing tasks, humans chased emotional dopamine hits; twins chose articles based on "civic usefulness.”

Across moral trade-offs, risk perception, and open-ended questions, humans showed contradiction, defensiveness, anxiety, boredom, and bias; twins delivered polished, consistent, moderate, well-reasoned answers every time.

The problem was that 500 survey responses were no match for the vastly deeper patterns baked into the LLMs through training that pushes the LLM to be measured, rational, avoid controversy, resolve ambiguity gracefully and present balanced perspectives. 

Even though the academics fed hundreds of real human responses, it was drowned by the training data..

The implication for market research is clear digital twins are not human substitutes; they obliterate the emotional, irrational, inconsistent behavior that actually drives markets. 

Real consumers misread instructions, get offended, contradict themselves, under-report bad habits, overstate good ones, abandon tasks, follow heuristics, and react through identity. 

Twins don't. They're too rational, too optimistic, too obedient, too moderate, and too similar to one another — idealized personas with all the human texture sanded off. 

They miss political heat, fear spikes, friction in UX flows, social desirability bias, brand grudges, and the emotional volatility that makes creative work succeed or fail.

The twins simulated the humans we wish we had, not the humans actually buying products.

“If you want to know if the elephant at the zoo has a stomachache, don’t ask the veterinarian, ask the cage cleaner.” — Robert Sapolsky

The accidental discovery of Type A behaviour.

In the 1950s, Meyer Friedman and Ray Rosenman kept replacing waiting-room chairs that were shredded only on the front edges. They ignored it. Then an upholsterer arrived and said what the cardiologists hadn’t even considered:

“What’s wrong with your patients? People don’t sit like this.”

Only later did the cardiologists realise the pattern he’d spotted instantly:
their patients were perching on the edge of their seats, fidgeting, clawing the armrests — primed for a quick getaway.

They even parked their cars facing the exit so they could leave without delay, and they booked the first appointment of the day so no one could hold them up.
All the clues were there, hiding in plain sight.
The upholsterer saw the anomaly the experts filtered out.

“A change of perspective is worth 80 IQ points.” — Alan Kay (computer scientist)

A crisp manufacturer faced a similar blind spot decades later. Their stacked crisps held too much oil, and every engineering fix shattered them. So they opened the problem on Innocentive.

The winning idea came from a violinist in the New York Philharmonic.

Oil and crisp vibrate at different frequencies, she reasoned.
Use sound waves, and the oil shakes loose without breaking the stack.

It worked.
Someone with no connection to food manufacturing solved a problem insiders couldn’t see.

And then there’s Goldcorp’s Red Lake mine — 55,000 acres of hard rock, blasting caps, and industrial machinery. By 1999 the mine was dying. Gold was drying up. Then CEO Rob McEwen returned from MIT with a wild thought:

“What if we put our geological data on the internet?”

In mining, secrecy was sacred. But they took the leap, posted thousands of pages of data online, and offered prize money for new targets.

Retired geologists, graduate students, military analysts — people far outside the industry — found 110 targets Goldcorp had missed.
Four-fifths contained gold.
The company went from $100m to $9bn.

Three very different contexts.
One pattern:

The upholsterer.
The violinist.
The global crowd of amateur geologists.

Breakthroughs often come from people you weren’t listening to — people who aren’t burdened by the assumptions of the field.

If you want real innovation, widen the aperture.
Not for opinions — for anomalies.

And pay attention to the person who notices the small, strange thing the rest of us step over on the way to being “busy.”

As Hal Gregersen once told me on The Innovation Show: “Instead of passively getting secondary data, actively seek passive data by seeking anomalies.”

The Buttered Cat Paradox

“The test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two opposed ideas in mind at the same time and still retain the ability to function.”
— F. Scott Fitzgerald

Physicist Robert Matthews once showed that toast lands butter-side down not because of bad luck but because of design.
The height of the average table gives toast just enough time to rotate half a turn before it hits the floor.
The outcome is due to structure.

That same logic applies to organisations.

We seek innovation, agility, experimentation.
Yet we design for efficiency, predictability, control.

We want exploration, but measure exploitation.

It’s the folly of expecting one behaviour while rewarding another — exactly what Steven Kerr described in On the Folly of Rewarding A While Hoping for B. Organisations frequently bonus people for behaviour A while hoping for behaviour B — then wonder why B never shows.

The contradiction is baked in.

Enter the Buttered Cat Paradox:

Cats always land on their feet.
Toast always lands butter side down.

So, what do you get if you strap buttered toast to the back of a falling cat?

Humourist John Frazee proposed they’d hover — spinning midair, caught between two forces, neither able to triumph.

Many organisations live in that kind of tailspin.

They know they need to exploit — drive performance, serve current customers, deliver returns.
They also know they must explore — search for new models, innovate, adapt for the future.

James G. March’s classic essay Exploration & Exploitation in Organizational Learning shows this tension is real, persistent, and often destructive if poorly managed. 

He argues that systems tend to drift toward exploitation — refining what they know to get more immediate returns — while exploration is neglected because it’s riskier, slower, and its rewards are distant. 

Exploration is uncertain, delayed, and doubtful. 
Exploitation is immediate, reliable, and proximal. 

March warns that this drift — favoring exploitation over exploration — is self-destructive in the long run. 

The paradox is not about desire but design.

If innovation keeps “landing butter-side down,” perhaps the system — the incentives, the KPIs, the processes — is rigged to favour safe bets.

To resolve the paradox, we must redesign the structure.
We must reward exploration as courageously as we reward exploitation.
We must structure time, slack, and autonomy so experiments can breathe.

Only then will the cat land on its feet — butter side up.

A bunch of "AI" companies are just thin wrappers around ChatGPT or even worse, a bunch of messy if-statements in a trench coat.

For every OpenAI, Anthropic, and Perplexity, there are 1,000 startups that end up on a journey to nowhere.

AI is incredibly far from perfect, but it's 100% here to stay. Every engineer who wants to grow their career should join an AI-first company.

The tricky part is figuring out which companies are actually integrating AI in an elegant way.

Uncomfortable truth most don't want to hear:

Running AI pilots won't lead to results.

Redesigning the processes and the org will. 

Everyone bought AI. 
Almost nobody changed how they work.

Look at the stats from McKinsey’s new State of AI in 2025 report (link in comments).

→ Bought AI tools - 88% 
→ Running AI pilots - 66%
→ Built AI agents - 33%
→ Scaled AI agents - 23%
→ Redesigned the organisation -  6%

This is where most companies are stuck.

They bought the tools. 
They ran the pilots. 
They even built a few agents.

But the org chart looks the same. 
The workflows haven't changed. 
The decision-making paths are untouched.

And then they wonder why ROI isn't materialising.

Here's the uncomfortable part:
The AI models are not the bottleneck. 
The operating model underneath is.

The companies pulling ahead aren't just adopting AI. 
They're rebuilding how work flows. 

How decisions get made. 
How knowledge moves through the system. 
How humans and agents collaborate.

Layering AI over legacy systems gets you marginal improvements

AI within a redesigned structure delivers transformation.

The gap between "experimenting" and "scaling" isn't more tools. It’s the willingness to redesign.

And that door is still wide open.

AI doesn’t need hype.

It needs solid fundamentals. 

(Thanks to Clare Kitching for the inspiration and image)

Your AI vision won't save you if fundamentals are broken.

Leadership wants to ship 2x faster with AI.
Autonomous coding. AI agents. GenAI everywhere.

But here is the truth:

AI doesn't fix broken product development.
Maybe it even amplifies it! 

Unclear requirements become unclear code.
Technical debt amplifies architectural chaos.

That doesn't mean you focus only on fundamentals. 

You need both: fix the basics AND pursue the AI vision.

Here is the real path to AI-accelerated development:

First, establish the foundation:
→ Diagnose your actual bottlenecks (not assumptions)
→ Create clear, executable specifications
→ Build architecture that AI can understand and extend
→ Address the technical debt blocking velocity

Then, work on the AI vision in parallel:
→ Start small: one workflow, one domain
→ Introduce AI where specs are solid
→ Scale what proves valuable
→ Keep iterating while strengthening foundations

This isn't about choosing between fundamentals and innovation.

It's about creating the conditions where AI actually delivers on its promise.

What's really blocking faster delivery in your organisation?

The fastest way to understand AI governance?
Stop thinking about data. Start thinking about decisions.

There's a lot of noise around governance in the age of AI.
And leaders cut through some of that noise by understanding the split between inputs and outcomes.

Data governance protects the inputs.
It’s about accuracy, privacy, lineage, access and compliance.
It answers: Can we trust the data going into our systems?

AI governance protects the outcomes.
It deals with fairness, explainability, drift and human oversight.
It answers: Can we trust the decisions made on outputs of AI systems?

Both matter.
Both are essential.
But they serve different purposes.

What I’ve seen inside organisations this year is that AI governance doesn’t sit neatly with one owner.
Legal, risk, product, ethics, engineering and the business all hold a piece.

That’s normal, because the outcomes affect so many parts of the organisation.

The companies making real progress connect the two:
They build strong data governance first. Clean foundations mean AI systems fail predictably, not catastrophically.

Then they layer AI governance on top as the mechanism that gives business leaders confidence to actually use what's been built.

Have a look at the two side by side to see where each plays a role.

What's your experience? Where does AI governance live in your organisation, and is it working?

Separate reports by the publicity firm Edelman and Pew Research (links in orig text, below) show that Americans, and more broadly large parts of Europe and the western world, do not trust AI and are not excited about it. Despite the AI community’s optimism about the tremendous benefits AI will bring, we should take this seriously and not dismiss it. The public’s concerns about AI can be a significant drag on progress, and we can do a lot to address them.

According to Edelman’s survey, in the U.S., 49% of people reject the growing use of AI, and 17% embrace it. In China, 10% reject it and 54% embrace it. Pew’s data also shows many other nations much more enthusiastic than the U.S. about AI adoption.

Positive sentiment toward AI is a huge national advantage. On the other hand, widespread distrust of AI means:
- Individuals will be slow to adopt it. For example, Edelman’s data shows that, in the U.S., those who rarely use AI cite Trust (70%) more than lack of Motivation and Access (55%) or Intimidation by the technology (12%) as an issue.
- Valuable projects that need societal support will be stymied. For example, local protests in Indiana brought down Google’s plan to build a data center there. Hampering construction of data centers will hurt AI’s growth. Communities do have concerns about data centers beyond the general dislike of AI; I will address this in a later letter.
- Populist anger against AI raises the risk that laws will be passed that hamper AI development.

To be clear, all of us working in AI should look carefully at both the benefits and harmful effects of AI (such as deepfakes polluting social media and biased or inaccurate AI outputs misleading users), speak truthfully about both benefits and harms, and work to ameliorate problems even as we work to grow the benefits. But hype about AI’s danger has done real damage to trust in our field. Much of this hype has come from leading AI companies that aim to make their technology seem extraordinarily powerful by, say, comparing it to nuclear weapons. Unfortunately, a significant fraction of the public has taken this seriously and thinks AI could bring about the end of the world. The AI community has to stop self-inflicting these wounds and work to win back society’s trust.

Where do we go from here?

First, to win people’s trust, we have a lot of work ahead to make sure AI broadly benefits everyone. “Higher productivity” is often viewed by general audiences as a codeword for “my boss will make more money,” or worse, layoffs. As amazing as ChatGPT is, we still have a lot of work to do to build applications that make an even bigger positive impact on people’s lives. I believe providing training to people will be a key piece of the puzzle. DeepLearning.AI will continue to lead the charge on AI training, but we will need more than this.

Is there an AI bubble? With the massive number of dollars going into AI infrastructure such as OpenAI’s $1.4 trillion plan and Nvidia briefly reaching a $5 trillion market cap, many have asked if speculation and hype have driven the values of AI investments above sustainable values. However, AI isn’t monolithic, and different areas look bubbly to different degrees.
- AI application layer: There is underinvestment. The potential is still much greater than most realize.
- AI infrastructure for inference: This still needs significant investment.
- AI infrastructure for model training: I’m still cautiously optimistic about this sector, but there could also be a bubble.

Caveat: I am absolutely not giving investment advice!

AI application layer. There are many applications yet to be built over the coming decade using new AI technology. Almost by definition, applications that are built on top of AI infrastructure/technology (such as LLM APIs) have to be more valuable than the infrastructure, since we need them to be able to pay the infrastructure and technology providers.

I am seeing many green shoots across many businesses that are applying agentic workflows, and am confident this will grow. I have also spoken with many Venture Capital investors who hesitate to invest in AI applications because they feel they don’t know how to pick winners, whereas the recipe for deploying $1B to build AI infrastructure is better understood. Some have also bought into the hype that almost all AI applications will be wiped out merely by frontier LLM companies improving their foundation models. Overall, I believe there is significant underinvestment in AI applications. This area remains a huge focus for my venture studio, AI Fund.

AI infrastructure for inference. Despite AI’s low penetration today, infrastructure providers are already struggling to fulfill demand for processing power to generate tokens. Several of my teams are worried about whether we can get enough inference capacity, and both cost and inference throughput are limiting our ability to use even more. It is a good problem to have that businesses are supply-constrained rather than demand-constrained. The latter is a much more common problem, when not enough people want your product. But insufficient supply is nonetheless a problem, which is why I am glad our industry is investing significantly in scaling up inference capacity.

As one concrete example of high demand for token generation, highly agentic coders are progressing rapidly. I’ve long been a fan of Claude Code; OpenAI Codex also improved dramatically with the release of GPT-5; and Gemini 3 has made Google CLI very competitive. As these tools improve, their adoption will grow. At the same time, overall market penetration is still low, and many developers are still using older generations of coding tools (and some aren’t even using any agentic coding tools).

I recently received an email titled “An 18-year-old’s dilemma: Too late to contribute to AI?” Its author, who gave me permission to share this, is preparing for college. He is worried that by the time he graduates, AI will be so good there’s no meaningful work left for him to do to contribute to humanity, and he will just live on Universal Basic Income (UBI). I wrote back to reassure him that there will still be plenty of work he can do for decades hence, and encouraged him to work hard and learn to build with AI. But this conversation struck me as an example of how harmful hype about AI is.

Yes, AI is amazingly intelligent, and I’m thrilled to be using it every day to build things I couldn’t have built a year ago. At the same time, AI is still incredibly dumb, and I would not trust a frontier LLM by itself to prioritize my calendar, carry out resumé screening, or choose what to order for lunch — tasks that businesses routinely ask junior personnel to do.

Yes, we can build AI software to do these tasks. For example, after a lot of customization work, one of my teams now has a decent AI resumé screening assistant. But the point is it took a lot of customization.

Even though LLMs can handle a much more general set of tasks than previous iterations of AI technology, compared to what humans can do, they are still highly specialized. They’re much better at working with text than other modalities, still require lots of custom engineering to get it the right context for a particular application, and we have few tools — and only inefficient ones — for getting our systems to learn from feedback and repeated exposure to a specific task (such as screening resumés for a particular role).

AI has stark limitations, and despite rapid improvements, it will remain limited compared to humans for a long time.

AI is amazing, but it has unfortunately been hyped up to be even more amazing than it is. A pernicious aspect of hype is that it often contains an element of truth, but not to the degree of the hype. This makes it difficult for nontechnical people to discern where the truth really is. Modern AI is a general purpose technology that is enabling many applications, but AI that can do any intellectual tasks that a human can (a popular definition for AGI) is still decades away or longer. This nuanced message that AI is general, but not that general, often is lost in the noise of today's media environment.

AI agents are getting better at looking at different types of data in businesses to spot patterns and create value. This is making data silos increasingly painful. This is why I increasingly try to select software that lets me control my own data, so I can make it available to my AI agents.

Because of AI’s growing capabilities, the value you can now create from “connecting the dots” between different pieces of data is higher than ever. For example, if an email click is logged in one vendor’s system and a subsequent online purchase is logged in a different one, then it is valuable to build agents that can access both of these data sources to see how they correlate to make better decisions.

Unfortunately, many SaaS vendors try to create a data silo in their customer’s business. By making it hard for you to extract your data, they create high switching costs. This also allows them to steer you to buy their AI agent services — sometimes at high expense and/or of low quality — rather than build your own or buy from a different vendor. Unfortunately, some SaaS vendors are seeing AI agents coming for this data and working to make it harder for you (and your AI agents) to efficiently access it.

One of my teams just told me that a SaaS vendor we have been using to store our customer data wants to charge over $20,000 for an API key to get at our data. This high cost — no doubt intentionally designed to make it hard for customers to get their data out — is adding a barrier to implementing agentic workflows that take advantage of that data.

Through AI Aspire (an AI advisory firm), I advise a number of businesses on their AI strategies. When it comes to buying SaaS, I often advise them to try to control their own data (which, sadly, some vendors mightily resist). This way, you can hire a SaaS vendor to record and operate on your data, but ultimately you decide how to route it to the appropriate human or AI system for processing.

Over the past decade, a lot of work has gone into organizing businesses’ structured data. Because AI can now process unstructured data much better than before, the value of organizing your unstructured data (including PDF files, which LandingAI’s Agentic Document Extraction specializes in!) is higher than ever before.

In the era of generative AI, businesses and individuals have important work ahead to organize their data to be AI-ready.

P.S. As an individual, my favorite note-taking app is Obsidian. I am happy to “hire” Obsidian to operate on my notes files. And, all my notes are saved as Markdown files in my file system, and I have built AI agents that read from or write to my Obsidian files. This is a small example of how controlling my own notes data lets me do more with AI agents!

Is Transparency About AI Decisions More Important Than Accuracy?

I was experimenting with an AI system this week when something surprising happened.

The answer it gave me was so precise and so polished that I felt my brain relax a little too quickly.

And that moment made me pause.

What if accuracy becomes so good that it quietly replaces our instinct to question?

Here is what kept circling in my mind:
• People trust outcomes faster than reasoning
• When something feels correct, scrutiny dissolves
• Transparency becomes a rescue tool rather than a standard

Most debates treat transparency as a moral checkbox, but in my opinion, it is something far more practical.

It protects the cognitive effort that accuracy tends to erase.

I keep thinking of it like this:

Accuracy helps us move faster.

Transparency keeps us awake while we move.

A perspective most people overlook

When systems become highly reliable, we start outsourcing judgment without noticing it.

This gradual dependency is the real risk, not the model itself.

The brain adapts to whatever feels easy, even if that ease comes at the cost of long term reasoning.

So instead of choosing one side, here is what I think teams should build for:
✔ Clear explanations that reveal the logic behind decisions
✔ Light touch prompts that encourage users to question even strong answers
✔ Interfaces that show the source, the guess, and the reasoning separately
✔ A daily habit of checking one AI output for hidden assumptions
✔ Systems that preserve curiosity rather than suppress it

Accuracy gives us confidence today.

Transparency protects our cognition tomorrow.

A question I keep returning to
If AI becomes flawless, will humans forget how to challenge anything that feels correct?

Most early stage companies don't actually know how organizational structures should work. So they end up forcing reorgs. I've been through so many reorgs in my time at startups—and they're always a bit dramatic, political, and slow you down right when you need to move fast.

At Linear, we took a different approach: collapsible teams.

Here's how it works:

Traditional model: You're on the Payments Team. Or the Platform Team. Or the Growth Team. You work on that scope forever, even when it's not the highest priority for the company. Teams get territorial. Medium-priority work piles up. You're stuck in your lane.

Linear's collapsible model: Teams form around high-priority projects, not permanent departments. We spin up a cross-functional team, ship in a matter of weeks not months, move it to maintenance mode, then disband and reassign to the next most important thing.

Every time we do this, it's almost like a mini reorg—but we don't have to call it that. You're just moving on to the next thing that's highest priority for the company.

Why this matters:
→ Engineers and designers work across the full product surface area 
→ Resources flow to what's actually important right now, not what was important six months ago
→ People work on things that are, by definition, very important (because we're choosing to spin up a new project)
→ You stay nimble, while team mates feel informed on the company's priorities 
The question we ask: "We don't know what our most important priorities will be in 6 months. So why should we organize our team as if we do?"

This philosophy has let us serve 15,000+ customers with a small and nimble team. And we've never had to do a traditional reorg.

A message to 16 year old me.... one of the biggest unlocks early in your career will be "proximity"...

....not the proximity everyone talks about. Not "get in the room where it happens" or "you're the average of five people" or whatever that overquoted Jim Rohn line is.

I mean proximity to people actively explaining their craft while they're doing it.

The difference between watching someone successful and watching someone explain their thinking while they work is the difference between seeing a magic trick and learning how it's done...

When I was 19 I worked in San Fransisco for a CEO who narrated his thinking to me in real time... The confusion, the fifteen wrong attempts, the actual mess of thinking, why he was making decisions and every detail of why he believed what he believed.

You don't need access to the best people...you need access to good people who can explain themselves. 

Most proximity is useless because it's silent. You watch but don't understand. 

Being close to excellence that offers no explanation is useless.

Find the people who work with their mouth open. Who think out loud. Who show their rough drafts. Who explain their failures in real time.

I think about this a lot now as a leader - and I try my best with my team to think out-loud, to explain every detail of my thinking (even if it slows me down) and to avoid assuming everyone else has the context that I've spent 15 years building up.

That's the proximity that changes everything for junior team members...

Not being in the room where it happens. Being in the room where someone explains how it happens.....while it's happening.

Have you worked with leaders like this that took the time to explain things to you?

this is probably an unexpected opinion coming from me... entrepreneurship is over-sold and self-awareness is under-sold 👇🏾

The not so popular truth is, most people would be happier with a good salary than a successful startup.

Entrepreneurs like me get a lot of likes and followers when we tell people to quit their jobs and chase their dreams.

But here is the context that we nearly always miss👇🏾

Entrepreneurship can be really really boring - you will have to do things you do not enjoy.

You will deal with big, hard, stressful problems, every day - including bank holidays, christmas and any other time off - for years.

If you're lucky enough to be successful, the problems will get bigger, not smaller.

You will not have one boss. You will have hundreds - every customer, every investor, every employee. You will answer to them 24/7.

You will probably work 3x the hours you do now, have 10x the stress and a tiny probability of significant success.

A recent survey found 87.7% of founders deal with at least mental health issues. That's not a bug. It's a feature of entrepreneurship.

You will probably pay yourself last and as little as possible.

You'll struggle to switch off. Forever. Your phone will probably become a prison.

And here's the punchline: If you succeed, it all gets harder.

More money = more complexity. More growth = more anxiety. More success = more people depending on you.

In life, when you find yourself following someone else's playbook, you are at risk of winning someone else's prizes. All I'm saying is be intentional.

I'm not AGAINST entrepreneurship, I'm FOR self-awareness.

Truth "wealth" is probably👇🏾

Product people in startups and big companies must understand that in practice there’s no such thing as “making time for strategic thinking”.

In practice, every world-class product person thinks strategically through every decision, through all the ‘tactical work’ they need to do. It is embedded into every minute of every day.

It is true that you need to make time to *clarify and write down your product strategy*. But even with that, the real work of yourself getting clarity on your product strategy happens organically, over time, as you’re doing your day to day work of talking to customers, doing sales calls, reviewing product artifacts from your team, hiring for key roles, prioritizing between customer demands, etc.

That real thinking work cannot happen in a 2-day “strategy offsite” where you order high carb catered meals and do kumbaya exercises to boost x-fn morale in between BHAG sessions and colorful post it note sessions to cluster people’s random ideas.

The proof this strategy theater doesn’t actually work is already in front of you: you’ve done countless such strategy offsites before, never once formulating a true product strategy that your team actually implements over the next year or two. So what makes you think this next post it note session of yours will be any different?

For leaders willing to break from old habits, it is useful to differentiate between these:
- Strategic thinking (continuous, embedded)
- Strategic articulation (discrete, requires dedicated time, solo or a tiny tiny group)
- Strategic theater (offsites that produce nothing except *fleeting* vibes)

Understand that you work for your employees, they don't work for you.

This one is hard for a lot of leaders to understand.

Most new managers think that becoming a manager is the “graduation.” Truth is, it’s the reverse.

Leaders work for their employees.

That means you have to understand what your employees want at a deep level. You have to be constantly adapting to their needs and what they want from the organization.

For example, one of my employees might want higher pay when he’s 24. But maybe he falls in love at 28 and decides he wants to spend more time with his family. Another might be more interested in a fancy title. Another might want to get access to me and build a relationship. 

Another might want to go to one of our international offices and work there.

There are a million different variables, and it’s on you as a leader to adjust to reality as it changes.

When you go from being someone who “executes” to someone who’s managing a team, you go from trading on IQ to trading on EQ. You go from doing the actual work to listening to employees, catering to what they want, taking blame, and being the bigger person.

The best managers are actually the best mentors.

RFPs were created to bring clarity and fairness to the buying process.

But more often than not, they create:

- Vagueness (“We’re still figuring out what we need”)
- Checklist overload (hundreds of requirements, few that matter)
- Shifting priorities halfway through the process
- Wasted energy defending features that no one will ever use

In the end, buyers feel they’ve been “thorough,” vendors feel exhausted, and neither side is closer to solving the actual business problem.

Here’s what we can do differently:

1. Start with outcomes, not specs.
Don’t begin with a 200-point feature list. Begin with: “We want to cut processing errors by 80%.” Features should follow outcomes, not the other way around.


2. Allow iteration.
Issue lighter RFPs, run discovery workshops, or launch a short pilot. It’s faster and cheaper than producing binders of requirements that no one can realistically validate.


3. Reward transparency.
Good vendors will tell you where trade-offs exist. Encourage that instead of penalizing it.


4. Be honest about readiness.
If you’re not budget-approved or aligned internally, say it. Vendors would rather hear the truth than pour weeks into a phantom project.

When done right, RFPs become a collaborative exercise: a way to refine problems and align solutions.

When done wrong, they become bureaucratic theater.

And at Invofox, we only sell to software companies so we know exactly who, what, and why we’re selling.

That focus lets us skip the noise, speak the same language, and sell to teams that actually ship.

Strategy is not storytelling.

It took me years to understand that the industry’s devotion to narrative structure and beautifully crafted decks had moved the discipline toward performance (in the theatrical sense) rather than toward actual strategic rigour.

For most of my career I treated storytelling as the defining skill of a strategist. I believed that if a presentation moved people, if the arc felt elegant, the strategy would land. Agencies celebrated the strategist who could turn scattered observations into compelling narrative. Clients rewarded those who made complexity feel cinematic. I assumed narrative clarity and strategic clarity were the same thing.

The problem was that storytelling helped me sell strategy, but it did not help me do strategy. Narrative created coherence even when diagnosis was incomplete. It encouraged me to smooth contradictions, to round edges, to make work feel resolved before thinking was resolved. The more I relied on storytelling, the more it gave me the illusion of strategy rather than the discipline of it.

So over time, I changed my process.

The first shift was accepting that strategy begins with a constraint, not a narrative. Growth is blocked by something specific, and if that constraint is not identified correctly, everything built upon it becomes theatrically convincing but practically ineffective. Weak codes, fragile mental availability and poor distribution do not respond to elegant storytelling. They respond only to accurate diagnosis.

The second shift was understanding that the brain operates through prediction, not narrative interpretation. The brain reduces uncertainty by retrieving the simplest and most familiar expectation when a category cue appears. Storytelling tries to construct meaning. Strategy designs the expectation that fires automatically in the buying moment.

The third shift was moving strategy from meaning toward memory. Storytelling emphasises symbolism and cultural resonance, but advertising works by strengthening memory traces that survive low attention and can be retrieved at speed. Without mapping the existing memory structure and designing the system that reinforces it, the story becomes irrelevant.

The fourth shift was abandoning narrative arcs for reinforcement loops. Stories progress across time. Brands grow through consistency and repetition. Encoding happens through rehearsal, not through beautifully sequenced chapters. When codes change too frequently, memory collapses. When the reinforcement system is stable, the brand becomes easier to retrieve with every exposure.

The final shift was redefining creativity as the engine that encodes the pattern, not as narrative extension. Creative expression must evolve, but only within constraints that protect distinctiveness. Storytelling prioritises originality. Strategy prioritises continuity that compounds.

Story, and narrative, are vital. But they're not strategy.

Strategy is choice, sacrifice, and direction.

Which undergraduate programs have the highest likelihood of "producing" a unicorn founder? 

Attending Yale University increases the odds by 100%, while Stanford University and Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) increase the odds by 60%. Harvard University increases the odds by 50%, and University of California, Berkeley by 40%. 

How did we get these results? We analyzed data covering 1,404 US-based companies that became unicorns in private rounds or upon an exit between January 1997 and October 2025. We compared the frequency of universities that unicorn founders attended as undergraduates against the founders of the random sample of VC-backed companies. 

Stanford University holds the highest number of unicorns in our sample (256) with an odds ratio of 1.6. This means that getting a degree from Stanford increases one’s chances of achieving unicorn status (if one founds a VC-backed startup) by 60% compared to the baseline. 

Yale University stands out with the highest likelihood: an odds ratio of 2.0, suggesting a 100% increase in likelihood relative to the random sample. 

Other universities with significantly higher chances than average include Cornell University (40%), Princeton University (40%), and the University of Washington (30%). 

The University of Pennsylvania, Columbia University, and UCLA show a smaller lift (20%). The University of Michigan, despite producing a high number of unicorns (53), shows slightly lower chances than the random sample average. 

Note: This post features the top 12 universities ranked by the number of US-based unicorn founders.

When should I use a workflow, and when should I use a supervisor pattern?

The Workflow Agent Pattern – Control and Auditability:

A workflow is your structured teammate.
Every step is explicit — retrieving data, validating it, calling the agent, checking guardrails, then generating a response.

Use a workflow when:
✔ You need accuracy and consistency
✔ Every action must be logged and auditable
✔ You’re touching systems of record (finance, HR, service)
✔ The process should be predictable and repeatable

It’s the right choice when compliance, control, and reliability come first.

⸻

The Supervisor + Agents Pattern – Flexibility and Adaptability
A supervisor pattern is more like a team lead.
It listens, reasons, and routes work dynamically — deciding whether the Payroll Agent or the Tax Agent should respond, or if both should collaborate.

Use a supervisor when:
✔ The path to an answer isn’t always clear
✔ You want agents to reason across multiple domains
✔ You need faster experimentation and more natural conversations
✔ The task is exploratory or analytical, not transactional

You trade a bit of structure for adaptability and creative reasoning — a good deal when you want agility.

⸻

When to Choose Each
✔ Use workflows when you need control, traceability, and compliance
✔ Use supervisors when you need flexibility, reasoning, and context switching
✔ Combine both for complex, enterprise-scale use cases — a workflow shell with a supervisor inside for adaptive decision-making

⸻

That’s what makes this next generation of agents so exciting.

They’re not just smart individually — they’re collaborative systems that can reason, act, and adapt in context.

Workflows give you structure.
Supervisors give you intelligence.
Together, they make enterprise AI truly agentic.

Why do most product operating model implementations fail?

It's usually not because of bad frameworks or unclear processes. Most companies nail the mechanics: they define roles, set up governance, and create beautiful slide decks outlining their new approach.

The success of a product operating model depends far more on change management than on pure product expertise. The hard part isn't defining the model, it’s getting humans to adopt it.

I've seen beautiful operating models die on slide decks because no one could navigate the politics. Meanwhile, I've watched simpler approaches thrive because someone knew how to build trust, align incentives, and convince leaders to think in outcomes instead of outputs.

The people who drive these transformations need to excel at communication, influence, and stakeholder management. They need to understand organizational psychology and win hearts and minds, not just write PRDs.

Product mechanics can be taught. But the ability to lead change across entrenched systems and power structures? That's what actually determines whether your operating model sticks or becomes another failed initiative.

Transformation is fundamentally a human process, not a technical one.

Are you hiring for frameworks, or for the ability to drive real change?

Build what people hire, not what they want.

Most founders make this mistake early on...
They build what makes sense to them.

Then they wonder why nobody buys.

I’ve been there too.

The problem isn’t the idea.
It’s that we focus on what people say they want instead of what they actually do.

Here’s what I learned (the hard way):

1️⃣ Stop building based on assumptions
↳ What people say and what they do are rarely the same.

2️⃣ Watch behavior, not surveys
↳ The truth is in the actions, not the answers.

3️⃣ Find the real “job to be done”
↳ Every purchase solves a problem or fills a need.

4️⃣ Build around that job
↳ Every feature, service, or deliverable should serve the real reason they hire you.

This shift changed everything for me.

McDonald’s discovered morning commuters were hiring milkshakes to stay full and keep their hands busy during boring drives...
Not for taste.

Netflix wasn’t about “movie selection.”
It was about helping people relax at home without hassle.

Uber didn’t make a better taxi.
They made getting from A to B easy and reliable.

Same lesson every time...
People don’t buy what you offer.
They buy why it fits into their life.

Ask better questions.
Watch what they actually do.
Then build for the job they’re hiring you to do.

The best business advice I ever got?
Ignore most business advice.

Sounds crazy, right?

But here's what nobody talks about...

Just because it worked for them doesn't mean it'll work for you.

I've watched founders burn out following someone else's playbook.

Copying strategies that don't fit their style.
Building businesses they hate because some influencer said "this is the way."

The entrepreneurship space is drowning in advice.

"Do this if you want to succeed"
"You have to hustle 24/7"
"Follow my blueprint exactly"

Every guru has the answer.
Every course has the formula.
Every post has the secret.

Everyone's selling certainty when the truth is messier:

⇢ The course says "scale to 7 figures fast." But you just want a stable 10K/month
⇢ LinkedIn gurus say "post daily or die." But you'd rather focus on doing great work
⇢ That morning routine that "changed everything"? Useless if you have kids waking up at random times
⇢ The "quit your job and go all in" advice? Reckless if you've got a mortgage and family to feed

So how do you know who to actually listen to?

Look for people who:

⇢ Teach principles, not just tactics
⇢ Share their failures, not just wins
⇢ Show you options, not just one path
⇢ Admit when they don't know something
⇢ Have done what you're trying to do (not just talked about it)

Skip anyone who:

⇢ Promises overnight success
⇢ Never mentions their struggles
⇢ Sells urgency over understanding
⇢ Makes you feel behind if you're not doing it their way

The real secret?

Take what serves you.
Leave what doesn't.
Build YOUR way.

Your business should fit your life.
Not the other way around.

This article is a good read. It speaks to some of the backstory and foolishness around the recent AWS incident. Back when the AI hype explosion happened and I was still at AWS I was astonished by how the structure of the business got torqued around, and how teams got demolished. One of the saddest things that happened is that senior management became dominated by folks that knew almost nothing about how AWS worked. There was an influx of ridiculous spreadsheet pushers tasked with realigning staffing. But they were being idiotic about it: the only metric they cared about when measuring a service was ROI. How much money the service brought in from customers. Teams whose ROI didn’t measure up were decimated. Every team I was working with ceased to exist. 

This occasionally made sense, but many of the services have little to no direct revenue. And yet they are critical to the operation of the system. The internal DNS is one of those systems. I have no specific knowledge of what happened to that team, but I’m quite confident that they took a huge headcount hit. This damages the ability to improve, the ability to reduce technical debt, and the ability to respond to operational issues.

The ROI analysis was disastrously shortsighted. These systems are complex interconnected structures. Unless the whole ecosystem is comprehended in total, bad decisions are made.

It’s an epic clusterfuck that has been hard to watch. I spent a lot of time trying to get these shortsighted idiots to understand, but made zero progress.

I Rejected 3,000 AI Startups In 3 Months (Here's What They All Got Wrong)

Every single one pitched AI as the hero. So few pitched the problem as the villain.

We're building "AI for X" or "Lovable for Y" was the opening line for an embarrassing number of them. 

Close to zero started with why the CEO's of their customers are up late stressed about the problem they're solving.

The bar for AI startups has shifted dramatically. Six months ago, having a wrapper around GPT-4 could get you a meeting. Today, that same pitch gets deleted in seconds. The firms writing $1-3m checks aren't looking for AI experiments anymore, they want real businesses solving real problems that happen to use AI.

Five years ago, a pitch deck could get you a term sheet for your seed and $1m ARR could get you a Series A. Today, you might need $500K growing 5-10X year over year to even consider a priced round.

What these pre-revenue founders are missing: AI is table stakes now. Every company uses AI. It's like pitching "we use cloud computing" in 2015. The question isn't whether you use AI - it's whether you understand your customer's pain deeply enough to build something they'll rip out of your hands.

Stop pitching the technology. Start pitching the transformation. Your customers don't care about your multi-modal multi-agent architecture. They care about solving problems, saving money, and transforming themselves and their companies. 

The more you talk about AI, the less fundable you become.

We Don’t Need More Targeting. We Need More Memory.

For the past decade, digital media has sold us a seductive story. The promise that with enough data, we could find the perfect customer at the perfect moment with the perfect message. It sounded like progress. We could eliminate waste, optimise every penny, and turn marketing into a pure science of precision.

But the evidence tells a quieter, more frustrating truth.

The more precisely we target, the smaller the world in which memory can form. The tighter we draw the circle, the fewer people share the same experience of the brand. Fame, as the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute’s research shows, doesn’t grow from narrow relevance. It grows from broad mental availability, from the feeling that everyone, everywhere, seems to know who you are.

Sharp and Romaniuk have made this point repeatedly. Growth comes not from loyal buyers but from light buyers, the vast majority who buy infrequently and unpredictably. You can’t target them efficiently because they don’t behave predictably. The only way to reach them is to reach broadly.

When we hyper-target, we invert that logic. We trade mental availability for mechanical efficiency. We become invisible to the many in order to be relevant to the few. It feels rational because the metrics reward precision. Higher CTRs, lower CPAs, cleaner graphs. But what looks efficient in a spreadsheet often erodes the long-term structure of memory that brands rely on.

Memory doesn’t form through perfect relevance alone. It forms through redundancy across context, through repeated, emotionally charged encounters that help the brain build fluency. Think of the Cadbury Gorilla. You didn’t remember it because it was targeted at you. You remembered it because everyone saw it, talked about it, couldn’t escape it. Because it was brilliant. That’s how memory scales, not through precision, but through ubiquity.

Targeting still has its place. When behaviour is observable, when timing truly matters, when you are closing the gap between intent and purchase, precision delivers real value. CRM, retargeting and search all thrive on relevance and recency. 

The danger is when precision becomes ideology, when marketers believe that targeting is strategy rather than a tactical tool within it.

The paradox is simple. As targeting becomes more efficient, we strip away the very inefficiency that builds memory. We optimise for individuals but lose the collective recognition that makes a brand matter. Attention is individual. Memory is social. And markets are built in the social layer, not the personal feed.

The marketers who build enduring brands understand this. The real game is reach, repetition and recognition. The goal isn’t to find the right person once. It’s to make more people think of you more often, in more buying situations, for more reasons.

In the end, it’s not the precision of our data that decides who wins. 

It’s the scale of what people remember.

We just dropped what we believe is the world's largest study of AI conversations - and it found that what you talk to AI about actually has a lot to do with what time it is.

Researchers across both the Microsoft AI Futures team and Data Science team analyzed 37.5M de-identified conversations from between January and September in Copilot and looked for patterns in not just what people talked about, but when. They found 3 different trends by day, time of day, and time of year - and 1 rock solid constant.

1. The one thing on users' minds around the clock: health. When we ranked what topics people were talking to Copilot about, for mobile users health was #1 no matter what day, month, or time it was. This is a huge responsibility for us, and one we're super focused on.

2. Day of the week: They did find some big swaps between weekday and weekend trending topics. Gaming had a two day spike every week on Saturday and Sunday, while most coding-related conversations happened between Monday and Friday.

3. Time of day: We've all had those existential musings in the middle of the night - and that shows up in the data, with philosophical conversations increasing in the early hours of the morning. On the other hand, we saw upticks in travel-focused conversations happening most often during commute hours, with people daydreaming about where they'd rather be.

4. Time of year: Seasonal differences also affected trending topics - for example, in February, relationship-related conversations spiked on Valentine's Day. And interestingly, "Personal growth and wellness" related conversations surged over the 2-3 days before with people seeming to focus inward.

Zooming out, what the data means to me is people are truly turning to Copilot as a companion. Not just a specific type of tool, but a sounding board and support across all these different moments that make up our lives - from existential overthinking at 2 am to when you're stuck on a certain video game level over the weekend.

Studying these patterns is about understanding what matters most to our users - and where we can help. What's important when using desktop vs. mobile? How should Copilot adapt to you throughout the week? What features would help support people in these day-to-day rhythms, what topics should be our biggest priorities? It's fascinating stuff, and this is how we make AI more useful, for more people, more often.

seed investing is really about finding 1-2 really STRONG reasons to say yes. early on, every start-up has a hundred reasons to say no - the product is janky, the team is incomplete, the market is too competitive, etc

instead, look past the obvious weaknesses every early start-up has to find the unique strengths - why will this team succeed vs everyone else, what sets them apart and gives them the "right to win"?

similarly for early-stage founders, i think it's a more successful strategy to focus on strength of strengths vs shoring up weaknesses. this applies equally to company building (double down on what's working), fundraising (showcase what's working), hiring talent (hire for spikes vs lack of weakness), and even personal development

there are some caveats - glaring weaknesses like a career-ending work habit, a broken product etc should absolutely be addressed. but in general i find cultivating strengths > fixing weakness

a mistake we see many founders make is tackling too large of a vision starting off. you hear the truism that good start-ups tackle large markets, and so you start building a Maximum Viable Product that solves multiple problems or replaces an entire incumbent (ex. "Figma killer")

but the larger the vision, the longer it takes to build, and the more time and money you burn before you can tell whether your product theory is correct

instead i'm a fan of the "right size problem for the right size team" approach - what's an appropriately scoped problem so that a team with your (limited) resources can execute a first-class effort? ex. Amazon's books-only marketplace or Facebook's college photo sharing feature

early on speed is the main advantage start-ups have over large companies - you move faster, ship faster, iterate faster. by going after smaller, bite-sized problems, you can nail them quickly, establish dominant market share, and then expand to adjacent use-cases. over time, Amazon became the everything marketplace, and Facebook became a social network for everyone - but in the beginning they focused on tightly scoped problems that a small team could execute to perfection

but wait - "won't I have trouble raising VC money if my initial market is too small"? for fundraising, you want to articulate the story crisply that your initial product wedge is only the tip of the spear, and there is a much larger vision you plan to tackle over time. you should have a believable roadmap for how to get there. once you do this, most investors should understand the strategy - and if they don't, they probably just aren't the right fit!

A CMO recently asked me a question I hear all too often: “How do I help my CEO and CFO understand we need to move beyond MQLs and short-term tactics?”

Here's what I said.

THE PROBLEM WITH YOUR CURRENT METRICS

MQLs measure activity, not buying intent. More MQLs don’t mean more active buying cycles. They focus on individual leads instead of buying groups. They create turf wars over “marketing-sourced” vs “sales-sourced” pipeline. 

Worse, the attribution model behind MQLs is a logical fallacy. Marketing is not a gumball machine, e.g., insert budget, get leads. It’s a complex, non-linear system where multiple stakeholders interact over unpredictable timeframes. A deal that closes today was influenced by brand awareness built over months, content consumed 8 months ago, sales conversations, competitive dynamics, and interactions across 6-16 buying committee members.

Trying to isolate marketing’s “contribution” is like determining which raindrop caused the flood.

WHAT TO REPLACE YOUR METRICS WITH

✅ Brand:
• Awareness, consideration, preference in target segments
• Excess Share of Voice (ESOV)
• Compare to industry benchmarks
• Measure over 6-12 month horizons

✅ Demand:
• Pipeline quality: coverage, deal velocity, win rates
• New and expansion
• Account and buying group level engagement (MQA, QBG)
• Investment $ / pipeline $ [note: investment, not cost!]

✅ Overall System:
• Net Revenue Retention
• CAC Payback Period
• Net Promoter Score

HOW TO GET THERE

Build credibility first. Fix obvious operational leaks in 30-60 days: lead response time, MQL quality, marketing-sales handoff friction. Quick wins buy permission for bigger changes.

Reframe the risk. Show what status quo is costing: CAC rising, win rates declining, deals lost to competitors with stronger brands. Make inaction feel riskier than change.

Build your coalition. Form a cross-functional “GTM Evolution Council” with Sales, CS, RevOps. When the CFO hears multiple department heads say “our model is broken,” it becomes a business problem, not a marketing complaint.

(Let me know in the comments if you want more tips!)

THE RESULTS WHEN YOU GET THIS RIGHT

One B2B SaaS company moved from attribution battles to shared team metrics. Results after one year: pipeline up 32%, win rate increased from 29% to 36%, CAC down 18%.

Another B2B SaaS company had 11% brand awareness versus competitors at 35-40%. So they shifted from 70/30 demand/brand to 60/40, and after four quarters awareness reached 28%, win rate jumped from 23% to 31%, and CAC dropped from $18K to $14.5K.

THE JOB TO BE DONE

Your job isn’t to prove marketing “sourced” a specific percentage of pipeline. Your job is to improve the performance of the entire revenue system.

CFOs don’t need perfect attribution. They need confidence that marketing is improving system performance. Give them that, and you'll get the support you need.

What’s worked for you in getting buy-in for the new playbook and better metrics?

One of the greatest investors of all time just dropped an 18-page memo on AI, and it might be the most honest thing anyone has said about this industry 😳

Howard Marks, the man who has called more bubbles, cycles, and turning points than almost anyone alive, just asked a simple question:

Is AI a bubble?

And his answer is not what Silicon Valley wants to hear.

Marks says AI might truly be one of the most important technological shifts in human history…
and yet the excitement around it might already have crossed into classic bubble psychology.

And he knows a thing or two about bubbles - he’s lived through them, studied them, and warned about them at exactly the right moments.

What strikes him isn’t the technology itself, but the behavior around it.

→ Investors piling into companies with no product.
→ Founders raising $1-2B seed rounds on vibes.
→ Circular deals between hyperscalers.
→ Debt being used to finance massive AI infrastructure before profits exist.
→ And valuations anchored more in imagination than evidence.

But here’s the twist that only someone like Marks would articulate:

Some bubbles are necessary.

Not the destructive financial kind, but the “inflection bubbles” that accelerate technological revolutions.

↳ Railroads had one.
↳ Electricity had one.
↳ The internet had one.
↳ And AI, he argues, will almost certainly have one too.

Because when the world believes a technology is inevitable, money floods in.

Most of it gets burned. A tiny part builds the future.

The tragedy is that the technology wins. The investors don’t.

And yet Marks refuses to give a simplistic verdict.

He doesn’t say “run.”
He doesn’t say “go all in.”

He says something far more grounded:

“If this doesn’t turn into a bubble, it’ll be the first time in history a world-changing technology didn’t create one.”

So what do you do? 🤔

You stay in the game because AI is real.
But you stay sane because the hype is also very, very real.

→ You don’t try to pick the next NVIDIA.
→ You don’t assume today’s leaders will stay leaders.
→ You don’t confuse a technological revolution with guaranteed investment returns.

You hold two truths at the same time:

AI could be the opportunity of the century.
AI could also be a bubble.

Both can be true.

And then he ends with the part nobody wants to discuss:

AI may reshape markets, but it will reshape society even more: through job losses, productivity shocks, and deep social tension.

When the guy who has seen every cycle for 50 years says AI is both inevitable and dangerous, you pay attention.

My takeaway is this:

AI isn’t just another tech wave.

It’s the first revolution where the technology might thrive even if the investors don’t.

And the only winning strategy is to be bold enough to participate, and wise enough to stay humble.

Wild: Duolingo announced it was going “AI-first” just over 6 months ago. Since then its stock is down 64% 😳

Duolingo just discovered the hard way that AI can scale a product, but it can’t save a soul.

In April, they went “AI-first” 🤖

→ Fired human contractors.
→ Let AI write lessons.
→ Pushed out 50 new courses overnight.

And for a moment, Wall Street loved it.

- Stock pumped to $540.
- Efficiency! Margins! Infinite scaling!

Then reality hit.

Users opened the app… and got sentences like:

“The bears are drinking beer at the party.”
“The cows cleaned the kitchen yesterday.”
“The cats went to the market today.”

It felt less like learning Spanish and more like babysitting a hallucinating chatbot.

People with 500-day, 800-day, and even 1,000-day streaks quit overnight.

Competitors with human-curated content saw a surge.

But here comes the real story nobody’s talking about.

AI didn’t kill Duolingo. Duolingo killed Duolingo’s trust.

Language is deeply human.

Idioms, tone, context, culture - that’s where humans shine, and LLMs fail.

Yes, AI can accelerate content.

But when it replaces the very thing users value most, you don’t get efficiency. You get “enshittification.”

And once trust cracks, no streak counter can fix it.

This is the lesson every consumer company should remember:

AI is an amplifier.

If your product is good, AI makes it great.
If your product is fragile, AI makes it crumble faster.

Turns out, Duolingo just accidentally shipped a masterclass in what NOT to do with AI.

The most dangerous phrase in product management might be "What problem are we solving?"

Hear me out.

For years, we've been told to "fall in love with the problem, not the solution." It's become gospel in product circles—from the Stanford d.school to IDEO to Lean Startup. And it's saved countless teams from building beautiful solutions nobody wants.

But after 30+ years in product, I've seen how this framing inadvertently limits our thinking: Not everything is a problem.

When we only look for problems to solve, we miss opportunities to delight. We overlook chances to create entirely new value. We get stuck in a reactive mindset—fixing what's broken rather than imagining what's possible.

Teresa Torres opened my eyes to this reframing: from Problems to Opportunities. It's not semantic gymnastics. It fundamentally changes how teams approach their work.

This shift from "What's broken?" to "What's possible?" unlocks divergent thinking. It connects strategy to execution. It's why Opportunities became the crucial bridge between Objectives and Solutions in the Decision Stack mental model.

The ancient wisdom of Lao Tzu reminds us: "You can mould clay into a vessel; yet, it is its emptiness that makes it useful." We've been so focused on the problems (the clay) that we've forgotten about the possibilities (the emptiness).

Ready to move from problem-solving to opportunity-capturing? I explore this shift in depth in my latest article - link in the comments!

What opportunities are your team missing by only looking for problems?

A big VC told me this week that one of the top problems they see is this:

Every time a company hires a new CTO, the first thing that new CTO wants to do is rewrite all the software.

My first reaction was, then you’re not hiring CTOs.
You’re hiring senior engineers and architects who happened to get the title.

Because most true CTOs operate at a higher level. They care about customers, outcomes, and leverage, not showing off their preferred tech stack.

But here’s the twist.

Startup CTOs should still be hands-on. They should be close to the code and architecture.

Which means this:
If you hire a startup CTO who isn’t comfortable with your existing stack, you’re basically signing up for a rewrite. Not because the product needs it, but because they do.

And that’s a huge waste of time, money, and momentum.

Code exists to do a job.
The only real question is: Does it do that job?

If the answer is yes, rewriting is ego, not strategy.

The VC told me this problem got so bad at their venture studio that they stopped offering engineering services entirely. Every new CTO wanted to throw away the work and start over. 🤦🏼‍♂️

I’ve seen the same issue for years.

A great startup CTO is rare: product-minded, architecture-strong, and fluent in your stack. 

It's always hard to hire a startup CTO. Don't hire one that wants to start over.

Every week I meet three new LPs (investors in funds). Every week. Even when I am not fundraising. And... I never ever sell 20VC to them. 

Why? The single biggest reason VCs struggle with fundraising is they do not build relationships in between fundraises. 

They treat it like an on/off switch. This is wrong. You have to continuously build relationships and trust with LPs over time. 

I never sell 20VC to LPs. I understand what is important to them, what they like, what they do not like, how they think about building a venture portfolio. I then advise them. I try to provide as much value to them as possible, with nothing in return. 

I always get their Whatsapp at the end. Relationships are built, deepened and sustained when you get off email and onto Whatsapp. 

I also ask them, what three other LPs do you most respect? If I do not know them, I ask for an intro? Every week, I get 6-9 new LP intros from the LPs I meet. 

Lines not dots. Build relationships over time. Play the long game.

In late 1920s, the film industry underwent a shift that seemed, at first, almost impossible.

When sound entered cinema, many feared it would diminish the craft. Silent film actors worried about their careers. Directors resisted the loss of a purely visual language. Theaters needed new equipment. The cost and logistics were daunting. 

The transition from silent films to sound was disruptive, controversial, and technically messy. It also ended up being one of the great creative unlocks in modern history, expanding what stories could be told and who could tell them. It expanded creativity, formats, and the entire economic footprint of the industry.

Sound didn’t replace visual storytelling. It layered onto it, opening the door to musicals, radio-film crossovers, new genres, new stars, and entirely new business models. It was a Cambrian moment: not a substitution, but an explosion of possibility.

Today’s AI moment feels similar in that it has the potential to broaden not shrink the creative universe and associated economics. Tools like ChatGPT and Sora can help artists iterate faster and imagine more freely, while studios shape clear rules around rights, safety, and compensation. If we get that balance right, we might see a new wave of formats, characters, and creative opportunities that strengthen the entire industry. I’m excited about today’s step into the future.

AI isn't solving the build trap. It's making it worse… It’s more important now than ever to have strategic Product Managers.

This weekend at Product Weekend NYC, I discussed how to escape the “AI Build Trap" with a group of senior product leaders and the conversations have been eye-opening. It's crazy how the same antipatterns and challenges I highlighted in 2018 in Escaping the Build Trap are reemerging with a new coating and aggravated by the pace at which companies can now produce useless software.

Back then, companies were stuck building features without understanding customer problems. Today they're doing the exact same thing, just faster and with shinier AI labels.

Teams remain focused on outputs rather than outcomes. AI gives them superpowers to build the wrong things at scale. They're automating poor processes and slapping "AI-powered" on solutions that solve non-existent problems rather than taking time to validate assumptions and understand real user needs.

The build trap has always been about mindset, not technology. If you haven't fixed your strategic thinking, AI will amplify your existing problems exponentially.

In the 1920s, there was a toothpaste called Doramad. Its gimmick was that it was made with real thorium-232. Even in the 20s, there were nuclear scientists who knew exactly how dangerous a radioactive toothpaste was, and they were trying their hardest not to lose it every time they saw an ad for it.

This is what it feels like to be an engineer who understands LLM-based AIs right now. I'm constantly warning people not to let the AI agent have write access to production databases, or not to introduce data that would cause model collapse, or ESPECIALLY not submit unedited legal documents written by ChatGPT.

GenAI is a tool that can do amazing things, but you have to know what its limitations are to see the opportunities.

I sold my first startup for $35m+ after 8 years of bootstrapping.
The day the wire hit, I felt…nothing.

For years, I thought exit = happiness.
That once I sold, all the stress, late nights, and endless “founder anxiety” would finally disappear.

Spoiler: it didn’t.

What nobody tells you is that when the money lands… your purpose leaves.
The thing that gave you identity; your team, your customers, your fight - it’s gone overnight.

You go from mission mode to maintenance mode in a single email.

And that emotional hangover? It’s real.
After the exit, I took time off. 

But I couldn’t shake this feeling: I missed building.

So when I started my second company trumpet 🎺, this time VC-backed. I made a promise to myself: Never build for the exit again.

Build for impact, velocity, and obsession.

Because the truth is, the real win isn’t freedom from work, it’s freedom in your work.

Most founders don’t really want to sell, they just want to stop feeling owned by their own company.

Freedom isn’t selling.
It’s building again but on your own terms.

Having invested in 50 startups now, one pattern I can't ignore is how often founders trip over the same avoidable landmines.

It's rarely about a bad idea. It's almost always about execution blind spots that become fatal.

Here are my blunt truths I keep seeing:

- Outsourcing sales too soon: You are the sales engine until you truly hit product-market fit. Delegating before you deeply understand the buyer and sales motion means you can't teach anyone else to do it effectively.

- Building in a vacuum: Perfect decks and elaborate roadmaps mean nothing without customer validation. Ship ugly, get feedback, iterate. Fast. Your market doesn't care about your internal perfectionism.

- Avoiding tough conversations: Underperformance, bad hires, necessary pivots - delaying these is a death sentence. Rip the band-aid off. Quickly. The cost of inaction is always higher.

- Talking features, not problems: Buyers don't care about your tech. They care about their pain. If you can't articulate the exact problem you solve and how you make their life easier, you're just noise.

- Chasing vanity metrics: Downloads, sign-ups, PR mentions are nice. Revenue cures all. Focus on what puts cash in the bank. Everything else is a distraction from sustainable growth.

The patterns are clear. 

Do your best not to fall into these traps, founders.

I want to give every startup founder permission to hire candidates based off vibes. But I'm going to give you the vocabulary to feel better about it.

Leaders resist "vibes hiring" because it sounds lazy. Like you’re not doing due diligence. People don’t love feelings-based decisions and revert to only what the evidence says. But recruiting is a human game and humans have feelings. That’s why you must incorporate how someone makes you feel in the decision.

So, what does this mean in practice with candidates? It means that you pay attention to the intangible. Do you get energy from the conversations? Do they inspire you with confidence and peace of mind? Do you feel excitement not anxiety when you see their caller ID on your phone? Do you feel conversational chemistry? Do you naturally gravitate towards and want to spend more time with them? Good news - at a startup, you’re basically living with them.

With these hires, everything just feels right. Decisions are made easily, conflict and arguments resolve in seconds not hours, and the building blocks of trust stack on top of each other. You have people like this on your team right now. You know who they are.

Interviewing is an information gathering exercise, and how a candidate makes you feel (The Vibes) is as important as their take home assessment, behavioral interview and specific competencies. 

Even if someone is perfect on paper, you can’t get things done at the quality and speed a startup requires if you keep having those little moments of friction that fracture the trust and communication needed to excel.

You are about to spend your life building with them. You need to enjoy the vibes!

A VC told me competing with PowerPoint was impossible and hung up. He was right about one thing: you don't beat a 40-year-old incumbent at its own game.

There's a graveyard of presentation tools that tried to be 10x better. Same canvas, same mental model, slightly nicer templates. They all died. Being 10x better at one thing is no match for being pre-installed in every company in the world.

So how do you actually compete? Here's the framework we used to build Gamma:

1. Map their game board

Write down how the incumbent wins today. Not feature gaps - structural advantages:

- Distribution (bundles, default installs)
- File formats others must be compatible with
- The mental model (what is the "unit of work"?)
- What they're afraid to cannibalize

For PowerPoint: Office bundle, .pptx as universal format, "slide" as the atom of work.

2. Find the untouchable constraints

Ask: if they tried to copy a radically better version of my product, what would they have to give up?

Could they abandon their file format? Unbundle the product? Switch the unit of work without retraining millions of users?

If the answer is "yes" to all of those, your idea is too close. You're still playing their game.

3. Use a tech shift to change the primitives

Every generational shift - cloud, mobile, AI - gives you permission to change the building blocks. But combine the tech shift with a primitive shift.

When Figma went browser-based and multiplayer, they chose constraints Photoshop couldn't adopt without blowing up its file format and business model.

That's the pattern: pick constraints that make you worse at old tasks to be obviously better at new ones.

For us: AI to structure card-based narratives (not mindlessly auto-generate slides), mobile-responsive by default, progressive disclosure so the same artifact works for reading and presenting.

One warning: The new trap is building "AI-native" and letting the model do everything. You've outsourced your differentiation. Impressive demo, forgettable product. AI should accelerate your primitives, not replace them.

If you're competing with an incumbent, ask one question:

In what way could your product be so different that they can't follow without disrupting themselves?

If your answer is "better design" or "we use AI"...

You're focused on being better.

As Kevin Kelly says, "Don't be the best. Be the only."

The AI Scaling Laws — Your Kung Fu Is Strong! 🥋
 
Bruce Lee once said:
“Adapt what is useful, discard what is not, add what is uniquely your own.”
That is exactly what modern AI teaches us.
 
There is weak AI, and there is strong AI.
If you want your Kung Fu to be competitive, remember this:
 
🔥 AI strength grows through three forces:
 
1️⃣ Neural Network Architecture
We have entered the era of trillion-parameter models — and bigger is not better anymore. One of the latest advances: liquid neural networks, which dynamically adjust the parameter count and utilize only what makes sense for the task at hand.
 
2️⃣ Size and Quality of the Training Dataset
Raw internet scraping (even with GPTs) is no longer enough. Nor is dumping uncurated ERP data into an AI pipeline. Precision and Fit-for-Purpose come from domain-rich, RAG-augmented, audited and unbiased data — whether public or enterprise.
 
3️⃣ Compute Power (The Trump Card♟️)
Here is one truth many still underestimate: The power of compute is the final deciding force in the race towards AGI, enabling systems to outperform their competitors even when architectures and data are similar.
 
This is why OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, or xAI are building gigawatt-scale data centers, GPU clusters, liquid-cooled server racks, and massive power infrastructure — instead of pursuing ever-larger models.

Telecommunications is dead

Telecommunications was built to connect people. That idea shaped a trillion-dollar industry. 

But the traffic tells a different story. Human communication is now a minority workload. Calls and messages sit in the single digits of mobile load, while video dominates at 76%. Imperva confirms the real shift. More than 50% of global traffic now comes from machines, not humans. Automated feeds, API calls, crawlers, sync loops, and background inference. The network carries software-to-software communication.

At the same time, content no longer needs to travel. A 2 KB prompt can generate a 200 MB video through local inference. Companies already do this at scale. TikTok recomposes feeds in real time. Meta renders scenes on demand. NVIDIA’s edge platforms deliver petaflop compute at radio sites. The heavy work moves from transport to computation.

Add AI agents, and the picture is clear. Agents operate with tiny payloads but require sub-10 ms inference. Browsing shrinks. Execution grows. Compute moves outward.

This does not kill telecom. It changes its purpose. 

The network stops delivering information. It starts producing it, the physics shift from distance to inference.

Let's make the CMO be the CMO again. 

Your CMO should be the best storyteller at your company. 

Full stop.

The CMO is not the CFO (crazy, right?)

The CMO should be company's best storyteller.
