But if you don't nail the story to your market, a clear POV, and true differentiation -- none of the other stuff will matter.

And if the CMO can't get the company (internal team) excited and rallied around the company mission, how will you ever be able to get strangers (customers) onboard?

The most valuable asset in any company is its story. And the CMO must be the chief custodian of that story. 

Not a bean counter. 

Not a data wrangler. 

A storyteller.

(my opinion)

A CMO who cannot craft a compelling story - who cannot articulate a clear point of view or highlight what truly sets the company apart - will find that all the data in the world won’t move the needle.

PS. This is more important now than ever. In a world of AI slop and sameness and customers who don’t actually believe your claims in marketing - you need a strong POV and great storytelling. Led by a human.

Charge more if you want better clients.

Charge more.
For better clients.

It's counterintuitive. I know.
You're afraid if you raise your rates, they will run.

Some will.
Some won't.

And that's okay.

Why would you want to risk this?
It's because… Pricing is Positioning.

We associate certain intangible ideas with how high or low something is priced relative to it's competitors.

Seth Godin writes in "All Marketers Are Liars", that we have an implicit bias. We determine the taste of the wine before we drink it based on preconceived ideas of the wine—the price, the label, the vintage, etc…

We, as consumers, are complicit in the lie.
We want to be lied to.

This is true for: cars, watches, clothes, even healthcare.
Basically, everything.

So, if you want better clients,
charge more.

They will assume you are better, more qualified, give better customer service, and the work becomes more desirable.

What's been your experience when you discount your prices?
What about when you raise your prices?

I see so many founders today running Zombie companies. 

The product hasn't really worked. The brand promise hasn't delivered in any meaningful way. Innovation seems tired. 

But it's not a full-blown failure. Not the kinds where money runs out quickly and nothing has worked. Where a painful but quick shut-down is the only option. 

It's quite the opposite - Chugging along aimlessly, some growth here, some store there. 

Just enough to keep founder and team 'kinda interested'. Investors position company as 'Decent, let's see' in next fund pitch decks - far away from winner, but not a write-off yet. 

It's a horrible place to be. 

Founder is possibly in delusional mode - reading all sorts of quotes on compounding and 'staying power' - appearing on podcasts trying to explain why their company has been the Nexy Big Thing for 10 years now. Feels like they're also not convinced with own narrative but have no choice at all.

Goalposts keep shifting conveniently. Growth not happening? Lets get profitable. Not profitable? Let's pivot. Pivot not working? Let's say 'we are early, market will move'. Always changing goalposts, distracted vision and increasingly defensive founder. 

If the inputs in the business are as bad as the outcomes - someone has to pull the plug. It's a massive disservice to all stakeholders to continue this facade. 

And it's rarely the founder who can do it. They're too vested, too blind. 

The objectivity burden is unfortunately external.

Everyone talks about the upside of joining fast growing companies -> 

- high empowerment, small teams
- agility of decisions, no bureaucracy
- exciting day everyday
- insane wealth creation opportunity via early equity/esops
- work directly with founders 

People believe the only risk is mortality. Which is there but not as much. 

The REAL risk of joining fast-growing young companies is, well, they're fast growing. 

And if the organisation is growing faster than you, then the slippery slope to irrelevance is quick, undignified and confidence-killing. 

I've seen so many situations now across so many companies for early employees/even cofounders where 

- founder recruits someone 'above' because you aren't stepping up 
- your role becomes smaller 
- decisions start happening in rooms without you

And people characterise this as favouritism or bad luck or politics or whatever. 

Rarely true. Because founders are by alignment of incentive, company-first. 

The uncomfortable truth is one joined a fast growing company and just didn't keep up.

80% of CEOs don't trust their CMOs (McKinsey). 34% of Fortune 500 companies eliminated the role entirely (Spencer Stuart).

I've been on both sides of this relationship: CMO at Marketo and Demandbase, CEO at Engagio and my current startup. Having sat in both chairs gives me perspective on why this disconnect exists.

THE CMO PERSPECTIVE
You're drowning in tactical work while being asked to do more with less. Marketing budgets fell 15% (Gartner), yet the pressure to deliver immediate results keeps intensifying.

Everyone has an opinion about your work. The CEO wants leads. The CFO questions every dollar. Sales complains about lead quality. You're managing fire drills while trying to build long-term brand equity that takes quarters to show results.

THE CEO PERSPECTIVE
You can't connect marketing spend to revenue. When you ask "what's the ROI on this campaign?" you get complex explanations about attribution models and conversion rates.

It's unclear how all the marketing activity impacts the business. You see a team of 15 people running webinars, sending emails, posting on social media — but you can't trace a direct line from that work to closed deals. 

Marketing feels like the only department that can't give you straight answers about what's working.

TURNS OUT, BOTH PERSPECTIVES ARE RIGHT
The disconnect results from the fact that the system changed, but our mental models didn't keep up.

Marketing used to work like a gumball machine: budget in, MQLs out. CEOs understood that model. It was measurable, predictable, linear.

But B2B buying completely changed. With 6-10 people in every buying committee (Gartner), 70% of the journey happening anonymously, and 80% of buyers choosing vendors before ever talking to sales (6sense), the gumball machine is broken.

Brand, positioning, and market perception now drive revenue more than any single campaign. But these operate on different timeframes and defy the clean attribution CEOs want.

THE PATH FORWARD
CEOs need to understand marketing the same way they understand finance or sales. Not become experts, but grasp how it actually works now. Recognize that the gumball machine model is dead. Product market fit and brand are what creates the conditions that make revenue possible.

CMOs need to speak the language of business outcomes the CEO tracks — pipeline efficiency, CAC trends, competitive win rates, deal velocity — while also educating leadership on why modern B2B buying makes short-term measurement meaningless. The complexity isn't an excuse. It's the reality of how buying committees make decisions across months-long journeys with multiple anonymous touchpoints.

Mutual understanding starts with both sides acknowledging that marketing isn't what it used to be. And that's not anyone's fault.

What's been your experience bridging this gap? Where do you see the biggest misalignment between marketing and the C-suite?

From a dorm room search engine to the world’s digital brain, Alphabet's (Google's) journey is a masterclass in strategic evolution.

Vibe coding is bullshit and every team I met that tried it ended up rebuilding everything.


Okay, okay - maybe I'm being dramatic. Not EVERY vibe coding attempt ends in disaster.

But the data shows a pattern that's hard to ignore:
AI coding traffic dropped 76% in 12 weeks. 
Most platforms saw 60% to 80% churn. 
Over 57% of teams hit the complexity wall and could not ship.

Here's what the data is actually showing:
This isn't an AI failure. It's mindset failure.

The teams churning? 
They came in looking for a shortcut. 
They thought AI would do the work FOR them.

And yes, AI gets them 80% there - a working demo, something they can show.
But then they hit the last 20%. The part that requires actual thinking.
And they're stuck.

Because they never saw AI as a tool to amplify their work. 
They saw it as a replacement for doing work.

The teams succeeding with AI coding? Completely different approach.
They're not asking AI to replace their judgment. 
They're using it to execute faster on ideas they already understand.

They know what they're building. 
They know what good looks like. 
They use AI to get there faster.

For them, AI becomes leverage. Not a replacement.

The real lesson here - AI isn't a skill equalizer. It's a capability amplifier.

First-wave thinking was: "AI will do it for me"
Second-wave reality: "AI helps me do it better"

The vibe coding collapse isn't showing AI's limits.
It's proving that in the AI era, your actual understanding matters more than ever.

The HUGE problem with the FIRE movement.

Jenna has $2.3M saved. She's 34. Last week, she sobbed herself to sleep because she wanted a $500 dress but couldn't pull the trigger.

This is what extreme frugality does to your brain.

FIRE (Financial Independence, Retire Early) has done something incredible. It has convinced people to save 50%+ of their income while most Americans can't save 5%.

But I keep hearing these FIRE "success stories" and thinking... is this really success?

Here's what the FIRE movement gets wrong:
1/ Money is meant to be used, not worshipped.

I see people with millions who guilt themselves over a latte. They've trained themselves so hard to save, they literally forgot how to live. It's like training for a marathon by holding your breath. Sure, you built discipline, but you missed the point entirely.

2/ Toxic competition is real.

Jump on any FIRE forum. It's a pissing contest of who can live on less. "I spent $18k last year!" "That's nothing, I lived on $12k!"

3/ The hamster wheel never stops.

Most folks think happiness lives in their magic "FI number." Hit $1.2M and boom. Fulfilled! Except that's not how humans work. The goalpost just moves. Now it's $1.5M. Then $2M. The hamster wheel never stops, it just changes colors.

I'd rather work an extra decade doing something meaningful than spend 40 years afraid to order guac.

As a venture capitalist, three sayings have shaped the way I look at my work. They helped to give me the stomach for it, teaching me to take investing very seriously on the one hand, while also holding it lightly enough to take the risks required to back outlier companies. 

“Do not be a surgeon who cannot see blood.”
“Whatever correction the market brings, fear cannot have a seat at the table.”
“We are not saving lives here.”

Now, they are utterly failing me and our entire industry.

Two main observations from my SF trip: 1) founding a company is fame now and 2) founders are getting younger and younger. Silicon Valley is turning into Hollywood and creating its very own Mickey Mouse Club in the process: an industrialised pipeline of teen founders.

1) Silicon Valley is turning into Hollywood: 
In a market that is as competitive as it has ever been, founders need to differentiate. Using consumer tactics, AI startups reach tens of millions in revenue over the course of months. Builders are becoming famous in the process. Many product launches now include Hollywood-level launch videos. Influential VCs are advising their founders to become Twitter-famous. PR manifestos herald the age of the "going direct”, making communication the founder’s job.

2) Founders are becoming younger and younger. The median age of founders in YC’s most recent batch was 24, down from 31 just a few years ago. This has a few reasons: 

1) AI gives young folks superpowers and radically brings down the barriers of building a product. 12-year olds are now vibe coding and use their unfiltered imagination to build apps and games.
2) Second, starting a company is now a desired career path (which is AWESOME).
3) VCs are pushing earlier and earlier to get access to talent and build ownership at digestible prices. They are now scouting talent at the best high schools, maths olympiads and science competitions, often with the goal of convincing gifted teens not to study at university, but to start a venture-backed company instead.
4)Young founders worry that AI erodes the value of their intelligence. They are (half) joking that is the last window to escape the permanent underclass, i.e. the last time to build up wealth before their worth will be eroded by artificial general intelligence. 

Hollywood first invented a format for the industrialisation of teen talent: the Mickey Mouse Club. The MMC was a children’s TV show that made the career of countless Hollywood legends, including Britney Spears, Christina Aguilera, and Justin Timberlake. However, the mental toll that child stardom took on the stars was immense and famously ended in meltdowns, drug addiction and shaved heads. 

In the fierce race for returns, the superpowers and pressures that AI is bringing upon us, and an increasing search for fame, we are industrialising entrepreneurial teen talent in the same way Hollywood industrialised teen performers. In the end, Silicon Valley will likely gain new breakout companies. And if entrepreneurship is something that enables teens to do more of what they enjoy, great! The world certainly needs more incredible entrepreneurs. But the venture capital machine is unforgiving. It aims for a narrow set of excellence. It copiously rewards greatness and swiftly moves on from its failures. It will relentlessly and carelessly forget about most of us, so it’s up to us to make our young years worth remembering.

Buyers are showing up to sales calls more informed than ever. That means sellers need to show up more prepared than ever.

In the past, buyers would reach out to sales when they still knew very little about a product. They wanted to learn more about the features, pricing, and how it compares to competitors – and they expected the salesperson to provide that information.

Today, buyers are gathering all that information (and more) long before they talk to sales. 

They’re reading review sites like G2.
They’re scrolling communities like Reddit.
They’re watching product walkthroughs on YouTube.
Most significantly, they’re doing deep research with LLMs like ChatGPT, asking questions like “Is Product A or Product B better for my business?”

Now, when a buyer gets on a call with sales, they expect more than basic information. Instead, they're looking for:

Detailed examples of how other companies in their industry are using the product.
Custom demos that show how the product works in their specific use case.
Clear plans for how the product will be implemented and adopted.

Here’s the good news. Just as buyers use AI to learn more about products, salespeople can use it to learn more about prospects. If I were in sales again, I would:

1. Use an AI assistant to do advanced research about your prospects before every call.
2. Use AI to find the best examples of similar companies seeing success with your product.
3. Build bespoke demos that highlight the most relevant features.

Buyers today are more informed than ever. The best sellers I know are more prepared than ever. The result? More productive conversations, deeper connections and higher trust.

The anatomy of a sales call has changed dramatically. 

Last week, I shadowed some of HubSpot’s top reps and what struck me was how differently the best sellers work today. They’re using AI at every stage: before, during, and after the call. And the results are real.

The brain: before the call.

AI does the heavy research — scanning 10Ks, news, emails, and past calls to surface the insights that matter most. Tools like Breeze Assistant can prep a full company overview in seconds. According to our State of Sales Report, 74% of sellers say buyers are showing up to calls more informed than ever before. Salespeople need to be just as ready.

The heart: during the call.

AI notetakers capture everything: next steps, budget mentions, open questions, so reps can focus on listening, not typing or scribbling notes on the side. 
Also, AI assistants surface the right case study or testimonial in real time, making every answer sharper and every example more relevant. That means as a sales rep you are more engaged and relevant. 

The muscle: after the call.

AI follows through fast. It drafts personalized follow-up emails in your own voice, outlines next steps, and flags what needs attention. More time with customers and less time writing emails. 

The result: sellers who prepare better, connect deeper, and close faster. The anatomy of a great sales call used to be manual effort and hustle. Now, it’s human connection powered by intelligence.

I spoke with a first-time founder earlier today who told me something I think every founder has felt at some point.

He said, “No matter how much I work, I constantly feel guilty for not working more.”

He knows everything rides on his shoulders: every decision, every mistake, every bit of progress. And even when he tries to rest, he can’t shake that feeling that he should be doing more.

He asked if I’ve ever felt that way. I laughed and said, “Have I ever not felt that way?”

That guilt is almost built into being a founder. You care so deeply that it feels wrong to step away, even for a moment.

What I told him was this: taking care of yourself is part of the job. You can push harder, sleep less, and squeeze in another hour every night, but if it’s not sustainable, eventually you’ll break, and when you do, the business will likely break with you.

Your well-being isn’t a side quest. It’s a core responsibility.

That also means knowing your limits and setting real boundaries. Deciding when to stop. Closing the laptop without guilt. Remembering that rest isn’t the opposite of work, it’s what keeps you capable of doing great work over the long run.

Microsoft released a new wave of Copilot features earlier this week. One of them - around more social ways of working with AI - pulled on a thread that’s been sitting at the back of my mind for a while. Because most of us are using AI alone.

We prompt alone. We explore alone. We refine alone. Even inside agencies, where the real creative spark has always come from people thinking together - volleying, provoking, nudging ideas into bigger, stranger shapes - our workflows have quietly shifted into solitary ones.

Someone disappears to “go do something with AI” and reappears later with an output the rest of the room reacts to. And something subtle, but important, is lost there.

Because the value of an agency has never been the output alone or access to individual genius. It’s the alchemy of the collective. The unpredictable, atmospheric, slightly feral electricity of shared imagination. The moment where one half-sentence from someone across the room unlocks the whole idea. That atmosphere is the product.

AI, as it stands, interrupts that - not intentionally, but structurally. The tools optimise for individual speed, not collective meaning-making. So the move toward social AI isn’t a feature update. It’s a chance to rebuild the conditions creativity depends on.

We need tools and workflows that allow us to think in the same imaginative field again. Not to replace the room, but to resource it.

Things like:

Brain-Swarming - where AI live-maps ideas across a group, surfacing where we’re converging and where the white space is. AI as the cartographer of imagination, not its source.

Emergent Rooms: where AI acts as a facilitator - adjusting tempo, constraints, provocations - shaping the conditions for creativity rather than generating the work itself. Atmosphere as a creative tool again.

Threaded Ideation: where the evolution of an idea is tracked and held across people, sessions, and time - the spark doesn’t get lost just because the meeting ended. Continuity becomes a core feature.

None of these are fully formed, but if we don’t start shaping for this now, we’ll wake up to a studio full of people working alone, together - busy, efficient, and completely disconnected from the alchemy that makes the work good.

So for me, the question ahead isn’t: What can I make with AI?
It’s: What becomes possible when we make with AI, together?

That’s where the magic is.
And that is the part we cannot afford to lose.

Most AI systems still rely on solid foundations from classic software + reliability engineering.

In the real infra stack, you have secure endpoints, private subnets, monitoring, identity, data stores…
It’s all serious system design.

Agentic AI isn’t a “top layer” sitting above all this.
An agent is really a set of components working together:

--- LLMs for reasoning
--- MCP (or similar protocols) for tool coordination
--- Tools and integrations for real actions
--- Data layers for context
--- An environment that defines what it can touch

They form a system, and the agent operates within it - not above it.

This is where things get interesting for teams.

Most companies don’t want to (or can’t) build the entire tooling and integration stack from scratch.
They need a way for agents to actually update systems, move data, send alerts, or trigger actions.

That’s where platforms like Zapier become surprisingly powerful.
Not as part of the agent, and not as infrastructure, but as the action layer an agent can call when something needs to be done.

The agent decides what should happen.
Zapier helps make sure it actually happens across the tools you already use.

💡For example:
An agent can read a support message, classify it, detect urgency…
But the escalation, logging, notifications, and cross-tool updates?
That runs through integrations.

Building AI agents = 5% AI + 95% software engineering
Not exactly accurate, but you get the point.

We don’t really build AI agents - we architect systems, and let the AI sit inside them.

This diagram from Microsoft Azure shows what real, enterprise-ready AI infrastructure looks like.

It’s software architecture, system design, and reliability engineering:

✅ Identity management
✅ Document filtering (ACLs, redaction, PII masking)
✅ Schema mapping
✅ Human-in-the-loop escalation
✅ Infra that scales across both vector + SQL

Think of AI agents like APIs that reason, not magic.
They still need:

- Fine-grained access control
- Separation of structured vs unstructured knowledge
- Observability, fallback routing, and data traceability
- Flows connecting document pipelines with model orchestration

We’re starting to get a clearer sign of how vast the surface area of context engineering is going to be.

To build AI agents, in theory, it should be as simple as having a super powerful model, giving it a set of tools, having a really good system prompt, and giving it access to data. Maybe at some point it really will be this simple. 

But in practice, to make agents that work today, you’re dealing with a delicate balance of what to give to the global agent vs. a subagent. What things to make agentic vs. just a deterministic tool call. How to handle the inherent limitations of the context window. 

You had to figure out how to retrieve the right data for the user’s task, and how much compute to throw at the problem. How to decide what to make fast, and suffer potential quality drops, vs. slow but maybe annoying. And endless other questions.

So far there’s no one right answer for any of this, and there are meaningful tradeoffs for any given approach you take.

And importantly, getting this right requires a deep understanding of the domain you’re solving the problem for. Handling this problem in AI coding is different from law, which is different from healthcare. This is why there’s so much opportunity for AI agent plays right now.

I turned down 30 clients last month.

People think I'm crazy. 

"That's like $200K you could've made."

But here's what happened instead: 


We signed 2 perfect-fit clients at higher rates, got 3 referrals, and our retention jumped to 100%.

Most agency owners fail because they're counting clients instead of building a brand that attracts the right ones.

The Birdhouse will do about $2M this year doing the OPPOSITE of conventional wisdom.

Here's what actually matters:


1. Take care of your team like they're partners

Every quarter, I sit down with each person and ask: 

"What are your goals? Are we helping you hit them?"

When your team is growing personally and financially, they'll run through walls for clients.


2. Invest in networking without calculating ROI

I spent $30K flying to events my first year. 

No immediate return. 

Just to have conversations and build relationships.

A year later, those "random" conversations turned into $200K in referrals.


3. Build a brand people want to be part of

We have

Custom jackets
Bird-themed Slack emojis
Vintage cartoon aesthetics
Pokemon cards at my desk (bird-types only)

Sounds extra? Maybe. 

But the brand isn't just marketing. 

It's retention, recruitment, and referrals wrapped into one.


4. Be "the guy" in your niche

We're not the best marketers in the world. 

But we are THE BEST at what we do for Twitter, LinkedIn, and Threads.

When someone asks "Who should I talk to about growing on X?" they mention The Birdhouse. 

That positioning is worth more than any ad spend.

But here's the most counterintuitive part:


5. I refer out more people than I sign. 

Dozens of leads every month, but we only take 1-2 clients.

Why? 

Because I'd rather have 10 perfect clients getting incredible results than 50 mediocre ones churning every quarter.

When someone isn't a fit, I send them to another agency I trust. 

That person becomes a fan. The agency sends business back. My reputation stays intact.

One more thing: 

Do the unscalable stuff as long as you can.

I still do every client onboarding call. 

I still build their profile setup. 

I still send resources that aren't part of our service.

We're at $2M/year. 

I could delegate it.

But clients pay a premium because I'm in the trenches with them.

If you're under $10M, white glove service isn't a luxury.

It's your competitive advantage.


The bottom line:

Your agency isn't a numbers game. 

It's a reputation game.

• Turn down the wrong clients.
• Invest in your team.
• Build a brand people want to be associated with. 
• Be the obvious choice in your category.
 
The money follows when you stop chasing it.

Most people think building a personal brand is free.

It's not.

The investment is either your time or your money.

Pick one.


2. Living a Life Worth Posting About

3. Building Your Own IP

All of a sudden, everyone's fractional.

Fractional CMO. Fractional CFO. Fractional CRO. Fractional sanity.

It's the new epidemic of modern business......leadership as a subscription service.

The model sounds brilliant in theory: get senior talent without the full-time cost. In practice? It's a carousel of consultants playing executive dress-up. They parachute in, write a strategy deck, host a few "alignment" calls, and disappear before the hard part starts.

I've watched three portfolio companies burn through five fractional CMOs in 18 months. None of them remember the company names. One billed for a "brand refresh" that was literally the same deck they'd delivered to another portco six months earlier. Just find-and-replace on the logo.

There's a use case. If you need a specialist to clean up a mess, prep for a transaction, or set a short-term direction, a fractional operator can be a great stopgap. But as a system? It's unsustainable.

You can't build real accountability on part-time contracts. You can't expect cultural transformation from someone who's never been to the office. And you can't build compounding performance when your entire C-suite has other clients and other priorities.

For PE, fractional leadership is a massive red flag. It signals either a company too cheap to commit to real talent, or management too weak to attract it. Either way, you're buying a business held together with contractor agreements and Google Calendar invites.

The rise of "fractional everything" says more about the market than the model. It reflects executives burned out on corporate politics, and companies too confused to build real teams. It's the illusion of sophistication......strategy without ownership, expertise without execution.

Real leadership isn't divisible. You can't fractionalise conviction, culture, or consequence.

So yes, hire a fractional CFO to stabilise your numbers. Bring in a fractional CMO to reset your funnel. But if your long-term plan relies on fractional leadership, you don't have a strategy.

You have a holding pattern.

And no business ever scaled in fractions.

Most PE firms don’t have an operating model. They have a PowerPoint.

The “Value Creation Plan” (VCP) has become a kind of corporate theatre.....a glossy internal pitch deck designed to impress LPs, not to actually change how portfolio companies run. It’s full of big promises: digital transformation, pricing excellence, sales enablement. Then it disappears into the ether the moment the deal closes.

In reality, most firms are still managing value creation through ad hoc emails, Excel trackers, and quarterly updates. They talk about playbooks, but they don’t operate from one. The “ops model” is a myth built to sell credibility, not capability.

A real operating model isn’t a presentation. It’s infrastructure. It’s what happens every Monday morning, not every QBR.

Here’s what that would actually look like:

1. Live dashboards, not static reports.
Every portfolio company should feed performance data into a single platform, updated weekly. You don’t need 40 KPIs per company, you need 6 that matter. Revenue, CAC, LTV, conversion rate, retention, and cash. Real-time visibility drives action.

2. Cross-portfolio benchmarks.
Stop pretending every company is unique. The point of owning multiple businesses is to spot patterns and act on them. If one business converts at 8% and another at 3%, that’s an immediate playbook opportunity. You don’t need consultants to find that; you need standardised data.

3. Capability pods.
Hire functional experts.....in digital marketing, pricing, M&A integration....and let them operate across the portfolio. These aren’t advisors or slide-makers. They’re doers who drop into companies to fix, build, and leave systems behind. Think of it as shared services for execution.

4. Cadence and accountability.
Weekly stand-ups. Monthly performance reviews. Clear owners. If the only time your ops team meets management is before board meetings, you don’t have a system, you have a reaction function.

5. Shared language of performance.
Right now, every portfolio company defines “success” differently. Some measure top-line growth, others focus on EBITDA, others on margin expansion. A real model creates a unified framework: one vocabulary, one scorecard, one rhythm.

When you see the few firms that do this well, the difference is obvious. Their portfolio dashboards look like trading floors. Their operators sound like GMs, not consultants. And their CEOs know where they stand every week, not every quarter.

Until then, “value creation” will stay exactly where it began: inside a PowerPoint, somewhere between the “synergy pipeline” slide and the “next steps” placeholder.

The most effective Operating Partner I ever met?

Didn't have a title. Didn't have a playbook. Didn't even call himself an Operator.

He just showed up at a struggling portfolio company, quietly watched for a few weeks, and then made the call nobody else would: the CEO had to go.

No politics. No 90-day transition plan. Just execution.

He rebuilt the funnel. Realigned incentives. Cut the waste. Simplified the offer. Got sales and marketing talking to each other again. Eighteen months later: revenue had tripled.

No fanfare. No podcasts. Just results.

Problem is, that story should be a case study in operating excellence. Instead, it's proof that the private equity model is broken.

Operating Partners only get power when everything else has failed. They're handed the keys after the board's exhausted, the deal team's reputation is at risk, and the fund can't afford another write-down.

By that point, it's triage. The business is bleeding, the data's unreliable, and everyone's pretending the model's still fine.

We call it "value creation," but really it's crisis management with better branding.

The irony is obvious......if the Operator had been empowered before the wheels came off, the CEO transition could've been orderly, the fixes incremental, and the value compounding long before the fund was in trouble.

It's always easier to change the roof while the sun's shining. But private equity only starts patching leaks in a storm.

Until firms trust Operators with real influence before the problems start, "Operating Partner" will remain a title that means "clean up someone else's mess."

And that's why the model, as it stands, is still more reactive than strategic.

Everyone’s got capital. Everyone’s got deal flow. Almost no one has a real talent strategy.

Capital is commoditised.
So is M&A.

Everyone has access to debt. Everyone’s getting banker decks. Everyone’s playing the same playbook from 2012, just with higher entry multiples.

The last true edge?
Talent.

Not the “we’ll bring in a new CEO post-close” kind of talent.
A proper, institutionalised strategy to identify, attract, deploy, and retain world-class operators across the portfolio.

But most firms aren’t even close.
Because it’s hard. And because they’re not built for it.

PE misses this because the entire ecosystem is optimised around capital deployment and transaction speed. Talent doesn’t fit neatly into that machine. It’s slow. It’s qualitative. It requires network, judgment, and long-term thinking—none of which gets you carry faster.

Here’s why it’s so hard:

 • Talent is nonlinear. The difference between good and great can’t be seen on a CV.
 • There’s no process for it in most firms. No bench. No real pipeline. Just a mad dash post-close.
 • PE still overvalues credentials (fancy brand names, MBAs, prior exits) instead of capability and cultural fit.
 • Most firms don’t have the internal experience to assess operators properly—especially in growth and commercial roles.

So what happens?
Same cycle every time.
Close the deal. Scramble for a CEO. Hope for the best. Replace them 18 months later.

Here’s what needs to change:

 1. Build a bench before you buy
You wouldn’t wait until you sign a term sheet to raise capital. Stop waiting until close to find leadership. Build a shadow org chart during diligence.
 2. Invest in talent infrastructure
This means internal talent partners who aren’t just recruiters. Operating partners who understand what great looks like. Systems for assessing and tracking execs across the funnel.
 3. Stop hiring off brand alone
Track record is useful. But functional depth and cultural alignment matter more. A second-tier CEO who understands your customer can outperform a McKinsey-SVP-turned-God-complex nine times out of ten.
 4. Treat talent like an investment, not a transaction
Pay to get it right. Onboard properly. Support your operators with coaching, data, and systems. And reward performance in a way that drives outcomes—not just optics.

In a market where everything else is commoditised, execution is the only real alpha.
And execution is powered by talent.

If you don’t have a strategy for it, you’re not doing value creation. You’re just hoping the spreadsheet was right.

What’s the Ethical Limit of Automating Performance Evaluations?

I’ve been thinking about this a lot lately - especially as more companies quietly plug AI into how they measure people.

At first, it sounds efficient.
AI can analyze output, detect bias, flag patterns humans miss.
But here’s the paradox: when you quantify everything, you risk missing what makes someone truly valuable.

AI evaluates consistency.
Humans grow through contradiction.
The algorithm rewards what’s stable; the person improves through what’s uncomfortable.

A model can tell you who performs best today.
But it can’t predict who’s learning fastest, who’s quietly mentoring others, or who will outgrow the system itself.
It sees outcomes, not evolution.

That, to me, is the ethical boundary we rarely discuss.
Automation in evaluation isn’t just about fairness - it’s about the kind of excellence we end up promoting.

Because when judgment becomes data-driven, we stop noticing the things that don’t fit the data.

✅ So what’s the solution?

I believe we shouldn’t eliminate human judgment - we should redefine it.
 → Let AI handle pattern detection (who’s burning out, who’s excelling faster than average).
 → Let humans handle interpretation (why it’s happening, and what growth looks like next).
 → And most importantly, introduce reflection loops - where employees challenge or explain their own data story.

That’s how performance stays both measurable and meaningful - powered by data, but guided by discernment.

Because the real danger isn’t that AI will judge us.
It’s that we’ll stop learning how to judge ourselves.

So here’s my question:
If performance is increasingly quantified, how do we protect the parts of human growth that can’t be measured?

If AI Handles Reports and Scheduling, What Exactly Should Managers Manage?

I’ve been sitting with this question for days.
Not because it’s new, but because it’s uncomfortable.

Every new AI update promises to “free up managers for higher-value work.”

But if AI now writes reports, tracks goals, summarizes meetings, and even drafts feedback - what is left that’s uniquely human about management?

Here is what I think most people miss.
AI isn’t just automating management. It’s redefining what it means to manage.

The real erosion isn’t in tasks. It’s in how managers think.
For decades, managers built authority through control - owning information, structuring schedules, reviewing output.
Now AI does all of that faster and with fewer mistakes.

So, in my opinion, the role of the manager is quietly shifting from control to context.
→ Control was about tracking who did what.
→ Context is about understanding why it matters.
→ Control relies on rules.
→ Context depends on judgment.

This is the part most leaders aren’t preparing for.
Because judgment can’t be automated - and it’s also incredibly hard to teach.

AI handles knowledge, but it can’t handle knowing what matters.
That’s where the next generation of managers will earn their value.
Not by being the smartest in the room, but by being the clearest in the noise.

Here’s how I think managers should start adapting:
✅ Design clarity, not control. Use AI to run the systems, but stay focused on the mental models your team uses to decide.
✅ Reward discernment. Ask not just “Was this done?” but “Was this the right thing to do?”
✅ Protect focus. AI creates information abundance - great managers create attention scarcity around what truly matters.

AI won’t replace managers. It will reveal which ones were only managing systems, and which ones were actually shaping thinking.

So maybe the real question isn’t “What should managers manage?”
Maybe it’s “What kind of thinking will management still require?”

#Leadership #FutureOfWork #AITransformation #HumanSkills #WorkplaceEvolution #AIDebate

Slack’s founder noticed something weird as they grew. Every young PM they hired immediately wanted to hire someone. When he asked why:

"That person would do the product management, and I would do strategy."

That response reveals a physics problem eating your company.

Because these weren't bad people. They were smart and ambitious.

That's what made it dangerous.

Given their system, asking for headcount was the rational move.

Career progression correlates with team size. Comp bands jump when you become "manager of X." Your peers build empires and you get praised for scale.

So they asked for more people. Everyone does.

In budget meetings, no head of engineering or sales ever says "Good news - we can do this with fewer people."

The slider only moves one way.

C. Northcote Parkinson saw this in the 20th century.

He studied the British Navy. Between 1914 and 1928, they cut ships by two-thirds and sailors by a third.

Yet, strangely, administrators grew 78%.

Ships down. Sailors down. Bureaucrats up. What?

But swap "admiralty officials" for "product managers" and you get modern tech.

Parkinson found two mechanisms:

1. Officials want subordinates, not rivals.
2. Officials create work for each other.

Stewart called what comes next "fake work" - activities that look like work but don't move any customer outcome.

"Let's explore this space."
"We should have someone thinking about X."
"Can you own the strategy for Y?"

That fog is where Parkinson's Law thrives.

Every new hire makes it worse. Double the team, quadruple the meetings. Most of what they'll talk about is themselves.

The antidote is "known valuable work."

"Known" means a smart outsider could understand why it matters.

"Valuable" means a customer would be noticeably worse off without it.

The funniest thing Parkinson said:

"No one has been idle. All have done their best."

Everyone in the bloated Admiralty was busy.

They just weren't doing anything that mattered.

My thoughts on the AI bubble…

AI is not profitable yet. 

Big companies, like Oracle, and small companies, like Lambda, are borrowing money to fund their AI ventures. 

If AI becomes profitable, a lot of this debt will be paid back. But even if AI becomes profitable, some companies will fail, and some will succeed. This is normal. 

However, if AI fails to become profitable, asset backed securities (sound familiar? Think 2008 housing crisis) that are propping up some of these AI companies also fail. As companies default on their loans, these ABS will become devalued and can trigger other defaults. 

About $13.3B in ABS have been created and issued as companies race to build data centers. 

How widespread the effects of default could be depends on how this debt is being traded and who is holding the bag if things go south. 

Making AI profitable should be a key concern for everyone involved. But as we rob Peter (massive layoffs) to pay Paul (fund AI), the idea that consumers will consume enough for AI to be profitable seems pretty far fetched. 

I do believe there is a path forward for AI to become profitable, but too many AI strategies are to layoff employees to fund simple (and, quite frankly, unimaginative) chatbot projects which are NEVER going to move the needle towards profitability. 

Profitability requires novelty and innovation.

I used to believe MVPs (Minimal Viable Products) are the way to go when building. Now I think it’s killing great products. Let me explain:

If you only read one post about marketing this week it should be this one.

We are drowning in AI slop.

But I am going to throw you a life jacket.

ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Perplexity, this agent or that. It's all great. But here's what you really need to know about the craft of marketing.

• People do not care about your company.
• They don't care about your product and your features.
• They don't care about the new integration you just shipped.

What they care about is themselves. 

We are biologically wired to be selfish.

So: if you can talk to someone about themselves, you’ll have their full attention.

Focus on helping them first.

What is the selfish benefit to them? Why should they care?

Here are five timeless principles you can think about for your customers:

1. Make Me Look Good

Whether it’s to a boss, their team, or their industry, people are wired to care about status. Can you help them impress someone? Can you give them an edge? Can they copy/paste your idea and look smart in the next meeting? I remember impressing my boss when I was 23 year old marketer being asked to build a marketing plan for a product launch for the first time. I had no idea what to do, found a great resource from HubSpot blog (the OG) and used it. She was impressed.

2. Save Me Time

Time is the one thing nobody has enough of. If you can help someone do something faster, with less friction or fewer steps, that's the way. People will pay for time savings. BUT they have to believe this. Can't just be a marketing claim. You need proof. Show the receipts. The examples.

3. Make Me Money

Capitalism seems to be the best system that exists in the world. I don't make the rules I am just observing. It doesn't seem like someone has created a better way, so yes - money matters. And whether they will admit it or not, your customers want to make more money (because they are people). Whether directly or indirectly, people are tuned in to ROI. If you can help someone drive revenue, get more leads, reduce churn, or land a raise then you’ve got their attention. Again though: MUST BE BELIEVABLE. So you must have PROOF.

4. Help Me Avoid Pain

We are wired to avoid pain. We'd almost always rather avoid pain than get some type of gain. No one likes to look stupid, feel behind, or get in trouble. If you can speak to something they’re secretly worried about -- a mistake, a missed goal, a clunky process -- and show a way out, they’ll lean in.

5. Help Me Reach The Next Level

Great marketing often speaks to ambition. Career growth. Mastery. Confidence. People want to feel like they’re getting better — at their job, in their life, as a person.

***

There's a lot of noise in marketing right now because of tech/AI, but I'm more bullish than ever on understanding the timeless principles in marketing, and that starts with understanding people.

I hope these are helpful for whatever you're working on right now.

MIT literally built an AI that can rewrite its own code to get smarter.

MIT's new Self-Adapting Language Model (SEAL) is a game-changer in AI evolution. Unlike traditional models that require manual fine-tuning, SEAL generates its own training data and optimization strategies.

 LLM's are frozen snapshots of the past.

Another day at the office. You promise yourself that today you will finally work on a long-term strategy.
And then reality kicks down the door.

Suddenly, you are juggling things like:

• Server costs exploding
• A critical bug in production
• A second critical bug in production
• Analytics breaking and killing insights
• Experiments cannibalizing each other
• Rumours of a reorg are drifting around
• A roadmap that feels like fortune-telling
• A partner API is going down without warning
• Sales promising a custom feature by yesterday
• Compliance demanding an impossible deadline
• Tech debt that should have been fixed years ago
• A migration nobody owns but everyone claims is urgent

Take a deep breath.

It is not your company.
Not your product.

This is what happens when something big is built by many people over many years. Complexity grows. Mess grows. Always.

And here is the plot twist.
You will see the same chaos everywhere with different flavours.

Should you despair and quit product management?

No.

You adapt.
You prioritize.
You accept that it will never be perfect and that there will always be more work than any team can deliver. AI will not change that.

So what should you do?

1) Prioritize relentlessly.
Your backlog is a funnel of trade-offs. Every yes kills five other things. Prune without guilt.

2) Learn from each near catastrophe and prevent the next one.
Fix the root cause. Adjust the system. Let chaos teach you.

3) Help the team get the same perspective.
A calm PM creates a calm crew. Chaos is normal, not a sign of failure.

4) Get the money in order, then focus on tech excellence, unless tech issues are why the money is not in order.
Products die from empty wallets before bottom-rock code quality.

5) Not your circus, not your monkeys.
Focus on your mission. You cannot fix everything, and you are not supposed to.

6) Speak in data, profit, and risks, not emotions.
Clarity cuts through noise when everything feels urgent.

7) And if things feel truly dire, shout.
Escalate before morale hits zero. Protect the team. Silence never saved anyone.

Things will never be perfect, but they should never be so bad that they steal the joy of building.

What about you? Are you happy creating or dreading the next catastrophe?

Who really invented AI? Spoiler: it wasn’t Hinton, LeCun, and it didn’t begin in 2012.
The term “artificial intelligence” was coined by John McCarthy at Dartmouth in 1956, decades before deep learning existed. And early AI wasn’t built around neural networks at all, but around logic, search, and symbolic reasoning.
Artificial intelligence didn’t begin with ImageNet.
The field goes back to the 1950s and 1960s, when researchers were building systems that could reason, plan, and solve problems.

Neural networks were just one branch, and they’ve been around far longer than most people realize:
·        Perceptrons (1957)
·        Backpropagation (1960s)
·        Boltzmann machines (1985)
·        Parallel distributed processing (1980s)

By the mid-1980s, neural networks were hyped as the future of intelligence.
People confidently predicted that neural nets would:
·        learn
·        reason
·        generalize
·        replace symbolic AI

I lived through this wave firsthand.
Many of us were writing backprop-based solvers for constraint problems in the late 1980s, convinced we were inches away from machines that could think. It didn’t happen.

The 1980s boom collapsed because neural networks hit architectural limits that remain true today:
·        no explicit knowledge representation
·        no symbolic inference
·        no causality
·        no abstraction
·        no compositionality
·        catastrophic forgetting
·        extreme data dependence
·        no explainability

Neural nets excelled at pattern recognition but failed at explicit reasoning.
The field shifted back to the systems that actually worked:
·        expert systems
·        Bayesian inference
·        constraint solvers
·        fuzzy logic
·        probabilistic models
And neural nets faded, until GPUs, big data, and better regularization resurrected them in the 2010s.
The critical point:
The “deep” in deep learning wasn’t a breakthrough in intelligence.
It was a breakthrough in scale, compute and data infrastructure. 
The architecture didn’t become smarter, it just became larger.

So today, people are once again claiming that neural networks will:
·        think
·        reason
·        generalize
·        understand
Just like they claimed in the 1980s, and once again, we’re facing the same architectural limits.
Transformer-based LLMs are extraordinary at:
·        prediction
·        compression
·        linguistic style
But they still cannot:
·        represent explicit knowledge
·        reason about truth
·        perform symbolic inference
·        build abstractions

They are statistical engines operating without semantics.
We learned that lesson decades ago.
There is a much longer story here, about how AI moved from logic to statistics, and why the hard problems we abandoned decades ago are returning with urgency.
I’ll write a deeper piece soon that explores the history and architecture in more detail.

When You Code AI, You See Why Hallucinations Can’t Be Fixed

I’ve stopped believing hallucinations are a solvable problem.

Everyone treats hallucinations like bugs to patch: ‘Better training data will fix it’; ‘Smarter prompting will eliminate them’; & ‘Next-generation models won’t have this issue’.

They’re wrong.

When you’ve work at the architecture level: writing the scripts, tweaking the attention mechanisms, debugging the loss functions, you’ll see the uncomfortable truth. 

Hallucinations aren’t accidents. They’re mathematics.

The same softmax operation that forces attention weights to sum to 1.0 also forces the model to attend to something, even when nothing is relevant. There’s no ‘none of the above’ option built into the architecture. The autoregressive generation that produces fluent text also locks the model into completing patterns it started, even when those patterns are fiction. Once it begins hallucinating the first part of a ‘fact’, it will confidently finish the sentence. The cross-entropy loss that optimises next-token prediction rewards pattern-matching, not truth-seeking. No penalty exists for why something is likely, whether it’s a true fact or a common misconception.

I recently read a paper arguing hallucinations ‘originate simply as errors in binary classification’. At first, this seemed reductive. But having driven deep into model development, I realised they’re right. 

When you compress billions of statements into fixed parameters, statistical errors aren’t bugs, they’re mathematically inevitable. The model must generalise to survive as general-purpose AI. That same generalisation that makes it useful also makes it guess when uncertain.
The architecture that enables LLMs: attention mechanisms, fixed embeddings, layer normalisation, residual connections, simultaneously guarantees they’ll hallucinate. 

These aren’t separate features. You can’t eliminate one without destroying the other. Remove the compression, lose the generalisation. Remove the confident completion, lose the fluency.

So, we’ve been asking the wrong question. It’s not ‘how do we fix hallucinations?’ It’s ‘how do we build systems that account for them?’

Even if you want to fix the problem, the real fix isn’t better models. It’s better incentives. 

Current benchmarks reward confident guessing over honest uncertainty. Models are optimised like students taking multiple-choice tests: wrong answer scores zero, ‘I don’t know’ scores zero, so always guess. Until we fundamentally change how we evaluate AI; penalising overconfidence, rewarding acknowledgment of uncertainty, we’re still training systems to be confidently wrong.

Hallucinations are a feature of the mathematics, not a flaw in engineering. The sooner the field stops treating them as fixable bugs and starts designing verification systems around this fundamental limitation, the sooner we’ll build actually trustworthy AI.

I tried building three startups and invested in many others. Here’s honest advice. Keep the team small for as long as possible. A few solid engineers, designers, and people who can tell a story are enough to win. More employees create power games you can’t control. Build products customers genuinely need. Make them a paying hostage. Don’t chase investors. They will chase you if you get the above right. The best investments always work that way. Avoid stupid PR and podcast tours. Build your own distribution and own your narrative.
That’s a moat money can’t buy. Conferences are overrated and networking is mostly noise. Buy books for your team and meet their families.
They won’t remember team dinners, but they’ll remember being respected. Most startup jargon like burn, CAC, TAM was created by people who’ve never built anything meaningful. Real builders focus on profits. Don’t lose money. Don’t follow investor trend reports. Create your own trend. That’s the real power of entrepreneurship. Don’t waste energy arguing online. Build something so good that others point to it for inspiration.

In the last few weeks, a bunch of companies went IPO and it’s a reminder that entrepreneurship can create enormous wealth for founders and their teams. But it’s also a reminder that most founders will spend years just trying to survive. Thousands are still surviving on belief, debt, delayed and no salaries. I know a few we invested in. And this is what most VCs don’t share. 

Entrepreneurship looks glamorous in hindsight but in reality, it’s years of uncertainty, low pay and stubborn belief. Till the age of 40, my average monthly salary as a founder was ~30k. Barely any savings after 15 years of building. Out of the three startups I built, one paid back moderately. Two were write offs. Only in the last two years did I see a 6 figure salary for the first time in my career.

But I’ll be honest, if you stay long enough in the game, sometimes it pays back in ways you never planned for. What I get to do now wasn’t even on my bucket list. But entrepreneurship created that pathway.

So, not every founder will ring the IPO bell or build a unicorn and that’s okay. The win isn’t always the valuation. Don’t fool yourself and build something meaningful. It will take time and it will take more out of you than you planned. It’s the resilience you build while fighting to survive that creates your pathway. 

Stay stubborn & keep building.
The world may not notice you yet.But when it finally does, it’ll act like it believed in you from day one.

‘Is AI about to replace humans?’

Short answer: Not anytime soon

To match a single human brain using today’s AI approaches, you’d need something close to a football-field-sized data centre running flat out - just to mimic how one person sees, thinks, learns, and decides in real time.

All that to replicate what your brain does on 20 watts.

If you go further and try to replicate the brain 𝘣𝘪𝘰𝘭𝘰𝘨𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘺- neurons, synapses, chemistry, plasticity - you’re no longer talking about a data center.

Theoretically, it can be done but you need a computer the size of planet Earth 🌍

That’s the gap people forget 🤷

AI is exceptional at automation, repetition, and pattern extraction at scale.
But judgment, creativity, context, taste, and knowing when 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘵𝘰 𝘢𝘤𝘵 aren’t just more parameters.

They’ve evolved over millions of years & are emergent properties of a system that learns continuously, reasons across domains, and compresses reality with extreme efficiency.

Until we see a fundamental shift in how intelligence is computed - not just bigger models and more GPUs - replacing human creativity isn’t a near-term threat.

The real miracle of the brain isn’t intelligence.

It’s all the complexity that fits inside your skull, runs on sugar and oxygen, and dissipates less heat than a light bulb.

That’s the human flex AI can’t replicate 😅

Most marketers aren’t “bad at measurement.” We’ve just been lied to about how measurement should always work.

Digital advertising trained an entire industry to believe that if a result isn’t instant, it isn’t real. 

But real marketing doesn't work that way, and real growth definitely doesn't. 

This week on The Marketing Architects Podcast, we sat down with our Chief Analytics Officer, Matthew Hultgren. He walked us through our new report, "Measuring the Long and the Short," which breaks down the biggest failures in marketing measurement today: siloed teams, unclear KPIs, budget too small to ever move a number, and attribution built for speed > truth. 

The best marketers plan measurement upfront, align on a "North Star" metric instead of a dozen competing KPIs, and test intentionally, not desperately.

Every strategist talks about “the insight.” Almost none have ever found one.

And that doesn’t matter.

Advertising has built an entire mythology around the idea of the insight. It’s treated as the secret code that unlocks creativity, the revelation that turns observation into gold. But it hides a truth most strategists won’t admit: the insight is an illusion. It’s a story we tell ourselves after the fact, a way of intellectualising work that actually succeeded through far simpler mechanisms: attention, repetition, and memory.

The industry loves to confuse three words: observation, truth, and insight. An observation describes what people do. A truth you can verify. An insight supposedly transforms one into the other through revelation. But in practice, that distinction has collapsed. “People feel anxious before flying” becomes “Flying connects you to what matters most.” We’ve turned observation into greeting-card wisdom and called it strategy.

Psychology shows we don’t buy through revelation. We buy through habit and familiarity. Kahneman’s System 1 explains that most decisions are fast and automatic. Byron Sharp proved that distinctive brand assets like colours, sounds, and jingles drive growth because they make brands easier to recall and find. Mental availability beats emotional depth every time.

Even the campaigns we call “insight-led” prove the point. Dove Real Beauty didn’t work because it unearthed a hidden truth about self-esteem. It worked because it built distinctive memory structures and repeated them for two decades. Apple Think Different succeeded because it borrowed cultural prestige from Einstein and Picasso and repeated that aesthetic until it became the brand. The “insight” was the story we told afterwards.

Robert Heath’s work on low-attention processing confirms this. Advertising doesn’t persuade through revelation; it primes through familiarity. Emotional response builds implicitly, without conscious engagement. The audience isn’t decoding strategy. They’re storing feelings and associations that resurface later. 

That’s the real illusion. We don’t find insights; we construct them. They’re useful fictions for aligning teams internally, but they’re not how advertising works in the world. Advertising isn’t the science of revelation. It’s the science of memory.

The job isn’t to uncover hidden human truths. It’s to build mental availability. Don’t ask “what’s the human truth?” Ask “what will people remember in three seconds at the shelf?” The best campaigns don’t change what people believe; they make the brand impossible to forget at the moment of choice.

What actually sells: a gorilla drumming to Phil Collins. A ribbon of red on a bottle. Four notes that mean Intel. Cadbury purple. The McDonald’s jingle you heard once in 1987 and can still sing today.

None of those came from insight. They endured because they understood how memory works.

Most insights don’t sell. But a jingle from a decade ago? I’m lovin’ it.

Under 16% of people will buy your product if they haven't already been primed by your brand.

84% of purchases consist of people choosing brands they're already biased towards *before* they start shopping.

WPP Media and Saïd Business School, University of Oxford just released a killer new study with compelling new evidence supporting the need for brands to create Future Demand.

In their analysis of 1.2 million consumer journeys, they found that the overwhelming majority or purchases are pre-determined by the priming the buyer has had by being previously exposed to brand marketing.

"The result of this priming bias," they report, "is that much of people's purchase-journey behaviour is predetermined: in every category they buy from, most shoppers will already know which brands they're going to consider; will typically consider fewer than three brands; and will end up choosing the brand they're most biased towards.

The proportion of purchases governed by brand priming is huge: 84% of purchases consist of people choosing brands they're already biased towards before they start shopping."

If you haven't created Future Demand, you're highly unlikely to be purchased by most of the market.

What tech stacks are top early-stage AI startups actually using? 🤔 

Below are some of the data points I've gathered after speaking with hundreds of AI startups.

1️⃣ Frontend:

Next.js dominates across almost all teams; Tailwind CSS is the standard choice for styling; ShadCN UI components are somewhat common; some use Chakra UI; TypeScript is universal (no exceptions).

2️⃣ Backend:

Most teams use Python (typically with FastAPI); Node.js + Express remains popular for lightweight services; Go is used selectively for performance-critical microservices; Postgres is the main database; Widespread use of SQL + vector DBs, with pgvector dominating (I'm a bit worried for standalone vector DB startups).

3️⃣ Deployment:

AWS and GCP remain most popular, BUT Azure is seeing a notable increase (especially compared to post-ChatGPT); Many combine Vercel for the frontend with AWS/GCP for backend infrastructure; GitHub Actions is the most common CI/CD choice; Docker is standard (Kubernetes is used more selectively); Serverless adoption is rising (with growing use of e.g. Modal for on-demand compute).

4️⃣ AI Model Usage:

In proprietary APIs OpenAI/GPT, Anthropic/Claude, and Google/Gemini dominate; In open-source not seeing e.g. Meta or Mistral almost anywhere, Qwen is rising fast.

Almost no startups train models from scratch, and very few fine-tune. The real constraint is data, not model access.

Success increasingly depends on:

➡️ Backend optimization
➡️ Data structuring and formatting
➡️ Integration of AI into business processes

Roughly 90% of effective AI implementation is data engineering and operational design, not model architecture.

Fine-tuning should be a last resort. Before committing to it, most successful teams first:

➡️ Improve prompts
➡️ Add post-processing scripts
➡️ Fix their data pipelines

E.g. one startup reduced 90% of model errors with a simple script. Fine-tuning adds long-term maintenance overhead and should be reserved for cases where every other lever has been optimized.

Some leading AI companies are moving toward more efficient systems languages:

➡️ Lovable migrated 42,000 lines of Python to Go, improving concurrency, deployment speed, and maintainability.

➡️ OpenAI transitioned its Codex CLI (now ChatGPT CLI) from Node.js to Rust, prioritizing performance and reliability.

OpenAI’s codebase is known to be a large monorepo, primarily written in Python, with increasing usage of Rust and Go. It combines scalable infrastructure built by seasoned engineers with research-grade code from PhDs. Most APIs are built around FastAPI with Pydantic for validation, but style consistency varies widely.

Key takeaway:

The differentiator in AI startups is no longer model access - it’s data quality, backend optimization, and system architecture.

The teams that invest early in clean infrastructure and efficient data pipelines will outperform those focused solely on fine-tuning or proprietary models.

OH MY SWEET SUMMER CHILD, NO.

Anyone telling you Zero-Click Marketing is about optimizing for people > algorithms has fully misunderstood the concept. In brief, it's just two things:

1) Over the last 10 years, people have been trained NOT to click on links. Humans now prefer to scroll natively in platforms vs. following links to the web.
2) Many platforms no longer enable links at all (TikTok, Instagram, many subReddits, YouTube, lots of AI answers) and almost every other one biases against links in their algos or information display (Google, Facebook, LinkedIn, Threads, Twitter, etc.)

Now tell me how the Fudge that means you should optimize for people?! If anything, it means algorithms control information access more than ever. Zero-click search results, AI answers, social feeds - it's machines deciding what to show. And thus, optimizing for machines is the game. Sure, you could get all semantic and claim those machines are, themselves, optimizing for addiction/engagement by humans, but we all know it's an indirect, mathematical machine-learning process filled with dark patterns and monetization incentives that's plenty divorced from what people want.

Nail in the coffin for me is what the platforms look like and what they promote -- is that really what *you want*? Or is it what's most likely to maximize the metrics Wall Street looks at in these companies' quarterly numbers?

Right. So learn the algos. Watch the algos. Optimize for the algos. And when/where you can, overlap that with what people want, too. But don't think you can ignore machines 🙄

A startup with 7 engineers asked me if they should start estimating work using story points. I told them it's a waste of time for any team with fewer than 20 engineers. Here's why:

𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗽𝗼𝗶𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺
Story point estimates are a core part of Agile. Estimate how many points each task is worth, look at historical velocity, and plan what fits into the next sprint.
This lets you communicate with stakeholders and (theoretically!) plan more predictably. When someone asks if their feature will be ready, you can say "it's 8 points, we do 80 points per sprint, and we've allocated 75 points, so it'll be in the next sprint." The stakeholder gets a timeline without understanding technical complexity.

𝗦𝗺𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺𝘀 𝗱𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀
At 7 engineers, everyone knows what everyone else is working on. When something needs to be done, someone picks it up. You don't have external stakeholders asking for specific project deadlines. The CEO tells you directly what they want. You know roughly when things will be done without checking a sprint plan. At this phase, adding lots of additional process is counterproductive.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝘁 𝟮𝟬+ 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀
At this scale, you have multiple teams, product managers fielding requests from different departments, and quarterly planning sessions where executives need to understand what's feasible.
You can't coordinate informally anymore. You need a standardized way to set expectations. This is when sprint planning and story points start making sense.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗽 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱
The biggest mistake is using story points to measure productivity.
Story points are gameable and engineers struggle to accurately estimate how much each task takes. Great engineers often underestimate the amount of time and work they put into something, which makes the estimates unreliable for measurement. 

If you want to measure actual engineering output, it's far more accurate to use a code complexity model like Weave's. You can correlate story points to actual output to see how good your team is at estimating - but story points alone don't measure productivity!

AI didn’t create fake work. It just exposed it. 

Headlines are blaming AI for polished but hollow output-decks, memos, reports that look good but add no value. It's being called 'work slop'. But the real problem isn’t the tech in my opinion. It’s the way companies reward busyness over outcomes. This is not new, it’s just been ‘hidden’ in the busyness for years.

If the incentive is to show how much you’ve done, AI will gladly generate more slides and summaries than anyone can process. If the incentive is to achieve goals efficiently, AI becomes a useful tool that can cut through the noise.

The real fix is cultural. Always was, always will be.

➡️ Measure thinking processes, not not just outputs. 
Too many workplaces still track how much someone produces instead of what they accomplish. Decks, memos, and reports pile up because they signal activity, not impact. A better measure is the quality of thought behind the work. Did it clarify a problem? Did it open a path forward? Did it connect to a real outcome? When leaders start rewarding process and clarity instead of volume, the work shifts from performance theater to genuine progress.

➡️ Eliminate fake work.
AI has shown us just how much of what fills a calendar or inbox serves no real purpose. Legacy tasks hang around because no one questions them. Meetings to prepare for meetings. Reports that no one reads. Assignments that exist to check boxes. AI can do them faster, but that doesn’t make them necessary. The harder work is cutting them out entirely. Leaders who are serious about productivity will need to start by pruning the busywork that clogs the system.

➡️ Give people space to learn how to use AI well and to edit, refine, and aim for quality.
Handing someone a new tool without time to practice is like giving a violin to an office worker and asking for a recital by the end of the day. Skill comes from reps, feedback, and examples of what good looks like. The first draft from an AI model is rarely the finished product. The real value comes in the editing, the refining, the sharpening of ideas until they hold weight. Organizations that build room for this kind of learning will not only avoid slop, they will create a culture where quality thinking is visible in every output.

AI is holding up a mirror to how organizations work. Some won’t like what they see.

I've done 200+ AI deployments. The patterns are clear:
→ Companies automate email responses with complex AI when templates would work
→ They ignore predictive maintenance and fraud detection where AI creates real value
→ They chase trends instead of solving actual problems

The best AI strategy isn't about using AI everywhere. It's about knowing where it creates value and where simpler solutions work better.

Before any AI initiative, ask three questions:
→ What's the actual problem we're solving?
→ What's the simplest solution that works?
→ Does AI genuinely add value here?

Not every nail needs a sledgehammer.

The best implementations use AI strategically and have the discipline to choose simpler tools when they work better.

Sometimes the smartest move is choosing the scissors.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗩𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗟𝗶𝗲.

“I built an app in 3 hours.” 

Sure.
You built a demo. It will take you at least 3 weeks to make it production-ready. And 3 months to clean up the mess.

Vibe coding is fun until you have to ship something real.

LLMs make development feel effortless. A polished UI with a a hosted backend. Everything responds instantly. Anyone who can write a prompt can spin up something that looks like a product. But the hard part was never building fast. The hard part is building to last.

You would not build a house without a foundation. Yet that is exactly what vibe coding encourages.

𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗽 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹𝘀:
→ Infrastructure design
→ Security boundaries
→ Deployment strategy
→ Error handling
→ Logging for diagnosis
→ Monitoring for failure detection
→ Alerts when things break at 2 a.m.

AI-assisted development is genuinely powerful. I have seen delivery timelines compress from weeks to days. Prototyping and early validation have never been faster. I use it myself, and I enjoy it.

But here is the uncomfortable truth: AI optimizes for plausibility. Not for simplicity and also not for long-term correctness. Left unconstrained, it produces architectures and technical debt that look reasonable but age badly: 
→ Abstraction layers nobody can explain
→ Blurred component boundaries
→ “Best practices” added before there is a problem to solve

More code.
Lower quality.
Slower teams over time.

Vibe coding optimizes for speed as the primary metric. Engineer-guided AI treats software as long-lived infrastructure that must be operated, understood, and evolved. AI does not reduce the need for engineering judgment. It increases it. The engineer’s role is shifting: From writing code to constraining, reviewing, and shaping it. AI is an accelerator. Without direction, it accelerates technical debt just as efficiently as it accelerates delivery.

Stop Calling Them AI Product Managers

Some of the job descriptions out there right now feel like they were written in an alternate universe. AI has been in most products for, what, a year? Yet companies are proudly asking for three to five years of AI product management experience. It is impressive confidence in a timeline that does not exist.

This trend is more than amusing. It is short-sighted. It signals that organizations still believe product management is a vertical specialty instead of a horizontal discipline. And it puts them at risk of missing strong candidates who absolutely could lead AI initiatives but will never apply because the requirements feel disconnected from reality.

A product manager does not need a special title to work on AI. They need the same fundamentals they use everywhere else: market understanding, problem framing, prioritization, hypothesis testing, and the ability to align the business behind the right bets. AI becomes a new tool in the toolbox, not a new profession.

When companies insist on titles like AI Product Manager, they limit their candidate pool and unintentionally signal that they do not yet understand how product actually works. The irony is hard to miss. The best product managers are already learning, adapting, and delivering AI capabilities without needing a label to do it.

If you want great PM talent driving your AI roadmap, focus on the craft. Focus on strategic thinking, business acumen, and the ability to deliver real outcomes. The rest will follow.

And if a company truly believes they need someone with half a decade of AI product experience, I hope they enjoy interviewing the time travelers who apply.

As amazing as LLMs are, improving their knowledge today involves a more piecemeal process than is widely appreciated. I’ve written before about how AI is amazing... but not that amazing. Well, it is also true that LLMs are general... but not that general. We shouldn’t buy into the inaccurate hype that LLMs are a path to AGI in just a few years, but we also shouldn’t buy into the opposite, also inaccurate hype that they are only demoware. Instead, I find it helpful to have a more precise understanding of the current path to building more intelligent  models.

First, LLMs are indeed a more general form of intelligence than earlier generations of technology. This is why a single LLM can be applied to a wide range of tasks. The first wave of LLM technology accomplished this by training on the public web, which contains a lot of information about a wide range of topics. This made their knowledge far more general than earlier algorithms that were trained to carry out a single task such as predicting housing prices or playing a single game like chess or Go. However, they’re far less general than human abilities. For instance, after pretraining on the entire content of the public web, an LLM still struggles to adapt to write in certain styles that many editors would be able to, or use simple websites reliably.
After leveraging pretty much all the open information on the web, progress got harder. Today, if a frontier lab wants an LLM to do well on a specific task — such as code using a specific programming language, or say sensible things about a specific niche in, say, healthcare or finance — researchers might go through a laborious process of finding or generating lots of data for that domain and then preparing that data (cleaning low-quality text, deduplicating, paraphrasing, etc.) to create data to give an LLM that knowledge.
Or, to get a model to perform certain tasks, such as use a web browser, developers might go through an even more laborious process of creating many RL gyms (simulated environments) to let an algorithm repeatedly practice a narrow set of tasks.
A typical human, despite having seen vastly less text or practiced far less in computer-use training environments than today's  frontier models, nonetheless can generalize to a far wider range of tasks than a frontier model. Humans might do this by taking advantage of continuous learning from feedback, or by having superior representations of non-text input (the way LLMs tokenize images still seems like a hack to me), and many other mechanisms that we do not yet understand.
