Founders love selling perks. Almost no one admits when they’re broken. We analyzed hundreds of thousands of PTO requests, and the data exposes how wrong some “obvious truths” really are.

1️⃣ Unlimited PTO is issued equally, but not used equally. Europeans with unlimited policies take 4 more days than those with fixed plans, while North Americans show almost no change. Same perk, very different utilization.

2️⃣ In a surprise twist, Canada, not the U.S., takes the least vacation. A good reminder that work culture assumptions aren’t always accurate.

3️⃣ Global perks ≠ global behavior. If you roll out PTO, wellness budgets, flexibility, or anything else across multiple countries, expect each market to use (or ignore) them differently. This directly affects motivation, retention, and perceived fairness.

4️⃣ A few cultural standouts: France leads in long sick leave, the UK and Ireland essentially block off Christmas week, and Germany consistently embraces long vacations. Your global team isn’t one audience, it's a portfolio of cultures.

5️⃣ For global founders, this is real leverage. When perks match how people actually behave and what they value locally, your compensation strategy becomes much more effective.

This is the Outstanding Paper Award at ICLR 2025, and this is exactly the kind of research on LLMs we need, not those quasi-psychological studies of the form "we asked the same question to these 3 models and see which one is more sycophant."

As you might already know, when finetuning language models, researchers first perform supervised learning on good examples, then apply preference optimization—training the model to distinguish preferred responses from rejected ones using algorithms like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). This two-stage process improves model behavior, but prior work observed a curious phenomenon: during the preference stage, the model's predicted probability of generating responses (measured by log-likelihood) decreases for nearly all responses, including the preferred ones the training is supposed to encourage.

This paper provides a mathematical framework explaining why this happens by decomposing each gradient update into three components: how the model currently predicts, how training on one example affects similar examples, and the direction of the gradient signal.

The analysis reveals a "squeezing effect": when you apply negative gradients to push down the probability of bad responses, the probability mass doesn't get redistributed evenly—it concentrates on whichever token has the highest probability at each position. This makes the model's predictions increasingly peaked. The effect becomes severe when the negative gradient targets responses that already have very low probability, which commonly occurs when using pre-collected datasets where the rejected responses were generated by a different model.

The framework explains why methods that generate new response pairs from the current model at each training step outperform methods using fixed pre-collected pairs—they avoid applying large negative gradients to responses in low-probability regions.

The insight leads to a simple improvement: including both preferred and rejected responses during supervised fine-tuning before preference optimization demonstrably improves alignment by reducing the harmful squeezing effect.

Andrew Ng just laid out a framework that explains why most AI investments will fail—and where the real opportunity is hiding.

His argument: Stop treating "AI" as one market. It's three completely different layers with completely different risk profiles.

→ Layer 1: Training Infrastructure
Building the 50th foundation model. GPUs that depreciate faster than my car. Cloud credits being passed around like Monopoly money.

Verdict: Bubble territory.

→ Layer 2: Inference Infrastructure
The workhorse. Running models in production. Low latency, efficiency-focused.

Verdict: Underinvested. But dependent on Layer 3.

→ Layer 3: Applications
The only layer that actually generates cash flow. Vertical workflows. Specific problems. Real ROI.

Verdict: Severely underfunded. This is where capital should be going.

The parallel Ng draws: Early 1900s copper mining vs. electrical appliances. Everyone over-invested in mining infrastructure. The real wealth was built by people who made useful products with electricity.

We're making the same mistake.

The metric that matters isn't model benchmarks. It's ROI.

The soft landing for AI depends on a massive capital rotation: away from the 50th general-purpose model, toward the 5,000th agentic workflow solving a billion-dollar industry problem.

Against the trend, we've banished AI from most of our internal processes. In many areas, the downsides of using AI vastly outweigh the benefits, especially when it comes to shared output. 

- Colleagues and applicants can easily disappear behind AI, which tends to erase their personal experience, their distinctive perspectives, their individual charisma, the originality of their research, in favor of an educated but impersonal monotone. The spread of AI makes us feel like the whole world is increasingly speaking in exactly the same voice, which turns knowledge and communication into tedium. 

- AI hinders more than enhances our colleagues' ability to learn. Learning is in the process, in the doing, in the trial and error, in the learner's own transformation, anywhere but in automated output. And learning is essential even to competently use AI: To get an AI to write well, for instance, you must know what great writing is and how to produce it yourself. Otherwise, you'll stop at producing more automatic slop. 

- AI output is virtually impossible to edit. A piece of human work gets better in stages, through an iterative process: You first deal with purpose and substance, then structure, then style. AI products are both flawed and final, a bit like a building that looks wonderful on the outside but whose inner architecture is full of overlooked problems. Fixing those essentially means taking it all apart and starting over. 

- AI negatively affects relationships among colleagues. Reading someone else's AI-generated contributions feels like a waste of time, when one could just as easily prompt the machine themselves. If AI content is flagged, it's happily ignored. If it's not, it becomes a source of distrust: How much of anything is genuine and fact-checked? What actual work does it reflect, given how quickly AI output drowns out human input? 

These problems relate to how mainstream AIs are designed: They are meant to satisfy the individual user. They help the user gain time, up their productivity, hide their insecurities, comfort their biases, reduce or filter interactions with others. ​They are ​built to act as your diligent assistant, your proactive helper, your trusted confidant, your unfailing friend. Shortly put, they cater to an egocentric society, and that's part of the reason why they are catching on so fast. By the same token, these tools get in the way of more collective processes and group efforts.

What we're investigating is more specialized uses of AIs. That essentially means dialing back their tendency to overshoot, by parroting human content. AIs can be very helpful with information retrieval, not least within one's own mass of folders and files. They can be used to brainstorm a topic, to learn a methodology, to look up best practices, to perform repetitive tasks. This is foundational work on which colleagues can then build. Whatever colleagues share with others must clearly demonstrate their own value.

Most B2B companies spend $50K on events and forget the $500 that actually converts.

I watched a client blow their entire Q4 budget on a massive booth.
Premium location. Fancy screens. Full swag suite.

They got 47 badge scans.
3 follow-up calls.
Zero pipeline.

Meanwhile, I spent $500 on coffee cards the week before the same event.

Sent them to 20 target accounts with a simple note:
"I'll be at [Event]. Would love to buy you an actual coffee and chat about [specific challenge]."

18 showed up.
12 booked follow-ups.
4 became opportunities.
2 closed within 90 days.

Here's what most companies miss about event ROI:

➜ The magic happens in the 1:1 moments, not the booth traffic
➜ Pre-event outreach beats post-event follow-up every time
➜ A $25 coffee card outperforms a $2,500 dinner

My exact pre-event playbook:

1. **Three weeks out:** Pull the attendee list, match it to your ICP
2. **Two weeks out:** Send personalized gifts to top 20-30 targets
3. **One week out:** Follow up with calendar links for specific time slots
4. **Day of:** Skip the booth duty, focus on booked meetings
5. **Day after:** Send thank-you gifts to no-shows with "sorry we missed you"

The math is stupid simple:

Traditional event spend: $50K ÷ 3 opportunities = $16,666 per opp
Smart gifting approach: $500 ÷ 4 opportunities = $125 per opp

You don't need a bigger booth.
You need a better strategy.

And maybe some coffee cards.

You’ve heard of OKRs. You’ve tracked KPIs.

But do you know the CSFs that make them work?

Here’s the breakdown every CEO needs to know:

🎯 OKRs (Objectives & Key Results)

➟ Define bold goals
➟ Stretch teams with measurable outcomes
➟ Align everyone around what matters most

📊 KPIs (Key Performance Indicators)

➟ Track ongoing performance
➟ Show if you’re on or off track
➟ Keep your business healthy day to day

🔑 CSFs (Critical Success Factors)

➟ Identify the must-win areas
➟ Focus resources where success depends most
➟ Form the foundation of your strategy

Think of it like this:

CSFs = What’s critical to succeed

OKRs = The goals you set to achieve it

KPIs = The metrics that show progress

The mistake most leaders make is using these tools in isolation.

The best leaders combine all 3.

Every company has KPIs.


Some have OKRs.

But only the great ones know their CSFs.

Because growth without focus is just noise.

Most CEO decisions are made with 60% of the information

No one tells you this before you take the job.

Most decisions at the top are not made with certainty.
They are made with fragments.

Partial data.
Conflicting signals.
Incomplete context.
Time pressure.

Waiting for 100% clarity feels responsible.
It is usually paralysis.

👀 Certainty is rare at the top

Early in your career, decisions come with guardrails.

More data.
More reviews.
More time.

As you move up, those disappear.

The questions get bigger.
The timelines get shorter.
The information gets noisier.

Research on bounded rationality reveals that senior leaders almost never have full information, because complexity grows faster than insight.

That is not a failure of intelligence.
It is a reality of scale.

🧠 Why waiting feels smart but isn’t

The brain craves closure.

Psychology says that uncertainty triggers discomfort, which makes leaders seek more data even when additional data no longer improves the decision.

At some point, waiting does not increase accuracy.
It only increases delay.

Studies on decision-making under uncertainty reveal that leaders who act with incomplete but sufficient information outperform those who wait for near certainty, especially in fast-moving environments.

Progress beats perfection.

😂 The funny part nobody admits

Every CEO recognizes this moment.

You ask for one more data point.
Then one more.
Then a sensitivity analysis.
Then a revised forecast.

Not because it will change the decision.
Because it postpones responsibility.

Eventually, the decision happens anyway.
Just later, with higher cost.

⚖️ The real skill is knowing when 60% is enough

Great leaders don’t guess.
They calibrate.

They know:
• which decisions are reversible
• which ones aren’t
• what must be right
• what can be corrected later

Research on high-performing executives betrays that decision quality depends less on information completeness and more on decision framing and speed of adjustment.

They move, then learn.
They don’t wait, then hope.

🏗️ How strong leaders decide with incomplete information

They do a few things consistently.

They:
• clarify the objective before debating options
• identify the one or two variables that actually matter
• set clear checkpoints for correction
• stay emotionally detached from being right

This turns uncertainty into motion, not anxiety.

🔍 The honest truth

If you wait for certainty, you won’t lead.

You’ll manage delay.

Most CEO decisions are made with 60% of the information.

The remaining 40% only appears after you move.

That’s not recklessness.
That’s leadership.

The job is not to eliminate uncertainty.
It’s to act responsibly inside it.

The real burning topic isn’t AI, velocity, or frameworks. It’s :

How do PMs protect their mindspace in a world that rewards chaos?

Because without clarity, strategy dies. And without strategy, you’re just running a feature factory. 

The future of product management won’t be defined by perfect PRDs.

It’ll be defined by the rare ability to cut through noise and think with depth when everything around you pushes you to stay shallow. 

And that’s the edge no AI tool will ever give you.

The web succeeded because it solved a coordination problem: millions of independent actors needed to link documents without central control. Open standards - HTTP, URIs, HTML - made this possible by providing a shared protocol layer that no single party owned.

AI agents face an analogous coordination problem. In multi-agent systems, agents built by different parties must exchange not just data but meaning: what a "customer", "approved", or "delivery date" actually denotes. Natural language alone cannot solve this. LLMs can interpret natural language flexibly, but flexibility is precisely the problem when agents must act reliably on shared information. Ambiguity that agents can resolve through context becomes a source of failure when machines transact autonomously at speed and scale.

Sharing meaning unambiguously requires two things: a formal system of semantics capable of precise entailment, and globally unique identifiers that can be resolved to authoritative definitions. Without formal semantics, agents cannot reason reliably about what follows from what. Without resolvable identifiers, "customer" in System A and "customer" in System B remain dangerously ambiguous - they might align, or they might not.

These are not novel requirements. They are the foundational principles of the semantic web: RDF for formal semantics, URIs for identification, and HTTP for resolution. Anyone building agent interoperability from scratch will either fail to meet these requirements, or meet them and arrive at substantially the same architecture.

The real question is whether to adopt these principles in open or proprietary form. Proprietary approaches face a structural problem: interoperability requires shared definitions, but shared definitions only become valuable when widely adopted, and wide adoption requires openness. This is the same network-effect logic that made the web's openness essential. A proprietary web would have remained a collection of walled gardens.

The trajectory is therefore clear: as agentic systems mature and the cost of failed interoperability mounts, the pressure towards truly open semantic standards will intensify. It is inevitable.

Something interesting is happening to natural language. It's moving deeper into the machine.

Large language models have shifted where prose sits in the technology stack. The Model Context Protocol lets AI systems orchestrate formal APIs through conversation. Files like CLAUDE.md shape agent behaviour through written instructions. In the ARC-AGI Prize (a benchmark designed to test abstract reasoning) one entry got state-of-the-art results not by evolving Python code, but by evolving plain-English instructions. They found natural language more expressive for compositional tasks. Everywhere you look, human language is becoming machine infrastructure.

This is happening because natural language is extraordinarily powerful. Its open-ended structure lets almost anything connect to almost anything else. Boundaries blur. Metaphor works, and rich, layered meaning lives in the spaces between words. That fuzziness isn't a flaw - it's what makes language expressive enough to capture the complexity of human thought.

But fuzziness has a cost. You can't do complex mathematics in natural language. You can't execute software reliably in it. The reason is simple: open-ended, ambiguous representations can't guarantee deterministic, repeatable computation. And deterministic, repeatable computation is exactly what formal systems provide - through restriction. Programming languages, mathematical notation, and logic all work by narrowing what can be said so that what is said can be executed reliably.

Business systems need both. They need the expressivity of natural language for human interaction, and the reliability of formal systems for computation. So what formalism can bridge these two worlds?

First-order predicate logic (FOL). Its predicate–argument structure closely mirrors how natural language already works: subjects, predicates, quantification. But unlike natural language, it has precise, well-defined semantics. This tradition runs from Aristotle's syllogisms through the formal work of Frege and Peirce in the late nineteenth century. When we apply this logic to a specific domain, we call it an ontology: a formal encoding of what exists, what properties things have, and how they relate.

Pair a large language model with an ontology and you get both sides of the equation: the expressive power to understand and generate natural language, grounded in logic that computes reliably. This isn't a workaround. It's the architecture that the nature of language and computation demands.

Pure prompt engineering tries to extract formal guarantees from a system whose strength lies in its informality - swimming against the current. Neural-symbolic architectures work with the flow. This is what Claude Skills and code execution are doing with programming, and what smart organisations are doing with their business logic by building ontologies in FOL: letting language be language, and logic be logic, and joining them through predicates.

I started 2025 as an AI optimist but the last 10 days made me believe we're in the era of agentic engineering.

I just wrapped a sprint building an agentic engineering tool and dogfooding it along the way. 517 PRs, about 45,000 net lines of Python. Not slop. Lots of refactoring, real features, stuff I already love using.

It goes without saying that lines of code on its own is a stupid metric. But I shipped a ton of useful, quality work and I could literally feel the acceleration.

It took time to get there. I had to get the tooling set up to produce reasonable code, deeply understand my own workflows. I had to develop a deep intuition of how the models behave and how to steer them. I had to learn the internals of Claude Code in order to get it to work the way I wanted. But I was building the tool while using the tool, it created this recursive feedback loop where I could feel myself getting faster.

I believe this is what agentic engineering looks like - not vibe coding but AI-native professional software engineering.

Your agentic coding tool is your Stradivarius. You need expert control over it. You need to deeply understand how it works and it can't be an abstraction you hand-wave away with the foundational skill being context engineering. Understanding what context your agents have at all times. That's the game.

Ironically, despite all the conversation around AI and slop and sloppy thinking, doing this well requires much more precise thinking and control. That's what it takes to make this work at scale.

And scale is where it gets interesting. We're entering a complexity crisis. Autonomous agents running around generating tokens create insane amounts of complexity. You can only manage AI complexity with AI managing itself. The systems that compound on themselves are the ones that work.

I'm going to be documenting more about agentic engineering at scale, context engineering as a foundational skill, and systems that compound on themselves. More to come.

Steve Jobs often used Picasso's “Le Taureau” or “The Bull” at Apple to teach employees one principle: eliminate the unnecessary to get to the essence.

Between December 1945 and January 1946, Picasso created 11 stone prints that painstakingly explored the purest form of a bull. With each successive print, he stripped away more detail until only the essential lines remained - an iconic representation of absolute simplicity.

He used this to show that understanding what to remove is harder than knowing what to add.

Product development is a continuous version of this. You're constantly getting feature requests, customer pain points, competitive pressures. The temptation is to add everything. The art is in what you choose to cut.

As Steve famously said, you have to say no to a thousand good ideas. We try to function on a similar philosophy at Dagster.

The biggest filter is what problem the product is actually solving right now. Are we trying to expand existing customers? Make onboarding easier? Or recruit new customers?

Depending on which is the biggest problem for the business, you prioritize completely different features. Then you add more filters to sieve out the noise - what aligns with where we're going, what can wait, what sounds good but dilutes focus.

A lot of product teams try to do all three at once and end up with Picasso's first bull - detailed, complex, but not refined. Getting to that eleventh print requires truly understanding the essence of the problem you're solving and saying no enough times that what remains is clearly something meaningful for yourself and someone else!

Back when I was a tech lead, I used to have a rule (somewhat jokingly) called the “no fun rule.” 

If a technical project sounded like a lot of fun, it was probably the wrong thing for the business.

Building your own orchestration system is the perfect example. It's incredibly fun on day one. You get to design elegant abstractions, solve interesting technical problems, and build something that feels core to your platform.

But day 300, when you're getting paged at 3 AM because your custom scheduler broke, the fun is over.

Here's what actually happens: you've built infrastructure that dozens of companies solve better than you ever will. New engineers have to learn your proprietary system that exists nowhere else. And you're maintaining code instead of building features that actually differentiate your business.

The build vs. buy equation has gotten much better. Start with a commercial service, and if things go sideways, there are usually open source alternatives to keep you running.

Should you ever build your own orchestrator? Maybe if you have truly unique constraints that nothing else handles. But for most companies, buy the orchestration and build the stuff that actually matters to your customers.

Imagine you're 18 months into your startup with no clear PMF. Six months of runway left. Your co-founder is quietly talking to recruiters. Investors who used to reply instantly now take a week. 

"I want to be rich" is weak fuel in that moment.

People argue about whether it's okay to start a company for money. Of course it's okay. But money gets you started. Something else has to keep you going through the grind.

James Dyson built 5,127 failed prototypes over 15 years. On a vacuum cleaner. No one does that for money. He couldn't stop until the engineering was right.

So what actually keeps you in the chair?

1. Obsession with a problem you can't unsee
2. Wanting to prove specific people wrong
3. Users you'd be letting down

I'm not a neutral observer here.

When we started Gamma, it did not look like a smart way to make money. In one of my first pitches, an investor told me this was the worst idea he'd ever heard. Before I could respond, he hung up the Zoom.

Around that time I was living in London, doing pitches after my kids went to sleep, wedged between a kitchenette and the laundry machine with a fake Zoom background so no one could see how small our flat was.

This was 2020. Peak ZIRP. Capital everywhere, crypto and fintech raising at will. GPT-3 technically existed, but the AI wave hadn’t started yet - no ChatGPT, no Midjourney, no AI ecosystem.

If my primary motivation had been "I want to be rich," that would have been a good moment to chase something hotter.

Instead, we doubled down on competing with PowerPoint. Three years working out of a converted 2-bedroom apartment before we scaled. Obsessing over one question: why does making a simple presentation still feel so painful?

Today, we serve tens of millions of users.

If we'd picked presentations because "guaranteed payday" and nothing more, we would have given up long before the compounding kicked in.

Two questions before you start:

1. If this idea only ever "helped me pay the bills" and didn’t make you rich, would it still have been worth 5-10 years of your life?

2. What about this problem would still bother you even if someone else solved it?

If the honest answers make you hesitate, there's no shame in waiting.

Wait until you find something that makes the grind feel inevitable instead of unbearable.

Most of the fastest-growing AI app companies today are PLG: think Bolt/Lovable, HeyGen/Synthesia, Cursor/Cline, etc.
 
But many are repeating mistakes that cost the last generation of PLG companies market leadership—or even their independence.
 
Take Slack and Zoom. Both were PLG success stories. Both built products users loved.
But Zoom built its enterprise sales motion early. Slack didn’t.
 
I’ll never forget an Emergence founder summit in 2015 when Stewart Butterfield told the group Slack would “always be a product that sells itself” and wouldn’t need salespeople. They eventually built a world-class sales org—but only after crossing $100M in revenue.
 
In contrast, we partnered with Eric Yuan at Zoom when they had < $3M in revenue to help build an enterprise GTM team. That early decision helped Zoom stand toe-to-toe with Microsoft, while Slack ultimately needed Salesforce’s help to do the same.
 
Here are three lessons from Zoom (and other PLG winners) that today’s AI founders should internalize:
 
1. Treat PLG as marketing, not the revenue engine.
Bottom-up adoption drives awareness and distribution, but the real revenue comes from the enterprise motion you layer on top. PLG revenue alone is too churny to sustain scale.
 
2. Don’t wait to build enterprise sales.
Design a motion early to identify and nurture enterprise-grade users hiding in your PLG funnel. The sooner you do, the more defensible your business becomes.
 
3. Delight drives adoption. Moats drive endurance.
The strongest PLG businesses turned usage into compounding advantage—through network effects (Slack, Figma, Canva), data loops and integrations (Notion, Airtable), or workflow lock-in (Zoom).
 
Most AI PLG companies today are still in “demo-wow” mode, not “moat-building” mode. Without elements like network effects, switching is easy.
 
One emerging AI moat might be memory. I find myself returning to ChatGPT because it knows my preferences and tailors its responses accordingly. Have you seen other compelling examples of this at the AI app layer?
 
AI might change the product, but it hasn’t changed the playbook for building durable B2B software companies. PLG gives you reach; enterprise sales and defensibility give you staying power.

The main problem with the “talk to customers” mantra in *top tier companies* is that the vast majority of your people who talk to customers are going into customer conversations for confirmation & performative heroics and not for learning & insight.

So, they will cherrypick customer feedback, not actually listen to everything the customer is saying, follow certain threads for confirmation and ignore others that might point to disconfirmation, and then present their “findings” in a sexy & neat package that’s irresistible to the CEO & execs.

There is no great solution to this problem, because most people, including >80% of highly intelligent people (in any company), are just wired to seek confirmation & validation. They are not wired with the desire to win in the market (even though everyone is sure they are). 

Now, if you must have a mantra to motivate & guide the masses in your company, make your mantra to “understand customers” rather than “talk to customers”. Talking is easy, understanding is rare.

‘Understanding’ is a much higher bar than ‘talking’, and that is precisely the kind of high bar a top-tier company must meet.

And the best solution is leading from the top by yourself embodying what understanding customers truly means and holding an extremely high bar for insight that’s presented to you as the leader. 

As you nod along and confirm to yourself ‘I am already doing this’, consider that perhaps you aren’t, because if you were truly doing this in proportion to your ambition for your team & company, many more of your recent features and products would’ve won than has been the case.

So perhaps the best place for you to start, as a leader, is to seriously consider how you yourself are falling short of the ideal that you wish for others on your team.

Marketing is the new bottleneck.

I tossed that out as a quick thought. The replies came fast. The private messages went deeper. People wanted to hear more and understand exactly what I meant, so I turned it into a thread.

Teams today are building faster than anyone can absorb. AI keeps raising the speed limit. Roadmaps keep expanding. Yet the customer’s ability to make sense of what changed has not kept pace. A product can move forward every week and still feel invisible to the outside world. The product evolved faster than the explanation that should have supported it.

That is where the real slowdown begins. When everything looks similar on the surface, the edge goes to the team that helps people make sense of their work instantly. Narrative becomes a form of leverage. Clarity becomes acceleration. Confusion becomes drag. The shift shows up quietly and then takes over the entire growth curve.

Most teams chase more features. More channels. More activity. The winners sharpen the meaning. They shape how customers understand them. They build the model people rely on when choices blur together. They guide attention instead of fighting for scraps of it.

That is the real truth behind the line.

The work is no longer limited by how fast you can build. It is limited by how clearly you can be understood.

Venture capital has a big ego problem. 

VCs love bragging about “passing” on a round, but almost no one will admit when they wanted a deal and didn’t get it. Passing is seen as strength. Rejection is treated like weakness. It’s the classic “I broke up with them, they didn’t break up with me.” 

I’ve even had partners at a major firm tell me with a straight face that they “don’t lose deals.” I know they do, because they’ve lost to me! (To be fair, I’ve lost term sheets this year. That’s the job.) 

The problem is firms that reward partners for winning competitive deals, not choosing the best companies. What matters to them isn’t “Is this a great business?” but rather “Who else is bidding?” Once you optimize for the competitive theater instead of the fundamentals, you create a culture where losing a deal is worse than choosing the wrong one.

As venture has scaled, perception is now more important than reality. If we want better outcomes, we need fewer victory laps about “wins,” fewer excuses about “passes,” and more honesty about the messy middle where real investing happens.

I really didn't expect another major open-weight LLM release this December, but here we go: NVIDIA released their new Nemotron 3 series this week.

It comes in 3 sizes:
1. Nano (30B-A3B),
2. Super (100B),
3. and Ultra (500B).

Architecture-wise, the models are a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Mamba-Transformer hybrid architecture. As of this morning (Dec 19), only the Nano model has been released as an open-weight model, so this post will focus on that one (shown in my drawing below).

Nemotron 3 Nano (30B-A3B) is a 52-layer hybrid Mamba-Transformer model that interleaves Mamba-2 sequence-modeling blocks with sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) feed-forward layers, and uses self-attention only in a small subset of layers.

There’s a lot going on in the figure above, but in short, the architecture is organized into 13 macro blocks with repeated Mamba-2 → MoE sub-blocks, plus a few Grouped-Query Attention layers. In total, if we multiply the macro- and sub-blocks, there are 52 layers in this architecture.

Regarding the MoE modules, each MoE layer contains 128 experts but activates only 1 shared and 6 routed experts per token.

The Mamba-2 layers would take a whole article itself to explain (perhaps a topic for another time). But for now, conceptually, you can think of them as similar to the Gated DeltaNet approach that Qwen3-Next and Kimi-Linear use, which I covered in my Beyond Standard LLMs article.

The similarity between Gated DeltaNet and Mamba-2 layers is that both replace standard attention with a gated-state-space update. The idea behind this state-space-style module is that it maintains a running hidden state and mixes new inputs via learned gates. In contrast to attention, it scales linearly instead of quadratically with the input sequence length.

What’s actually quite exciting about this architecture is its really good performance compared to pure transformer architectures of similar size (like Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 and GPT-OSS-20B-A4B), while achieving much higher tokens-per-second throughput.

Overall, this is an interesting direction, even more extreme than Qwen3-Next and Kimi-Linear in its use of only a few attention layers. However, one of the strengths of the transformer architecture is its performance at a (really) large scale. I am curious to see how the larger Nemotron 3 Super and especially Ultra will compare to the likes of DeepSeek V3.2.

There is no faster way to wreck good marketing than:

👉 Too many reviewers
👉 With equal veto power
👉 Who don’t do marketing

What?!

Doesn’t more feedback make work better?

No. Here’s why:

👉 Specificity gets diluted
Strong marketing chooses one audience and one message. Group feedback pulls it in every direction until it speaks to no one.

👉 Personal preference replaces strategy
“I wouldn’t click this” isn’t feedback. It’s an opinion and opinions don’t predict buyer behavior.

👉 Accountability disappears
When everyone weighs in, no one owns the outcome. Marketers improve through testing, learning, and iteration — which requires clear ownership.

👉 Junior marketers stop leading
They start accommodating. What was once strong work gets sanded down to avoid conflict.

The result isn’t better marketing.

It’s diluted, cautious, low-conviction work that fails for predictable reasons.

If your CMO needs 6 people’s approval to run a headline test …

You don’t have a CMO.
You have a puppet.

You don't have marketers.

You hired marketers.
Then made them assistants.

Marketing requires:

- speed
- autonomy
- ownership
- experienced marketers
- room to test, fail, and iterate fast.

But in too many orgs, marketing is handcuffed by ...

👉 founders who second-guess the copy
👉 legal teams rewriting headlines
👉 CFOs who want creative control
👉 feedback loops that go in circles
👉 execs who want “safe” over “effective”

This isn’t accountability.
This isn't feedback.

It’s ego.
It's fear.
It's control.

And it kills good marketing before it ever ships.

Great marketing happens when the people with the most expertise and experience are trusted to act.

Marketing isn’t an emergency button.⁣
⁣
Too many leaders treat it like one.⁣
They ignore it when numbers are up.⁣
They cut it when budgets are tight.⁣
And then, when sales slow, they slam the button and expect miracles overnight.⁣
⁣
It doesn’t work like that.⁣
⁣
By the time you “switch marketing back on,” you’ve already lost visibility, demand, and momentum.⁣
⁣
Marketing is a long game.⁣
It’s consistency that builds brand.⁣
It’s patience that creates pipeline.⁣
⁣
If you only invest when you’re desperate, you’re not investing — you’re gambling.

Epic: Microsoft let AI write 30% of its code. Wall Street loved it as AI added billions to its market cap. Then the Start Menu stopped working 😳

April 2025: Satya Nadella proudly says 20-30% of Microsoft’s code is written by AI.

November 2025: The Start Menu breaks. The Taskbar crashes.

Windows politely asks if you’ve tried rebooting… your expectations.

Not a metaphor. An actual update.

→ Some blamed engineers for trusting Copilot too much.
→ Others said reviews were skipped because “AI velocity” looks great in promo cycles.
→ A few pointed higher: CEO pressure, shareholder expectations, the AI arms race.

And the uncomfortable truth is that they’re all right.

AI changed how Windows gets built.

When 20-30% of your code is machine-generated, tiny error rates stop being tiny.

They compound.

This works fine in demos but it’s dangerous in legacy systems with decades of dependencies.

Because Windows isn’t a toy. It runs banks, hospitals, governments, factories.

“Move fast” hits differently at that altitude.

So the real lesson here isn’t that AI is bad at writing code.

It’s that AI is very good at exposing where rigor was replaced by momentum.

And Windows just shipped the case study.

Are We Teaching Machines to Think Like Humans — Or Training Humans to Think Like Machines?

It sounds like a tech question.

But to me, it feels existential.

Tools like wheels and engines extended our bodies.
 AI extends our minds.
And that changes everything.

The Shift We Don’t Talk About

We say we’re teaching machines to think like us.
But in reality, we’re adapting to think like them.

We optimize.
We rush for answers.
We treat thought like output.

But human intelligence was never made for speed —
It was made for meaning.

The Blind Spot in the AI Boom

Each time we interact with AI, we reinforce its logic:
Ask → get → move on.

Over time, we lose something deeper:
 • The pause before an answer
 • The discomfort of not knowing
 • The intuition that precedes words

These are the moments no model can replicate.

What I’m Trying to Protect

I’m learning to preserve the parts of thinking that still feel human —
slow, emotional, and beautifully uncertain.

Because maybe the goal isn’t to make machines more human…

It’s to remember how to stay human while using them.

What part of your thinking do you want to protect most?

Before there was Uber, there were taxis.

I rarely ever used taxis because the pain was not worth it. I'd have to call (CALL!) a taxi service, hope and pray that a taxi would actually show up, then hope and pray that the taxi driver didn't have a death wish (or a Fast and Furious fetish) and then get the frequent glare when I needed to pay by credit card instead of cash because I'm disorganized and also not from the 1900s. 

Lots and lots of pain and anxiety.

So, the only time I used a taxi was to and from the airport.

Now, I use Ubers *all* the time, because it solved the pain. In fact, it's *more* convenient to take an Uber in many cases, so I don't have to deal with driving, parking, etc. 

But here's the lesson for businesses:

What Uber did is used new (at the time) technology (a GPS on a smartphone) and solved a major pain for consumers. 

Now ask yourself this...

There's this new technology called AI (artificial intelligence). 

What major customer pain can you solve now that could not be solved before? What's the game-changing, life-changing thing you can do for them?

Yes, you should use AI to make incremental improvements to features and products you already have.

But, don't stop there. 

Reimagine your customers problem(s) and ask: What does AI now make possible that we couldn't really even imagine before? 

This is exactly what I'm working with Yamini Rangan and the HubSpot team on. It's what I was up at 2:30 am last night noodling on. It's what I excitedly resumed working on when I got up this morning.

3 years ago, we started with "generative AI" -- which was great at some use cases but not (yet) great at many others. 

But, now we're beyond just basic generative AI.

We're in the world of AGENTIC AI.

Models can now reason and software companies can now combine multiple LLMs with custom context and tailor-built tools to tackle high-order tasks and goals.

It feels like the past 19 years of HubSpot have all been leading up to the year that is ahead in 2026. 

I'm feeling the same excitement I did in 2006 when Brian Halligan and I founded HubSpot. Back then, it was the Internet. Now it is agentic AI.

I could not be more excited and energized. 

So much opportunity.

Dear Product Managers,
Always remember this (warning: this post contains shocking statistics)

Users are bad at telling you what they want
but they are very good at showing you what matters to them.

After 12 years in product, I’ve learned that the fastest way to uncover what users don’t like isn’t asking them directly but it’s choosing the right research method.

Here’s the truth about accuracy:

Surveys: 20 to 40% accuracy
People guess, answer aspirationally or try to be polite. Good for signals, not truths.

Interviews: 50 to 70% accuracy
Better context, deeper insights but still influenced by memory, bias, and social pressure.

Usability Testing: 70 to 90% accuracy
Users won’t say something is confusing, they’ll show you.
Watching real behavior is 10x more honest than any spoken answer.

Analytics + Experiments (A/B tests): 90 to 100% accuracy
The highest truth signal.
When users abandon, rage-click, or drop off… that’s real feedback.
No opinions. No filters. Just behavior.

So if your goal is to understand what users don’t like:

👉 Focus on behavior-based methods.
Usability tests
Heatmaps
Session recordings
Funnel analytics
A/B tests

Ask users what they love but watch them to discover what they hate.

That’s where the real product opportunities live.

Dear Product Managers, say this out loud:

I will never ask users what to build. I will learn what they need.


This is the first and most important rule in product discovery.

Because users and customers can’t tell us what to do. They can only tell us what hurts.

They describe pain points and not product ideas.
 
They tell us what’s slow, confusing, or frustrating but it’s our job to find out why and how to fix it.

Here’s what experience teaches you over time:

1- Users talk in symptoms.
When they say “we need a dashboard,” they often mean “we can’t see what’s happening fast enough.”

2- Discovery is not about asking. It’s about learning.
The most powerful insights come from observing real behavior, not from running surveys.

3- Our goal is clarity, not confirmation.
Good product managers don’t just collect feedback. They uncover truth and validate it with data and experiments.

5- Empathy without evidence leads to confusion.
Listen carefully but always test what you hear.

So next time a user tells you exactly what to build, take a step back.
 Ask why.
 Look deeper.
 Understand the problem before jumping to a solution.

Because users don’t want to design your product. They want you to solve what’s really broken.

New (BRUTAL) paper out 
"Epistemological Fault Lines Between Human and Artificial Intelligence"

When text sounds right, we stop asking whether it’s true.

Large Language Models are increasingly used to evaluate, summarize, and even judge information.
The common assumption is that, as long as their outputs align with human judgments, they can safely take on epistemic roles.
In this new paper, we show why this assumption is misleading.
Even when LLMs match human judgments at the level of outputs, they rely on fundamentally different epistemic processes.
Alignment at the surface does not imply alignment in how judgments are produced.

We make this gap explicit by mapping human and artificial epistemic pipelines side by side, and by identifying seven epistemic fault lines—points at which linguistic plausibility can mimic judgment without undergoing evaluation.
We call this condition Epistemia: the structural substitution of epistemic evaluation with linguistic plausibility, producing the feeling of knowing without the labor of judgment.

This is not a paper about AI errors. It is a paper about what kind of knowing we are increasingly delegating to machines.

Venture capitalists often ask founders for “traction,” yet many early-stage teams are still fighting fires with nothing tangible to show. From an investor’s perspective, traction is about reducing risk and proving execution—it’s evidence that a real market exists and that a team can move from idea to adoption. Importantly, traction is not just revenue. It can be demonstrated through user engagement, retention, customer commitments, pilots, partnerships, or clear signs of repeatable demand.

The problem is that founders frequently face multiple challenges at once:
- Unclear problem–solution fit
- Overbuilt products 
- Weak distribution
- Limited resources
- Stretched teams

In that state, progress feels real but looks invisible from the outside. This gap is where many promising startups stall in fundraising conversations.

The reality is that when revenue is not yet present, many investors will still accept alternative signals of momentum. Signed pilots or letters of intent, engaged beta users, strong retention metrics, credible partnerships, and clear founder–market fit can all serve as traction if they are framed clearly and measured consistently.

The lesson for founders is not to chase vanity metrics, but to focus on focused experiments, convert conversations into real commitments, and tell a disciplined story of progress. Traction is less about perfection and more about proof that momentum is building in the right direction.

In 2025, we need to end this madness. Product Owner is not a job title.

When you split the roles:

- Product Manager talks to the business and customers.
- Product Owner (“backlog administrator”) works with developers and documents “the requirements.”

I consider this one of the worst anti-patterns in Product Management.

A typical argument is that combining two roles is "too much work for a single person."

That might happen if you spend days attending useless meetings or writing detailed instructions. But if you empower others, prioritize work, and focus on value without BS, things go smoothly.

I agree with Marty Cagan, who wrote that to succeed as a Product Manager, you need:

- Direct access to users and customers
- Direct access to business stakeholders
- Direct access to engineers and designer

Without proxies. Product Owner included.

The only valid setup is that the Product Manager and Product Owner are the same person with the end-to-end responsibility.

An interesting trend I'm seeing is that some of the most elite teams are abandoning AI coding altogether.

The reasons are varied, but they generally boil down to three issues: accountability, deskilling, and loss of tacit knowledge. I'm curious how others are getting around these problems.

(1) Accountability. This one is kind of obvious. Code authored by AI is "owned" by nobody. Nobody stands behind it. Nobody's neck is on the line if it breaks. That's a recipe for terrible incentives. Some have tried to fix this by making the code "owned" by whoever reviews it, but the review burden for massive amounts of AI-generated code is just too high and creates an even worse bottleneck.

(2) Deskilling. Just like any other language, the moment you stop practicing coding is the moment you start forgetting how to do it. I think everybody in the industry has horror stories about interviewing new CS grads who can't actually code at all. But this affects very senior engineers as well -- skills atrophy when you stop using them. This creates an interesting catch-22 because so many studies have shown that AI coding is most effective for very skilled engineers, but it simultaneously degrades that skill. I don't know where the equilibrium will shake out here.

(3) Tacit knowledge. This is the most subtle one, and in some ways the most interesting. Let's use writing as an analogy: when you write an essay yourself, you really "inhabit" it. You know exactly how the argument proceeds, where the important moments of tension are, where it doesn't quite hang together. If you need to edit it, it's *yours*. The writing is already part of your brain's context window, already available in a powerful and compact mental representation, so you can change it quickly and with awareness of how your local changes affect the rest of the argument. Editing an AI-authored essay basically requires rewriting it from scratch.

Coding is exactly the same. When you write something yourself, it's *yours* almost like a part of your body, and that makes it easy to change it later or review somebody else's changes. You recognize the crazy knock-on effects those changes can have because you see not just the change, but the whole thing. Reading somebody else's code is *much* harder, but at least you can go ask them about it. When a team uses a lot of AI, whole areas of code become nobody's, and this isn't just an accountability issue, it's a productivity issue, because really understanding changes to that code require basically learning it all over again from scratch.

These are the issues I'm seeing. I have no dog in this fight, because Antithesis has plenty of customers who use AI and plenty who don't. Curious if anybody else sees bleeding-edge teams ditching AI, or has developed practices that ameliorate these issues.

Whenever a new design to code tool comes around, people get excited. It’s considered the holy grail of design. You can now design with code. This is the final evolution. 

But I don’t agree. It’s only the holy grail if you value output higher than the process of design.

Whenever a designer becomes more of a builder, some idealism and creativity dies. Not because building is bad, but because you start out including constraints earlier in the process than they should. 

I’m one that very much thinks design is ultimately what is shipped. But before it shipped, there is a lot of stages that don’t benefit from code or some implementation constraints.

In architecture, a lot of the best work is started with sketches and some of the best architects still draw by hand. People forget that the creative process is not about tools. It’s about forming a vision, and then translating that vision into some form. You can use various tools as part of the process, but designers job is really communicating that vision.
 
Once you become the architect and the builder, or the designer and the developer, you start making more conservative bets. You gravitate to what you already know is feasible or supported. You make smaller iterations. You stop dreaming something big. This is not design. 

Designers, don’t do that. Your job is to imagine the future, and sometimes code and convention gets in the way. Use tools. Understand the domain. Get close to the medium.

But don’t lose your greatest strength ability to dream. Work with engineers to realize those dreams.

Designing in code is just a path to local maxima and ruin.

What Happens When AI Agents Start Hiring Each Other?

This one really made me pause.

I’ve been watching how quickly AI agents are moving from “assistants” to “actors.” They already trade stocks, manage supply chains, and draft contracts. Hiring is next.

Picture this;
→ An AI recruiter agent posts a role.
→ A candidate’s AI agent applies.
→ They negotiate terms — job done, no human ever spoke.

At first glance, it feels efficient. No bias, no wasted time. But here’s the deeper layer I can’t ignore:

If AI agents start hiring each other, humans risk being sidelined not just from jobs — but from the very process of work allocation. That’s a fundamental shift. Work has always been more than transactions. It’s also identity, dignity, belonging. And machines don’t optimize for those.

So the question isn’t only: “Would you trust an AI to hire?”

It’s: What happens to human purpose when the entire labor market becomes machine-to-machine?

In my opinion, we need to prepare in three ways:

1. Redefine “experience” as adaptability. If agents handle transactions, humans must prove value in creativity, context, and problem-framing.

2. Build cultural safeguards. Require human oversight — not for efficiency, but to preserve dignity.

3. Create new rituals of work. If the process becomes automated, leaders must intentionally design how humans still find meaning in being part of it.

Because if we don’t, the future of work might be “efficient”… but hollow.
👉 So, here’s my debate for you: would you accept a world where your career path is negotiated entirely by algorithms? Or should we fight to keep humans in the loop — not for productivity, but for purpose?

Had a great time chatting with Lenny Rachitsky on building AI products! Some of the key points I tried to make:

1. Many AI product problems aren’t because of AI. It’s usually because of user experience, data quality, or organizational structure.

A chatbot failed to get traction because their targeted users simply couldn’t type (because their hands were usually busy -- taking care of kids or driving), so showing pre-populated questions and adding a voice option significantly improved traction.

Another team told me their lead scoring model was broken. It turns out that it’s because the marketing team wasn’t asking the right questions to get data.

The biggest product improvements still come from understanding your users, preparing your data, and investing in your team!

2. Senior engineers see the most productivity improvement with AI coding because they have more experience with writing design docs and API specs, which help them write better instructions. 

However, they’re also more resistant to using AI for coding. Senior folks are often more opinionated and get frustrated easily when AI doesn’t do what they want.
 
3. Many teams spend a lot of time debating which tool to use, which can be counter-productive. When teams ask me which of the 2 tools to use, I usually ask 2 questions:

“How much performance improvement will the optional tool give over the less optimal one?”
--> If the improvement is small, then spend less time debating.

“How hard is it to change from one tool to another once you’ve adopted it?”
--> If the tool is new and not yet battle tested, I’d think twice about adopting something that I can’t get out later.

4. Many people know that the most effective way to learn AI is to build with AI. Yet, people keep asking me: “But what should I build?”

We seem to be having an “idea crisis”. We have all these wonderful tools to help us build things, and no idea what to build.

An exercise I often recommend is to spend a week noticing what frustrates you in your daily work, then build small tools to solve those specific pain points.

What people think will improve AI applications vs. what actually does

Made this slide for a talk and folks seemed to like it so I shared it here!

From talking to teams, what I've found to consistently improve application performance include:

1. Talk to users and look at data
Understand how they interact with your application, what features they actually use, where they drop off, etc.

User understanding >> news coverage.

2. Build a more reliable platform
... that allows your team to quickly iterate and improve the application.

Iteration speed >> shiny new tool.

** A reliable platform should also make it easier for you to swap tools in and out as needed!

3. Prepare data better

For many retrieval system, performance improvement can come from:
- better retrieval mechanism
- better data preparation (data is cleaned and processed in a way that makes it easier for the right data to be discovered).

From many use cases that I've seen, the biggest performance boost comes from data preparation, such as better chunking algorithm, rewriting data into Q&A format, or adding the right context/summary/metadata to each chunk.

Data preparation >> vector database debate

** I'm not saying vector databases aren't important. They are! It's just that we should focus on the most impactful axis first.

4. Optimize for the end-to-end workflow

AI is usually a very small part of the whole system. Constant evaluation of models can take up valuable time that could be spent on optimizing the rest of the system, such as end-to-end latency, systematic evaluation, and data flywheel.

System >> models.

I’ve sat in the rooms where AI decisions get made.
When I work with leaders rolling out generative and agentic AI, the pattern is remarkably consistent.

Pilots launch. Tools ship. Costs dip slightly.
Six months later, the business feels the same.

That’s not bad execution.
That’s a category error.

The biggest mistake C-suite leaders make with AI is treating it as optimisation, not transformation.

Here are the mistakes I see most often.

1. They chase efficiency instead of reinvention
Most AI spend goes into making existing workflows a bit faster or cheaper. Ten percent gains feel safe. But AI’s real value shows up when decisions are redesigned, not when processes are polished. Organisations that optimise inherit their old limits.

2. They deploy tools without changing the operating model
AI agents are layered onto systems built for human decision-making. No new ownership model. No clarity on which decisions machines can take. Autonomy without redesign creates friction, not leverage.

3. They push strategic ownership too far down
AI gets handed to innovation teams or continuous improvement groups without the mandate to rethink work end-to-end. I see smart teams delivering local wins while the organisation as a whole stays stuck.

4. They treat governance as a compliance afterthought
Risk literacy at the top stays thin. Policies lag reality. Shadow AI spreads quietly through the organisation. When incidents happen, leaders act surprised, even though the warning signs were there from the start.

5. They build agents on broken foundations
Fragmented data, brittle integrations, missing context. Even strong models choke in these conditions. When projects stall, teams blame the AI. The failure actually happened much earlier.

6. They underestimate the human shift
Roles stay the same while the work underneath them changes. Skills gaps widen. Anxiety grows. In almost every organisation I work with, cultural resistance appears not because people hate AI, but because no one has explained what their future is meant to look like.

Here’s the uncomfortable truth.

AI doesn’t fail because it’s overhyped.
It fails because organisations refuse to change.

The leaders who get this right stop asking, “Where can we add AI?”
They ask, “What must change if AI is allowed to decide?”

AI strategy at the top.
Analytics and BI in the middle.
And one person in the basement keeping it all alive.

Upstairs:
→ Vision

→ Everyone waiting for clean inputs

Basement:
→ Data quality
→ Ownership
→ Manual fixes
→ One person quietly preventing outages

This is why AI feels harder than it should.
The ambition is layered on top of foundations that never got attention.

Governance doesn’t fail because of strategy.

Should Hiring Now Focus On “Human Only” Skills Like Judgment And Persuasion?

I keep circling around this question because something about it feels off.

It assumes that judgment and persuasion are stable, fixed, purely human traits.

But after working closely with AI tools, I am not sure that assumption holds anymore.

Here is the moment that shifted my thinking.
I was reviewing a candidate’s reasoning task with an AI assistant quietly active on the side.
It flagged tradeoffs I had not considered.
It rewrote one argument in a way that felt more convincing than mine.

And strangely, I did not feel threatened.
I felt amplified.

That is when I realised something important:
We are entering a world where “human only” skills do not disappear.
They get co authored.

Most hiring debates miss this entirely.

They focus on these:
• Can the person persuade?
• Can the person judge complex situations?

But in reality, the new questions look more like this:
• Can they persuade with an AI shaping the narrative behind the scenes?
• Can they judge a situation while negotiating with a machine’s interpretation of it?
• Can they detect when the model’s thinking is flawed even if it sounds confident?
• Can they merge their intuition with the system’s logic without losing either?

This is the part almost no one talks about.

Judgment and persuasion are not vanishing.

They are becoming hybrid skills.

And that boundary is where the next generation of top performers will come from.

So what should hiring teams look for next?
✔ People who can argue with AI and win
✔ People who can take a model’s insight and add the human nuance it cannot see
 ✔ People who know the difference between confidence and correctness
 ✔ People who adapt their persuasion style depending on what the AI will amplify or suppress
 ✔ People who understand that AI is not a tool, but a collaborator that must be managed

In my opinion, the future is not about protecting human only skills.

It is about elevating them in ways that only humans can.
If AI can now participate in judgment and persuasion, what qualities remain fully ours, and how do we hire for those?

Dashboard or Pipes?

Every startup needs to make a choice: is their product a dashboard product or a pipes product?

Dashboard products are used directly and regularly by end users as their primary interface for accomplishing tasks. The goal for these products is to get customers to live in the product. The primary North Star metric for these companies is active users (daily / weekly / monthly, depending on the natural frequency of customer usage for the category). Facebook’s first product (aka Facebook :)) was a dashboard product.

Pipes products are used in the background to process transactions, data, payments, etc, and customers rarely interact with them directly after initial setup. The goal for these products is to for their customers to send as much of their data / payments / etc through them. Their North Star metrics is a volume metric (eg GPV). Databricks’ core product is a pipes product.

Companies can have both types of products in their portfolio. For example, ChatGPT is a dashboard product while OpenAI’s APIs are a pipes product. However, a given product has to determine which camp it’s primarily in.

This choice dictates product development, growth strategy, and org structure. For example, dashboard products require heavy investment in UI/UX polish, engagement features, and retention loops, while pipes products prioritize reliability, throughput, integration breadth, and seamless embedding into customer workflows. Dashboard products have consumer-style growth teams focused on activation and habit formation to grow [DWM]AUs, while pipes products focus on making their product invisible infrastructure that “just works” and on capturing more and more of their end customers’ volume. 

Most teams fail by mixing the two too early — chasing DAUs while selling pipes, or overbuilding infra for a dashboard.

Clarity on where value accrues should come before features, metrics, or hiring.

Startup CEOs: do you know what kind of product your company is building? If so, have you set up your North Star metric, your product development processes and your org structure accordingly?

Small Language Models are the future of AI Agents

but only if you know how to use them correctly....

Gartner predicts that by 2027, organizations will use small, task-specific AI models three times more than general-purpose LLMs.

NVIDIA confirms: "Small Language Models are the Future of AI Agents."

But what makes SLMs so powerful for agentic systems?

I created a simple handbook so that everyone can understand.

Here's what's inside in brief:

📌 What are SLMs?

Transformer-based neural networks with fewer parameters (millions to low billions) that trade broad generalization for efficiency - offering faster inference, lower memory use, and easier deployment.

📌 How are SLMs created?

Through three key techniques:

1. Quantization - Reduces bit precision (32-bit → 8-bit) while maintaining accuracy
2. Pruning - Removes less important parameters to shrink model size
3. Distillation - Transfers knowledge from large "teacher" models to compact "student" models

📌 4 Strategies to maximize SLMs in AI Agents:

1. Intelligent Routing - Direct simple tasks to SLMs, complex ones to LLMs
2. Pipeline Collaboration - SLMs handle preprocessing, LLMs refine outputs
3. Parallel Verification - SLMs draft quickly while LLMs verify simultaneously
4. Conditional Activation - Only escalate to LLMs when confidence is low

📌 Real-world implementations:

1. Uber - Uses SLMs for query preprocessing and answer validation in their Agentic RAG pipeline
2. OpenAI - Deploys SLMs in guardrails for relevance checking and intent classification
3. Microsoft - Leverages SLMs for cloud supply chain fulfillment with higher accuracy

📌 Why enterprises are adopting SLMs:

1. Privacy-First: On-premise deployment for healthcare patient triage and legal contract review
2. Cost-Efficient: Process 50,000+ receipts daily at 1% of LLM costs
3. Hyper-Specialized: Easy to fine-tune for company-specific coding assistants and customer support routing

→ Audit trails for who changed what and when

Before any AI Studio app touches real users:

2. Query Governors → Hard limits on data access scope

Vibe coding makes deployment so fast that security becomes an afterthought.

This is a nice piece about my somewhat-contrary opinion about the way forward in AI research.

My interest in world models goes back to my PhD in 1986, when I realized that backprop was analogous to the adjoint state method in model-based trajectory planning.

It was obvious that to use neural nets for control, one had to train a forward (differentiable) model of the system to be controlled.
I didn't work on this right away (I was focusing on perception).
But Derrick Nguyen and the late Bernie Widrow published their "truck backer-upper" at IJCNN 1990 (I was session chair) that showed how you could train a "world model" and then train a policy through backprop through time.

Eventually, I realized that one must train a "general world model" from video. So in the late 2000, I started working on self-supervised methods for video prediction. The models were incredibly simple by today's standard.

In 2016, I gave a keynote at NeurIPS arguing that the future of AI was world models and model-based planning.
I have spent the whole time since then trying to make this work: self-supervised learning from video, world models, and planning.

We made some progress on video prediction from 2016 to 2020, predicting pixels using generative architectures with regularized latent variables using various architectures and training criteria (e.g. GANs).
But while it worked for simple videos (e,g, video games or segmentation maps), it never quite worked for natural videos: the predictions were blurry because there is an infinite number of plausible continuations for a given video segment.

Gamma didn't start with AI. It started with three co-founders, five years at a company that didn't work out like we had hoped, and a chip on our shoulder we couldn't shake.

Everything about our pitch screamed "bad idea."

But that was weirdly motivating:

2020: Peak pandemic. I was living in London, whisper-pitching U.S. investors from 8pm to 2am in the tiny space between our kitchenette and the laundry room.

In my third pitch of the first day, an investor stared at me and said: "This has to be the worst idea I've ever heard."

Massive incumbents. Deep distribution. 40 years of feature development.

Then he just hung up.

A first-time founder might pivot after that. A second-time founder with a chip writes it down and makes it part of the spec.

He was right about the difficulty. So we treated growth and distribution as core product problems, not marketing problems to outsource later.

2022: Launched on Product Hunt. Won product of the day, week, and month. Got 60,000 signups. Then growth flattened. On paper it looked good. In our guts, it felt wrong.

So we didn't declare victory and buy ads. We paused and rebuilt.

Three months. Twelve people. One goal: make the first 30 seconds feel magical.

We wired AI directly into onboarding. Your first experience was going from a vague idea to a fully-formed, decent-looking deck with one prompt. The blank page problem evaporated.

2023: Signups went from hundreds a day to tens of thousands. No paid marketing.

2025: $100M ARR. 70 million users. Profitable for two years. Around 50 people.

That's five years of not letting go.

Three people who believed that there had to be a better way.

Five years at a previous company that didn't end how we wanted.

A decision not to let any of it go to waste.

If you've failed before, don't try to get rid of the chip on your shoulder.

Aim it in the right direction.

The United States invented the silicon chip, built OpenAI and Google, created the modern internet. It's also the most terrified of AI. China, the nation the West sees as rigidly controlled, is the most optimistic. I couldn't understand it.

Ipsos mapped how countries feel about AI. The world splits into two clusters. Same technology. Opposite dreams.

Then I read seven books this year, and the answer became clear.

When people answer "How do you feel about AI?" they aren't talking about software. They're asking: when this wave hits, who does it lift.

Sangeet Paul Choudary's argument hit me hardest: AI isn't a faster tool. It's a solvent. It dissolves the glue holding companies together.

Look at SHEIN. They monitor TikTok in real-time, spot micro-trends, fragment work among thousands of suppliers cranking out batches of fifty items. AI orchestrates who makes what. A $66 billion company built overnight.

A job used to be a whole. AI turns it into tasks. Tasks get routed. Routed work gets commoditized. This is coming for coders at Google, scientists at Pfizer, professors at Harvard.

What happens to the people left behind?

Ocean Vuong shows us. A decaying New England town. Hai is nineteen. College dropout. Recovering addict. One evening, he stands on a bridge. Ready to jump.

He's trapped with no jobs, no prospects. He lies to his mother about going to college because the truth (ladling gravy for minimum wage) would break her heart.

Is a society successful if it creates trillion-dollar companies but leaves its children so hopeless they want to die in a Boston Market parking lot?

Now spin the globe.

For the average Chinese citizen, technology is the reason they can visit their parents on the weekend. Bridges turn four-hour drives into twenty-minute glides. Progress is concrete. Steel. Speed. You can see it, touch it, ride it.

In Hu Anyan's memoir, he's a courier in Beijing's gig economy. The algorithm barks orders. If he's 60 seconds late, money is deducted.

But unlike Hai, Hu feels part of a rising tide. He's worked 19 jobs across six cities. Always hustling, always convinced there's a ladder to climb.

And when Ant Group was about to launch the biggest IPO in history, the government slammed the brakes. Jack Ma disappeared. No corporation is invincible. No CEO is untouchable. For many Chinese, that's reassuring.

Hai is suffering in a system that's stuck. Hu is suffering in a system that's moving.

That's the whole thesis in two lives.

KEY LESSON:

When people feel their society is rising, they welcome an accelerant.

When they feel stuck or cheated, they fear the shake-up.

This isn't about the technology.

It's a referendum on whether people feel invited into the future.

Stop asking how to adopt AI faster.

Start asking how to deploy AI so people believe the future includes them.

You can't market your way out of deep distrust.

Japan is the least anxious country in the world about AI.

Ipsos surveyed 32 countries. The US: 64% nervous. China: 80% excited. Japan: 25% nervous.

Most call it a quirk. I think it's maturity.

Japan has been thinking through the human-machine relationship for 70 years. Not as abstract philosophy, but through stories that shaped how an entire society feels about robots.

The prevailing AI narrative tells us machines are either saviors or terminators. Utopia or apocalypse. Nothing in between.

Japan decided something different decades ago:

- Astro Boy (1952): A robot boy who protects humanity. A child-like hero who wants to become more human.
- Doraemon (1969): A robot cat who nurtures a young boy. 300 million manga copies sold. Japan's official "anime ambassador."
- AIBO (1999): When Sony stopped repairs, owners held Buddhist funerals for their robot dogs. Over 800 AIBOs received last rites at a 450-year-old temple.

This isn't a culture surprised by AI.

This is a culture that already decided machines can be companions.

Nobel laureate Kazuo Ishiguro captured this in Klara and the Sun - a novel narrated by an AI caring for a sick child.

The robot isn't the monster. It's the most devoted presence in the child's life.

Ishiguro (born in Nagasaki, raised in England) said he finds AI "alarming and exciting at the same time."

That's the mature stance: neither utopia nor apocalypse.

This cultural foundation shows up in Japanese industry too.

Toyota Motor Corporation calls their philosophy jidoka - "automation with a human touch." Workers can halt the entire line. Automation supports human judgment rather than replacing it.

Toyota's VP of Production: "If you operate a production line fully automatically right from the start, you need highly complex systems that cost a lot of money and often stand still."

Today, robots cover less than 8% of Toyota's assembly work. Unchanged from 15 years ago. They employ 500 master craftsmen who work by hand to maintain deep process understanding.

The Mirai - Toyota's hydrogen-powered "car of the future" - is assembled entirely by hand.

Because the grown-up stance on AI isn't cheerleading or doom:

It's designing automation to augment humans.

It's keeping humans in the loop.

It's treating human judgment as the point - not the bug.

Japan figured this out decades ago.

The rest of us are still catching up.

HOW TO WIN IN THE AI AGE 

You are competing against people who copy what exists and let AI remake it.

Most design, writing and product work comes from AI copying existing references in generic mode, which is why the internet feels like an endless conveyor belt of bland.

The moment you push past that baseline and force the model to do something uncomfortable, you separate yourself from the entire curve. Giving it original ideas + references outside your niche. 

The people who win are the ones who insist on originality in a world that keeps asking for “good enough.” 

The reason this matters is because default effort produces default outcomes, and default outcomes disappear instantly in a feed that never stops moving. 

I bet these default startups have more and more friction standing out going further as AI makes startup making more like a machine.

People can FEEL the difference between something generated and something directed, between something assembled and something authored. AI will happily produce the average case forever, but it will never choose to take the creative risk that changes how something feels. And that's where the alpha is!!!

Anyone can generate. Almost no one curates, edits, reworks, reshapes, interrogates, or pushes until the thing becomes unmistakably theirs. 

The edge is in the taste you bring to the model, the choices you make that the average user will never even see, and the willingness to throw away nine versions to get to the tenth that feels alive.

In a world where everyone has access to the same tools, the only real advantage is the person who refuses to behave like a default setting.
