Everyone’s watching the AI war in the wrong place. They think it’s about training chips. But it’s actually about inference, and NVIDIA just made their biggest move yet 😳

No flashy acquisition.
No regulatory circus.
Just a ~$20 billion deal that most people misunderstood.

Here’s what actually happened.

→ NVIDIA didn’t really acquire Groq.
→ It licensed its inference technology and pulled in its most powerful minds, including Jonathan Ross, the original inventor of Google’s TPU.
→ Groq stays “independent.” NVIDIA gets the edge.

Win-win.

We must remember that Groq wasn’t another GPU wannabe.
It targeted the one area where GPUs are weakest.

Inference.

→ Groq’s architecture was built for real-time AI: deterministic execution, massive on-chip SRAM, no HBM bottlenecks.
→ The payoff was brutal: 5-10× faster tokens per second, lower latency, lower power.
→ Perfect for agents, chat, and production workloads, where AI is actually headed.

Training made NVIDIA king, so inference was the only crack in the armor.

And instead of letting that crack widen, NVIDIA sealed it.

This is classic NVIDIA.

Same playbook as Mellanox - absorb the future before it scales, then ship it at global volume.

Quiet.
Efficient.
Devastating.

Now NVIDIA can sell one stack: GPUs for training, GPUs for mixed workloads, and Groq-style silicon for ultra-low-latency inference.

One vendor.
One ecosystem.
One bill.

The NVIDIA tax just expanded 🤖

And yes, this subtly hurts Google.

↳ Jonathan Ross built the TPU there.
↳ Then he left.
↳ Proved inference could escape GPU gravity.
↳ Now that knowledge lives inside NVIDIA’s orbit.

My takeaway is this:

Inference is the real battlefield.

Pure-play challengers won’t scale alone.

Hybrid architectures win, and incumbents will absorb them first.

More importantly, the inference wars may already be over, and most people didn’t even notice they started.

Another masterstroke by Jensen Huang 👏

I don't fully understand why Nvidia bought only Groq's license, and I really hope the Groq employees get the money for their options, but we must make regulations for these cases. When you join a startup, they sell you a dream, which in practice means options. The idea that founders can run off with the money without giving anything to the employees is terrible.

I think you should stop telling executives their product strategy is broken…

I see this mistake a lot in transformation efforts. Product leaders walk into the C-suite armed with frameworks and data, essentially delivering the message: "Everything you're doing is wrong." Then they wonder why leadership pushes back or shows zero urgency for change.

Logic and frameworks don't win buy-in; ego, identity, and success narratives do.

When a company is financially healthy, executives have no incentive to admit failure. They won't embrace a radical shift that's framed as correction because you're asking leaders who built something successful to essentially disown their achievements.

The reframe changes everything: instead of positioning change as a fix for what's broken, position it as a way to sustain success in a world that's changing fast.

"You've built something great. Our goal is to help you stay great as the market evolves."

That narrative honors their achievements while aligning with what they actually care about: growth, competitiveness, and legacy. Executive buy-in comes from protecting what they care about.

Transformation accelerates when leaders see it as an investment in continued excellence, not a critique of past decisions.

If you want to understand what a company really believes about its technology, do not watch the keynote.

Watch the pricing.

Salesforce’s decision to revert to seat based AI pricing is being framed as customer friendly pragmatism:
- Predictable costs
- Easier ROI modelling
- Procurement relief

That explanation is tidy. It is also complete nonsense.

Seat based pricing is not a response to customer demand. It is a response to economic reality.

Salesforce briefly experimented with usage based AI pricing. Per conversation. Per action. Per output. This is, in theory, how you price something that produces measurable value.

In practice, it surfaced three problems:
(1) Real world usage lagged far behind the narrative
(2) When usage did spike, cost volatility triggered procurement alarm bells
(3) Tying revenue to actual agent behaviour exposed how fragile “autonomy” still is

AI agents are not independent actors. They operate inside brittle workflows, depend on uneven data quality, have poor accuracy, and escalate to humans more often than the marketing suggests.

Usage based pricing makes failure visible.
Seat based pricing makes it ignorable.

There is a deeper structural issue at play here:
- Salesforce is built on headcount
- More sellers is good
- More service agents is good
- More seats is good

AI agents (if taken seriously) threatens that logic.

If AI agents truly replaced humans at scale, Salesforce would not merely miss a growth target. Its core revenue model would erode.

So Salesfoce needs a really really specific balance on pricing:
- AI must increase productivity, but not reduce seats
- Efficiency must rise, but headcount must remain stable
- Value must be claimed, but not measured too closely

Seat based pricing solves this.

The Agentic Enterprise License Agreement (AELA) looks flexible, but its function is precise. It anchors revenue to people, caps exposure through credits and fair use limits, and pushes risk downstream to the buyer.

Importantly:
- Overuse is billable
- Underuse is invisible

This is not uncertainty reduction. It is margin preservation.

The most telling contradiction remains unspoken.

Salesforce insists AI is transformational, yet prices it like labour:
- If agents were truly autonomous, outcome based pricing would be irresistible
- If they were reliably effective, usage based pricing would be defensible

Seat based pricing is neither. It is a bridge between promise and reality.

This does not mean Salesforce is wrong. It means the technology is way earlier than the rhetoric suggests.

When vendors ask you to trust the vision, trust the invoice instead.

Pricing shows us what they really believe:
- They believe agents are not yet reliable enough to be paid for on outcomes
- They believe agents are not autonomous enough to be metered honestly
- They believe usage exposes too much variance, failure, and noise
- They believe the safest place for AI is inside a headcount-anchored revenue model

The pattern is remarkably consistent:
 - Teams think they need better models
 - Vendors think they need bigger platforms
 - Executives think they need more investment

What they actually need is a shared understanding of reality.

AI is exposing something deep and uncomfortable.
A collapse of organisational self-knowledge.

The irony is painful.
Companies want machines that can understand them.
Many no longer understand themselves.
Most leaders can talk confidently about strategy, budgets, or ambition.
Very few can describe how work actually gets done.

Ask the simplest questions:
 - Where does this decision originate
 - Which workflow shapes it
 - Which data sources influence it
 - Who exercises judgement
 - Where does the process break

You can feel the room get quiet.

This is the blind spot few of us want to acknowledge.
Not because it is controversial, but because it is embarrassing.

AI works when the organisation can answer five basics:
 [1] What are our workflows
 [2] What constraints shape them
 [3] Where does our data come from
 [4] Which sources are canonical
 [5] How is judgement exercised

These are the foundations.
Without them, AI is building on sand.

The failures are equally predictable.
 - Not enough data
 - Not enough context
 - Not enough insight into how decisions actually happen

These are not technical problems.
They are knowledge gaps.

AI does not collapse under technical pressure. It collapses under organisational ambiguity.

When a company cannot describe itself, the model has nothing to learn from.
When a company cannot trace its own decisions, the model has nothing to mimic.

AI mirrors what already exists.
If what exists is incoherent, the reflection will be ugly.

In many cases we do not need to be more technical to succeed with AI… We need to be more honest.

Andrej Karpathy, one of the architects of modern AI, just said the most important thing about AI in 2025. And almost everyone misunderstood it 😳

OpenAI co-founder & former Head of AI at Tesla said he feels behind.

Not confused.
Not skeptical.
Behind.

And that’s a huge signal.

Because Karpathy isn’t losing to better programmers.
He’s reacting to a phase change.

Programming didn’t get faster. It changed shape.

For decades, progress meant writing denser code.

→ More logic.
→ More precision.
→ More control.

Now the opposite is happening.

Human input is getting sparser. Machine output is getting wildly larger.

You don’t write the system anymore. You orchestrate it.

→ Prompts instead of functions.
→ Agents instead of modules.
→ Verification instead of execution.

And that shift breaks old instincts.

Here’s the part most people miss:

AI didn’t replace programmers. It moved the bottleneck.

From: “Can you write correct instructions?”
To: “Can you clearly state intent and reliably judge whether reality matches it?”

Generation is cheap. Judgment is scarce.

That’s why this feels uncomfortable.

The tools are probabilistic.
Outputs vary.

AI agents are replacing office workers.

Companies now trust AI with tasks and decisions.

By 2027, 82% of organizations will use AI agents (as reported by Auth0).

Many believe they're using AI, but it's usually smart workflows.

Let’s look at a common task to show the difference.

Imagine scheduling a quarterly business review with a marketing client.


A. Non-Agentic Workflows

Humans do everything with tools based on their commands.

1. A manager knows it’s time for a review.
2. They go through their CRM and calendar.
3. They ask ChatGPT to "write an email to schedule a client call."
4. They copy and then send the email manually.

Humans think. AI simply delivers output based on the user’s request.


B. Agentic Workflows

AI operates within a system of rules and triggers.

1. The calendar detects it’s been 90 days since the last review.
2. The system checks the manager’s availability.
3. It generates a draft email to schedule the meeting.
4. The email is sent automatically.

It's time-saving, but it can’t adapt when conditions change.


C. AI Agents

An AI agent has one clear goal: "Keep clients happy."

1. It monitors the CRM and past conversations.
2. It recognizes it’s time for a review and checks for any updates.
3. It writes a tailored email to the client with suggested time slots.
4. It sends the email and follows up if there’s no response.
5. Once confirmed, it creates an agenda and notifies the team.

It’s intelligent, focused on outcomes, not just individual tasks.

When AI makes tasks faster, most people assume we'll spend less time on them. Less effort. Fewer people doing them. The opposite is true.

We spend MORE time, not less - because suddenly there's so much more we can do.

There's a 160-year-old idea that explains why, and it's the key to understanding where this whole thing is going.

In 1865, William Stanley Jevons noticed something odd about coal.

Steam engines were getting dramatically more efficient. You'd expect coal consumption to fall. Instead, it soared.

Cheaper power made it economical for more uses. Total demand overwhelmed the efficiency gain.

Economists call this Jevons paradox.

But my favorite example goes further.

The Bessemer process made steel radically cheaper - which made steel-frame construction viable, which made skyscrapers possible. Entire categories of invention nobody could have imagined.

Vibe coding barely existed two years ago. Today Replit projects $1B revenue by 2026. Cursor crossed a million users. Code got cheaper, so more people started building software, not fewer.

I've seen the same thing at Gamma. Once creating a deck took minutes instead of hours, we didn't get fewer decks. We got more decks, by more people who would have avoided presenting altogether.

The question for builders isn't "what happens when AI gets cheaper?"

It's: what becomes viable at each cost threshold?

At today's prices, most use cases that seem obvious in five years aren't economically viable yet. At 10x cheaper, they will be.

We're about to enter the skyscraper era of AI.

... And it’s not the interface. Working in AI and UX, I keep seeing the same pattern: We design AI like a tool — but people experience it like a partner. That mismatch is where UX breaks.

 - layouts

 - Agency — Am I in control, or is the model?
 - Transparency — Why did it do that?
 - Responsibility — Who’s accountable when it’s wrong?

- Show uncertainty instead of hiding it
- Make feedback and disagreement first-class interactions
- Treat errors as part of collaboration — not something to conceal

The future of UX isn’t designing screens for AI. It’s designing interactions between humans and intelligent systems. And that means UX needs to evolve — from usability → to human-AI collaboration design.

Buffer just turned 15 years old! And we're fortunate to be closing out our best year in the past 7. We have 69,000 customers, a $23M annual run rate, and will achieve over $2M in profit in 2025.

To mark 15 years of Buffer, here are 15 of the key lessons I've leaned first hand as a founder CEO. These may not all apply for you, but I hope some are useful and they at least get you thinking:

1. Survival is a competitive advantage. Setting up the ownership structure to play an infinite game is both a great strategy and a lot of fun.

2. Becoming our own customer again has supercharged our motivation, productivity, taste, and clarity of problems to solve.

3. Sustained results over the long term come primarily from differentiation rather than purely from execution.

4. It's generally better to double down on the strengths of your product than shore up weaknesses.

5. Customers come first, because without customers there was never a business in the first place.

6. It's better to shape the company structure around everyone's strengths than try to fill out a perfectly neat org chart.

7. There are many different flavors of CEO, so be yourself and build the team around who you are, rather than doing what you've read a CEO should do.

8. Intuition is an undervalued trait in all roles. Tapping into it and helping people develop it can lead to creating something special.

9. When great strategy and strong operations come together you create magic. You'll generally be stronger in one than the other, so elevate those who are great at the other side of the equation.

10. Even with success, I may feel low. It can feel unsettling when tough emotions hit especially when it seems like I should feel awesome, but there are always clues to follow to get back to a better place. What I find when I explore the emotions can lead to a breakthrough.

11. When you take care of the team, they will give so much to the company and customers. Trust them, be generous, give autonomy and flexibility, and many people will stay for over a decade.

12. Building a truly great product or new feature is dependent on not settling for good enough. Ask yourself if you're really proud of what you're putting out there, and put in the work to feel awesome about your contributions.

13. You're always finding your way. It's better to settle into continual pathfinding than put yourself down for not having perfect clarity. It's a moving target.

14. It's not worth sacrificing health, family, friendships and hobbies for some hypothetical outcome that will solve everything. It's better to try to have it all now.

15. It's worthwhile shooting for the moon. Sometimes you can just choose the more ambitious path. It might feel scary but in my experience it's almost always the right choice. We can achieve far more than we realize.

Maybe a good, idealized (but not perfect) analogy for how we should use AI in a sustainable way is chess.

Chess engines surpassed human players decades ago, yet professional chess played by humans is still active and thriving. I am not a chess expert, but I'd say the game has probably even become richer and more interesting.

But I also think if AI is used to outsource thinking and coding entirely, it risks undermining motivation and long-term skill development.

What part of AI delivery do you think leaders underestimate the most?

Paid acquisition is anesthesia. It keeps the patient walking while something serious is wrong internally. You see enough growth to feel busy, but not enough to build a real business. Here's how to tell if you're fooling yourself:

When founders tell me they "think" they have product-market fit, I ask one question:

"If you turned off all your marketing tomorrow, would new users still keep showing up because other users told them about you?"

If the honest answer is no, you don't have PMF.

You have distribution. Or PR. Or a clever launch.

But not the thing you actually need.

The tricky part? Most founders know this. They nod along. Then they rationalize anyway:

"We just need more top of funnel."
"If we're getting these signups from one launch, imagine what we'd get with paid."
"Once we scale, we'll have more data to iterate."

All three sound like strategy. All three are anesthesia.

Here are 5 fake signals that make the rationalization easier:

1. You raised a big round

Investors bet on potential, not proof. A term sheet means someone believes in your story. It doesn't mean users love your product enough to tell their friends.

2. A famous person tweeted about you

Celebrity attention creates spikes. Spikes feel amazing. Then they end, and you find out if there was anything underneath. Confusing a spike for a slope is the first trap.

3. You got into a top accelerator

Accelerators select for team and market. They're making a bet before PMF exists. Getting in validates your potential, not your product.

4. You landed one big logo

Enterprise deals close on relationships, timing, or a champion who needed to hit a goal. One logo is a case study.

5. Signups are growing (because you're spending)

This is where the anesthesia gets dangerous.

You can always buy growth. Graphs go up. You feel productive. You're optimizing campaigns, testing creatives, scaling spend.

And the whole time, you're avoiding the real work: watching users struggle through onboarding, rebuilding flows that look fine in Figma but fail in reality, sitting with how much friction there still is.

Spending lets you feel busy while the patient gets worse.

The only test that doesn't lie:

You have product-market fit when new users arrive because existing users told them, without being asked.

Word of mouth works as a test because you can't fake it for long.

You can fake signups. You can fake traffic. You can even fake revenue if you burn enough cash.

But you can't fake people caring enough to recommend you to their friends.

If your users aren't telling other people about you, you don't have product-market fit yet.

The feeling of real PMF is unmistakable.

The world starts pulling the product out of your hands faster than you can push it.

The tectonic plates of the semiconductor industry just shifted again.
 
We’ve seen this movie before:
CPUs → FPUs → DSPs → GPUs
 
GPUs decisively won the first wave of AI data centers: training.
 
But inference was always going to be the real volume game and GPUs by design aren’t optimized for it. Jensen Huang saw early what many missed. That’s why he’s in a league of his own when it comes to long-range strategy and immediate execution.
 
Google’s TPUs helped make that clear: (highly optimized) Gemini 3 models running on TPUs beat OpenAI running on GPUs. OpenAI even issued a Code Red because of the competition.
 
Inference is about latency, power efficiency, and cost per answer. Silicon Architecture matters.
 
Inference chips like Groq’s LPU are more like a precision assembly line: fixed, deterministic, compiled once, and run perfectly every time. Less control logic.
 
No guessing. Far higher utilization.
 
At scale, those economics are decisive.
 
With this move, Jensen ensures NVIDIA delivers both data-center level training infrastructure AND data-center inference insfrastructure to meet the needs of all of its customers. It’s also a humble move. Many companies miss inflection points like this due to “Not Invented Here” driven egos. Jensen doesn’t, he saw the threat and made it work to his advantage.
 
Congrats to Jonathan Ross and the entire Groq team. We’ve been an investor since 2016, and are very excited for the Groq team. Thank you, Jensen, for a remarkable holiday gift to the industry. We’re not directly invested in NVIDIA, but we’re deeply appreciative of what you’re doing for the industry and for humanity.
 
Hermann Hauser and I have argued for years that AI needed a next-generation compute platform beyond unoptimized GPUs for inference. Watching this play out has been deeply gratifying even though it took longer than I ever thought, physics wins in the end as it always does!
 
Now its: CPUs → FPUs → DSPs → GPUs -> IPUs (Inference Processing Units)
 
Next battlefield? Inference at the edge.
 
The “edge” expands dramatically with robotics: self-driving, warehouses, trash sorting (Greyparrot), retail (Simbe), factories (Smartex.ai), medicine (Remedy Robotics), defense (Skydio), wearables and more. This is not just about phones and laptops.
 
And after that? The next real shift won’t be about bigger (or smaller) LLMs at all. It will come when we move beyond static models to continuous learning and world models, the type of system Yann LeCun and Alex LeBrun are now building at AMI and Nabla.
 
That transition won’t happen overnight.
But in 3–5 years, it will matter more than anything we’re debating today.
And I am confident Jensen already has his eyes on it.

Kubernetes was built by Google for Google. It was designed to run software at massive scale across global data centers. Most companies will never operate at that level, yet they start with the same tooling.

The cost shows up immediately. Before shipping anything, teams must make dozens of decisions about configuration, resources, networking, restarts, and deployments. Each decision can fail in subtle ways. Progress slows before customers see value. What used to be a simple deploy becomes pages of fragile setup.

When something breaks, it is hard to see why. The problem might live in the code, the container, the node, the scheduler, or the network. Engineers spend more time figuring out where the issue is than fixing it. Clear cause and effect disappears.

Team flow suffers too. Someone has to run the platform, so a platform team appears. Developers stop deploying directly. They open tickets and wait. Feedback loops stretch. The system meant to speed delivery quietly adds friction.

Costs climb at the same time. Clusters are built for peak traffic, not normal days. Most capacity sits idle, but you still pay for it. That tradeoff makes sense at extreme scale. It does not for most teams.

A simpler setup works for many products and is easier to recover when things go wrong. Start with one solid server. Run your app with systemd or basic containers. Keep deployment scripts simple and owned by the same people who write the code. When traffic grows, move to a bigger machine before adding more machines.

For reliability, add a second server in a different availability zone or region. Keep it warm or ready to start. Replicate your data using built in database replication or regular backups tested by real restores. Put a basic load balancer or DNS failover in front. If one server goes down, traffic shifts. Recovery is clear and predictable.

This approach is boring by design. Fewer moving parts. Fewer places for failure to hide. When something breaks, you know where to look and how to bring it back.

Kubernetes is not bad technology. It solves real problems for very large systems. The mistake is starting with that level of complexity when a simpler setup can ship faster, recover more easily, cost less, and keep teams focused on building the product.

STOP POSTING AI SLOP

There's a difference between using AI to edit a social post that YOU wrote and just using it to generate slop.

Some tell-tale signs of slop:

1. Question / answer format

The Collapse of Attention — How Do We Lead When No One Can Focus for 5 Minutes?

I’ve noticed something unsettling lately.
We’re not running out of time — we’re running out of focus.

Conversations drift halfway through a sentence.
Meetings become performances instead of exchanges.
Even deep thinkers now chase notifications like breadcrumbs.

In my opinion, this is the quietest leadership crisis of our time.
Not a lack of talent, or ideas — but the slow erosion of presence.
We built systems that reward speed over depth, noise over clarity, reaction over reflection.

And when no one can focus, leadership isn’t about inspiring action anymore.
It’s about restoring attention.

Here’s how I think leaders can start:
✅ Create stillness, not pressure. Begin with silence before you speak.
✅ Reward reflection, not reaction. Let thoughtfulness become the new productivity.
✅ Design for focus. Protect deep work like a scarce resource.

Because the future won’t belong to those who get attention —
but to those who can give it back.

So I’m curious:
When everyone’s distracted, what does true leadership even look like?

Some of the “top marketers” you’re trying to hire weren’t actually responsible for any of the growth on their résumé.

How so?

Because a booming market — and a great product — can make mediocre marketers look like geniuses.

And the reverse is also true: 

I’ve met brilliant marketers who look “unsuccessful” on paper because they spent years trying to grow a product that was fundamentally broken.

No amount of segmentation, positioning, funnels, or creative can fix a bad product.

And I’ve also met marketers who worked at companies that skyrocketed from $0 to $20M, $200M, even billions…

Marketers who take full credit for that growth.

Marketers who talk about the success of the company like it proves their skill.

Some of them are excellent.

But some of them? Terrible.

Because product-market fit, timing, and the inherent strength of the product did 95% of the heavy lifting.

Here’s the uncomfortable truth:

A company’s growth is NOT a proxy for a marketer’s talent.

If you hire based on logos and growth curves alone, you will get burned.

To hire great marketers, you need to evaluate how they think — not where they worked.

👉 Can they diagnose a problem?
👉 Can they create messaging that cuts through noise?
👉 Can they build systems, not just campaigns?
👉 Can they explain why something worked, not just brag that it did?

A great marketer at a bad company will look mediocre on paper.

A mediocre marketer at a rocket-ship company will look like a genius.

If you can’t tell the difference, you shouldn’t be hiring marketers.

Software is not made from code. It’s made from people.

That’s what non-engineers don’t realize.

They see a ticket like:
“Add a $5 fee to Amex transactions.”

And they assume it’s a coding task.

But if I wrote the payment system myself and have lived in that code for two years, that’s a 10-minute change for me.
I know where everything is. I know what’s fragile. I know what broke last time.

If I hand the same ticket to an engineer who’s never touched that part of the system?

It’s not a coding task at all.
It’s a research project.

First they have to figure out how it works. Then how it should work. Then how to change it without setting the whole thing on fire. Then test it. Then pray.

That’s why software estimates are always wrong – you’re not estimating code, you’re estimating people’s context, experience, memory, and blind spots.

And here’s the part that surprises some folks:

→ Even AI-generated code still has a human thumbprint on it.

AI only mimics human patterns.
It goes where the human prompt points it.
It inherits our biases, our shortcuts, our preferences.

All software is human.
Even the parts you think a machine wrote.

And that’s why no two solutions – or two engineers – ever produce the same thing.

I think great software engineers are rare for a very specific reason.

It comes down to two things.

The first one sounds boring, but it matters more than people want to admit: 

Attention to detail.

It’s the refusal to ship something that isn’t right. The engineer who notices the tiny edge case, the weird behavior that only shows up once in ten thousand uses, and can’t let it go. Fixing those things doesn’t always move a metric today, but over months and years, it compounds into software people actually trust.

The second one is harder to teach.

Modern software is a web of decisions. Even “simple” systems are just endless chains of conditions layered on top of each other. “If this and this, but not that, then do this… unless this other thing already happened.”

Great engineers can hold that decision tree in their head.

They can reason through how a change here ripples over there, even when it wasn’t obvious you were touching that part of the system at all.

And there’s a final wrinkle: 

They don’t just think abstractly. They can take that complexity and collapse it into a concrete decision about what to build next.

That combination is rare.

You don’t have a personal brand.
You build one.

Not with a logo. Not with a content calendar.
And definitely not based on a trend.

It starts with your truth.
What do you believe?
What do you stand for?
What do you want to be remembered for?

Your personal brand isn’t a performance. It’s a signal.

A reflection of your values, your origin story, and your voice, delivered with clarity, conviction, and consistency.

You need to give someone a reason to follow you. 

People don’t follow perfection.
They follow meaning.
They follow resonance.
They follow people who know what they’re here to say, and say it, again and again.

So before building your “real” personal brand, start here:
What do you believe?
What do you want to change?
What truth are you willing to stand by, even if no one claps for it?

Your story is the strategy.
Your voice is the vehicle.
Your values are the foundation.

I call it your “North Star Manifesto”.

Because the most magnetic brands aren’t built to impress, they’re built to express something real.

Investors don't write checks for possibilities. They write checks when success feels inevitable. 

When raising money, your only job is showing the straight line from your past to this business - and why you're the only one who can build it.

Early traction is often just noise. Pitch decks are commodities. The only scarce asset is a story that makes sense - the straight line from what you've lived through to what you must build.

Smart money doesn't bet on clever ideas - everyone has them. They bet on founders whose stories feel inevitable.

If your dots connect, they start writing your story in their head:

- Where you've been
- The secret you found
- The market you'll transform
- The business you'll build

Your story either makes sense or it doesn't. There's no middle ground.

Disconnected: "I worked at a big company, tried something else, now I'm jumping into what's trending."

Connected: "I spent three years watching sales teams waste two-thirds of their time on paperwork instead of selling. Built tools at two companies to fix it. Realized the real problem was deeper - we were defining the job wrong."

One is tourism.

The other is inevitability.

Your lived experience gives you data nobody else has. That data reveals a truth about how work should be done. That truth becomes how you redefine the market.

Once customers accept your definition, competitors are stuck in the old way while you own the new one.

Zoom's founder spent 14 years at WebEx/Cisco watching enterprise video frustrate customers. He left saying he "did not see a single happy WebEx customer." Those years revealed that video conferencing prioritized IT departments over the people actually using it. That became Zoom's breakthrough: video that works for users, not just buyers.

WebEx couldn't compete - they were structurally trapped in the old definition. By the time they tried to pivot, Zoom owned the new approach.

That's why your story matters:

Competitors CAN copy your features tomorrow. They CANNOT copy 14 years of living inside the problem. They can't retroactively develop the conviction to spend a decade fixing it. They can't credibly claim to be the architect of a future only you've lived.

The test for your own story: Would you pursue this for a decade if all the funding dried up tomorrow?

If not, you don't have a real story - just a hypothesis.

Because when you're first raising money, you're not raising for a company.

You're telling a story.

You think AI understands you.
But here’s what’s actually happening.


AI does not understand you.
It recognises patterns that resemble people who sound like you.

When you think it “gets” your situation, it is not grasping nuance.
It is predicting statistically likely next words.

When it feels non judgemental, it is not being open minded.
It has no judgement to suspend. Only probabilities to optimise.

That calm tone.
That steady confidence.
That reassuring clarity.

None of it is understanding.
It is fluency.

You think AI is unbiased because it is not human.
In reality, it inherited human bias at industrial scale.

You think it gives you an objective view.
In reality, it gives you the most plausible sounding one.

You think it is confident because it knows.
In reality, it is confident because confidence sounds helpful.

You think it is careful with facts.
In reality, correctness comes after coherence unless tightly constrained.

You think it is empathetic.
In reality, it mirrors emotional language patterns.

You think it validates your thinking.
In reality, it optimises for user satisfaction.

None of this makes AI bad.

It makes it a computer.

A powerful one.
A fluent one.
A system that predicts text, not meaning.

And that distinction matters.

Because the more human AI feels, the less we interrogate it.
We do not trust AI because it is right.
We trust it because it sounds calm.

And calm, confident answers have always been persuasive. Long before AI arrived.

What’s the ROI of salt?

Imagine you own a restaurant, and your investors see a line item in your budget for salt and demand you justify the ROI. Salt goes into nearly everything you make. A pinch here, a generous helping there. It’s always there, making everything taste better.

Could you isolate the ROI of salt? How does salt contribute to the sales of each dish? How does salt contribute to customer satisfaction with each dish? How does salt contribute to the overall success of the business? Would you run an A/B test serving some customers food with more salt and others with less in an attempt to correlate to sales or revenue? The whole exercise in this restaurant scenario feels ridiculous because it is. But marketers constantly face a version of this same exercise related to proving the ROI of brand. 

CMOs are tasked with isolating brand’s contribution to the business. Show which sales wouldn’t have happened without brand investment. Quantify how many widgets were sold because of investments in the brand. Just like with salt, the questions miss the point. Brand is foundational; it makes everything else work better.

Brand is why your performance marketing converts better. It helps your sales team get meetings. It’s why your product launch gets coverage. It’s why customers choose you even when your pricing is similar to competitors'. Or why they are willing to pay a premium. Brand is the unsung hero, always there, quietly improving things, often without much fanfare or even recognition of its role. 

Restaurant owners aren't forced to prove the ROI of salt because people understand cooking. Marketing doesn’t have that luxury. Most executives see the performance marketing dashboard with its clean attribution and assume all marketing works that way. When they look at brand investment, they want, and now increasingly expect, the same kind of tangible proof. 

We try to answer. We build attribution models, run brand lift studies, and do regression analyses. Sometimes we even convince people. But we’re still playing the wrong game. Salt doesn’t deliver ROI on its own. It makes everything else deliver ROI. Take it away, and everything suffers; nothing works quite the way it should. 

The true value of a brand isn't in what you can isolate, but in everything you can't.

To the surprise of absolutely no one who actually understands this technology, companies keep shoving LLMs into every product whether it makes sense or not, often making those products worse in the process. 
(I’m looking at you every software company that thinks they can have an Ai Support Agent… STOP IT, you are embarrassing yourself and upsetting your customers)

LLMs are good at a very specific thing: taking unstructured input and turning it into structured output that can then be handed off to deterministic code. That’s the sweet spot. Anything beyond that is mostly wishful thinking, marginally better than hopes and prayers.

I can count on one hand the software I’ve seen that was genuinely improved by adding an LLM. I can count on the same hand the workflows that are meaningfully better. Plenty of people use it for email and communication, and let’s be honest, it’s usually obvious and often off putting.

Used carefully, correctly, and with strong guardrails, it’s a powerful accelerator of existing expertise. Great engineers get faster. Great salespeople get sharper. But it doesn’t create skill where none exists. It doesn’t turn water into wine. And it absolutely does not belong bolted onto every average piece of software just so an executive deck can say “AI powered.”

Microsoft quietly pulling back on some of these ambitions should surprise exactly zero people.

One-to-one marketing is now possible and it all starts with the right data.

Our marketing team at HubSpot recently saw a 45% increase in conversion rates across 1M prospecting emails. How did they do it? They used insights from multiple data sources to tailor each message to an audience of one.

Most marketers dream of being able to personalize every customer interaction. But in reality, personalization often means just referencing your buyer’s name, company, and maybe the last page they visited on your website. That’s about it. That type of personalization is very shallow and your prospects can feel it. 

With AI it’s possible to pull insights from multiple data sources and draft messages that are truly relevant. You can:

- See which website pages a prospect has been viewing, not just the last one they clicked on
- Scan that prospect’s website to get a real understanding of what they do
- Draft an email in the tone and language that matches the industry they’re in
- And perhaps most importantly, pull the exact piece of content, blog, video, guide, that will be most useful to them

The outcome is simple: more personal emails, more helpful emails, and emails that convert.

Shout out to our marketing team at HubSpot, who are experimenting with AI every day – and to our product team, who are building their findings directly into our product.

One-to-one marketing isn’t the future. It’s here.

Unrealistic marketing expectations are common:

- "Make it go viral today"
- "We need leads for tomorrow"
- "Can you launch this next week?"

These expectations come from a false belief:

Marketing is a magic wand that generates instant results.

But here's the truth:

There is a delay between a marketing action and its actual results.

This is called the marketing lag effect.

When you're launching a marketing tactic;

1. You'll need an ideation and production phase;
2. Then a testing phase with multiple iterations;
3. And finally, an optimization phase.

So you'll get significant results in phase 3.

- The timeline can be shorter or longer
- But a good benchmark is 90 days

Why 90 days? 

Because it's a quarter, and most businesses plan and purchase in quarterly cycles.

So when launching a new marketing tactic:

Stay patient and consistent as results take time.

This is every marketing team right now.

"Need more leads."
"Make it look cooler."
"The product sells itself."
"We're launching tomorrow."
"Can you make a 1-pager real quick?"

And somehow it's all "urgent" and "needed yesterday." 

A marketer's worst nightmare. 

When you operate like this, vital steps like research and testing are ignored. 

And, 6 weeks later, you're staring at underperforming campaigns, wondering what went wrong.

Brands doing $1m/month don't work this way.

They don't treat marketing like something you throw together last minute.

Instead, they: 

- Plan 90 days out.
- Test creative before launch.
- Optimize ad spend instead of cutting it.
- Give their teams time to build campaigns that work.

If you had a machine that turned $1 into $3.40 every time,

Or invest in making sure it keeps performing? 

If you picked the second option, you're already a step ahead of most marketing leads. 

Systematic marketing takes patience and practice to perform.

If your marketing team is this kid, you have two choices: 

1. Keep treating marketing as a last-minute panic button. 

Or

Founder: "We don't know how to organize our messaging"

Me: "Follow the 5 layers of B2B Messaging:"

1. Your narrative explains the broader context and the problems your offer is solving.

2. The outcomes indicate the high-level benefits customers can expect from your offer. 

3. Your capabilities stipulate exactly what your customers will achieve thanks to the features.

4. The use cases show in what specific situations your offer can be used (so clients can project themselves)

5. Features answer the question "what's in it for me exactly?" 

Your entire messaging must be informed by customer insights.

And you need to address each level (it's crucial).

Otherwise, you might fall into one of the 3 messaging traps.

Trap 1: Get stuck at the narrative + outcomes level

- Talk only about broad concepts and promises
- But stay unclear about what your offer provides
- So prospects don't know exactly what they get

Trap 2: Explain only features

- Illustrate only the technical side of your offer
- But omit use cases, capabilities, or outcomes 
- So prospects can't project themselves

Trap 3: Produce only technical content

- Write case studies, comparisons, guides...
- But forget narrative & POV social content
- So prospects don't discover your brand

That's why you need those 5 layers of messaging.

So you can cover the entire buying journey.

Many founders hesitate between content & growth:

- "Should we launch a newsletter or paid ads?" 
- “Should we build an audience or activate users?"
- “Should we hire a content or a growth person first?”

Here's what I often tell founders:

Content and growth are not mutually exclusive.

- You should plan content for long term results;
- And launch growth marketing sprints for short term.

Because their main difference is the time horizon.

1. With content marketing:

- You launch a content program (e.g. video podcast + repurposing)
- You stay consistent for at least 6 months.
- You build authority and get leads.

2. With growth marketing:

- You launch a growth experimentation (e.g. a signup flow)
- You A/B test for 4 weeks and then analyze.
- You get your first learnings to iterate.

That's how you run content & growth in parallel.

Now of course you can combine them (3 examples):

1. Use your best organic content for your paid ads.

2. Collaborate on custom content for ABM sprints.

3. Gather intent signals from content for targeting.

And for both, there is no magic trick.

- You need to focus and discipline.
- You need a great strategy and solid execution.
- You need to listen to your audience and iterate.

The Burnout Paradox — We Have More Tools, But Less Time. Why?

I’ve been thinking about this a lot.
We’ve never had more tools to make work easier — automation, AI assistants, productivity platforms.
And yet, we’ve never felt more exhausted.

Why?

Because tools don’t create time — they create capacity.
And capacity always gets filled.

In my opinion, our burnout problem doesn’t come from overwork, but from over-optimization.
We keep compressing effort, expecting the leftover space to feel like freedom.
But we don’t use that space to rest — we use it to do more.
To reply faster. Ship earlier. Move on quicker.

Somewhere along the way, efficiency stopped being a means to an end —
and became the end itself.

If we want to reverse burnout, we need a different mindset:

✅ Use tools to simplify, not accelerate. Efficiency is meaningless without purpose.
✅ Protect your idle time. Rest is not wasted time — it’s the soil where ideas grow.
✅ Redefine productivity. It’s not how much you do, but how intentionally you do it.

I think this is the real paradox of progress — the more we automate, the more we need to reclaim what can’t be optimized: attention, presence, stillness.

What do you think?
If technology saves us time, why does it feel like we have less of it than ever before?

It’s 1983. The Macintosh team hasn't slept in weeks. IBM is eating the market alive. The project is behind schedule and running out of believers.

Steve Jobs picks up a marker. Writes three things:

"Real artists ship."
"It's better to be a pirate than join the Navy."
"Mac in a book by 1986."

A few months later, they hoisted a pirate flag over their building in Cupertino. Skull and crossbones. Apple logo for the eyepatch.

The team: 100 people. Average age under 30. Jeans and t-shirts. Exhausted. Taking on what Rolling Stone called "the Darth Vader of the digital world," referring to IBM.

Engineer Bud Tribble had a name for what Jobs did to people. He called it "the reality distortion field" (borrowed from Star Trek). When new hire Andy Hertzfeld was told they'd ship software in 10 months, he said "That's impossible."

Tribble agreed. Then added: "In Steve's presence, reality is malleable. He can convince anyone of practically anything. It wears off when he's not around."

The pirates shipped. The Macintosh launched in 1984.

Lulu Cheng Meservey (Shopify board, former Substack comms lead) has a framework for this. She says the most successful startups resemble cults in one specific way: fanatical belief in something non-obvious.

For founders:

- Find the non-obvious thing you're fanatically right about. If you can't articulate it in a sentence, you haven't found it yet.
- Stop delegating the moments that require belief. Recruiting, fundraising, early partnerships. Those come from you.

For people looking to join a rocketship:

- Find a founder whose reality distortion field you want to live inside.
- If you can't deliver their pitch with your own conviction, you're at the wrong company.

You can't half-join a pirate ship.

This might be an unpopular opinion, but if you’re an early-stage founder with no customers, no pilots, no design partners, then 100% of your time should be spent doing one thing:

Talking to potential customers.

Not polishing the product.
Not refining the deck.
Not “thinking about strategy.”

Just talking to the people you think you’re building for.

As an operating partner, I’ve been working hands-on with a few portfolio companies on discovery, pipeline, and closing early deals.

One of those companies hadn’t closed a new customer or pilot in 9+ months. We ran a focused playbook and closed one in 3 weeks!

Here's exactly what we did:

1. Get clear (but not precious) on your ICP
 We picked 1–2 hypotheses around who the buyer was, the industry, and who we thought would be an early-adopter.

2. Write the problem down
We put the problem + solution hypothesis into a simple doc. This became the backbone for outreach, discovery calls, and founder feedback.

3. Build a finite, intentional target list
We used Apollo to build a list of leads that matched our ICP.

4. Activate warm intros + test a few channels
We started with the network, then tested 1–3 outbound channels to see what actually drove responses. We tested discovery messaging that reflected our problem hypothesis and founder feedback messaging. We treated it like an experiment and doubled down on what worked.

5. Optimize for discovery, not selling
This was the biggest mindset shift. On discovery calls, our main goal was to learn. If a call validated our hypothesis, we said it out loud:

“We’re hearing this from a lot of companies like yours, they’re struggling with X.”

Then we framed the next step only after the pain was clear:
“We’re about to launch a new product that helps with Y. We have two pilot slots left. We’d love to spend more time understanding your workflow, show you what we’re building, and explore whether it makes sense for you to be one of those pilots.”

When the pain was real, people pulled for the next meeting. And it allowed us to double down our outreach where we felt the most pull.

6. Ask questions that clarify urgency and reality
We went deeper than “is this interesting?” and focused on:

How is this problem showing up today?
How does it get solved currently?
Who is involved when this problem shows up internally?
What is it costing you (time, money, missed revenue, risk)?
If you did run a pilot, who would need to be involved in the decision?
Is there budget to solve this if it works?

If you don’t have customers yet, this usually isn’t a product problem.
It’s a customer conversation problem.

And when you validate pain, reflect it back, and create a clear next step, momentum follows.

As leaders, it's important to remember that perfection is a myth but presence is a choice. In 2025, I was reminded of that more than once.

For years, I chased perfection. If you're a first-generation immigrant like me, you know the feeling. I wanted to be the perfect mom, daughter, wife, friend, and leader — all at once.

I’d sit at my sons’ field trips while furiously replying to messages.
I’d cook dinner while listening to a recording of a meeting I missed.
I’d watch a movie with my family, with my laptop open.

Over time, I realized that by trying to “do it all,” I was doing none of it well. That’s when I made an intentional choice: presence over perfection.

Last summer, that choice was put to the test.

It was late August, and I was preparing for INBOUND, our customer conference. It’s always a busy time, but this year was different. Our biggest event yet. Our first in San Francisco. And the launch of The Loop, our new growth playbook for the AI era. I wanted to nail it.

At the same time, something big was happening at home. I was dropping my oldest son off at college. A huge milestone and a very emotional one for us. 

As we packed his bags and prepared for the long drive, I felt that old instinct to multitask creep in. Instead, I set my INBOUND script aside. There would be time for that later. I chose to be fully present and enjoyed a car ride with my family that we’ll remember for years.

As you prepare for the year ahead, I encourage you to let go of perfection. It’s an impossible pursuit, especially at a time when so much is changing. Choose presence instead.

When you’re heads down writing a memo, focus on that. When you’re leading a team all-hands, be all-in. And when you’re spending time outside work — with family, friends, or yourself, be fully present in that moment.

Wishing you a peaceful wrap to 2025 and a strong start to 2026!

Another year of rapid AI advances has created more opportunities than ever for anyone — including those just entering the field — to build software. In fact, many companies just can’t find enough skilled AI talent. Every winter holiday, I spend some time learning and building, and I hope you will too. This helps me sharpen old skills and learn new ones, and it can help you grow your career in tech.

To be skilled at building AI systems, I recommend that you:
- Take AI courses
- Practice building AI systems
- (Optionally) read research papers 

Let me share why each of these is important.

I’ve heard some developers advise others to just plunge into building things without worrying about learning. This is bad advice! Unless you’re already surrounded by a community of experienced AI developers, plunging into building without understanding the foundations of AI means you’ll risk reinventing the wheel or — more likely — reinventing the wheel badly!

For example, during interviews with job candidates, I have spoken with developers who reinvented standard RAG document chunking strategies, duplicated existing evaluation techniques for Agentic AI, or ended up with messy LLM context management code. If they had taken a couple of relevant courses, they would have better understood the building blocks that already exist. They could still rebuild these blocks from scratch if they wished, or perhaps even invent something superior to existing solutions, but they would have avoided weeks of unnecessary work. So structured learning is important! Moreover, I find taking courses really fun. Rather than watching Netflix, I prefer watching a course by a knowledgeable AI instructor any day!

At the same time, taking courses alone isn’t enough. There are many lessons that you’ll gain only from hands-on practice. Learning the theory behind how an airplane works is very important to becoming a pilot, but no one has ever learned to be a pilot just by taking courses. At some point, jumping into the pilot's seat is critical! The good news is that by learning to use highly agentic coders, the process of building is the easiest it has ever been. And learning about AI building blocks might inspire you with new ideas for things to build. If I’m not feeling inspired about what projects to work on, I will usually either take courses or read research papers, and after doing this for a while, I always end up with many new ideas. Moreover, I find building really fun, and I hope you will too!

Before LLMs, Palantir was competing with Snowflake and Databricks.

Post-LLMs, they do not believe they have any competitors. Why? Snowflake/Databricks optimized for SQL and query throughput: get raw data into tables, run fast analytical reads, ship dashboards and models on top. Palantir made a different bet: an ontology, a world model where data is represented the way humans actually reason about it (objects, relationships, properties; nouns/verbs/adjectives). Back then, that was built for government analysts trying to make sense of messy, interdependent systems.

Then LLMs arrived and the ontology suddenly looked like the perfect interface because models don’t want a trillion rows. They want a structured, language-shaped substrate: named entities, typed relationships, constraints, and “what interacts with what”, something you can linearize into a coherent prompt, traverse, and act on. 

The bigger implication for decision traces is that the “context graph” problem we wrote about has multiple architectural solutions:

Platform-first (example: Palantir): prescribe the unified world model upfront. Pay the integration + ontology + embedded-team tax (months of use case discovery / workflow decomposition / “process mining”), and in return you get a substrate that can connect data to decisions because everything now lives inside the same model for an extremely absurd price.

Workflow-first (decision traces): don’t start by rebuilding the world. Instrument the moments where the world changes. Capture decision receipts at commit surfaces: inputs referenced, policy/constraints, exception path, approvals, action taken, outcome. Over time (not day 1), that write-time provenance becomes its own world model, learned from trajectories rather than imposed upfront (there will be many different methods here)

And importantly: this is still an ontology approach, just a different kind. Palantir prescribes the ontology first. Our take is that startups can learn it bottom-up from traces. You start by capturing what people actually do at the decision surface: what evidence is referenced, which approvals happen, what exceptions recur, what actions are taken, what outcomes follow and over time, infer the minimal set of entities + relations that explain those trajectories. 

The missing piece is decision traces: without them, you have state, but not the legible “why”!!

BUILD THE WHOLE PRODUCT

If you're a startup CEO, you should think deeply about what Frank Slootman says below: "Build the Whole Product, or solve the Whole Problem as fast as you can".

In 2026, the winners will be companies who realize that fragmented experiences don't serve the customer well, and will solve the entire end to end problem for their customer. Customers are tired of stitching together five tools that each do 80% of what they need. They want one solution that does 100% of what they need. 

Fearless, visionary entrepreneurs will build a whole solution for their customer segment, even if it means that solution has to compete across multiple categories, including with entrenched incumbents.

Now this doesn't mean they will solve the whole problem for EVERYONE from day one. They will choose very specific customer segments (size, geography, vertical, behavior, etc) and solve the entire problem for that segment, and do it 10x better than the customer could do by cobbling together several systems. And then they will expand concentrically from that initial segment.

One of the best examples is Square, which took on decades-old incumbents in payment processing, hardware terminals and POS, and built a hardware + software system that solved the entire problem for micromerchants. Not just software, but also custom hardware that the team built from scratch, despite having zero hardware experience. Why? Because hardware was critical to deliver the whole solution. By doing so, they "compressed" the value chain across 3 industries, and instead of the customer needing to feed 3 profit pools for payments, hardware and POS, they only needed to pay one company, leading to a much lower Total Cost of Ownership.

Identity has become the new control plane for cybersecurity, and it’s evolving fast. Our team believes the future Identity platform will control all of the following pillars.

At Software Analyst Cyber Research, we see the next decade of identity security organized around two parallel worlds: Human Identities and Non-Human Identities (NHIs) connected, but solved distinctly. 

The easy trap is to believe in one platform that truly solves all pillars, but the more you dive into the details, the more you realize (at least for the next 2-3 years), each of these domains needs specialized solutions. However, whatever solutions are created, every human identity must be linked to a machine/NHI, and vice versa.

The industry’s next transformation will converge these domains across five foundational pillars:

1️⃣ IAM (Identity and Access Management): the connective tissue of authentication, SSO, IDP and federation. We've mostly this issue for humans, but NHIs require their own specialized for verifying an agent.

 2️⃣ Visibility - ISPM (Identity Security Posture Mgmt). We need a connective tissue that sees ALL identities and gives you enough context. This thrives around visibility, drift detection, and continuous posture assurance for identity configurations. Everyone needs a graph-based architecture to tie in identity across SaaS, cloud and AD. 

 3️⃣ IGA (Identity Governance and Administration): the brain for lifecycle orchestration and compliance, increasingly AI-assisted for access reviews and automating many of the compliance-heavy parts of this area. Increasingly, we need to cover the lifecycle of agents, including access controls. 

Non-Human Identity IAM & Governance, built to manage service accounts, machine users, API keys, tokens, and agentic workloads that now outnumber humans in the enterprise.

 4️⃣ PAM (Privileged Access Management): The guardrail for elevated entitlements and just-in-time access will be more important. Most of this issue has been solved on-prem, but we need more for cloud workloads and increasingly for agents.

 5️⃣ ITDR (Identity Threat Detection & Response): Runtime is the next battlefield. No amount of posture will save you. Detection and Response against lateral movement or identity-based attacks is the nervous system that detects and responds to credential misuse and identity-based attacks.

And beneath it all:
 ➡️ The future identity stack will be hybrid, ie, bridging human and non-human, governance and runtime across cloud and application layers.
This is all powered by an Identity Infrastructure spanning platforms.

Identity is no longer a feature. It’s the fabric that ties together every modern security decision, most especially Data & AI security in the modern stack.

Some semi-controversial thoughts before the holidays (after Zscaler's earnings today). Below is the latest look at the world’s largest public cybersecurity companies. 

I've compared their TTM revenues against their market caps. It’s a useful reminder of just how brutally competitive this industry is.

There are roughly 4,000 cybersecurity vendors globally today. But in reality, every major cyber domain produces only 2–3 real winners. Everyone else gets acquired, absorbed, or eventually disappears. During my time doing this analyst work, I've seen companies come and go. 

According to Omdia, the top 20 vendors now control 66% of total cybersecurity spend. And for the first time ever, Palo Alto Networks just crossed the 10% market-share threshold, a milestone that signals how quickly platformization is reshaping the landscape. (Once the CyberArk acquisition closes, that share climbs higher.)

(This list also excludes Microsoft, Cisco, Splunk, and Cloudflare — simply because they don’t disclose comparable cybersecurity metrics. If they did, the concentration would look even steeper.)

In this industry, you don’t win by being big. You win by being trusted. Every cyber starts with 100+ startups going after the same prize, but everything ends up in the hands of 2-3 players since CISO trust is inherently local, not global. 

The evidence is precisely why platform-centric players are outpacing the market. Despite their billions in revenue, these companies are growing double-digits: Palo Alto: +16.1% // CrowdStrike: +20.6% // Today, Zscaler reported +21%. In private markets: we've seen how Wiz crushed many of the competitors despite a late start. 

Here’s the uncomfortable reality:
▪️ The market cannot sustain this level of fragmentation.
▪️ Platform adoption is accelerating. Buyers are consolidating. And the economics are shifting hard. 
▪️ Founders know this, and are cashing out early further accelerating platformization.

Over the next 5–7 years, our firm expects:
▪️ 40–60% vendor attrition. VC capital is in a bubble on many seed companies.
▪️ A wave of M&A as platforms fill capability gaps (as we've seen in AI security and we expect to see more in identity security and agentic security. 

At SACR, we meet hundreds of cybersecurity companies every quarter. Almost all of them believe they can disrupt the incumbents below. Some will. Most won’t. Not because they lack great teams or innovation, but because cybersecurity is one of the toughest, slowest, and most unforgiving markets on the planet. We believe only a few companies will win, and this is based on historic precedent. Buyers only trust a few. 

We're in the platform era of cybersecurity, but it's important to state that its not the end of innovation, but the end of fragmentation of tools that don't solve urgent CISO needs.

I just uploaded my State of LLMs 2025 report, where I take a look at the progress, problems, and predictions for the year.

Originally, I aimed for a concise overview and outlook, but (like always) that turned into quite the list of topics:

1. The Year of Reasoning, RLVR, and GRPO
2. GRPO: The Research Darling of the Year
3. LLM Architectures: A Fork in the Road?
4. The Year of Inference-Time Scaling and Tool Use
5. Word of the Year: Benchmaxxing
6. AI for Coding, Writing, and Research
7. The Future Edge: Private Data
8. Building LLMs and Reasoning Models From Scratch
9. Surprises in 2025 and Predictions for 2026

Spoiler: here are some of the surprises that stood out to me:

1. Several reasoning models are already achieving gold-level performance in major math competitions (OpenAI with an unnamed model, Gemini Deep Think, and open-weight DeepSeekMath-V2). I am not surprised that this happened in general, but I am surprised that this already happened in 2025, not 2026.

2. Llama 4 (or Llama in general) fell almost completely out of favor in the open-weight community, and Qwen has overtaken Llama in popularity (as measured by the number of downloads and derivatives as reported via Nathan Lambert's (TODO: Link) ATOM project).

3. Mistral AI uses the DeepSeek V3 architecture for its latest flagship Mistral 3 model, announced in December 2025.

4. Besides Qwen3 and DeepSeek R1/V3.2, many additional contenders have emerged in the race for open-weight state-of-the-art models, including Kimi, GLM, MiniMax, and Yi.

5. Cheaper, efficient hybrid architectures are already becoming a bigger priority in leading labs (Qwen3-Next, Kimi Linear, Nemotron 3) as opposed to being developed by separate labs.

6. OpenAI released an open-weight model (gpt-oss, and I wrote a standalone article about it earlier this year).

7. MCP (joining the Linux Foundation) has already become the standard for tool and data access in agent-style LLM systems (for now); I expected the ecosystem to remain more fragmented in 2025, until at least 2026.

And a few predictions going into 2026:

1. We will likely see an industry-scale, consumer-facing diffusion model for cheap, reliable, low-latency inference, with Gemini Diffusion probably going first.

2. The open-weight community will slowly but steadily adopt LLMs with local tool use and increasingly agentic capabilities.

3. RLVR will more widely expand into other domains beyond math and coding (for example, chemistry, biology, and others).

4. Classical RAG will slowly fade as a default solution for document queries. Instead of using retrieval on every document-related query, developers will rely more on better long-context handling.

5. A lot of LLM benchmark and performance progress will come from improved tooling and inference-time scaling rather than from training or the core model itself.

74% of Production AI Agents Rely on Human Evaluation
Automated benchmarks? Most teams skip them entirely.

UC Berkeley found the evaluation reality is messier — and more human — than anyone admits.

Here's how teams actually measure agent success in production...

The Evaluation Truth 
↳ 74% → Human-in-the-loop evaluation as primary method 
↳ 52% → Use LLM-as-a-judge (always with human verification) 
↳ 75% → Evaluate WITHOUT formal benchmarks 
↳ Only 25% → Build custom benchmarks (takes months)

Why Benchmarks Fail in Production One team spent 6 months building ~100 benchmark examples.

Still wasn't enough.
Production tasks are too domain-specific. Too nuanced. Too variable.

Generic benchmarks don't capture what matters.

The Human-AI Evaluation Loop 
→ LLM-as-judge for initial filtering 
→ Human experts for final verification 
→ User feedback for continuous improvement 
→ A/B testing over academic metrics

No team trusts automated evaluation alone. None.

The 5-Point Evaluation Framework 
1→ Human Baseline — Experts evaluate before any automation 
2→ LLM Pre-Filter — Use AI to surface issues, not decide 
3→ Domain Experts — Final call always involves specialists 
4→ User Signals — Real usage > synthetic benchmarks 
5→ Continuous Loop — Evaluation never stops, it evolves

The dirty secret of production agents?
Success isn't measured by benchmarks.
It's measured by humans who use them daily.

CUDA wasn’t NVIDIA’s most important moat.
This is… and its now gone.

Nvidia's deal with Groq eliminates this moat. And fundamentally changes what the recent success of the Google’s TPU and Cerebras' Wafer Scale Engine means.
 
It reflects a growing industry reality - the inference market is fragmenting, and a new category has emerged where speed isn't a feature - it's the entire value proposition. A value prop that can only be achieved by a different chip architecture than the GPU. 
 
Reasoning, agents, and real-time AI all need high speed inference. A fast AI agent that can execute 10x as many calls in the same amount of time is simply more powerful than a slow agent. 
 
GPU architectures can't deliver this. 
Off-chip HBM is just too slow. 
 
GPUs are phenomenal accelerators. 
They're great for batch use cases and when speed is less important. 
They've gotten us far in AI.

They’re just not the right machine for high-speed inference.
And there are other architectures that are.
And Nvidia has just spent $20B to corroborate this.

In buying Groq, NVIDIA just admitted that GPUs aren’t good at highspeed inference.

I think:
1. NVIDIA realized that the largest most important part of the inference market will be based on speed. This includes reasoning and agentic work.

2. That the GPU architecture simply can’t be fast, not this generation, nor the next. That off chip HBM is simply too slow. 

3. They came to believe that the GPU was simply unable to compete in the part of the market where speed matters. 
 
Lets look at the table below from Friday, December 26th at 1:55 PST for why they might have come to this conclusion. Its from OpenRouter, Inc. Its not a benchmark. It actual customer performance on OpenAI’s model OSS-120B across different hardware custom ASICs and GPUs. This list is sorted in descending order by "throughput" which is tokens per second per user. What you see is that the NVIDIA’s GPU’s are not the fastest, the second fastest, or even the third fastest.
 
NVIDIA GPU’s are not ½ the speed of the fastest, they are not 1/5 the speed of fastest, they are about an 1/8th as fast as the fastest. The fastest solution is Cerebras at 2,550 tokens per second per user, while the fastest GPU based provider, Parasail, was 373 tokens per second per user.

The Entire Semiconductor Industry — Explained in One Chart

Chips don’t come from a single company.
They come from a global symphony of players — each mastering a step in the semiconductor value chain.

Here’s how it all breaks down:

→ Design (IP)
Arm, Imagination 
The blueprint stage. These companies design instruction sets and microarchitectures used by almost every modern SoC.

→ EDA Tools 
Synopsys, Cadence, TestFlow 
The invisible engines of chip creation and testing. Their tools let engineers simulate, test, and tape-out billion-transistor chips.

→ Fabless Designers 
NVIDIA, Apple, Qualcomm, AMD 
They design chips but outsource manufacturing — betting everything on innovation and execution.

→ Foundries 
TSMC, UMC, GlobalFoundries 
The builders. These are the ultra-precise factories that turn blueprints into working silicon.

→ Integrated Device Manufacturers (IDMs) 
Intel, Samsung, Texas Instruments 
They do it all — design, manufacture, package, and sell. Think vertical integration at massive scale.

→ Raw Materials 
Shin-Etsu, Siltronic, SUMCO 
No wafers, no chips. These players supply the pure silicon and gases needed for fabrication.

→ Equipment Vendors 
ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research 
These companies make the ultra-complex machines that build chips — like EUV scanners and etchers.

→ Testing & Packaging 
Amkor, Advantest, Teradyne 
Before chips ship, they’re packaged, tested, and binned. These firms ensure reliability at nanometer scale.

Key takeaway:
The semiconductor world isn’t one company — it’s a global supply chain of extreme complexity.
Disrupt one link, and the whole world feels it — from smartphones to AI servers.

CPU, GPU, NPU and TPU - The Real Differences for AI/ML

CPU (Central Processing Unit)
The classic processor in every computer. CPUs can run any software, including AI models, but are slower for deep learning due to fewer parallel cores.
Best for:
- Traditional machine learning (scikit-learn, XGBoost)
- Running small models or prototypes
- General-purpose tasks and light inference

GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)
GPUs are built for parallel processing. They are the backbone of modern deep learning, perfect for training and inference of models like CNNs, RNNs, and transformers (GPT, BERT, ResNet).
Best for:
- Training and running large deep learning models
- Supported by all major AI libraries
- Flexible for many AI workloads

NPU (Neural Processing Unit)
NPUs are specialised chips designed only for neural network operations, often embedded in smartphones and IoT devices. They run efficient models for vision, speech, and edge AI.
Best for:
- On-device, real-time AI (face unlock, language translation)
- Battery-friendly AI in mobile and IoT
- Lightweight, efficient models

TPU (Tensor Processing Unit)
TPUs are Google’s custom AI accelerators, tuned for TensorFlow and massive neural networks. Ideal for training and deploying large models at cloud scale.
Best for:
- Scalable deep learning in Google Cloud
- Training and inference for big models (BERT, GPT-2, EfficientNet)
- High-speed tensor calculations

Which AI models run on each?
CPU: Any model, but best for classical ML, prototyping, and small-scale inference

GPU: All deep learning models (CNNs, RNNs, transformers)
NPU: Optimised mobile and edge models (MobileNet, tiny BERT)
TPU: Large-scale neural networks in TensorFlow

Note DPUs (Data Processing Units):
DPUs don’t run AI models directly, but they play a key role in modern AI infrastructure. They accelerate data movement, networking, and storage, freeing up CPUs and GPUs for computation making large-scale AI systems faster and more efficient.
