2 of the 3 tech unicorns I've worked at train their AEs on 2 core concepts that salespeople don't typically learn: 
 
 
--> Towards v. Away language 
--> Above the Line (ATL) v. Below the Line (BTL) 
 
 
1) Towards v. Away language 
 
-> 80% of people are motivated by moving Away from Pain (more people would run from a German Shepherd than towards a Gold medal*) 
 
-> But sales reps talk about Gain, aka moving Towards: "time back in your day", efficiency & ROI 
 
 
Here's how it sounds IRL: 
 
-> Towards sounds like, "Because my product automatically detects buying signals like hiring, promotions, and champion job changes...your sales reps will save 29% of their time and you'll see a 18x ROI." 
 
-> Away sounds like, "What goes away [by using my product] is sales rep's spending hours on manual research, low morale from not seeing results, and the frustration of your team missing their number." 
 
 
->>>TL;DR = talk about what problems go away 
 
 
 
 
2) Above the Line (ATL) v. Below the Line (BTL) 
 
-> "The Line" is the 'power line', so Directors, VPs, CXOs = Above the Line 
 
-> BTL thinks about their team v. ATL thinks about market share & risk 
 
 
IRL example: 
 
-> ATL value prop sounds like, "With [my product] you reduce 3 risks: Your top competitor doesn't get in the door first, you don't lose more market share, and you don't have to explain your inaction to investors." 

-> BTL value prop sounds like, "What goes away [with my product] is worrying about too little pipeline, missing quota & losing your job." 
 
 
 
->>> TL;DR = choose the right language for your audience 
 
 
 
It takes time and effort to wrap our heads around these. 
 
But there's a reason the unicorns teach them. They win deals. 
 
Wanna try your hand at it? Write your value prop in "Away" language in the comments. (I double 🐶 dare you. It's harder than it sounds.)
 
 
 

 
*ATL/BTL concepts from Skip Miller’s book Proactive Selling. Credit for "German Shepherds v. Gold Medals" comes from the book Secrets of Question-Based Selling by Thomas Freese

After 15yrs in sales, I can tell you…

The person who responds the fastest usually wins. Communication doesn’t have to be perfect, but it needs to be fast. 

Speed builds priority and priority builds trust.

While others craft the perfect response, you’re winning the deal.

There are NO shortcuts in Enterprise Sales. Period. Tell your CEO. Tell your board. Rush your $200K deal – and it collapses. Guaranteed. Here are 8 ways you’re failing upmarket by running it like SMB (and why slowing down is the only way to close faster):

1. Cut Corners? Execs Will Kill it in 5 Minutes

You spent months, heck, maybe years. Execs need just one 5-min hallway chat to kill your deal. DON’T skip steps – build a bulletproof business case: unignorable problem, rock-solid ROI, strategic, risk mitigation. Or say goodbye.

2. Procurement Smells Desperation

Your champion says, “EOY close works, thanks for the discount!” Procurement? They’ll squeeze more, introduce extra steps, and question your credibility if you seem desperate. Stop discounting. Stop pressuring. Bring the best deal.

3. Fast POCs = Endless Evaluations

This is Enterprise graveyard. Quick, poorly planned POCs drag deals indefinitely, or worse – fail to achieve solution fit. Take the time to set explicit success criteria, time frames, and stakeholders involved upfront. Treat the POC like a mini-project with regular check-ins and outcome tracking.

4. Skipping Stakeholders = Going to Court w/o a Lawyer

Getting to the CxO, isolating the Tech Buyer…feels like hard work? It is, but Ent Sales isn’t Vegas – don’t gamble. Engage stakeholders EARLY. Map their needs, concerns, and build separate threads. Multi-threading is your insurance policy.

5. Security Sees Your Rush as a Threat

Their timelines and concerns always come first. Pressuring will only raise red flags: “What loopholes are they pushing past us?”. Provide docs proactively, run calls early, and treat them as allies, not obstacles.

6. Legal Has NO Quota

Legal doesn't care about your quarter-end. They're there to kill risk, not help your sales quota. Start legal reviews early, surface red flags ASAP, and don’t leave it to legal teams to run. Orchestrate the process. Easy = Faster.

7. Rushed Discovery = Dysfunctional Buying Groups

Fast disco stacks risk. Time bombs waiting to explode when new stakeholders are looped in. The more you involve them early and co-create a compelling problem statement together, the faster everyone rallies to make a deal happen.

8. Rushed Next Steps = More Steps

Booking quick next steps? Good luck. You’ll soon face endless unexpected steps that go around in circles. Slow down. Create a clear Mutual Action Plan. Lay out future steps explicitly, confirm buy-in, and execute intentionally.

——

Enterprise deals dragging 18 months?

Not because you aren’t pushing hard enough.

But because you’re pushing TOO HARD.

Shortcuts just leave you chasing stakeholders.

Or juggling endless surprise steps.

You CAN'T hack Enterprise Sales.

Solid case. Consensus. Processes.

All take time.

So go slow. Build it right.

And watch it fly.

Slow IS fast.

When I was CRO of a $200M SaaS, doing POCs almost destroyed us – months wasted, team exhausted, buyers constantly delaying. Until my VP Sales said, “Kill the POC. We’ll validate value clearly in 3 hours flat”. Here's exactly how we rebuilt our sales process and cut our sales cycle by 50%:

BACKGROUND:

We were selling 100% enterprise. POCs were the automatic default:

Heavy, technical validation lasting 1-3 months. It was painful…

- Sales Engineers were overloaded
- Buyers kept delaying due to resource issues
- Buyers kept wanting more “just one more test”

Initially, I thought: It’s Enterprise, that’s the game right?

Until our VP Sales, Idan Arealy, joined.

Two weeks in, he tells me:

“No offense – but these POCs are total overkill.”

“Buyers don’t need these endless tests.”

“They’re not doubting the tech.”

“They’re doubting the value.”

“And we don’t need this complexity to prove value.”

So he suggested a simpler, smarter alternative:

The 'Use Case Workshop' – and it changed everything.

Here’s the step-by-step:

——

1. Kickoff (45 min)
- AE positions the workshop immediately post-demo:
“Here’s what we typically do next to help validate real-world
scenarios in just hours—no heavy lift needed. Shall we set it up?"
- SE runs deeper disco into problem root causes in a kickoff call
- AE sets clear Mutual Action Plan (MAP)

2. Internal Alignment (60 min)
- SE & AE clearly define and build initial use-case solutions
- Output: Slides outlining impactful solutions & open questions

3. Use Case Co-Design (45 min)
- Live session with buyers walking through scenarios
- Collaboratively refine solutions LIVE (e.g. Miro, slides):
“Walk me through this problem in more detail—we’ll map
exactly how solving it looks."

4. Prioritization & Wrap Up (30 min)
- Jointly prioritize top 3 impactful use cases clearly:
"Which scenarios, solved, would immediately solve [Problem]?"
- Lock down committed next steps

↳ Result?
- 3 focused hours (instead of months)
- Clear, confident buyers ready to champion
- 100% faster sales cycles & higher win rates

——

POCs are NOT mandatory.

Buyers don't want endless tests.

Don’t default to what most buyers ask.

Design what will solve what they need – 

With as little friction as possible.

That's: Sales Process Design 101.

Enterprise sales does NOT mean slow. ‘Enterprise’ is not an excuse. If your deals take 12–18 months, something in your sales motion is broken. Unless you’re running a truly strategic, 7-fig Fortune 100 cycle... it should never take this long. I’ve worked 100s of $6-7-fig deals. Here are the 7 big mistakes that make enterprise deals slow (and fragile):

1. You Speak With Anyone 

Large corp working bees have a secret talent: Time Vampires. They have time. You don’t. They love hopping on calls, talking about problems they heard from their boss’s boss, “figuring out procurement,” and waiting months for the “right time” to pitch – even though it’s way above their pay grade. Then their 6-figure initiative gets shut down in 3 minutes. Stop talking to everyone. Start talking to the few who matter.

2. You Confuse Access with Progress

Just because you’re “talking to more people” doesn’t mean you’re advancing the deal. If your enterprise motion isn’t about building real champions and exec approvals, you’re swinging on a rocking horse: Lots of movement, but going nowhere. Don’t optimize for meetings. Optimize for the right meetings.

3. You Let the Buyer Lead the Process

Slow deals are almost always buyer-led. “Champion” says, “We’ll get back to you,” and you wait. They say, “Next step is X,” and you comply. Top sellers don’t follow – they co-create the process. They’re experts in buying. They suggest or challenge next steps, timelines, and alignment like a project manager in disguise.

4. Your Stage-1 Conversion Rate Is Sub-25% 

If opportunities sit in Stage-1 for months, and ¼ moves, you do NOT have opportunities. These are leads. You’re working (and forecasting) noise. Root causes: poor qualification criteria, weak disco/demo, or next step management. Stage-1 is for building problem consensus and willingness to evaluate. Push too fast, and you’re burning enterprise running it like SMB.

5. Your “Deals” Stay at the Same Next Step for Months

NO – it’s not “how enterprise is.” A real enterprise deal has a pulse: weekly or every-other-week touches. Yes, even Apple. If it’s important to the company (and to the champion), it should move. All the freaking time.

6. You Treat “Enterprise” Like an Excuse

“Yeah, but it’s enterprise… things take time.” NO – things take clarity. The more complex the org, the tighter your alignment must be. That means more steps, not more time between them. If you’re waiting on the buyer to figure it out internally – you’ve already lost. Enterprise ≠ slow. Enterprise = deep.

#7 in the comments

——

Slow deals aren’t “enterprise.”

Slow deals are a sign you lost control.

Real Champions don’t wait.

Real Problems don’t wait.

Only broken processes wait.

Depth, not time.

Your board wants $30M next year. You closed $23M this year. You added 2 reps.

Coolio. Where's the other $7M coming from? Magic?

Leadership sets aggressive growth targets without doing the capacity math. Then they blame sales for missing when it was a math problem from day one.

Wanting 30% growth is fine. But if you only funded 12% capacity increase, the numbers will never work.

Here's why: Most VPs build headcount models that assume perfect conditions.


They assume:

- Every new hire ramps on schedule (they don't).
- Nobody quits (they do).
- Every ramped rep hits quota (60% actually do).
- Territory productivity stays constant (it declines as you add reps).


Then Q3 hits and you're at 70% of plan. The board asks what happened.

What happened is the math never worked!


Here's how to fix it:


1. Model ramp by time (not title).

Every new hire isn't a quota-carrying AE. They're an investment curve:

- Month 1-2: Training, zero pipeline.
- Month 3: Pipeline opens, close rates low.
- Month 6: First meaningful bookings.
- Month 9-12: Full ramp (maybe).

If you don't know this curve by role, you can't predict bookings. 

Add 5 AEs in Q1 modeled at full quota? 

Reality: 0% for 3 months, 30% for 3 months, 60% for 3 months, MAYBE 100% by EOY. 

Your $30M plan just became $24M.


2. Track productivity per ramp stage.

Build bands:

- Early Ramp (0-3 months): 0% of quota.
- Mid Ramp (3-6 months): 25-50%
- Late Ramp (6-12 months): 75%
- Fully Ramped (12+ months): 100%+

Model quarterly revenue based on how many reps fall into each stage.


3. Run capacity math before asking for headcount.

Before accepting a $30M target, build bottom-up:

- Target: $30M.
- Avg quota per ramped AE: $1.2M.
- Ramped AEs needed: 25.
- You have 15 today.
- Need 10 more ramped equivalents.

New hires aren't ramped for 9-12 months, so hire 15-18 to get 10 ramped by EOY.

Factor in 15-20% attrition? You'll lose 3-4 reps. Now you need to hire 18-22 just to net the 10 you need.

Suddenly "add 2 reps" looks bonkers.


4. Add drag factors.

No model survives reality. Build in:

- Ramp delays.
- Attrition (10-20% annual).
- External shocks (macro headwinds, comp changes).

Your model should NEVER presume perfection.


5. Present capacity constraints.

Don't say "I need 18 more reps."

Say: "To hit $30M with our current productivity and ramp curve, we need 25 fully ramped AEs by EOY. We have 15 today. After factoring ramp time and attrition, that means hiring 18-20 starting Q1."


Now THAT'S a business case.


The hardest part? Telling leadership their target isn't realistic given current investment.

But have that conversation in January. Not October when you're $5M behind.

Remember that a headcount plan is nothing more than a capacity forecast.

Your CEO, CFO, and board don't want to hear how many reps you hope to hire. They want to know how many fully ramped, productive reps you'll have when it matters.

So don't ask for headcount. Prove the need, then hit the number.

Prompt engineering is dead. 
Most people are still learning yesterday's skill.
Architecture is what matters now.

Two years ago, we became "Prompt Wizards". 

“Act as an expert.” 
“Think step by step.” 
“Explain it like I’m five.”

It felt powerful. 
It wasn’t.

We were chasing the wrong thing.

Andrew Ng showed why.

On HumanEval, a standard coding benchmark:

• Old ChatGPT (single prompt): 48.1% 
• New ChatGPT (single prompt): 67% 
• Old ChatGPT + an agentic workflow: 95.1%
 
A weaker model with a better process crushed the newer one.

Recently he compared one-shot prompting to writing an essay without a backspace key. 

You get one shot at perfect. 
It almost never works.

The future isn't the perfect prompt. 
It's the perfect flow.

We're moving from Prompt Engineering to 𝗙𝗹𝗼𝘄 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴.

The difference:

The Prompt Engineer asks: 
"What specific words will get the right answer?"

The Flow Engineer asks: 
"What is the architecture that solves this problem?"

Four patterns make the difference:

𝟭. 𝗥𝗲𝗳𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 
One agent does the work. Another critiques it. 
The debate catches what a single shot misses.

𝟮. 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹 𝗨𝘀𝗲
Stop making AI guess. Give it search. 
Give it calculators. Let it test and retry.

Heard a gangster idea from a RevOps leader this week who built something simple that's saving their CS team from getting blindsided by churn risk:

They set up a Slack channel called "churn-risk" that auto-populates with alerts.

Here's how it works:


Zapier scrapes every call (they use Sybill, but any call recorder should work) looking for specific words and phrases that signal trouble:

- "Frustrated"
- "Not what we expected"
- "Talking to [competitor]"
- "Our exec team is asking..."
- "Budget review coming up"


When those phrases hit, the system drops the call snippet into Slack with the account name, CSM owner, and exact timestamp.


EVERY leader in the company can see it. No waiting for the CSM to flag it. No hoping someone notices declining health scores.

They're catching problems two months before they would have surfaced in a QBR.


The best part is that they're not just using it for firefighting, but they actually run quarterly analyses on the Slack channel to identify patterns:

- Which objections come up most often?
- Which product gaps keep causing frustration?
- Which customer segments have the highest risk language?


Then they fix the systemic issues instead of just playing whack-a-mole with individual accounts.


One more thing they're doing that's legit:

Before every renewal season, they scrape all calls and emails from accounts renewing in the next 90 days.

Feed it into ChatGPT with a prompt: "What are the biggest risk factors across these accounts?"

Gives their CSMs a heads-up on what to address before it becomes an escalation.


The best way to handle an escalation is always going to be to prevent it from happening in the first place.

And this AI stuff makes that possible at scale now.

Spoke with a guy launching a company in stealth. His entire outbound strategy has been hundreds of handwritten notes. 6 months in, they're deploying ten Fortune 500 clients.

Not emails. Not LinkedIn messages. Not AI-powered video prospecting.

Actual handwritten notes. With a QR code to book time. Mailed to every executive he'd ever worked with.


Sounds bonkers, but actually makes perfect sense. I mean, I get that it's 2025. Sure, we have AI that writes perfect emails. Sequences that personalize at scale. Prospecting tools that know what cereal your buyer ate for breakfast.

But the gangster move is a stamp.


He told me what happened at dinner that kicked off his whole strategy.

He's breaking bread with an old contact. CRO of a $12B company. 3,000 employees. The exact buyer every SaaS vendor is chasing.

My guy mentions he's been emailing him. Ten times over a few months. Zero response.

The CRO laughs. "Oh, I don't read my inbox anymore. Just email my chief of staff if you want to get a hold of me."

Not "Sorry man I'm swamped." Not "Shit I think it went to spam."

He literally doesn't open his inbox. Ever.


Here's what's wild about this moment we're in:

We've spent three years building AI that writes better emails, crafts better subject lines, personalizes at scale. Meanwhile, the entire ENT buying committee has collectively decided to ignore ALL of it.


Every inbox will be perfect in 12 months. Every email will look hand-crafted. Every message will reference that podcast you were on or that article you wrote.

Which means every email will still be just noise. It'll be "better" by today's standards, but noise nonetheless.


But a handwritten note? That's a human being who gave a shit for 90 seconds.

That CRO gets 847 emails a day. 840 of them are vendors who "noticed he recently raised a Series C" or "thought his CEO's comments on the last earnings call were really compelling."


AI didn't kill email outbound. AI made it so good that everyone's doing it. 

Which is what killed it.


There's a huge shift right now going back towards relationship selling. It would be a bit romantic to think it's because it's noble or pure.

But in reality, we're going back to it because AI made everything else worthless.


Handwritten notes. Showing up at the conference. Actually knowing someone before you try to sell them something.

Sure, old farts like me might think it's romantic. But at the same time, you can also just consider it math.


When everyone can send a perfect email, nobody's reading email.

And yeah, the kinda stuff this guy did is harder. That's why it works.

The CMO role isn't dead.

It's splitting in two.

And most companies don't realize they need to choose.

I've been tracking a shift that's gone mainstream over the past year.

According to the Financial Times, the CMO title is quietly being replaced across major FMCG companies.

Not eliminated. Redefined.

→ Mars moved former CMO Andrew Clarke to Chief Growth Officer - spanning marketing, digital, commercial, and strategic growth
→ Unilever restructured with Chief Business Officers and Global Growth titles replacing traditional CMO roles
→ Coca-Cola created Chief Growth Officer positions combining marketing, customer, and commercial leadership
→ Mondelēz shifted from CMO to Chief Growth Officer structure

The pattern is clear.

CEO's aren't only asking: "How's the brand performing?"

They're asking: "What's driving revenue?"

And that question requires a different skill set.

If we zoom out, we can see what's actually happening.

The CMO role is evolving into two distinct functions:

1️⃣ Chief Brand Officer

→ Owns brand, identity, and long-term equity
→ Focuses on awareness, perception, and cultural relevance
→ Measures share of voice, brand health, and positioning
→ Protects brand integrity across touchpoints

2️⃣ Chief Growth Officer
→ Owns customer acquisition, retention, and monetization
→ Spans marketing, digital, commercial, and strategic growth
→ Measures contribution margin, LTV:CAC, and profitability
→ Operates like a GM with a P&L

Both are critical.

But they require completely different skill sets.

The "CMO is dead" narrative you've probably been reading about...

It's only true if you keep trying to hire one person for two jobs.

The hard part for companies isn't the reorganization.

It's knowing which function you need, and when.

Early stage? You need growth.

Someone who can build the acquisition engine, prove unit economics, and scale profitably.

Later stage? You might need brand.

Someone who can protect positioning, expand into new markets, and build long-term equity.

But this is the mistake I keep seeing:

Companies hire a Brand CMO and expect growth outcomes.
Or they hire a Growth CMO when what’s required is shaping the brand’s strategy: who we serve, why we win, and where we go next.

The gap isn't talent.

It's clarity.

If your CMO's scope includes both brand identity and revenue generation,
You're not setting them up to win.

You're asking one person to be an artist and an operator.

The companies that are winning are making a choice:

→ Split the function into Chief Brand Officer and Chief Growth Officer
→ Or prioritize one: "Right now, we need growth. Brand comes later."

But they don't pretend one person can do both at scale.

We introduce ZIP-RC, a method that equips language models with real-time introspection at zero computational overhead. Instead of blindly generating tokens and hoping for the best, models can now predict their own success and estimate how much effort a problem actually requires.

The core insight is elegant: reuse unused logits during generation to output a joint distribution over final reward and remaining length. No extra models. No architecture changes. No added inference cost. The model essentially learns to think about its own thinking while it's thinking.

📊 On mixed-difficulty math benchmarks, ZIP-RC improves accuracy by up to 12% over majority voting at equal or lower average cost 
⚡ Enables adaptive test-time compute, spending resources only where they matter 
🎯 Traces smooth Pareto frontiers between quality, compute, and latency

This matters because current approaches like Best-of-N sampling waste compute on easy problems and underinvest on hard ones. ZIP-RC lets models allocate effort intelligently, know when to try again, and signal confidence to users and downstream systems.

When Google acquired my startup, I was promptly informed that I was "Google quality PM."

(Ouch.)

So for three years, I worked in Partnerships. And while I didn't love it, it taught me more about product strategy than any comfortable PM role could have.

If you’ve just been hired into a new Partnerships role, or you’re a founder trying to make one work, here is the cold reality:

1. You are the least important problem your colleagues have. Everyone else can hit their OKRs without your help. You, however, cannot hit yours without them. You are structurally set up to beg for resources.

2. You are downstream from everyone else's strategy. Product is building for an ICP different from the one you’re trying to partner with. GTM is focused on lead gen and closing velocity for their funnel, not yours.

3. Partners don't actually care about you. Sure, someone signed a contract. But the folks on the ground? If they kill it for you, they get a pat on the back. If their core numbers slip, they're fired. You are the first thing they will ditch when things get stressful.

So, how do you actually win?

You treat the partnership like a 0-1 product.

Most people try to fix a struggling partnership by adding features—custom integrations, co-marketing plans, tweaked rev-shares. This is a trap.
Instead, do what you’d do for a startup: Niche down.

Stop trying to demonstrate broad value. Find the incredibly narrow audience (narrower than you think!) for whom this partnership solves an oxygen-level problem.

If you find that audience, you’re golden. If you fail, no amount of "strategic alignment" meetings will save you.

I'm amazed by how many companies talk about shifting from "project" to product funding, that can't assemble a basic business case for each team.

Start here...

For each team, write a business case explaining the financial rationale for why the team exists. What should this include?

What are the enduring areas of focus, the “levers,” that this team has shown a track record of moving (or could spend more time moving if they were a better-defined team)? These levers should be stable and solution-agnostic, not fleeting or project- or work-focused.

How has money in translated to money out? Remember second- and third-order effects, and time. Not every team will “create value” in the near term, but you should be able to frame a sensible guess or estimate here. Start from the thought experiment of the team not having done anything. What happened? What was the impact? The delta should get you in the vicinity.

Put on your finance hat. What should the “pivot vs. proceed” tests be for this team in general? Is it even possible to “add money” to the team? What would happen? Could the team actually absorb more investment?

How much of the team’s effort over the last year was squarely in line with the team’s mission and reason for being, versus adjacent, unacknowledged areas of helping?

Now start answering some questions

1. What problem gets materially worse if this team pauses for six months?

2. What measurable outcomes would most clearly justify this team’s continued funding?

3. Which costs would increase elsewhere in the organization if this team disappeared?

4. What assumptions about demand, risk, or growth does this team carry?

5. What work does this team do that no other team could realistically absorb?

6. What signals would tell us this team’s model is no longer viable?

7. What is the smallest version of this team that could still deliver its core value?

8. Where does this team create optionality rather than immediate returns?

9. If this team were funded as a contract instead of headcount, what would be in scope?

Here's why so many companies that are adopting the "product operating model" will fail.

Whenever a “model” gets popular, you see a rush to break it down into structures, roles and responsibilities, competencies, assessments, etc. These instincts make sense, because to “do” something, you need ways to assess whether you are “doing” it, and what doing it actually looks like.

But it also makes a critical error.

It assumes that the best way to get somewhere, is to behave and act like you’re already there. You substitute signals of a state (roles, behaviors, artifacts) for the conditions that actually produce that state (judgment, learning, constraint, responsibility). This is a form of means–ends inversion, where the artifacts of a (theoretically) mature system are treated as the path to maturity itself.

It is an error grounded in the fundamental belief that “the problem” is one of individual capability or mindset. If people just had the right skills, the right incentives, the right “thinking”, the organization would behave differently.

But what if the path to the “product operating model” looked nothing like the product operating model?

What if it involved large groups of people collaborating instead of neat, discrete teams? What if it required big-bang, project-like refactoring efforts rather than incremental, team-level change? What if it depended on sustained top-down decision making, centralization, and repeated re-orgs?

In messy, political, path-dependent systems, the early phases often require counter-intuitive moves:
-Temporary centralization to establish coherence
-Heavy-handed decisions
-Project-like efforts to untangle deeply coupled systems
-Many stepping stone re-orgs

Many of the companies held up as paragons of product-oriented thinking didn’t arrive there by installing a clean operating model and then refining it at the edges.

They went through multiple waves of reworking their operating system. Structures changed. Decision rights shifted. Interfaces broke. Things that worked at one scale failed spectacularly at the next.

They were in near-constant flux.

At times, their organizations looked chaotic, overly centralized, project-heavy, or bluntly top-down, the very things today’s product narratives warn against. Most of the learning happened not when things were humming, but when they broke.

Most companies treat this as a transformation with an end point.

But the companies they’re trying to emulate were in near-constant flux. Their operating systems kept changing as things broke at scale.

The “product operating model” wasn’t a destination. It was the ability to keep adapting without losing coherence.

If you are considering your next role in product management, be very intentional about the company you choose.

Not every company truly understands what product management is, or what it is supposed to do. That matters more than most people realize.

Over the years, some large technology companies have taken it upon themselves to redefine the product management role. In many cases, that redefinition strips away core responsibilities like market discovery, problem framing, and business ownership. What is left often looks more like delivery coordination or backlog administration. That does a real disservice to people who want to practice the craft of product management in its fullest sense.

I once worked at a company that decided to split product management into two roles. One group was called product managers. Another group was called solution managers. No one could clearly explain where strategy lived, who owned the market problem, or who was accountable for outcomes. Decision making slowed. Accountability blurred. Morale plummeted. The model failed, predictably and expensively.

Titles are easy to create. Clarity is harder.

If you are on the job market, ask direct questions during the interview process. Who owns product strategy? Who talks to customers and the market? Who is accountable for outcomes, not output? Who decides what gets built and why?

Also listen carefully to how roadmaps are discussed. If the roadmap already exists and the role is primarily about executing it, that is a signal. You may be stepping into a role with responsibility but little authority (those of you that follow me know what I've said about the responsibility/no authority myth).

Strong product management environments are incredibly rewarding places to work. They respect the discipline, they invest in discovery, and they trust product leaders to help run the business.

Choose carefully. Your growth as a product manager depends on it.

Product Managers: What happens in your nervous system when pressure hits?

I asked an overwhelmed PM:
"When someone expects quick results, what thought comes up?"

She said, "If I don't say yes, I will get fired."

That moment hits hard.

Stakeholders drop bombs on you like:
"A customer found a bug, we need to fix it now or we'll lose them!"
"Drop everything, this feature is what matters now."
"Can you squeeze this into the current sprint?"
"Quick meeting ASAP."

So—what happens inside you then?
• Panic
• Fear of not delivering
• “Yes, I can do it!” (…but you’re not sure you can)
• People pleasing
• Worrying about not being “productive” or “adaptable”

That creeping thought:
“If I don’t say yes, the product fails. And I get fired.”

Oof. You’ve been there, right?

Now—let's take a breath together. Here's what great PMs do:

✅ Step 1: Create space for calm.
Before replying, breathe in AND out. Ground yourself. The goal isn’t to react fast—it’s to respond wisely.

✅ Step 2: Get curious.
Try this: “Oh, that’s interesting. Tell me what made you notice this?”
Open up to their world. You don't need to judge, fix, or agree just yet.

✅ Step 3: Shift from "giving in" to "getting alignment".
Connect to the bigger picture—roadmaps, priorities, strategies
– “What problem would this feature solve?”
– “Is this request based on customer data?”
– “Are other customers feeling this pain?”
– “Where in the journey does this matter most?”

✅ Step 4: Close out with clarity.
– URGENT: “Give me 30 minutes to outline a plan.”
– PROMISING: “Great idea—I’ll validate it and add to next sprint.”
– TRIVIAL: “Not a current fit, but it clarifies our scope.”

In this one shift, you move from chaos to collaboration. And it's the difference between PMs who react—and stay overwhelmed—and those who lead—and keep growing.

💡 Curious: what’s your default reaction when tension hits?

The best engineer I ever worked with had three words tattooed in his brain: Figure. It. Out.

Street smarts beats book smarts when the docs are wrong. When the Stack Overflow answer is from 2015. When your manager needs it working by morning and you've never touched this part of the codebase.

He didn't know everything. He knew how to become the person who knew.

He'd DM people on Slack. He'd spin up a test environment and break things until they made sense. He'd admit "I have no idea" and then add "...but give me 2 hours."

The dirty secret of senior engineers is they’re just really, really good at figuring it out.

Get the answer. Ask for help. Read the source code. Message that person who seems to know. Try the thing that feels too simple to work.

But get it done.

In this 1,000 word post I’m going to cover:

1. Bootstrap vs Venture Capital
2. The 4 skills that matter now
3. Where the biggest opportunities are

5. What success looks like

INTRODUCTION

In 2025, what should be the goal of starting a new venture?

Simple.

Generate +$10m ARR with less employees than fingers on one hand.

Either sell that company, or replace yourself, take some time off, and do it again. And again. 

Why?

Because the relative attractiveness of a business like RB2B… 

…which LITERALLY RAN ITSELF as a $6m ARR company as the 3 FTE’s went off the grid for a week…

vs a VC-backed startup is GREATER than it’s ever been.

BOOTSTRAP VS. VC

The risk-adjusted financial outcome of bootstrapping has always been far greater than VC. 

Most VC founders don’t make money. (The few that have will comment that I don’t know what I’m talking about. Hi guys 👋)

Your best PMs are leaving because they're doing product operations on the side of their desk.

This happens frequently: companies hire talented product managers, then wonder why they burn out or leave after 18 months. The problem isn't the workload, it's what that workload contains.

Take a look at the image attached from our book Product Operations: it shows the split between PM responsibilities and Product Operations Manager responsibilities. If you don't have Product Operations, guess where all those operational tasks end up? Right on your PM's plate.

You're asking PMs to be data-driven while building their own analytics dashboards. To prioritize excellently while creating their own frameworks from scratch. To collaborate cross-functionally while also playing product marketing, data analyst, and strategy facilitator.

This becomes organizational gap-filling rather than focused product management.

When your PM spends three hours building a stakeholder alignment template instead of analyzing user behavior, you're paying premium talent to solve infrastructure problems. When they're manually pulling data for executive reports instead of driving product strategy, you're burning money and morale.

Product Operations exists to handle exactly these challenges. It creates the infrastructure, frameworks, and processes that let PMs focus on what you actually hired them for: driving business growth and customer value.

The companies that figure this out keep their best talent. The ones that don't lose them to organizations that understand the difference between strategy and setup.

What operational tasks are your PMs doing that pull them away from strategic work?

Whenever I see a prediction on how AI will result in fewer hours worked, I think of the opposite happening at AI startups:

These companies have no budget limit on how much devs can spend on AI, and encourage spending as much as possible - so devs working here spend a TON - and their output will naturally be higher than devs working at other places (let's just take this at face value for this post - and run with the implications of this assumption) So significantly more output per dev would mean that compared to their peers at places with less AI budgets, devs at AI startups could work less, and still produce more output. So surely they would work less, right?

And yet, these devs work MORE and longer hours than they would anywhere else! The reason? To win, they need to outcomplete their competitors: other AI startups that also give the same "unlimited AI usage" to devs, and so those devs are just as productive! And so more AI usage results in longer hours, to hopefully outcomplete other, similar startups!

Work is always relative to what others are doing, using available tools. To be best-in-the industry, you need to have either higher quality output than everyone else, or more output than everyone else.

So if everyone uses better tools: you'd either do better work with them (achieve better mastery than the rest of the industry)... or just work more!

AI doesn't change this!

Every company talks about adopting AI — but few talk about adapting humans.

but those who can think with it.

So I’ll ask you —
as AI becomes your colleague, not your competitor,
what skills will define your edge?

When you see how people use Claude Code/Codex/etc it becomes clear that managing agents is really, unsurprisingly, a management problem

Can you specify goals? Can you provide context? Can you divide up tasks? Can you design checkpoints? Can you give feedback?

These are teachable skills. 

But also UIs need to support management. Using a command line interface built for coding is not the best way to manage dozens of asynchronous tasks over a long term timeline. A big question for the coming year is whether the major AI labs can rethink the experience to be more suited towards delegation on other tasks.

Microsoft’s (and many other traditional software vendors’) bet that people will want each program or application suite infused with its own focused AI is looking like a risky one in the face of Codex, Antigravity, and Claude Code.

It is becoming clear that, with good enough models, people will want to delegate to an agent that uses tools and works across apps (or builds its own) to do tasks. Why open individual applications and use disconnected AI tools with limited context? The applications were always a means to an end, not a goal in themselves.

I bet you that many of the people who had the biggest impact on my company are names you've never heard...👇🏾

They almost never post, never go on podcasts, never want the spotlight...some of them actively avoid it.

And I've been sitting with this thought for a while now, because I think there's something we need to talk about more honestly...

Over the last decade, we've built this entire economy around personal branding.

I've pushed it. I've benefited from it enormously. I literally incentivise my team to post more, to build their audiences, to shout about our company, to put themselves out there.

I genuinely believe it can change the fortunes of a company, it can build careers and change lives - and our team posting about our work has had a huge impact.

But I think as leaders we need to make sure we don't confuse public visibility with private value...

This is a delicate dance.

We want our teams to post more, to build their profiles, to talk about their work, but we don't want to overlook those who choose not to...

I think we've started assuming that the people who talk the most about their work are the ones doing the most important work.

Let me tell you something about the Indian market that nobody prepares you for.

Everyone talks about product-market fit. But in India, it’s less about product-market fit and more about founder-market truth.

Because India is not one market.
India is 30 different economies pretending to be one.

And if your product doesn’t understand that, no amount of “fit” will save you.

When we were building Quick Heal, we learned this the hard way.

The same antivirus that worked perfectly in Pune failed in Kolhapur because internet speeds were different.

The features people appreciated in Mumbai were irrelevant in Nashik because their fears were different.

The UI that made sense in English speaking offices didn’t work in tier-3 towns because “Scan” and “Fix” were abstract words.

And here’s what nobody wants to admit:

Most Indian startups fail because the founder builds for India… as if India behaves like a single user persona. It doesn’t.

In India, markets don’t respond to features. They respond to familiarity.
And the “real user” is rarely the buyer. And the buyer is rarely the user.

People love talking about PMF like it’s a clean equation.
But in India, market reality looks more like this:

01. Your users don’t tell you what they want.
They show you what they fear.

02. Price sensitivity is not poverty. 
It’s risk sensitivity. Indians will pay for gold, land, coaching classes, but not for something they don’t feel the value of yet.

03. Word-of-mouth travels faster than marketing.
And bad experiences travel even faster.

04. The micro markets matter more than the macro numbers.
A feature that wins you Gujarat could fail in Tamil Nadu.
A workflow that works in tier 1 breaks in tier 3.

05. And most importantly, “market education” is not a marketing strategy.
It's a decade-long tax founders pay when they misjudge their audience.

When we finally accepted these truths, everything changed. Because we stopped building for a hypothetical “Indian user,” and started building for how India actually behaves.

That was our founder market truth.

"I'm so glad for AI," I overheard behind me at a restaurant. "I used to be AWFUL at making presentation slides. Now? AI gets me to 'good enough' instantly. My manager fixes the rest." 

The admission was distracting enough to make me put my fork down. I couldn't help but envision the harm this person's "good enough" was having on his organization, in light of not only my own experiences as a consultant and a consumer, but recent research on the detrimental impact of low-quality AI "workslop" on the workplace. 

Earlier this year, while trying to catch a flight, I desperately messaged a "customer service representative" who assured me, "I really want to help you," "I got you, Lily!" and "You can do it and make your flight!" but offered only generic information about rebooking when I asked how. At the airport, human representatives told me there was nothing to be done — the automated system had just been regurgitating the bare minimum, with a new AI-driven layer of false hope painted over it. 

One manager I work with shared how her workload has nearly doubled as her direct reports have increasingly sent in polished-looking reports riddled with inaccuracies and misinformation, forcing her to waste time by sending deliverables back for basic corrections, and eroding her trust in these colleagues. As her senior leaders have increasingly encouraged workers to offload as much as possible onto AI, she feels like her pushback is increasingly meaningless. 

I wish these were the only stories. I've spoken with workers straining to interpret AI-written communications from their leadership teams, senior engineers wasting countless hours cleaning up "vibe coding" from colleagues who can't interpret their own code, professors lamenting AI-written papers from students and students lamenting AI-written feedback from professors. Slop is everywhere right now. And too many leaders are only egging more of it on with little heed for its consequences — or worse, cynically hoping that we will all come to accept a lower standard for our products, services, and work.

What stands out to me isn't simply that AI tools are being abused. It's that the nature of the tool displaces the burden of truth and the cost of accountability away from the user — with the externalized costs always borne by someone else, from the creators whose data is scraped to train datasets to the low-paid data workers traumatized by labeling disturbing content and poorer communities blighted and displaced by data centers, all the way to consumers experiencing lower-quality products and managers dealing with lower-quality work. 

We can't "un-invent" AI. It's here, for better or worse. (Worse, so far.) But what we can do is step off of the hype train and take a measured approach to how and to what extent we adopt these technologies to solve real problems, to gauge whether the value AI creates is worth its real costs. If it's not? Staying slopless just might end up your competitive advantage.

Who does the CISO report to?

The one who owns uptime shouldn’t own breach reporting. It’s like the fox guarding the henhouse.

What % of their job is just reactive firefighting?

Is identity owned, or just duct-taped across apps?
(If no one owns IAM end-to-end, the CISO is just mopping the floor with the tap still running.)

Do they control budget or just beg for it?
(If security is a cost centre with no autonomy, it gets cut before it gets heard.)

Are they heard before a breach, or only after?
(If they’re introduced in board meetings post-incident, it’s already too late.)

A weak org design can kill even the best CISO.

It’s not about tools. It’s about power, clarity, and leverage. If your CISO can’t say “no” to business decisions that invite risk, then don’t be surprised when the breach comes.

CISOs don’t fail in the SOC. They fail in the boardroom.

Why DPI (Distributions to Paid-In capital) will replace IRR (internal rate of return) as the true badge of performance

For years, venture capital worshipped IRR - the magic percentage that made every fund look like a rocket ship.

But here’s the uncomfortable truth: IRR is a story. DPI is reality.

You can look great on paper with zero cash returned.

All it takes is one overpriced round and a friendly co-investor to “validate” your NAV.

Take a simple example:

A fund invests $10M in a startup valued at $50M.

One year later, a new investor comes in at a $200M valuation.

The original fund marks its stake up 4x - and its gross IRR jumps by 300%.

On paper, they look like heroes.

In reality, they haven’t returned a single dollar to LPs.

Now imagine another fund invests the same $10M, exits a company two years later and get $30M, and distributes 3x cash back to LPs.

Their IRR might look lower (gross number 70%+) - but their gross DPI is 3.0x.

That’s real money in the bank.

For a large share of early-stage funds, median DPI remains below 1x after a decade.

That means LPs would have made more money buying the S&P 500.

Yet decks still open with “top quartile IRR.”

LPs are catching on.

In an era where exits are rare and valuations are inflated, paper returns no longer inspire trust.

They want dollars, not decimals.

The next generation of GPs won’t brag about how much their portfolio is “worth.”

They’ll brag about how much they’ve returned.

In this environment, IRR reflects expectations.

DPI reflects outcomes — and secondaries are a powerful way to convert value into cash.

Happy 2026! Will this be the year we finally achieve AGI? I’d like to propose a new version of the Turing Test, which I’ll call the Turing-AGI Test, to see if we’ve achieved this. I’ll explain in a moment why having a new test is important.

The public thinks achieving AGI means computers will be as intelligent as people and be able to do most or all knowledge work. I’d like to propose a new test. The test subject — either a computer or a skilled professional human — is given access to a computer that has internet access and software such as a web browser and Zoom. The judge will design a multi-day experience for the test subject, mediated through the computer, to carry out work tasks. For example, an experience might consist of a period of training (say, as a call center operator), followed by being asked to carry out the task (taking calls), with ongoing feedback. This mirrors what a remote worker with a fully working computer (but no webcam) might be expected to do.

A computer passes the Turing-AGI Test if it can carry out the work task as well as a skilled human.

Most members of the public likely believe a real AGI system will pass this test. Surely, if computers are as intelligent as humans, they should be able to perform work tasks as well as a human one might hire. Thus, the Turing-AGI Test aligns with the popular notion of what AGI  means.

Here’s why we need a new test: “AGI” has turned into a term of hype rather than a term with a precise meaning. A reasonable definition of AGI is AI that can do any intellectual task that a human can. When businesses hype up that they might achieve AGI within a few quarters, they usually try to justify these statements by setting a much lower bar. This mismatch in definitions is harmful because it makes people think AI is becoming more powerful than it actually is. I’m seeing this mislead everyone from high-school students (who avoid certain fields of study because they think it’s pointless with AGI’s imminent arrival) to CEOs (who are deciding what projects to invest in, sometimes assuming AI will be more capable in 1-2 years than any likely reality).

The original Turing Test, which required a computer to fool a human judge, via text chat, into being unable to distinguish it from a human, has been insufficient to indicate human-level intelligence. The Loebner Prize competition actually ran the Turing Test and found that being able to simulate human typing errors — perhaps even more than actually demonstrating intelligence — was needed to fool judges. A main goal of AI development today is to build systems that can do economically useful work, not fool judges. Thus a modified test that measures ability to do work would be more useful than a test that measures the ability to fool humans.

AI is the most transformative technology of all time. So why are we trying to describe the future with metaphors of the past? We're missing the words to truly have a conversation about what it is - and what it could be.

I've said before that I think through writing - which means I spend a lot of time trying to pick the right words. And it's been getting harder and harder to choose from the imperfect terms we have to describe AI's capabilities (or the ones it lacks). Feels like we're losing a lot in translation. It's hard to describe something new when all your words are old.

No "assistant" has ever been able to run 100 billion operations per second while you meal plan for the week.

No "tutor" before AI remembers hundreds of terabytes of knowledge and examples.

No "tool" has ever come with emotional intelligence and infinite patience.

On one hand, the terms we have are too limiting. Sell it too short.

On the other, many of them make AI too human. Something more than it is.

It doesn't have feelings. It's not conscious. It's not your therapist or your friend. And we need the vocabulary that leaves no argument for that.

These new goldilocks terms won't appear out of thin air - it'll be because we decide we need them. Because we start inventing them. 

So weigh in - are there terms you do like? Ones you hate? Points I missed? Do you even feel the same way? Let me know below.

I Just Discovered How CISOs Really Pick Vendors (Analyzed 70 Decisions)
We think CISOs buy on features. I analyzed 70 decisions. 76% came down to one thing.

And it has nothing to do with product.

**The Discovery That Changed Everything**

I've facilitated 3,000+ CISO meetings through Execweb. Always wondered why technically inferior products won.

So I tracked 70 vendor selections over 18 months. Documented every factor.

The pattern shocked me.

**What Vendors Think Matters**

Every vendor pitches the same things:
- Superior technology (mentioned in 94% of pitches)
- Better features (89%)
- Lower price (71%)
- ROI calculations (68%)

They're solving for the wrong equation.

**What Actually Drives Decisions**

**Factor #1: Personal Risk Mitigation (76%)**

"Will this decision make me look stupid?"

CISOs don't buy the best solution. They buy the safest career move.

**Factor #2: Internal Politics Navigation (71%)**

"Who else needs to say yes?"

The best product with the wrong internal champion loses every time.

I watched a superior product lose because the vendor pitched the CISO but ignored the CFO who controlled budget.

**Factor #3: Peer Validation (67%)**

"What are my peers buying?"

CISOs have private Slack channels. They ask each other constantly.

Your product review happens in channels you'll never see.

**Factor #4: Vendor Relationship (63%)**

"Do I trust these people?"

One CISO chose a vendor because: "Their sales engineer reminded me of myself 10 years ago."

Relationships beat features.

**Factor #5: Implementation Reality (58%)**

"Can my team actually deploy this?"

The best product that requires 6 months to implement loses to the good-enough product that works tomorrow.

**The Shocking Non-Factors**

What barely mattered:
- Price (only decisive in 12% of decisions)
- Feature comparisons (8%)
- Analyst rankings (6%)
- Company size (4%)

**The Hidden Decision Framework**

CISOs run this mental calculation:

(Career Safety × Peer Approval × Political Win) ÷ Implementation Pain = Purchase Decision

Product quality? It's assumed. Table stakes.

**What This Means for Vendors**

Stop selling product. Start selling:
- Career insurance
- Political capital
- Peer acceptance
- Relationship trust
- Implementation ease

**The Brutal Truth**

The best product rarely wins.

The product that makes the CISO look good always wins.

Your superior technology means nothing if buying it makes them look risky.

**The Bottom Line**

We've been solving the wrong problem.

CISOs don't have a technology problem. They have a career risk problem.

Solve that, and the purchase order follows.

Which problem are you solving?

At the end of last year, AI agents really came alive for me. Partly because the models got better, but more so because we gave them the tools to take their capacity beyond pure reasoning. Now coding agents are controlling the terminal, running tests to validate their work, searching the web for documentation, and using web services with skills we taught them in plain English. Reality is fast catching the hype!

See, I never really cared much for the in-editor experience of having AI autocomplete your code as you were writing it. That was the original format pioneered by GitHub's Copilot and Cursor, but it left me cold. When I code, I want to finish my own thoughts and sentences. That was the sentiment I expressed on the Lex Fridman podcast last summer.

But with these autonomous agents, the experience is very different. It's more like working on a team and less like working with an overly-zealous pair programmer who can't stop stealing the keyboard to complete the code you were in the middle of writing. With a team of agents, they're doing their work autonomously, and I just review the final outcome, offer guidance when asked, and marvel at how this is possible at all.

So I'm ready to give the current crop of AI agents a promotion. They're no longer just here to help me learn, answer my questions, or check my work. They're fully capable of producing production-grade contributions to real-life code bases. 

Now, it all depends on what you're working on, and what your expectations are. The hype train keeps accelerating, and if you bought the pitch that we're five minutes away from putting all professional programmers out of a job, you'll be disappointed.

I'm nowhere close to the claims of having agents write 90%+ of the code, as I see some boast about online. I don't know what code they're writing to hit those rates, but that's way off what I'm able to achieve, if I hold the line on quality and cohesion.

But I'll forgive folks for getting excited! Because you don't have to connect many future dots on the current trend line to get dizzy by the prospects. The leaps of improvement that AI agents took in 2025 is simply incredible. This is the most exciting thing we've made computers do since we connected them to the internet back in the '90s. So what might things look like in 2026 or 2027? I get the exuberance.

I also get that some programmers are eager to tune it all out. The hype drones on relentlessly, the most fantastical claims are still far off from being substantiated, and there's real uncertainty about where all this will leave the profession in the future. But that's still not reason enough to miss out on this incredible moment in human and computing history! 

You gotta get in there. See where we're at now for yourself. Download OpenCode, throw some real work at Opus or the others, and relish the privilege of being alive during the days we taught the machines how to think.

AI Engineers are just Backend Developers with API keys.

After building a 40-person AI team, I can promise you: The hype is magic. The work is plumbing.

The fundamentals haven't changed. The stack just evolved.

Here is the shift we see every day:

1. The Logic Earlier, we built APIs and static business rules. Now, we build workflows that call models and reason over text.

2. The Data Earlier, we worried about rows, columns, and SQL. Now, we worry about embeddings, vector stores, and context windows.

3. The Testing Earlier, we tested for bugs and coverage. Now, we test for hallucinations and unpredictability.

4. The Monitoring Earlier, we watched CPU usage and uptime. Now, we watch for model drift, latency, and prompt health.

5. The Shipping Earlier, shipping meant writing clean code. Now, it means orchestrating code + prompts + models + guardrails.

If you can't build a robust backend, you can't build production AI.

Don't skip the boring stuff. That is the foundation for the cool stuff.

AI isn’t magic. 
It’s a stack.
 
AI is built in layers. Each one sits on top of the last.
 
Here's how it works:

𝟭. 𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗜
Pure logic. “If this, then that”.

Symbolic AI and Expert Systems that follow rigid rules to make decisions. 
It doesn't learn, it just follows the rules.

𝟮. 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
We stopped writing rules and started using math. 

Algorithms analyse data to find patterns. 
They classify, predict, and optimise based on history, not hard-coded instructions.

𝟯. 𝗡𝗲𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗡𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀
Copying biology.

We built systems inspired by the human brain with nodes and connections. 

This layer allowed computers to begin processing complex, messy inputs like signals and raw pixel data.

𝟰. 𝗗𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
Neural networks got massive. 

Transformers and LSTMs live here. 
This is the engine room that made computer vision and natural language processing actually work.

𝟱. 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗔𝗜
The shift from analysis to creation. 

These models don’t just classify a cat, they can draw one. 
They write code, draft emails, and compose music. 
This is the layer that woke up the general public.

𝟲. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗔𝗜
The frontier. Generative AI chats. Agentic AI acts. 

These systems possess memory, planning capabilities, and tool use. 
They move beyond passive output to autonomous execution. 
They do the work for you

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗼𝘁𝘁𝗼𝗺 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲:
You cannot understand the penthouse if you ignore the foundation.

We are currently transitioning from the 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 era to the 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰 era. 
The models are no longer just dreamers. 
They're becoming employees.

Where are you spending most of your time right now? 


Want to go deeper? I'm building a newsletter: Build What Matters.
Weekly drops on AI agents + emerging workflows.

Sales leaders: After working with 5,000 revenue orgs, I've seen 5 patterns in every great sales team.

From InsideSales, to Gong, to pclub.io – my career has been in the walls of revenue teams.

5 things the best do:


1. They know where they win.

They don’t chase the market.

They chase the segment where they have unfair advantage.

They define a surgical ICP and stop wasting cycles on deals that never close.

They’re obsessed with:

• Where they win
• Where they lose
• Where win-rate is too low

Then they operationalize it.

They don’t just "know" where they win.

They run the business around it.

One CRO I talked to said this:

“If you want higher close rates, stop chasing bad deals.”


2. They’re obsessed with narrative.

Once they know the territory, they design the narrative that unlocks it.

They refine messaging until buyers think:

“They understand my world better than I do.”

Narrative isn’t a marketing exercise.

It’s fuel that drives revenue.

When you nail it, everything is easier.

Whether it’s the CMO, CRO, or even CEO, someone holds this job:

“Chief Narrative Officer.”


3. They build a performance culture.

The best sales teams take a page from Netflix:

“We’re not a family. We’re a pro sports team.”

• Camaraderie? Yes.
• Psychological safety? Yes.

But also:

We’re here to perform.

If someone isn’t pulling their weight, the culture addresses it.

Elite teams balance two forces:

A) High standards
B) High safety

The paradox:

The more transparent you are about:

• Performance expectations
• PIP criteria

…the less fear exists.

Performance expectations create short-term fear.

But ambiguity creates permanent fear.

Open expectations remove "wondering."

Reps know where they stand.

That frees them.


4. They build rock-solid stages & exit criteria.

Great teams don’t use vague stages like Discovery → Demo → Proposal.

They design a sales process that exposes the reality of a deal.

• Clear stage definition
• Binary exit criteria
• Aging discipline

This clarity drives predictability:

• Reps stop guessing
• Managers coach w/precision
• Forecasts stop lying

Process definition is the compass.

But here’s the trap:

Having a clean process still isn't enough for consistency.

Sales stages and exit criteria only define what to do.

They do not equip reps with how to do it.


5. They treat skills like a performance system.

Strong leaders don’t just tell reps what to do.

They build the skill capacity to do it.

Once you define a great process, a hard truth emerges:

Many reps don’t have enough skill capacity to do it.

Great teams systematize skill excellence.

They treat skill capacity like a monetizeable asset.

These teams don’t view skills as “our people should already have these.”

They design skill profiles, measure them, train them.

Process without skill is academically strong, commercially weak.

Skill without process is chaos.

Do both?

You unlock revenue excellence.

Which of these 5 stood out most?

I've seen corporate Product Managers dreaming of working in a start-up and vice versa. Which side is better, though? Let's settle this once and for all:

I've worked in both types of companies, and here's my breakdown:

𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁-𝗨𝗽
Working in an early company can be appealing. You have the real power to shape the product, and only the CEO can stand in your way. You are free to take risks and move fast. Your small team will feel like brothers in arms, and you’ll often have direct access to users, enabling quick iterations.

But that freedom comes with stress. Start-ups are unpredictable. You carry the weight of the product’s success on your shoulders. It can be exciting, but also exhausting, with long hours and few processes to guide you. That is no 9-5 job - it's an ongoing battle for survival.

If you prefer to relax and calm over a stressful roller-coaster ride, perhaps you should consider:

𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗽𝗼𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲
Big company PM jobs, especially at big names like FAANG or MANGA, offer structure, stability, and opportunities to focus on your core Product Manager responsibilities. You’ll have clear processes, a system of benefits, and room to grow within different teams or levels of seniority.

However, it's not all sunshine and rainbows. Decision-making is slower, takes almost forever, and is bogged down by dependencies and approvals. You might be far removed from users, relying on analytics and aggregated feedback rather than direct insights. And let’s be honest, not every team in a big company has the same passion or drive you’ll find in start-ups.

That said, there’s a built-in safety net. Even if your project fails, you’re less likely to lose your job. And great managers (if you’re lucky enough to work with them) can turn this structured environment into a growth powerhouse for your career.

With all that, a question remains:

𝗪𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗖𝗵𝗼𝗼𝘀𝗲?
Neither start-ups nor corporates are universally better. It boils down to the people you’ll work with and the culture of the company. The best environment is the one where you’ll feel supported, inspired, and empowered to succeed. It's also not universal: Some start-ups will just develop nicely without you sacrificing your soul, and some corporations will keep you at night with deadlines.

Thus, focus less on the size of the company and more on the vibe and reputation. If the people are passionate, the process is reasonable, and the vision excites you, go for it! Ignore whether it’s a scrappy start-up or a corporate giant, great people make all the difference.

Which one would you prefer?

Stop asking ChatGPT about how it works (it's making stuff up)

We've all seen this: Someone gets a weird answer from an LLM and asks, "Why did you say that?" The LLM replies, "I mentioned X because of Y..." And the user thinks they've uncovered some underlying LLM logic.

The model is just confidently hallucinating its own 'reasoning'.

LLMs are not decision trees. They are not SQL databases that you can query. They are not logging anything internally to read back to you. They are not truth engines. 

They are probability machines.

When you ask a model "why did you answer in that way?" the model isn't looking back at its own architecture or code, it's just predicting: [What would a helpful AI assistant say in this situation?]

This is called Post-Hoc Rationalization: inventing a story/reason that fits or justifies the answer it just gave you. 

Not to mention, LLMs are tuned (via RLHF) to be helpful and agreeable. -Sycophants by design, telling you what they think you want to hear.

Best analogy I can come up with for these sycophantic tendencies: Ever asked a kid who has chocolate all over their face, "Did you have a cookie?" (when they weren't supposed to)? 

They'll think up & give you an answer that's most likely to make you happy (and keep them out of trouble). "The dog ate one!"

The big difference? Kids know the truth! LLMs don't. They just invent one.

Building a "Council of LLMs" in n8n 🤖⚖️

Inspired by the LLM Council from Andrej Karpathy (link to repo in comments), I jumped onto n8n last night to see if I could replicate the concept.

Instead of trusting a single model, you create a "council" of different LLMs (GPT-5.1, Claude 4.5, Gemini 3, etc.). They all answer your prompt, critique each other's reasoning, and then a designated "Chairman" model synthesises the best parts into a final answer.

Building the concept in n8n was much more straightforward than I was expecting:

🤖 The Council: A set of AI nodes (GPT-5.1, Claude 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro, Perplexity Sonar Pro) answer the original question.

⚖️ The Critique: Another set of nodes runs anonymous peer analysis on each other's answers.

🧠 The Chairman: A final AI Agent synthesises the data into a definitive answer.

📩 The Delivery: The result is formatted and sent to me as an email report.

Gemini helped with the prompts for the peer review and chairman analysis steps.

My next step is to spin up a frontend to allow our team to use this for complex questions and research tasks.

It took James Dyson 5,127 prototypes to invent the bagless vacuum.

Every manufacturer refused to license it. So he flew to Japan, sold it for $2,000, won a design prize, and used the royalties to launch back home.

But think about that number for a second:

5,127.

That's three prototypes a day. Every day. For five years.

His wife was a schoolteacher. Her salary kept them alive. By prototype 2,627, they were "really counting our pennies."

Imagine that breakfast table. Your spouse leaves to teach other people's kids so you can keep trying to make air spin the right way. And you're not even halfway there yet.

You can't explain this to anyone. Not really. Your in-laws think you've lost it. Your friends stopped asking. Every manufacturer who looked at your invention said no.

And every morning you wake up and build another one.

Prototype 3,000. Prototype 4,000. The design tightens. The pennies get tighter.

Then - Japan. A different market, a different door. The G-Force sold for $2,000, became a status symbol, won the 1991 International Design Fair prize.

Dyson took the royalties and launched the DC01 under his own name in the UK.

18 months later: best-seller.

But for most of those five years, there was no proof he was right. No way to know if prototype 4,500 was getting closer or just getting older.

Just a man in a room. A wife at school. And the quiet bet that tomorrow's version might be the one.

Everyone at prototype 1 thinks they have grit. Prototype 3,000 is where you find out.

I think the center of software work is moving.
The middle of software work has been the most important part for a long time. You started with an idea, and eventually you shipped something, but almost all of the effort lived in between. Turning intent into something real meant opening the codebase, booting up the environment, and writing the code. That middle absorbed most of the time, attention, and craft of software teams.
My belief is that this is changing.
Pure coding agent workflows can now produce working code from goals, context, and tasks. They operate more independently, requiring you to touch the code less and rely on the IDE less. The IDE becomes more of a code viewer than a writing tool.
As these systems improve, this middle becomes thinner. Less time is spent manually translating intent into implementation.
What actually needs to be built is still the important question. Understanding the problem, gathering the right context from customers and internal teams, and shaping the work so it can be acted on effectively matters more because agents act directly on that input.
Design, in this sense, is not about artifacts or tools. It is about forming and shaping clarity of the intent through ideas, exploration, research, and discussion. It is about deciding what matters, what constraints apply, and what tradeoffs are acceptable. Good product work is seeking clarity. What would make this execution actually matter. 
In this era, directing and managing agent work becomes the craft. Writing code is less like constructing a solution and more like setting up the conditions for a good solution to emerge. This might not be even an individual task, but an organizational one: how can you create these conditions as to the whole product team. 
This is what we work on. The Linear workspace is the coordination and context layer for product work. It captures intent, needs, constraints, and ownership in a way that makes work understandable before, during and after execution. There is still a lot for us to do here, but we are starting to see how effective AI and agents can be in a context-rich environments that connect directly customer feedback, the tools an have structured entities, workflows, that have intended outcomes. A bug reported on an iOS app in triage, has very clear expected outcome, it should be debugged and fixed. 
Structure in tools works for humans and agents the same way, it reduces the ambiguity what is expected or what capabilities exists. 
Additionally happens when the middle starts producing large amounts of output with less direct supervision, is more pressure on the end of the work: reviewing, testing, and releasing code. The tooling or workflows need to improve and change to handle it, and potentially also blend in to the overall process, not be a stopgap at the very end.
When the middle disappears or blends in, what becomes more in focus is the work of forming the right intent and making sure the outcome actually meets it.

There’s been a lot of excitement about context graphs lately. Jaya Gupta and Ashu Garg recently called them a “trillion-dollar opportunity,” and they’re right. Almost everything in the enterprise is now digital, yet AI still doesn’t understand how work actually happens. Systems of record capture the final state—the opportunity stage, ticket status, purchase orders, inventory levels—but everyday work happens in many more places: Slack threads, docs, Jira comments, incident escalations, and meetings.

People often assume the breakthrough will come from AI understanding why decisions get made. But a lot of “why” will always live in human judgment, experience, tradeoffs, and team dynamics that aren’t written anywhere. The real unlock is helping AI understand how work moves in real life—how it travels across tools, how decisions build over time, how things stall, get nudged, recover, and finally ship. That part is observable. It can be modeled. And it’s exactly what agents need to safely take on meaningful work.

Getting to the “how” is a deep technical investment, and it’s one we’ve been making at Glean. 

It starts with observability: tapping into large amounts of enterprise data, not just a narrow view from a couple of tools. You can’t understand real work from a sliver of reality. 

Then it requires understanding activity in sequence: what happened, in what order, and how state changed between each step. 

Finally, it means lifting that raw activity into a higher-level understanding of tasks, projects, and initiatives by recognizing repeated patterns and applying semantic understanding. That’s how you move from “logs of events” to “understanding how the organization actually operates.”

All of this has to be done with privacy, governance, and security as first principles, built inside each enterprise.

If we want AI that can actually automate meaningful work, it needs context—across systems and over time. Context is the new data platform.

It’s that time of year when everyone starts making predictions about AI and work in 2026.

Here’s mine: Workplace AI will know you better than your manager.

As a manager, I have a limited view of how work actually gets done. I see the final doc, the meeting, the missed or met deadline—but not the ten interruptions, the unclear handoff, or the moment someone got stuck and didn’t ask for help.

AI can fill in those gaps. When it has full context, it can see where work slows down, where context is missing, and where people are overloaded. 

Over time, AI starts to learn not just what people are working on, but how they work best. It begins to guide tasks and handoffs with more precision than static processes or weekly check-ins ever could.

By the end of 2026, the best managers won’t rely on gut feel or after-action reviews alone. They’ll rely on AI that helps them see problems forming—and keeps work moving before those problems turn into meetings, delays, or burnout.

Over the last weekend I decided to build an internal facing workflow app using AI. I pushed it to production in 2 days. By day 3, real users were testing it and sending feedback. Here are the steps I took:

Set up codex 5.1 max as my coding agent in VS Code
Set up my virtualenv
Set up an initial scaffold in a github mono repo using a FastAPI-based Python 3.14 backend and React based frontend. 
Set up a gcp cloud project to use google as an IDP for signups.
Set up end to end tests using Cypress and asked AI to first create a test for every new feature I asked it to build
Deployed my docker image to GCP using CloudRun with CloudSQL
Set up load balancing 
Set up observability
Set up Google Vertex AI for in-app inference
Set up separate staging and production environments on GCP
Set up Postgres using CloudSQL with periodic backups
Used SQLAlchemy for ORM
Used Alembic for DB migrations
Set up CICD using Github Actions
Made sure I had a single click deployment
Constantly kept an eye on context and compacted it whenever it was close to overflowing the window

Other than jumping in to guide AI to fix a few bugs it was struggling with, I wrote almost no lines of code. But I reviewed *almost* every line of code AI produced and I made all of these decisions to ensure the app was production-ready before asking for AI’s help in implementing my decisions. AI does not make any of these decisions for you, at least not if you don’t know what to ask for. However, if a) you know how you want to architect your app, b) it’s a greenfield project and c) you write it in a popular language like Python or Javascript, you can make REALLY REALLY fast progress.

I wonder if a newer generation developing apps using AI may skip some of these essential steps for production apps, leading them to write very brittle non-production code. And, in a few years, will we still have people who understand system design choices because they spent days and nights fixing production issues in the pre-AI days?

I killed an AI project earlier this year.

But I'd do it again. Here's why: 
We had a file management problem. Lost documents, wasted hours, frustrated team. 
 
The proposed solution? AI-powered document intelligence. 
It checked all the boxes: 
✅ Buzzword compliant 
✅ "Future-proof" 
✅ Impressive demo 
Budget: $400K+ 
 
I spent a week asking uncomfortable questions: 
"What's the ACTUAL cost of the current problem?" 
→ ~3 hours/week per person × 10 people = 30 hours/week 
→ At fully loaded cost: ~$12K/year problem 
 
"What happens if the AI is only 80% accurate?" 
→ We'd still need human review 
→ So we're adding complexity, not removing work 
 
"What's the simplest solution we haven't tried?" 
→ Better file naming standards 
→ Proper onboarding for new team members 
→ Actually using the search features we already pay for 
 
The answer was obvious. 
We didn't need AI. We needed discipline.
