Thoughts:

1. In the future, the probability something is generated entirely by AI will be inversely proportional to its intended lifespan.

2. For conceptually simple artifacts that are intended to have short lifespans, humans will still be involved just at a different level of abstraction. For example, I'm super excited about @Weavy_ai (Figma Weave) because it shows what's possible when you treat AI generation like clay to shape rather than the final output. Workflow building is a new skill to explore and learn.

3. If you intend for an artifact to have a long lifespan (ex: software, a novel, a movie), then AI might still aid you in your creative process. But you will bring great intention to the work. You will think through many different approaches. You will care about the smallest of details. You will lean into the craft. Because if you don't, it won't be good enough to last. It won't be noticed. It won't be loved. It won't matter.

4. Focusing just on software now... people don't like it when software changes. Everyone who has shipped a redesign knows this! So you might be generating new content within a piece of software frequently but of course you wouldn't redesign the fundamental UX of the software all the time. Users would hate it.

As a grounding metaphor, consider a house. Yes, you might change the photos and papers and magnets stuck to your fridge a few times a week. Once in a while, you reorganize stuff or move furniture around. After living in the house for a while, you maybe notice issues around how you use the space and — with great intention — embark on a remodel.

Some parts of the house, like the fridge, change a lot. But the overall structure of the house changes less. When asking what will be generated by AI, don't confuse the whole for the parts, the long lasting for the ephemeral.

5. It's intellectually interesting to ask whether a brand might want to adapt their software on a user by user basis. (Certainly individuals will be able to make more software for themselves if they are so inclined; see Figma Make.) 

My strong gut is that we will not end up in a world where brands customize software on a per user basis. 

People learn how to use software from other humans. Snapchat is a great example. For a new user, Snapchat is kind of confusing. You can see this as a design issue or an advantage... I argue it's an advantage.

By leaning into custom patterns and a learnable (but arguably non-intuitive) interface, the resulting network is a more intentional space. If you're young, you'll learn how to use Snapchat by watching your friends use Snapchat. And if you're older, well, you might not be the intended demographic.

6. To wrap up... we are in a world where the amount of software is growing at an exponential rate. If you want to win, invest in design, craft, storytelling and a bold point of view.

Use AI as a tool, but don't expect it to make something that no one has ever seen or imagined before. That's your job.

Should Employees Disclose When AI Contributed to Their Work?

I’ve been thinking about how strange authorship has become.
Ten years ago, every word, slide, or line of code came from a single mind.
Now, it often comes from a conversation between human instinct and machine intelligence.

So who owns the outcome?
And should we say that AI helped?

In my opinion, this isn’t just a policy question — it’s a trust question.
Because when we hide AI’s involvement, we’re not just concealing a tool —
we’re concealing a process.

But here’s the twist: disclosure might not build trust the way we think it does.
Tell someone you used AI, and they might question your originality.
Hide it, and they might question your integrity.
Either way, it’s not just your work being judged — it’s your judgment.

I think the future of transparency will look different:
✅ We’ll value context, not confession. The question won’t be “Did you use AI?” but “How did you use it?”
✅ We’ll measure contribution, not creation. Great work will be defined by insight — not authorship.
✅ We’ll teach discernment. Knowing when not to use AI will matter as much as knowing when to.

Maybe the future of work isn’t about proving what’s human.
It’s about proving what’s thoughtful.

So, what do you think —
should AI’s fingerprints be visible, or has authorship itself already evolved?

Being able to make decisions is one of the most important skills a CEO - or anyone, really - needs to develop. Top CEOs get a high percentage of decisions right; this is well known. 𝐆𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐨 𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐬𝐞 𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 “𝐫𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭” 𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐟𝐫𝐚𝐦𝐞, 𝐡𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫, 𝐢𝐬 𝐞𝐪𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐨𝐟𝐭𝐞𝐧 𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐝.

Every decision-making process has a natural timespan. Some decisions need five minutes of thinking, while others need five months. Recognising the point at which further analysis no longer materially improves the outcome is a vital skill. One must also factor in the opportunity cost of waiting for more data or more analysis.

At InCred, I see this play out every single day. Many decisions get taken in five minutes rather than thirty, simply because spending more time wouldn’t add any new insight. That pace is a big part of why we’re able to make the progress we do. It’s not about being rushed; it’s about matching the time spent on a decision to the nature of the problem at hand.

At the same time, there are larger, more consequential calls - especially inorganic ones - where taking the time to go deep really matters.

Recently, we were shown what initially looked like a very compelling fintech acquisition - strong headline numbers, an impressive tech stack, an extensive branch network, and a high calibre founder. We were told that moving faster than others would allow us to win the asset at a meaningfully better price.

We didn’t fall for that pressure. We took the time the decision deserved, and over a five month diligence process uncovered issues that made us uncomfortable. In the end, we walked away.

That was the right outcome - because we matched the time spent to the decision at hand.

But this cannot become an excuse for the many decisions that can and should be taken in five minutes. Confusing one kind of decision for the other is where decision-making usually goes wrong.

We may want to model every scenario, calculate every probability, and run every number. And to be clear, numbers matter. A lot! But analysis is the means to an end, not the end itself. Over analysing beyond a point doesn’t just delay decisions; it frustrates teams below you and quietly builds a culture of procrastination.

𝐃𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐦𝐚𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭 𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐤.
It’s about understanding it, acting with conviction, and staying flexible enough to adapt as the world changes around you.

And I’ve found it helps to have friends who’ll indulge you in endless debates 🙂
Sometimes that’s exactly what reminds you to stop overthinking and just decide.

I'm an engineer at Lovable and I spent the holidays improving our system prompt.

Lovable is now 4% faster and a lot better at creating good designs. The crazy part is that this also ended up decreasing our LLM costs by $20M per year—with help from my mother.

Here's how it happened:


The holidays are the perfect time to take a step back and zoom out. My mother wanted to know more about my strange job, so we actually sat down over Christmas and I showed her an LLM trace. She's a historian and the best text reviewer I know.

She asked things like "Why are you saying this 5 times in different places?"

I just said "I guess it's important?"

What we realized is that our system prompt is constructed dynamically from lots of different files. As we've been optimizing each part, no one had looked at the coherence for a while. Together we found duplication, inconsistencies, and overly verbose formulations.

You could practically see the engineer's thinking process: "I need to prompt harder for X, let me add another sentence." What this does is implicitly dilute other parts. A while later another engineer notices Y now needs more weight. The cycle continues.

We removed duplicates, tightened language, and kept roughly the same intent balance. Went aggressive just to see how it'd do. Did the first few paragraphs manually, and when I got home after Christmas I told Opus to do the rest following the same style. I reviewed the diff line-by-line and re-added a few critical constraints.

AB-tested it over New Year's. Lovable followed instructions better, was overall faster, and at our scale that optimization ends up saving us $20M per year.

Lessons:
• Prompt quality compounds at scale
• A fresh perspective can beat "prompting harder"
• Fast, safe experimentation is a superpower
Best part: it wouldn't have happened without my mother showing interest.

Took a very large main function I've found in production code, full of if/else statements configuration, and passed it to ChatGPT. I asked the following:

1. How would this be refactored to give lower cognitive load for readers?
2. Are there missing abstractions, whole-value classes, or types?
3. How would one refactor this code in light of the first two questions.

The results, frankly, are impressive. They may even be accurate. The outcome provides much better testability and readability at least. I think there might be a lot of reusability in the patterns it suggests, though not the code. 

See, this is a useful technology, but the human-in-the-loop who knows what questions to ask is crucial. I much prefer humans augmented with AI over "AI writes the code." The outcomes are much stronger.

We are obsessed with optimizing the 5 minutes of coding, while ignoring the 3 days of waiting.

The secret isn't to code even faster. It is Value Stream Management (VSM).

Organizations are systems, not just individuals.

If you use AI to churn out code faster without looking at the testing, review, and deployment queues, your overall delivery pace won’t change.

The 2025 DORA report warns that without intentional changes to the workflow, AI tools will likely provide a negligible boost to overall performance.

Flow is not just about speed.

It is a shift in mindset. You move from optimizing local efficiencies to optimizing the whole system. To do this, you need to measure what matters to find your true constraints.

For example, if you use AI to generate more code when your Pull Request queue is already clogged, you just create a bigger bottleneck. You should instead use VSM to identify the PR review as the constraint and apply AI to summarize reviews.

You improve the process and reduce blockage. This optimizes flow instead of the speed of a single step.

But you can't have flow without a foundation. Smooth flow typically requires the high-quality internal platform I wrote about in my last post.

The best performing organizations don’t just adopt new tools. They have a proven ability to "get better at getting better."

So where do you start?
1. Create a Value Stream Map.
2. Identify the constraint.
3. Apply AI to the friction point.
4. Revisit regularly.

Clarity from VSM ensures you aren't just throwing tools at a problem. It ensures you are delivering value.

Otherwise, we are just shipping chaos and complexity at a faster pace than ever before.

What AI agents reveal is that if you can clearly describe your goal, code is no longer the bottleneck. 

That applies to us at Google and most companies. The bottleneck moves up stack. Problem framing. Operational constraints. Quality. Review.

The best news about this new layer: traditional engineering skills are more valuable than ever, not less. It helps us minimize shipping slop.

Developers who already invested in CI/CD, testing, documentation, and code review are having the most success with AI tools. These "boring" foundations are accelerators. They turn agents from chaos generators into productivity multipliers.

The real opportunity is learning to work at a different altitude. Instead of typing syntax, we're reviewing implementations, catching edge cases, and shipping features in hours that used to take days. That's genuinely exciting.

Yes, there's a learning curve. Understanding how to provide context, iterate on plans, and review AI-generated code quickly takes practice. But this is learnable through doing - build small tools, review everything, develop intuition through repetition.

The multiplier potential is real when you combine AI speed with engineering judgment. We're not replacing coding skills but we're finally able to focus them on the interesting problems while delegating the tedious parts.

I’m seeing some hot takes that AI assisted coding means that you don’t have to be technical anymore. That’s only gonna last you until the first database migration, or the first security issue, or the first cloud migration, or the first scale out, or the first major regression, or the first refactor that ends in slop. I am finding that I’m learning more and I have to be as technical or more technical than ever before to get the kinds of high-quality output that I expect of any code, regardless of whether it comes from my fingertips or someone else’s - including an AI.

Whether your source comes from open source libraries, your own hands, or an AI via your clever prompt, there is exactly one responsible person for the output. That is you.

I never want to be accused of gatekeeping AI assisted programming, as non-technical people can get a lot of interesting work done. Until they hit a wall, and it’s gonna surprise them how quickly they either need to get technical, or get a technical person to help untangle the mess they’ve made.

The art and science of programming is taking intent and turning it into shipping products. I will never blame an AI - nor should you - for bad output. Own the code that you ship.

A 7-month-old startup I interviewed for in August had 450,000 lines of code. Bloom automated KYC, KYB, underwriting, integrated 20 APIs and raised $377M with 4x less. When I asked why they had so much code, they said I wasn't a culture fit.

When did generating as much code as quickly as possible become a proxy for progress? What happened with achieving business outcomes with as little code as possible? Have we forgotten that more LOC = more bugs = wider attack surface?

I saw a project get rebuilt recently. First version: Lovable (AI code generator) + React + Vite + Supabase serverless. This is exactly what the modern playbook recommends - AI-assisted development, latest UI frameworks, serverless python and DB. Four months in: 4000 commits, stuck at 80% complete, features breaking constantly.

Different developer rewrote it. Rails + Hotwire. Four weeks part-time. Feature complete.

You are supposed to be on Vercel, Railway, AWS, GCP. You are supposed to use serverless. It's more expensive, but more reliable, more secure, more scalable.

Is it?

November 18, 2025: Cloudflare down for 3 hours globally. December 5: down for 25 minutes affecting 28% of all HTTP traffic. The second outage happened while they were patching CVE-2025-55182 - a CVSS 10.0 critical vulnerability in React Server Components allowing remote code execution. This affected Next.js, Vercel, the entire React ecosystem. Active exploitation started within 24 hours of disclosure.

Same month, npm got hit with Shai-Hulud - the first self-replicating worm in npm's history. 796 packages compromised. 25,000 malicious GitHub repositories created in 48 hours. It spread by phishing developer tokens and automatically publishing malicious versions.

October 19-20, 2025: AWS us-east-1 down for 15 hours. DynamoDB DNS failure cascaded to EC2, Lambda, ECS. Snapchat, Fortnite, Slack, Amazon.com itself, McDonald's, Ring - all offline. Estimated financial losses: $500-650 million. The cloud provider selling you multi-region redundancy and high availability concentrated their own critical infrastructure in a single region that became a single point of failure.

Contrast this with:

Lichess serves 5+ million games daily on a single monolith server (96 cores, 192GB RAM). Stack Overflow runs 9 on-premise web servers and serves 2 billion page views per month with a monolithic architecture.

Discord uses Elixir on the BEAM VM with selective Rust integration for performance-critical operations - five engineers manage 20+ services handling millions of concurrent users.

Basecamp published their AWS bill: $3.2M/year. They bought $700K in Dell servers, moved to owned hardware. First year savings: $1M. Five-year projection: $10M+ saved. Annual infrastructure cost now: under $1M.

The companies building at scale rejected the complexity. The 7-month-old startup with 450,000 lines of code thinks I'm not a culture fit for questioning it.

If you were to double-down on a single trait to future-proof your PM career in 2026, it shouldn't be a technical skill or a new methodology. It should be "Agency".

In a domain where technical knowledge and intelligence are now table stakes, Agency is the only attribute that creates outsized impact. We can hire for IQ, but hunger cannot be manufactured. A clever PM with a bias for action will always out-deliver a paralyzed genius every time.

The best PMs I have worked with treat strategy as a catalyst for action rather than a substitute for it. They avoid the trap of over-intellectualizing every possible failure. Instead of waiting for the myth of perfect alignment, they create consensus through progress. They possess the courage to be directionally right and the resolve to navigate the details in real time.

Agency is the difference between being a spectator and the protagonist of your product’s story. A passenger PM treats the roadmap like a set of instructions. When a project stalls, they point to a lack of resources or shifting priorities. They are technically correct but effectively useless, waiting for a permission slip that does not exist.

In contrast, a driver PM recognizes that obstacles are simply variables to be solved. They do not wait for a strategy to be clarified; they draft the proposal and force the decision. They do not complain about a lack of data; they find a proxy and move. While your title gives you a role, your agency gives you the power to shape the outcome. Your agency is your only non-commodity.

As we move further into the age of Agentic AI, routine logic has become a utility. AI can draft PRDs, plan sprints, and predict churn. What remains uniquely human is the appetite for ownership. The machine can provide the "what," but it will never have the accountability to drive the "how" when things break. The real career unlock is the courage to define the problems worth solving in the first place. A great idea has a value of zero until it is executed into reality.

When you find yourself stuck in compliance mode, use a simple mental reset. Ask yourself: "If I shifted into Founder Mode right now, acting as the absolute owner with total freedom to move, what radical move would I make?"

Product Management isn't rocket science, it's simply a high-stakes game of navigating ambiguity. The company gave you the title, but only you can give yourself the authority to matter.

We have become obsessed with the "culture of writing." While Amazon proved that narratives are superior to slides, we have accidentally created a generation of PMs who believe that producing a doc is the same as forming a strategy.

"The doc" is often treated as the engine of strategy when it is actually just the exhaust. That’s the dark side of writing no one talks about. Writing is not thinking.

Strategy is the grueling, invisible work that happens before the first word is ever typed. It is the result of deep research and exhausting mental cycles. Yet, most PMs jump straight to writing a doc because that is what the reporting chains can see. We measure what we can track, and since "thinking" is intangible, we reward the artifact instead of the insight.

We have become experts at hiding behind crutches. 
- we use complex frameworks to make generic ideas look proprietary
- we lean on market research to justify being average
- we cite expert opinions to mask a fear of being wrong.

Real strategy requires the courage to have an independent, original opinion. It is the act of subtraction: the resolve to say "no" to 99% of "good" ideas to focus on the 1% that matters. If your doc is forty pages long, you are not being thorough; you are being indecisive.

The hardest part of this job is having the conviction to decide what you will absolutely not do. This requires nth-order thinking and a brutal assessment of your own execution limits. Writing is helpful for iterating on that logic, but it should never be confused with the strategy itself.

In the age of Agentic AI, the machine can draft your PRDs and summarize your research. It cannot, however, have an original opinion or the accountability to stand by it. That remains the only human job left in the room.

If your PM role is 90% coordination and documentation, you're in direct competition with an AI agent that never sleeps.

AI has changed the game. Already.

Product teams are evolving, and the "PM as scribe" is an endangered role. AI is already mastering the art of the perfect PRD, the crisp status update and the meticulous backlog groom.

Hiring PMs just to maintain these artifacts is a losing strategy.

The future-proof PM adds value where AI cannot. They'll be indispensable for their: 

✅ Taste: A strong, opinionated sense of the why behind the what. A deep, intuitive understanding of user needs, market dynamics and what makes a product "feel" right. This is vision, not just execution. 

✅ Complex Empathy (EQ): The uniquely human ability to navigate ambiguity, inspire teams, manage complex stakeholder relationships, understand the unspoken needs of a customer, and read the room.

✅ System Leadership (AI Management): Moving from "using" AI tools to actively managing a suite of AI agents - knowing what to ask, how to prompt, and how to synthesize AI-generated outputs into a coherent product strategy.

The next generation of great PMs will be hired for taste, EQ and the ability to lead both humans and AI.

Too many people still call the PM role a "mini-CEO." This misconception creates problems throughout the industry.

Product managers who think they should operate like CEOs quickly discover they can't fire anyone, can't unilaterally change company direction, and don't control budgets. Yet they still try to lead through authority they don't have.

The result? Alienated engineering teams, frustrated designers, and stakeholders who see PMs as obstacles rather than partners.

Real product managers succeed through influence, not authority. They build consensus by deeply understanding customer problems and translating them into clear opportunities. They facilitate decisions rather than dictate them.

Instead of telling engineering what to build, a good PM starts bringing customer insights and asking, "How might we solve this together?" This attitude makes engineers actively seek the PM input on technical decisions.

The best PMs I know act more like conductors than CEOs. They don't control every instrument, but they help the orchestra create something beautiful together.

If you're hiring PMs or stepping into the role yourself, drop the CEO comparison. Focus on influence, customer empathy, and cross-functional partnership instead.

What's been your experience with the "mini-CEO" expectation?

🚨 Breaking: First Andrew Ng, now NVIDIA’s own researchers are backing the same two conclusions: 
- Small Language models are better than LLMs for Agentic AI
- Better orchestratrion leads to cost efficient AI Agents

Here are two papers explaining this 👇

📌 Paper 1: "SLM is the Future of Agentic AI"
- NVIDIA argues that agents don't need general conversational genius 100% of the time.
- Actual agentic workflows are mostly narrow tasks :
• "Format this JSON"
• "Call the weather API"
• "Check if this date is valid"

The SLM Advantage
- For these tasks, Small Language Models (SLMs <10B params) are not just "good enough", they are better

• Latency: They react instantly (critical for multi-step agents)
• Cost: 10-30x cheaper per token
• Control: Easier to fine-tune for strict protocols than a stubborn giant model

But wait...

"If we use small models, won't the agent get dumber?"
This is where the second paper changes the game
"Intelligence isn't just raw parameter count; it's about organization"

🔗 https://lnkd.in/dajzm3TV

📌 Paper 2: "ToolOrchestra"
NVIDIA introduced Orchestrator-8B
It’s not a genius at everything, but it’s a genius at management. 
It acts as a "Router"

It analyzes a request and decides: "Do I need the expensive reasoning model for this? Or can I use a cheap calculator tool?"

- The Results are Wild
They tested this on the "Humanity's Last Exam" (HLE) benchmark.
• GPT-5 (Monolithic): 35.1% accuracy
• Orchestrator-8B (Router): 37.1% accuracy

The kicker? 
The Orchestrator system was 2.5x more efficient and cost ~30% of the monolithic baseline

TLDR:
Building "Agentic Systems" by just wrapping a prompt around a massive API is a financial dead end

As usage scales, your margins vanish
Orchestrated approach keeps intelligence high but slashes the compute bill by ~70%
• Monolithic Agents: Expensive, slow, wasteful
• The NVIDIA Way: Orchestrate specialized SLMs.

🔗 https://lnkd.in/dJc8-Res

📌 Key Insight: Intelligence is about routing to the right tool, not being the tool

You can’t outsmart bad data.
Not with agents.
Not with GenAI.
Not even with humanoids that salsa and write poetry.

If your data is trash, your AI will just make…
💩 but faster.
💩 but with a unicorn emoji.
💩 at scale.

Everyone’s chasing copilots, agents, and AGI.
But skip the “boring” stuff like data quality?
And your AI becomes a very expensive fiction writer.

Here’s what most don’t realize:

🔹 Machine Learning needs labeled, trustworthy data
🔹 Generative AI needs high-context, human-like data
🔹 Agentic AI needs clean, structured, real-time data
🔹 Superintelligence? Still needs a foundation it can trust

What to do instead:

→ Map your messy sources
→ Define “good enough” for each use case
→ Align teams on what “clean data” actually means
→ Govern it like your product depends on it (because it does)

AI doesn’t fail because the models are broken.
It fails because the data is.

Coding was never the bottleneck.

Typing code has rarely been the slowest part of software development. Thinking has.

AI makes code cheaper and faster, and that’s useful. But it doesn’t touch the hardest parts of the job.

👨🏽‍💻We still have to decide what problem is worth solving.
👨🏽‍💻We still have to turn fuzzy needs into clear requirements.
👨🏽‍💻We still have to judge whether the thing we built actually works, for users and for the business.

AI can help across the lifecycle, not just at the keyboard:

👀 Explore problem spaces and edge cases.
🙋🏼‍♀️ Challenge assumptions in designs and APIs.
💡Generate test ideas and failure modes.
👓 Review trade-offs, not just syntax.

But none of that removes responsibility. It shifts it upstream.

If you don’t know what “good” looks like, AI will happily produce a lot of very confident nonsense. Faster.

The leverage isn’t replacing engineers with AI. It’s using AI to amplify good engineering judgement.

Code was never the bottleneck. Clarity was.

What’s the best way to document what AI decided—and why?

Most people think AI documentation is about logging data — capturing outputs, timestamps, and probabilities.
But I think that’s missing the real point.

When humans make decisions, we can explain our intent.
When AI makes a decision, it can’t.
Because AI doesn’t have reasons — it has optimizations.

And that, to me, changes everything.

We don’t just need to record what the AI did.
We need to explain what we taught it to optimize for.

Right now, most documentation serves auditors and regulators.
But the real value lies elsewhere — in understanding the alignment gap between human intent and machine logic.
That gap is where trust, accountability, and learning live.

Here’s how I think we can fix it:
✅ Document the goal — what was the AI trying to achieve?
✅ Note the key factors — what influenced the outcome most?
✅ Capture human intent — what assumptions shaped the model?
✅ Acknowledge uncertainty — what could still be wrong?

This way, documentation becomes more than proof — it becomes memory.
A shared record of how humans and machines reason together.

So, what do you think — should AI documentation serve compliance, or should it serve understanding and accountability first?

Google just did to commerce what HTTP did to the web - Universal Commerce Protocol, or UCP, is a new open standard for AI agent shopping 😳

Put simply, Google just dropped a new default for how buying works.

UCP is an open, shared language for AI agents, merchants, and payments to transact end-to-end - autonomously 🤖

Discovery → comparison → negotiation → checkout → returns.

All handled by software.

For 20 years, commerce followed the same flow:

Search → ads → product pages → checkout.

UCP breaks that flow. Now it’s:

Intent → agent reasoning → purchase.

No clicks.
No SEO games.
No funnels.

This isn’t theoretical. UCP launched with some of the biggest names in tech & finance:

Shopify, Walmart, Target, Etsy, Wayfair, Visa, Stripe, Adyen 💳

20+ partners on day one. That’s how standards win.

What UCP actually unlocks:

→ AI agents discover merchant capabilities automatically
→ Negotiate prices, bundles, loyalty, subscriptions
→ Execute payments securely
→ Handle post-purchase support
→ All without custom integrations

This kills the N×N integration problem that has crippled e-commerce for decades.

More importantly, UCP doesn’t replace existing rails. It connects them.

↳ A2A → agents talking to agents
↳ MCP → shared context
↳ AP2 → cryptographically signed payment mandates

UCP is the glue for AI-powered commerce.

And Google is uniquely positioned to win this.

Because at the core, commerce = matching intent with supply at scale.

Google already owns:

→ Global search intent
→ The largest product graph
→ AI models (Gemini) embedded everywhere
→ Distribution (Search, Android, YouTube)

UCP turns that into rails, not a walled garden.

Merchants stay merchant-of-record.
Users keep choice.
Agents do the work.

That’s why this can become the HTTP of commerce.

This marks the beginning of the real disruption.

Brands won’t compete for attention anymore.
They’ll compete to be chosen by machines.

Websites become optional.
Checkout buttons become legacy UI.
Loyalty shifts from humans → agents.

And this is the start of non-human commerce.

Quiet.
Autonomous.

Apple CEO Tim Cook just sent this letter to Apple shareholders.

First thing worth noting is AI. Apple mentions it, but not in the way most companies do. There’s no promise of AGI, no claims about being ahead, no model comparisons. Instead, AI is framed as something built end to end, from silicon to software, designed to be useful, private, and deeply integrated into products people already use. 

Second is hardware leadership. The letter highlights iPhone 17 Pro, the ultra-thin iPhone Air, M5-powered Macs and iPads, Vision Pro, and AirPods Pro 3. They’re not presented as separate launches, but as one system getting tighter, more powerful, and more unified across devices.

Third is ecosystem and values. Services, original content, health, safety, privacy, and accessibility all show up again. This part is classic Apple, but it’s also a signal to investors that revenue is diversified and the model is stable, even as new technologies like AI get layered in.

TL;DR: Apple isn’t trying to win the AI hype cycle. They’re trying to absorb AI without breaking what already works. Slow, controlled, and at scale.

Same Fear, Different Decade
In 1986, teachers took to the streets to protest the calculator. The fear? They feared that the use of calculators would prevent students from learning "real" math concepts. They thought the tool would replace the skill.

Fast forward 40 years. We didn’t stop teaching math. We just stopped wasting time on long division so we could focus on complex problem-solving.

Today, we see the same headlines about AI. "It’s cheating." "It kills creativity." "Juniors won't learn the basics."

History is clear: Banning the tool doesn't save the skill - it just leaves you behind. The calculator didn't replace mathematicians; it made them faster. AI won't replace professionals; it will replace those who refuse to use it.

The most intelligent individuals in the room are not the ones protesting against the future. They are the ones figuring out how to leverage these tools to become 10x more efficient.

Don't be the person with the sign. Be the expert with the tool.

Do you think AI is different, or is this exactly the same situation?

Anthropic just shared a really great read on building quality Agents. Perfect for the weekend.

Here are the main takeaways:
• Start small. You don’t need massive datasets to start. A "Golden Set" of 20–50 high-quality examples is enough to detect regressions and tune performance significantly.
• Use what you have. Don't waste time inventing hypothetical scenarios. Turn your existing manual QA checklists and production bug reports into your first automated test cases.
• Code > LLM Judges. Simple deterministic assertions (exact string match, regex, JSON schema) are faster, cheaper, and more reliable than model-based grading. Maximize these before adding complex LLM graders.
• Outcome over Output. For Agents, don’t just grade the final text response. Evaluate the side effects—verify correct tool usage, API payload structure, and the actual state change in the environment.
• Eval-Driven Development. Write the test case before you prompt-engineer the solution. If you can't measure the failure, you can't reliably fix it.

How to kill a product → Add a software architect to the team

It doesn’t have to be a bad architect. Simply having someone whose sole job is “architecture” is enough. 

Why?
Because doing nothing doesn’t justify a title or a salary. The architect must add something: a new technology, another layer, another pattern. Over time, these “improvements” pile up. I’ve seen far more projects collapse from over-engineering than from under-engineering.

This concept is called “Iatrogenics” i.e. harm caused by the healer. An expert provides small identifiable gains at the cost of exposure to delayed invisible large side effects. In “Antifragile” the author “Naseem Taleb” has mostly applied this concept to medicine and gave examples where the wealthy and famous, with endless access to treatments, often die earlier from the accumulated side effects of unnecessary interventions.

Software is not different. Sometimes the best architecture is restraint. Don’t fix what isn’t broken.

If your product works on a single VPS, you don’t need Kubernetes.
If database-backed queues are fine, you don’t need Kafka or SQS.
If you aren’t drowning in traffic, you don’t need microservices.

Everyone sees the $45M we raised.
Nobody saw me standing in Gulistan-e-Johar thinking, "What the hell have I done with my life?"

I had combined all my McKinsey leave into 5 weeks to test if Retailo could work, and I was going shop to shop in the Karachi heat, pitching small retailers on this B2B aggregation idea.

First pitch: "No way in hell. You'll get me caught by the FBR."

Second pitch: "We've had the same supplier for 20 years. Why on earth would we buy from you?"

I remember standing there after those two rejections, shirt drenched in sweat, thinking this entire experiment was about to blow up in my face.

Third pitch: He bought.

Our conversion rate ended up being 33% - one in three, exactly like those first three pitches.

Everybody sees the funding announcements and the growth numbers. Nobody sees you standing outside a shop questioning everything, wondering if you should just go back to your consulting job and forget this ever happened.

Startups are not for everyone, and that is completely fine. But if you are going to do it, understand that the gap between the polished LinkedIn post and the reality of those early days is where all the actual building happens.

I am writing this on LinkedIn, so obviously we made it work. But standing there after pitch number two, I had no idea if pitch number three was coming.

If I became CMO of a $10M ARR B2B startup, here's what I'd do to kickstart 2026 & 2x ICP pipeline in my first 30 days:

1. Create a list of every target account in my ICP.

I'd ask sales & CS about which factors make a great account. Then I'd bring in real-life data, analyzing all won/lost deals over the past 18 months to see which factors led to the highest expected ARR per 100 opps (equation: win rate x average ACV x NRR x 100).

These factors would almost certainly include industry, company size, and geo. But I'd look to go a level deeper factoring in tech signals, hiring signals and other indicators of buying intent.

2. Identify all the most relevant contacts at those target accounts.

I'd start with initiator and champion personas. Users, influencers, and exec buyers could come later. This data used to be hard to access, unreliable & expensive -- that has quickly changed.

3. Figure out where those target accounts are in their buying journey.

If there were 1,000 accounts in my ICP, how many were we able to identify & market to? How many are aware of us (i.e. visiting the website, engaging with ads, opening emails, etc.)? How many are interested (i.e. viewing an interactive demo, visiting high-intent pages, starting a free trial)? And how many are considering a purchase (i.e. they're sales pipeline)?

From there, the bottlenecks become obvious to the entire team. It's time to tackle them. 

4. Build a pod around the biggest bottleneck.

I'd spin up 10+ tests in the first week. The variables I'd play with: the account signal, the message, the channel, the offer, and the level of 1:1 personalization. (A manual approach would be fine to start with -- I can automate what works later.)

The lowest hanging fruit is usually to convert aware/interested accounts into pipeline. This might mean warm outbound to convert website visitors, testing personalized video over LinkedIn, offering exec access, etc.

5. Create a 🔥 content asset that I know will resonate with my target accounts.

My starting hypothesis: a State of X report featuring interviews & quotes from my ICP. I'd start with highly referenceable existing customers for social proof (low-hanging fruit). I'd then use the report as an excuse to message ICP prospects (the side-benefit: account-specific insights to personalize campaigns).

I'd build this report in public -- creating a content <> community flywheel -- to already drum up interest from my ICP well before the report was published. 

--

Why this 30 day plan works: (a) it's focused on the best-fit accounts, (b) it brings tight alignment between marketing <> sales, (c) it creates quick wins -- earning trust to take bigger swings, (d) it's the best possible onboarding, and (e) it's aggressive -- setting the bar high.

And, if it doesn't work, perhaps I'd propose another brand refresh 🙃

"Agents kill everything" vs "Long live systems of record" is the wrong debate. 

The real question: who captures the layer that actually runs enterprises?

Not the data in your CRM. The decision traces - exceptions, overrides, precedents, cross-system context - that live in Slack threads, deal desks, and people's heads.

Rules tell agents what should happen. Decision traces show what actually happened, who approved it, and why.

Jaya and I call the accumulated structure of those traces a context graph - a living record of how decisions get made, stitched across entities and time.

That's the trillion-dollar opportunity: systems of record for decisions, not just objects.

Big question this time of year: how to run a great Sales Kickoff.

After running and attending kickoffs for more than 20 years, one thing is clear to me: the best kickoffs do two things exceptionally well. They ground the team in the fundamentals, and they energize them to win.

Grounding starts with clarity. Who is our customer? What is our strategy? How do we win?

Energizing comes from making the path to quota real. Every rep wants to believe they can succeed. A leader’s job is to make that belief credible by showing how you’re investing in the right products, the right systems, and the right teams to support them.

This year, there’s a real opportunity to reimagine sales. 

Get back to customer basics and help your team see what is changing. Buyers are more informed than before. They’re reading reviews, browsing communities, and doing deep research in LLMs. Sellers need to provide insights, not just information, from the very first call. SKO should be explicit about what’s changing and what’s expected to change in how sellers show up.

Get back to core sales skills. AI is accelerating research, personalization, and follow-ups. At the same time, human skills - earning trust, asking better questions, navigating real objections - matter more than ever. Pick the few skills that truly move the needle this year and show your team a new way to win.

Energize your team by removing friction. The work that used to slow sales teams down—manual work, notes, follow-ups—can now be automated by capturing conversations and flowing data into the right systems. Be clear about how you’re making their jobs easier and where you’re investing to support them.

Get your Q1 right to get your year right. 

How are you preparing for Sales Kickoff 2026? What new opportunities are you excited to discuss with your team?

"Marketing Metrics" - new cartoon and post:

I recently spoke at a conference with Rory Sutherland, Vice Chairman of Ogilvy, and he shared a fascinating insight about the influence of ad platforms on how we think about marketing.

Rory said that many of the metrics that marketers chase are not designed to serve the brand. They're designed to serve the platforms that provide those metrics.

Rory coined a term to describe this phenomenon: "technoplasmosis."

Referencing a parasite called "toxoplasmosis" that takes over the behavior of cats, Rory draws an analogy that ad tech platforms are similarly influencing the behavior of marketers.

Behavior science consultant Adel Borky expanded on Rory's concept of technoplasmosis in The Drum a few months ago (and first introduced me to this concept):

"We congratulate ourselves on being “data-driven” and “efficient”, when in fact we’ve been induced into mistaking a platform’s KPIs for our own...

"Technology doesn’t just give us new tools; it changes what we see as important. Once dashboards and click-through rates exist, they begin to dictate the questions we ask and the answers we accept. If it can’t be measured by the platform, it starts to look irrelevant. If it can, it begins to feel like truth.

"That is the first symptom of technoplasmosis: a slow shift in our evaluative criteria. We stop asking “Does this build a lasting brand?” and start asking 'Will this improve our campaign dashboard next week?'"

"A platform optimises for its own engagement, growth and revenue. If we aren’t careful, we end up serving those imperatives rather than our own.

"Brands disappear into the machine... Instead of building desire, we build compliance with an algorithm."

In our push to be data-driven, we always have to question the source and incentives behind the data we have access to.

There's a famous quote from William Bruce Cameron in 1963 that is newly relevant in today's data-driven world:

“Not everything that counts can be counted, and not everything that can be counted counts.”

Foundation Capital's piece on Context Graphs as AI's next trillion-dollar opportunity is making the rounds. For good reason.

For those who’ve been following my posts, the thesis will feel familiar. But the article offers a fresh and timely articulation of why this moment matters now.

What really resonated - both for me and in the follow-up commentary - is how clearly it identifies the missing layer in the enterprise data stack.

Today, we tend to think about enterprise data in two places: 

🔹 inside systems of record 
🔹 aggregated downstream into data warehouses

There is almost nothing in between.

But real decisions are never made in a single system.

They are made by stitching together signals from CRM, finance, operations, support systems, policy documents, Slack threads - often with human judgement applied at the seams.

A simple example: 
An agent proposes a 20% discount. 
Policy caps it at 10% unless an exception is approved. 
The agent pulls service incidents, an open escalation, and a prior approval thread. 
Finance signs off. 
The CRM records one fact: “20% discount”.

The reasoning trace - the why - disappears.

Once the decision is made, the connective tissue is gone.

And that’s the key insight:

⚡the most valuable data for enterprise AI is not just what happened, but how and why a decision was reached.⚡

This mirrors exactly what foundation model companies discovered when they started building reasoning models. Performance didn’t improve just by scaling data - it improved when they began collecting reasoning traces.

In the enterprise, that trace is the human decision process itself.

The article’s framing is spot on. 
Systems of record and data warehouses excel at storing facts, events, and transactions. What they don’t capture is the context that explains them - the exceptions, precedents, constraints, and trade-offs that shaped a particular outcome.

That context is where meaning lives.

And yes - that is semantics in action.

What’s been most interesting is how widely this idea resonates: 

🔹 builders see it as the architectural gap holding back agentic AI 
🔹 strategists see it as the next durable data moat 
🔹 practitioners recognise it as tacit knowledge that currently lives only in people’s heads

If we can persist decision context as a first-class asset, we don’t just get better AI.  We get explainability, auditability, organisational memory, and safer automation.

The authors are right. This is a trillion-dollar market.

Here's what they didn't say: the technology to build this already exists. Open standards. Battle-tested. Mostly free. Ready now.

The gap isn't technical. It's awareness.

Lots of people much smarter than me have been talking about this compelling new idea called the "Context Graph". I love the idea, but am a wee bit skeptical of far away we are from it being something businesses can use.

What a year it has been. Leadership in 2025 quietly changed shape. 

AI stopped being a thought experiment and needed to become a functional part of enterprise infrastructure. But did it work, really? Could it?

The leaders who earned real trust were not the ones shouting the loudest about AI, but the ones willing to admit that without governance, security, and trust, “AI at scale” is just expensive chaos.

We watched it happen in real time, organizations racing ahead on models and features while lagging badly on the fundamentals – data quality, guardrails, business context, accountability, and resilience. 

Leading meant taking a dynamic approach to balance: when to prioritize speed, when to prioritize caution, always staying comfortable with novelty and disruption. 

That is why 2025 was the year ServiceNow went all-in on becoming an AI‑native enterprise – not just in what is shipped, but in how we build, operate, and help customers transform. We shipped autonomous workflows that can sense, decide, act, and govern across systems at a speed and scale that would have been unthinkable a few years ago. 

AI Control Tower, Build Agent, Voice Agent, AI Experience, AI Agent Fabric, Workflow Data Fabric, and so much more… Orchestrated, fast, and, just as importantly, safe.

If 2025 was the year of truths being revealed, 2026 will be the reckoning.

Many organizations will discover that what they have been sold are isolated agents that cannot talk to each other, models that hallucinate in production without guaranteeing an outcome, and architectures that leave security and risk teams paralyzed. 

In the year ahead, leadership beyond certainty will be defined by this: who treats governance, security, and trust as the non-negotiable foundation of any scaled AI deployment, and who gets left behind?

It can no longer be that confidence equals control and speed equals secrecy. True leaders of tomorrow are stewards, not commanders.
AI strategy and risk strategy are now the same conversation; no more innovation roadmaps that sit apart from context, cyber, compliance, and ethics. Bringing them together is where competitive advantage quietly compounds. That’s where an AI control tower stands strong. 

So as we move into next year, here's my commitment: We will keep earning your trust, and your business.

The hard work is just beginning, but I can't think of a more important mission or a better team to make it real.

The most dangerous trait in a Product Manager is the inability to be mediocre.

In high-growth environments, the most valuable asset you have isn't your time - it's your cognitive bandwidth. Elite PMs recognize that true excellence is not a blanket state; it is a finite resource that must be deployed with extreme prejudice.

True mastery requires the discipline of "strategic mediocrity". It is the intentional choice to underperform on the trivial to over-deliver on the vital. This operating model is built on three pillars:

1. The power law of impact: Impact is never linear. Most activities occupy the long tail of near-zero contribution, while 1–2 variables drive the entire outcome. Success is the discernment to isolate these needle-movers. A completed list of low-value tasks is not productivity; it is a sophisticated form of procrastination.

2. Triage as a primary competency: In high-growth environments, your workload is a crisis ward. You must categorize with clinical detachment:

- The signal: Where the product’s future is won. This requires 100% of your craft

- The utility: Necessary but non-differentiating. "Functional" is the target; anything more is waste.

-The noise: Unavoidable org friction. This requires the bare minimum effort to remain compliant.

3. Selective imperfection as a defensive tactic: Cognitive load is finite capital. Every "extra 10%" spent polishing a low-stakes artifact is a direct tax on the next breakthrough insight. You reclaim world-class energy by letting the "noise" be imperfect. Mediocrity in the periphery is the necessary price for making the signal undeniable.

The bottom line: Average performers try to raise the floor of their entire workload. Outliers choose to shatter the ceiling on the vital, even if it means letting the floor drop on the trivial.

If you aren't being criticized for "doing just enough" on the small things, you likely aren't doing enough on the things that matter.

One of the earliest customer calls I took completely changed the way I think about product development. He used Gamma heavily, and I wanted to understand why.

Ironically, he spent almost the entire call talking about Notion.

He showed me his workspace. His personal wiki. His daily notes. His project trackers.

He could not stop raving about Notion.

At the end I finally asked: "What do you love about Notion so much?"

He paused. Then said, almost embarrassed:

"It makes me feel smarter."

Before that conversation, I thought about Gamma in functional terms. Reimagining PowerPoint. New primitives instead of slides. Web-like, mobile-friendly, interactive.

But that user forced me to see something I'd been ignoring:

Products don't just do things for people. They make people feel a certain way about themselves.

"Feel smarter" is an emotion. Once you notice it, you see it everywhere.

Notion makes you feel like someone who has a system.

Superhuman makes you feel like you're finally in control of your inbox.

Stripe makes you feel like you're running a "real" company.

So the obvious question became: "What do we want people to feel when they use Gamma?"

The feeling that kept coming up was: imaginative.

Most of us are visual learners, but very few of us are visual designers. That gap is where people get stuck. They have ideas worth sharing but feel blocked the moment they open a blank slide.

The feeling I wanted was the one you have as a kid, sitting on the floor with markers and too much paper. No one's grading you. You're not "presenting." You're just making things.

Most of us lose that. You go into the workforce and your imagination gets compressed into bullet points and slides.

We built Gamma to bring that feeling back.

Liquid Death paid $0 for distribution and still got into 60,000 stores

It looks like beer
It's marketed like energy drinks
It's priced at $1.50-3 per can

They launched in 2019
By 2023, they were in 60,000+ retail locations

Whole Foods, 7-Eleven, Target, Walmart

Here's what nobody talks about: They didn't pay for any of that shelf space.

Retailers came to them

How?

They built proof before they needed distribution.

The playbook:

Phase 1: Amazon only (2019-2020)

They didn't launch in stores. They launched where they could buy reviews.

Tactic: Send cases to people who agreed to leave verified reviews. Not fake reviews. Just incentivized honest ones ($10-15 Amazon gift cards for review).

Within 6 months: 12,000+ verified 5-star reviews on Amazon.

Phase 2: Data package (2020-2021)

They took that Amazon data to retailers:

"We have 12,000 5star reviews. Here's our velocity (units sold per week). Here's our repeat purchase rate. Here's our customer demos."

Retailers saw proof of demand before taking the risk.

Phase 3: Social proof (ongoing)

By the time they hit Whole Foods, their Instagram had 2M+ followers.

Whole Foods didn't discover them. Whole Foods customers were already asking for them.

But here's the actual innovation:

The product positioning wasn't "healthy water".

It was "water for people who hate water brands".

The tagline: "Murder Your Thirst"

The cans: Covered in skulls and death imagery

The marketing: Looked like Monster Energy or craft beer

They made water cool by making it not look like water.

Now the retention play:

Once someone bought Liquid Death once, they bought it 4.2 times in the first 90 days.

Why? Because it's a conversation starter.

You drink it at a party, someone asks "what's that?" You explain. They try it. They're now in the funnel.

For your brand:

You don't need retail distribution on day one. 

You need proof.

The path:

1. Launch where you can control reviews and velocity (Amazon, your DTC site)
2. Build 1,000+ verified reviews and real sales data
3. Package that data for retailers: "Our customers are asking for us in your stores"
4. Use the data to negotiate better terms (retailers give better placement to proven products)

Liquid Death didn't convince retailers to take a risk

They eliminated the risk by proving demand first

In 10+ years leading CS teams, I’ve never missed an annual target.

Is that because I’m special?
Not at all.

It’s because I've always implemented a strong operating cadence with my teams.

→ We didn’t have the fanciest tools.
→ We definitely didn’t have the biggest budgets.
→ But we always had a robust execution rhythm.

One that turned strategy into results.

Week after week.
Quarter after quarter.

Here are 6 critical cadences every CS team should build in 2026:

🎯 Annual Goal Setting
Once a year, define what success looks like. Set measurable goals. Make them visible. Everything else should ladder up to this.

🔮 Weekly Forecasting
No surprises. No end-of-quarter scrambles. That $50K renewal sliding to 'at risk'? You know about it as soon as it happens and can act on it early. Weekly forecasting keeps your pipeline actionable and visible.

♟️ Bi-Monthly Leading Indicators Review
Leading indicators show you what's coming. Review engagement, adoption, and usage. That helps you catch issues before they become critical. This is where prevention happens.

🚩 Monthly Red Account Review
Troubled accounts need sunlight, not blame. Get a cross-functional team in a room, unpack the issues, assign clear owners, and build action plans.

🏆 Monthly Win/Loss Review
Every churn is a lesson. Every renewal is a clue. This cadence is about pattern recognition, what's working, and what needs to change. Make sure you share the insights with the team (I send a loom).

🗓️ Quarterly Review & Planning
Zoom out. Are we on track? What needs a reset? Use this time to course-correct, celebrate wins, and set priorities for the next 90 days.

If you establish these cadences, you create a heartbeat.

A rhythm that keeps your team focused, aligned, and high-performing.

I die a little inside every time I hear someone say “Customer Success is about retention.”

I’ve heard it from students when I started.
From people outside CS.
From very senior Sales leaders.

And yeah, I understand where it comes from.
Churn hurts. Revenue needs to be protected.

But that sentence still misses the point.

I didn’t get into Customer Success to stop people from leaving.
I got into it to help customers succeed.
Solve real problems and grow their business.

When retention becomes the goal, everything turns defensive.
You start at 100% and your only job is not to lose anything.

Budget cuts. Bad timing. Wrong fit.
Things you don’t control… but still get blamed for.

So CS turns reactive.
Firefighting.
Saving accounts at all costs.
Treating every customer the same.
Spending energy where there’s no upside.

That’s exhausting.

Thinking about CS as a growth function changes the posture.

Instead of “how do we keep everyone?” it becomes “where can we actually make a difference?”

Helping the right customers expand their usage, their results, their business.

Some customers will leave. That’s just reality. But others will grow faster because you focused on them.

You get to prioritize and to do work that compounds.
You stop running endless rescue missions.

Retention still matters. Obviously.
But it’s a consequence, not the mission.

For me, that shift is the difference between being in constant emergency mode
and actually being a partner.

If Customer Success was only about retention, I wouldn’t want this job.

The hardest lesson in Customer Success? 


Not every account needs the same attention.

I've seen too many CS teams burn out trying to give white-glove service to every single customer. Meanwhile, their highest-value accounts aren't getting the strategic partnership they need to expand.

Here's the framework that works for me:

📍MAINTAIN (Low Risk, Low Value)
Your efficiency plays. Automated onboarding, self-service resources, and health-check emails. Keep them healthy without burning CSM hours.

📍RETAIN (High Risk, Low Value)
Your fire drills. Rapid risk diagnosis, short-term recovery plans, executive escalation. Get them stable or let them go gracefully.

📍EXPAND 
→ 𝐇𝐢𝐠𝐡 𝐕𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞, 𝐋𝐨𝐰 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐤
Your growth engine. This is where the magic happens -QBRs, strategic roadmap discussions, champion programs, and co-marketing opportunities.
→ 𝐇𝐢𝐠𝐡 𝐕𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞, 𝐇𝐢𝐠𝐡 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐤
Your rescue missions have a massive upside. Jump in fast, diagnose issues, build recovery plans, then shift to expansion mode.

𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐂𝐒 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐭'𝐬 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐤 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐟𝐢𝐥𝐞.

Your CS team shouldn't be stretched thin - they should be strategically deployed.

The most dangerous person in a Customer Success org is the "Hero."

Early in my career, I wanted to be the hero. I thought saving a churn-threatened account at 11:00 PM on a Friday was the ultimate sign of leadership.

It wasn't. It was a sign of a broken process.

Now, as a CSM at Cloudflare managing mid-market accounts with enterprise complexity, I’ve realized that "Heroics" are actually a form of operational debt.

When you rely on heroes:
• Results are tied to individuals not thecompany.
• Your best people burn out.
• The "fix" is never documented or repeatable.

Executive-minded leadership is about moving from Heroics to Health.

Heroics look like:
• Manual workarounds to fix a product gap.
• Last-minute "save" calls with executives.
• CSMs knowing more than the CRM does.

Operational Health looks like:
• Playbooks that trigger before red flag rise.
• Data that predicts friction points 6 months out.
• A repeatable rhythm that makes the renewal feel inevitable.

My TORCHED framework (Trust, Ownership, Responsibility, Credibility, Honesty, Empathy, Discipline) demands Discipline. 

And discipline means trusting the process enough to stop acting like a firefighter.

Real growth doesn't come from the fire you put out today. It comes from the system you build to make sure the fire never starts.

What’s one part of your process that still relies too much on "heroics" and not enough on a system?

I was having dinner recently with the CEO of a super fast-growing AI-enabled service. He has tremendous demand for his product from trendsetter customers. But he's worried serving this demand could in fact kill his company. Here's why:

While his company has the capacity to deliver great results for these customers, doing so will distract them from the necessary learning/product work to develop AI to improve the efficiency of service delivery. Getting hooked on the revenue growth drug can mean the "AI" part of the AI-enabled service gets neglected.

PMF is a different beast in AI-enabled services. Strong revenue growth and NDR can mask a lack of true AI enablement. We call it "Mirage PMF".

Real PMF in AI-enabled services requires proving you can scale non-linearly relative to your costs. To get there, your AI must drive measurable improvements in cost, quality, or speed—or ideally, all 3.

I work with another AI-enabled services founder who lost one of their biggest customers after an unexpected merger. While initially quite disappointing, this loss was a blessing in disguise; it freed up capacity to accelerate AI productization efforts, and the company is now much further down the non-linear growth/cost curve.

TL;DR- if you're building an AI-enabled service, don't let revenue growth alone fool you into thinking you've found PMF. In theory, AI should make things dramatically cheaper, faster, or higher quality. Otherwise, you're just scaling human effort, not tech.

Without that, you might have a good business—just not a venture-scale business.

Reflecting on the past w/ an eye towards the future:

2025 was one of the most special years in tech.

Not because we shipped more features, but because the pace of change stopped being a tech-industry thing and became an every-industry thing.

I like to reflect on two questions:
 1. What did I underestimate?
 2. What should I pay attention to next?

Here’s my take.

THE thing I underestimated in 2025: culture of rapid adaptability is the force multiplier.

I found it extremely difficult to predict which companies to bet on & which to write off. There’s an engrained duality in every company:

• fighting irrelevance
• staring at an opportunity 1–2 orders of magnitude bigger than anything they’ve seen before

In that world, strategy matters. But it’s not enough.

What moved the needle most was a company’s internal “operating system”: its norms, decision speed, and willingness to change course without drama.

In an era of hyper-automation, I underestimated how much a company’s ceiling is set by:

• how fast it can learn
• how quickly it can unlearn old defaults
• how consistently it can adapt as conditions change

The winners are not just the ones with the best tech. They’re the ones with the highest rate of adaptation.

Looking ahead to 2026+, AI is still constrained on 3 dimensions:

• infrastructure availability
• trust deficit
• data gap

So here are 3 mega-trends I’m watching because they bend those constraints in real ways.

 1. Space Datacenters
On Earth, AI hits hard walls: power, cooling, land, and time-to-build. Space changes the equation with far more consistent solar, and in vacuum, optical links become more viable for long-distance connectivity. If this becomes real, the constraint shifts from electrons to launches: can rockets reliably lift, service, and upgrade massive payloads in orbit at the cadence and cost that make sense?

 2. Large World Models
Language is important, but the world is 3D: space, motion, physics, cause-and-effect. Large World Models are the shift from “predicting words” to “understanding worlds,” including simulating what happens next. The hard part is data: text is abundant; high-quality 3D + time + action data is not, so simulation and synthetic data will matter. If this clicks, AI shifts from “answering” to “anticipating,” and from “chatting” to “planning.”

 3. Agentic getting to production-grade
2025 was the transition from chatbots to agents. We saw a ton of prototypes (coding moved fastest), but most was still prototype-grade. In 2026, we’ll see production-grade agents: reliable, safe, auditable, and governed by permissions and policy. Better reasoning loops and reinforcement learning will help agents plan, self-correct, and work in teams. The real shift won’t be tactical task automation. Instead entire workflows and lifecycles will get reimagined

Exciting times ahead but ones where adaptability will REALLY matter!

SaaS isn’t slowing down. It’s getting eaten alive ... cannibalized.

Look at the Aventis Advisors growth chart. The trend isn’t subtle, it’s a cliff.

We went from 36 percent growth to 12 percent. Almost a decade of down and to the right.

With forecasts pointing to 11 percent … and falling, this feels like a pretty big structural shift. SaaS is starting to look like utilities and pipelines, durable and necessary, but no longer where the real upside lives.

And the reason is pretty simple. AI is cannibalizing the very work SaaS used to monetize. 

Here’s what the chart doesn’t show, but every operator feels.

1) SaaS used to sell “workflows.” AI sells “outcomes” (OaaS). Agents do the work inside the tool, so the tool stops being the product. The labor becomes the product. 

2) Budgets (and investors) are leaving SaaS and flowing to digital labor. CFOs aren’t buying more seats. They’re buying fewer humans. AI fits. SaaS doesn’t.

3) Feature parity killed differentiation. Entire categories are indistinguishable. CRM, CX, marketing automation … all the same. AI exposes how thin the moats always were.

4) Enterprises hit peak-SaaS years ago. Now they’re consolidating and cutting 20 to 40 percent of their stack. AI accelerates that purge.

5) AI startups are growing at speeds SaaS can’t touch. When companies hit nine figures in months, not years, investor expectations reset. SaaS looks slow, expensive, and operationally bloated.

6) Value is moving down the stack. The action is in compute, data, agents, and orchestration. SaaS is becoming a UI layer that AI sits on, not the engine driving the work.

The growth-rate collapse isn’t a mystery, it’s more of a transfer of value. SaaS is maturing into a stable, cash-flow asset class. 

AI is becoming the new growth engine of the enterprise.

That means founders have a choice, build SaaS and optimize it like infrastructure, or build AI agents that replace the workflows SaaS was built to capture.

One path gives you stable multiples, the other gives you growth.

A lot of companies want CMO-level impact… layered under a CRO.

Or they level the entire LT as CXOs and downgrade marketing to VP.

That setup tells me one thing:

They don’t understand or value marketing.

When marketing rolls up to sales, the aperture shrinks. Everything gets measured on immediacy.

If it doesn’t convert fast, it’s deprioritized.

Brand? Cut.
Community? Later.
Bold positioning & category work? “Cool, but what does it do this quarter?”

Before you know it, you’re only speaking to the 5% of the market that’s ready to buy right now… and ignoring the 95% who’ll decide whether you even get a shot later.

Then you’re blamed when pipeline dries up.

I had a moment where this crystallized for me.

Every month, our sales team showed our biggest deals at all-hands, 
highlighting the *last touch*.

I suggested showing the full buyer journey instead of just the last few emails. 

Looking at last touch, nothing marketing ran to reach these accounts had “worked.”

When we dug deeper, those same accounts had been orbiting the brand for months:

- connecting & engaging with our team on LI
- showing up to webinars & workshops
- attending events
- engaging with ads in a flurry right before the demo form submission

When they finally converted, sales saw a timely email.

But it wasn’t luck.

Here’s what we uncovered when we mapped true buyer journeys with Dreamdata + customer feedback:

🔥 8 channels on avg worked together as an ecosystem across every closed-won deal
 ⏳ Months of buyer engagement & discovery before the last touch
 🕵🏻‍♀️ Our buyers were “dark funnel” shoppers: they decided who to trust long before the demo
 📈 120% larger deal sizes & 11% increase in win rates when buyers followed our team on LI
 🎯 Gifting & events drove larger deals bc multithreading + deeper relationships = trust

And here’s what changed:

We stopped fighting for credit between sales & marketing

We stopped wasting budget on what was easiest to track in the CRM

We got the receipts to show that brand marketing drove the majority of pipeline & revenue

Marketing gets mis-leveled when leadership only sees the last click.

When the only stories told in the room are short-term ones.
When the metrics that matter most to future revenue never make it into the narrative.

A CMO can’t do their job if their remit is limited to “feeding the quarter.”

A VP title with CMO expectations creates a credibility gap that no dashboard can fix.

And a marketing org graded solely on short term pipeline speed is doomed to atrophy the exact muscles that create real demand.

CMOs deserve a seat where their work is evaluated on business impact, not just how fast they fed the outbound team this month.

Real alignment is marketing and sales partnering together from the same truth.

It’s sales caring about the 70% of the deal that happens before the demo and marketing caring about the 30% that gets it across the line.

That only happens when the CMO is treated as a peer and a partner.

Your content marketing team should stop creating content that doesn’t include external voices. Period.

Most content practitioners are still living in a 2010 dreamland of distribution lists and nurture tracks. The world has changed. The way we buy has changed. The BUYER has changed! 

When I think about content marketing today, I bucket it into four pillars:

1. Market & External Comms – Shape market perception through strategic narrative and media.

2. Internal Comms & Advocacy – Keep teams aligned and turn employees into storytellers.

3. Executive Thought Leadership – Elevate exec voices and communities to build category leadership.

4. Benchmarking & Research – Establish authority with original data

External voices should run through all four of these. 

Here’s something you can literally do tomorrow:

Take your targeted account list, identify the people you want relationships with, and reach out:

“We’re publishing a research report on [topic]. We’d love to include your perspective. Can we grab 10 minutes and ask you two questions?”
Interview them. Ship the report. Feature their names, quotes, and stories.

I’ve done this multiple times, and the interviews often turned into large deals later, because when they are ready to buy, they remembered us. They remembered that we wanted their expertise, not a demo.

Everyone can ship an AI ebook in an afternoon. That’s noise. Your buyers are increasingly influenced by mini communities and peer groups, not your homepage.

Your customers are your best salespeople. And your community deserves to be included in your “thought leadership.”

Let your competitors crank out junk content at scale. You can go talk to the humans. They’re the ones making the buying decisions.

It's clear we're approaching 2026 and most of us are right where we were 15 years ago. In fact, it might be worse.

I've been doing 4-5 calls with Pavilion Members a day for the past few weeks as we near the holidays. The goal is to help as many people as possible during what can be a high anxiety time.

The list goes on and on.

My friend proudly announced a $40M exit on LinkedIn.
The comments said "life-changing money." 
His wire transfer said $1.4M for 7 years. 

$1.4M for initial distribution after escrow and withholdings.
You’ll receive $2.8M total. 

That’s $400K a year for seven years of work

You build the company for 7 years.
Raised $12M across two rounds.

Liquidation preferences meant investors got paid first 
(1x non-participating.)

$40M sale price.
Investors recoup $12M off the top.
$28M remaining.

You own 18% after dilution 
(a normal outcome after two rounds and option pool refreshes).

That's $5M before taxes.

Federal capital gains. 
State taxes. 
Legal fees.

QSBS didn’t fully apply. some founders qualify, many don’t.

$5M became $3.2M.

But wait.

You had a $400K loan against equity that you took during a rough patch.
Margin loans are common for founders pre-liquidity.

$3.2M became $2.8M.

Your co-founder got the same deal.
Their proceeds were split across multiple tranches.
Early employees got less.

Some got nothing after the preference stack.

$2.8M for 7 years.

That's ~$400K per year in risk-adjusted, salary-equivalent terms.

NOT a paycheck. 
This is a lump sum after seven years of concentrated risk.

A senior director at Salesforce makes $450K with RSUs, benefits, and weekends off.

You don’t make "life-changing money."
You made a good salary with 10x the stress and no safety net.

A LinkedIn post announcing the exit will get you 2,000 likes.

Nobody asked what you actually took home
Or when, or how much was held back.

I'm not saying don't build but do the math before you celebrate.
Or before you decide to take VC dollars. Run some scenarios. 

Preference stacks. 
Dilution. 
Taxes. 
Loans.

A $40M exits sound like freedom and a windfall.
Most startups with this outcome are just jobs with bad terms.
