Teaching an experimental class for MBAs on “vibefounding,” the students have four days to come up and launch a company. More on this eventually, but quick observations:

1) I have taught entrepreneurship for over a decade. Everything they are doing in four days would have taken a semester in previous years, if it could have done it at all. Quality is also far better.

2) Give people tools and training and they can do amazing things. We are using a combination of Claude Code, Gemini, and ChatGPT. The non-coders are all building working products. But also everyone is doing weeks of high quality work on financials, research, pricing, positioning, marketing in hours. All the tools are weird to use, even with some training, but they are figuring it out.

3) People with experience in an industry or skill have a huge advantage as they can build solutions that have built-in markets & which solve known hard problems that seemed impossible. (Always been true, but the barriers have fallen to actually doing stuff)

4) The hardest thing to get across is that AI doesn’t just do work for you, it also does new kinds of work. The most successful efforts often take advantage of the fact that the AI itself is very smart. How do you bring its analytical, creative, and empathetic abilities to bear on a problem? What do you do with access to a very smart intelligence on demand?

I wish I had more frameworks to clearly teach. So many assumptions about how to launch a business have clearly changed. You don’t need to go through the same discovery process if you build a dozen ideas at the same time & get AI feedback. Many, many new possibilities, and the students really see how big a deal this is.

In early 2016, I met with Sam Altman when OpenAI was an idea and Cerebras was a PowerPoint.
 
AI was nothing like it is today.
 
Back then, we were classifying cats and dogs.
 
Sam became an investor in Cerebras Systems.
We became advocates of OpenAI.
 
I remember leaving that meeting thinking… holy sh*t.
They were talking about building AGI, superintelligence, and AI safety when most people were still arguing about image recognition benchmarks.
 
We were thinking about wafer scale chips,
when the world was sure that was impossible.
 
Real vision often looks a little crazy at first.
The line between science fiction and vision is often only clear in hindsight.
And even then not always.
 
You have to believe.

In our case, that shared belief has carried both our companies a long way.

The most expensive mistakes in history weren’t made by weak companies.

They were made by dominant ones.

Blockbuster wasn’t clueless.
Kodak wasn’t behind. 

Nokia, BlackBerry, Yahoo, Sears, Xerox, MySpace, Toys “R” Us all had talent, capital, reach, and trust.

They didn’t fail because they lacked resources.
They failed because they protected what worked longer than they questioned what was coming.

That’s the part people skip.

Blockbuster didn’t lose to Netflix.
They lost to the belief that DVDs were “good enough.”

Kodak didn’t miss digital photography.
They invented it.
They just couldn’t accept what it would cost them.

Nokia and BlackBerry weren’t slow.
They were confident. 

And confidence turned into blindness.

Yahoo didn’t miss Google once.
They said no twice.

Xerox saw the future in their labs and let someone else build it.

The pattern is always the same.

Here’s what history keeps repeating if you’re willing to look 👇

1/ Success creates attachment.
What made you win becomes what you defend.

2/ Ego delays adaptation.
Change feels like admitting the past is expiring.

3/ Comfort sounds rational.
Until the market stops caring about your reasons.

4/ Ideas alone don’t protect you.
Execution does. Timing does. Courage does.

5/ The future rarely announces itself loudly.
It shows up quietly, looking optional.

None of these companies failed overnight. 

They declined slowly while still believing they were fine.

That’s the real danger.

Because failure doesn’t usually come from doing the wrong thing. It comes from refusing to let go of the right thing after its time has passed.

History doesn’t punish lack of intelligence.
It punishes resistance to change.

👉 What’s something that works well for you today
 that you might need to question before it becomes a liability?

That’s the question that decides whether success compounds
 or quietly expires.

What Happens to Competitive Advantage When Every Firm Uses the Same AI?

Lately, I’ve been thinking about this.
If every company is powered by the same models, trained on the same data, and chasing the same efficiencies, what’s left to compete on?

For years, technology created differentiation.
Now, it risks creating uniformity.

I think the real advantage is quietly shifting —
from access to AI to the ability to apply it uniquely.

Because tools don’t create advantage — taste does.
The same algorithm in two different hands can lead to entirely different outcomes.

Here’s what I’ve noticed in the most forward-thinking companies:
→ They don’t just ask, “What can AI automate?”
They ask, “What do we want to automate — and what should remain human?”
→ They treat data not as an asset, but as a perspective — a way to see opportunities others can’t.
→ They use AI to amplify their culture, not replace it.

In my opinion, the firms that win won’t be the ones using the best models —
but the ones asking the best questions.

So maybe the new competitive edge isn’t technical at all.
It’s philosophical.

✅ The courage to stay different when technology pushes sameness.
✅ The judgment to know when to trust data — and when to trust instinct.
✅ The wisdom to use AI as leverage, not identity.

Because when everyone has the same intelligence,
the only true advantage left is how you think.

So, what do you think?
does AI level the playing field, or just raise the bar for originality?

Can technology preserve humanity’s soul—or rewrite it?

I’ve been thinking about this a lot lately.

Every breakthrough in AI feels like a mirror — showing us both our genius and our fragility.

In my opinion, technology doesn’t just extend human capability. It reshapes what it means to be human.

Because every time we let a machine think, decide, or create for us, we’re quietly outsourcing a part of our inner world — intuition, reflection, even empathy.

And that makes me wonder: are we preserving our essence through technology, or gradually rewriting it line by line?

I think the answer depends on how consciously we design this relationship.

If technology amplifies our best traits — creativity, compassion, curiosity — it becomes a mirror that reflects the soul.

But if it amplifies only efficiency and speed, it risks becoming a replacement, not an extension.

So here’s what I believe we should do:
✅ Design systems that prioritize meaning over metrics.
✅ Teach machines to serve human values, not replace them.
✅ Keep humans in the loop — not because we’re perfect, but because we care.

Technology will rewrite many things about our world.

The question is whether we’ll stay the authors of that story.
What do you think — is technology preserving humanity’s soul, or quietly rewriting it?

The fastest way to understand AI governance?
Stop thinking about data. Start thinking about decisions.

There's a lot of noise around governance in the age of AI.
And leaders cut through some of that noise by understanding the split between inputs and outcomes.

Data governance protects the inputs.
It’s about accuracy, privacy, lineage, access and compliance.
It answers: Can we trust the data going into our systems?

AI governance protects the outcomes.
It deals with fairness, explainability, drift and human oversight.
It answers: Can we trust the decisions made on outputs of AI systems?

Both matter.
Both are essential.
But they serve different purposes.

What I’ve seen inside organisations this year is that AI governance doesn’t sit neatly with one owner.
Legal, risk, product, ethics, engineering and the business all hold a piece.

That’s normal, because the outcomes affect so many parts of the organisation.

The companies making real progress connect the two:
They build strong data governance first. Clean foundations mean AI systems fail predictably, not catastrophically.

Then they layer AI governance on top as the mechanism that gives business leaders confidence to actually use what's been built.

Have a look at the two side by side to see where each plays a role.

What's your experience? Where does AI governance live in your organisation, and is it working?

Non-PMs: Bro just disable gallery uploads. Problem solved. Simple!
True PMs: Is this even a real problem?

Here’s where real product management begins. Before fixing, a PM applies first-principles thinking and asks whether there is a real problem. 

Zomato is a massive marketplace. On the food delivery side alone, it serves millions of orders every single day (e.g., reports have shown days with >2 million orders placed) - nearly 1,400 orders per minute on some days. 

When your product has tens of millions of DAU/MAU and millions of daily transactions, even a small % of disputable orders can potentially signify a large absolute issue or just noise. 

So a thoughtful PM would first explore:
- How many daily refunds involve photos?
- How many are disputed?
- What’s the trend over time?
- What’s the actual financial impact - on Zomato and restaurants?
- Is this impacting trust, support costs, or partner churn?
- Are restaurant partners raising consistent complaints?

Only after validating that this is material, should we ask: What problem are we truly solving?
- Prevent fraud and financial loss?
- Improve operational efficiency of dispute resolution?
- Preserve marketplace trust for users and restaurants?
Different problem may result in fundamentally different solutions.

Options PMs could explore:
1. Disable Gallery Uploads
 Pros: Simple, cuts off many external images.
 Cons: Hurts genuine users (poor lighting, bad camera quality), doesn’t stop real-time AI generation, introduces friction.

2. AI/ML-Driven Detection
 Pros: No UX change. Scales, can spot manipulation signals across images.
 Cons: False positives/negatives, model training cost, ongoing iteration.

3. Reputation/Karma Scoring (already in use. So, no change)
 Pros: Penalizes repeat abusers, rewards trustful behavior.
 Cons: Must be transparent and fair, needs guardrails.

4. Richer Evidence Requirements
 E.g., short videos, time-stamped sequences, optional geolocation.
 Pros: Harder to fake, richer context for decisions.
 Cons: UX friction + privacy thresholds to manage.

You do not wait.

You play with the tools.

Because growing evidence shows that overreliance dulls judgement and weakens memory.

What works well.
What quietly goes wrong.

Exploring ideas.
Scanning research.
Spotting trends.
Compressing complexity so you can think better.

Bad uses look like this.
Letting the model decide for you.
Letting it replace judgement.
Letting autonomous systems run without checks because it feels efficient.

But uncertainty does not mean helplessness.

There are still things AI struggles with.
Context.
Taste.
Responsibility.
Knowing when to stop.

Use the tools.
But do not hand them the steering wheel.

The AI doesn’t know who you are.
Not your identity.
Your authority.

We’ve gotten very good at teaching AI to talk. We haven’t taught who it’s allowed to act for.

So when AI moves from advising to acting: moving money, changing records, and making commitments, it improvises authority from patterns in text.

That works … right up until it doesn’t.

Authority isn’t a prompt. It isn’t a memory. It isn’t a role.

It’s a structural property of systems.

And it’s a missing primitive behind enterprise AI failures.

The AI industry is making a fundamental category mistake: confusing language with thought.

Benjamin Riley just published a great piece in The Verge explaining why scaling LLMs won’t magically produce reasoning.

He cites neuroscience (Fedorenko et al.) showing that language and thought activate completely different cognitive networks.

Language is how we communicate thought.
Thought is how we produce reasoning.
The two aren’t interchangeable.

This explains why enterprises keep falling into what I call the “Inference Trap.”

Feed an LLM 10,000 contracts (language), and we expect it to deduce the authority policy (reasoning). But all it learns are statistical patterns in text, not the underlying constraints, judgments, or causal logic.

Riley puts it bluntly: “Take away language from a large language model, and you are left with literally nothing at all.”

If we want reliable agents, we need to stop treating language models as reasoning engines.

Reasoning isn’t something a model stumbles into — it’s something we have to represent explicitly.

I passed on Airbnb in 2008.

My note to Brian Chesky said I had "concerns about travel-oriented startups." A masterpiece of missing the point.

I've thought about that mistake for 16 years. Here's one of the things I learned:

Most founders are told to find painful problems. But painful problems are obvious. And obvious attracts competition.

In 2007, if you asked travelers what frustrated them, they'd say hotels were too expensive. Nobody said "I wish I could sleep in a stranger's apartment." 

The contradiction Airbnb spotted was different. People actually would trust strangers with accommodations. The entire industry's assumption was wrong. Reality and consensus had quietly diverged.

That's the distinction that took me years to see:

Pain invites you to improve the current game. Contradictions invite you to change which game gets played.

So why did I miss it?

Because my identity was tangled up in the old explanation. I had beliefs about what made marketplaces work. Airbnb violated those beliefs. Instead of questioning my frame, I assumed it was still valid.

The other lesson I took: Solving for pain can be an effective way to find a startup problem. But solving for contradictions that don't add up is the better path to monopoly. The reason is that the only way you can resolve the contradiction is to come up with a new explanation that no one has figured out yet. And that leads to being a monopoly rather than a commodity.

Perfect products kill more startups than bad ones.

Here's what happens: 

Founders & leaders from big companies think "At Google/Amazon, we spent 6 months perfecting this before launch. That's how you build quality." But in a startup, that’s the wrong playbook.

Because, the real danger isn’t launching something rough, it’s building something nobody wants.

That's why launching early & getting real user feedback is super important.

And if you’re worried about being too scrappy, here’s how some of today’s biggest companies started:

1. Airbnb
The first version of Airbnb didn't even have payments integrated, you had to pay by cash when you went to the property.

2. Zomato: 
Zomato's first version was just a bunch of menu cards of restaurants around Deepinder Goyal's Bain & Co office. 

3. Dropbox
Before writing storage sync code, Dropbox founders made a simple demo video explaining the product. They validated demand with 70,000+ signups.

4. Zappos
Nick Swinmurn wanted to test if people would buy shoes online without trying them on? So he took photos in local stores, posted them, and when someone bought, he went back and bought the shoes himself.

5. Groupon
Started as a WordPress blog. Deals were PDFs emailed manually. That was enough to test the concept.

At Supernova, when we had the idea for an AI Tutor for spoken English, we sold the course first with a later start date & some demo activities. 
If there was no demand, we were going to refund and move on. But it worked & we got clarity in weeks, not months.

So if you’re an early-stage builder, remember:
There is always a way to launch fast, launch like your life depends on it, because it does.

I’ve raised two funding rounds - $1M in our first, and most recently $3.6M at a ~$20M post-money valuation. 

Here’s what nobody tells you about fundraising (that I wish I knew before starting).

How to run the process:

1. Warm intros >> everything else: Cold emails and LinkedIn DMs rarely work. Try to get intros from portfolio founders of the VCs you’re targeting.

2. Batch your meetings back-to-back: Don't fundraise in drips - it kills your leverage. Hit the market hard over 2-3 weeks with tightly clustered meetings. Investors want deals that other investors want. Without urgency, it usually makes sense for them to just wait & watch.

3. Start small: Begin with angels to perfect your pitch and get early commits. Then move to smaller funds for practice before approaching your target lead investors. This builds confidence and helps you refine your story with lower stakes.

4. Set a hard deadline: Announce upfront- “We’re closing this round by [date] and moving on.” This forces decisions and prevents endless diligence cycles. Investors respect founders who control their process.

Remember these points:

- Associate outreach ≠ real interest: Associates reaching out on LinkedIn is normal - they’re doing their job (meeting lots of founders). Don’t mistake it for serious interest or momentum.

- Time commitment is brutal & it's a huge distraction: Budget 3-6 months of full-time CEO attention. From first meetings to signed docs and wired funds, it's a complete distraction from building. The quicker you finish and get back to work, the better.

- The only hard part is finding the lead: You'll hear "we'd love to participate once you find a lead" for weeks - it means nothing. Close your lead investor and suddenly everyone who was "interested" wants in immediately. Your round goes from hard to close to oversubscribed in 48 hours.

Final thoughts: Run a tight, time-boxed process. Get warm intros, batch your meetings, set a deadline, and focus entirely on finding your lead. Everything else is noise until that lead commits.

Can we please stop calling everything "agentic AI"?

I sat through three demos last week. Every single one had AGENTIC AI in massive letters on slide 2. 

You know what they actually were? ChatGPT with API access and a nice UI.

Don't get me wrong - they were solid products. But agentic? Come on.

This is driving me crazy because the term actually means something specific, and we're just... using it for anything that involves AI doing stuff.

Real talk:

If I ask your AI a question and it gives me an answer and then I have to go DO something with that answer → that's generative AI. It generates content. That's it.

If I tell your AI "I need to increase qualified demo bookings by 20%" and it goes off and figures out how to do that, makes a plan, executes across multiple tools, adjusts when things don't work, and comes back with results → THAT'S agentic.

The test is simple. Does your AI need you between steps? Then it's not agentic, it's assistive.

Can it:
- Break down a complex goal without you mapping it out
- Make decisions on its own (within boundaries you set, obviously)
- Actually execute actions across different systems
- Handle failures and try different approaches
- Complete an entire workflow while you're literally doing something else

If the answer is no to any of these... it's not agentic. And that's totally fine! Generative AI is incredibly useful. We don't need to oversell it.

Example: ChatGPT writing my outbound emails = generative and I love it

AI that researches prospects, scores them, identifies the best timing, writes personalized emails, sends them, tracks who opened what, categorizes responses by intent, and schedules follow-ups based on behavior patterns without me touching anything = agentic

One needs me to execute. The other executes for me.

The thing that bugs me most? When actual agentic systems become normal (and they will), nobody's going to care because we've already used up all the hype budget on things that weren't even close.

We're the boy who cried wolf except the wolf is actually coming and we've already told everyone about 47 fake wolves.

Generative AI is powerful. It's changing how we work. It doesn't need fake labels to be impressive.

Just call it what it is.

Am I being unreasonable here or does this actually matter?

The Age of Pretend
The AI industry just spent $200 billion on the wrong problem.

Everyone assumes AI’s biggest bottleneck is compute.
More GPUs. Bigger clusters. The GPU arms race.

That assumption is wrong.

The real bottleneck isn’t compute.
It’s architecture, specifically, a design decision made in 1945.

Let me explain.

In a recent interview, Ilya Sutskever, co-founder of OpenAI and arguably the most important technical mind in modern AI, said this:

“I don’t think current hardware is a limitation. It’s just not the case.”

Read that again.

The person who helped build GPT is not worried about compute.
He’s worried about ideas.

The real constraint: the von Neumann bottleneck

Modern computers separate memory and processing.
Data has to move back and forth between them.

For most software, that’s fine.
For AI, it’s catastrophic.

Some numbers the industry rarely highlights:

• Accessing off-chip memory consumes ~200× more energy than the computation itself
• Roughly 80% of Google TPU energy goes to electrical connections, not math
• A 70-billion-parameter model moves ~140 GB of data just to generate one token

The computation itself?
Simple matrix multiplication. Modern chips are excellent at it.

The problem is data movement.

The scaling mismatch

Here’s the part that should stop everyone cold:

• Training costs for frontier models have scaled ~750× every two years
• Memory bandwidth has grown ~1.6×
• Peak hardware FLOPS increased ~60,000×

We’re building faster engines, and forcing them to drink through a straw.

This is why Sutskever declared the Age of Scaling effectively over.
From roughly 2020–2025, brute-force compute worked.

That era is ending.

“The next major improvements will likely come from smarter algorithms, not more expensive hardware.”

Why this matters

• Companies pouring billions into GPU clusters may be solving yesterday’s problem
• The next real breakthrough is likely architectural, not incremental
• Smaller teams with novel approaches may leapfrog massively funded incumbents

IBM Research is already exploring in-memory computing—processing data where it lives. Early results suggest 100–1,000× energy efficiency gains.

That’s not optimization.
That’s a paradigm shift.

Yet most AI discourse remains fixated on NVIDIA earnings calls, GPU supply, and capex arms races.

The industry made a classic mistake: confusing correlation with causation.

GPT-3 was big and good → therefore bigger = better.

I started the "vibefounding" MBA class where students launched startups in a couple of days by giving students what I called the Voight-Kampff Quiz (named after the fictional test in Blade Runner, and using the image below): 

1) What are 2-3 industries or types of work in which you have deep experience?
2) What are 2-3 skills (broadly defined) where you would consider yourself world class? These are areas that have traditionally gained you notice among your peers or where you know you are particularly good.
3) What are 2-3 things outside of work that you LOVE and have knowledge about? This could be hobbies (you knit, you play a sport, you play Dungeons and Dragons, you volunteer at an assisted living home, you are an avid birdwatcher) or areas of particular interest (you are part of an internet community devoted to movies, you follow all the latest nutritional supplement news, you are really into modern art).

Their startups had to build on these very human edges. If you are just using the knowledge and abilities inherent in the model, you aren't really using your human advantage.

Most founders think happiness starts once millions hit the bank account.

I thought the same.

My plan was simple:
Build the company.
Sell it.
Be free.
Buy and do whatever I want.

What I learned instead is this:
Most founders chase the same few dreams and most of them don’t actually require millions.

1. “I want to drive a Porsche.”
I bought one.

The first weeks felt amazing.
Then it became… just a car driving from A to B.

Today, I want to sell it.
What I actually want is a practical, cool car with space not luxury for luxury’s sake.

2. “I want to buy my dream house.”
But why, really?

Owning means maintenance, responsibility, less flexibility.
Try this instead: rent something insanely nice for a few days or weeks.

You’ll find out very quickly whether it’s a real dream or just novelty.
Most things feel normal after a few days anyway.

3. “I want to travel the world for a year.”
That was my dream too.

Then I was honest with myself:
I didn’t want to live out of hotels for a year.
I wanted to stay close to friends and family.

So I chose longer stays, remote work, different locations.
You don’t need to be rich for that.
You need flexibility, not an exit.

4. “I want control over my time.”
This is the real goal.

And ironically, it’s the last thing many founders give themselves even after success.

Looking back, what I really wanted wasn’t a house, a car, or a world trip.

I wanted to choose:

- who I work with
- where I work
- when I work

That’s the real freedom formula.

In most cases, you don’t need millions to live the life you’re chasing.

You need clarity and the courage to stop postponing it.

“AI will replace software engineers because it can write all the code.”

That argument sounds convincing until you’ve actually built something valuable.
In a past role I built a server that unlocked over $10M a year in additional profit. Writing the code took just under two weeks. With today’s AI tools, I could probably get that done in under a week.

But that misses the point entirely.

The coding was the easy part. The real work took months and had very little to do with writing code. It involved understanding a messy domain, pressure-testing assumptions with domain experts, working through edge cases with other engineers, and making hard tradeoffs about what not to build.

Code is an output. Engineering is the legwork and judgment enables that output.

AI makes generating the output faster. The engineer still needs to do the legwork and provide the judgement.

We’re now in what I believe is the most important, and most misunderstood, phase of every tech revolution. It’s the moment when the deeper truths that will shape the next decade begin to emerge.

The dilemma in AI agent security in 2026 is that organizational attack surface is exploding but, at least as of January, the attackers haven’t yet shown up (at least at the level of serious attackers achieving serious objectives a la ransomware or nation state level compromise of a network via AI native risk vectors like prompt injection).

This means we lack high quality, real-world grounded empirical signals to tune / train defenses, and with which to help prioritize where leaders should pay the price in friction and resources to reduce security risk.

Again, even if we see some petty / script kiddie level attacks jailbreaking models, or some examples of attackers executing traditional kill chains with help from AI, real ransomware criminals and nation-states don’t commonly use prompt injections and model poisoning -- attacks on models themselves -- to achieve real goals (yet).

We also lack catastrophic, unambiguous horror stories where AI coding tools have quietly injected zero click exploitable vulnerabilities into scaled production codebases (yet).

The result is a tension between a discourse where attacks are identified via red teaming and the reality that we have little knowledge of which attacks will be practical for and actually exploited by real attackers. Meanwhile organizational leaders often feel we shouldn't let speculative-sounding security concerns slow down AI adoption.

This is a kind of AI security risk overhang; an exponentially growing attack surface is not being attacked yet, leaving security folks in a tough spot within our organizations and with respect to how we prioritize burning down this growing mass of poorly understood risk. 

Imagine having to set up strategic defenses for a magic, porous fortress whose surface area keeps expanding when you haven’t met or seen the enemy yet and the king keeps wondering why the defenses are so costly and whether enemy is a very big deal.

Dialable controls are the thing to build as fast as possible now

To address the cold start state we're in as defenders, we should build dialable controls that are lightweight, highly configurable, and low friction now and which can be easily dialed to be more restrictive as risks materialize ...

VERTICAL AI CHALLENGE

Vertical AI Founders: You've spent 2+ years building your agents, training your model on your customers' data, embedding into workflows, creating a powerful GTM motion, all the best practices. You've beaten back challengers and are the #1 or #2 player in your vertical.

I'm sorry, you cannot relax. In fact, you need to massively up your game.

Turns out you are facing an existential challenge: long-horizon agents (eg: Claude Code). Agents that are not trained on a specific domain, but can reliably work for hours or days on end in pursuit of a goal, self-correct, and actually do stuff. 

I'm sure many Vertical AI founders will say: "Oh, we are the system of record for decision traces (h/t Jaya Gupta and Ashu Garg). We train on enterprise-specific context. That's why these horizontal agents can never catch up with this."

You might well be right.

But, but, but ... you cannot afford to bury your head in the sand. These long-horizon agents will get better very, very quickly. You need to understand precisely how good they are at the exact jobs you've built your agents on. You cannot wait for someone else to do this. For example, if you're a legal AI company with an agent that automates contract review, you must compare how good your specialized agent is versus a general-purpose long-horizon agent that's simply given the contract and asked to perform the same review.

My challenge to you: Assign a strong engineer on your team to focus 100% on using long-horizon agents (with minimal context, other than just the contract in the example above) to compete with your custom-trained agents. Benchmark how the long-horizon agents perform vs your agent. Rinse and repeat it every few months. 

Like with most other things worth measuring, what matters is the rate of improvement (the "slope" vs the Y-intercept). If the long-horizon agent is 30% as good as your vertical agent on Day 1, but 50% as good on Day 60, and 70% as good on Day 120, you need to reassess your product strategy.

AGI is coming for everyone. Long-horizon agents are the closest we have to AGI, and as a Vertical AI company, you need to figure out how you compete and survive.

Sales is THE MOST misunderstood and brutal role in tech. One quarter, you’re a rockstar. The next, you’re doubted, blamed, and burned out. Usually unfairly. Before hiring (or firing) another seller, read this 👇🏽 Twice.

1. You can’t hide in sales. Everyone knows exactly how you’re doing. Every. Single. Month. Don’t only measure reps on quota attainment. Give them more ways to show progress: stage conversion, ACV, sales cycle, key logos, pipeline growth.

2. AEs hear more No’s in a week than most people hear in a year. And don’t get me started on SDRs… They don’t need more pressure in your 1:1 meetings; they need genuine emotional support.

3. One bad quarter and your job might be on the line—even if it wasn’t your fault. Everyone loves you when deals close, but the moment it’s tough? They panic. Sellers don’t need your stress or your micromanagement now. Instead, show you believe in them. Give them an easy win (like a board intro) and help them get their mojo back.

4. Every seller I know has felt impostor syndrome. Even the top ones. They think, “Maybe I’m just good at selling to non-tech people?” or “Maybe my last job was easy because of the brand, and I can’t really sell.” Poor management CAN make top 1% AEs doubt themselves and fail. Manage their mental game. See them as the superstars you want them to be, and they will believe it as well.

5. Sales isn’t a vacuum or a department. Product gaps, weak messaging, zero leads—yet sales always pays the price... You can’t hire a magical AE or VP and fix everything. Get your sh*t in place first. And get EVERY team: Product, Execs, CS actively supporting deals. Build a Sales Company, not just a Sales Team. Next quarter, the same reps will perform. Guaranteed.

6. If you crush it? People think “sales must be easy.” If you struggle? People think, “Maybe they’re not that good.” Stop judging superficially. Look deeper, the devil’s in the details. Are deals stuck at a certain stage? Are they receiving a similar volume/quality of leads? What strengths did they show last quarter?

7. Sales leaders carry enormous pressure for the whole team. They’re always just one missed quarter from risk—market shifts, hiring misses, fast-scale diminishing returns. It’s GUARANTEED to happen. Give them time to find root causes. Heck, roll up your sleeves and help them. Show you understand it’s never smooth and help them get back on track. 

8. We judge CV gaps or short employments, and optimize for Oracle or Salesforce logos, ignoring that many sellers get zero support, join bad product-market-fit companies, or work outside their strengths (inbound vs outbound, ACV, full cycle vs specialist, early vs established, etc.). Identify the exact seller type YOU need. Stop chasing a ‘perfect resume’.

——

Sales is brutal. Wildly misunderstood.

Great sellers aren’t flawless unicorns.

Don’t punish their struggles.

Support their fight.

Watch them thrive.

It’s pure irony. In 21 years in sales, I’ve almost never seen this taught. Yet it’s the #1 skill that separates most AEs from the ones buyers trust with $1M+ budgets.

It’s what every 2026 Revenue Kickoff should cover:

Almost none teaches how decisions actually move inside a company.

That’s the gap.

We teach negotiation, but reps don’t get how budgets are actually managed.

Confusing "product owner" with "product manager" is like confusing "Scrum Master" with "engineering leader."

Product Owner is a role in Scrum. Product Manager is a career.

This distinction matters more than most companies realize. When you only recognize Product Owner roles, you're signaling that product work is just about managing backlogs and running ceremonies. That's tactical execution, not strategic leadership.

The actual career path looks like this: Associate PM → PM → Senior PM → Director → VP → CPO. And as you progress, your work fundamentally shifts. Early in your career, you might spend 70% of your time on tactical work, like writing requirements, managing stakeholders, coordinating releases. But as you advance, that flips. Senior product leaders spend 70% of their time on strategy, vision, market positioning, and organizational alignment.

Companies that don't create proper career paths for product managers struggle to attract and retain top talent. Why would an experienced PM take a "Product Owner" role when they're ready to drive strategy at the director level?

If you want to build a strong product organization, start by recognizing product management as the strategic function it is, not just a project coordination role.

On podcast episode 252 of the Product Thinking with Melissa Perri, I explore the difference between Project and Product management, which have also created many misconceptions in the industry.

When I was 21, I spent $13,000 on a giant blue slide... It was the BEST WORST business decision I ever made 👇🏾

I'd just raised $300k for my first company.

We had ten± employees in a warehouse built for hundreds.

Before the desks had arrived, I installed a gaming room, a ball pool, and an enormous slide to get down from it.

Stupid. Immature. Completely irrational.

The best marketing investment we ever made.

Over the next few years, the BBC, Forbes, GQ, The Guardian, Vice, Channel 4, Buzzfeed, The Gadget Show and many more, all came to our office.

Every story featured that slide.

Every journalist asked me to pose in the ball pool. It became a running joke: whenever a reporter arrived, someone would shout "Get in the ball pool!" at me.

We grew 100%+ per year and never had an outbound sales team because our inbound was so strong...

I saw that slide get used seriously a handful of times in six years - but it taught me a crucial principle about marketing, branding and psychology that I've never forgotten:

Useless Absurdity will define your brand more than Useful Practicality!

The most absurd thing about your brand will have more impact than all the normal, practical things you do - so design absurdity into the experience.

The slide said something no marketing campaign could. It said: this company is different. This company doesn't play by normal rules. This company is disruptive.

The EXACT messages we wanted to communicate - delivered by accident through a big blue slide.

People have no incentive to talk about things that maintain the status quo.

But they have a tremendous incentive to share things that mock it, laugh at it, tear it down.

We are all storytellers, desperate for a good story. Absurdity hands the world a story to tell about you - and if that story propels your values, it's the best marketing investment you'll ever make.

Tesla famously had a $0 advertising budget. Their marketing strategy?

Absurdity.

They call their driving modes "Insane," "Ludicrous," and "Ludicrous+."

A karaoke function. A "Bioweapon Defense Mode." Easter eggs that turn the car into Santa's sleigh. A fart button.

They're using absurdity to scream "TESLA IS DIFFERENT" louder than any brand campaign ever could.

When you invest resources in something useless and absurd, you prove who you are. When you just say it, you're asking people to take your word for it.

Rory Sutherland put it perfectly: "Meaning is conveyed by the things we do that are not in our own short-term self-interest - by the costs we incur and the risks we take."

A slide in an office. A fart button in a car.

These things are useless. And that's precisely why they work.

Useful things are expected. They convey nothing about who you are.

Useless absurdity - the thing that makes an accountant ask "why would you spend money on that?" - that's what people remember...

That's what they share. 

That's what defines you the most.

What's your favourite example of useless absurdity?

There’s been increasing discussion around Zoom’s leading performance on Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE)—one of the most demanding benchmarks in AI today. I want to step back and highlight what truly matters here: this is not just a strong technical result, but a shift in how AI should be built and deployed in the real world.

For too long, progress in AI has been framed as a competition between individual models—their size, parameters, and raw capabilities. But in enterprise reality, value doesn’t come from having the “best” model. It comes from how well models are orchestrated: the ability to reason across steps, validate outputs, correct errors, and reliably drive work to completion in complex environments.

That’s why Zoom never bet on a single model. From the beginning, we’ve focused on building a federated, multi-agent AI system—one where multiple frontier models collaborate, cross-check one another, and complement each other’s strengths. This system-level approach is what enables Zoom AI to outperform any standalone model on a growing set of challenging tasks.

Humanity’s Last Exam is not a one-off result. The same methodology has delivered strong outcomes across other difficult evaluations as well, reinforcing our belief that the future of AI is collaborative, resilient, and built for real work—not just benchmarks.

🧠 Navigating enterprise risk in the AI era

Uncertainty has become the defining characteristic of the modern business environment, and artificial intelligence represents perhaps the most significant amplifier of that uncertainty today. 

Unlike previous technological shifts that unfolded over decades, AI’s capacity to transform entire value chains can render established business models obsolete in a matter of years. At the same time, the failure of an AI initiative can wipe hundreds of millions off the value of a company. 

Defensive restructuring in the face of this uncertainty is already underway at many Fortune 500 companies, often framed in terms of efficiency initiatives. Organizations now face a dual imperative that traditional risk frameworks were never designed to address: they must harness AI’s potential through their own initiatives while simultaneously defending against AI-driven disruption from both established competitors and new entrants. 

The organizations that harness AI effectively will define the next era of business, while those that mismanage its risks may not survive it. 

In this piece, drawing on research from our recent award winning bestselling book "TRANSCEND", we explore a critical distinction that every leader must understand to manage AI risk effectively: the difference between project risks (the challenges arising from your own AI initiatives) and enterprise risks (the threats to the enterprise from the broader evolution of AI). After sketching this distinction, we go on to provide practical guidance for managing both types of AI risk effectively.

RIP cold outreach. Welcome signal-based prospecting.

If you’re in sales this year, stop sending emails just because someone fits your Ideal Customer Profile (ICP). Traditional outbound was built on guesswork - you reached out based on firmographics and hoped the timing was right. Most of the time, it wasn’t.

Signals existed, but they were painful to find. Reps manually checked company pages for new hires, watched funding lists, scanned LinkedIn jobs, and stitched together scraps of information to guess who might be in market. Two years ago, I watched one of our top reps (in one of my gemba walks) painstakingly research one account at a time. Painfully slow. Completely broken.

Signal-based prospecting flips that model. Instead of guessing, you engage when there’s evidence of intent, when something has actually changed. What should you look for?

Account insights: who they sell to, their market, their gaps, their tech stack
Compelling triggers: funding, new leadership, major launches, hiring in key areas
Contact insights: promotions, site visits, social signals, prior champions moving

This isn’t about doing more outreach. It’s about reaching out only when it makes sense and showing up with context that earns the next meeting.

Always build reversible systems.

When you make an architectural decision that's hard to undo, you're essentially betting that you got it right. All it takes is one edge case, one wrong state, one user misconfiguration, one incorrect assumption, and your system crumbles.

Hence, it's always better to build a reversible system. The core idea is to roll out systems and features that can be reversed (disabled) easily.

Rollout confidence is often proportional to how easy it is to revert the change. Some ways to achieve this are:

- Loose coupling between components
- Feature flags for gradual rollouts
- Database migrations that can roll back
- Phased rollouts and deployments
- Define clear interfaces that hide implementation details

Bake this principle into your design process and decision-making framework. Most decisions you make should favor reversible systems, allowing you to move faster.

With this, you stop being afraid of being wrong.

The two things that matter

I’ve interviewed and evaluating 1000+ people over the years. The two attributes that are critical for every role (regardless of function, title, or seniority) are:

1. High Agency: being proactive and taking action, not waiting for someone to tell you what to do 
2. Problem-solving Ability: being able to diagnose, root cause and tackle any problem systematically and from first principles 

The younger the company and the smaller the team, the more important these two attributes are. If you’re (insert massive company name), you can get away without hiring for these. But if a good chunk of your first 50 employees don’t have these, you will be in a world of pain.

Startups should add interviews that separately test for each of these attributes. At the least, the final interview (with the startup CEO/founder) should focus on these two. When I interview candidates for startups as an investor or board member, I spend most of my time testing for agency and problem solving. 

The challenge is that startups need their employees and leaders to have agency and be able to solve problems, while big companies don’t screen for or reward either. This is the root of many hiring misfires when a big company hire joins a startup without being evaluated on these dimensions.

The loops of software delivery are out of sync.

The inner loop, where developers write, debug, and build, has never been faster. AI tools can generate code in seconds, fill in boilerplate, and explore solutions that would have taken hours to prototype manually.

But the outer loop, where teams test, review, and deploy, hasn’t kept up.
Every improvement in speed on the front end creates more pressure on the systems downstream. More code. More diffs. More builds competing for attention. The result is a bottleneck that doesn’t show up in productivity dashboards, but everyone feels it: longer queues, noisier failures, slower feedback.

We used to talk about the value of CI/CD as the thing that made integration safe and repeatable. Today, the challenge is different. It’s about making CI/CD adaptive, able to respond to the same scale and variability AI has introduced into the development process.

That’s where the next real gains will come from. Not in writing more code, but in helping delivery systems understand and keep pace with the way code is written now.

The difference isn’t about tooling or process maturity. It’s about how the team works together under pressure. I’ve always found that pressure reveals the true characteristics of a team. And the AI era is no different–DORA’s data makes that clear. AI doesn’t fix team dynamics, it amplifies them. Strong collaboration, clear ownership, and healthy engineering culture are all critical. Without those, AI just adds noise.

It’s easy to focus on adoption, but that doesn’t tell us anything about impact. Not even potential impact when teams are this different. The real work is building an environment where these new tools actually make things better.

The real question isn’t how quickly we’re adopting AI, but how well our teams are set up to use it effectively.

AI Doesn’t Scale Intelligence. It Scales Expertise.

This is the insight that struck me most while reading how NVIDIA and its partners are using specialized AI agents in the real world.

Most people experience AI through generic tools.

The future org chart won’t be humans vs AI.

I’ve been writing 99% of my code at Airbnb with LLMs (mostly GPT-5 w/ Codex). If you think this is a bold claim it means you’re doing it wrong 😅

When you're building high quality production software, writing code is always the 𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗽.

Your first step is to understand the problem that needs to be solved. Then ideate solutions, consider alternatives, explore tradeoffs and refine your exploration into a concrete plan.

Even as you implement the plan task by task you should not be coding a stream of conscious. That leads to bad code design. You should be considering the architecture of the code, abstractions and coming up with a clean way to write it.

Only after all this upfront design and planning work do you then start manually typing code with your fingers.

That last step is not necessary to do manually anymore. Whenever I think of coding, I immediately reach for an LLM because I use it like a power tool.

A carpenter does not leave their power drill on the table when they need to screw in a bolt. Why would you not use an LLM to execute on your plan?

This is not Vibe Coding, you are not relying on the LLM to decide on its own how to implement your solution.

You are in the driver's seat, providing direct technical guidance at every step. 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹 𝗱𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀.

No, this is not slower than doing it without LLMs. You should also use LLMs as power tools for research, planning and architecture. This will get you even higher quality software than without them. It allows you to go far beyond due diligence and truly explore, analyze and refine your design fully before any single line of code is written. 

I use the following workflow to naturally research, design and plan the feature I want to build in the form of a conversation which then gets converted to a formal Spec that the LLM can implement task by task:

 1. Explain the problem to the LLM.

 2. Give it your ideas for the initial solution 

 3. Tell it explicitly: “Propose an approach first. Show alternatives to my solution, highlight tradeoffs. Do not write code until I approve.”

 4. Review the proposal, poke holes in it, iterate

 5. Tell it to write the plan to disk as a spec so you can hand off to another session later

 6. Lastly, let it generate code.

I Asked 42 CISOs: "What Are Your Top 3 Priorities for 2026?"
The answers should reshape how every vendor approaches the market this year.

𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 #𝟭: 𝗔𝗜 𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 & 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆

By 2026, AI will be everywhere inside enterprises — often without CISO approval. CISOs have shifted from "Can we stop this?" to "How do we control and govern it?"

What they actually care about:

• Shadow AI usage (employees feeding sensitive data into ChatGPT-like tools)
• Model risk, prompt injection, data leakage, hallucinations
• Third-party AI embedded in SaaS platforms (unseen attack surface)
• Board-level accountability for AI misuse

Translation: CISOs don't want more AI tools. They want policy, visibility, guardrails, and auditability.

This aligns with what CSO Online found: AI-enabled threats ranked as a top-three organizational risk by 53% of security leaders, with 60% of organizations likely experiencing an AI-powered attack in the past year.

𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 #𝟮: 𝗜𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗮𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗿

Every major breach CISOs review still traces back to identity abuse — not malware.

2026 focus areas:

• Human + non-human identities (service accounts, APIs, bots, AI agents)
• Privileged access sprawl in cloud & SaaS
• Identity Threat Detection & Response (ITDR)
• Continuous authentication (not one-time MFA)

Key shift: IAM is no longer an IT project. It's the core security control plane.

Gartner's 2025 CISO Leadership Perspectives report confirms this: User Access, IAM, and Zero Trust ranked as the #2 priority for CISOs, with 43% prioritizing investments in IAM, MFA, and Zero Trust architectures.

𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 #𝟯: 𝗕𝘂𝗱𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗦𝗰𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘆 & 𝗩𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗿 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

Budgets are not collapsing — but scrutiny is brutal.

What CISOs are being asked by CEOs/CFOs:

• "Which tools actually reduce risk?"
• "Why do we have 70 vendors?"
• "What breaks if we cut this product?"

2026 priorities:

• Fewer vendors, broader platforms
• Measurable outcomes (risk reduction, incident prevention)
• Faster procurement decisions — but harsher renewals
• Security metrics that survive board questioning

Reality: A "nice-to-have" tool with weak differentiation is dead on arrival.

Foundry's Security Priorities Survey backs this up: 55% of CISOs report flat budgets, with tool consolidation and ROI justification becoming non-negotiable.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵

If you're a vendor still pitching features without addressing AI governance, identity sprawl, or measurable ROI — you're not solving 2026 problems.

CISOs don't have time for "interesting." They need "essential."

Which priority is shaping your GTM this year?

ZoomInfo Copilot just crossed $250M in ACV only 18 months after launch, while most AI pilots are failing. Here’s the exact playbook we used to build, launch and grow it:

1. Make your own company Customer Zero

Before a single customer saw Copilot, every seller and account manager used it in real workflows for 4 months.

We doubled down on 2 magic moments 1) rep-territory–specific signals tied to recommended actions and 2) full AI-driven account 360 - a chat where reps could ask Copilot anything about an account.

2. Build an unfiltered feedback loop and fast-track engineering

We created a dedicated Slack channel for unfiltered feedback straight from reps → PMs → engineers. Every piece of feedback had to be acted on fast - we were shipping updates multiple times per day.

3. Test willingness to pay before public launch

2 small GTM teams sold Copilot in live customer conversations months before GA, with real pricing, to learn what customers would actually pay for and which gaps were deal-breakers vs. planned follow-ons.

4. Launch with a narrow ICP

Copilot delivers the most value when a customer’s CRM is fully integrated with ZoomInfo, so instead of blasting everyone, we:
→ Filtered only CRM-integrated accounts
→ Prioritized those with the highest data quality and activity
→ Built the launch around this smaller, higher-probability segment

5. Expand post-sales capacity early

We took Copilot sales forecasts and fed them into a time-allocation model to understand:
→ How many onboarding hours we’d need
→ When those hours would hit
→ What headcount would be required by month

Then we layered that on top of existing post-sale work to plan capacity. No one was dedicated to Copilot - onboarding, L&D, and implementation teams flexed based on need.

6. Anchor selling to value

We rolled out customer-level guidance which varied by firmographics, technographics, usage, and readiness. 
Clear value → expand. Emerging value → keep scope tight.

7. Incentivize sales

We set explicit Copilot upsell and renewal attach targets for every manager, senior manager, and director. 

Targets were based on: 
→ their team’s expected monthly renewal ACV 
→ a Copilot attach-rate goal tied to that ACV

Leaders were paid cash for exceeding their quarterly Copilot targets. We also layered Copilot into existing upsell spiffs - often doubling payouts when a deal included Copilot.

8. Make all metrics visible

We tracked Copilot like its own business: ACV, pipeline, ASP, renewals vs. legacy, loss reasons, and internal adoption. All filterable by month, segment, team, and rep.

This gave sellers clear signal that Copilot was a core product.

We paired that with AI-powered enablement trained on real calls, decks, docs, Slack threads, and battlecards.

If your feedback loops are slow, your ICP is fuzzy, or your post-sale motion isn’t prepared, AI exposes it immediately.

There’s no special secret here. We just did the unglamorous work early and took it seriously.

The collapse of the AI GTM stack is already beginning! The early experimentation phase saw a lot of money spent, and a lot of data sprayed all over the place. The next phase is coalescing around a few primitives:

💻 Slack as 'messaging bus' : Instead of more UIs, just-in-time data is getting pumped into Slack for action by users.

🧑‍✈️ Copilot construct: Every role needs its own unique copilot. This copilot in Slack can provide a consolidated view of critical, timely, relevant data and enable user actions in a single place.

📊 Relevance: Smart scoring and high context enable systems to surface only what truly matters, when it matters, rather than flooding the user with information.

We're exiting the information overwhelm phase and entering a phase where relevance and context are Queen. 👑 

Open API and Agent-first platforms will typically win in this new landscape, rather than closed-loop, walled garden systems that try to own the entire interface. 

Now someone will own the interface... but that race is just beginning. But my money is NOT on CRM...

Over the last few days, there’s been a lot of discussion about audit quality and what “trust” really means in practice. I want to be clear: Vanta is not involved in these practices or associated with the firms in question.

The behaviors discussed represent a fundamental misunderstanding of what we are all here to do. Trust isn't a badge to buy; it's a standard to maintain.

When we started Vanta in 2018, the goal was simple: help startups earn trust early so they could compete for larger customers.

At the time, a SOC 2 report was often treated as the finish line. If you had the report, the conversation moved on.

That’s no longer how trust works; nor should it work that way.

A SOC 2 report is a snapshot. Snapshots only matter if they’re accurate, independently verified, and grounded in how systems actually work, and even then, they're just snapshots.

At Vanta, we’ve always believed that security and compliance should be two sides of the same coin. You shouldn’t “check the box” one way and secure your systems another. You should secure them and then let software prove that continuously.

That belief shapes how we think about audit integrity.

We don't issue reports. Independent auditors do. Today, tens of thousands of audits have been completed using Vanta. We maintain a network of more than 100 vetted, independent audit firms around the world that conduct audits using Vanta, and all of the auditors we introduce to our customers are AICPA peer reviewed, which is a baseline requirement for audit quality and independence.
Independence and rigor really matter here.

Automation should make audits faster, yes, but the goal is better audits, not just faster ones.

At the same time, the answer to audit cheat codes isn’t going backward to slower, more manual processes. The real improvement comes from knowing, day to day, whether your controls are actually working, instead of finding out months later during an audit.

SOC 2 still matters. It’s just not the whole story.

If you’re building or buying software today, trust increasingly comes from how well your controls hold up over time *and* from who’s actually validating them.

I’ve been a stubborn ChatGPT loyalist. Sure, I used everything else - but defaults harden quickly, and mine kept snapping back to Chat. Habit is a powerful moat. 

I can tell you the exact moment Google broke through my resistance: January 14th, when Sundar announced Personal Intelligence inside Gemini. I don’t plan to cancel my ChatGPT subscription but for the first time since Nov 22, my defaults are shifting. I have capitulated, friends. And not because Gemini out-benchmarked ChatGPT, but because it out-remembered me.

This launch didn’t get nearly the airtime of a model upgrade, but I think it matters more. We repeat “data is power” so often it’s dulled into abstraction. This is its concrete expression - decades of searches, emails, documents, photos, locations, and preferences are suddenly available as a single working memory. The context of your life, slowly compounding inside Google’s products for over a decade, finally got promoted from exhaust to interface - talk about data gravity! Some thoughts:

▪️ Longitudinal Context > Raw Intelligence.
The models are now good enough across the board that the benchmark race is increasingly academic. Consumers are not going to meaningfully perceive that Gemini 3 Pro scored marginally higher than GPT-5.2 on SWE-Bench, but they will absolutely perceive which one knows what you searched last year, what you wrote in Docs 5 years ago, and how your interests, habits, and priorities have evolved over decades. An AI that knows everything is less valuable than one that knows you over time.

▪️Identity Re-aggregation. 
For most of its history, Google has existed as a set of very powerful but oddly fragmented surfaces:
- Search understands intent
- Gmail maps relationships
- Docs captures how you think
- Photos preserve memory
- Maps records behavior
- Youtube knows preferences
Now it feels like all of it was in service of this moment, when they would recompose your unified self across those surfaces. Point solutions can personalize locally but platforms can personalize globally.

▪️Privacy feels easier. 
The obvious critique: this feels creepy. I get it. I couldn’t bring myself to give my Cameo to OpenAI for Sora either but somehow the mental gymnastics involved when it was Google felt less extreme. It went from ‘do I want to give them my data’ to ‘well they already have all my data, might as well get something useful from it’. 
Trusting any system with your emails, photos, and files is hard. Trusting a system that already holds all of that feels… different. Once trust is established, the sensitivity of the data doesn’t weaken the moat - it strengthens it. 

Models will keep leapfrogging each other but memory compounds. We’re still in Season 1 of Game of Thrones. There will be plenty more palace intrigue - and likely a few coups - but for now, Google’s claim in consumer AI feels the most credible.

Capital One bought Brex for $5.15B. Meanwhile, its peer Ramp just raised at $32B, despite Brex having had a crazy head start in this race. What happened?

When Ramp got started and raised its $4.5M seed at a modest $15M post-money valuation, Brex had already raised $282M at a $2.6B valuation from the who's who of the valley: KP, Ribbit, DST, IVP, GFC, Greenoaks, and more.

Before Ramp was even born, Brex was already "King."

For a couple of years, Ramp was the cute "me-too" to Brex. In Feb 2020, Brex raised again at a $2.75B valuation, while Ramp closed its round at a $75M valuation, a 37x difference.

But soon, what was obvious to Ramp's early investors became obvious to everyone: despite being late to the game, pure relentless execution could still put them ahead.

Brex raised its last monster round led by TCV and Greenoaks in Jan of 2022 at a $12.3B valuation. It never raised again.

Meanwhile, Ramp valuation continued to ratchet steadily from $5.8B (2023 valuation reset!) to $7.65B to $13B to $16B to $22.5B to $32B, closing four rounds of financing in 2025 alone.

I hear this from founders and VCs all the time: company ABC just raised $100M from A16Z. The game's over. How can startup XYZ possibly compete?

To that I say: the world belongs to the daring. Build. Sell. Execute.

Don't worry about the "King" of your category. Just out-execute them.

Uber's first investors got in at a $4 million valuation. Anthropic's first investors got in at $623 million.

Uber IPO'd at $82B, Anthropic is raising at $350B.

That massive difference in entry price is why Uber's seed investors made 5,000x returns at IPO and Anthropic's first investors are currently at 100x.

Both companies are generational. 

But the math on what that means for early investors couldn't be more different.
Uber's seed round in 2010 raised $1.25 million. First Round Capital put in $510,000 and turned it into $2.5 billion at IPO. Two angels invested $5,000 and walked away with nearly $25 million each.

Anthropic is now raising at $350 billion, a 500x+ valuation increase from their Series A four years ago. After dilution from $30B+ in funding rounds, early shareholders are looking at roughly 100x. Still exceptional. But not 5,000x.

The difference comes down to perceived risk at entry.

Uber in 2010 was two guys with a black car service in San Francisco. Mark Cuban passed. Gary Vaynerchuk passed twice. The valuation reflected that uncertainty.

Anthropic came out of the gate at $623 million because Dario Amodei was already a known quantity. Former VP of Research at OpenAI and early AI team at Google. He brought with him a crazy impressive team from OpenAI. The market priced in the potential before the company had a product.

Entry price is everything in this game. By the time everyone agrees something is valuable, most of the value has already been captured.

LLMs do not work anything like "thinking." And I can prove it with poetry.

Before tech, my passion was philosophy, psychology, and theory of the mind. Linguistics. I read Pinker and Chomsky, studied cognitive psychology. Read personality theory books. Argued in coffeeshops.

In tech, I did formal training on Machine Learning, going so far as to program my own Natural Language Processor for question categorization. 
I learned about CNNs and RNNs and had a great time. Also won the Kaggle competition against my coworkers.

On my MacBook, I would stumble bleary-eyed downstairs to see how my model performed and kick off another training run. This was 2018. 

With the explosion of LLMs, I find my combination of cloud, tech, GIS, and Linguistics uniquely shapes my perspective. 

Late last year, a paper fascinated the AI community: https://lnkd.in/eZMshtYR

The conclusion: "adversarial poetry functions as a universal single-turn jailbreak technique for Large Language Models"

In plain English: Poetry makes LLMs ignore their prompting and behave differently.

And this should not surprise us. Here's why. 

As I said, LLMs do not "think." 
They "read topology." 

This basically means they detect patterns in their training data. 
Subtle patterns which require analysis across many "dimensions" (mathematical projections of the same data across numerical transformations). 

But they cannot create patterns. And the "poetry jailbreak" explains how.

Why did Hannibal catch the Romans unawares?
He crossed the Alps instead of the sea or roads. See where this is going?

LLMs detect meaning across knowledge-space. But it's a SPACE. To defend "knowledge space," you have to know all possible ingress routes.

Like Hannibal circumventing the Romans, poetry takes an unexpected route to meaning. It arrives to the point via passes and ridges that system prompts never considered.

And therefore LLMs can only be secured as sub-systems within non-LLM systems. On premises.

If you read all of this I sincerely want to know you. Please connect.

I worry marketing is heading in the wrong direction. AI startups promising to reimagine it are reimagining it in a really bleak way.

I’ve watched as new AI marketing apps promise to learn ‘who you are’ and ‘mimic your style’ and release agents to post comments as you across the web. The agent researches content, creates content, and posts to communities at scale.

This is directionally bad for marketing; it paints marketing in such a negative light. It’s so far removed from the origins of marketing - De Beers inventing the engagement ring tradition with 'A Diamond is Forever’, Ogilvy transforming the fortunes of Rolls-Royce when he told people at 60 miles an hour, the loudest noise comes from the electric clock, or Hinge being honest and telling you it was designed to be deleted.

I don’t recognize marketing in being able to automate comments at scale. What part of marketing is that?

There’s no doubt marketing has become more technical over time. The internet led marketers to blend creativity with technical skills, requiring them to understand how to craft marketing while knowing how algorithms work to get their marketing seen. I’ve said many times that AI is a continuation of this; it will reward marketers who have an engineering mindset.

But we’re automating the wrong things, we’re focused on the wrong things. And losing the ‘craft’ part of marketing is the wrong use of AI.

To me, the beauty of AI is that it should give you more time to craft and create, with the AI as your assistant.

Crafting and creating better content
Crafting and creating more images, videos, and freemium tools.
AND more time to participate in communities, connect with people, and add more value.

Be what people are interested in

Secondly, what if these tools win? How does that help anyone? Agents speak to each other on open communities (Reddit, LinkedIn, other), the humans twiddle dials to optimise agents, whilst the actual humans all retreat to closed walled communties (WhatsApp, text message) because open communities die (see StackOverflow). 

Look at LinkedIn, it’s awash with profiles automating their liking of your posts, automating comments on your posts. These things make people want to avoid your brand rather than engage with it. All they do is make people retreat from the platform. Everyone loses.

I hope the majority of us choose AI as a craft tool rather than a slop machine.

You don’t beat competitors by doing the same thing slightly better.....you beat them by doing something different that matters more to your customer.

If everyone’s fighting over price, find a better experience. 

If they’re all optimizing convenience, find a niche they’re ignoring. 

The key is focus.

And solving one painful problem really well.

PEOPLE ARE LEAVING MARKETING FOR GOOD

And I don’t think we’re talking honestly enough about why.

I’ve started to notice a clear pattern. Senior marketers. Career marketers. People who have done everything “right”. Years in the craft. Proven results. Leadership scars to prove it.

And now they’re openly talking about leaving the function entirely.

Not pivoting to a new vertical. Not chasing the next fancy title. Actually considering completely different lines of work just to regain stability, dignity, or control.

When someone with a decade plus in marketing is genuinely weighing up a career shift, something is deeply broken.

The market right now is brutal. But not just because of layoffs.

It’s brutal because of how marketing talent is being treated.

• Roles sold as strategic that turn into execution factories.

• Leadership promises that quietly evaporate once you’re in.

• Under-resourced teams expected to deliver growth miracles.

• Marketers blamed for revenue while being excluded from decisions.

• Burnout framed as a personal failure instead of a structural one.

I’m seeing people taken advantage of because they care. Because they stay late. Because they “figure it out”. Because marketing has become the catch-all function that absorbs every organisational failure upstream.

And when the system keeps breaking, the marketer is the one expected to quietly fix it.

At some point, enough really is enough.

I don’t think people are leaving marketing because they don’t love the work. I think they’re leaving because the market has made the work unsustainable, undervalued, and in many cases, dishonest.

So I keep asking myself, and I’ll ask it out loud here.

How bad are things really, if this many experienced marketers are walking away entirely?

Talk to anyone who’s been consistently successful in marketing and you’ll notice something strange.

They’re not saying anything new.

You’ll hear the same ideas again and again:

👉 Know your customer
👉 Nail the value prop
👉 Be consistent
👉 Distribute aggressively
👉 Test and iterate

We all know this stuff.

And yet, most marketing still underperforms.

Why?

Ideas are cheap.
Execution is expensive.

And execution doesn’t mean “do more.”

It means:

👉 Doing fewer things well
👉 Ruthlessly prioritizing
👉 Going deep instead of wide
👉 Killing good ideas to focus on right ones
👉 Saying no to work that looks impressive but doesn’t move revenue

Great marketing isn’t doing everything.
It’s not constant reinvention.

It’s:

- disciplined execution.
- good bets.
- relentless follow-through. 
- a balance of speed and quality

Ideas are cheap.

Execution — done well — is expensive.

That’s why so few people actually do it.

Nobody’s clicking anymore.

And it's not because they're bouncing. They’re finishing their journey before they ever reach you.

Pew analyzed browsing behavior from March 2025 and found: when an AI Overview appears, people click a traditional search result 8% of the time (vs 15% without an AI Overview). And they’re more likely to end the session right there (26% vs 16%). 

Then Seer Interactive took a different cut: by Sept 2025, the organic click-through rate on queries with AI Overviews was 0.61%, down from 1.76% in June 2024 — a 61% decline.

So what are users doing?

• They read the AI Overview then stop.
• They search again instead of leaving Google.
• They default to Wikipedia, YouTube or Reddit.
• They talk it up with their LLM chatbot of choice.
• ...or they just scroll social media instead.

When I first introduced the concept of Zero-Click Content in 2022, I was talking about platforms rewarding in-app behavior. I didn't realize how fast we'd go from optimizing for impressions to optimizing to *be* the answer.

Now we’re already living the next era of Zero-Click Marketing: win attention where your audience already is, and earn the right to be cited, remembered, and searched for by name.

So what are you optimizing for in 2026? Clicks, citations, or recall?

Just pick up the f**king phone.

Business has never had more communication tools and somehow never communicated less effectively. Entire projects now die in group chats. Deals stall in inboxes. Relationships drift because everyone is too busy “circling back” to actually speak.

Messenger and email have their place, but they’ve also created an entire generation of professionals who will spend 45 minutes crafting a paragraph to avoid a five-minute conversation. The irony is that all the tools designed to speed things up have created more delays, more misinterpretation, and more people misreading tone like it’s a psychic exercise.

The reticence to actually call someone is getting worse. Younger teams, especially, treat the phone like an artefact from a lost civilisation. They’ll Slack, text, DM, send voice notes, @-tag you in three channels, and then wait for the universe to deliver clarity. Meanwhile the problem sits there, breeding.

So much of business is friction masquerading as communication.
A two-line misunderstanding becomes a project delay.
A vague message becomes a political issue.
A misread email becomes a week of unnecessary “alignment.”
All of which could be solved in one short, direct conversation.

In PE and operating roles, this becomes even more painful. Value creation slows down because people are talking around problems rather than through them. I’ve watched multimillion-dollar initiatives stall simply because nobody wanted to pick up the phone and ask the obvious question.

Here’s a simple rule that still works:
If a message takes longer to write than it would to say, CALL.
If there’s even a chance of misinterpretation, CALL
If the stakes are high, CALL.
If the relationship matters, CALL.
If you’re on message 14 in a thread that hasn’t moved an inch, definitely f**cking CALL.

The fastest way to accelerate execution is often the oldest.
Pick up the phone. Say the thing. Solve the issue. Move on.

Most friction in companies isn’t strategic. It’s people avoiding a conversation that would take less time than the messages they’re sending about it.

Everyone is trying to build “AI for Private Equity.”
The problem? Private equity doesn’t actually want it.

Here’s the thing: you can run a private equity firm on Outlook and Excel and do just fine.

PE isn’t starving for dashboards, copilots, or a 40-tab “deal OS.”
PE is starving for better decisions, and no software has ever replaced judgment, pattern recognition, or the ability to actually operate a company.

The industry keeps building tools to solve problems PE doesn’t think it has.

Because the real bottleneck isn’t data.
It’s interpretation.
It’s action.
It’s people who know what to do next.

AI will absolutely transform parts of the industry.....sourcing, research, diligence, portfolio analytics. But the firms who win won’t be the ones with the most software.

They’ll be the ones who use AI to sharpen human expertise… not replace it.

If you want to build something for private equity, stop trying to reinvent the fund in software.

Start by understanding why Outlook, Excel, and a handful of sharp operators have been enough to generate top-quartile returns for 30 years.

PE doesn’t need more tools.
PE needs leverage.

AI can provide it, just not in the way most people think.

Tailwind Labs laid off 75% of its engineering team this week.

The company behind one of the most popular styling frameworks for the last 10 years isn’t making money.

That’s the brutal impact Generative AI is having on the business.

According to Adam Wathan (creator of TailwindCSS), Tailwind is growing faster than ever. It’s bigger than it’s ever been. And yet revenue is down nearly 80%.

The core problem is discovery. Traffic to their docs is down roughly 40% compared to early 2023. And the docs are how people find Tailwind’s commercial products. When discovery disappears, so do customers.

People aren’t reading the docs anymore. LLMs are.

And LLMs don’t buy UI kits, templates, components or click the sponsor button. They consume them. They extract the value without converting into revenue.

Right now, there’s no clear link between making Tailwind easier to use and making its development sustainable. If that gap persists, the project risks becoming outright abandonware. Simply because there’s no one left employed to work on it.

This raises questions about the future of open source. The incentives are eroding. And losing open-source development at scale would be disastrous.

I hope they find a way to keep building.
