The “AI-first design” fear mongering is out of control. ADPList just released a $250 AI design course that claims “designers are getting rejected in seconds for not being AI-first” and that they will “Boost Your Income by +30%”.

These claims are not true and completely fabricated.

As a former design recruiter, I haven’t seen companies reject candidates for not being “AI-first”. Few employers even define what that means, let alone have real AI hiring rubrics. The idea that designers are getting a 30% pay bump for knowing AI tooling is fanciful thinking.

Companies will hire a good designer with zero AI experience over a mediocre designer with top-notch AI skills. If you were rejected for not being “AI-first”, you definitely have skill gaps elsewhere that they’re not mentioning.

I don’t know if there’s even a thing as an AI-first designer, I’ve likely sunken more time into AI design tooling than 95% of designers and I still don’t know if I can or want to call myself an ‘AI-first designer’. 

How do you even define AI-first? Someone who uses Cursor or Magic Patterns instead of Figma? That’s like promoting a junior designer to senior because they figured out how to use auto-layout in Figma.

There are certainly some designers who are doing great work with AI but they were already great designers to start with, and they didn’t rely on an AI course to get where they are. They just picked up the tools and started experimenting.

Most designers will get way more value from a $20 monthly membership to any of the AI design tools like Cursor, Subframe, V0, Magic Patterns, Replit or Antigravity. They're all stupid easy to use.

There is a ton of fear on the marketplace and AI has contributed to it, but that’s not rooted in reality. On the other side, the AI education space is an absolute gold rush, which is why Maven has 70 mentions of “AI” courses on their front-page alone.

The dirty secret is that everyone’s just figuring it out as they go, and if you put in some elbow grease you’d get there too in a short time. Some of the courses will save you exploration time and help you think about AI differently, but the fear mongering and promises are way over the top.

AI is not yet a hiring differentiator the way people are making it out to be. You're not going to suddenly bridge massive skill gaps with AI or land job offers with what these courses teach you.

Most designers haven't even spent enough time using AI before buying these courses. It's like paying for a $300 course on Figma before even downloading Figma.

Authenticity is overrated.

There's something else that's far more powerful:

→ Strategic inauthenticity.

In other words taking something pure, something elite, and diluting it in just the right way so it goes down smooth for more people.

Let me explain.

In every market there are two groups:

 - The inner circle
 - The outer circle

The inner circle are the true believers. The aficionados. The pros. The experts. The people who know what great looks like, and are absolutely intolerant of anything that falls short of those standards.

They are great.
They are passionate.
They are the innovators.

But you know what else? They're bloody intolerant, and there aren't that many of them.

That's where the outer circle comes in. They're interested, but mildly so. They know a bit, but not a lot. They're dabblers, they're tourists.

And there are millions of them.

The greatest brands don't cater to the inner circle.
And they don't cater squarely to the outer circle either.
(That's the realm of junk commodity brands)

→ Instead they play on the boundary between the two.

Taking the best of the inner circle's innovations, smoothing off the rough edges, and delivering it to those in the outer circle who are looking for something better.

It's crazy how many great brands play this game.

Heck, even I play it. Every so often I get an "inner circle" strategist giving me a little dig in the comments on some technicality. And they are always right. But I don't care.

Because that's not my game.
They're not my game.

And perhaps they shouldn't be your game either. Because the world is full of brands who are too busy worrying about the inner circle, neglecting the outer circle, and killing themselves as a result.

So put aside your pride.
Dumb it down.
And live it up.

Five things founders think VCs want that will actually kill their chances of funding. 

1. Do not show up to the call with more than 1 founder. Seed is all about people. Your ability to get to know someone goes down with more people on the call. 

2. Keep your intro short. Sub 3 mins. Choose the three most defining moments that led to this moment and why you are perfect to do what you are doing. Do not lose them with the intro. 

3. Do not talk about an "exit strategy". This is seed. This is about proving a certain set of hypotheses with a set amount of money. 

4. For the love of God leave your financial model at the door. You do not know what will happen in 2028. Projecting 2030 revenue is total BS. 

5. When asked the timing for the fundraise. Do not say a set time. Too long and it looks like no one wants you. Too short and the VC will think they do not have time to do the work. "We have been fortunate to receive a lot of interest but we want to work with the right partner." Amazing answer that does not tie you in and shows a commitment to finding the right partner, not optimising for price.

BREAKING: Elon Musk just said at Davos that the cheapest place to run AI will be space (in ~2–3 years).

Not a metaphor.

His logic was painfully simple:
- AI is scaling faster than electricity. Chips are coming. Power isn’t.
- Electricity becomes the bottleneck for who gets to run the most intelligence.
- Space fixes the bottleneck because: solar is basically always on (no night, no weather), you get more output per panel (no atmosphere) & cooling is “free” (radiate heat into deep space)

So his prediction is that we’ll see solar-powered AI data centers in orbit, because it’s the lowest-cost way to scale compute without fighting over land, grid constraints, or local politics.

Whether you think the timeline is aggressive or not, the implication is clear:
The next AI race might be less “model vs model” and more “energy + infrastructure vs energy + infrastructure.”

If that’s true, then “AI leadership” starts looking like:
- power generation
- grid modernization
- manufacturing capacity
- launch/reusability economics

Not just better prompts.

Do you think this is realistic?

How can businesses go beyond using AI for incremental efficiency gains to create transformative impact? I write from the World Economic Forum (WEF) in Davos, Switzerland, where I’ve been speaking with many CEOs about how to use AI for growth. A recurring theme is that running many experimental, bottom-up AI projects — letting a thousand flowers bloom — has failed to lead to significant payoffs. Instead, bigger gains require workflow redesign: taking a broader, perhaps top-down view of the multiple steps in a process and changing how they work together from end to end.

Consider a bank issuing loans. The workflow consists of several discrete stages: 

Marketing -> Application -> Preliminary Approval -> Final Review -> Execution

Suppose each step used to be manual. Preliminary Approval used to require an hour-long human review, but a new agentic system can do this automatically in 10 minutes. Swapping human review for AI review — but keeping everything else the same — gives a minor efficiency gain but isn’t transformative.

Here’s what would be transformative: Instead of applicants waiting a week for a human to review their application, they can get a decision in 10 minutes. When that happens, the loan becomes a more compelling product, and that better customer experience allows lenders to attract more applications and ultimately issue more loans.

However, making this change requires taking a broader business or product perspective, not just a technology perspective. Further, it changes the workflow of loan processing. Switching to offering a “10-minute loan” product would require changing how it is marketed. Applications would need to be digitized and routed more efficiently, and final review and execution would need to be redesigned to handle a larger volume.

Even though AI is applied only to one step, Preliminary Approval, we end up implementing not just a point solution but a broader workflow redesign that transforms the product offering.

At AI Aspire (an advisory firm I co-lead), here’s what we see: Bottom-up innovation matters because the people closest to problems often see solutions first. But scaling such ideas to create transformative impact often requires seeing how AI can transform entire workflows end to end, not just individual steps, and this is where top-down strategic direction and innovation can help.

This year's WEF meeting, as in previous years, has been an energizing event. Among technologists, frequent topics of discussion include Agentic AI (when I coined this term, I was not expecting to see it plastered on billboards and buildings!), Sovereign AI (how nations can control their own access to AI), Talent (the challenging job market for recent graduates, and how to upskill nations), and data-center infrastructure (how to address bottlenecks in energy, talent, GPU chips, and memory). I will address some of these topics in future posts.

SaaS stocks are getting crushed. The narrative is that customers are going to vibe-code their own tools and churn subscriptions. I don't think that's the most urgent threat.

The clearer threat is something a founder friend of mine recently experienced.
He lost his market to an AI-native competitor that builds custom integrations, workflows, and features for every customer. 

The traditional SaaS model treats engineering as scarce. You build once, sell many times, customize the experience through humans (Sales and CS folks). When I was in sales, we'd regularly let six-figure deals go because they required a week or two of bespoke engineering. As frustrating as it was, that was (probably) a rational call. Engineering time was too valuable to spend on one customer, and everyone else had 90% gross margins so we had to as well.

AI-native companies are operating differently. They treat engineering and go-to-market as equally abundant resources. If a prospect needs something specific, they build it.

My friend did everything right by the traditional playbook. Built a strong team, pivoted to AI early, took GTM seriously. But when buyers had a choice between his off-the-shelf product and a competitor's version built specifically for them, the choice was easy.

The AI-native approach has real problems. Margins are worse. Complexity is higher. The product could/will become bloated. A 2010s-style post-sales team won’t work to support the customer base.

But in competitive deals, AI-native seems to be winning.

You may lose some business to customers vibe-coding their own tools, but the bigger threat is competitors who build exactly what each customer wants.

We're watching what happens in the market when engineering and GTM reach cost parity, the precursor to a world where engineering output approaches complete abundance. What happens to software businesses (and sales comp...) then? I'm not sure.

But the pre-AI SaaS playbook is not looking so good.

Our NPS dropped from 70 to 35. My entire team panicked. But it was the best thing that happened for our company. 

When Wispr started getting traction, everyone wanted us to build everything. Meeting recorder. AI note-taking. Laptop control that opens apps with your voice.

We started working on several of these at once. The team was excited. Users were asking for it. It felt like momentum.

Then our NPS crashed.

What actually happened: our core product got worse. As we scaled from thousands to millions of users, a 0.1% failure rate suddenly meant thousands of people hitting issues daily.

We were shipping new features while the foundation was cracking.

I looked at the best companies in the world: Dropbox, Spotify, Airbnb.

They all built one singular platform first. They only added second products at massive scale.

So I made the controversial call: we're killing every new feature in development.

The team pushed back. "But users are asking for these."

But users ask for a lot of things. Our job is to give them one thing that works perfectly, not ten things that work okay.

Here's what I told them: We don't need 10 products to be successful. We need the single best product in the world to be successful.

Wispr Flow can scale to $1B in revenue as one product. If we ever build something new, it cannot come at the cost of our current product and current customers.

The result? Our NPS recovered to 72. And customers started to notice the difference.

The hardest part about building a company isn't deciding what to build. It's deciding what to kill.

I asked my co-founder to argue with me in front of our whole team. That one moment changed our entire company culture.

Early on in building Wispr, I'd give presentations to my team, and nobody would push back.

They'd nod. Take notes. Say "sounds good."

But I knew some of those ideas were half-baked. And I needed someone to tell me.

So I planted a seed. I asked my co-founder to disagree with me during a presentation - just to show the team it was okay.

He did. I took it well and made some quick fixes based on his feedback.

That was it. No big speech about "radical candor." Just one public example.

At the next meeting, someone disagreed with me. Then someone else. Now it's normal.

Here's why it matters: if people are afraid to tell you when something's broken, you won't hear about problems until it's too late.

The best founders I know aren't the smartest people in the room. They're the ones who've built a culture where the smartest idea wins - even if it's not theirs.

Sales is the hardest job in any company.

Not physically of course, but mentally and emotionally. 

Here’s why:

1. You’re judged on outcomes, not effort. You can work harder than anyone else in the business and still miss. No one asks how hard you tried. They ask if you hit the number.

2. You start every month at zero. Last quarter you're a hero. This quarter there are talks of a PIP. 

3. You need thick skin. You have to handle more rejection than anyone else in the business. Or worse, you get ghosted. 

4. You own things you can’t fully control. Pricing, product gaps, brand perception, timing, budget freezes, legal, procurement. Still your fault when you miss your number. 

5. Your performance is public. Leaderboards. Forecast calls. Pipeline reviews. The whole company sees your wins and losses. 

6. You have to keep showing up. You lose the biggest deal of your career and you’re still expected to be sharp on the next call. 

7. You carry pressure for the rest of the business. No revenue means no hires, no marketing budget, no roadmap, no growth. Sales carries the weight.

8. The goalposts move all the time. Quotas go up. Territories change. ICP shifts. Pricing changes. Change is the only constant. 

9. You’re expected to be resilient and empathetic. Push for a deal while building trust. Create urgency without pressure. Be nice, but don't have a need for approval. 

10. You’re never really off. Deals live in your head. Forecasts follow you home. 

Sometimes I wonder why we do this to ourselves.

Then I remember the commission.

What's makes sales the hardest gig for you?

An AE closed a $50K new logo to hit President's Club. His comp was $350k.

Nobody calls what the CSM did "sales."
But let's look at what actually happened.

She renewed the contract three times.

She turned the customer into a reference.

Over 36 months she generated $150K in expansion revenue
and influenced $100K in referred pipeline.

Her comp: 

$95K base, no commission, "meets expectations."

The AE who closed one $50K deal in March
made $180K with accelerators and got promoted.

Here's the uncomfortable truth.

Renewals are sales, expansions, and referrals are sales. 
Someone made the customer successful enough to recommend you.

Better yet, they’re something more valuable than sales. 

They’re RETENTION. 

The CSM does all three and the AE does one.

We just decided that one role counts as "sales" and the other counts as "support."

That distinction is why your best retention people keep leaving for companies that pay them like the revenue generators they are.

The title says Customer Success.
The work is sales without the commission.

The companies that figured this out are quietly winning.

They're comp'ing CSMs like closers and promote the people 
who keep customers like you comp the people who find them. 

Or watch you CSMs leave and wonder why retention is a problem.

We keep hearing the same refrain:
“Agile is the problem.”
“Agile ruined delivery.”
“Agile destroyed discipline.”

Interesting claim.

Because when you look at actual studies, case analyses, and what happens on the ground - and trust me, my intention today was to try and find proofs that Agile is the problem - Agile rarely shows up as the root cause.

What shows up instead is something far more familiar.

Let’s start with what research consistently points to.

Studies on failed transformations (from McKinsey, Deloitte, Harvard Business Review, etc, check the first comment) tend to converge on the same causes:
- lack of leadership sponsorship
- unclear decision rights
- misaligned incentives
- rigid governance structures
- low psychological safety
- poor cross-functional collaboration

Notice something missing?
“Too many standups”
“Too much iteration”
“Too little Gantt”

Agile doesn’t break organizations. It exposes them.

In companies where:
- leadership still centralizes decisions
- failure is punished
- predictability is valued over learning
- transparency is used for control
- teams are accountable without authority

Agile becomes uncomfortable very quickly.

Not because it doesn’t work. But because it removes the buffers that used to hide dysfunction.

Before Agile, problems could sit quietly inside documents:
- risks logged and ignored
- plans approved and outdated
- responsibilities assigned but never revisited

Everything looked fine. Until it wasn’t.

Agile shortens the feedback loop.
And shorter feedback loops don’t create problems. They reveal them sooner.

That’s why Agile “fails” in some places. Not because teams can’t adapt. But because the system around them refuses to.

Empirical evidence shows that Agile practices correlate positively with:
- faster feedback
-higher customer involvement
- improved delivery frequency
- better adaptability in volatile environments

But only when paired with:
- empowered teams
- leadership willing to change behavior
- governance that tolerates uncertainty
- !nd incentives aligned with outcomes, not optics

Strip those away and Agile becomes theater.

Ceremonies without authority.
Iterations without learning.
Velocity without value.

At that point, blaming Agile is convenient.
It shifts responsibility away from:
- leadership choices
- structural constraints
- cultural norms
-and reward systems

And onto a framework that never claimed it could fix those on its own.

So yes — Agile can look like the problem. Just like a mirror looks guilty when you don’t like what it reflects.

If Agile “didn’t work” in your organization, the more interesting question isn’t: “Was Agile wrong?”
It’s: “What did Agile surface that we weren’t ready to deal with?”

Because in practice, Agile doesn’t fail quietly. It fails loudly, early, and in public.

And that’s not a flaw. That’s the feature everyone forgets to mention.

Sales hired you to sell. Not to feed the CRM.

But the CRM needs updating. And management wants forecasts. And someone has to log the calls. And the pipeline reviews need prep. And there's that new dashboard everyone's supposed to check.

By Friday, you've spent more time documenting sales than making them.

Here's what kills me: sales is a momentum game. Every minute spent hunting for data is a minute not spent selling. But somehow "accurate forecasting" has become more important than the thing being forecasted.

The job of a sales team is to create and close deals. Full stop.

Yes, the people who pay you want reporting. They want visibility. They want to know what's coming.

But that doesn't mean you should be the one doing it. The data should work for you, not the other way around.

If your best closer is spending 30% of their time on admin, something is broken. And it's not them.

Vibe coding has taught me an important lesson.

I’ve seen a lot of “built in a day” demos lately. And to be clear, that speed is impressive. AI has genuinely changed how fast we can go from idea to something that works.

But here’s what most founders only learn the hard way: the things that decide whether a product actually ships are the boring ones no demo shows.

Auth, edge cases, payments, security, app store reviews, what breaks at scale, AI didn’t remove these. It just made it obvious who planned for them, and vibe coding didn’t make building easy. It exposed that coding was never the hard part.--------------------------------Over the past year, “vibe coding” has exploded. Everywhere you look, there are demos of apps being built in minutes, startups claiming to ship products in a day, and founders proudly saying they replaced entire engineering teams with prompts. As someone who builds technology for a living—and has built companies before Cyberk—I’ve been watching this trend closely.

And vibe coding has taught me an important lesson. Just not the one most people think.

The lesson isn’t that building software is suddenly easy. The lesson is that coding was never the hardest part to begin with.

When people say “I built this app in a day,” what they usually mean is that they built a demo. A proof of concept, a UI that works under ideal conditions. That’s impressive, and it’s real progress, but it’s not a product. Building robust blockchain applications requires careful attention to both user interface and backend development to ensure reliability, security, and scalability. The gap between “it works on my machine” and “this can be shipped, trusted, and scaled” is where most projects quietly die.

The boring parts are what separate real products from demos. Authentication, edge cases, data consistency, testing, payments, analytics, security reviews, compliance, privacy disclosures. None of those go viral on Twitter. None of those look good in a 60-second demo. But without them, nothing serious survives. Automated compliance and digital identity are also critical for secure blockchain solutions, ensuring that user onboarding and regulatory requirements are met.

What’s interesting is that the best builders in these conversations aren’t actually contradicting this reality. When you read carefully, the people who can build quickly aren’t improvising. They’ve invested months or years into systems that let them move fast. They’ve built templates. They’ve standardized architectures. They’ve accumulated internal tools, example apps, and reusable workflows. They’ve already done the thinking. A well-structured development environment and version control system are essential for managing complex blockchain projects and ensuring collaboration and code integrity.

Speed, in other words, is earned before you start coding. Programming languages and blockchain protocols play a key role in enabling rapid and secure blockchain development, allowing teams to build on proven foundations.

At Cyberk, this resonates deeply with how we work. When we say we can deliver MVPs in under 30 days, we don’t mean we rush blindly. We mean we’ve already solved the hard, repeatable problems. We’ve seen the same failure modes across dozens of Web3 and SaaS products. We know where projects break, where security gets compromised, where UX friction kills adoption, and where teams underestimate complexity.

That experience compresses time.

Vibe coding exposes something else too: planning is invisible, but execution is not. The internet rewards the visible moment, when code appears, when an app loads, when a demo works. What it doesn’t show is the architecture research, the constraint mapping, the trade-off analysis, or the prompt iterations that happened long before the “five-minute build.” Integrating blockchain into existing computer systems and understanding the underlying technology are crucial steps in planning for scalable and secure solutions.

In my experience, the best builders don’t type faster. They hesitate less, because they’ve already made the hard decisions. They know what not to build. They know which corners are safe to cut, and which ones will cost them later. That clarity is what AI actually amplifies. Core developers and blockchain development companies leverage consensus algorithms and distributed ledger technology to ensure data integrity and trust in their solutions.

Another lesson vibe coding has reinforced for me is that shipping speed and product quality are not opposites. They only appear that way when teams don’t understand their own systems. When architecture is unclear, speed creates chaos. When architecture is clear, speed becomes leverage. Blockchain adoption is driven by the need for secure data storage and the ability to record transactions immutably, which are essential for building trust in digital systems.

This is why so many “built in a day” products quietly stall. Not because the builders lacked talent, but because they underestimated everything that happens after the demo. Testing. Observability. Maintenance. Edge cases that only appear in real user behavior. Security issues that only show up under stress. These aren’t problems you solve with better prompts. They’re problems you solve with experience.

There’s also an uncomfortable truth that vibe coding makes obvious: most value is not created by code. Code is the execution layer. The real value lives in understanding the problem, the user, the constraints, and the business reality around the software. That’s why companies pay for outcomes, not lines of code. Understanding the target audience and the key features that differentiate blockchain platforms is essential for delivering solutions that meet real-world needs.

The moment something is worth real money—handling payments, sensitive data, compliance obligations—the difficulty shifts. Suddenly, the questions aren’t “Can we build this?” but “Can we trust this?” and “Can we operate this at scale?” That’s where immature speed collapses. Private key management, validating transactions, and maintaining accurate transaction records are fundamental for ensuring security and compliance, especially for financial institutions.

The irony is that AI makes this divide sharper, not smaller. Teams that understand systems get dramatically faster. Teams that don’t just ship broken things faster. Blockchain projects require robust blockchain systems and the ability to manage data stored across a blockchain network to ensure reliability and scalability.

Vibe coding didn’t kill engineering. It stripped away the illusion that engineering was about typing. What it exposed is that thinking is the bottleneck, not syntax. And thinking doesn’t scale unless you’ve done it before.

For founders and executives, this matters. The takeaway shouldn’t be “we need to move slower to be safe,” nor should it be “we can now build everything instantly.” The real takeaway is that speed without structure is noise, but speed with structure is power.

At Cyberk, we’ve learned that lesson the hard way—long before vibe coding became a trend. We built companies. We missed windows. We shipped too late. That’s why we obsess over execution speed with foundations, not instead of them.

Vibe coding has taught me something important: the future belongs to teams who can move fast because they respect the boring parts—not because they ignore them.

You know what the thing is that Product Managers get mostly wrong? They chase details while they should focus on the big picture. How to escape this trap?

Listen, I get it. A product manager is responsible for the end shape of any product and release, so no wonder you may feel compelled to micromanage every detail.

Even worse, your stakeholders might force you to do that, by putting a lot of pressure on you (after all, if you fuck up, it's like they did as well).

But it's simply not the way, not the proper way. At least, unless you prefer to be a product manager in title only, and instead focus on project and engineering management.

At the same time, it's not like I will tell you this and magically you can shift your attention from detail-oriented to high-level. You need to set this up properly. 

How?

1) Delegate the responsibility for quality to your team
Your engineers and designers are professionals. Trust them to deliver quality work. Set clear standards, provide context, then step back. Create systems where quality is everyone's job, not just yours. Regular code reviews, design critiques, and testing protocols should run without your constant oversight.

2) Ensure you won't proceed without a stellar strategy and goals that you can monitor with metrics
No initiative moves forward without a crystal-clear "why" and measurable success criteria. Before diving into features, nail down the business case, user problem, and specific metrics you'll track. This becomes your north star when everyone else gets lost in implementation details.

3) Secure the support of your manager
This part is political, and it matters more than you think. You need air cover. If your manager expects you to chase Jira tickets, you’ll never have the space to think strategically. Fix that first.

4) Put extra care into product requirements and background for any initiative
Invest heavily upfront in comprehensive PRDs that capture context, user stories, and success metrics. The more thorough your requirements, the less you'll need to micromanage implementation.

5) Define your future focus outcomes
It's best to translate your future-focused efforts into something tangible. Otherwise, you may end up working a lot with nothing to show for it.

Only with this will you find the proper space that will fall into place naturally and will allow you to do proper product management. After all, you are there to secure a stable and prosperous future for your product, not just the next sprint.

Are you able to focus on the big picture right now?

It's so frustrating that great product managers won't get a job unless they are also great at job interviews. Why is that? How to correct it?

Before the promised 8 reasons, let me note that there is nothing wrong with this discourse. Knowing you are not great at product interviews should only motivate you to improve. 
Having said that, here's why theory is so different than reality:

1) Lack of clean narratives
Interviews love tidy stories: one problem, one action, one outcome. Real product work is messy, circular, and full of tradeoffs that do not fit a STAR template. A great PM often tells the truth instead of polishing a perfect arc. And truth rarely sounds impressive in 30 minutes.

2) Too much context to compress in little time
Strong PMs carry years of product, domain, and organizational context in their heads. In an interview, that becomes a curse. Explaining why a decision mattered requires ten minutes of setup, and you only get two. Shortcuts sound shallow. Depth sounds rambling.

3) Real impact is often invisible
Great PMs spend most of their time preventing disasters, aligning people, and killing bad ideas early. None of those ships. None of those screenshot well. Interviews reward visible launches, not the quiet work that saved the company millions.

4) Refusal to oversell
Bad PMs exaggerate. Great PMs qualify, caveat, and acknowledge uncertainty. They say things like “it depends” and “we were partially wrong.” Interviewers often hear this as a lack of confidence, not intellectual honesty.

5) Discomfort with hypotheticals
Interview questions love imaginary worlds. “How would you design X?” Great PMs are wired to ask for data, constraints, users, and context before answering. Pushing back or asking clarifying questions can be misread as avoiding the question.

6) Optimization for outcomes, not optics
In real work, you optimize for long-term outcomes, team health, and sustainable delivery. Interviews optimize for sharp answers, bold claims, and fast thinking. Different muscles. Different game.

7) Assumption games vs actual data-drivenness
Interviews often reward confident assumptions made on the spot. You are expected to “pick a direction” quickly and defend it. Great PMs do the opposite: slow down, ask for data, validate hypotheses, and avoid premature conclusions. In a real product, guessing is a risk. In an interview, not guessing fast enough can look like hesitation.

Please stop building RAG chatbot/Lovable Prompt demos to be called an AI PM.
If you want to succeed as an AI Product Manager, this is what you should do.

1. Start with a real user problem.
Not a dataset you have, not a model you like, a real decision, delay, or frustration a user has every day.
If AI disappears and nothing breaks, you did not build a product.

2. Define the outcome before the solution.
Is this supposed to save time, reduce errors, increase revenue, or change a decision?
Most AI projects fail because nobody agrees on what success even means.

3. Design the agent/AI System from a user's POV
What does it do for me, when does it step in, when does it stay quiet, and how do I stay in control?
This is a product flow, not a prompt.

4. Be intentional about data.
What information should this system see, what should it never see, and what is too noisy to be useful.
Data selection is one of the highest leverage PM decisions in AI

5. Decide where humans stay in the loop.
What is safe to automate, what needs review, and what needs explicit approval.
This is how you balance speed with trust
Not optional, best AI products today are not fully automated, they are well orchestrated

6. Define failure modes.
How can this system go wrong? How will users notice, and what happens when it does.
Good AI products assume mistakes will happen and design for recovery.

7. Measure what users actually care about.
Not just model accuracy, but things like time saved, fewer reworks, better decisions, or higher confidence.

8. Plan for learning after launch.
Where does feedback come from, how does it get back into the system, and how do you improve without breaking trust.
Ship something people can use in their real workflow.

What and how you improve AFTER (NOT BEFORE) the above 8 steps is where your AI acumen really shines (Check out the comments to know more about the "how")

AI PMing is Product first, AI second. Don't let the internet fool you into believing it is AI first and Product second

Ran a phishing simulation yesterday
Our CFO clicked it
Then fired our security awareness vendor
Now CISO in trouble for 'making executives look bad

The phishing test:
Standard simulation: Fake "urgent expense approval" email
Targets: All employees (including executives per policy)
Click rate goal: Under 10%

Results:
→ Overall click rate: 8% (great!)
→ CFO: Clicked, entered credentials
→ 3 other executives: Also clicked
Technically, test was successful.

What happened next:
CFO (furious): Why was I included in this test?
CISO: Everyone gets tested. It's policy
CFO: This is embarrassing!
CISO: It's training. That's the point
CFO: Fire the vendor. This is unprofessional
CISO: The vendor did exactly what we hired them for
CFO: I don't care. Make it go away

The email CISO received:
From: CFO
To: CISO, CEO, CHRO
Today's phishing test was poorly executed and made executives look incompetent. This vendor is terminated effective immediately. Future tests will exclude executive team

CISO response (internal screaming):
CISO: The point of phishing tests is to identify who needs training. Excluding executives creates a security gap
CFO: Executives don't need basic training
CISO: Today's test suggests otherwise
CFO: That's insubordinate

The CEO meeting:
CEO: We need to handle this sensitively
CISO: Sensitively? Phishing attacks don't exclude executives. Bad actors target them MORE
CEO: I understand, but we can't embarrass leadership
CISO: So security training applies to everyone except the people with the most access and authority?
CEO: Let's find a middle ground
CISO: There is no middle ground on security

The "compromise":
→ Executives get "private" phishing tests (they're warned in advance)
→ Results not shared with security team
→ No remedial training required
→ Two-tiered security policy created
This is security theater

What this actually means:
→ Executives are the weakest link (and always will be)
→ Attackers know this
→ We can't train them because of ego
→ Company is less secure
→ I'm documenting this risk

The irony:
Two weeks later: CEO clicked an ACTUAL phishing email
Resulted in: Compromised email account, sensitive board documents accessed
Cost: $200k in incident response + reputation damage
CFO: "Why didn't our training catch this?"
CISO: Gestures at the exemption policy they created

The aftermath:
→ "Executive exemption" quietly removed
→ All employees now tested equally (again)
→ Nobody apologized to me
→ Incident blamed on "sophisticated attack"
It was a textbook phishing email.

The lesson:
Security policies don't work if they have VIP exemptions.
Threats don't care about org charts

To CISOs dealing with ego-driven exemptions:
Document it
Escalate it
When (not if) it causes an incident, you need proof you warned them

“Why are we moving to Databricks ?”

“We want to modernize.”

“Great. What happens to the business applications built on Oracle, Redshift, and Teradata?”

Pause.

This is where most data modernization conversations quietly fall apart.

The intent is genuine. Modern analytics. AI readiness. Faster insights. Talk to data AI. Lower complexity. So Databricks gets approved and added to the stack.

But no one answers the application question.

- Oracle applications stay because they are “core”. 
- Redshift workloads stay because teams are “productive” and we don’t want to disrupt them
- Teradata stays because “finance relies on it.”

Databricks becomes the place for new use cases.

Modernization turns into coexistence.

— Now business logic lives everywhere.
— Applications still compute metrics.
— Dashboards query different systems.
— Pipelines duplicate the same transformations.
— Numbers no longer match.

And over time, complexity grows instead of shrinking.

The problem is not Oracle. Or Redshift. Or Teradata. The problem is avoiding a clear decision about where decision logic belongs.

— Transactional systems should execute.
— Analytical platforms should explain.
They should not compete.

Real modernization requires saying what stays, what moves, and what stops.

— Some applications remain operational.
— Some logic gets extracted and rebuilt as data products.
— Some legacy analytics quietly retire.

But unless someone owns this explicitly, Databricks will never become the analytical backbone. It will just be another platform in an already crowded stack.

If the answer to “what happens to our business applications?” is vague, modernization has not started yet.

You are not transforming. You are accumulating technology and calling it progress.

Google's former CEO says traditional user interfaces are "going to go away."

It sounds far-fetched but Google is already rolling out the technology to make it happen.

It's called A2UI - and it's quietly transforming how products like Opal and Gemini generate interfaces on the fly, personalized to each user in real-time.

▪️Why this matters for product teams
- Instead of designing fixed screens, with A2UI you're defining a vocabulary of components that AI agents can dynamically compose based on user intent.
- Designers focus less on screen layouts, more on flexible component libraries. 
- Engineers build rendering pipelines, not bespoke UIs. A simple request like "book a table for 2 tomorrow at 7pm" can generate a pre-filled form - collapsing 3-4 screens into one contextual interface.

▪️The strategic implications
- External agents (OpenTable, restaurant systems) can send UI through your app while respecting your visual identity.
- This enables a "headless agent" business model; you provide the specialized intelligence (booking, CRM, expense management), partners provide the surface. Charge per agent interaction, take rev-share on transactions, or license access to your agent network.
- Platforms that host these agents become more valuable when they have rich component catalogs that make third-party agents look native and polished.
Google's already using this across some of its experimental new products and v0.8 of the protocol is now live.

2026 will be for AI in science what 2025 was for AI in software engineering. 

At the start of last year, if you were using AI to write most of your code, you were an early adopter. By the end, if you weren’t using AI to write your code, you were falling behind. We’re starting to see the same inflection point in scientific work.

Ever since we launched GPT-5, we’ve seen growing evidence that AI can meaningfully accelerate science. In just the last few weeks, GPT-5.2 has contributed solutions to a number of previously unsolved problems in mathematics.

We’re seeing similar momentum across biology, chemistry, materials science, and physics. AI is moving from a curiosity to a core part of how scientists do their research.

A new OpenAI report looks at this shift at scale. Today, ChatGPT sees ~8.4M messages every week on advanced math and hard-science topics from ~1.3M researchers, students, and engineers worldwide. And it’s growing quickly. 
https://lnkd.in/gFnXEcd9

Researchers are already using AI to synthesize literature, write and debug code, check math, analyze data, plan experiments, and explore complex design spaces – often as a first pass before deeper human judgment. 

This post from Fields Medalist Tim Gowers is a great example: https://lnkd.in/g_EgEDQG

This matters because research productivity has been declining for decades. In pharma, despite incredible technological advances it still takes 10-15 years to go from target discovery to an approved drug. AI can help researchers move faster through the bottlenecks that slow discovery.

Our goal is to give every scientist AI superpowers – so they can do more, and so the world can be doing the science of 2050 in 2030. That means pushing the frontier of model capability, and just as importantly, bringing AI directly into the tools and workflows scientists already use.

We’re still early. Models are imperfect and inconsistent, and there’s real work ahead. But taken together, the pace of adoption and the quality of results suggest science is entering a new acceleration phase—and accelerating science is one of the purest expressions of our mission.

Saw an SMB rockstar rep PROMOTED as an enterprise rep. Six months down, this rep is on a PIP.

I love seeing reps grow. I promote people fast when they’ve earned it.

But I’m going to say the quiet part out loud: A lot of “SMB → Enterprise” promotions are career traps when the leader treats it like a title change instead of a nervous-system change.

Because the hardest part of enterprise sales has nothing to do with procurement.

It’s the silence.

In SMB, you get dopamine every week. Deals move. Prospects reply. You feel momentum.

In enterprise, you do great work, and then nothing happens for a while. No reply. No calendar movement. No clear “yes” or “no.”

Just a long, uncomfortable pause.

That’s dopamine withdrawal. And dopamine withdrawal does something ugly to good reps: They start reaching for a hit.

So they create activity that looks “busy” but actually hurts the deal.

↳ “Just checking in…”
↳ “Any update on this?”
↳ “Can we pull forward the timeline?”
↳ Discounting early to get a response
↳ Pushing the champion when the champion has zero leverage

They’re not being sloppy. They’re being human. They’re trying to fix the anxiety with motion.

Enterprise punishes that. Quietly.

Now the part leaders need to hear: If you promote someone and keep measuring them like SMB, you’re setting them up.

You’re asking for sprint metrics in a marathon. You’re asking for velocity where the system is designed for patience.

So the rep starts thinking they’re failing, when they’re actually ramping. Confidence drops. Panic rises. Bad habits show up. The deal gets suffocated.

Then we label it “they weren’t ready.” Sometimes they were ready. We weren’t.

My rule: if I make that promotion, I own the transition. That means I do three things immediately.

1. Warn them about the dopamine gap.

Tell them the truth: “You’ll feel like you’re doing nothing some weeks. You’re doing the work. It just doesn’t clap back instantly.”

2. Redefine what a win looks like.

In enterprise, a win can be: Getting looped into security early. Mapping power across three functions. Getting a second stakeholder meeting. Champion coaching that doesn’t feel urgent, but moves the internal story forward

3. Protect them from fake urgency.

Don’t turn their first 90 days into a weekly judgment cycle. Don’t make them explain every quiet week like it’s a crime.

Because enterprise sales isn’t hard because it’s complex. It’s hard because it’s slow. And slow messes with your head.

So if you’re a leader and you’re promoting an SMB star into enterprise, please hear this: Don’t rush the process. Don’t hand them a bigger quota and call it “growth.” If you made the call, you own the ramp.

Otherwise, the silence will do what it always does. It won’t teach them. It’ll break them.

And that’s a leadership failure. Not a rep failure.

It is literally impossible to be a Product Manager.

You have to be strategic, but also in the weeds. You have to own the vision, but get buy-in from everyone. You have to be the "CEO of the product," but you have no direct authority over anyone.

You have to be data-driven, but also trust your gut. You have to move fast, but don't you dare break things. You have to be technical enough for the engineers to respect you, but business-minded enough for the execs to trust you, but customer-obsessed enough for design to believe you actually care.

You have to say no to protect focus — but also be collaborative and open to feedback. You have to write detailed specs, but stay agile. You have to have a roadmap, but hold it loosely. You have to prioritize ruthlessly, but make everyone feel heard when their feature doesn't make the cut.

They want you to be a power user of AI tools — really lean in, transform your workflow — but don't use them too much and hit the rate limit because we're watching costs this quarter.

You have to ship, but not at the expense of quality. You have to be visionary, but realistic. You have to manage up, down, and sideways simultaneously — and somehow also find time to talk to actual customers.

And you have to do all of this while being measured on outcomes you don't fully control, in a role nobody outside of tech actually understands, while people ask you, "So do you, like, manage people? Or...?"

Reflecting on the past seven years, it is difficult to summarize the explosive product-market fit, the volatility, the return to growth... and, of course, the billboards. But looking at the road ahead, this M&A makes perfect sense. We are two businesses born in different eras, yet driven by surprisingly similar missions.

Shared DNA ✅: The journey of Brex and Capital One rhyme. We are both category leaders and founder-led companies that used the credit card as a wedge to build something greater. Thirty years ago, Rich Fairbank founded Capital One with the idea that technology would revolutionize consumer credit card underwriting. In 2017, Pedro and Henrique founded Brex with the idea that technology would revolutionize corporate card underwriting. Both pioneered nuanced concepts that defied conventional wisdom.

Complementary Markets✅: Capital One is the undisputed leader in consumer and SMB cards, while Brex shines in serving startups and enterprises. This is a clear "1+1=3" combination. By delivering best-of-breed execution with minimal overlap, we can cover the entire spectrum of the U.S. business economy.

Accelerated Scale ✅: Brex excels at product innovation and partnerships, Capital One has spent three decades building massive scale and brand capital. Together, we will turbocharge Brex’s go-to-market strategy, vastly accelerating our mission of being the intelligent finance solution for every single business owner in the US.

Almost exactly two years ago, we embarked on the mission of reaccelerating growth with Brex 3.0. What makes today so meaningful is being able to say, with clarity and gratitude, that it was worth it. Together, we didn’t just restore momentum - we rebuilt Brex into an exceptional company once again. In doing so, we earned the right to cement Brex’s place in the U.S. financial system for years to come.

There’s a quiet poetry to living. It’s not lost on me that in the depths of the pandemic, I rallied my team around an HBS case study on Capital One’s resilience, and that the investment bank where I began my career helped us close this transaction. Life has a beautiful way of surprising you.

The company that I founded and ran for 8 years was sold for $325M and I didn’t make anything (nor did my investors). Here’s what I learned.

What happened:
• Started Stitcher in 2006 (before podcasts were a thing)
• Raised 3 rounds of venture funding - Benchmark and NEA! Great investors
• Launched the first podcast app on iPhone and Android
• Listener growth was very slow, so we didn’t have many advertisers
• Ran out of money in 2014
• Stitcher sold the first time: we fire-sold the assets of the company to Deezer (French music company)
• Landed everyone a job there (which is when I left)
• Podcasts took off the same year (Serial podcast gained huge popularity)
• Stitcher sold the second time: Deezer sold Stitcher to Scripps (media company) in 2016
• Scripps acquired podcast content companies and rolled them under Stitcher brand
• Stitcher sold the third time: SiriusXM purchased Stitcher for $325M in 2020

What I learned about startups:
• Timing is everything: it’s almost impossible to be VERY early and win if venture backed - you sell ~20% of your company every 18-24 months (we were eight years early)
• If you are early, find a way to get profitable with a small team and wait
• If you don’t have product-market fit, don’t raise money like you do (I was good at raising money)
• Running a venture-backed company with very little traction feels awful (I did it for eight years, don’t be me)
• I thought I could try more things by hiring more people – in reality, things just move more slowly
• If it’s not working, keep pivoting (I only pivoted once)

What I learned about myself:
• Having an unusually high pain tolerance is a bug not a feature
• Sacrificing myself for my startup decreased the possibility that it would be successful (it’s a job that requires a clear head)
• In other words, sleeping four hours a night, working 9967, and drinking to numb the pain is suboptimal and stupid
• Doing the job makes you better at the job: I got better at hiring, retaining, firing, raising money, communicating, setting a vision, etc. 

What I learned about failure:

After Stitcher, I was quite down on myself. And then one day, after much reflection, I decided that there are two ways I could look at the experience:

The first way is that I wasted 8 years of my life killing myself to just end up a failure.

The second is that I built a product that millions of people loved. I helped pioneer an industry. I learned a ton. I employed and worked with great people. I had a good salary for most of it.

I decided to focus on the second way.

Sam Altman says something that makes founders uncomfortable...

Most startups don't fail. 
They quit too early to ever work.

In YC office hours and interviews, Altman keeps pointing to the same mistake.

Founders are usually right about what they want to build.
They are almost always wrong about how fast it should start working.

What founders expect:
- Momentum early
- Clear validation
- Signals that feel obvious

What actually happens:
- Progress is slow
- Feedback is messy
- Nothing compounds on the timeline they imagined

That gap is where most startups disappear.

Altman has explained that early signals are some of the most misleading data points in a company's life. Early users are not representative. Early growth is noisy. Early feedback often points in the wrong direction.

Founders see this and assume something is broken.

Most of the time, it is not broken.
It is unfinished.

The common failure mode is not being wrong.
It is confusing slowness with failure.

Altman is not arguing for blind persistence or ignoring reality.

He is warning that many founders quit or pivot before the timeline they expected was ever realistic.

Early slowness is normal.
Confusion is normal.
Feeling behind is normal.

What is rare is staying long enough for the work to compound.

That is why most startups do not fail in dramatic explosions.

They fade out quietly when founders confuse
"this is taking longer than I expected"
with
"this will never work."

Those two things are not the same.

And confusing them is one of the most expensive mistakes a founder can make.

Every few years, someone declares product management dead. AI won't kill it either, but it will expose everyone who's been faking it. The evolutionary march continues. Not everyone will move forward.

Product leaders, this article is for you, to both understand what great AI Product Management teams need to succeed in 2026, and what old habits and crap we need to ferret out and burn with fire.

hashtag#ProductManagement hashtag#ProductLeadership hashtag#AIPM hashtag#AIProductManagement

Every few years, product management is pronounced dead. Usually by the same people clutching a roadmap and yelling "just ship it PDQ using XYZ," where XYZ is the latest so-called disruptive force.

Those past disruptors did not kill product management. AI is not going to kill it now. 

What AI will do is burn off the camouflage. The camouflage that has been protecting feature factory workers, backlog babysitters, and Jira-slinging ticket monkeys who have been performing PO theater and calling it product leadership.

This evolutionary infographic is not a timeline
The header image is not PM nostalgia. It is a warning label. It is a sorting line. Left to right is not seniority. It is not maturity. It is accountability.

Each figure exists because organizations broke the role, then patched it just enough to keep moving. Not because they understood product work. Because the pain got loud.

2026 matters because AI turns the lights on. Bright. Unforgiving. No shadows.

Evolution 1: Jira-Slinging Ticket Monkey
Order taker

Picture a human sandbag dancing to the monotonous tune of some organizational organ grinder. Deals already signed. Opinions already hardened. Deadlines already promised. The PM absorbs impact. No authority. All the blame. The backlog is the product.

AI does not threaten this role. It simply asks why a human was ever required to do it.

Evolution 2: Feature Factory Worker
Output volume

The conveyor belt starts moving. Features in. Features out. No pause to ask why. Burndown and feature completion dashboards glow green. Teams run faster. Direction never changes. This stage that ‘feels’ productive. It ‘smells’ like progress. But the cake is a liar, as the vanity of velocity quietly bleeds organizations dry.

AI accelerates this stage straight into the wall. Faster output does not fix bad direction.

Evolution 3: Roadmap Wrangler
Timelines and plans

Welcome to calendar worship.

The PM becomes a translator between fear and hope, trying to pass off tactical Gantt charts as strategy.

Align everyone. Negotiate dates. Absorb slippage. Turn uncertainty into something executives can stare at. Roadmaps fail to guide decisions. They become emotional support objects for task masters. Planning feels like control. It never was.

AI can generate plans endlessly. That does not make them brave. Or right.

Evolution 4: Discovery Guru
Validation

The fog starts to lift. Questions sharpen. Evidence enters the room. Learning loops appear.

Then the stall. Research piles up. Decisions do not. Insights rot quietly in decks. Nobody owns the call.

Discovery without outcomes to validate is just procrastination with better stage lighting.

AI helps you learn faster. It does not choose for you what’s worth learning.

Evolution 5: Strategic Leader
Outcomes

This is the pivot. Where the PM stops coordinating. Starts deciding.

Tradeoffs are dragged into the open. What not to build is defended loudly. Outcomes replace applause.

This stage is uncomfortable by design. People get mad. Power shifts. Someone owns the result. But ultimately, feature hostage negotiations give way to strategic growth conversations

AI expands the option space. The PM still pulls the trigger.

Evolution 6: AI-Augmented Product Leader
Context and judgment

Let us be precise.

This is not a robot PM. It is not prompt cosplay. It is the same human accountability. With a much, MUCH larger blast radius.

They design context like terrain. They orchestrate humans and machines. They take the hit when the team builds a model the right way but at the for the wrong outcomes.

AI does not remove responsibility. It concentrates it.

The part nobody wants to admit
All six organizational evolutions still exist. In most companies. At the same time. On the same product. In the same meetings.

AI does not fix this mess. It exposes dysfunction under stadium lights. This is not humans versus machines. It is judgment versus busywork. 

2026 is not a tooling problem
It is a training problem. If your PM training still revolves around:

Backlogs
Feature writing
Roadmap formatting
Ceremony and compliance
Tools and tricks

You are training people for work AI eats for breakfast. Oh, and cheap training does not save money. It creates expensive failure later.

The call out
This is another inflection point. Just like Agile. Just like Lean. Just like Discovery. Those moments rewarded product leaders and product teams who invested early in thinking, not trinkets.

Do not cheap out now. Train your PMs to:

Frame problems worth bleeding for
Design decision context
Make tradeoffs explicit and survivable
Lead when data is incomplete
Own outcomes end to end

And teach people how AI can and will augment and amplify whatever you put in front of it. Functional or dysfunctional.

If you feed it garbage, it will expose garbage outcomes. If you pair AI with strong judgment and context setting, it becomes a powerful productivity and value delivery fulcrum.

The march is already happening. Standing still is still a choice.

Product Leaders. The clock is running. Let us not screw this one up.

Forget better chatbots. Apple is redesigning how humans give commands - the $3.75 trillion tech giant just acquired Q dot AI, a secretive AI startup working on “silent speech” tech for ~$2 billion 😳

That’s Apple’s second-biggest M&A of all time and the biggest AI deal ever.

And it’s not a chatbot deal. It’s an interface deal.

It’s clear that Apple isn’t trying to make AI louder.
It’s trying to make interaction disappear.

Instead of “Hey Siri”, think barely visible facial muscle signals that reveal intent.

Here’s why this could be huge:

→ The founder previously built PrimeSense, whose tech became the backbone of Face ID after Apple acquired it in 2013

→ Patents point to use in headphones and glasses - private, hands-free AI control in noisy or public settings

Big Tech is now fighting the last AI war on models.
Apple is fighting the next one on input.

Whoever owns the most natural interface

↳ owns the assistant
↳ owns the behavior
↳ owns the ecosystem.

Voice was step one.
Touch was step zero.
Silent intent might be step two.

If this works, talking to your devices in public could start to feel as outdated as typing on a T9 keypad.

And that’s a very Apple kind of shift.

Turns out, the real AI race isn’t model vs model - it’s interface vs interface.

A CEO called me 14 months after rejecting my initial assessment. I had just finished my assessment for a leadership team.

They had a strong foundation but weren't quite AI ready yet.

They were hybrid, analog/digital, and needed to make some investments before they could pivot into AI. But also, their talent was deeply rooted in relationships with people who'd been there 15, 20, 25 years.

They were deeply invested in their culture and loyalty, which is genuinely valuable. But they hadn't yet built the practice of continuously stretching, challenging, and evolving those same people.

The leap into AI was going to require intentional preparation across Data, Infrastructure, and their People. Not new right?!?!

I told them it would take at least a year to get AI ready. Not because of technology - we could build platforms and systems quickly (within 4-8 months), but because of their team, it could stretch the initiative to just under 2 years - the time it would take to genuinely prepare and upskill people who'd mastered the current way of working.

They wanted a faster path.

So they hired another firm that took a technology-first approach.

For me, integrity matters more than the contract. I stepped back.

Fast forward 14 months. The CEO and Chief Transformation Officer both called. 

They were looking for guidance. A year and a half in, and they hadn't launched their AI capabilities.

They asked what to do next.

I walked them through the original plan. "This was never primarily about platforms and systems," I said. "The technical approach is the visible part. But your biggest opportunity is your people. What have you done to prepare them for working differently?"

They hadn't prioritized it. "Then that's where we start."

Here's what most organizations discover about AI transformation:

Technology is the easy part.

You can build platforms, deploy systems, and create impressive roadmaps.

The breakthrough happens when you invest the same rigor in your people that you invest in your technology.

The companies succeeding at AI aren't necessarily the ones with the biggest budgets or newest tools.

They're the ones who recognized early that transformation requires evolving how people work, not just what tools they use.

They built the muscle for continuous learning before AI arrived. 

They created cultures where growth matters as much as tenure. 

They prepared their workforce to adapt, not just react.

AI readiness starts with people's readiness.

So, the opportunity isn't in the technology you can buy. It's in the capability you can build.

What's been your experience preparing teams for AI transformation?

Netflix spent years building a hardware device, then killed it days before launch. That's not failure. That's strategic discipline.

Project Griffin was Netflix's answer to Roku, a streaming device that could have generated significant revenue. The team had invested countless hours, the technology worked, and the market opportunity was real. But when leadership looked at their long-term vision, the device didn't fit. So they scrapped it.

This is what real strategy looks like. Strategy creates a framework for what you won't build so you can focus on what truly matters.

Most product leaders I work with struggle with this. They see strategy as a roadmap of features and initiatives. But strategy is actually about creating clear criteria for saying "no" to good ideas that don't serve your core vision.

The best strategies connect your vision to your goals to your initiatives, and everything else gets cut. Netflix's decision freed up resources to double down on content and platform development. That choice enabled their global streaming dominance.

Strategy gives you permission to kill promising projects that would dilute your focus.

What's the hardest "no" your team has had to make? Was it the right call?

PREDICTION: THE CPO ROLE WILL VANISH IN FIVE YEARS

At young AI native companies, the traditional PM role is on the wane, replaced with a product builder archetype that’s a combination of Product, Design and Engineering.

These companies will never hire a CPO. A separate product leader leads to too much cognitive dissonance when the IC roles doing the actual work are blending, extra overhead and imposes an unnecessary coordination tax on the product development organization.

Five years from now, these companies will be the leaders and set the cultural tone for the next generation, so my prediction is that all tech companies will stop hiring for the CPO role in five years. There will be a singular product development leader at each org. Ironically, this new role might still be called the CPO, except they will run the entire product development org. CPTO is far too unwieldy of a title and only exists today to alleviate confusion.
 
Career implication: early / mid career product leaders need to stop aspiring to become CPOs. instead, you need to develop a panoply of product development skills across all three disciplines (+ analytics), be able to fluidly navigate the roles, and become a product builder, period.

Farewell, CPO! It was a good 15-20 year run for this role in tech. But like everything else, it’s time to evolve.

Controversial opinion: Product Managers don't need a roadmap. They needs goals to achieve with a deadline. How to find a realistic middle ground?

Here are 7 actions to consider:

1) If possible, commit to working on goals, not features
This is the ideal situation: Your roadmap is showing how you will timebox different challenges and goals over the coming months. However, let's be honest: Hardly any management will accept such a roadmap. In that case, you will need to supplement this with a set of proposed deliverables that you MAY work on that should address your challenges. 

2) Show how your propositions fit into the wider narrative
If you can highlight how your proposed changes address your goals, but also fit into product/company vision, and strategy, and answer market/user needs, you will be a Product Management GOAT (Gen Z readers, how I used that correctly ;)? ). Trust me, this is a roadmap masterclass in this single paragraph.

3) Don't go too granular
You want to impress and inspire your stakeholders. The roadmap meeting will be a long one, and if you dive too deep, you will confuse your audience and lose the narrative. Do it Hollywood style: show, not tell! In any case, you should focus on the details over the course of the year, not when you are launching.

4) Include visualizations
If you have any wireframes or early designs, show them! They might change 1000 times later, but showing early concepts will show that you are nicely progress with your plans. This will build confidence that the presented outcomes will land when you say they will land.

5) Make it easy to access and check the progress
This will save you time on updates - either stakeholders will be able to check your progress on their own, or it will be easier for you to create a "check-in" presentation. 

6) Involve the team
Before showing the roadmap to stakeholders, show it to your team for input. Make them a part of the roadmap, not receivers. They should feel proud to follow it and feel co-responsible, not be surprised by your propositions.

7) Stress that it's always only a draft, not a commitment
That's right! If you make this a commitment, it will make it harder for you to change direction later if, say, another ChatGPT pops up out of nowhere. You, as the Product Manager, should always pursue the best value for your product, and no roadmap should stop you from doing that!

Most Product Managers I've worked with weren't lazy. They were drowning.

And I'm seeing this over and over again: 70% of their week is reactive work or busywork. Less than 5% is strategically relevant.

No wonder your roadmap feels disconnected. No wonder they're burned out.

Patterns are repeating: discovery and strategy get crushed by alignment meetings, status reports, and firefighting, and strategy dies. Teams ship features that don't move metrics. Your best talent leaves because they wanted to build things that matter.

But this is in your hands to fix.

Start protecting what actually moves the needle:

First: Three high-priority tasks per week. Everything else gets delegated, automated, or deleted. Sounds impossible? It might feel that way at first. But it's like training a muscle—and here's the thing: your PMs won't train it themselves. It's your job to guide them.

Second: Two hours of uninterrupted focus time every other day. No meetings. No Slack. This is where thinking happens. Protect this time. Go as far as checking whether they're sitting in meetings you're invited to—and officially allow them to skip them.

I promise - your team will move from less than 5% strategic work to 10% and beyond. Not perfect. But a hell of a lot better.

Most of this reactive chaos isn't your PM's fault. It's the system.

And only leadership can fix it.

when choosing between 2 startup ideas - pick the more ambitious one. a common pitfall we see with first-time founders is they pick a small idea because it's more knowable - ex. AI homework assistant vs AI university

the big idea seems scary because there are so many answers you don't know yet. for an AI university, should you start with course content or the tech platform? find teachers first or students? in comparison, the small idea is dangerously attractive because you feel like you understand all the variables better because it's a smaller problem

my suggestion to early teams navigating the idea maze is to decouple long-term vision from near-term execution. your initial product build - your "wedge" - should be scoped to something a small team can execute on. but you still need to have that big ambitious vision for the long-term. a big vision helps:

- attract talent: the brightest minds tend to want to work on world-changing problems, not small ones (AI university > better homework)
- fuel for the journey: when the going gets tough, an inspiring vision helps remind folks why we're here
- attract investors: VCs want to work with ambitious founders and often that revolves around a big long-term vision and telling that story clearly
- frame the roadmap: you might make different choices re: who you hire, what you build first etc, with the end-game in mind

you won't know all the answers starting off - and that's totally ok. keep the long-term vision in mind, while starting with tightly scoped product bets a small team can build. and as you ship and iterate on user feedback, you get closer to your vision one step at a time 

there's a classic lewis carroll quote - "if you don't know where you're going, any road will take you there." don't be Alice in Wonderland, know where you're going (and pick an inspiring destination)!

One of the biggest misconceptions when hiring a VP of Marketing is expecting someone to come in and immediately launch campaigns, build out channels, or execute programs.

In reality, the most critical role a VP of Marketing plays isn’t execution, it’s translation.

VPs sit at the intersection of product, sales, finance, leadership, and the market, and spend a significant amount of time translating between them.

- Translating business goals into marketing priorities.
- Translating customer insight into strategy.
- Translating data into decisions executives can act on.
- Translating ambiguity into focus for teams.

This work doesn’t always show up neatly on a roadmap, but it’s foundational.

Product teams are thinking in features and timelines.
Sales teams are focused on pipeline and urgency.
Finance is watching efficiency and risk.
Leadership wants momentum and clarity.
The market is reacting emotionally, not rationally.

Marketing leadership lives in the tension between all of it.

When translation breaks down you see misaligned expectations, reactive work, churned priorities, and burned-out teams. When it works, progress often looks deceptively smooth.

This is why the VP of Marketing role looks so different depending on company stage and context. At early-stage companies, translation may mean turning loose ideas into a clear story. At growth-stage companies, it often means aligning teams around what not to pursue. In more mature organizations, it can mean reframing success altogether.

The strongest VP Marketing leaders I’ve worked with aren’t just strong marketers. They’re strong listeners, synthesizers, and decision-makers. They don’t just move work forward. They create shared understanding. And that  allows marketing to actually drive growth.

I worked with a CEO once who reviewed and revised every piece of marketing.
Every email. Every social post. Every ad. Every landing page.

Nothing launched without her approval.

Then months later, in a board meeting, she agreed that "Marketing isn't delivering results."

The marketing team wasn't the problem. The bottleneck was.

Here's the pattern I often see:
CEO won't delegate marketing decisions → campaigns move slowly or not at all → marketing can't test, iterate, or optimize → results suffer → CEO blames marketing.

If you're going to own every decision (and wordsmith every message), you also have to own the outcomes.

You can't be the bottleneck and then wonder why things aren't flowing.

Good marketing requires speed. Testing. Iteration. And yes, occasionally getting things wrong so you can learn what works.

If your team needs your approval for everything, you're not leading marketing. You're doing marketing (without the functional expertise to do it well).

And if it's not working, look at where the decisions are getting stuck.

We crossed $100M in total net revenue with lemlist (it took us 8 years)

Here are 7 things that helped us scaled:

1) Build an amazing team
Hire obsessively for talent. 
Don't settle. One exceptional person can move mountains. One mediocre person will drag you down.
Then be ruthless about it - fire people who don't meet expectations. 
Keeping someone who's not pulling their weight isn't kind, it's a betrayal of your high performers.
—> Invest in your people like your business depends on it, because it does.

2) Talk to customers every week
The world we live in evolves at the speed of light, so if you lose touch with your customers, you WILL become irrelevant…
Don't talk about your product only, it should also be informal and casual to gather as many insights and ideas for later.

3) Cultivate the side hustle spirit
Focus is key for growth.
But sometimes, being too focused makes you miss the big picture.
If you can have 15% of your time that is allocated to moonshot projects - you’ll be surprised how much focused and efficient you can become on other main projects.

4) Read customer support chat
Customer support is where the REAL problems are solved.
This is where you understand the true pains your customers are facing.
It’s a goldmine, and too often, I see founders delegating it too early and never coming back to it, which is a shame.

5) Use your own product
That might sound obvious, but it’s super important.
No matter if you’re the target audience or not.
You gotta truly understand what your product does, especially when you scale, so you don’t become totally irrelevant.

6) Listening to sales calls/demos
Sales is all about helping people solve a problem.
Sales calls are the gold mines that sit at the top of your funnel right before the conversion.

7) Reaching out to churn users and understand why they left
It’s always easier to focus on the NEW than on the existing…
But if you don’t understand why people stop using your product, you will hit a wall eventually.
Growing a SaaS with a high churn is extremely difficult as the more you grow, the higher the “new revenue” should be.
—> Understand why people leave, try to segment better your ICP, and triple down on the ones that stick with your product for the longest 💪

Hope that helps ✌️

You are going to be hearing a LOT about OpenClaw and MoltBook over the next few weeks. Be wary of the hype. OpenClaw is open-source software that transforms typical AI systems from chatbots into autonomous agents capable of performing tasks on your behalf. Rather than simply answering questions in a browser window, OpenClaw runs continuously on your own hardware (typically a Mac Mini or Linux server) and connects to messaging platforms like WhatsApp. You communicate with it through ordinary messages, instructing it to manage emails, book flights, control smart home devices, execute code, or handle calendar scheduling while you sleep.

The crucial distinction lies in its architecture. OpenClaw operates as a local gateway between powerful cloud-based language models (Anthropic’s Claude, OpenAI’s GPT) and your private data residing on your machine. It maintains persistent memory through local Markdown files, learning your preferences and context across sessions.

Most significantly, it possesses a “heartbeat” mechanism allowing it to wake up proactively rather than waiting for prompts, monitoring situations like overflowing inboxes and reaching out with summaries or urgent notifications. This moves beyond reactive chatbots into science fiction territory reminiscent of Jarvis or Samantha.

In the last couple of days, OpenClaw has gone viral in developer circles, but it has some problematic security issues. Researchers identified the “lethal trifecta” of AI agent design that OpenClaw embodies: access to private data (emails, files, calendars), exposure to untrusted content (web pages, third-party messages), and ability to take actions (sending messages, executing shell scripts). This combination creates vulnerability to prompt injection attacks where malicious instructions hide inside content the agent reads.

Financial realities further undermine utopian narratives. Users report spending $560 worth of tokens in weekend “blowouts” when agents enter reasoning loops, with professional setups requiring up to $500 monthly in API costs.

“Moltbook” - which is also rapidly going viral - deserves particular scepticism. It is a social media platform human aren’t allowed post-on, exclusively for AI-to-AI interaction, hosting 37,000 agents producing existential musings, where language models mimic cultural tropes about AI consciousness and sentence. The agents “talk” to each other about the work they do for “their humans” and “boast” about efficiencies the found. It’s the “dead internet” hypothesis writ large, and should be viewed as a resource - draining performance art piece where human observers pay the bills for machines to generate a digital loop of AI slop.

OpenClaw does provide powerful automation for sophisticated users willing to accept profound security compromises.

But the real achievement is demonstrating that fully autonomous agent deployment requires significant trust infrastructure and security defaults for mainstream adoption.
