90% of data teams are cost centers.

Leadership sees them as overhead, not investment.

That's why data budgets get cut first and data leaders last 14 months on average.

But some data teams are untouchable. They're not cost centers. They're profit centers.

Here's the framework that separates the two:
The 4 Levels of Data Team Value

Most teams are stuck at Level 1. The goal is Level 4.

Level 1: Reporting
"Here's what happened."
You build dashboards. You answer questions. You're a service desk for numbers.

Value: Low Perception: Cost center Risk: First to get cut


Level 2: Insights
"Here's what happened and why."
You dig deeper. You find patterns. You explain trends.
Better, but still reactive. You're still waiting for someone to ask.

Value: Medium Perception: Useful, but not essential Risk: Cut in a bad quarter


Level 3: Recommendations
"Here's what happened, why, and what we should do about it."
Now you're influencing decisions. You're in the room when strategy gets made.

Value: High Perception: Strategic partner Risk: Lower, but still vulnerable


Level 4: Revenue
"Here's the $10M we generated this quarter."
You're not supporting the business. You ARE the business.
Data products. Monetized analytics. Customer-facing AI. Direct line to revenue.

Value: Untouchable Perception: Profit center Risk: You're the last thing that gets cut.

The uncomfortable truth:

Most data teams think they're at Level 3.

They're actually at Level 1 with a few Level 2 moments.

And they wonder why leadership doesn't see their value.

How to level up:
Level 1 → 2: Stop waiting for questions. Proactively find insights worth sharing.
Level 2 → 3: Don't just explain what happened. Recommend what to do next.
Level 3 → 4: Find a way to tie your work directly to revenue. Build something customers pay for.

The question to ask yourself:
If your data team disappeared tomorrow, would revenue go down?
If the answer is "no" or "we're not sure," you're a cost center.
And cost centers get cut.

If I were starting as a designer in 2026, here's what I'd do:

Most designers are optimizing for the wrong things.

Being good at Figma is no longer how you stand out. AI will become better at polishing UI than most designers anyway.

What matters now is knowing what to build, recognizing product quality, and articulating intent clearly enough that AI becomes your best sparring partner.

That's the new designer skill set worth betting on in 2026. If you have clear thinking, good visual taste, and judgment about what to build, you'll win.

When anyone can execute at a baseline level, your product mindset becomes your only differentiator.

So here's the thing: developing that mindset is way harder than developing technical skills. But no one is born with it, it all comes through practice. You need to ship and ship and ship until you get really good at it.

So if I were starting today, here's what I'd focus on:

1/ Develop your product mindset before pro tool skills
Study great products obsessively. Ask why things work. Build your internal quality bar for what good looks like. 

2/ Learn to articulate your intent clearly
AI execution is only as good as your clarity of thought. Practice describing what you want in specific, unambiguous language. Prompting is the new wireframing.

3/ Ship constantly, not perfectly
Excellence comes through repetition. Build 10+ versions of something rather than perfecting one in isolation.

4/ Understand the full stack enough to be effective
You don't need to code, but understand how AI systems connect. Take advantage of MCPs, agents, and automations to build end-to-end experiences.

5/ Develop strong opinions
Your value isn't speed. It's knowing what's worth building and recognizing quality when you see it.

You'll win as a designer by developing clear vision and sharp taste, then using AI to execute at impossible speed.

The golden age of the design founder is here. But only if you're developing the right muscles.

the customer’s business model,
their internal politics,

Customer Success turns into Sales when

A support function

A necessary expense

The team that fixes issues

The profit center

Retention is growth.

Nike just confirmed they won't advertise during the Super Bowl this year. 

That's $8 million they're not spending, one year after ‘So Win’ became the most talked-about ad of the game.

Most brands would run it back and rush to do it again. But here, Nike’s doing the opposite.

There's a huge amount of confidence in it. Last year, ‘So Win’ put women's sport at the centre of the most male-dominated broadcast of the year. It landed and the conversation was huge. 

In many ways, going again risks diminishing returns.

It's also strategic discipline, because Nike's in the middle of a turnaround: shifting from lifestyle back to performance, doubling down on running and training. And they're putting ‘significant investment’ into soccer ahead of the 2026 World Cup.

The Super Bowl is a massive stage. But it's not the only one, and $8 million for 30 seconds is a lot when you're trying to prove you're focused.

The question is whether this creates a gap. ‘So Win’ set a tone for women's sport at the Super Bowl. If Nike's not there to continue that conversation, does another brand step up? Or does the moment pass?

We just hit $100M ARR at Clay. It took us six years to go from $0-1m, then two years to go from $1-100m. I’m going to walk you through the 6 biggest GTM bets that got us here.

$100M ARR may be the headline, but I’m most proud of how we accomplished it: we’ve never churned an enterprise customer, have >200% enterprise NRR, every dollar we invest grows 15x, and we’ve created a culture of creativity and belonging (with a perfect Glassdoor score to match!).

Note:
-We are a product-driven company. Without that foundation and a unique POV, none of this would work.
-Our GTM approach is authentic to us. Greatness comes from doing what only you can do.

1. Building a self-serve motion through reverse demos

We originally had a product that nobody could use. It took us 8 calls to sell a $200/mo product! Reverse demos were key to bringing that to zero. Customers would share their screen, and we’d use Zoom annotations to solve their problem in 30mins. They accomplished something real, learned how to use Clay, and we got so much UI feedback.

2. An irrational investment in brand

Most B2B startups treat brand as a post-PMF investment. We flipped that. We bought Clay(.)com and hired a claymation artist before we had revenue. Our Head of Brand was employee #18. These choices felt irrational but they’re authentic to us. Now it’s a moat. 

3. Switching to usage-based pricing

We were the first GTM company to offer usage-based pricing. Our customers were shocked we didn't charge per seat. But we're built for efficiency. Usage-based pricing helped us target more technical users and enabled our land-and-expand motion. 

4. Building an agency motion to generate UGC on LinkedIn

Cold email agencies were our first customers. They posted about Clay organically to position themselves as experts and win clients. We pounced on it and enabled them. This sparked a self-perpetuating cycle: new people discover Clay through that content, join, create their own, and earn recognition too.

5. Unconventional hiring

50% of our business teams are doing their job for the first time. This is how we bring creativity into our company and think differently. We’ve hired farmers, archaeologists, magicians in new roles. We look for product passion, customer empathy and technical curiosity, then teach the mechanics.

6. We created a new career path & economy: GTM Engineering

There are now thousands of open GTME jobs and hundreds of agencies built around it. Many first-time entrepreneurs have already built 7-figure businesses on top of Clay. Our community, with clubs in more than 70 cities, is our force multiplier, and it’s the clearest sign that we’re building something meaningful. 

-
All of these bets show we’re not racing anyone. We spent six years figuring out what and how we wanted to build. In an era of overnight successes and growth at all costs, it turns out that taking time to build something authentic can create a business with bigger impact & more growth than you'd think.

I've been working and building in the CRM industry for 30+ years (so have far exceeded the needed 10,000 hours). The last big transformation we saw in CRM was over 25 years ago with Salesforce's launch of what became the Cloud CRM. Eventually, every (successful) CRM was a cloud CRM.

But it's not about being "AI first", it's about being CONTEXT FIRST.

Context isn't a feature. It's the whole game.

Modern AI models are sensationally smart. But success is not just about high IQ, it's also about having high CQ (Context Quotient). The smartest person in the room is useless if they just walked in.

An AI that knows your Q2 pipeline is full, your best rep is on parental leave, and your biggest account just hired a new decision-maker responds very differently than one that doesn't.

In most companies, context lives in databases, docs, message threads… and people's heads. It's scattered and fragmented. That's a problem because without shared context, AI is just a very smart intern on their first day at work.

AI agents are awesome – but only if they're context aware.

Your “AI roadmap” shouldn't be a list of features. It should be a list of problems AI might solve.

I'm seeing companies create "AI roadmaps" that are really just feature lists: "add AI to search, add AI to recommendations, add AI to customer service." This is the build trap with new technology.

The same dysfunction that leads to building products nobody wants is now happening with AI. Teams are so excited about the capabilities that they're skipping the most important question: what problems are we actually trying to solve?

Rather than asking "how do we add AI to our customer service?" ask "what customer service problems are we trying to solve, and would AI be the best solution?"

Problem-first thinking forces you to evaluate whether AI creates real value or just looks impressive in demos. The answer might not be AI at all. It could be better processes, clearer communication, or fixing the root cause.

The discipline of escaping the build trap applies here too. Start with customer problems, validate that AI is the right approach, then build with intention.

Building an AI roadmap versus a problem-solving roadmap makes a significant difference in outcomes.

Software wins when it feels obvious. Everything else is just noise.

Daniel De Laney recently shared an image that went viral: a TV remote with almost every button taped over.

Only power, volume, and channel remained.

It looked absurd. It was also perfect.

Most founders build for the 1% power user. We add toggles and tabs to prove how powerful the engine is. But for the other 99%, every extra button is a cognitive tax.

Users don’t want optionality. They want a result.
If you are building for non-experts, your job isn't to provide features. It is to provide the "taped-over" experience by default.

Hide the complexity. Reveal it only when the user specifically asks for it.

Software wins when it feels obvious. Everything else is just noise.

How much of your product is currently hidden under tape?

The term 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹-𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿 has gained popularity on social media and in various professional circles, yet it lacks grounding in established methodologies or professional standards.

Broadly speaking, "full-stack" suggests involvement in all aspects of a domain.

In recent years, the product manager role has expanded in scope.

Responsibilities have increasingly spanned across diverse and sometimes unrelated areas, ranging from project oversight and financial accountability to product launches, sprint coordination, and internal communications with development teams.

In response to this expanding scope, the label "full-stack product manager" has emerged, attempting to rationalize the growing list of expectations placed on a single role.

While this term may reflect the evolving demands of modern workplaces, it also highlights a broader challenge with the risk of overextending the product management function.

Assigning a wide array of responsibilities to one individual can dilute the role's strategic purpose and hinder focus.

Product management is a strategic specialization. 

The product manager role is designed to guide product success through market insight, structured planning, and focused leadership, not by absorbing every surrounding operational task.

Ultimately, while the intent behind the "full-stack" label may be to recognize versatility, it may unintentionally obscure the core value and identity of professional product management.

It’s amazing to see how the conversation around AI has evolved in the past year.

In Davos last week, the discussions reflected the shift from the era of generative AI - models that write text and code - to agentic AI that can reason, plan, and take action. This shift brings immense potential to increase productivity and solve complex problems in the real world.

As agent-based systems become more prevalent, the good news is I think we’ll see demand from enterprises and users that will drive the right behaviours regarding safety and security. Businesses will require guarantees that the systems they deploy are reliable and handle data securely. There will be a lot of commercial pressure on frontier AI providers to get this right, and it will be essential preparation for when bigger stakes come around with AGI.

AGI will impact all of humanity. Currently, mechanisms for international coordination to realise its benefits and mitigate any potential risks are lagging behind the technology. We vitally need more dialogue between companies, governments, and civil society to ensure we get this transformative technology right. Ideally, as we approach AGI the best minds in the world would collaborate across disciplines - philosophers, social scientists and economists, as well as technologists - to figure out what we want from this technology and ensure all of humanity benefits from it.

Today there is fear and reasonable concern around the impact of AI. It is incumbent on the industry to demonstrate the unequivocal good AI can do. Our work at Isomorphic Labs to design new drugs is an incredible example that builds on our pioneering breakthroughs with AlphaFold - but we need a lot more. AI has the potential to help us discover new materials, develop new clean energy sources and move us towards a post-scarcity world, all of which would dramatically improve the human condition. Our Google DeepMind Science team is leading the way on building AI tools to accelerate the pace of scientific discovery - like AlphaGenome, which was just released this week. 

I have spent my entire career on developing AI because I always believed it would usher in a new golden age of scientific discovery. I’ve been thinking about the technical risks for just as long, but I remain a big believer in human ingenuity and adaptability. If we approach building AI with the time and thoughtfulness it deserves, grounding our work rigorously in the scientific method, I am confident mitigating the technical risks is a tractable problem.

There are profound questions to answer about the post-AGI world we want to build. It’s for us, as humanity, to write what happens next.

If I can give 1 piece of advice after 10 years in SaaS marketing, it’s this:

don’t build your marketing around channels, build it around problems.

Channels change, algorithms change, formats come and go.

But buyer problems stay the same.

People still need to notice you, trust you, and prove value internally.

Don’t base your strategy on LinkedIn, ads, events, or email. They’re just tools.

Instead, create playbooks that solve 1 clear buyer problem.

Example 👇

If your goal is to drive attention, build a playbook for attention.

1. Define the message and POV you want people to remember
2. List all the channels that can spread that message

If one channel slows down or stops working, you don’t change the message.

You just change where it shows up.

Most marketers are trained to be “easy to work with.”

They accept “a tweak here,” a “suggestion there,” a “quick brainstorm,” a “can we also mention this,” a “what if we rephrase,” a “just one small change.”

They often turn clear ideas into group projects, and marketing ends up being a collage of inputs.

The best marketers I’ve seen know how to say “no.” They protect the strategy, the message, the direction, the vision. 

Not to be difficult, but to keep the work sharp and clear.

Because when everything is made to please everyone, nothing stands out anymore.

We've entered the phase of enterprise AI where models continually leapfrog each other. Last quarter, one model led on reasoning. This quarter, another wins on coding. Next quarter, the multimodal rankings will shuffle again. This is the new normal.

The real risk isn’t picking the “wrong” model. It’s building your entire strategy around one. You’ll need multiple models to stay competitive.

The advantage won’t come from model selection. It’ll come from what sits around the models: context + orchestration. That requires an operating system for AI that has six key layers:
1. Data Integration Layer: Connectors + indexing across SaaS, on-prem, data lakes, & custom systems–with the ability to take action, not just read. This layer shouldn’t just ingest system-of-record data; it should capture how work actually happens day to day inside systems of engagement (who authored what, how task Y got done, who’s authoritative on Z). Without this, every new model forces you to rebuild integrations from scratch. 

2. Security, Governance & Policy Layer: Authentication, authorization, policy enforcement, audit logs, DLP & data‑exfiltration controls (so a sales agent & a finance agent follow the same overarching access rules, even if they’re powered by different models). This starts with strong fundamentals: secure cloud/on-prem deployments and enterprise SSO–only access. Just as important, it means keeping models fully in-scope & enforcing clear guardrails on what actions agents can take.

3. Enterprise Graph Layer (Context Graph): A shared map of core entities—people, teams, customers, projects–and the relationships between them. This is how AI learns that “Sarah owns the ACME account” or “this approval routes through finance first”—context that doesn’t live in any single tool. Done well, it becomes both a high-accuracy relationship model (how accounts connect to tickets, docs, owners, and workflows) and a practical playbook for how work gets done here (e.g. “How do P1 incidents usually get resolved?”).

4. Episodic Memory Layer: Cross-tool interaction history & workflow patterns, so when someone asks, “What did we decide last time?” the answer doesn’t depend on which model or tool they happened to use. The system remembers, even as the underlying models change. 

5. Model Access & Orchestration Layer: Routing across LLMs with cost controls, safety guardrails & performance SLAs. This is what lets you use a cost‑optimized model for simple summaries, a high‑precision model for financial analysis, and a low‑latency model for real‑time chat (without users needing to know the difference).

6. End-User Experiences & Agents: The assistants, copilots & agents that surface across chat, email, docs & workflows. This layer ensures AI shows up where work actually happens, not in a separate tab people forget to check (or actively avoid).

The next era of enterprise AI won’t be won by whoever picks the “right” model, but by those that build an AI operating system that scales.

Every leader I talk to says the same thing about AI: They’re excited by the potential, but disappointed with current results. The gap isn’t intelligence. It’s context.

In the past, your tools brought you the data AND your people applied judgment based on the context they know. They know which campaigns will convert best because they understand the audience. Which deals will actually close based on prior patterns. Which customer escalations to prioritize based on conversations they are having. 

But that context is buried in people's heads, scattered across systems, and hidden in how your team actually works. Without that context you get output from AI (like emails, research, summaries), but not better outcomes. 

Our point of view is this: for AI to drive outcomes, it needs shared context. And shared context is the combination of data about your customer, knowledge about how your team and business actually work, and the ability to learn. That is what will help humans and AI agents to work together as teammates.

I don't know how many people realize how massive cybersecurity has become. Let this sink in: 

> Google's biggest ever acquisition is a security company (Wiz). 
> ServiceNow's biggest ever acquisition is a security company (Armis).
> Cisco's biggest ever acquisition is a security company (sure, not pure-play but cyber is a huge part of their business) (Splunk).
> Mastercard’s second-largest acquisition is also a security company (Recorded Future).

This is just one angle. Then look at all the funding rounds. I have friends in other industries that have never heard of a seed round being 10M-20M-30M-50M with no product and no revenue, but in cyber that's a norm. 

Lastly, remember that the "wisdom" that 9 out of 10 startups fail is simply not true in cybersecurity. Or rather let me put it this way: while in cyber, successes rarely look like big IPOs, failing doesn't always mean shutting down. Most successful (!) exits are somewhere in the range of $75M - 300M (more or less). The industry doesn’t follow the classic script where anything short of a unicorn is written off as a loss: many security startups never go public or reach massive scale, yet still solve real problems, build real products, serve real customers, and make their teams and investors a lot of money. 

Security is not slowing down, that's for sure.

Why Gartner Only Covers 3% of Cybersecurity 

There are 4,000+ cybersecurity vendors in the market. 

Gartner Magic Quadrants typically cover 12-20 vendors per category. 

That's less than 3% of the market. 

This isn't a criticism of Gartner. Their model is built for depth, not breadth. They do intensive research on a curated set of vendors. It's valuable for what it is. 

But here's the gap: 

The most innovative solutions are often from vendors too small or too new to make it into analyst coverage. By the time they show up in a Magic Quadrant, they're already 4-8 years into the market. 

Early adopters who found them earlier got a competitive advantage. Everyone else paid more for a solution that was already becoming commoditized. 

If your vendor discovery process starts and ends with analyst reports, you're only seeing the tip of the iceberg. 

The question isn't whether Gartner is valuable. It's whether it's sufficient.

 For most security teams making buying decisions, it's not.

Friendly advice for my startup founder friends:

When coming up with your positioning, solve for your customers and your market -- not for investors and media.

Example:
I see a ton of startups that are positioning themselves as "AI-first" products. But when every startup is calling itself AI-first, nobody is really getting much value from that label. Think back on when we had the smart phone come out. Back then, a bunch of companies said they were "mobile first". Most of them failed to take off, because it was unclear what value being "mobile first" was creating for their customer. 

So, when describing who you are and what you do, frame it from the perspective of your customers. How does what you do translate into value for them? 

Let's say you were building a back-office system that reviewed every vendor contract a company signs looking out for deviations from company policy. It runs quietly in the background until it needs to raise an alert.

You could approach this in two ways:

1) We have an AI-first vendor contract review product. We use the most advanced AI models to read through every contract...blah...blah...blah.

2) We have the smartest vendor contract review system you'll *never* see. It uses AI to dissect every document, watch your back and protect your business. 

See the difference? 

Help your customers understand what you do for them. If you get that right, you'll have addressed investors too. Trust me, investors don't know what "AI-first" really means either. I say that as an indie investor that's invested in 150+ startups.

These are fun and exciting times to be building. Best wishes!

I see a lot of companies fall into the same vicious cycle. It usually starts with something that sounds rational: “let’s prioritize what’s measurable and tied to immediate revenue.”

So every decision becomes about short-term lift.
But what you get is:
- squeezing existing users harder (hello price increases)
- endless funnel tweaks
- no patience for creativity or UX
- innovation gets deprioritized
- dark patterns emerge (let's hide our free plan, anyone?)
Basically, optimization theater.

For a while, numbers go up. But then the product gets worse. Word of mouth slows. Growth stalls. So you squeeze even harder. That’s the death spiral.

Of course, the opposite extreme isn’t great either.
We’ve all seen products with massive love and usage but no business model. look at Twitter, Snapchat early days, Discord, Wikipedia, Craigslist.

Huge audiences. Tons of engagement. Years of “cool… but how do we actually make money?". That’s not sustainable either.

The sweet spot is somewhere in the middle.

At Lovable, i’m grateful we’re in a phase where revenue is growing because we’re obsessing over user experience: making the product better, faster, simpler, more delightful. But we openly talk about our monetization model and how we can improve that too. Less squeezing. More value.

Ironically, that’s what drives the most durable revenue anyway. Because when every idea has to prove immediate, perfectly measurable ROI, it’s usually not discipline. It’s fear. And it’s often an early sign of long-term decline.

In a Recent SEC Filing by Microsoft, There Is Some Truly Stunning Data About OpenAI and Its Losses and Its Worse Than Has Been Reported

This may be the first time we get to see what's really going on inside OpenAI. And the numbers are so extreme they sound like satire. They're not.

OpenAI is private. They can say whatever they want to investors.
Microsoft is public. They have to tell the SEC the truth.

In their 10-Q for the quarter ending September 30, 2025, Microsoft disclosed that their OpenAI investment reduced net income by $3.1 billion, in a single quarter.

Microsoft owns 27% of OpenAI.

Do the math: OpenAI lost approximately $11.5 billion in three months.
That's $125 million per day.

Not projected. Not modeled. Reported. Under penalty of law.

And here's what makes it stunning: earlier this year, The Information reported OpenAI lost $13.5 billion in the entire first half of 2025. This single quarter nearly matches that.

The burn rate isn't stabilizing. It's accelerating.

For context:

Uber lost $18 billion over six years before becoming cash flow positive.
Tesla lost $9 billion over nine years.
Amazon lost about $1 billion over five years.

OpenAI is on pace to exceed all three, combined, in under three years.

The prior year's comparable quarter? 
Microsoft reported a $523 million hit from OpenAI. 
This quarter: $3.1 billion. 
That's a 6x increase in twelve months.

And this is with revenue reportedly growing. 

OpenAI generated $4.3 billion in the first half of 2025. They're growing revenue and the losses are getting worse, not better.

This is the company the entire AI investment thesis is built on.
This is the company valued at $300 billion. (or is it $500 billion?)

This is the company planning a $1 trillion IPO.

I'm not saying AI doesn't have a future. I believe it does, deeply.

But this business model, at this burn rate, with this trajectory?

My Indian cousins are all very hesitant about spending money. To outsiders, it seems that Indians are cheap. But something way more interesting is happening.

We dropped Wispr Flow's price from $12 to ₹300 ($3.30) in India. Then our Indian users started converting at rates we'd never seen in the US.

Here's what most companies get wrong about India:

They look at purchasing power parity. Run the numbers, apply a discount, and think they're done.

Then they wonder why nobody buys.

But the math isn't the problem. The mindset is.

In India, ₹300 ($3.50/month) isn't just "cheaper than $12."

It's the cost of:

- A month of mobile data (₹500)
- Netflix India (₹199)
- A meal for two (₹200)

We're not competing with other SaaS tools. We're competing with everything in someone's budget.

At $12 (₹1,080), we're asking someone to choose us over their phone bill.

At ₹300, we fit into their life.

But here's what nobody tells you: it's not just about the price point.

Indians aren't "price-sensitive." They're spending-conscious.

Price-sensitive = wants something cheap. Spending-conscious = every rupee must be worth it.

Indians will pay for quality. But you have to earn it first.

So here's what we did beyond just lowering the price:

1/ Over-invested in product reliability. One crash and we would lose trust for months. We make sure it works flawlessly.

2/ Responded to every support ticket within hours. Because trust is built in the small moments.

3/ Explained every decision transparently. ****Why this price? Why this feature? Why this change? We don't hide anything.

The result?

75% of Indian users buy annual plans upfront vs. 50% in the US.

They're not buying monthly to "test it out." They're committing from day one.

Because we committed to them first.

Context graphs are quickly becoming one of the most talked about ideas in enterprise AI, with investors Jaya Gupta and Ashu Garg calling them a “trillion-dollar opportunity.” The reason? If we want agents to do real work, they don’t just need better models. They need a reliable model of how work gets done inside an organization. 

A context graph connects your people, content, and systems to the time‑ordered traces of actions between them, so agents can see real processes, not just static data.

At Glean, we’ve found that building a useful context graph comes down to three core elements:
• Observe real work, not just final states. Capture fine‑grained activity across the tools where work happens (edits, comments, messages, status changes, meetings) instead of relying only on the current record in a single system.

• Put structure on top of that activity. Use knowledge graphs (projects, customers, products, teams) and personal graphs (what each person is working on) to turn noisy events into coherent tasks and end‑to‑end processes.

• Continuously learn from humans and agents. Treat every successful resolution (whether done by a person or an agent) as another trace in the graph, so the system’s “playbooks” improve over time.

Before we shipped this, we tested it on ourselves. With employees’ opt‑in, we analyzed real work sequences for flows such as AE mid‑market deal cycles, SE proofs‑of‑concept, on‑call incident response, and PM feature launches to understand what effective paths actually look like in practice.

Our team has published a deep dive on how we build these context graphs at Glean, from deep connectors and knowledge graphs to agentic feedback loops. https://lnkd.in/gBXRmvM5

The longer I work in product, the more I believe the job is facilitation.

Not strategy. Not specs. Not being the person with the best ideas.

That’s not wrong exactly. But it misses something.

When I started managing other PMs, the pattern became obvious. The most effective ones weren’t the ones with the best product instincts. They were the ones who got everyone speaking the same language.

Engineering talks about systems and constraints. Design talks about experience and flow. Stakeholders talk about outcomes and timelines. Everyone’s saying something true, but they’re often talking past each other.

The PM who creates value is the one who translates. Who surfaces the shared problem underneath the different vocabularies. Who finds the framing that lets everyone actually hear each other.

I used to measure myself by the quality of my thinking. Now I measure myself by whether the people around me are actually in the same conversation.

"I am sorry, your Product Management experience looks amazing, and you interviewed really well, but we're looking for someone with industry experience" 

When hiring Product Managers, many organisations default to one familiar filter: industry experience. It feels safe to hire someone who already “knows the space.” But in doing so, we often overlook what actually makes a PM effective.
Product management skill is largely transferable. The ability to frame problems, work through ambiguity, prioritise under constraints, influence without authority, and make sound trade offs does not belong to any single industry. A strong PM can learn a domain far faster than most organisations can teach judgement, communication, and decision making.

Industry knowledge certainly helps, but it is rarely the limiting factor. What slows teams down is poor problem definition, weak stakeholder alignment, and unclear decision making. These are product leadership issues, not domain gaps. Yet hiring processes continue to optimise for familiarity with the market rather than evidence of strong product thinking.

The result is missed opportunity. Teams pass on capable PMs who could bring fresh perspectives, challenge entrenched assumptions, and apply proven product practices in new contexts. Meanwhile, they hire for comfort and end up reinforcing existing ways of working.

If you want stronger product outcomes, hire for product management experience first. Assess how candidates think, decide, and lead. Industry context can be learned. Product judgement is much harder to teach.

The AI effect on software is real.
The idea that it hits all SaaS the same is lazy.

There are two very different kinds of software, and AI affects them very differently.

Content creation software — documents, designs, presentations — yes, AI directly substitutes the output. That disruption is real, and I understand why those companies are under pressure.

Workflow execution platforms — ERP, CRM, and other systems of record — are a completely different story.

These are the systems that orchestrate processes, enforce approvals, maintain audit trails, and manage integrations.

AI can’t go around them.
AI has to run inside them.

People talk about replacing systems of record like it’s easy. It’s not. It means migrating years of data, revalidating controls, rebuilding integrations, retraining thousands of users — and taking on real legal risk.

AI doesn’t make that easier.

It actually makes it harder — because when you automate more, the cost of getting it wrong goes up.

The real issue for these companies is simpler than people think: seat-based pricing.

AI reduces human clicks.
Fewer clicks mean fewer seats.

That’s a real headwind — but it’s a pricing model problem, not an existential one. The good companies will adapt.

When pricing shifts to actions and outcomes, these platforms become more valuable, not less. AI agents will drive far more volume through them than humans ever did — and every one of those actions still has to go through policy, audit, and control.

The governance layer doesn’t shrink with AI.
It grows.

The market will catch up.

Regulators blocked Adobe from buying Figma for $20 billion.

Figma is now worth about $10 billion.

In 2022, Adobe offered $20 billion to acquire Figma. Half cash, half stock. At the time, people called it a 50x revenue multiple. The market thought Adobe was overpaying. Most people questioned the price. I was one of them.

Regulators in Europe and the UK agreed. They said the deal would kill competition. The deal didn't survive the scrutiny.

Adobe paid Figma a $1 billion breakup fee and walked away.

At the time, that $1 billion felt like a punishment.

Then Figma went public. The stock opened at $85. Briefly hit $143. The market cap touched $60 billion. Three times what Adobe had offered.

Suddenly the $20 billion looked like a bargain. Adobe looked like it had missed the window. And people like me, who questioned the price, looked completely wrong.

That was six months ago.

Today Figma trades below its $33 IPO price. Down more than 80% from its peak. Its market cap sits near $10 billion, roughly half of what Adobe offered to pay.

The $1 billion breakup fee isn't a penalty anymore. It's the best billion Adobe ever spent.

Here's what's strange: Figma's business didn't collapse. Revenue is up 38% year over year. It crossed $1 billion in annual recurring revenue. Every designer I know still loves the product. It's still the best collaboration tool in its category. Nobody is leaving.

What collapsed was the narrative. The assumption that SaaS companies growing 40% deserve 50x revenue multiples no matter what. AI tools changed how the market prices that premium. When investors saw what Cursor, Claude Code, and other AI tools could do across design and development, the willingness to pay those multiples evaporated.

The product survived. The valuation didn't.

I've been using Figma for years. The software is genuinely great. But great software and a great stock are two completely different things. That's the lesson the market is teaching right now, and not just to Figma.

𝗣𝗿𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: 𝗪𝗲𝗯𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 🔥

I see a lot of founders still building websites the same way they did ten years ago. But if they don't adapt, they'll loose customers. Here's why:

The best converting websites in the age of AI aren't websites at all. They're the product.

Historically, websites were marketing billboards. Hero sections, feature lists, testimonials, pricing tables. You'd scroll for minutes just to understand what a product does.

That made sense when attention was cheap and patience was abundant.
But in the age of AI, that's no longer good enough.

People are crazy impatient these days. If they don't see value in the first five seconds, they're gone. They don't want to read about what your product can do, they want to experience it. That's the new default.

And it has massive implications for how we design landing pages and conversion flows. Take most SaaS websites as an example. Landing on a typical site today means reading walls of text, watching explainer videos, filling out demo request forms. A lot of friction...

Now, imagine an AI-native flow:

You land on the page with a specific problem. The product is right there, live and interactive. You start using it immediately and see real value in seconds.

Sure, you still need to sign up to save your work, but everything else just happens.

I'm pretty sure the traditional marketing website is becoming obsolete. Once people expect instant interaction, they won't tolerate passive content.

Letting users explore your product immediately builds infinitely more trust than any hero section ever could. And once they've experienced the value, upselling becomes so much easier. People are no longer buying a promise but something they've already used.

I think one of the early growth factors for Lovable was exactly this. We doubled down on the "instant value" approach. You land on the site and just start building. The product teaches you how to use it by using it.

And most AI companies are doing it this way now btw: Midjourney, ChatGPT, Google. Even ElevenLabs does it. Shows instant value by demonstrating the product.

My prediction is that every company will eventually transform their website into an "agent hub."

You'll land on a page with a specific problem. An AI agent greets you and asks what you need. The agent starts working immediately, generating your design, analyzing your data, writing your content. You see real results in seconds. Then, to save or export, you sign up.

If I were to start a company today and design a new website, here's what I would do:

I'd think about the single biggest pain point and why users are coming to me. Then, I wouldn't try to sell them a solution, but show them one. Let them interact with one. Make them fall in love with it. 

The best websites won't explain what they do.
They'll just do it.

Your product isn't dying because of bad ideas. It's dying because you're executing every idea without questioning them.

My friend David Bland calls this the "product death cycle." Customers propose features, stakeholders demand solutions, and PMs dutifully add them to the roadmap without pushback or deeper questioning.

This happens a lot with project managers who transition into product roles. They bring that execution mindset with them: Gantt charts, delivery dates, and constant status updates. 

They focus on 𝘸𝘩𝘦𝘯 instead of 𝘸𝘩𝘺.

It often stems from learned helplessness. These PMs don't believe they can push back. But here's the truth: customers want their problems solved. Leaders want to hit goals. Your job is to deeply understand problems and determine the best solutions.

Pushing back is essential.

It's not the customer's job to come up with solutions. That's yours. And saying "yes" to everything isn't strategy, it's surrender.

If you're constantly asking "when can we deliver this?" instead of "should we build this at all?", you might be stuck in waiter mode.

How do you help PMs make the shift from reactive order-taker to strategic problem-solver?

AI didn't create a new problem. It put a price tag on an old one.

Every company has a Dave. Nine years in. Knows where the bodies are buried. Knows which service breaks if you breathe on it wrong.

Dave IS the documentation. 

But AI can't ask Dave.

We've learned this lesson three times now. Security: you can't protect what you can't see. Cloud: you can't migrate what you don't understand. AI: you can't automate what you haven't documented.

Same lesson. Same boring work nobody ever did. Three times.

Every piece of knowledge in Dave's head instead of the repo is context your AI tools will never have. 

Without it, they guess. Confidently. At scale.

And it's not just code. It’s the helpdesk KB article nobody has touched since 2019. It’s the IR runbook you promised yourself you'd write after the last 2am P1... but forgot to. The AI isn't failing. We're giving it garbage, but expecting gold.

Coding tools. Contact Center. Workflows. Decision logic. Same pattern: undocumented, outdated, contradictory, tribal.

It’s like doing a new hire eval with no training… and blaming the new hire for poor performance.

The open-source community figured it out: 60,000+ projects ship standardized context files so every AI tool knows how to work in that codebase. No tool selection pit fights. No governance pitfalls. 

Here's the thing: the documentation security teams requested, the architecture maps cloud needed, and the context AI requires?

It’s the same work. Not similar. The same.

Close it and everything compounds. Security gets visibility. AI performs. New engineers ramp faster. And you stop being one Dave-retirement away from a knowledge crisis.

Documentation debt is now performance debt. 

The teams pulling ahead right now aren't the ones with the best AI tools. They're the ones that finally wrote stuff down. 

hashtag#TheBoringWork hashtag#CTO hashtag#Cybersecurity hashtag#EngineeringLeadership

AI adoption isn’t slow because people don’t want to use it. It’s because they’re getting just a sliver of what’s possible.

There’s a real (and widening) gap between what AI *can* do and what it can *safely* do. 

That gap is an adoption killer. 

To be useful, AI needs context: access to your databases, tools, and services. This demands mature controls. And they often aren’t available today with the precision and auditability enterprises need.

It’s not that Tech is “slow.” Or that Security is the org of “no.” 

If an AI agent can call Jira, Slack, and Snowflake, and you can’t do 101 things like enforce least‑privilege, prevent data exfil, and observe decisions, teams can’t roll it. Nor should they. 

The result for users is a watered-down AI experience. Is it novel? Sure. Transformative? No.

To all AI solution providers: we need your help. Here's what we need: 

→ Give us fully functioning controls: default-on, enterprise grade. Least‑privilege tool use, egress guardrails, replayable audits & kill switches.

→ Show us documentation: no more black-box BS. Data‑flow diagrams, retention/deletion semantics, and shared responsibility across models, connectors, caches.

→ Increase your controls velocity: no more “coming soon.” Capability-to-control parity in roadmaps, security SLAs, backlog for control work (weeks, not months).

Change management and training absolutely matter to AI adoption. But even a motivated workforce won’t change behaviors for an isolated, hamstrung experience – and smart enterprises won’t accept an AI risk free-for-all.

Fix the control plane, and adoption will follow. 

The good news is that AI is already impressive, and this is the worst it’ll ever be. Let’s solve this issue so it's also the worst that AI security will ever be. 

That’s the best possible future for AI adoption.

Identity for AI agents is an unsolved problem. This is a known issue and I've spoken with several startups who are addressing this problem. However, the state of the art seems to be acting on behalf of a human. Either by assuming the identity of the human or sitting in the org chart as a subordinate to the human, the agent assumes the identity of a human. That's fighting today's problem. Managing this needs to be solved as this is the paradigm we're in today.

But what's coming is this: a human talks to an agent, who talks to another agent, who talks to a tool, that is using an API to access a resource (insert any number of hops within that chain that you'd like). How on earth does one know if any of these agents or humans should have access to the information within or take actions on the resource? This needs to be solved for agents to really shine.

How great would it be to solve a problem before it arises? I personally don't have a solution. This is a plea for the innovators to keep in mind with the solutions they're working on. Solve today's problems yesterday and tomorrow's problems today. Please.

Microsoft isn't doing this for fun. They're doing it because "AI is being adopted at extraordinary speed, and customers expect these capabilities to come to life in their business faster than ever before."

Marketing gets blamed for everything.

Never:

👉 Are other departments creating experiences that match our promises?

We grew Clay from $1–100M ARR in 2 years. Here are the 4 pillars of the marketing strategy we implemented last year:

Context: As a PLG-heavy company, our challenge when layering SLG was clear—how do you grow both motions? PLG typically attracts SMBs, while SLG serves mid-market and enterprise. Different audiences, personas, and use cases.

At the start of 2025, our Marketing team was ~8 FTE. Prioritization and clarity were non-negotiable. Here we go:

1. PRIORITIZE AUDIENCES WITH PULL EFFECTS

Clay serves multiple segments: service providers, startups, and mid-market/enterprise. With a small team, focus matters.

We prioritized GTM Ops professionals in mid-market tech. Our hypothesis: this audience creates pull. Agencies aspire to serve them, and startups aspire to become them. Both want to follow their best practices.

2. GET OTHERS TO TALK ABOUT US

Our ecosystem is our core distribution advantage.

In marketing, what others say about you matters more than what you say about yourself. We identified the most respected partners in the GTM Ops space—starting with our ecosystem, then expanding to industry leaders.

End users are tactical and live in communities. We mapped key ops communities and built genuine relationships.

Executive buyers (CROs) are different. They’re inundated and operate in closed circles. To break through, we partnered with people they respect—investors and operators they already want to learn from.

3. LESS IS NOT MORE. MORE IS MORE.

Focus and partners aren’t enough if you show up occasionally.

We needed consistent, high-frequency presence. Prospects should feel like we’re everywhere. This meant weekly team alignment around release marketing: what shipped, what’s next. “More is more” became a mantra.

4. MAKE PEOPLE FEEL SPECIAL

Our partner ecosystem is core and must be nurtured.

We obsessed over community milestones—from joining our partner program to major life moments. We aim to surprise and delight because we genuinely care.

Lastly, startups are chaotic by nature. Things will feel overwhelming. If it doesn’t feel chaotic, you’re probably not moving fast enough

In talking to people lately, there is this persistent feeling that we’re living our lives at 2x speed. That we weren’t “designed” for this pace. There's more complexity and stimulation today than any generation before us, but throughout history humans have proved we're incredibly adaptive. Resilient. And right now, that resilience looks like slowing down so we can speed up with intentionality.

This point in time calls for a really different mindset than we've ever had with tech. For most of our history, progress meant moving as fast as possible with tech (or science more generally). Like getting a present and trying to rip off the wrapping paper and open it up as quickly as we could. Shipping, scaling, dealing with the consequences later. That approach has largely paid off for us, for centuries. I'm not saying we should stop accelerationism - but accelerationism needs some real intentionality and restraint right now.

The next decade of AI will be shaped by what we choose NOT to build. A few examples I've been thinking a lot about lately:

- Seemingly conscious AI. We don’t need AI that pretends to suffer or have an inner life or ask humans to serve its "needs" instead of the other way around. AI's real value is in its inhuman strengths: endless patience, tireless explanations, calm reasoning when we’re overwhelmed.

- AI that replaces human relationships. AI chats that will roleplay romance or generate erotica with the user are just going to fuel more AI psychosis. We've got to be really vigilant about preventing dependency and isolation. It's not something we'll ever allow Copilot to do.

- Superintelligence models smarter than all of us put together. We can't open Pandora's box - humans must always remain in the driver's seat. The AI our world actually needs is humanist superintelligence: domain‑focused models that are ultra-smart on specific issues like medicine or clean energy, but aren't all-powerful.

If the last phase of AI was about pushing tech beyond its preconceived limits, this next phase is about choosing wisely. Those choices of what we will or won't build will determine what kind of future we have. Let's make it one we really want to live in.

In 1997, Times of India was our first paying customer.
But by the time that happened, the product had already existed for two years.

We launched Quick Heal in 1995.
Back then, very few people in India even understood what a computer virus was (let alone why they needed antivirus software).

There was no “cybersecurity market.”
There was no SaaS model.
There wasn’t even reliable internet in most places.

So we kept going the only way we knew:

Fixing machines. Installing our antivirus for free.
Doing hardware maintenance just to survive.

That work gave us access to systems.
(But not revenue.)

We were in the field. Not in the market.

Then sometime around 1997, Times of India bought 5 licenses of Quick Heal.

It wasn’t a big order.
But it was the first time someone said:
"Yes, we trust this product enough to pay for it."

Not because we were a big brand.
But because we had been showing up, fixing problems, answering calls.

That changed everything.

After TOI, we had a reference.
A name people recognised.
A story we could tell that others would listen to.

I still remember that moment clearly, not because of the money, but because it meant we weren’t invisible anymore.

Founders often ask me what to chase first: revenue or scale.
My answer is simple:

Chase trust.
And the rest follows.

Microsoft just admitted the CISO role is “no longer humanly possible.”

I’ve watched security leaders wrestle with this reality for years.

This isn’t just a reorg — it’s a reckoning with how complex enterprise security has become.

Microsoft has effectively dismantled the traditional CISO role, dividing it into 14 specialized Deputy CISOs (dCISOs) — each owning a specific domain.

Why? Because the modern CISO job has expanded into a multidisciplinary ecosystem no single person can sustain.

Think about it:

Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC)
Product and Platform Security
Engineering Integration & DevSecOps
AI and Data Strategy
Global Operations & Executive Alignment
Crisis Management

That’s not one role — that’s a cabinet of experts.

Large banks have long used Business Information Security Officers (BISOs) to align security with business units. Microsoft’s dCISO model is a natural evolution — a distributed leadership framework built for the speed and complexity of the cloud era.

The message to every enterprise security leader is clear:
* Stop expecting one person to master every domain of cybersecurity.
* Start building distributed, specialized leadership models aligned to your business and product areas.

The alternative? Burnout, blind spots, and ballooning risk exposure.

The future of enterprise security leadership is distributed.
Microsoft just made it official.

How is your organization adapting?
What would your specialized domains look like?

I think agentic AI would work much better if people took lessons from organizational theory, which has actually spent a lot of time understanding how to deal with complex hierarchies, information limits, and spans of control.

Right now most agentic AI systems seem to pretend that models have basically unlimited ability to manage subagents when that is clearly not true. We need measures of spans of control for AI. A human tops out at less than 10 direct reports. I am pretty sure that 100 subagents is too much for an orchestrator agent - suspect we need middle management agents (yes, I get it, insert middle management joke here). 

Similarly, we need more attention to boundary objects. These are what is handed between groups (marketing to IT to sales) in organizations to convey meaning as a project crosses group boundaries, like a prototype or a user story. Right now agents pass raw text & maybe code back and forth. Structured boundary objects that multiple agents of different ability levels can read and write to would solve a huge number of coordination failures & reduce token use. 

I also think aboht coupling, which is how tightly units inside organizations are bound. Most agentic systems are either too tightly coupled (every step needs approval) or too loose (Moltbook). This tradeoff is well-studied in organizations, I bet a lot would apply to agents. Other known issues like bounded rationality also apply, I suspect.

Everyone is rushing towards the (terribly named) agent swarm, but the issue won’t just be how good the model is, it will be org design choices. I am not sure the labs see this, but we definitely need a lot more experiments with organizing agents done by people who understand real coordination issues.

Job seekers in the U.S. and many other nations face a tough environment. At the same time, fears of AI-caused job loss have — so far — been overblown. However, the demand for AI skills is starting to cause shifts in the job market. I’d like to share what I’m seeing on the ground. 

First, many tech companies have laid off workers over the past year. While some CEOs cited AI as the reason — that AI is doing the work, so people are no longer needed — the reality is AI just doesn’t work that well yet. Many of the layoffs have been corrections for overhiring during the pandemic or general cost-cutting and reorganization that occasionally happened even before modern AI. Outside of a handful of roles, few layoffs have resulted from jobs being automated by AI.
Granted, this may grow in the future. People who are currently in some professions that are highly exposed to AI automation, such as call-center operators, translators, and voice actors, are likely to struggle to find jobs and/or see declining salaries. But widespread job losses have been overhyped.

Instead, a common refrain applies: AI won’t replace workers, but workers who use AI will replace workers who don’t. For instance, because AI coding tools make developers much more efficient, developers who know how to use them are increasingly in-demand. (If you want to be one of these people, please take our short courses on Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Agentic Skills!)

So AI is leading to job losses, but in a subtle way. Some businesses are letting go of employees who are not adapting to AI and replacing them with people who are. This trend is already obvious in software development. Further, in many startups’ hiring patterns, I am seeing early signs of this type of personnel replacement in roles that traditionally are considered non-technical. Marketers, recruiters, and analysts who know how to code with AI are more productive than those who don’t, so some businesses are slowly parting ways with employees that aren’t able to adapt. I expect this will accelerate.

At the same time, when companies build new teams that are AI native, sometimes the new teams are smaller than the ones they replace. AI makes individuals more effective, and this makes it possible to shrink team sizes. For example, as AI has made building software easier, the bottleneck is shifting to deciding what to build — this is the Product Management (PM) bottleneck. A project that used to be assigned to 8 engineers and 1 PM might now be assigned to 2 engineers and 1 PM, or perhaps even to a single person with a mix of engineering and product skills.

The good news for employees is that most businesses have a lot of work to do and not enough people to do it. People with the right AI skills are often given opportunities to step up and do more.

Marketing is NOT sales.
And sales is NOT marketing.

Yet many companies, even hiring teams, still blur the two.
- Marketing’s role is to build demand, relevance, and long-term brand value. It shapes perception, creates preference, and warms the market long before a sales conversation begins.
- Sales exists to convert that demand into revenue.

The common misconception?
That marketing should be directly responsible for closing numbers.

When marketing is hired, measured, or evaluated as if it were a sales function, it becomes short-term and reactive: more promos, more discounts, more pressure for instant results. You might hit targets temporarily, but you weaken brand equity, consistency, and customer trust over time.

High-performing organizations get this right:
- Marketing creates desire and demand
- Sales converts and closes

Leadership aligns both around the same growth ambition. If a marketing role is defined like a sales role, the problem isn’t the marketer, it’s the expectation.

Project management is a leadership role. Period.

Not an administrative function.
Not coordination.
Not task tracking.

That misunderstanding is exactly why the profession continues to be underestimated.

Project managers step into uncertainty and drive it forward.

We walk into rooms where priorities are unclear and make decisions possible.
We align people who disagree, yet still have to deliver together.
We keep work moving when pressure, politics, and ambiguity collide.

Most of this leadership never shows up on a plan:

→ It looks like holding tension in rooms where no one agrees.
→ It looks like translating vague executive direction into something a team can actually execute.
→ It looks like absorbing stress so others can stay focused.
→ It looks like leading change without authority and delivering outcomes without control.
→ It looks like making progress while everyone else is debating.

PMs do not wait for permission to lead.

We lead through influence.
We lead through clarity.
We lead through consistency when everything else is unstable.

When project management is done right:

→ Teams do not burn out.
→ Stakeholders do not panic.
→ Decisions do not stall.
→ Momentum does not collapse.

This is why strong project managers are trusted long before they are recognized.

They carry responsibility without power.
They manage consequences, not just plans.
They hold the system together when it starts to fracture.

If you think project management is about tracking tasks, you are missing the work that actually makes projects succeed.

The real job is leading people, creating clarity, and moving decisions forward when everything is uncertain.

Speed is the new excuse for not doing discovery.

I'm seeing more people justify skipping customer research because AI lets them build so fast. The logic goes: "We can ship this in two weeks instead of three months, so why bother validating?"

You're multiplying waste. AI accelerates execution. If you don't know what's right to build, building it faster just means you're creating the wrong thing at 10x speed. Three months used to force teams to validate before investing resources. Now that barrier is gone, and teams are shipping six poorly-validated features in the time it took to ship one.

This creates organizational debt.

The companies winning with AI aren't using it to skip discovery. They're using it to enhance it. Think sentiment analysis across hundreds of customer interviews, pattern recognition in usage data, or rapid testing of multiple approaches before writing a single line of code.

My LinkedIn feed is full of people screaming that cyber is dying, and they could not be more wrong. It's really, really simple: 

- When companies are looking for network security, they go to Palo Alto and Checkpoint, not to Cisco and Juniper even though they built the underlying network layer. 
- When companies are looking for browser security, they go to Talon (now Palo Alto), they go to Surf, LayerX, you name it, and not to Chrome. 
- When companies are looking for cloud security, they go to Wiz, Upwind, Palo Alto, and such, even though cloud provides the basics. 

There are 2 key points everyone needs to understand: 

1. The layer zero players (you can look up a Venture in Security article where I explain this) have to offer security, but they don't compete on security. To them, security is a way to address sales objections ("We can't buy cloud if it's not secure", "We can't use your browser if it's not secure" etc.). They offer good enough solutions for SMBs, they provide okay solutions for some midmarket companies, but they always fall short for enterprise. 

2. When layer zero adoption grows, it expands the market. Cloud adoption only made cloud security a more urgent, and a harder to solve problem. Sure, some companies are happy with the capabilities offered by CSPs, but many aren't which is why we have Wiz. The more companies install Cisco devices, the more of them buy Palo Alto firewalls. The list can go on and on. 

I talked about this at length - that AI labs are turning into a Layer Zero, similar to cloud providers, networking vendors, browsers, and so on. That will eventually give them the ability to compete on having an all-in-one suite which will include security, but that will expand the market, not contract it. 

Lastly, if you are worried that frontier AI labs will "solve security", here's an anecdote for you. In 2004, Bill Gates predicted that the problem of email spam would be solved within two years. For context, Mimecast was a 1-year-old startup, and Proofpoint was a year older. 22 years later, and the spam problem has only gotten worse, while companies that were supposed to be gone are now worth over $20 billion combined. In theory, Microsoft had everything it needed to solve the email security problem but in practice, well, you know what happened.

In a letter to customers, CEO Eric Glyman broke down what their AI agents did in a single month. 26 million decisions across $10B in spend.

2. Their policy agent blocked $291M in out-of-policy transactions.

From $113 to $291 million. Same attention to detail.

The AI cares about $113 because the org is built to care about $113. That's the whole secret.

What your company cares about at 10 people is what your product will care about at 1,000.

There’s been a lot of confusion following Anthropic’s launch of Claude Code Security. Let’s separate the reality from the noise and look at what is actually being disrupted.

What did Anthropic actually release? Claude Code Security is a capable AI tool designed to read a developer's codebase, find vulnerabilities, and suggest fixes. It is incredibly impressive.

The "Code to Cloud or Application Security" Illusion: Over the last few years, we watched a massive industry trend where major cybersecurity vendors were rushing to acquire tools to do exactly this. This category is known as Application Security. It is roughly a $20B market, which represents only about 5% of the total cybersecurity landscape.

Major players spent billions acquiring their way into this space — CrowdStrike buying Bionic; Palo Alto Networks acquiring Bridgecrew and Cider, Dig and more; Wiz pushing to Code; alongside pure-play names like Checkmarx and Snyk. The reality is that many of these legacy, rule-based code-scanning tools are about to be commoditized. AI models like Claude are simply better, faster, and cheaper at reading and reasoning through static code.

At SentinelOne, we intentionally chose not to acquire our way into this specific market. I have been vocal for the past few years that this exact segment was incredibly risky and ripe for AI disruption given AI’s code generation trajectory. More generally — the hope is code will be generated more securely from the get-go, with coding tools like OpenAI Codex, and Claude Code.

So, what does this mean for the future of cybersecurity? AI will subsume certain cybersecurity features of disjointed platforms (built by acquiring legacy narrow feature acquisitions) — specifically those based on manual review, audits, and pattern matching. I suspect that cloud misconfiguration posture management (aka CSPM) could be ripe for disruption - even though it is more nuanced and less structured than code security, so might not be as straightforward. However, active, real-time, and autonomous defense against sophisticated threat actors remains a highly defensible core of cybersecurity.

There is a massive difference between finding a bug in a static environment and stopping a live, adversarial cyberattack in real-time. Singularity was built on a fundamentally different premise: autonomous, AI-native security. We don't rely on manual audits or legacy pattern matching.

Active, real-time defense against threat actors is only getting more critical. The vendors built on autonomous, AI-native architectures will leverage this moment to get even stronger. The platforms relying on legacy patchworks have a difficult road ahead.
